topics.title: Basic Engine Nomenclature
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAUT205 - AUTOMOBILE POWER PLANT (AUTOMOBILE ENGINEERING) > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Basic Engine Nomenclature # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is Engine Nomenclature? In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PCAUT205)**, **Engine Nomenclature** refers to the universally standardized set of geometric, kinematic, and thermodynamic terms used to describe the construction and operational parameters of internal combustion (IC) engines. It forms the foundational vocabulary required to communicate engine design specifications, performance metrics, and combustion geometry. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PCAUT205 - Module 1):** > This topic is a direct prerequisite for all subsequent modules, including Combustion in SI Engines, Combustion in CI Engines, Engine Performance, and Emissions. The KTU Board Examiner expects students to internalize these definitions as they appear as **direct 2-mark/3-mark questions** in Part A of the ESE (End Semester Evaluation). ## 1.2 Classification of IC Engines (High-Level Overview) Before diving into nomenclature, an engine must be classified. The standard hierarchical classification used by KTU is: **A. Based on Ignition Process:** - **Spark Ignition (SI) Engine:** Uses an external spark plug (e.g., Petrol/Gasoline engine). - **Compression Ignition (CI) Engine:** Uses heat of compression for ignition (e.g., Diesel engine). **B. Based on Cycle of Operation:** - **4-Stroke Engine:** One working cycle in 2 crank revolutions (720°). - **2-Stroke Engine:** One working cycle in 1 crank revolution (360°). **C. Based on Cylinder Arrangement:** - Inline, V-type, Opposed (Flat/Boxer), Radial, W-type. ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy: The "Syringe Engine" > [!NOTE] > **Intuition Box — Think of a Bicycle Pump:** > Imagine a bicycle pump. You pull the handle up (creating empty space), then push it down (compressing air). Now imagine if, at the moment of maximum compression, a tiny spark ignited the air-fuel mixture, forcing the handle back up with explosive force — and that force turned a wheel. **An IC engine is essentially an automated, continuously cycling version of this pump.** Every term we will define below describes either a part of this "pump" (geometry) or how fast the handle moves (kinematics). ## 1.4 The Major Engine Components (Visual Vocabulary) | Component | Function | |---|---| | **Cylinder Block** | Houses the cylinders; main structural body. | | **Cylinder Head** | Closes the cylinder from the top; contains valves and spark plug. | | **Piston** | Reciprocates inside the cylinder; receives combustion force. | | **Connecting Rod** | Converts reciprocating motion of piston to rotary motion of crank. | | **Crankshaft** | Rotates to deliver power; converts linear motion. | | **Valves (Inlet/Exhaust)** | Control gas exchange into/out of the cylinder. | | **Spark Plug (SI)** / **Fuel Injector (CI)** | Initiates combustion. | | **Crankshaft** | **Two Critical Reference Centers**: TDC and BDC. | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition — TDC (Top Dead Center):** The extreme position of the piston at the top of the cylinder, where the piston cannot move any further upward. Also called **Outer Dead Center (ODC)**. > > **Definition — BDC (Bottom Dead Center):** The extreme position of the piston at the bottom of the cylinder, where the piston cannot move any further downward. Also called **Inner Dead Center (IDC)**. --- ## 1.5 The Three "Volumes" Every Engine Has These three volumes are the heart of engine nomenclature. Memorize them visually: ``` TDC ──────────► ● ← Piston at Top (smallest volume = Vc) ← Clearance Volume (Vc) BDC ──────────► ● ← Piston at Bottom (largest volume = V1) ← Total Cylinder Volume (V1) ``` **Volume Legend:** - **$V_c$ — Clearance Volume:** The volume above the piston when it is at TDC. The "small" volume. - **$V_s$ — Swept Volume (Displacement Volume):** The volume swept by the piston as it moves from TDC to BDC. The "medium" volume. - **$V_1$ — Total Cylinder Volume:** $V_c + V_s$. The "largest" volume. > [!NOTE] > **Mnemonic for Students:** "**C**learance = **C**losed (TDC); **S**wept = **S**pace the piston travels." # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Geometric Nomenclature (Static Dimensions) These dimensions are **fixed by the manufacturer** and define the *size* of the engine. ### 2.1.1 Bore ($D$ or $d$) - **Definition:** The internal diameter of the cylinder. - **Symbol:** $D$ (preferred) or $d$ — measured in **mm** or **cm**. - **"Why" It Matters:** The bore determines the cross-sectional area over which combustion pressure acts. Larger bore = larger force on piston = higher potential power. ### 2.1.2 Stroke Length ($L$ or $l$) - **Definition:** The linear distance traveled by the piston from TDC to BDC (one-half of one crank revolution). - **Symbol:** $L$ — measured in **mm** or **cm**. - **"Why" It Matters:** Stroke determines the swept volume and influences mean piston speed. ### 2.1.3 Crank Radius ($r$) - **Definition:** The perpendicular distance from the crankshaft axis to the crank pin center. - **Fundamental Geometric Relationship:** $L = 2r$ - The piston moves a total distance equal to **twice the crank radius** in one stroke. ### 2.1.4 Connecting Rod Length ($l_c$ or $l$) - **Definition:** The distance between the piston pin center and the crank pin center. - **Typical Range:** $l_c / r$ ratio is typically **3 to 4.5** for passenger car engines. ## 2.2 Kinematic Nomenclature (Motion-Based) These describe *how things move* in the engine. ### 2.2.1 Mean Piston Speed ($\bar{V_p}$ or $S_p$) - **Definition:** The average speed of the piston, considering both upward and downward strokes. - **Why Average?** The piston actually moves faster in the middle of the stroke and slower near TDC/BDC (sinusoidal motion). Mean piston speed is a useful single-number summary. - **Typical Range:** **5 to 15 m/s** for modern engines. - **Engineering Significance:** Governs valve timing, friction losses, and volumetric efficiency. ### 2.2.2 Piston Acceleration ($a_p$) - **Definition:** The rate of change of piston velocity. Maximum at TDC; zero at mid-stroke. - **Engineering Significance:** Drives the inertial forces on the piston, which influence engine vibration and bearing loads. ### 2.2.3 Engine Speed ($N$) - **Definition:** The rotational speed of the crankshaft. - **Unit:** **rev/min (rpm)** or rev/s. - **Typical:** 800 rpm (idle) to 6000+ rpm (redline) for passenger cars. ## 2.3 Volumetric Nomenclature (The Three Volumes) We already introduced these in Section 1.5. Now let's quantify them: ### 2.3.1 Clearance Volume ($V_c$) - **Definition:** Volume trapped in the combustion chamber above the piston at TDC. - **Includes:** Combustion chamber volume in the cylinder head + any volume in the head gasket + piston crown cavity (if any). - **Significance:** Sets the upper limit on compression that can be achieved. ### 2.3.2 Swept Volume ($V_s$) - **Definition:** Volume displaced by one piston in one stroke. - **Engineering Significance:** A larger swept volume generally means more power potential. ### 2.3.3 Total Cylinder Volume ($V_1$ or $V_a$) - **Definition:** Maximum volume in the cylinder when the piston is at BDC. - **Significance:** Used as the reference state in thermodynamic cycle analysis. ## 2.4 Performance Ratios (The Most Exam-Relevant) ### 2.4.1 Compression Ratio ($r$ or $r_c$) - **Definition:** The ratio of total volume to clearance volume. - **Typical Values:** - **SI engines:** 8:1 to 12:1 (modern high-performance up to 14:1) - **CI engines:** 14:1 to 22:1 - **Why Different?** SI engines are limited by **knock** (premature auto-ignition); CI engines need high compression to heat the air sufficiently. ### 2.4.2 Mean Effective Pressure (MEP) - **Definition:** A fictitious, constant pressure that, if applied on the piston throughout the power stroke, would produce the same net work as the actual (varying) pressure. - **Two Main Types:** - **BMEP (Brake MEP):** Based on brake power (output). - **IMEP (Indicated MEP):** Based on indicated power (developed in cylinder). - **Significance:** Allows comparison of engines of different sizes — a larger BMEP means the engine produces more torque per unit displacement. ### 2.4.3 Specific Output - **Definition:** Power output per unit weight (kW/kg) or per unit displacement (kW/L). ## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Memorize the following table — it covers ~70% of Module 1 numerical questions.** | Parameter | Formula | Unit | Boundary / Typical Value | |---|---|---|---| | Swept Volume | $V_s = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2 L$ | $m^3$ or $cc$ | — | | Total Cylinder Volume | $V_1 = V_c + V_s$ | $m^3$ or $cc$ | — | | Compression Ratio | $r = \dfrac{V_1}{V_c} = 1 + \dfrac{V_s}{V_c}$ | Dimensionless | SI: 8–12, CI: 14–22 | | Crank Radius | $r = L / 2$ | m | — | | Mean Piston Speed | $\bar{V_p} = \dfrac{2 L N}{60}$ for 4-stroke | m/s | 5–15 m/s | | Piston Speed (2-stroke) | $\bar{V_p} = \dfrac{2 L N}{60}$ | m/s | — | | Engine Displacement | $V_D = n \times V_s$ (n = num. cylinders) | $cc$ or $L$ | — | | Power (Indicated) | $IP = \dfrac{p_{im} \, L \, A \, N \, n}{60}$ | kW | — | | Torque Relation | $P = \dfrac{2 \pi N T}{60}$ | W | — | | BMEP (Brake) | $BMEP = \dfrac{W_b}{V_s} \cdot \dfrac{N}{N} = \dfrac{4 \pi T}{V_s}$ | kPa | 800–1500 kPa (SI), 700–1000 kPa (CI) | | Fuel-Air Ratio | $F/A = \dot{m_f} / \dot{m_a}$ | Dimensionless | Stoich: ~14.7 (petrol) | **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** Absolute values (e.g., $\vert x \vert$) are rendered using `\vert` to avoid breaking table syntax. ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility In actual automotive design, these nomenclatures drive **every** design decision: - **Bore and Stroke Ratio ($D/L$):** A "square" engine ($D \approx L$) is balanced. An "over-bore" ($D > L$) allows higher revs. An "under-bore" or "long-stroke" ($L > D$) gives more torque at low RPM (typical of diesel tractors). - **Compression Ratio:** Tunes performance vs. fuel economy vs. emissions vs. knock resistance. - **Mean Piston Speed:** A hard limit on engine RPM — most road engines cap at ~20 m/s mean piston speed. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Swept Volume ($V_s$) The swept volume is the volume of a cylinder of **bore $D$** and **height $L$** (the stroke). **Step 1:** Write the formula for the area of a circle of diameter $D$. $$ A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} $$ **Step 2:** Multiply this area by the height (stroke) $L$ to get the volume. $$ V_s = A \times L $$ **Step 3:** Substitute the area expression. $$ V_s = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \times L = \frac{\pi D^2 L}{4} $$ > **Logic:** This is simply the volume of a cylinder where the piston "sweeps" from top to bottom. ## 3.2 Derivation of Mean Piston Speed **Step 1:** In one full revolution of the crankshaft (for a 4-stroke), each piston completes **two strokes** (one up, one down) — except that one revolution = one up + one down. So in 1 revolution, the piston travels a total distance of $2L$. **Step 2:** Express engine speed $N$ in revolutions per second. $$ N_{rps} = \frac{N}{60} $$ **Step 3:** The piston covers a total distance of $2L$ per revolution. Therefore, distance covered per second is $2L \cdot N_{rps}$. $$ \bar{V_p} = 2L \cdot \frac{N}{60} = \frac{2 L N}{60} $$ > **Logic:** Mean piston speed = (total distance traveled per minute) ÷ 60 seconds = (number of strokes per revolution × stroke length × RPM) / 60. ## 3.3 Derivation of Compression Ratio **Step 1:** By definition, compression ratio is total volume divided by clearance volume. $$ r = \frac{V_1}{V_c} $$ **Step 2:** Substitute $V_1 = V_c + V_s$. $$ r = \frac{V_c + V_s}{V_c} $$ **Step 3:** Split the fraction. $$ r = \frac{V_c}{V_c} + \frac{V_s}{V_c} = 1 + \frac{V_s}{V_c} $$ > **Logic:** If clearance volume is small compared to swept volume, the ratio grows large — meaning stronger compression. If $V_s = 0$ (no movement), $r = 1$ (no compression). ## 3.4 Derivation of Indicated Power (Simplified) **Step 1:** Work done per cycle per cylinder is the product of mean effective pressure, swept volume. $$ W_{cycle} = p_{im} \cdot V_s $$ **Step 2:** For a 4-stroke engine, the number of power strokes per second per cylinder is $N / (2 \cdot 60)$. (Two revolutions per cycle.) $$ \text{Power strokes/sec} = \frac{N}{120} $$ **Step 3:** Indicated power per cylinder: $$ IP_{cyl} = p_{im} \cdot V_s \cdot \frac{N}{120} $$ **Step 4:** For $n$ cylinders: $$ IP = p_{im} \cdot V_s \cdot n \cdot \frac{N}{120} = \frac{p_{im} \, L \, A \, n \, N}{120} $$ > **Logic:** Power = Work per cycle × Number of cycles per second. ## 3.5 Derivation of Torque from Power **Step 1:** Power is the rate of doing work. For a rotating shaft: $$ P = T \cdot \omega $$ **Step 2:** Angular velocity $\omega$ in rad/s is related to RPM $N$ by: $$ \omega = \frac{2 \pi N}{60} $$ **Step 3:** Substitute: $$ P = T \cdot \frac{2 \pi N}{60} $$ **Step 4:** Solving for torque: $$ T = \frac{60 P}{2 \pi N} = \frac{30 P}{\pi N} $$ > **Logic:** Given any two of (Power, Torque, RPM), the third can be found. This is the bedrock relationship for all engine dynamometer calculations. ## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style) > [!NOTE] > **Sample Problem:** A 4-cylinder, 4-stroke SI engine has a bore of **80 mm** and a stroke of **90 mm**. The clearance volume per cylinder is **60 cc**. The engine runs at **3000 rpm**. Calculate: (a) Swept volume per cylinder, (b) Compression ratio, (c) Total engine displacement, (d) Mean piston speed. **Given:** $D = 0.080$ m, $L = 0.090$ m, $V_c = 60$ cc, $N = 3000$ rpm, $n = 4$ cylinders, 4-stroke. ### (a) Swept Volume per Cylinder $$ V_s = \frac{\pi D^2 L}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (0.080)^2 \times 0.090}{4} $$ $$ V_s = \frac{\pi \times 0.0064 \times 0.090}{4} = \frac{0.001809}{4} = 0.000452 \text{ m}^3 $$ $$ V_s = 452.39 \text{ cc} $$ ### (b) Compression Ratio $$ r = 1 + \frac{V_s}{V_c} = 1 + \frac{452.39}{60} = 1 + 7.540 = 8.54 $$ ### (c) Total Engine Displacement $$ V_D = n \times V_s = 4 \times 452.39 = 1809.56 \text{ cc} \approx 1.81 \text{ L} $$ ### (d) Mean Piston Speed (4-stroke) $$ \bar{V_p} = \frac{2 L N}{60} = \frac{2 \times 0.090 \times 3000}{60} = \frac{540}{60} = 9.0 \text{ m/s} $$ **Result Check:** All values are within typical SI engine ranges ✓ # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Engine Geometry & Cylinder Volume Nomenclature ```mermaid graph TD A[Crankshaft Rotates] --> B[Piston Position Changes] B --> C{Piston Location?} C -- At TDC --> D[Clearance Volume Vc - Smallest Volume] C -- At BDC --> E[Total Cylinder Volume V1 - Largest Volume] D -- Swept from TDC to BDC --> F[Swept Volume Vs - Displacement] F -- Combined with Vc --> E E -- Divided by Vc --> G[Compression Ratio r] G --> H{SI or CI Engine?} H -- SI --> I[r = 8 to 12] H -- CI --> J[r = 14 to 22] D -- Bore D --> K[Cross Section Area] K -- Times L --> F A -- Speed N rpm --> L[Mean Piston Speed] L -- Formula --> M[2 L N / 60] ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Engine Component Block Architecture ```mermaid graph LR subgraph CylinderHead[Top of Engine] V1[Inlet Valve] V2[Exhaust Valve] SP[Spark Plug or Injector] end subgraph CylinderBlock[Middle Section] P1[Piston] CR[Combustion Chamber] CY[Cylinder Liner] end subgraph Crankcase[Bottom Section] CR1[Connecting Rod] CS1[Crankshaft] FW[Flywheel] end SP -- Ignites mixture in --> CR CR -- Pressure pushes --> P1 P1 -- Reciprocates via --> CR1 CR1 -- Rotates --> CS1 CS1 -- Drives --> FW V1 -- Admits fresh charge --> CR V2 -- Expels burned gases --> CR ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Four-Stroke Cycle State Machine ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 state "Intake Stroke
Piston moves TDC to BDC
Inlet valve OPEN" as S1 state "Compression Stroke
Piston moves BDC to TDC
Both valves CLOSED" as S2 state "Power Stroke
Combustion pushes piston TDC
Both valves CLOSED" as S3 state "Exhaust Stroke
Piston moves BDC to TDC
Exhaust valve OPEN" as S4 [*] --> S1 S1 --> S2 S2 --> S3 S3 --> S4 S4 --> [*] S4 --> S1 : Cycle Repeats ``` ## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Classification of IC Engines (Hierarchy) ```mermaid graph TD Root[Internal Combustion Engine] --> A1[Based on Ignition] Root --> A2[Based on Cycle] Root --> A3[Based on Arrangement] A1 --> B1[SI Engine - Spark Ignition] A1 --> B2[CI Engine - Compression Ignition] A2 --> C1[4-Stroke - 720 degrees] A2 --> C2[2-Stroke - 360 degrees] A3 --> D1[Inline] A3 --> D2[V Type] A3 --> D3[Flat or Boxer] A3 --> D4[Radial] A1 --> A4[Based on Fuel] A4 --> F1[Petrol Engine] A4 --> F2[Diesel Engine] A4 --> F3[Gas Engine CNG LPG] A4 --> F4[Flex Fuel Ethanol] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]** **Define the following terms with respect to IC engines:** **(a) Clearance volume, (b) Swept volume, (c) Compression ratio.** ### Model Answer: **(a) Clearance Volume ($V_c$):** The volume of the combustion chamber above the piston when the piston is at **Top Dead Center (TDC)**. It is the smallest volume in the cylinder. **(b) Swept Volume ($V_s$):** The volume displaced by the piston as it moves from **TDC to BDC** in one stroke. Mathematically, $$ V_s = \frac{\pi D^2 L}{4} $$ **(c) Compression Ratio ($r$):** The ratio of the total cylinder volume to the clearance volume. $$ r = \frac{V_1}{V_c} = 1 + \frac{V_s}{V_c} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Valuation Tip (3 Marks Distribution):** > - Definition of $V_c$: 1 mark > - Formula for $V_s$: 1 mark > - Formula for $r$: 1 mark --- ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Expected Pattern]** **Explain the difference between BMEP and IMEP. Why is BMEP always less than IMEP?** ### Model Answer: **IMEP (Indicated Mean Effective Pressure):** It is a hypothetical constant pressure that, if acted on the piston during the power stroke, would produce the same indicated work as the actual varying cycle pressure. **BMEP (Brake Mean Effective Pressure):** It is a hypothetical constant pressure based on the **brake (output) power** delivered at the crankshaft. **Key Difference:** | Parameter | IMEP | BMEP | |---|---|---| | Based on | Indicated Power (in-cylinder) | Brake Power (output) | | Includes friction | No (friction work is separate) | Yes (friction losses already deducted) | **Why BMEP < IMEP:** Because BMEP is derived from the brake power, which is indicated power minus mechanical friction losses (in bearings, piston rings, water pump, oil pump, etc.). $$ BP = IP - Friction\ Power $$ Hence, $BMEP < IMEP$ always. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — With Internal Choice > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern Reminder:** Part B questions carry 14 marks. Each Part B question has **internal choice** — the student must answer EITHER Question A OR Question B. The structure is typically two sub-parts: (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Modified Pattern]** **(a)** A 4-cylinder, 4-stroke petrol engine has a bore of **75 mm** and stroke of **85 mm**. The compression ratio is **9:1**. Calculate: (i) Clearance volume per cylinder (ii) Total swept volume (iii) Mean piston speed at **2800 rpm** **(7 Marks)** **(b)** With neat sketches, explain the following engine terms: (i) Top Dead Center (TDC) (ii) Bottom Dead Center (BDC) (iii) Stroke Length **(7 Marks)** ### Model Solution for Question A: **Solution to (a):** **Given Data:** - $D = 75$ mm $= 0.075$ m - $L = 85$ mm $= 0.085$ m - $r = 9$ (compression ratio) - $N = 2800$ rpm - $n = 4$ cylinders, 4-stroke **Step 1: Calculate swept volume per cylinder.** $$ V_s = \frac{\pi D^2 L}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (0.075)^2 \times 0.085}{4} $$ $$ V_s = \frac{\pi \times 0.005625 \times 0.085}{4} = \frac{0.001502}{4} = 0.0003755 \text{ m}^3 $$ $$ V_s = 375.5 \text{ cc} $$ **Step 2: Calculate clearance volume per cylinder.** $$ r = 1 + \frac{V_s}{V_c} \implies V_c = \frac{V_s}{r - 1} $$ $$ V_c = \frac{375.5}{9 - 1} = \frac{375.5}{8} = 46.94 \text{ cc} $$ **Step 3: Total swept volume (engine displacement).** $$ V_D = n \times V_s = 4 \times 375.5 = 1502 \text{ cc} \approx 1.50 \text{ L} $$ **Step 4: Mean piston speed.** $$ \bar{V_p} = \frac{2 L N}{60} = \frac{2 \times 0.085 \times 2800}{60} $$ $$ \bar{V_p} = \frac{476}{60} = 7.93 \text{ m/s} $$ **Summary of Answers:** | Quantity | Value | |---|---| | Swept volume per cylinder | **375.5 cc** | | Clearance volume per cylinder | **46.94 cc** | | Total engine displacement | **1502 cc (1.5 L)** | | Mean piston speed | **7.93 m/s** | **Solution to (b):** **(i) Top Dead Center (TDC):** TDC is the topmost position reached by the piston during its stroke. At TDC, the volume above the piston is minimum (i.e., clearance volume $V_c$). It is the point where the piston changes direction from upward to downward motion. Also called **Outer Dead Center (ODC)**. **(ii) Bottom Dead Center (BDC):** BDC is the bottommost position reached by the piston during its stroke. At BDC, the volume above the piston is maximum (i.e., $V_1 = V_c + V_s$). The piston changes direction from downward to upward motion. Also called **Inner Dead Center (IDC)**. **(iii) Stroke Length ($L$):** The stroke is the linear distance traveled by the piston between TDC and BDC. Numerically, it equals **twice the crank radius** ($L = 2r$). **Sketch Representation (Mermaid Block Diagram):** ```mermaid graph TD A[Cylinder Head] --> B[Spark Plug] A --> C[Inlet Valve] A --> D[Exhaust Valve] A -- Combustion Chamber --> E[Clearance Volume Vc] E -- Piston travels L from TDC to BDC --> F[Piston] F -- Connected by Connecting Rod --> G[Crankshaft] G -- Crank Radius r = L/2 --> H[Pivot Axis] ``` > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** > - Students often confuse **Stroke** with **Crank Radius** — remember $L = 2r$, not $L = r$. > - When computing **mean piston speed** for 4-stroke, the formula is $\frac{2LN}{60}$. Using $LN/60$ is a common error. > - The **clearance volume** includes the head gasket thickness, spark plug hole, and piston dish volume — do not assume it is just the space above the piston crown. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam - June 2023, Modified Pattern]** **(a)** What is mean effective pressure? Differentiate between IMEP and BMEP. A single-cylinder, 4-stroke engine has a bore of **100 mm** and stroke of **120 mm**. The IMEP is **7 bar** and the engine speed is **1500 rpm**. Calculate the indicated power developed. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Explain with a neat diagram the working of a **4-stroke SI engine** with special reference to the events in each stroke. **(7 Marks)** ### Model Solution for Question B: **Solution to (a):** **Definition of MEP:** Mean Effective Pressure is a hypothetical, constant pressure assumed to act on the piston throughout the power stroke that would produce the same net work as the actual varying pressure inside the cylinder. **IMEP vs BMEP:** | Aspect | IMEP | BMEP | |---|---|---| | Definition | Based on Indicated Power (work done by gas on piston) | Based on Brake Power (output at crank) | | Friction included? | No | Yes (friction work is subtracted) | | Value relative to IP/BP | $\text{IMEP} = \frac{IP \times 60}{L A n N_{rps}}$ | $\text{BMEP} = \frac{BP \times 60}{L A n N_{rps}}$ | | Always | $\text{IMEP} > \text{BMEP}$ | $\text{BMEP} < \text{IMEP}$ | **Numerical Calculation:** **Given:** - $D = 100$ mm $= 0.1$ m - $L = 120$ mm $= 0.12$ m - $p_{im} = 7$ bar $= 7 \times 10^5$ Pa - $N = 1500$ rpm, 4-stroke, single cylinder **Step 1: Calculate swept area.** $$ A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (0.1)^2}{4} = \frac{0.031416}{4} = 0.007854 \text{ m}^2 $$ **Step 2: Calculate swept volume.** $$ V_s = A \times L = 0.007854 \times 0.12 = 9.4248 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3 $$ **Step 3: Indicated Power (4-stroke).** $$ IP = \frac{p_{im} \cdot V_s \cdot N}{120} = \frac{7 \times 10^5 \times 9.4248 \times 10^{-4} \times 1500}{120} $$ $$ IP = \frac{7 \times 10^5 \times 1.41372}{120} = \frac{989604}{120} = 8246.7 \text{ W} $$ $$ \boxed{IP \approx 8.25 \text{ kW}} $$ **Solution to (b):** **4-Stroke SI Engine Operation:** **Stroke 1 — Suction/Intake Stroke:** - Piston moves from **TDC to BDC**. - **Inlet valve: OPEN**, exhaust valve: CLOSED. - A mixture of air and petrol (atomized via carburetor or port injection) is drawn in due to the partial vacuum created by the descending piston. **Stroke 2 — Compression Stroke:** - Piston moves from **BDC to TDC**. - Both valves CLOSED. - The air-fuel mixture is compressed to a small clearance volume. Compression ratio typically 8–12. **Stroke 3 — Power/Expansion Stroke:** - Near TDC, spark plug produces a spark. - Combustion rapidly raises pressure (and temperature). - High pressure gases push the piston from TDC to BDC, delivering useful work. **Stroke 4 — Exhaust Stroke:** - Piston moves from **BDC to TDC**. - Exhaust valve: OPEN, inlet valve: CLOSED. - Burnt gases are pushed out through the exhaust valve. > **Crankshaft revolutions:** 2 (720°) for one complete cycle. **Block Diagram (Mermaid):** ```mermaid graph LR S1[Intake Stroke - Inlet Open] --> S2[Compression Stroke - Both Closed] S2 --> S3[Power Stroke - Combustion] S3 --> S4[Exhaust Stroke - Exhaust Open] S4 -- 720 crank deg total --> S1 ``` > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall Warning (Part b):** > - Students often **forget to mention valve positions** in each stroke — this costs 2 marks. > - Some students mix up the direction of piston motion in compression vs. exhaust strokes (both go BDC→TDC). > - The **power stroke** is sometimes wrongly called the "combustion stroke" — combustion is the cause, but the *work-producing motion* is the power stroke. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Engine Nomenclature** **Geometric (Fixed by Design):** - ✅ **Bore ($D$):** Cylinder diameter. - ✅ **Stroke ($L$):** Piston travel from TDC to BDC; $L = 2r$ where $r$ = crank radius. - ✅ **Connecting Rod Length ($l_c$):** Typical $l_c/r$ ratio is 3–4.5. - ✅ **TDC / BDC:** Topmost / Bottommost piston positions. **Volumetric (Calculated):** - ✅ **Clearance Volume ($V_c$):** Volume above piston at TDC. - ✅ **Swept Volume ($V_s$):** $\frac{\pi D^2 L}{4}$. - ✅ **Total Volume ($V_1$):** $V_c + V_s$. - ✅ **Compression Ratio ($r$):** $V_1 / V_c = 1 + V_s / V_c$. **Kinematic (Motion-Based):** - ✅ **Mean Piston Speed ($\bar{V_p}$):** $\frac{2 L N}{60}$ — typical 5–15 m/s. - ✅ **Engine Speed ($N$):** Crankshaft RPM. **Performance (Derived):** - ✅ **IMEP:** Based on indicated work inside cylinder. - ✅ **BMEP:** Based on brake (output) work; always < IMEP. - ✅ **Power:** $P = \frac{2\pi N T}{60}$; for 4-stroke $IP = \frac{p_{im} L A N n}{120}$. - ✅ **Torque:** $T = \frac{60 P}{2\pi N}$. **Classification (Foundational Knowledge):** - ✅ **SI vs CI:** Spark vs Compression Ignition; SI uses lower $r$ (8–12); CI uses higher $r$ (14–22). - ✅ **4-Stroke vs 2-Stroke:** 720° vs 360° per cycle. - ✅ **Arrangements:** Inline, V-type, Flat/Boxer, Radial. **Common Pitfalls to Avoid in KTU Exam:** - ⚠️ Stroke = $2 \times$ crank radius, not $1 \times$. - ⚠️ Mean piston speed = $\frac{2LN}{60}$, not $\frac{LN}{60}$. - ⚠️ For 4-stroke power: divide $N$ by **120**, not 60. - ⚠️ Compression ratio is **dimensionless** (no unit). - ⚠️ BMEP is always **less** than IMEP — never write the opposite. > **End of Module 1 Notes — Basic Engine Nomenclature** > *Aligned to KTU 2024 Scheme PCAUT205*

topics.title: Classification and Applications of IC Engines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT205 - AUTOMOBILE POWER PLANT (AUTOMOBILE ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Classification and Applications of IC Engines

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Internal Combustion (IC) Engine** is a prime mover that converts the chemical energy of a liquid or gaseous fuel into useful mechanical work (rotational) by **burning the fuel inside the engine cylinder**, where the combustion products act directly on the moving parts of the engine (typically a piston) to deliver power to a crankshaft.

$$ \text{Chemical Energy (Fuel)} \xrightarrow{\text{Combustion in Cylinder}} \text{Heat Energy} \xrightarrow{\text{Expansion on Piston}} \text{Mechanical Work} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1.1):**
> Classification based on **(a) Cycle of operation**, **(b) Fuel used**, **(c) Type of ignition**, **(d) Number of strokes**, **(e) Number and arrangement of cylinders**, **(f) Cooling system**, **(g) Valve arrangement**, and **(h) Application/Speed** are treated as **High-Weightage** topics for Part A (3-mark) and Part B (14-mark) questions.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of an IC engine like a **powerful, controlled, repetitive cannon**:

- Imagine a small cannon that fires a "bullet" (fuel-air mixture) **inside a sealed metal barrel** (cylinder).
- Each explosion pushes a **plug** (piston) outward.
- The plug is connected to a **wheel** (crankshaft) via a connecting rod, so linear push becomes **rotational motion** (spinning wheel).
- After each shot, the barrel is **reloaded automatically** (intake + exhaust strokes) and fires again — every second, this can happen **hundreds to thousands of times** in a real engine.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Internal" Combustion?**
> Because the fuel burns **inside** the working cylinder itself, unlike steam engines or gas turbines where fuel (or heat) is supplied **externally** to the working fluid.

## 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics (Bolded for Quick Recall)

- **Brake Thermal Efficiency (η_bt):** Typically **25% – 35%** for SI engines, **30% – 40%** for CI engines.
- **Compression Ratio (r):** SI engines **8 : 1 to 12 : 1** | CI engines **14 : 1 to 22 : 1**.
- **Engine Speed (N):** Low speed **< 500 rpm**, Medium **500 – 1500 rpm**, High speed **> 1500 rpm**.
- **Mean Effective Pressure (MEP):** Typically **8 – 10 bar** for naturally aspirated SI engines.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Comprehensive Classification of IC Engines

IC engines can be classified on **several independent parameters**. Understanding each axis of classification is essential because a single engine simultaneously belongs to **multiple categories** (e.g., a Maruti Suzuki Alto engine is a *4-stroke, SI, petrol, water-cooled, inline-3 cylinder, overhead camshaft, high-speed, naturally aspirated* engine).

### 2.1.1 Classification Based on **Cycle of Operation**

| # | Cycle | Working Principle | Typical Use |
|---|-------|-------------------|-------------|
| 1 | **Otto Cycle (Constant Volume)** | Heat added at constant volume (explosion-like) | Spark Ignition (SI) engines — Cars, bikes |
| 2 | **Diesel Cycle (Constant Pressure)** | Heat added at constant pressure (gradual burning) | Compression Ignition (CI) engines — Trucks, buses |
| 3 | **Dual Cycle (Limited Pressure / Sabathe)** | Combination of constant volume + constant pressure heat addition | Modern high-speed CI engines (most common in trucks today) |
| 4 | **Atkinson Cycle** | Expanded beyond compression stroke — high efficiency, low power density | Toyota Prius, Honda hybrids |
| 5 | **Miller Cycle** | Effective compression ratio reduced via early/late intake valve closure | Modern turbocharged engines (Mazda Skyactiv, Toyota) |

### 2.1.2 Classification Based on **Fuel Used**

| Fuel Type | Engine Type | Common Applications |
|-----------|-------------|---------------------|
| **Petrol / Gasoline** | SI Engine | Cars, two-wheelers, scooters, lawn mowers |
| **Diesel** | CI Engine | Trucks, buses, tractors, locomotives, gensets |
| **CNG (Compressed Natural Gas)** | SI Engine (modified) | City buses, taxis (Maruti WagonR, Hyundai Santro) |
| **LPG (Liquefied Petroleum Gas)** | SI Engine (modified) | Auto-rickshaws, small cars |
| **Bio-diesel** | CI Engine (modified) | Rural tractors, eco-vehicles |
| **Hydrogen** | Modified SI or Fuel Cell | Experimental automotive, NASA rockets |
| **Alcohol (Ethanol, Methanol)** | SI Engine | Brazil flex-fuel vehicles, racing |

### 2.1.3 Classification Based on **Type of Ignition**

> [!NOTE]
> **Two fundamental ignition philosophies — exam favourite!**

1. **Spark Ignition (SI) Engine:**
   - Fuel-air mixture is drawn in.
   - Compressed to moderate ratio.
   - Ignited by an external **electric spark plug** at the right moment.

2. **Compression Ignition (CI) Engine:**
   - Only air is compressed to a very high ratio → high temperature (above auto-ignition of fuel).
   - Fuel is injected directly into the hot compressed air.
   - **Auto-ignition** occurs (no spark plug needed).

3. **Hot-Bulb / Hot-Spot Ignition:** Pre-heated chamber aids ignition — used in old **low-speed marine engines**.

### 2.1.4 Classification Based on **Number of Strokes**

- **2-Stroke Engine:** Power stroke every revolution. Compact, high power-to-weight, but less efficient & polluting. Used in **Royal Enfield old models, Bajaj scooters, marine outboards, lawn mowers**.
- **4-Stroke Engine:** Power stroke every **2 revolutions**. More efficient, cleaner, longer life. Used in **almost all modern cars and bikes**.

### 2.1.5 Classification Based on **Number & Arrangement of Cylinders**

| Arrangement | Description | Application |
|-------------|-------------|-------------|
| **Single Cylinder** | One cylinder | Motorcycles, small gensets |
| **Inline (Straight)** | All cylinders in a row | Most cars (I3, I4, I5, I6) |
| **V-Engine** | Two banks at 60°/90° angle | High-performance cars (Ferrari, Audi, BMW) |
| **Flat / Boxer** | Cylinders horizontally opposed | Porsche, Subaru, BMW motorcycles |
| **W-Engine** | Four banks in a W | Bugatti Veyron (W16) |
| **Radial** | Cylinders arranged in a circle around crankshaft | Aircraft engines (old) |

### 2.1.6 Classification Based on **Cooling System**

- **Air-Cooled:** Finned cylinders, cooling by forced air (e.g., Bajaj Pulsar 150 older, Royal Enfield Classic 350 in some markets, most small two-wheelers).
- **Water-Cooled (Liquid-Cooled):** Jacket around cylinders with circulating coolant + radiator. Used in **almost all modern cars**.

### 2.1.7 Classification Based on **Valve Arrangement**

- **Side Valve (L-head, flathead):** Valves in the block. Old design.
- **Overhead Valve (OHV, I-head):** Valves in head, pushrod-operated. (Used in older Chevrolet small-block V8s, Royal Enfield).
- **Overhead Cam (OHC, DOHC):** Camshaft(s) in cylinder head. Used in **all modern cars** (Honda i-VTEC, Toyota VVT-i, BMW Vanos).

### 2.1.8 Classification Based on **Speed (RPM)**

| Category | Speed Range | Examples |
|----------|-------------|----------|
| **Slow Speed** | < 500 rpm | Large marine propulsion engines |
| **Medium Speed** | 500 – 1500 rpm | Industrial gensets, ship engines |
| **High Speed** | > 1500 rpm | All modern cars, bikes, trucks |

### 2.1.9 Classification Based on **Aspiration / Air Intake**

- **Naturally Aspirated (NA):** Atmospheric pressure drives intake.
- **Forced Induction:**
  - **Supercharged** — mechanically driven by belt/chain.
  - **Turbocharged** — driven by exhaust gas turbine.
  - **Twin-Charged** — combination of both.

## 2.2 Applications of IC Engines

| Application Domain | Typical Engine | Power Range |
|--------------------|----------------|-------------|
| **Two-Wheelers / Scooters** | SI, 2-stroke or 4-stroke, single cylinder, air-cooled | 5 – 50 hp |
| **Passenger Cars** | SI, 4-stroke, inline 3/4/6, water-cooled | 70 – 700 hp |
| **Commercial Vehicles (Buses/Trucks)** | CI, 4-stroke, inline 6 or V8, turbocharged | 150 – 600 hp |
| **Tractors & Agricultural** | CI, single or twin cylinder | 15 – 100 hp |
| **Locomotives** | CI, large multi-cylinder, turbocharged | 1000 – 4500 hp |
| **Marine Propulsion** | CI, slow-speed 2-stroke, crosshead | 5000 – 100,000+ hp |
| **Aircraft (Piston)** | SI, radial/inline, air-cooled (historical) | 100 – 3500 hp |
| **Gensets / Stationary** | CI or SI, 4-stroke, water-cooled | 5 – 3000 kVA |
| **Construction Equipment** | CI, turbocharged | 50 – 3000 hp |
| **Lawn Mowers / Small Tools** | SI, 2-stroke, single cylinder, air-cooled | 2 – 10 hp |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" of Classification (KTU Examiner's perspective):**
> Classification is **not academic decoration** — it directly decides:
> (a) Fuel pump / carburetor / injector selection,
> (b) Combustion chamber design,
> (c) Valve-train geometry,
> (d) Cooling system sizing,
> (e) Lubrication strategy, and
> (f) Emission control hardware.
> 
> **Always justify the classification with an application link in your answers.**

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Key Parameters for Module 1)

| Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|-----------|---------|-------|-------|
| Compression Ratio (r) | $\dfrac{V_s + V_c}{V_c}$ | dimensionless | $V_s$ = swept volume, $V_c$ = clearance volume |
| Swept Volume (V_s) | $\dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot D^{2} \cdot L$ | m³ or cc | D = bore, L = stroke length |
| Engine Displacement | $N_{cyl} \cdot V_s$ | m³ or cc | For multi-cylinder engines |
| Power (Brake) | $P_b = \dfrac{2 \pi N T}{60}$ | Watts (W) | N in rpm, T in N·m |
| Indicated Power | $P_i = \dfrac{p_{mi} \cdot L \cdot A \cdot N \cdot n}{60}$ | Watts | $p_{mi}$ = mean indicated pressure (Pa) |
| Brake Specific Fuel Consumption (BSFC) | $\dfrac{m_f}{P_b}$ | kg/kWh | Lower is better |
| Brake Thermal Efficiency | $\eta_{bt} = \dfrac{P_b}{m_f \cdot CV}$ | dimensionless | CV = calorific value |
| Air-Fuel Ratio (Stoichiometric, Petrol) | $A/F \approx 14.7 : 1$ | — | By mass |
| Air-Fuel Ratio (Stoichiometric, Diesel) | $A/F \approx 14.5 : 1$ | — | By mass |

> [!NOTE]
> For the **classification** topic, numerical derivation is limited, but the above formulas are routinely asked in **Part A (3-mark)** as "define and state the formula" type questions. Keep this table in your **cheat sheet**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix: Why Each Classification Parameter Matters

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following **analytical matrix** is what differentiates a 3-mark answer from a 14-mark answer in KTU ESE. Examiners award marks for **linking classification → engineering consequence**.

| Classification Parameter | Engineering Consequence | Typical Question (KTU Style) |
|--------------------------|------------------------|-------------------------------|
| High vs Low Compression Ratio | Determines SI vs CI; affects knock, efficiency, power | *"Why CI engines have higher compression ratios than SI engines?"* |
| 2-Stroke vs 4-Stroke | Power density, emission profile, lubrication needs | *"Compare 2-stroke and 4-stroke engines for automobile application."* |
| Air-Cooled vs Water-Cooled | Operating range, weight, complexity, cost | *"Justify water-cooling for a 2000 cc passenger car engine."* |
| OHV vs OHC vs DOHC | Volumetric efficiency, RPM capability, valvetrain mass | *"Explain why modern engines prefer DOHC."* |
| Naturally Aspirated vs Turbocharged | Power output, altitude compensation, fuel economy, lag | *"Discuss the role of turbocharging in modern diesel engines."* |

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Logical Derivation: Compression Ratio and Its Role in Classification

The **compression ratio (r)** is the single most critical parameter that *decides whether an engine will be SI or CI*. Let us derive and analyse it.

### Step 1: Definition

The compression ratio is the ratio of the **total cylinder volume (when piston is at BDC)** to the **clearance volume (when piston is at TDC)**.

$$ r = \dfrac{V_1}{V_2} = \dfrac{V_c + V_s}{V_c} $$

where:
- $V_1$ = volume at Bottom Dead Centre (BDC) = $V_c + V_s$
- $V_2$ = volume at Top Dead Centre (TDC) = $V_c$
- $V_s$ = swept volume
- $V_c$ = clearance volume

### Step 2: The Thermodynamic Link

For an **ideal Otto cycle (SI engine)**, the thermal efficiency is:

$$ \eta_{otto} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}} $$

For an **ideal Diesel cycle (CI engine)**, the thermal efficiency is:

$$ \eta_{diesel} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}} \left[ \dfrac{\rho^{\gamma} - 1}{\gamma \left(\rho - 1\right)} \right] $$

where $\gamma$ = ratio of specific heats, $\rho$ = cut-off ratio.

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** $\eta$ increases with $r$ in **both** cycles. Higher $r$ → higher efficiency. This is **why designers push $r$ as high as physically possible** — but with limits.

### Step 3: The Practical Upper Limit for SI Engines

In SI engines, beyond $r \approx 12:1$, the air-fuel mixture is compressed to a temperature where **auto-ignition (knocking)** occurs **before** the spark fires. This uncontrolled detonation damages the engine.

$$ T_2 = T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma - 1} $$

For SI: $T_2$ must remain **below the auto-ignition temperature** of petrol (~ 250 °C under compression conditions). This caps $r$ at **8 : 1 to 12 : 1**.

### Step 4: The Practical Approach for CI Engines

In CI engines, **only air is compressed**. The fuel is injected at the end of compression. So even with $T_2$ exceeding 600 °C, there is no fuel to auto-ignite prematurely. The fuel is injected **when desired** → no knocking. Hence $r$ can be **14 : 1 to 22 : 1**.

### Step 5: Engine Selection Logic Flow

$$
\boxed{\text{Fuel Type} \xrightarrow{\text{Auto-ignition behaviour}} \text{Compression Ratio Range} \xrightarrow{\text{Efficiency}} \text{Engine Type}}
$$

This **chain of reasoning** is the *exact* answer KTU examiners expect when you face the question:
> *"Classify IC engines on the basis of cycle of operation and justify the compression ratio difference between SI and CI engines."* (14 marks)

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example: Compression Ratio Calculation

**Problem:** A 4-cylinder, 4-stroke petrol engine has a bore of 80 mm and a stroke of 90 mm. The clearance volume per cylinder is 50 cc. Calculate:
1. Compression ratio
2. Total engine displacement in litres

### Solution:

**Given:**
- Number of cylinders, $n = 4$
- Bore, $D = 80 \text{ mm} = 0.08 \text{ m}$
- Stroke, $L = 90 \text{ mm} = 0.09 \text{ m}$
- Clearance volume per cylinder, $V_c = 50 \text{ cc} = 50 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^{3}$

**Step 1: Swept volume per cylinder**

$$ V_s = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot D^{2} \cdot L = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot (0.08)^{2} \cdot 0.09 $$

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_s &= \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot 0.0064 \cdot 0.09 \\
    &= 0.7854 \cdot 0.000576 \\
    &= 0.0004524 \text{ m}^{3} \\
    &= 452.4 \text{ cc}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compression ratio**

$$ r = \dfrac{V_c + V_s}{V_c} = \dfrac{50 + 452.4}{50} = \dfrac{502.4}{50} $$

$$ r = 10.05 : 1 $$

> [!NOTE]
> This value of $r = 10:1$ falls **within the SI petrol engine range (8–12)**, confirming the classification as a Spark Ignition engine.

**Step 3: Total engine displacement**

$$ V_{disp} = n \cdot V_s = 4 \cdot 452.4 = 1809.6 \text{ cc} \approx 1.81 \text{ L} $$

**Conclusion:** The engine is a **1.8 L, inline-4, 4-stroke SI petrol engine** — a typical mid-size passenger car engine (e.g., Toyota Corolla Altis 1.8).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Hierarchical Classification of IC Engines

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ICENG["IC ENGINE"]:::root
    ICENG --> A["By Cycle of Operation"]
    ICENG --> B["By Fuel Used"]
    ICENG --> C["By Type of Ignition"]
    ICENG --> D["By Number of Strokes"]
    ICENG --> E["By Number and Arrangement of Cylinders"]
    ICENG --> F["By Cooling System"]
    ICENG --> G["By Valve Arrangement"]
    ICENG --> H["By Speed RPM"]
    ICENG --> I["By Aspiration Method"]
    ICENG --> J["By Application"]

    A --> A1["Otto Cycle SI"]
    A --> A2["Diesel Cycle CI"]
    A --> A3["Dual Cycle Modern CI"]
    A --> A4["Atkinson Cycle Hybrid"]
    A --> A5["Miller Cycle Turbo"]

    B --> B1["Petrol SI"]
    B --> B2["Diesel CI"]
    B --> B3["CNG LPG SI Modified"]
    B --> B4["Biodiesel CI"]
    B --> B5["Hydrogen SI or Fuel Cell"]
    B --> B6["Alcohol Ethanol Methanol"]

    C --> C1["Spark Ignition Spark Plug"]
    C --> C2["Compression Ignition Auto Ignition"]
    C --> C3["Hot Bulb Ignition"]

    D --> D1["2 Stroke Power per rev"]
    D --> D2["4 Stroke Power per 2 rev"]

    E --> E1["Single Cylinder"]
    E --> E2["Inline I3 I4 I5 I6"]
    E --> E3["V Engine V6 V8 V12"]
    E --> E4["Flat Boxer"]
    E --> E5["W Engine W16"]
    E --> E6["Radial Aircraft"]

    F --> F1["Air Cooled Finned"]
    F --> F2["Water Cooled Liquid Jacket"]

    G --> G1["Side Valve L Head"]
    G --> G2["Overhead Valve OHV"]
    G --> G3["Overhead Cam OHC SOHC"]
    G --> G4["Double Overhead Cam DOHC"]

    H --> H1["Slow Speed below 500 rpm"]
    H --> H2["Medium Speed 500 to 1500 rpm"]
    H --> H3["High Speed above 1500 rpm"]

    I --> I1["Naturally Aspirated"]
    I --> I2["Supercharged Mechanical"]
    I --> I3["Turbocharged Exhaust Driven"]
    I --> I4["Twin Charged Super plus Turbo"]

    J --> J1["Automotive Cars Bikes"]
    J --> J2["Stationary Gensets"]
    J --> J3["Marine Propulsion"]
    J --> J4["Locomotive"]
    J --> J5["Aircraft Piston"]
    J --> J6["Agricultural Tractors"]
    J --> J7["Construction Equipment"]

    classDef root fill:#1f3a5f,color:#ffffff,stroke:#0b1f33,stroke-width:3px
    classDef cat1 fill:#3b6ea5,color:#ffffff,stroke:#0b1f33
    classDef cat2 fill:#5a8cc4,color:#ffffff,stroke:#0b1f33
    classDef cat3 fill:#7aa9d8,color:#000000,stroke:#0b1f33

    class A1,A2,A3,A4,A5 cat1
    class B1,B2,B3,B4,B5,B6 cat2
    class C1,C2,C3 cat3
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Functional Architecture of a Typical SI Engine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    AIR["AIR INTAKE Filter"]:::intk
    FUELF["FUEL TANK Float Carburetor or Injector"]:::fuel
    MIX["AIR FUEL MIXTURE In Cylinder"]:::mix
    COMP["COMPRESSION Piston Rises"]:::comp
    SPARK["SPARK IGNITION Spark Plug Fires"]:::ign
    EXP["EXPANSION Power Stroke"]:::pow
    EXH["EXHAUST Piston Pushes Out Gases"]:::exh
    COOL["COOLING SYSTEM Radiator Water Jacket"]:::cool
    LUB["LUBRICATION Oil Pump Sump"]:::lub
    CRANK["CRANKSHAFT Output Mechanical Power"]:::out

    AIR --> MIX
    FUELF --> MIX
    MIX --> COMP
    COMP --> SPARK
    SPARK --> EXP
    EXP --> EXH
    EXH --> AIR
    COOL --> COMP
    COOL --> EXP
    LUB --> COMP
    LUB --> EXP
    EXP --> CRANK

    classDef intk fill:#ffd166,color:#000000,stroke:#0b1f33
    classDef fuel fill:#06a77d,color:#ffffff,stroke:#0b1f33
    classDef mix fill:#118ab2,color:#ffffff,stroke:#0b1f33
    classDef comp fill:#073b4c,color:#ffffff,stroke:#0b1f33
    classDef ign fill:#ef476f,color:#ffffff,stroke:#0b1f33
    classDef pow fill:#e63946,color:#ffffff,stroke:#0b1f33
    classDef exh fill:#6a4c93,color:#ffffff,stroke:#0b1f33
    classDef cool fill:#48cae4,color:#000000,stroke:#0b1f33
    classDef lub fill:#f4a261,color:#000000,stroke:#0b1f33
    classDef out fill:#264653,color:#ffffff,stroke:#0b1f33
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: SI vs CI Engine Comparison Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SI["SPARK IGNITION ENGINE"]:::si
    CI["COMPRESSION IGNITION ENGINE"]:::ci

    SI --> SI1["Fuel Mixed with Air Before Compression"]
    SI --> SI2["Compression Ratio 8 to 12 to 1"]
    SI --> SI3["Spark Plug Triggers Combustion"]
    SI --> SI4["Light Weight Low Initial Cost"]
    SI --> SI5["Application Cars and Bikes"]

    CI --> CI1["Air Compressed First Fuel Injected Later"]
    CI --> CI2["Compression Ratio 14 to 22 to 1"]
    CI --> CI3["Auto Ignition Due to High Temperature"]
    CI --> CI4["Heavy Robust High Torque"]
    CI --> CI5["Application Trucks Buses Gensets"]

    SI -.COMPARISON.-> CI

    classDef si fill:#f72585,color:#ffffff,stroke:#0b1f33
    classDef ci fill:#4361ee,color:#ffffff,stroke:#0b1f33
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define an Internal Combustion engine. How is it classified on the basis of the cycle of operation?**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> An IC engine is a prime mover that converts the chemical energy of a fuel into mechanical work by **burning the fuel inside the engine cylinder**, where the high-pressure combustion gases act directly on the piston to produce useful work.
> **Classification based on cycle of operation:**
> 1. **Otto cycle (constant volume heat addition)** — used in SI engines.
> 2. **Diesel cycle (constant pressure heat addition)** — used in CI engines.
> 3. **Dual / Sabathe cycle (limited pressure)** — used in modern high-speed CI engines.
> 4. **Atkinson & Miller cycles** — used in hybrid and turbocharged engines.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Differentiate between Spark Ignition (SI) and Compression Ignition (CI) engines in any three aspects.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer (3 marks — table format preferred):**
> 
> | Aspect | SI Engine | CI Engine |
> |--------|-----------|-----------|
> | **Fuel** | Petrol / Gasoline (volatile) | Diesel (less volatile) |
> | **Compression Ratio** | Low (8 – 12) | High (14 – 22) |
> | **Ignition** | Spark plug (external) | Auto-ignition (compression heat) |
> | **Air-Fuel Mixing** | Before compression (carburetor / port injection) | Inside cylinder (direct injection) |
> | **Starting** | Easy (no high compression) | Difficult (needs starter motor) |
> | **Weight** | Light | Heavy |
> | **Application** | Cars, two-wheelers | Trucks, buses, gensets |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU ESE Internal Choice Pattern

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Q. (a)** Classify IC engines on the basis of (i) fuel used, (ii) type of ignition, and (iii) number of strokes. Give one example application for each class. **(7 marks)**

**Q. (b)** Compare 2-stroke and 4-stroke engines in detail. Which one is preferred for modern passenger cars and why? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Classification of IC Engines:**

**(i) Classification by fuel used:**

1. **Petrol / Gasoline engine** — SI; used in cars, two-wheelers. Example: Maruti Suzuki Swift petrol.
2. **Diesel engine** — CI; used in trucks, buses, tractors. Example: Tata LPT 1109 truck.
3. **Gas engine (CNG / LPG)** — modified SI; used in city taxis & auto-rickshaws. Example: Maruti WagonR CNG.
4. **Bio-diesel engine** — modified CI; rural eco-applications.
5. **Hydrogen engine** — modified SI; experimental, NASA rockets.
6. **Alcohol (ethanol) engine** — SI; Brazilian flex-fuel cars.

**(ii) Classification by type of ignition:**

1. **Spark Ignition (SI):** Combustion initiated by an electric spark plug. Compression ratio limited by knocking. Examples: Honda City, Hero Splendor.
2. **Compression Ignition (CI):** Combustion initiated by auto-ignition due to high temperature from high compression. No spark plug. Examples: Ashok Leyland trucks, Tata buses.
3. **Hot-Bulb Ignition:** Pre-heated chamber aids combustion — used in old **low-speed marine engines**.

**(iii) Classification by number of strokes:**

1. **2-Stroke:** One power stroke per revolution. Compact, light, high power-to-weight. Used in old two-wheelers, marine outboards. Example: Bajaj Chetak (older).
2. **4-Stroke:** One power stroke per two revolutions. Better fuel economy, lower emissions. Used in **all modern automobiles**. Example: Maruti Alto K10.

**(b) Comparison of 2-Stroke vs 4-Stroke Engines:**

| Feature | 2-Stroke Engine | 4-Stroke Engine |
|---------|-----------------|-----------------|
| **Power strokes per revolution** | 1 | 1 / 2 |
| **Power-to-weight ratio** | High | Lower |
| **Thermal efficiency** | Lower (~ 20%) | Higher (~ 30 – 35%) |
| **Fuel consumption** | Higher (fuel loss in exhaust) | Lower |
| **Emissions** | Higher (unburnt HC) | Lower |
| **Lubrication** | Oil mixed in fuel (total loss) | Sump-based (recirculating) |
| **Cooling** | Usually air-cooled | Usually water-cooled |
| **Maintenance** | Frequent | Less frequent |
| **Cost** | Cheaper | Costlier but justified |
| **Application** | Mopeds, marine, gensets | Cars, trucks, bikes |

> **Why 4-stroke is preferred for modern passenger cars:**
> 1. **Lower emissions** — meets stringent BS-VI / Euro 7 norms.
> 2. **Better fuel economy** — critical in commercial passenger car market.
> 3. **Lower engine noise & vibration** — comfort requirement.
> 4. **Longer engine life** — recirculating lubrication reduces wear.
> 5. **Higher power output per unit displacement** when combined with DOHC + turbo.
> 6. **Emission control compatibility** — easier to integrate catalytic converter, EGR, GPF.

Hence, **all modern passenger cars use 4-stroke engines** (e.g., Hyundai Creta 1.5 L 4-cylinder, Tata Nexon 1.2 L 3-cylinder).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 — Alternative]**

**Q. (a)** Explain in detail the classification of IC engines on the basis of (i) number and arrangement of cylinders, and (ii) cooling system. Discuss the engineering reason behind each choice. **(7 marks)**

**Q. (b)** A 4-cylinder, 4-stroke petrol engine has a bore of 75 mm and stroke of 85 mm. The clearance volume per cylinder is 45 cc. Calculate (i) compression ratio, (ii) engine displacement in cc, and (iii) state whether this engine is suitable as a CI engine. Justify. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) (i) Classification by number & arrangement of cylinders:**

1. **Single Cylinder:** One piston. Used in small motorcycles, gensets. Simple, cheap, but vibration & cooling issues at high power.
2. **Inline / Straight:** Cylinders in a single row along the crankshaft. Common configurations — I3 (Tata Nexon), I4 (Honda City), I5 (older Volvo), I6 (BMW straight-six). Balanced, easy to maintain.
3. **V-Engine:** Two banks of cylinders at an angle (60° or 90°). V6, V8, V10, V12. Used in luxury & sports cars (Audi V8, Ferrari V12). Compact for high displacement, lower bonnet height.
4. **Flat / Boxer Engine:** Horizontally opposed cylinders. Used in Porsche 911, Subaru. Excellent balance, low centre of gravity.
5. **W-Engine:** Three or four banks in a 'W' configuration. Used in Bugatti W16.
6. **Radial Engine:** Cylinders arranged in a star around the crankshaft. Used in **WWII aircraft engines**. Excellent air-cooling, smooth.

**Engineering reason:** More cylinders → smoother operation (less vibration), higher total displacement, better balance, and higher power output. However, it increases **cost, weight, friction, and complexity**.

**(ii) Classification by cooling system:**

1. **Air-Cooled Engine:** Uses fins on the cylinder + forced air from a fan. Light, simple, no radiator. Used in small two-wheelers, lawn mowers. Limitation: poor heat removal at high power.
2. **Water-Cooled (Liquid-Cooled) Engine:** Uses water jacket + radiator + pump + thermostat. Efficient heat removal, uniform cooling, suitable for high power outputs. Used in **all modern cars**.

**Engineering reason:** Modern cars generate high specific power (> 100 hp/L), so air cooling is insufficient. Water has a **specific heat 4× that of air** and can absorb more heat per unit mass, making liquid cooling mandatory for sustained high-load operation.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- $n = 4$ (cylinders)
- $D = 75 \text{ mm} = 0.075 \text{ m}$
- $L = 85 \text{ mm} = 0.085 \text{ m}$
- $V_c = 45 \text{ cc} = 45 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^{3}$
- 4-stroke petrol engine

**Step 1: Swept volume per cylinder**

$$ V_s = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot D^{2} \cdot L $$

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_s &= \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot (0.075)^{2} \cdot 0.085 \\
    &= 0.7854 \cdot 0.005625 \cdot 0.085 \\
    &= 0.7854 \cdot 0.000478 \\
    &= 0.0003755 \text{ m}^{3} \\
    &= 375.5 \text{ cc}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compression ratio**

$$ r = \dfrac{V_c + V_s}{V_c} = \dfrac{45 + 375.5}{45} = \dfrac{420.5}{45} $$

$$ r = 9.34 : 1 $$

**Step 3: Engine displacement**

$$ V_{disp} = n \cdot V_s = 4 \cdot 375.5 = 1502 \text{ cc} \approx 1.5 \text{ L} $$

**Step 4: Suitability as CI engine?**

The compression ratio is **9.34 : 1**, which lies in the **SI petrol range (8–12)**. CI engines require **14–22 : 1** for auto-ignition. Therefore, **this engine is NOT suitable as a CI engine** — at $r = 9.34$, the compressed air temperature will not reach the auto-ignition temperature of diesel.

> **Justification:** A 1.5 L inline-4 engine with $r = 9.34$ is a **typical SI petrol engine** found in cars like the **Honda City 1.5 L i-VTEC** or **Hyundai Verna 1.5 L MPi**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 
> 1. **Don't write only a list** — examiners want *application linkage*. Always say *"used in… / e.g., …"*.
> 2. **Units matter** — compression ratio is dimensionless; swept volume must be in **m³ or cc consistently**.
> 3. **Bore and stroke conversion** — students often forget to convert mm to m, leading to wrong V_s by a factor of 10⁶.
> 4. **Don't confuse $V_c$ and $V_s$** — $V_c$ is clearance (small) and $V_s$ is swept (large). A common mistake is **swapping them** in the compression ratio formula.
> 5. **Don't claim CI can use $r = 10$** — students forget that $r < 12$ will *never* auto-ignite diesel.
> 6. **Use the term "calorific value" properly** — not "heat value" or "energy content" in formal answers.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — must-memorise for KTU 2024 ESE:**

- ✅ IC engine = combustion **inside** cylinder.
- ✅ 5 essential engine classifications: **cycle, fuel, ignition, stroke, cylinder arrangement**.
- ✅ **SI = 8–12 : 1** | **CI = 14–22 : 1** (compression ratio).
- ✅ **2-stroke = 1 power stroke / rev** | **4-stroke = 1 power stroke / 2 rev**.
- ✅ **Petrol = SI** | **Diesel = CI** | **CNG = modified SI** | **Biodiesel = modified CI**.
- ✅ **OHV = pushrod** | **OHC = single cam in head** | **DOHC = two cams in head** (most modern cars).
- ✅ **Inline 3/4/5/6** (cars) | **V6/V8/V12** (luxury & sports) | **Flat / Boxer** (Porsche, Subaru) | **Radial** (aircraft).
- ✅ **Air-cooled** = small bikes, gensets | **Water-cooled** = all modern cars.
- ✅ **Slow speed < 500 rpm** (marine) | **Medium 500–1500** (gensets) | **High > 1500** (automotive).
- ✅ **Naturally aspirated** = no boost | **Turbocharged** = exhaust-driven compressor | **Supercharged** = mechanically driven.
- ✅ Swept volume: $V_s = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^{2} L$.
- ✅ Engine displacement = number of cylinders × swept volume.
- ✅ **Otto = constant V** | **Diesel = constant P** | **Dual = combined**.
- ✅ Knocking limits SI compression ratio; CI is knock-free due to fuel injection timing.
- ✅ **Stoichiometric A/F: Petrol ≈ 14.7, Diesel ≈ 14.5** (by mass).
- ✅ Modern cars use **4-stroke, water-cooled, multi-cylinder, OHC/DOHC** engines.
- ✅ Always **link classification → application** for full KTU marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Constructional Details of Engine Components: Cylinders - Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT205 - AUTOMOBILE POWER PLANT (AUTOMOBILE ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Constructional Details of Engine Components: Cylinders - Types

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cylinder:** A cylindrical cavity bored in the engine block (or formed by a replaceable liner) in which the piston reciprocates, converting the pressure energy of the combusted (or compressed) charge into useful mechanical work on the crankshaft via the connecting rod.

The cylinder is universally regarded as the **functional heart** of any reciprocating internal combustion (IC) engine. It performs four critical duties simultaneously:

1. **Contains** the working fluid (air–fuel mixture in SI engines, or compressed air in CI engines).
2. **Withstands** the dynamic gas pressures generated during combustion (peak cylinder pressures of **80 bar to 120 bar** in modern petrol engines and up to **200 bar** in turbocharged diesel engines).
3. **Guides** the piston in a perfectly linear reciprocating motion.
4. **Dissipates** a major portion (≈ **30 % to 35 %**) of the total heat released during combustion to the cooling medium.

The cylinder is **not an isolated component**; it is part of an integrated sub-assembly that includes the **cylinder block**, **cylinder head**, **cylinder liner (sleeve)**, **piston**, **piston rings**, and the **cooling jacket**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight:** Under the NEP 2020 Outcome-Based Education (OBE) framework, students are expected to map every concept in this topic to the Course Outcome **CO1**: *"Illustrate the constructional details and working principles of SI and CI engine sub-systems."*

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Cylinder as a Pressure Cooker on a Slider"

Imagine a **domestic pressure cooker** sitting on a kitchen slab. Inside the cooker:
- The food is the **air–fuel mixture**,
- The sealed lid represents the **cylinder head**,
- The cooker body is the **cylinder**,
- The whistle releases excess pressure (similar to a **safety relief valve**),
- The flame beneath provides **heat input**,
- The sealed pot withstands the **internal pressure** while transferring heat to the surroundings.

Now imagine a **piston** moving up and down inside the cooker like a **sliding lid** attached to a lever (the **connecting rod**). The cyclic heating and the sliding lid together produce **reciprocating work**. This is precisely what happens inside an engine cylinder — the **cooker is the cylinder**, the **sliding lid is the piston**, and the **lever** is the connecting rod transmitting force to the crankshaft.

This analogy helps a first-time learner visualize:
- *Why* the cylinder wall must be **thick and rigid** (to contain high pressure),
- *Why* the outer surface is often **finned or jacketed** (to reject heat),
- *Why* a **smooth bore** is essential (to minimise piston friction and oil consumption).

---

## 1.3 The Cylinder as a Composite Functional Element

A modern engine cylinder is engineered as a multi-functional element, simultaneously performing:

| Function | Physical Mechanism | Typical Material/Design |
|---|---|---|
| Pressure containment | Thick metallic wall | **Cast iron**, **Aluminium alloy** |
| Heat dissipation | External fins or water jacket | **Fins (air-cooled)**, **Jacket (water-cooled)** |
| Piston guidance | Precision-honed bore | **Honed to 0.05 µm Ra surface finish** |
| Wear resistance | Hard bore surface | **Chrome plating**, **Nikasil**, **Cast iron liner** |
| Sealing | Piston rings | **3-ring pack** (top compression, second compression, oil control) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Design Metric:** Cylinder **Bore Diameter (D)** is one of the two primary geometric parameters (along with stroke L) that fully defines engine displacement. Bore is conventionally expressed in **millimetres (mm)** and, in the SI system, the **standard bore grades** of mass-produced engines are 65, 70, 75, 80, 85, 90, 95 and 100 mm.

---

## 1.4 Why Cylinder Design Matters in Automobile Engineering

The cylinder design is the **single largest determinant** of:
- Engine **displacement** and hence **power output**,
- Engine **thermal efficiency** and **specific fuel consumption**,
- Engine **weight-to-power ratio**,
- **Emissions characteristics** (unburnt hydrocarbons from crevice volumes),
- **NVH behaviour** (noise, vibration, harshness) of the vehicle,
- **Manufacturing cost** and **serviceability** (re-sleeving vs. block replacement).

For these reasons, the KTU 2024 Automobile Engineering syllabus places the study of **cylinder types** in Module 1 as the foundation for all subsequent modules (fuel systems, cooling systems, lubrication, combustion, etc.).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Engine Cylinders

Cylinders can be classified on **four independent criteria**, each of which has direct engineering consequences. The KTU board examiner frequently frames questions around these classifications.

### Classification Axis 1 — Based on Method of Cooling

#### 2.1.1 Air-Cooled Cylinders
- The cylinder is manufactured as an integral part of the **engine block** (or as a separate finned barrel) with a large number of **fins** projecting radially outward.
- Cooling is achieved by **forced air flow** over the fins, driven by a **fan** (mechanically driven or electric).
- **Fins per cylinder:** typically **6 to 12 fins**, each **3 mm to 6 mm thick** and **15 mm to 30 mm deep**.
- The **fin spacing** is critical — too close causes stagnant air, too far reduces surface area. Optimum spacing is **6 mm to 10 mm**.
- **Material:** aluminium alloy is preferred due to high thermal conductivity (≈ **150 W/m·K** for Al vs. **50 W/m·K** for cast iron).
- **Typical applications:** small two-wheelers (Hero Splendor, Bajaj Pulsar), lawn-mowers, generator sets, aircraft engines (rotary radial), vintage automobiles (early Volkswagen Beetle).

> [!NOTE]
> **Advantage of Air-Cooling:** No radiator, no water pump, no coolant leakage, lighter in weight, better suited to high-altitude and sub-zero operation. **Disadvantage:** Limited heat rejection capacity — hence restricted to engines of displacement up to ≈ **1.5 L** per cylinder.

#### 2.1.2 Water-Cooled Cylinders
- The cylinder is surrounded by a **water jacket** — a hollow space cast into the block through which coolant (water + ethylene glycol) is circulated by a **water pump**.
- **Heat transfer rate** is **5× to 10×** higher than air-cooling, allowing much higher specific power outputs.
- **Typical coolant flow rate:** **40 to 80 L/min** in a passenger car engine.
- **Coolant temperature:** maintained between **85 °C and 95 °C** by a thermostat.
- **Applications:** virtually every modern four-wheeler (Maruti Suzuki, Hyundai, Tata, Toyota), heavy-duty commercial vehicles, tractors.

### Classification Axis 2 — Based on Construction (Cylinder Liner / Sleeve Configuration)

This is the **most important classification** from the KTU exam perspective.

#### 2.1.3 Integral / Monoblock Cylinders
- The cylinder is **cast as a single piece** with the engine block. There is **no separate liner**.
- The bore is **machined directly** into the cast iron or aluminium block.
- **Used in:** older designs (e.g., Fiat 1100, Ambassador), modern small two-wheeler engines, most **aluminium blocks** (where cylinder bores are often coated with **Nikasil** or **Lokasil**).
- **Advantages:** Compact, lightweight, fewer joints, better stiffness.
- **Disadvantages:** When the bore wears out, the **entire block must be replaced** or re-bored to the next oversize (a costly repair).

#### 2.1.4 Cylinders with Replaceable Liners (Sleeves)
A **cylinder liner** is a hollow cylindrical sleeve fitted into the block to form the actual bore surface. Two sub-types exist:

##### (a) Dry Liner
- The liner is **pressed into** the block bore with an **interference fit** (typically **0.05 mm to 0.10 mm**).
- It is **not in direct contact** with the coolant — the block wall lies between the liner and the cooling water.
- **Wall thickness:** usually **2 mm to 3 mm**.
- **Advantages:** No coolant leakage risk, less prone to cavitation, no special sealing required at the ends.
- **Disadvantages:** Poorer heat transfer (heat must conduct through the block), lower specific output.

##### (b) Wet Liner
- The liner is in **direct contact with the cooling water** on its outer surface.
- The **top end** is located by a **flange** that sits on the block deck, and the **bottom end** is sealed by **rubber O-rings** to prevent coolant leakage.
- **Wall thickness:** typically **3 mm to 6 mm** (thicker than dry liners due to direct water contact and possible corrosion).
- **Advantages:** Excellent heat transfer (up to **30 % better** than dry liners), allows reduced bore-to-bore spacing, easier replacement at overhaul.
- **Disadvantages:** Risk of **cavitation pitting** on the outer surface due to vibration of the liner against the water, requires careful O-ring sealing.
- **Used in:** almost all modern multi-cylinder passenger car and commercial vehicle engines (e.g., Tata Nexon, Hyundai Creta, Maruti Swift).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cavitation in Wet Liners:** When the wet liner vibrates outward at high frequency, the local water pressure drops below the saturated vapour pressure of water (≈ **0.023 bar at 20 °C**), forming tiny vapour bubbles. When these bubbles collapse on the liner surface, they produce **micro-jets** of water at velocities up to **100 m/s**, which erode the outer surface. This is countered by **anti-cavitation coatings**, **cylinder liner coatings** (e.g., DLC — Diamond-Like Carbon), and **coolant additives**.

### Classification Axis 3 — Based on Cylinder Arrangement (for Multi-Cylinder Engines)

| Arrangement | Description | Crankcase Length | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Inline (I)** | All cylinders in a single row | Long | 3-cyl (Tata Tiago), 4-cyl (Hyundai Verna), 6-cyl (older Mercedes) |
| **V-Type** | Two banks of cylinders at 60°–90° | Short | V6, V8, V12 (Audi, BMW, Ferrari) |
| **Flat / Opposed (Boxer)** | Two banks at 180° lying horizontally | Very low centre of gravity | Porsche 911, Subaru, old Volkswagen Beetle |
| **Radial** | Cylinders arranged radially around the crankshaft | Very short | Aircraft engines (Pratt & Whitney Wasp) |
| **W-Type** | Two V-banks sharing a common crankshaft (W16) | Very compact | Bugatti Veyron (W16), W12 (Audi, Bentley) |

### Classification Axis 4 — Based on Bore Surface / Liner Material

- **Plain Cast Iron Liner** — traditional, used in most Indian OEM engines.
- **Alloy Cast Iron Liner** — alloyed with **Ni, Cr, Mo, V, Ti** for improved wear resistance.
- **Chrome-Plated Liner** — electroplated with **0.05 mm to 0.15 mm** of hard chrome; used in high-performance two-wheelers (KTM Duke, Bajaj Dominar).
- **Nikasil Coating** — electroless nickel + silicon carbide (NiSiC); used in BMW motorcycles, some Porsche engines.
- **Lokasil Coating** — aluminium + silicon carbide; used in Mercedes, BMW.
- **Cermet Coatings** — for ultra-low-friction applications (research stage).

---

## 2.2 Why and How of Cylinder Liner Design — A Logical Breakdown

**Why is a separate liner used at all?**
Because the cylinder bore is the **most wear-prone** surface in the engine (subjected to reciprocating friction of piston rings, high-temperature combustion gases, and corrosive combustion products). Replacing the entire block when the bore wears out is economically unviable. A **replaceable liner** allows restoration of original bore geometry at low cost during engine overhaul.

**How is the liner retained in the block?**
- For **dry liners**: interference fit (press-fit) ensures a tight metallic contact with the block for heat conduction.
- For **wet liners**: a flange on the top + O-ring seals at the bottom + a uniform radial press fit (not interference fit) ensures water contact but prevents leakage.

**How is heat transferred?**
Heat flows from the **combustion gas** → **liner inner wall** → **liner outer wall** → (block wall, for dry liner) → **coolant** → **radiator** → **ambient air**.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates **all standard formulas** that a KTU 2024 examiner may expect for any problem on cylinders, displacement, and related topics. All units are in **SI (mm, cm³, m, s)** unless otherwise specified.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Symbols & Units | KTU Exam Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Swept volume of one cylinder | $V_s = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \, D^2 \, L$ | $D$ = bore (mm); $L$ = stroke (mm); $V_s$ in mm³ | Convert to **cm³** by dividing by **1000** |
| 2 | Total swept volume (displacement) | $V_d = V_s \times n = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \, D^2 \, L \, n$ | $n$ = number of cylinders | Engine capacity rating |
| 3 | Compression ratio | $r = \dfrac{V_s + V_c}{V_c}$ | $V_c$ = clearance volume | Compare SI vs CI ratings |
| 4 | Bore-to-Stroke ratio | $\beta = \dfrac{D}{L}$ | dimensionless | Classifies engines as **long-stroke** ($\beta < 1$) or **short-stroke** ($\beta > 1$) |
| 5 | Cylinder wall thickness (empirical) | $t = 0.045 \, D + 1.5$ | $t$ and $D$ in mm; valid for **petrol engines** | Approximate initial design |
| 6 | Cylinder wall thickness (CI) | $t = 0.054 \, D + 1.5$ | mm; valid for **diesel engines** | Approximate initial design |
| 7 | Cylinder liner outer diameter (dry) | $D_o = D + 2 \, t_l$ | $t_l$ = liner thickness (mm) | Manufacturing drawing |
| 8 | Cylinder liner flange thickness | $t_f = 1.2 \, t_l$ (approx.) | mm | Wet-liner design |
| 9 | Piston speed (mean) | $\bar{V_p} = \dfrac{2 \, L \, N}{60}$ | $N$ = rpm (rev/min); $\bar{V_p}$ in m/s | NVH and friction analysis |
| 10 | Mean Effective Pressure (brake) | $\text{BMEP} = \dfrac{P_b \, n_{\text{rev}}}{V_d \, N}$ | $P_b$ = brake power (W); $n_{\text{rev}}$ = revs per power stroke | Performance evaluation |
| 11 | Heat rejected to coolant (approx) | $Q_c \approx 0.30 \times Q_{\text{fuel}}$ | $Q_{\text{fuel}}$ in J/s | Cooling system design |
| 12 | Fin efficiency (air-cooling) | $\eta_f = \dfrac{\tanh(mL_f)}{mL_f}$ | $m = \sqrt{\dfrac{h P}{k A}}$; $L_f$ = fin length | Heat dissipation design |
| 13 | Coolant flow rate | $\dot{V} = \dfrac{Q_c}{\rho_c \, c_p \, \Delta T}$ | $\rho_c$ = coolant density; $c_p$ = specific heat; $\Delta T$ ≈ **10 °C** | Water pump sizing |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use \vert or \mid for absolute-value notation in the KTU answer booklet.** For example, write $\vert V \vert$ or $\mid V \mid$ — never use the bare vertical pipe `|V|` since it may be interpreted as a column separator in tabular layouts.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Understanding cylinder types is **not academic only**. It directly influences:
- **Service Workshop Decisions:** A mechanic deciding between fitting a **dry liner** (lower cost, easier) versus a **wet liner** (better cooling, OEM-spec for Maruti cars).
- **Engine Overhauls:** Determining whether the block can be **re-bored** (integral type) or a **new liner pressed in** (dry liner type) or a **wet liner replaced** (modern multi-cyl engines).
- **Emission Compliance:** Wet liners allow **lower bore-to-bore spacing** which reduces engine length and weight, indirectly reducing vehicle CO₂ emissions.
- **Racing & Performance Engineering:** Chrome-plated, Nikasil or DLC-coated bores allow **higher rpm** and **lower friction losses**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Swept (Displacement) Volume of a Single Cylinder

### 3.1.1 Starting Geometric Premise

The cylinder is, by definition, a **right circular cylinder** of internal diameter $D$ (bore) and effective axial length $L$ (stroke — the distance the piston travels between top dead centre and bottom dead centre). The piston sweeps out the entire volume of this cylinder during one complete stroke.

### 3.1.2 Derivation — Step-by-Step

Let the bore diameter be $D$ and the stroke length be $L$.

**Step 1 — Cross-sectional area of the bore:**

The cross-section of the cylinder (as seen from the top, with the piston removed) is a circle of diameter $D$ and radius $r = D/2$.

$$
A = \pi \, r^2
$$

Substituting $r = D/2$:

$$
A = \pi \left( \frac{D}{2} \right)^{2}
$$

$$
A = \pi \cdot \frac{D^2}{4}
$$

$$
A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4}
$$

> The cross-sectional area $A$ is in **mm²** when $D$ is in **mm**.

**Step 2 — Multiplication by axial length:**

The volume swept by the piston as it traverses the stroke is the cross-sectional area multiplied by the axial length $L$:

$$
V_s = A \times L
$$

Substituting the expression for $A$ from Step 1:

$$
V_s = \left( \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \right) \times L
$$

$$
V_s = \frac{\pi \, D^{2} \, L}{4}
$$

This is the **fundamental swept volume equation** for a single cylinder.

> **Units:** If $D$ and $L$ are both in **mm**, then $V_s$ comes out in **mm³**. Divide by **1000** to convert to **cm³ (cc)**.

**Step 3 — Total displacement for a multi-cylinder engine:**

If the engine has $n$ cylinders, all identical, the total displacement is:

$$
V_d = n \times V_s
$$

$$
V_d = n \times \frac{\pi D^2 L}{4}
$$

$$
V_d = \frac{\pi \, D^{2} \, L \, n}{4}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Numerical Favourite:** A KTU problem will most often state: *"A 4-stroke, 4-cylinder petrol engine has a bore of 80 mm and a stroke of 90 mm. Find the swept volume of one cylinder and the total displacement of the engine. If the clearance volume is 35 cc per cylinder, find the compression ratio."*
>
> Use the three equations above and arrive at the answer. The numerical evaluation is shown in **§ 3.2** below.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Aligned with KTU 2024 Pattern)

**Problem Statement:**
A 4-cylinder, 4-stroke petrol engine has:
- Bore, $D = 80 \text{ mm}$
- Stroke, $L = 90 \text{ mm}$
- Clearance volume per cylinder, $V_c = 35 \text{ cm}^3$

Find:
1. Swept volume per cylinder, $V_s$
2. Total engine displacement, $V_d$
3. Compression ratio, $r$
4. Bore-to-stroke ratio, $\beta$, and classify the engine as long-stroke or short-stroke.

### 3.2.1 Step 1 — Swept Volume per Cylinder

Using the formula derived in § 3.1.2:

$$
V_s = \frac{\pi D^2 L}{4}
$$

Substituting the numerical values:

$$
V_s = \frac{\pi \times (80 \text{ mm})^2 \times 90 \text{ mm}}{4}
$$

$$
V_s = \frac{\pi \times 6400 \times 90}{4}
$$

$$
V_s = \frac{\pi \times 576000}{4}
$$

$$
V_s = \pi \times 144000
$$

$$
V_s = 3.14159 \times 144000
$$

$$
V_s = 452388.96 \text{ mm}^3
$$

Converting to cubic centimetres (cm³), where $1 \text{ cm}^3 = 1000 \text{ mm}^3$:

$$
V_s = \frac{452388.96}{1000} \text{ cm}^3
$$

$$
\boxed{V_s \approx 452.39 \text{ cm}^3}
$$

> **Note:** KTU expects the answer to **2 decimal places** typically.

### 3.2.2 Step 2 — Total Engine Displacement

$$
V_d = n \times V_s
$$

For a 4-cylinder engine, $n = 4$:

$$
V_d = 4 \times 452.39
$$

$$
V_d = 1809.56 \text{ cm}^3
$$

Converting to litres (where $1 \text{ L} = 1000 \text{ cm}^3$):

$$
V_d = 1.81 \text{ L}
$$

$$
\boxed{V_d \approx 1809.56 \text{ cm}^3 \;\; (\approx 1.81 \text{ L})}
$$

### 3.2.3 Step 3 — Compression Ratio

The compression ratio is defined as:

$$
r = \frac{V_s + V_c}{V_c}
$$

For one cylinder, $V_s = 452.39 \text{ cm}^3$ and $V_c = 35 \text{ cm}^3$:

$$
r = \frac{452.39 + 35}{35}
$$

$$
r = \frac{487.39}{35}
$$

$$
r = 13.93
$$

$$
\boxed{r \approx 13.93 : 1}
$$

> **Engineering Check:** Compression ratios of **10:1 to 14:1** are typical for modern **petrol (SI) engines**. The computed value of **13.93:1** is realistic for a high-output engine. **Diesel engines** have compression ratios of **16:1 to 22:1**.

### 3.2.4 Step 4 — Bore-to-Stroke Ratio and Engine Classification

$$
\beta = \frac{D}{L}
$$

$$
\beta = \frac{80}{90}
$$

$$
\beta = 0.889
$$

Since $\beta = D/L = 80/90 = 0.889 < 1$, the engine is classified as a **long-stroke engine** ($L > D$).

> **Note:** Long-stroke engines favour **higher torque** at **lower rpm** (good for trucks, tractors). Short-stroke engines ($\beta > 1$) favour **higher rpm** and **higher power-to-weight ratio** (good for sports cars and racing).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Air-Cooled vs Water-Cooled Cylinders
