| S.No. | Parameter | Air-Cooled Cylinder | Water-Cooled Cylinder |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cooling medium | Ambient air (forced by fan) | Water + ethylene glycol (pump-circulated) |
| 2 | Heat-transfer coefficient | 50–250 W/m²·K (with fin) | 1000–15000 W/m²·K (with convection) |
| 3 | Specific heat of coolant | 1.005 kJ/kg·K (air) | 3.8 kJ/kg·K (water-glycol mix) |
| 4 | Max power per cylinder (typical) | 8–12 kW | 25–60 kW |
| 5 | Weight of cooling system | Very low | Heavy (radiator + pump + hoses + coolant) |
| 6 | Temperature uniformity along cylinder | Poor (top is hot, bottom is cool) | Excellent |
| 7 | Cold-start warm-up time | Quick (≈ 1 min) | Slow (≈ 5 min via thermostat) |
| 8 | Risk of cavitation | None | Yes (in wet liners) |
| 9 | Maintenance | Low | Moderate (coolant replacement every 2 years) |
| 10 | Common applications | Two-wheelers, aircraft, gensets | Cars, SUVs, trucks, tractors |
| 11 | Cost | Lower | Higher |
| 12 | Noise (cooling fan) | Higher (large fan needed) | Lower |

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Dry Liner vs Wet Liner

| S.No. | Parameter | Dry Liner | Wet Liner |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Coolant contact | **No** (separated by block wall) | **Yes** (direct) |
| 2 | Heat-transfer rate | Lower | Higher (≈ 30% better) |
| 3 | Wall thickness | 2–3 mm | 3–6 mm |
| 4 | Cavitation risk | None | Yes (countered by coatings) |
| 5 | Replacement difficulty | Moderate (press-out) | Easy (pull out from top) |
| 6 | Sealing required | No (interference fit) | Yes (top flange + bottom O-rings) |
| 7 | Bore-to-bore spacing | Larger | Smaller (compact engine) |
| 8 | Applications | Older cars, some tractors | Modern passenger cars |
| 9 | Cost | Lower | Slightly higher |
| 10 | Engine displacement limit | Practically unlimited | Better for small, high-output engines |

---

## 3.5 Step-by-Step Derivation of Cylinder Wall Thickness (Empirical Method)

**Why this derivation matters:** KTU frequently asks for the **approximate wall thickness** in design problems.

**Given:** Bore diameter $D$ (mm), engine type (petrol or diesel).

**Step 1 — Use the empirical formula for petrol engine:**

$$
t = 0.045 \, D + 1.5
$$

For $D = 80 \text{ mm}$:

$$
t = 0.045 \times 80 + 1.5
$$

$$
t = 3.6 + 1.5
$$

$$
t = 5.1 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Use the empirical formula for diesel engine:**

$$
t = 0.054 \, D + 1.5
$$

For the same $D = 80 \text{ mm}$:

$$
t = 0.054 \times 80 + 1.5
$$

$$
t = 4.32 + 1.5
$$

$$
t = 5.82 \text{ mm}
$$

> **Conclusion:** A **diesel engine cylinder wall** is thicker than a petrol engine cylinder wall of the **same bore** because the **combustion pressure in a diesel engine is nearly 2× higher** than in a petrol engine.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Marking Pattern (2024 Scheme):** Part A questions test **Remembering and Understanding** levels of Bloom's Taxonomy. Answers should be **brief (3 to 5 lines)** with a **neat sketch** wherever applicable. Avoid lengthy derivations.

---

### Question A1 — `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**Differentiate between a dry liner and a wet liner cylinder with a neat sketch.**

#### Model Answer (3 marks):

A **dry liner** is a thin-walled cylinder sleeve that is press-fitted into the engine block with an **interference fit** (typically 0.05 mm to 0.10 mm). It does **not come in direct contact with the cooling water** — the block wall separates the liner from the coolant. Its wall thickness is usually **2 mm to 3 mm**.

A **wet liner**, on the other hand, is in **direct contact with the cooling water** on its outer surface. It is held in position by a **flange at the top** (sitting on the block deck) and **sealed at the bottom by rubber O-rings** to prevent coolant leakage. Its wall thickness is **3 mm to 6 mm** to provide structural strength against water pressure and cavitation.

| Feature | Dry Liner | Wet Liner |
|---|---|---|
| Coolant contact | No | Yes |
| Heat transfer | Lower | Higher |
| Wall thickness | 2–3 mm | 3–6 mm |
| Cavitation | None | Possible |

*(1 mark for each: definition, working, neat comparison table.)*

---

### Question A2 — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Why are fins provided on the cylinders of an air-cooled engine? What happens if the fin spacing is too close or too wide?**

#### Model Answer (3 marks):

**Purpose of fins:**
Fins are thin metallic projections (usually **6 to 12 fins per cylinder**, each **3 mm to 6 mm thick** and **15 mm to 30 mm deep**) provided on the outer surface of an air-cooled cylinder. Their primary purpose is to **increase the effective heat-dissipating surface area** of the cylinder, thereby enhancing the rate of heat transfer from the hot cylinder wall to the surrounding air forced over them by a fan.

**Effect of fin spacing:**

- **If spacing is too close** (less than **4 mm**): The airflow becomes **stagnant** between the fins (boundary layer interference), the heat-transfer coefficient drops, and the fins may **overheat**. The hot fins may also burn the rider/driver in two-wheeler applications.
- **If spacing is too wide** (greater than **12 mm**): The total **surface area available** for heat dissipation reduces, lowering the **overall heat-rejection rate**, and the engine may overheat.

**Optimum fin spacing** is therefore in the range of **6 mm to 10 mm**.

*(1 mark — purpose; 1 mark — close spacing effect; 1 mark — wide spacing effect.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Marking Pattern:** Part B questions test **Applying and Analysing** levels of Bloom's Taxonomy. Each Part B carries **14 marks**, usually split as **7 + 7** for sub-parts **(a)** and **(b)**. A **neat labelled sketch** is mandatory and carries **2 to 3 marks** by itself.

---

### Part B — Question A — `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**Question A (a) [7 marks]:** With neat sketches, explain the **different types of cylinder construction** used in IC engines.

**Question A (b) [7 marks]:** Compare **air-cooled and water-cooled cylinders** in a tabular form, listing at least **eight points of difference**.

---

#### Model Solution — Question A (a)

The cylinder of an IC engine can be constructed in **three principal ways**:

**1. Integral or Monoblock Cylinders (1.5 marks + 1 mark sketch):**
In this design, the cylinder is **cast as a single, inseparable part** of the engine block. There is no separate liner. The bore is **machined directly** into the block. The material is usually **cast iron** or, in modern engines, **aluminium alloy** with a special bore coating (Nikasil, Lokasil, or a chrome-plated ring). Examples include older cars (Fiat 1100, Hindustan Ambassador) and many small two-wheelers.

*Advantages:* Compact, lightweight, fewer parts, good stiffness, low cost.
*Disadvantages:* When the bore wears out (typically after **150,000 to 200,000 km**), the **entire block must be replaced** or re-bored to the **next oversize** (0.5 mm increments).

**2. Cylinder with Dry Liner (1.5 marks + 1 mark sketch):**
A separate **thin-walled sleeve (dry liner)** is press-fitted into the bore of the block with an **interference fit** of about **0.05 mm to 0.10 mm**. The liner is **not in direct contact** with the cooling water — the block wall lies between the liner and the jacket. Wall thickness of the dry liner is **2 mm to 3 mm**.

*Advantages:* No leakage of coolant, no cavitation, easy to manufacture.
*Disadvantages:* Lower heat transfer, larger bore-to-bore spacing, slightly heavier.

**3. Cylinder with Wet Liner (1.5 marks + 1 mark sketch):**
A **wet liner** has its **outer surface in direct contact with the cooling water**. The liner is held in place by a **flange on the top** (which sits on the block deck) and **O-rings at the bottom** that seal against coolant leakage. Wall thickness is **3 mm to 6 mm** to withstand water pressure and resist **cavitation pitting**.

*Advantages:* Excellent heat transfer, compact engine design, easy replacement at overhaul.
*Disadvantages:* Requires careful O-ring sealing, risk of cavitation erosion, slightly more expensive.

**Sketches:** All three cylinder types must be drawn with **neat labels** — including block, liner, water jacket, flange, O-ring, and piston rings.

---

#### Model Solution — Question A (b)

| S.No. | Parameter | Air-Cooled Cylinder | Water-Cooled Cylinder |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cooling medium | Air (forced by fan) | Water + glycol (pump-circulated) |
| 2 | Heat-rejection mechanism | Direct (fins) | Indirect (jacket → radiator) |
| 3 | Specific power | Low (8–12 kW/cyl) | High (25–60 kW/cyl) |
| 4 | Engine size suitability | Small engines | All engine sizes |
| 5 | Weight of cooling system | Very low | Heavy |
| 6 | Cold-start performance | Excellent | Slow warm-up |
| 7 | Cost | Lower | Higher |
| 8 | Maintenance | Low | Moderate (coolant change) |
| 9 | Temperature uniformity | Poor | Excellent |
| 10 | Typical applications | Two-wheelers, gensets | Cars, trucks, tractors |

*(0.5 marks per point × 8 points = 4 marks; 3 marks for neat tabular presentation, table title, and engineering interpretation.)*

---

### Part B — Question B — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Question B (a) [7 marks]:** Explain the **different types of cylinder arrangements** used in multi-cylinder engines with neat sketches. State **two advantages** of a V-engine over an inline engine.

**Question B (b) [7 marks]:** A **4-cylinder, 4-stroke petrol engine** has a **bore of 80 mm** and a **stroke of 90 mm**. The **clearance volume per cylinder is 35 cm³**. Calculate:
1. Swept volume of one cylinder
2. Total engine displacement in **litres**
3. Compression ratio
4. Bore-to-stroke ratio, and classify the engine as **long-stroke or short-stroke**.

---

#### Model Solution — Question B (a)

**Cylinder Arrangements in Multi-Cylinder Engines:**

**1. Inline Engine (1.5 marks + 0.5 marks sketch):**
All cylinders are arranged in a **single row** along the length of the crankshaft. Common configurations are **I3, I4, I5, and I6**. The crankshaft has a **single crank throw per cylinder** (for even-firing) or paired throws. Examples: Maruti Suzuki Swift (I4), Tata Tiago (I3).

*Advantages:* Simple design, low cost, easy maintenance.
*Disadvantages:* Long engine, less rigid crankshaft at high cylinder counts.

**2. V-Type Engine (1.5 marks + 0.5 marks sketch):**
Two banks of cylinders are arranged in a **V configuration** on a single crankshaft, with a typical **V-angle of 60° to 90°**. Common configurations: V6, V8, V10, V12. Examples: Audi V8, Ferrari V12.

*Advantages:* Shorter engine length, better primary and secondary balance, higher specific output.
*Disadvantages:* Wider engine, two cylinder heads, more complex.

**3. Flat / Opposed / Boxer Engine (1 mark + 0.5 marks sketch):**
Two banks of cylinders are arranged at **180°** lying horizontally on either side of the crankshaft. The pistons in each bank move towards and away from each other like a boxer's fists. Examples: Porsche 911, Subaru, vintage VW Beetle.

*Advantages:* Lowest centre of gravity, excellent balance, very low vibration.
*Disadvantages:* Wider engine, two oil sumps, complex intake/exhaust routing.

**4. Radial Engine (0.5 marks + sketch not mandatory):**
Cylinders (typically **7 or 9**) are arranged **radially around the crankshaft** in one or two star patterns. Used almost exclusively in **aircraft engines** (e.g., Pratt & Whitney R-2800 Wasp). Excellent power-to-weight ratio and air-cooling.

**5. W-Type Engine (0.5 marks + sketch not mandatory):**
Two V-banks share a common crankshaft at a narrow angle, forming a "W" shape. The Bugatti Veyron's **W16** engine is the most famous example. Very high power (1500 hp) in a compact package.

**Two Advantages of V-Engine over Inline Engine:**
1. **Shorter overall engine length** for the same number of cylinders (e.g., a V8 is roughly the same length as an inline-4).
2. **Better inherent balance** — primary and secondary forces cancel within the V-angle, allowing smoother high-rpm operation.

---

#### Model Solution — Question B (b)

**Given Data:**
- Number of cylinders, $n = 4$
- Bore, $D = 80 \text{ mm}$
- Stroke, $L = 90 \text{ mm}$
- Clearance volume per cylinder, $V_c = 35 \text{ cm}^3$

**Step 1 — Swept Volume per Cylinder:**

$$
V_s = \frac{\pi D^2 L}{4}
$$

$$
V_s = \frac{\pi \times (80)^2 \times 90}{4}
$$

$$
V_s = \frac{\pi \times 6400 \times 90}{4}
$$

$$
V_s = \pi \times 144000
$$

$$
V_s = 452388.96 \text{ mm}^3
$$

$$
V_s \approx 452.39 \text{ cm}^3 \quad \boxed{V_s \approx 452.39 \text{ cm}^3}
$$

*(2 marks — 1 for formula, 1 for numerical evaluation with unit conversion.)*

**Step 2 — Total Displacement:**

$$
V_d = n \times V_s = 4 \times 452.39 = 1809.56 \text{ cm}^3
$$

Converting to litres (1 L = 1000 cm³):

$$
V_d = 1.81 \text{ L} \quad \boxed{V_d \approx 1.81 \text{ L}}
$$

*(1.5 marks — formula, numerical substitution, and correct unit.)*

**Step 3 — Compression Ratio:**

$$
r = \frac{V_s + V_c}{V_c} = \frac{452.39 + 35}{35} = \frac{487.39}{35}
$$

$$
\boxed{r \approx 13.93 : 1}
$$

*(2 marks — formula, substitution, final ratio.)*

**Step 4 — Bore-to-Stroke Ratio:**

$$
\beta = \frac{D}{L} = \frac{80}{90} = 0.889
$$

Since $\beta < 1$, the engine is a **long-stroke engine** (stroke is greater than bore).

$$
\boxed{\beta = 0.889 \;\; \text{(Long-stroke engine)}}
$$

*(1.5 marks — formula, numerical value, correct classification with engineering interpretation.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Cylinder-Type Questions:**
>
> 1. **Unit Mismatch in Swept Volume Calculation:** Many students substitute bore and stroke in **mm** but forget to convert the answer from **mm³** to **cm³** by dividing by 1000. Always state: *"V_s = X mm³ = Y cm³"*. **Marks lost: 1 mark per question.**
>
> 2. **Confusing Wet Liner with Dry Liner:** A common error is to state that a *wet liner* is in contact with **oil**, when in fact it is in contact with **coolant (water-glycol mix)**. Remember — *wet* refers to water contact, not oil.
>
> 3. **Forgetting to Specify "4-Stroke" or "2-Stroke":** In numerical problems, the **number of power strokes per cycle** matters for power calculations. For displacement, it does not — but examiners check for **clarity of mention**.
>
> 4. **No Sketch in 14-mark Questions:** A 14-mark Part B question without a neat labelled sketch will lose **at least 2 to 3 marks** even if the theory is perfect. Always draw.
>
> 5. **Mixing Up Compression Ratio Sign:** Compression ratio is **always written as X:1** (e.g., 13.93:1), **never as 1:13.93**. The larger number comes first.
>
> 6. **Forgetting to Convert to Litres:** Total displacement is conventionally expressed in **litres (L)** in commercial engine specs. Always state the answer in **both cm³ and L**.
>
> 7. **Bore-to-Stroke Classification:** Long-stroke means $L > D$ (i.e., $\beta < 1$); short-stroke means $D > L$ (i.e., $\beta > 1$). Square engine means $D = L$ ($\beta = 1$).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **The following is a high-density rapid-revision checklist.** Read it twice before entering the KTU examination hall.

### A. Core Concepts to Remember

- **Cylinder = pressure vessel + heat-transfer element + piston guide**, all in one.
- The cylinder accounts for **≈ 30 % to 35 %** of total heat rejection from the engine.
- Bore $D$ and stroke $L$ are the two fundamental **geometric parameters** defining an engine.
- Swept volume $V_s = \dfrac{\pi D^2 L}{4}$; total displacement $V_d = n V_s$.
- Compression ratio $r = \dfrac{V_s + V_c}{V_c}$ — typical values: petrol **8:1 to 14:1**, diesel **16:1 to 22:1**.

### B. Cylinder Types — Key Identifiers

- **Air-cooled:** fins on outside, fan-forced air, used in small engines.
- **Water-cooled:** water jacket, pump-circulated coolant, used in cars and trucks.
- **Integral / Monoblock:** no separate liner, bore machined in block.
- **Dry liner:** pressed into block, **no water contact**, wall thickness 2–3 mm.
- **Wet liner:** in **direct water contact**, flange + O-rings, wall thickness 3–6 mm.
- **Cavitation** is a wet-liner-only phenomenon.

### C. Cylinder Arrangements — Quick Identification

- **Inline (I):** single row, simplest, most common.
- **V-type:** two banks, short and rigid, high-performance.
- **Flat / Boxer:** 180° banks, lowest centre of gravity.
- **Radial:** star-shaped, aircraft engines only.
- **W-type:** narrow-angle W, exotic supercars.

### D. Key Numerical Values to Memorise for KTU

- Compression ratio — petrol: 8–14, diesel: 16–22.
- Cylinder wall thickness: $t = 0.045D + 1.5$ (petrol), $0.054D + 1.5$ (diesel).
- Dry liner thickness: 2–3 mm; Wet liner thickness: 3–6 mm.
- Fin spacing optimum: 6–10 mm; fin thickness: 3–6 mm; fin depth: 15–30 mm.
- Coolant flow: 40–80 L/min; coolant temperature: 85–95 °C.
- Specific heat of water-glycol: ≈ 3.8 kJ/kg·K.
- Peak cylinder pressure: petrol 80–120 bar, diesel up to 200 bar.
- Cavitation vapour pressure of water at 20 °C: ≈ 0.023 bar.

### E. Exam-Day Strategy

1. **Read the question twice** — identify the cylinder type, the data given, and what is asked.
2. **Draw a labelled sketch** first for 14-mark Part B questions (2–3 marks guaranteed).
3. **State all formulas in symbol form** before substitution.
4. **Maintain unit consistency** — convert mm to cm where required.
5. **End with a one-line engineering conclusion** — e.g., "Since $\beta < 1$, the engine is a long-stroke engine suitable for high-torque applications."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cylinder Liners
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT205 - AUTOMOBILE POWER PLANT (AUTOMOBILE ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Cylinder Liners

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Cylinder Liner** (also called a cylinder sleeve) is a precision-engineered, hollow cylindrical component that is inserted into the cylinder bore of an internal combustion (IC) engine block to form the working surface against which the piston reciprocates. It serves as the wear-resistant interface between the piston rings and the engine block, and is designed to be replaceable once wear exceeds serviceable limits, thereby extending the operational life of the engine block itself.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** A cylinder liner is a cylindrical metal sleeve fitted into the cylinder bore of an engine to provide a hard, wear-resistant, and replaceable surface for piston operation. It forms the combustion chamber wall on the bore side and participates in heat transfer to the cooling medium.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are wearing an expensive, formal **leather shoe**. The shoe itself (the engine block) is costly and difficult to manufacture. However, the **inner sole** of the shoe receives all the friction, sweat, and wear every single day. Instead of throwing the entire shoe away when the inner sole wears out, you simply **replace the inner sole** — keeping the outer shoe intact.

In an automobile engine, the **engine block** is the "shoe," and the **cylinder liner** is the "replaceable inner sole." The piston continuously rubs against the liner thousands of times per minute, generating enormous friction and heat. Over time, the liner surface gets scored, worn, and glazed. By making this surface a separate, easily replaceable component, the engine block (the most expensive part of the engine) is protected, and maintenance becomes simple and economical.

### 1.3 Why Are Cylinder Liners Used?

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Reasons (High-Yield KTU Concept):**
> 1. **Replaceability** — Liners can be replaced when worn, without scrapping the block.
> 2. **Wear Resistance** — Liners are made of superior materials (alloy cast iron) compared to the block (often plain cast iron or aluminum).
> 3. **Heat Dissipation** — Especially in *wet liners*, direct contact with coolant ensures efficient heat removal.
> 4. **Manufacturing Economy** — Blocks can be cast in softer, more machinable alloys; liners are precision-finished separately.
> 5. **Standardization** — Allows the use of common liners across different engine variants.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

The following standard design parameters are used in liner specifications:

- **Bore Diameter (D):** Typically **65 mm to 150 mm** for passenger car and commercial vehicle diesel engines.
- **Liner Wall Thickness (t):** Usually **1.5 mm to 6 mm**.
- **Interference Fit Pressure (dry liners):** Typically **30 MPa to 80 MPa**.
- **Surface Hardness:** Generally **180 to 250 BHN** (Brinell Hardness Number) for centrifugally cast liners.
- **Operating Temperature at Combustion Side:** Can reach **250°C to 300°C** at full load.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Types of Cylinder Liners (KTU Board-Favorite Topic)

Cylinder liners are classified into **three** primary categories based on their contact with the coolant:

#### A. Dry Liners (Non-Sleeve Type)

A **dry liner** does **NOT** come into direct contact with the engine's cooling water (coolant). It is press-fitted into the cylinder block with a slight **interference fit** and is completely surrounded by the metal of the block.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Characteristics of Dry Liners:**
> - Wall thickness: **1.5 mm to 3 mm** (thinner than wet liners).
> - Installed by **press-fitting** (interference fit).
> - Requires **precision boring** of the block.
> - No sealing gaskets required against coolant.
> - Found in **most modern petrol/gasoline engines** and small diesel engines.

#### B. Wet Liners (Sleeve Type)

A **wet liner** is in **direct contact with the coolant** on its outer surface. Coolant circulates around the outside of the liner, providing direct and very efficient heat transfer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Characteristics of Wet Liners:**
> - Wall thickness: **3 mm to 6 mm** (thicker to withstand coolant pressure).
> - Sealed at the top and bottom with **rubber O-rings** or **copper gaskets**.
> - Top flange rests on the cylinder head gasket surface.
> - Used in **heavy-duty diesel engines** (trucks, buses, tractors, marine engines).
> - Allows **liner replacement** without engine block removal in some designs.

#### C. Finned Liners (Air-Cooled Engines)

These are used in **air-cooled engines** (e.g., older Volkswagen Beetles, small motorcycles, aircraft engines). The outer surface of the liner is cast with **cooling fins** to increase surface area for heat dissipation into the surrounding air stream.

### 2.2 Materials Used for Cylinder Liners

| Material | Composition/Property | Application |
|----------|----------------------|-------------|
| **Plain Cast Iron** | Fe + 3.0–3.5% C + alloying | Older engines, low-stress |
| **Alloy Cast Iron (Pearlitic)** | Cr, Mo, Ni added | Most modern diesel liners |
| **Centrifugally Cast Iron** | Dense grain structure, high wear resistance | Heavy-duty applications |
| **Chrome-Plated Liners** | Hard chrome coating (0.05–0.15 mm) | High-performance, racing |
| **Nikasil Coating** | Nickel-Silicon-Carbide | BMW, premium European engines |
| **Cast Iron with Inductively Hardened Bore** | Surface hardened to 55+ HRC | Modern high-output engines |

### 2.3 Manufacturing Processes

1. **Sand Casting** — Traditional, used for low-volume production.
2. **Centrifugal Casting** — Produces dense, defect-free liners; **industry standard**.
3. **Die Casting** — High-volume, thin-wall liners.

### 2.4 Design Considerations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Design Parameters (KTU High-Yield):**
> - **Wall Thickness:** Must be sufficient to withstand combustion pressure (typically 7–25 bar in petrol, 40–200 bar in diesel).
> - **Flange Design (Wet Liners):** Top flange must seal against the head gasket.
> - **Anti-Cavitation Design:** Lower outer surface of wet liners is often coated or shaped to resist **cavitation pitting** caused by coolant bubble collapse.
> - **Coolant Jacket Clearance:** Adequate space must remain for coolant flow around wet liners.
> - **Top Stop Ring:** Prevents the liner from rising due to combustion gas pressure.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following formulas are essential for KTU university exam numerical and derivations.

| Formula | Description | Units |
|---------|-------------|-------|
| $D_{bore} = D_{piston} + 2 \cdot g_{p}$ | Bore diameter based on piston diameter and piston-to-bore clearance | mm |
| $t_{liner} = 0.05 \cdot D_{bore}$ to $0.08 \cdot D_{bore}$ | Empirical wall thickness guideline | mm |
| $\sigma_{hoop} = \dfrac{p \cdot D}{2 \cdot t}$ | Hoop stress in thin-walled cylinder | MPa |
| $\sigma_{long} = \dfrac{p \cdot D}{4 \cdot t}$ | Longitudinal (axial) stress | MPa |
| $\Delta D = \dfrac{p \cdot D^{2}}{4 \cdot t \cdot E} \cdot (1 - \nu)$ | Diametral expansion due to internal pressure | mm |
| $P_{fit} = \dfrac{\delta}{D} \cdot \dfrac{(D_{o}^{2} - D^{2})}{(2 \cdot D^{2})}$ | Interference fit pressure (thick cylinder) | MPa |
| $h_{c} = \dfrac{\ln(D_{o}/D)}{2 \pi k L}$ | Convective heat transfer coefficient estimation | W/m²K |
| $Q = \dfrac{k \cdot A \cdot \Delta T}{t}$ | Heat conduction through liner wall | W |

Where:
- $D$ = Liner inner diameter
- $D_{o}$ = Liner outer diameter
- $t$ = Wall thickness
- $p$ = Internal (combustion) pressure
- $E$ = Young's modulus (~100 GPa for cast iron)
- $\nu$ = Poisson's ratio (~0.25 for cast iron)
- $\delta$ = Radial interference
- $k$ = Thermal conductivity of liner material
- $L$ = Liner length

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Hoop Stress in a Cylinder Liner

Consider a thin-walled cylinder liner subjected to internal combustion pressure $p$. The liner behaves as a **thin pressure vessel** because $t / D < 0.1$.

**Step 1: Free Body Diagram Setup**

Cut the liner along a longitudinal axial plane. Consider a half-cylinder of length $L$ and internal diameter $D$. The internal pressure $p$ acts on the projected area $D \cdot L$.

**Step 2: Force Balance**

The bursting force on the projected area is balanced by the hoop (circumferential) stress $\sigma_{h}$ acting on the two cut sections of wall.

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{pressure} &= p \cdot D \cdot L \\
F_{stress} &= 2 \cdot \sigma_{h} \cdot t \cdot L
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Equilibrium Equation**

Equating the two forces (since the half-cylinder is in static equilibrium):

$$
\begin{aligned}
2 \cdot \sigma_{h} \cdot t \cdot L &= p \cdot D \cdot L \\
\sigma_{h} &= \dfrac{p \cdot D}{2 \cdot t}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Numerical Verification Example**

For a diesel engine with $p = 80$ bar = 8 MPa, $D = 100$ mm, $t = 5$ mm:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{h} &= \dfrac{8 \cdot 100}{2 \cdot 5} \\
&= \dfrac{800}{10} \\
&= 80 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since cast iron has a tensile strength of $\approx 200$–$300$ MPa, the liner operates with a **factor of safety of about 2.5–4**, which is acceptable.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Interference Fit Pressure (Lamé's Equations)

For a **dry liner** press-fitted into the block, the radial interference $\delta$ generates a contact pressure $P_{fit}$ at the liner-block interface.

**Step 1: Radial Displacement in Thick Cylinder (Lamé Equation)**

For a thick-walled cylinder under internal pressure $P_{i}$ and external pressure $P_{o}$, the radial displacement at any radius $r$ is given by Lamé's equation:

$$
u_{r} = \dfrac{1}{E} \left[ (1 - \nu) \cdot \dfrac{P_{i} \cdot r_{i}^{2} - P_{o} \cdot r_{o}^{2}}{r_{o}^{2} - r_{i}^{2}} \cdot r + (1 + \nu) \cdot \dfrac{(P_{i} - P_{o}) \cdot r_{i}^{2} \cdot r_{o}^{2}}{(r_{o}^{2} - r_{i}^{2}) \cdot r} \right]
$$

**Step 2: Displacement at Outer Surface of Liner**

For the liner (internal pressure = 0, external pressure = $-P_{fit}$ — compression):

$$
u_{r,outer}^{liner} = \dfrac{-P_{fit} \cdot r_{o}}{E_{liner}} \cdot \left[ \dfrac{r_{o}^{2} + r_{i}^{2}}{r_{o}^{2} - r_{i}^{2}} - \nu_{liner} \right]
$$

**Step 3: Displacement at Inner Surface of Block**

For the block (internal pressure = $-P_{fit}$, external pressure = 0, treated as a semi-infinite body):

$$
u_{r,inner}^{block} = \dfrac{P_{fit} \cdot r_{o}}{E_{block}} \cdot \left[ 1 - \nu_{block} \right] \quad \text{(for thick block)}
$$

**Step 4: Compatibility Condition**

The total radial interference $\delta$ equals the sum of these two displacements (with appropriate sign):

$$
\delta = r_{o} \cdot P_{fit} \cdot \left[ \dfrac{1}{E_{liner}} \left( \dfrac{r_{o}^{2} + r_{i}^{2}}{r_{o}^{2} - r_{i}^{2}} + \nu_{liner} \right) + \dfrac{1 - \nu_{block}}{E_{block}} \right]
$$

**Step 5: Simplified Form for Thin Liners in Stiff Block**

If the liner is thin and the block is much stiffer ($E_{block} \gg E_{liner}$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{fit} &\approx \dfrac{\delta \cdot E_{liner}}{r_{o} \cdot \left[ \dfrac{r_{o}^{2} + r_{i}^{2}}{r_{o}^{2} - r_{i}^{2}} + \nu_{liner} \right]} \\
P_{fit} &\approx \dfrac{\delta \cdot E_{liner}}{2 \cdot r_{o}} \quad \text{(for very thin liners)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Numerical Example**

Let $\delta = 0.05$ mm, $r_{i} = 50$ mm, $r_{o} = 52.5$ mm, $E_{liner} = 100$ GPa, $\nu = 0.25$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{fit} &= \dfrac{0.05 \cdot 100{,}000}{52.5 \cdot \left[ \dfrac{52.5^{2} + 50^{2}}{52.5^{2} - 50^{2}} + 0.25 \right]} \\
&= \dfrac{5000}{52.5 \cdot \left[ \dfrac{2756.25 + 2500}{2756.25 - 2500} + 0.25 \right]} \\
&= \dfrac{5000}{52.5 \cdot \left[ \dfrac{5256.25}{256.25} + 0.25 \right]} \\
&= \dfrac{5000}{52.5 \cdot (20.51 + 0.25)} \\
&= \dfrac{5000}{52.5 \cdot 20.76} \\
&= \dfrac{5000}{1089.9} \\
&\approx 4.59 \text{ MPa (interference contact pressure)}
\end{aligned}
$$

This pressure generates sufficient friction to hold the dry liner in place against combustion forces.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Liner Types

| Parameter | Dry Liner | Wet Liner | Finned Liner |
|-----------|-----------|-----------|--------------|
| **Coolant Contact** | None | Direct | None (air-cooled) |
| **Wall Thickness** | 1.5–3 mm | 3–6 mm | 2–4 mm |
| **Heat Dissipation** | Moderate (via block) | Excellent (direct) | Good (via fins + air) |
| **Replacement** | Difficult (requires rebore) | Easy (pull out) | Easy |
| **Sealing** | Interference fit | O-rings + flange | Press fit |
| **Cavitation Risk** | None | High (lower outer surface) | None |
| **Typical Application** | Petrol cars | Heavy-duty diesel | Air-cooled engines |
| **Manufacturing Cost** | Low | Moderate | Low |
| **Weight** | Lightest | Heaviest | Light |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Classification of Cylinder Liners

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[CYLINDER LINERS] --> B[DRY LINER]
    A --> C[WET LINER]
    A --> D[FINNED LINER]
    A --> E[PLATED LINER]

    B --> B1[Press fitted in block]
    B --> B2[No coolant contact]
    B --> B3[Thin wall 1.5 to 3 mm]
    B --> B4[Used in petrol engines]

    C --> C1[Direct coolant contact]
    C --> C2[Sealed with O rings]
    C --> C3[Thick wall 3 to 6 mm]
    C --> C4[Used in heavy diesel]

    D --> D1[Air cooled engines]
    D --> D2[Cooling fins on OD]
    D --> D3[High surface area]

    E --> E1[Chrome plated]
    E --> E2[Nikasil coated]
    E --> E3[High wear resistance]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Architecture: Dry Liner Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Combustion Gas] --> B[Inner Liner Surface]
    B --> C[Liner Wall]
    C --> D[Interference Fit Interface]
    D --> E[Engine Block Wall]
    E --> F[Coolant Jacket]
    F --> G[Coolant Outlet]
    H[Press Fit Pressure 30 to 80 MPa] --> D
    I[Heat Flow Q] --> B
    I --> C
    I --> E
    I --> F
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Architecture: Wet Liner Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Combustion Gas] --> B[Inner Liner Surface]
    B --> C[Liner Wall 3 to 6 mm]
    C --> D[Coolant Direct Contact]
    D --> E[Coolant Inlet]
    E --> F[Heat Absorption]
    F --> G[Coolant Outlet]
    H[Top Flange Seal] --> C
    I[Bottom O Ring Seals] --> C
    J[Cavitation Pitting Zone] --> C
    B --> K[Piston Rings Contact]
```

### 4.4 Schematic Description: Physical Layout (Text Representation)

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Layout of a Wet Cylinder Liner (Cross-Section, Top to Bottom):**
> 1. **Top Flange** — Rests on the cylinder head gasket surface; provides upward stop.
> 2. **Combustion Zone Region** — Inner bore where piston rings slide; hardest, most wear-resistant area.
> 3. **Mid-Section** — Surrounded by coolant; subject to cavitation pitting on outer surface.
> 4. **Bottom Support Region** — Seated in the engine block with anti-cavitation flange.
> 5. **O-Ring Grooves (Top and Bottom)** — Rubber sealing rings prevent coolant leakage into crankcase or combustion chamber.

### 4.5 Mermaid Decision Flow: Liner Selection Logic

```mermaid
graph TD
    Start[Engine Design Requirements] --> Q1{Engine Type?}
    Q1 -->|Petrol| P[Light Duty]
    Q1 -->|Diesel| Q2{Load Type?}
    Q1 -->|Air Cooled| R[Finned Liner]

    Q2 -->|Heavy Duty Truck Bus| S[Wet Liner]
    Q2 -->|Light Commercial| T[Dry Liner]

    P --> P1[Thin Wall Dry Liner]
    S --> S1[Thick Wall Wet Liner with O Rings]
    T --> T1[Press Fit Dry Liner]
    R --> R1[Fin Outer Surface Cast Liner]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Differentiate between a dry liner and a wet liner in an IC engine.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Dry Liner | Wet Liner |
|---------|-----------|-----------|
| Coolant Contact | Not in direct contact with coolant | In direct contact with coolant |
| Wall Thickness | Thin (1.5–3 mm) | Thick (3–6 mm) |
| Installation | Press-fitted (interference fit) | Held by flange + sealed with O-rings |
| Replacement | Difficult; requires reboring | Easy; can be pulled out |
| Heat Transfer | Indirect (via block) | Direct and very efficient |
| Application | Petrol/gasoline engines | Heavy-duty diesel engines |

*(3 marks — clear differentiation with examples)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**List any three functions of a cylinder liner in an internal combustion engine.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Provides a hard, wear-resistant working surface** for the piston rings to slide against, protecting the engine block from wear.
2. **Forms a sealed combustion chamber** by precisely defining the bore diameter and sealing against combustion gases.
3. **Facilitates heat transfer** from the combustion gases to the cooling medium (especially in wet liners), preventing overheating of the piston and rings.

*(3 marks — one mark for each correctly stated function)*

---

### 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> When answering liner questions, students commonly lose marks by:
> 1. **Confusing dry and wet liner applications** — always state the *application* explicitly.
> 2. **Skipping material specifications** — examiners award 2 marks for naming materials like "alloy cast iron, pearlitic, centrifugally cast."
> 3. **Not drawing/sketching** — even a simple labeled diagram in your answer book earns 1–2 easy marks.
> 4. **Ignoring cavitation and O-ring sealing** for wet liners — these are **guaranteed 2-mark sub-questions** in KTU papers.
> 5. **Not justifying why** a particular liner type is chosen for a given engine — always give the engineering *reason*.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Module 1, Liner Types

**Q.A. (a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **construction and working of a wet cylinder liner** used in heavy-duty diesel engines. Discuss the materials used and the sealing arrangement. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Construction:**
A wet cylinder liner is a hollow cylindrical sleeve with a top flange. The flange rests on the top deck of the engine block and forms a seal with the cylinder head gasket. The body of the liner is surrounded by engine coolant (water + ethylene glycol mixture). Rubber O-rings or copper gaskets are placed in grooves at the top and bottom of the liner to prevent coolant leakage.

**Working:**
During engine operation, hot combustion gases heat the inner surface of the liner to temperatures of 250–300°C. This heat flows radially outward through the liner wall (thickness 3–6 mm) and is absorbed directly by the surrounding coolant. Coolant enters from the bottom of the jacket, absorbs heat as it rises, and exits from the top — naturally driven by thermosiphon or forced by the water pump.

**Materials Used:**
- **Pearlitic alloy cast iron** (Fe + 3.0–3.5% C + Cr, Mo, Ni) — provides high wear resistance and strength.
- Manufactured by **centrifugal casting** for uniform grain structure.
- Outer surface often coated or hardened to resist **cavitation pitting**.

**Sealing Arrangement:**
- **Top Seal:** Top flange compressed between block and head gasket.
- **Bottom Seals:** Two or three **rubber O-rings** seated in grooves to seal against coolant leakage into the crankcase and to prevent combustion gases from escaping downward.
- **Anti-cavitation flange** at the lower outer surface reduces coolant bubble collapse damage.

*(7 marks: 2 for sketch/description, 2 for materials, 2 for sealing, 1 for working)*

---

**Q.A. (b)** Explain the **causes of cylinder liner wear** in a diesel engine. Discuss the methods to **measure liner wear** and **state the limits** beyond which a liner must be replaced. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Causes of Liner Wear:**

1. **Abrasive Wear** — Dust and carbon particles entering through the air intake act like sandpaper on the bore surface.
2. **Adhesive Wear** — Direct metal-to-metal contact when the oil film breaks down under high load and temperature (boundary lubrication).
3. **Corrosive Wear** — Combustion products (especially SO₂ and SO₃ from sulfur in diesel fuel) combine with moisture to form **sulfuric acid**, which corrodes the bore.
4. **Cavitation Pitting** (Wet Liners Only) — High-frequency oscillation of the liner in coolant creates vapor bubbles that collapse violently, pitting the outer surface and eventually cracking through.
5. **Thermal Fatigue** — Repeated heating-cooling cycles cause micro-cracks on the bore surface.

**Measurement of Liner Wear:**
- **Bore Gauge Measurement** — A dial bore gauge (DBG) is used at multiple heights (top, mid, bottom of stroke) and at two perpendicular axes (X and Y).
- **Top Dead Centre (TDC) and Bottom Dead Centre (BDC)** measurements reveal **taper** (ovality).
- Wear is reported as the **difference between maximum and minimum bore diameter** along the stroke.

**Replacement Limits:**
- **Taper limit:** Typically **0.05 mm to 0.10 mm** per cylinder.
- **Ovality limit:** Typically **0.03 mm to 0.05 mm**.
- When the bore diameter exceeds the **service limit** (e.g., +0.50 mm over standard bore), a new liner or rebore is mandatory.

*(7 marks: 3 for causes with examples, 2 for measurement, 2 for limits)*

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Q.B. (a)** Compare the **materials used for cylinder liners**. Explain why **centrifugally cast pearlitic alloy cast iron** is the industry-preferred material for modern heavy-duty diesel engine liners. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Comparison of Materials:**

| Material | Wear Resistance | Cost | Typical Use |
|----------|-----------------|------|-------------|
| Plain Cast Iron | Moderate | Low | Old engines |
| Alloy Cast Iron | High | Moderate | Modern diesel |
| Centrifugally Cast Iron | Very High | Moderate | Heavy-duty |
| Chrome Plated | Excellent | High | Racing/performance |
| Nikasil | Excellent | Very High | Premium European |

**Why Centrifugally Cast Pearlitic Alloy Cast Iron?**

1. **Dense Grain Structure** — Centrifugal casting forces molten iron against the mold walls under high G-forces, eliminating porosity and producing a fine, dense, directional grain structure aligned with the direction of stress. This dramatically increases fatigue strength.

2. **Pearlitic Matrix** — The pearlitic microstructure (alternating ferrite and cementite lamellae) provides an excellent combination of **hardness (200–250 BHN)** and **toughness**, ideal for sliding contact with piston rings.

3. **Alloying Additions (Cr, Mo, Ni)** —
   - **Chromium (0.5–1.5%)** — Forms hard chromium carbides, increasing wear resistance.
   - **Molybdenum (0.3–0.8%)** — Improves high-temperature strength and reduces temper brittleness.
   - **Nickel (0.5–1.5%)** — Enhances toughness and corrosion resistance.

4. **Cost-Effectiveness** — Compared to Nikasil or ceramic coatings, centrifugally cast iron is **5 to 10 times cheaper** while delivering acceptable performance for most commercial applications.

5. **Manufacturability** — Easy to machine, bore, and hone to tight tolerances.

*(7 marks: 2 for comparison table, 5 for justification)*

---

**Q.B. (b)** A diesel engine has a cylinder bore diameter of **100 mm**, a liner wall thickness of **5 mm**, and operates at a peak combustion pressure of **80 bar**. Calculate the **hoop stress** and **longitudinal stress** in the liner wall. Also, determine the **factor of safety** if the liner material has an ultimate tensile strength of **250 MPa**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given Data:**
- Bore diameter $D = 100$ mm
- Wall thickness $t = 5$ mm
- Peak combustion pressure $p = 80$ bar = $8$ MPa
- Ultimate tensile strength $\sigma_{u} = 250$ MPa

**Step 1: Hoop (Circumferential) Stress**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{h} &= \dfrac{p \cdot D}{2 \cdot t} \\
\sigma_{h} &= \dfrac{8 \cdot 100}{2 \cdot 5} \\
\sigma_{h} &= \dfrac{800}{10} \\
\sigma_{h} &= 80 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Longitudinal (Axial) Stress**

For a closed-ended cylinder (engine combustion chamber is closed at TDC):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{L} &= \dfrac{p \cdot D}{4 \cdot t} \\
\sigma_{L} &= \dfrac{8 \cdot 100}{4 \cdot 5} \\
\sigma_{L} &= \dfrac{800}{20} \\
\sigma_{L} &= 40 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Maximum Principal Stress**

The hoop stress is the maximum, so:

$$
\sigma_{max} = \sigma_{h} = 80 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 4: Factor of Safety**

$$
\begin{aligned}
FOS &= \dfrac{\sigma_{u}}{\sigma_{max}} \\
FOS &= \dfrac{250}{80} \\
FOS &= 3.125
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:**
The liner operates at a factor of safety of **3.125**, which is well within the safe design range (typically 2.5–4 for cast iron components under static and low-cycle fatigue loading). The design is **safe**.

*(7 marks: 2 for hoop, 2 for longitudinal, 1 for max stress identification, 2 for FOS calculation)*

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- **Cylinder Liner:** A replaceable cylindrical sleeve forming the piston bore in IC engines.
- **Dry Liner:** No coolant contact, press-fitted, thin wall, used in petrol engines.
- **Wet Liner:** Direct coolant contact, O-ring sealed, thick wall, used in heavy diesel.
- **Finned Liner:** For air-cooled engines; cooling fins on outer surface.
- **Plated Liner:** Chrome or Nikasil coated for high wear resistance.
- **Preferred Material:** Centrifugally cast pearlitic alloy cast iron (Cr, Mo, Ni added).
- **Hoop Stress Formula:** $\sigma_{h} = \dfrac{p \cdot D}{2 \cdot t}$ — *memorize this; KTU favorite*.
- **Longitudinal Stress:** $\sigma_{L} = \dfrac{p \cdot D}{4 \cdot t}$.
- **Interference Fit:** Dry liners rely on press fit (typically 30–80 MPa contact pressure).
- **Cavitation Pitting:** A wet liner failure caused by coolant bubble collapse on the outer surface.
- **Common Wears:** Abrasive, adhesive, corrosive, cavitation, and thermal fatigue.
- **Wear Measurement:** Dial bore gauge at TDC, mid-stroke, BDC; along X and Y axes.
- **Taper/Ovality Limits:** 0.05–0.10 mm taper and 0.03–0.05 mm ovality for replacement.
- **Top Flange Function:** Provides upward stop and seals against head gasket.
- **Liner Replacement Advantage:** Cheaper than replacing the entire engine block.
- **Standard Bore Range:** 65 mm (small) to 150 mm (large commercial diesel).
- **Surface Hardness:** 180–250 BHN typical for centrifugally cast liners.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Exam Tip:** Always draw a **labeled diagram** when explaining dry/wet liner construction — it is worth 2 free marks in a 7-mark question. Also, remember the difference between **taper** (top-to-bottom wear) and **ovality** (X vs Y axis wear) — these terms appear in almost every KTU past paper.

## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Cylinder Liner** (also called a cylinder sleeve) is a precision-engineered, hollow cylindrical component that is inserted into the cylinder bore of an internal combustion (IC) engine block to form the working surface against which the piston reciprocates. It serves as the wear-resistant interface between the piston rings and the engine block, and is designed to be replaceable once wear exceeds serviceable limits, thereby extending the operational life of the engine block itself.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** A cylinder liner is a cylindrical metal sleeve fitted into the cylinder bore of an engine to provide a hard, wear-resistant, and replaceable surface for piston operation. It forms the combustion chamber wall on the bore side and participates in heat transfer to the cooling medium.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are wearing an expensive, formal **leather shoe**. The shoe itself (the engine block) is costly and difficult to manufacture. However, the **inner sole** of the shoe receives all the friction, sweat, and wear every single day. Instead of throwing the entire shoe away when the inner sole wears out, you simply **replace the inner sole** — keeping the outer shoe intact.

In an automobile engine, the **engine block** is the "shoe," and the **cylinder liner** is the "replaceable inner sole." The piston continuously rubs against the liner thousands of times per minute, generating enormous friction and heat. Over time, the liner surface gets scored, worn, and glazed. By making this surface a separate, easily replaceable component, the engine block (the most expensive part of the engine) is protected, and maintenance becomes simple and economical.

### 1.3 Why Are Cylinder Liners Used?

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Reasons (High-Yield KTU Concept):**
> 1. **Replaceability** — Liners can be replaced when worn, without scrapping the block.
> 2. **Wear Resistance** — Liners are made of superior materials (alloy cast iron) compared to the block (often plain cast iron or aluminum).
> 3. **Heat Dissipation** — Especially in *wet liners*, direct contact with coolant ensures efficient heat removal.
> 4. **Manufacturing Economy** — Blocks can be cast in softer, more machinable alloys; liners are precision-finished separately.
> 5. **Standardization** — Allows the use of common liners across different engine variants.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

The following standard design parameters are used in liner specifications:

- **Bore Diameter (D):** Typically **65 mm to 150 mm** for passenger car and commercial vehicle diesel engines.
- **Liner Wall Thickness (t):** Usually **1.5 mm to 6 mm**.
- **Interference Fit Pressure (dry liners):** Typically **30 MPa to 80 MPa**.
- **Surface Hardness:** Generally **180 to 250 BHN** (Brinell Hardness Number) for centrifugally cast liners.
- **Operating Temperature at Combustion Side:** Can reach **250°C to 300°C** at full load.

## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Types of Cylinder Liners (KTU Board-Favorite Topic)

Cylinder liners are classified into **three** primary categories based on their contact with the coolant:

#### A. Dry Liners (Non-Sleeve Type)

A **dry liner** does **NOT** come into direct contact with the engine's cooling water (coolant). It is press-fitted into the cylinder block with a slight **interference fit** and is completely surrounded by the metal of the block.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Characteristics of Dry Liners:**
> - Wall thickness: **1.5 mm to 3 mm** (thinner than wet liners).
> - Installed by **press-fitting** (interference fit).
> - Requires **precision boring** of the block.
> - No sealing gaskets required against coolant.
> - Found in **most modern petrol/gasoline engines** and small diesel engines.

#### B. Wet Liners (Sleeve Type)

A **wet liner** is in **direct contact with the coolant** on its outer surface. Coolant circulates around the outside of the liner, providing direct and very efficient heat transfer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Characteristics of Wet Liners:**
> - Wall thickness: **3 mm to 6 mm** (thicker to withstand coolant pressure).
> - Sealed at the top and bottom with **rubber O-rings** or **copper gaskets**.
> - Top flange rests on the cylinder head gasket surface.
> - Used in **heavy-duty diesel engines** (trucks, buses, tractors, marine engines).
> - Allows **liner replacement** without engine block removal in some designs.

#### C. Finned Liners (Air-Cooled Engines)

These are used in **air-cooled engines** (e.g., older Volkswagen Beetles, small motorcycles, aircraft engines). The outer surface of the liner is cast with **cooling fins** to increase surface area for heat dissipation into the surrounding air stream.

### 2.2 Materials Used for Cylinder Liners

| Material | Composition/Property | Application |
|----------|----------------------|-------------|
| **Plain Cast Iron** | Fe + 3.0–3.5% C + alloying | Older engines, low-stress |
| **Alloy Cast Iron (Pearlitic)** | Cr, Mo, Ni added | Most modern diesel liners |
| **Centrifugally Cast Iron** | Dense grain structure, high wear resistance | Heavy-duty applications |
| **Chrome-Plated Liners** | Hard chrome coating (0.05–0.15 mm) | High-performance, racing |
| **Nikasil Coating** | Nickel-Silicon-Carbide | BMW, premium European engines |
| **Cast Iron with Inductively Hardened Bore** | Surface hardened to 55+ HRC | Modern high-output engines |

### 2.3 Manufacturing Processes

1. **Sand Casting** — Traditional, used for low-volume production.
2. **Centrifugal Casting** — Produces dense, defect-free liners; **industry standard**.
3. **Die Casting** — High-volume, thin-wall liners.

### 2.4 Design Considerations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Design Parameters (KTU High-Yield):**
> - **Wall Thickness:** Must be sufficient to withstand combustion pressure (typically 7–25 bar in petrol, 40–200 bar in diesel).
> - **Flange Design (Wet Liners):** Top flange must seal against the head gasket.
> - **Anti-Cavitation Design:** Lower outer surface of wet liners is often coated or shaped to resist **cavitation pitting** caused by coolant bubble collapse.
> - **Coolant Jacket Clearance:** Adequate space must remain for coolant flow around wet liners.
> - **Top Stop Ring:** Prevents the liner from rising due to combustion gas pressure.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following formulas are essential for KTU university exam numerical and derivations.

| Formula | Description | Units |
|---------|-------------|-------|
| $D_{bore} = D_{piston} + 2 \cdot g_{p}$ | Bore diameter based on piston diameter and piston-to-bore clearance | mm |
| $t_{liner} = 0.05 \cdot D_{bore}$ to $0.08 \cdot D_{bore}$ | Empirical wall thickness guideline | mm |
| $\sigma_{hoop} = \dfrac{p \cdot D}{2 \cdot t}$ | Hoop stress in thin-walled cylinder | MPa |
| $\sigma_{long} = \dfrac{p \cdot D}{4 \cdot t}$ | Longitudinal (axial) stress | MPa |
| $\Delta D = \dfrac{p \cdot D^{2}}{4 \cdot t \cdot E} \cdot (1 - \nu)$ | Diametral expansion due to internal pressure | mm |
| $P_{fit} = \dfrac{\delta}{D} \cdot \dfrac{(D_{o}^{2} - D^{2})}{(2 \cdot D^{2})}$ | Interference fit pressure (thick cylinder) | MPa |
| $h_{c} = \dfrac{\ln(D_{o}/D)}{2 \pi k L}$ | Convective heat transfer coefficient estimation | W/m²K |
| $Q = \dfrac{k \cdot A \cdot \Delta T}{t}$ | Heat conduction through liner wall | W |

Where:
- $D$ = Liner inner diameter
- $D_{o}$ = Liner outer diameter
- $t$ = Wall thickness
- $p$ = Internal (combustion) pressure
- $E$ = Young's modulus (~100 GPa for cast iron)
- $\nu$ = Poisson's ratio (~0.25 for cast iron)
- $\delta$ = Radial interference
- $k$ = Thermal conductivity of liner material
- $L$ = Liner length

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Hoop Stress in a Cylinder Liner

Consider a thin-walled cylinder liner subjected to internal combustion pressure $p$. The liner behaves as a **thin pressure vessel** because $t / D < 0.1$.

**Step 1: Free Body Diagram Setup**

Cut the liner along a longitudinal axial plane. Consider a half-cylinder of length $L$ and internal diameter $D$. The internal pressure $p$ acts on the projected area $D \cdot L$.

**Step 2: Force Balance**

The bursting force on the projected area is balanced by the hoop (circumferential) stress $\sigma_{h}$ acting on the two cut sections of wall.

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{pressure} &= p \cdot D \cdot L \\
F_{stress} &= 2 \cdot \sigma_{h} \cdot t \cdot L
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Equilibrium Equation**

Equating the two forces (since the half-cylinder is in static equilibrium):

$$
\begin{aligned}
2 \cdot \sigma_{h} \cdot t \cdot L &= p \cdot D \cdot L \\
\sigma_{h} &= \dfrac{p \cdot D}{2 \cdot t}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Numerical Verification Example**

For a diesel engine with $p = 80$ bar = 8 MPa, $D = 100$ mm, $t = 5$ mm:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{h} &= \dfrac{8 \cdot 100}{2 \cdot 5} \\
&= \dfrac{800}{10} \\
&= 80 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since cast iron has a tensile strength of $\approx 200$–$300$ MPa, the liner operates with a **factor of safety of about 2.5–4**, which is acceptable.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Interference Fit Pressure (Lamé's Equations)

For a **dry liner** press-fitted into the block, the radial interference $\delta$ generates a contact pressure $P_{fit}$ at the liner-block interface.

**Step 1: Radial Displacement in Thick Cylinder (Lamé Equation)**

For a thick-walled cylinder under internal pressure $P_{i}$ and external pressure $P_{o}$, the radial displacement at any radius $r$ is given by Lamé's equation:

$$
u_{r} = \dfrac{1}{E} \left[ (1 - \nu) \cdot \dfrac{P_{i} \cdot r_{i}^{2} - P_{o} \cdot r_{o}^{2}}{r_{o}^{2} - r_{i}^{2}} \cdot r + (1 + \nu) \cdot \dfrac{(P_{i} - P_{o}) \cdot r_{i}^{2} \cdot r_{o}^{2}}{(r_{o}^{2} - r_{i}^{2}) \cdot r} \right]
$$

**Step 2: Displacement at Outer Surface of Liner**

For the liner (internal pressure = 0, external pressure = $-P_{fit}$ — compression):

$$
u_{r,outer}^{liner} = \dfrac{-P_{fit} \cdot r_{o}}{E_{liner}} \cdot \left[ \dfrac{r_{o}^{2} + r_{i}^{2}}{r_{o}^{2} - r_{i}^{2}} - \nu_{liner} \right]
$$

**Step 3: Displacement at Inner Surface of Block**

For the block (internal pressure = $-P_{fit}$, external pressure = 0, treated as a semi-infinite body):

$$
u_{r,inner}^{block} = \dfrac{P_{fit} \cdot r_{o}}{E_{block}} \cdot \left[ 1 - \nu_{block} \right] \quad \text{(for thick block)}
$$

**Step 4: Compatibility Condition**

The total radial interference $\delta$ equals the sum of these two displacements (with appropriate sign):

$$
\delta = r_{o} \cdot P_{fit} \cdot \left[ \dfrac{1}{E_{liner}} \left( \dfrac{r_{o}^{2} + r_{i}^{2}}{r_{o}^{2} - r_{i}^{2}} + \nu_{liner} \right) + \dfrac{1 - \nu_{block}}{E_{block}} \right]
$$

**Step 5: Simplified Form for Thin Liners in Stiff Block**

If the liner is thin and the block is much stiffer ($E_{block} \gg E_{liner}$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{fit} &\approx \dfrac{\delta \cdot E_{liner}}{r_{o} \cdot \left[ \dfrac{r_{o}^{2} + r_{i}^{2}}{r_{o}^{2} - r_{i}^{2}} + \nu_{liner} \right]} \\
P_{fit} &\approx \dfrac{\delta \cdot E_{liner}}{2 \cdot r_{o}} \quad \text{(for very thin liners)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Numerical Example**

Let $\delta = 0.05$ mm, $r_{i} = 50$ mm, $r_{o} = 52.5$ mm, $E_{liner} = 100$ GPa, $\nu = 0.25$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{fit} &= \dfrac{0.05 \cdot 100{,}000}{52.5 \cdot \left[ \dfrac{52.5^{2} + 50^{2}}{52.5^{2} - 50^{2}} + 0.25 \right]} \\
&= \dfrac{5000}{52.5 \cdot \left[ \dfrac{2756.25 + 2500}{2756.25 - 2500} + 0.25 \right]} \\
&= \dfrac{5000}{52.5 \cdot \left[ \dfrac{5256.25}{256.25} + 0.25 \right]} \\
&= \dfrac{5000}{52.5 \cdot (20.51 + 0.25)} \\
&= \dfrac{5000}{52.5 \cdot 20.76} \\
&= \dfrac{5000}{1089.9} \\
&\approx 4.59 \text{ MPa (interference contact pressure)}
\end{aligned}
$$

This pressure generates sufficient friction to hold the dry liner in place against combustion forces.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Liner Types

| Parameter | Dry Liner | Wet Liner | Finned Liner |
|-----------|-----------|-----------|--------------|
| **Coolant Contact** | None | Direct | None (air-cooled) |
| **Wall Thickness** | 1.5–3 mm | 3–6 mm | 2–4 mm |
| **Heat Dissipation** | Moderate (via block) | Excellent (direct) | Good (via fins + air) |
| **Replacement** | Difficult (requires rebore) | Easy (pull out) | Easy |
| **Sealing** | Interference fit | O-rings + flange | Press fit |
| **Cavitation Risk** | None | High (lower outer surface) | None |
| **Typical Application** | Petrol cars | Heavy-duty diesel | Air-cooled engines |
| **Manufacturing Cost** | Low | Moderate | Low |
| **Weight** | Lightest | Heaviest | Light |

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Differentiate between a dry liner and a wet liner in an IC engine.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Dry Liner | Wet Liner |
|---------|-----------|-----------|
| Coolant Contact | Not in direct contact with coolant | In direct contact with coolant |
| Wall Thickness | Thin (1.5–3 mm) | Thick (3–6 mm) |
| Installation | Press-fitted (interference fit) | Held by flange + sealed with O-rings |

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**List any three functions of a cylinder liner in an internal combustion engine.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Provides a hard, wear-resistant working surface** for the piston rings to slide against, protecting the engine block from wear.
2. **Forms a sealed combustion chamber** by precisely defining the bore diameter and sealing against combustion gases.
3. **Facilitates heat transfer** from the combustion gases to the cooling medium (especially in wet liners), preventing overheating of the piston and rings.

*(3 marks — one mark for each correctly stated function)*

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Module 1, Liner Types

**Q.A. (a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **construction and working of a wet cylinder liner** used in heavy-duty diesel engines. Discuss the materials used and the sealing arrangement. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Construction:**
A wet cylinder liner is a hollow cylindrical sleeve with a top flange. The flange rests on the top deck of the engine block and forms a seal with the cylinder head gasket. The body of the liner is surrounded by engine coolant (water + ethylene glycol mixture). Rubber O-rings or copper gaskets are placed in grooves at the top and bottom of the liner to prevent coolant leakage.

**Working:**
During engine operation, hot combustion gases heat the inner surface of the liner to temperatures of 250–300°C. This heat flows radially outward through the liner wall (thickness 3–6 mm) and is absorbed directly by the surrounding coolant. Coolant enters from the bottom of the jacket, absorbs heat as it rises, and exits from the top — naturally driven by thermosiphon or forced by the water pump.

**Materials Used:**
- **Pearlitic alloy cast iron** (Fe + 3.0–3.5% C + Cr, Mo, Ni) — provides high wear resistance and strength.
- Manufactured by **centrifugal casting** for uniform grain structure.
- Outer surface often coated or hardened to resist **cavitation pitting**.

**Sealing Arrangement:**
- **Top Seal:** Top flange compressed between block and head gasket.
- **Bottom Seals:** Two or three **rubber O-rings** seated in grooves to seal against coolant leakage into the crankcase and to prevent combustion gases from escaping downward.
- **Anti-cavitation flange** at the lower outer surface reduces coolant bubble collapse damage.

*(7 marks: 2 for sketch/description, 2 for materials, 2 for sealing, 1 for working)*

---

**Q.A. (b)** Explain the **causes of cylinder liner wear** in a diesel engine. Discuss the methods to **measure liner wear** and **state the limits** beyond which a liner must be replaced. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Causes of Liner Wear:**

1. **Abrasive Wear** — Dust and carbon particles entering through the air intake act like sandpaper on the bore surface.
2. **Adhesive Wear** — Direct metal-to-metal contact when the oil film breaks down under high load and temperature (boundary lubrication).
3. **Corrosive Wear** — Combustion products (especially SO₂ and SO₃ from sulfur in diesel fuel) combine with moisture to form **sulfuric acid**, which corrodes the bore.
4. **Cavitation Pitting** (Wet Liners Only) — High-frequency oscillation of the liner in coolant creates vapor bubbles that collapse violently, pitting the outer surface and eventually cracking through.
5. **Thermal Fatigue** — Repeated heating-cooling cycles cause micro-cracks on the bore surface.

**Measurement of Liner Wear:**
- **Bore Gauge Measurement** — A dial bore gauge (DBG) is used at multiple heights (top, mid, bottom of stroke) and at two perpendicular axes (X and Y).
- **Top Dead Centre (TDC) and Bottom Dead Centre (BDC)** measurements reveal **taper** (ovality).
- Wear is reported as the **difference between maximum and minimum bore diameter** along the stroke.

**Replacement Limits:**
- **Taper limit:** Typically **0.05 mm to 0.10 mm** per cylinder.
- **Ovality limit:** Typically **0.03 mm to 0.05 mm**.
- When the bore diameter exceeds the **service limit** (e.g., +0.50 mm over standard bore), a new liner or rebore is mandatory.

*(7 marks: 3 for causes with examples, 2 for measurement, 2 for limits)*

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Q.B. (a)** Compare the **materials used for cylinder liners**. Explain why **centrifugally cast pearlitic alloy cast iron** is the industry-preferred material for modern heavy-duty diesel engine liners. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Comparison of Materials:**

| Material | Wear Resistance | Cost | Typical Use |
|----------|-----------------|------|-------------|
| Plain Cast Iron | Moderate | Low | Old engines |
| Alloy Cast Iron | High | Moderate | Modern diesel |
| Centrifugally Cast Iron | Very High | Moderate | Heavy-duty |
| Chrome Plated | Excellent | High | Racing/performance |
| Nikasil | Excellent | Very High | Premium European |

**Why Centrifugally Cast Pearlitic Alloy Cast Iron?**

1. **Dense Grain Structure** — Centrifugal casting forces molten iron against the mold walls under high G-forces, eliminating porosity and producing a fine, dense, directional grain structure aligned with the direction of stress. This dramatically increases fatigue strength.

2. **Pearlitic Matrix** — The pearlitic microstructure (alternating ferrite and cementite lamellae) provides an excellent combination of **hardness (200–250 BHN)** and **toughness**, ideal for sliding contact with piston rings.

3. **Alloying Additions (Cr, Mo, Ni)** —
   - **Chromium (0.5–1.5%)** — Forms hard chromium carbides, increasing wear resistance.
   - **Molybdenum (0.3–0.8%)** — Improves high-temperature strength and reduces temper brittleness.
   - **Nickel (0.5–1.5%)** — Enhances toughness and corrosion resistance.

4. **Cost-Effectiveness** — Compared to Nikasil or ceramic coatings, centrifugally cast iron is **5 to 10 times cheaper** while delivering acceptable performance for most commercial applications.

5. **Manufacturability** — Easy to machine, bore, and hone to tight tolerances.

*(7 marks: 2 for comparison table, 5 for justification)*

---

**Q.B. (b)** A diesel engine has a cylinder bore diameter of **100 mm**, a liner wall thickness of **5 mm**, and operates at a peak combustion pressure of **80 bar**. Calculate the **hoop stress** and **longitudinal stress** in the liner wall. Also, determine the **factor of safety** if the liner material has an ultimate tensile strength of **250 MPa**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given Data:**
- Bore diameter $D = 100$ mm
- Wall thickness $t = 5$ mm
- Peak combustion pressure $p = 80$ bar = $8$ MPa
- Ultimate tensile strength $\sigma_{u} = 250$ MPa

**Step 1: Hoop (Circumferential) Stress**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{h} &= \dfrac{p \cdot D}{2 \cdot t} \\
\sigma_{h} &= \dfrac{8 \cdot 100}{2 \cdot 5} \\
\sigma_{h} &= \dfrac{800}{10} \\
\sigma_{h} &= 80 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Longitudinal (Axial) Stress**

For a closed-ended cylinder (engine combustion chamber is closed at TDC):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{L} &= \dfrac{p \cdot D}{4 \cdot t} \\
\sigma_{L} &= \dfrac{8 \cdot 100}{4 \cdot 5} \\
\sigma_{L} &= \dfrac{800}{20} \\
\sigma_{L} &= 40 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Maximum Principal Stress**

The hoop stress is the maximum, so:

$$
\sigma_{max} = \sigma_{h} = 80 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 4: Factor of Safety**

$$
\begin{aligned}
FOS &= \dfrac{\sigma_{u}}{\sigma_{max}} \\
FOS &= \dfrac{250}{80} \\
FOS &= 3.125
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:**
The liner operates at a factor of safety of **3.125**, which is well within the safe design range (typically 2.5–4 for cast iron components under static and low-cycle fatigue loading). The design is **safe**.

*(7 marks: 2 for hoop, 2 for longitudinal, 1 for max stress identification, 2 for FOS calculation)*

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- **Cylinder Liner:** A replaceable cylindrical sleeve forming the piston bore in IC engines.
- **Dry Liner:** No coolant contact, press-fitted, thin wall, used in petrol engines.
- **Wet Liner:** Direct coolant contact, O-ring sealed, thick wall, used in heavy diesel.
- **Finned Liner:** For air-cooled engines; cooling fins on outer surface.
- **Plated Liner:** Chrome or Nikasil coated for high wear resistance.
- **Preferred Material:** Centrifugally cast pearlitic alloy cast iron (Cr, Mo, Ni added).
- **Hoop Stress Formula:** $\sigma_{h} = \dfrac{p \cdot D}{2 \cdot t}$ — *memorize this; KTU favorite*.
- **Longitudinal Stress:** $\sigma_{L} = \dfrac{p \cdot D}{4 \cdot t}$.
- **Interference Fit:** Dry liners rely on press fit (typically 30–80 MPa contact pressure).
- **Cavitation Pitting:** A wet liner failure caused by coolant bubble collapse on the outer surface.
- **Common Wears:** Abrasive, adhesive, corrosive, cavitation, and thermal fatigue.
- **Wear Measurement:** Dial bore gauge at TDC, mid-stroke, BDC; along X and Y axes.
- **Taper/Ovality Limits:** 0.05–0.10 mm taper and 0.03–0.05 mm ovality for replacement.
- **Top Flange Function:** Provides upward stop and seals against head gasket.
- **Liner Replacement Advantage:** Cheaper than replacing the entire engine block.
- **Standard Bore Range:** 65 mm (small) to 150 mm (large commercial diesel).
- **Surface Hardness:** 180–250 BHN typical for centrifugally cast liners.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Exam Tip:** Always draw a **labeled diagram** when explaining dry/wet liner construction — it is worth 2 free marks in a 7-mark question. Also, remember the difference between **taper** (top-to-bottom wear) and **ovality** (X vs Y axis wear) — these terms appear in almost every KTU past paper.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
