### QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q. A (a)** Explain with a neat sketch the constructional features of a connecting rod. List at least **five (5) parts** and state their functions. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

The connecting rod consists of the following major parts:

1. **Small End (Eye / Gudgeon Pin End):** Connects to the piston via the gudgeon pin. It is fitted with a **bushing** (usually bronze) to allow relative motion.
2. **Shank / Web (I-Section):** The main body of the rod, designed with an I-section for optimum strength-to-weight ratio. It transmits the load from the small end to the big end.
3. **Big End (Crank Pin End):** Connects to the crank pin of the crankshaft. It is a **split-type** construction with a cap and two cap bolts for ease of assembly.
4. **Big End Cap:** The removable upper (or lower) half of the big end. It contains the bearing shell and is held by two high-strength bolts.
5. **Bearing Shells (Big End):** Thin half-shells (typically steel-backed with a lining of babbit, copper-lead, or aluminum alloy) that provide the bearing surface at the big end.
6. **Cap Bolts (Big End Bolts):** Two high-tensile steel bolts (often drilled for oil passage) that clamp the big end cap to the big end body.
7. **Lubricating Oil Hole:** A drilled passage through the shank from the big end to the small end, enabling oil flow for lubrication of the gudgeon pin.

**Function:** The connecting rod must (i) transmit compressive load during the power stroke, (ii) resist buckling under compression, (iii) withstand tensile inertia loads during other strokes, and (iv) resist bending due to its angularity with the cylinder axis.

---

**Q. A (b)** A connecting rod is to be designed for the following data: **[7 Marks]**
- Cylinder bore = 75 mm
- Stroke = 100 mm
- Length of connecting rod = 250 mm
- Max gas pressure = 2.5 N/mm²
- Speed = 2000 rpm
- Mass of reciprocating parts = 1.0 kg
- Factor of safety = 6
- Material: Forged steel with $\sigma_c = 320$ MPa, $a = 1/7500$

**Find: (i) Maximum inertia force, (ii) Maximum gas force, (iii) Buckling load on the rod, and (iv) Cross-sectional area of the I-section shank assuming standard proportions.**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Maximum Inertia Force (at TDC, $\theta = 0$):**

$$\omega = \frac{2 \pi \times 2000}{60} = 209.44 \text{ rad/s}, \quad r = 50 \text{ mm} = 0.05 \text{ m}$$

$$F_i = m \cdot r \cdot \omega^2 \left( 1 + \frac{r}{L} \right) = 1.0 \times 0.05 \times (209.44)^2 \times \left( 1 + \frac{50}{250} \right)$$

$$F_i = 1.0 \times 0.05 \times 43{,}865 \times 1.2 = 2631.9 \text{ N}$$

**(ii) Maximum Gas Force:**

$$F_g = 2.5 \times \frac{\pi}{4} \times (75)^2 = 2.5 \times 4417.86 = 11{,}044.7 \text{ N}$$

**(iii) Maximum Compressive Load and Buckling Load:**

$$F_c = F_g - F_i = 11{,}044.7 - 2631.9 = 8412.8 \text{ N}$$

$$P_R = F_c \times n = 8412.8 \times 6 = 50{,}476.8 \text{ N}$$

**(iv) Cross-Sectional Area:**

Using standard I-section proportions: $A = 5.75 d_1^2$ and $k = 1.897 d_1$:

$$P_R = \frac{\sigma_c \cdot A}{1 + a (L/k)^2}$$

$$50{,}476.8 = \frac{320 \times 5.75 d_1^2}{1 + \frac{1}{7500} \left( \frac{250}{1.897 d_1} \right)^2}$$

Solving (similar to the worked example in Section 3.2), we obtain $d_1 \approx 24$ mm.

$$\boxed{A = 5.75 \times (24)^2 = 3312 \text{ mm}^2}$$

**Verification of Direct Compressive Stress:**

$$\sigma_d = \frac{8412.8}{3312} = 2.54 \text{ N/mm}^2 \ll 320 \text{ MPa} \quad \text{[SAFE]}$$

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**Q. B (a)** Discuss the various **types of connecting rods** based on (i) cross-section and (ii) end construction. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Based on Cross-Section:**

1. **I-Section (Most Common):** Provides high moment of inertia with minimum material; best for resisting bending and buckling.
2. **Rectangular Section:** Used in older, low-cost engines; inferior strength-to-weight ratio.
3. **Circular Section:** Used in small 2-stroke engines; uniform in all bending planes.
4. **Tubular Section:** Used in high-performance aircraft/racing engines; high specific strength.

**(ii) Based on End Construction:**

1. **Plain (Solid Eye) Type:** Small end is a closed, forged eye. Big end may be split or solid.
2. **Marine Type:** Both ends are split (small end and big end are both clamped) to facilitate assembly of very large pistons/connecting rods.
3. **Master-Slave Type:** One master rod connects directly to the crank pin, while slave rods are connected via knuckle pins for multi-cylinder arrangements.
4. **Articulated (Crosshead) Type:** Used in very large engines; the connecting rod is connected to a crosshead, which separates the lateral thrust from the piston.

---

**Q. B (b)** Explain the various **causes of failure of a connecting rod**. **[7 Marks]`

**Model Solution:**

1. **Buckling Failure:** The rod acts as a slender column and may fail by buckling when subjected to high compressive gas loads, especially during the power stroke. Designed against using the **Rankine–Gordon formula** with a high factor of safety.

2. **Tensile Failure:** The rod may fail in tension due to the **inertia force of reciprocating parts** at high RPM, particularly near BDC. The shank cross-section must have adequate tensile strength.

3. **Bending Failure:** Due to the **angularity** of the rod with the cylinder axis, a bending moment is induced (maximum at the small end). This bending moment is most significant in long-stroke engines.

4. **Fatigue Failure:** The cyclic nature of the loading (compressive → tensile → compressive) leads to **fatigue cracking**, typically at the fillet radii of the small end or big end.

5. **Big End Bolt Failure:** Improper tightening or fatigue of the big end cap bolts can cause catastrophic failure of the big end.

6. **Bearing Failure (Wear):** Insufficient lubrication or excessive bearing pressure leads to wear of the small end bushing or big end bearing shells.

7. **Bearing Seizure:** Loss of oil film at high temperatures causes the bearing shells to seize, leading to localized melting/welding of the contact surfaces.

8. **Crankshaft Journal Scoring:** Debris from a worn bushing or improper assembly can score the crank pin journal.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Examinations:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $\frac{r}{L}\cos 2\theta$ term** in the inertia force — KTU examiners allocate 1–2 marks specifically for using the *complete* expression, not the approximate one.
> 2. **Wrong sign convention** in inertia force — Students often forget that the inertia force acts **opposite to the acceleration** of the piston. Always state $F_i = -m a$ and then use the magnitude.
> 3. **Not using the maximum (TDC) inertia force for compressive design** — the maximum compressive load on the rod is $(F_g - F_i)$ at TDC, and the maximum tensile load is $F_i$ at BDC. Both must be checked.
> 4. **Omitting the Rankine constant $a$** — KTU 2024 explicitly requires stating the assumed value of $a$ (e.g., $a = 1/7500$ for mild steel).
> 5. **Using Euler's formula directly** — Euler's formula is only valid for *very slender* columns (slenderness ratio > 80–100). For connecting rods, **Rankine–Gordon is the KTU-prescribed method**.
> 6. **Forgetting to convert crank radius from mm to m** when computing $\omega^2 r$ — a dimensional slip-up leads to a 1000× error in the inertia force.
> 7. **Confusing big-end and small-end bearing pressures** — small end typically has *higher* permissible pressure (15–25 N/mm²) than big end (10–20 N/mm²) because the small end sees oscillating load only.
> 8. **Not showing the cross-section diagram** for the I-section dimensions in design problems — KTU awards 1–2 marks for a properly labeled I-section sketch.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quick-Fire Revision — Connecting Rod**

- ⭐ **Primary function:** Convert reciprocating motion of piston → rotary motion of crankshaft.
- ⭐ **Standard cross-section:** I-section (best strength-to-weight).
- ⭐ **Most common material:** Forged steel (mild steel: $\sigma_c = 320$ MPa, $a = 1/7500$).
- ⭐ **Typical Factor of Safety:** 5 to 8 for I.C. engine connecting rods.
- ⭐ **Small End:** Connected to **gudgeon pin** via **bushing**; usually a **closed eye** design.
- ⭐ **Big End:** Connected to **crank pin**; **split-type** with **two cap bolts**.
- ⭐ **Bearing Pressure Limits:** Small end = 15–25 N/mm²; Big end = 10–20 N/mm².
- ⭐ **Critical Design Formula (KTU standard):** **Rankine–Gordon** for buckling:
  $$P_R = \frac{\sigma_c \cdot A}{1 + a (L_e / k)^2}$$
- ⭐ **Inertia Force (Full expression):**
  $$F_i = m r \omega^2 \left( \cos\theta + \frac{r}{L}\cos 2\theta \right)$$
- ⭐ **Maximum Compressive Load:** Occurs at **TDC** of power stroke → $F_c = F_g - F_i$.
- ⭐ **Maximum Tensile Load:** Occurs near **BDC** of exhaust stroke → $F_t = F_i$.
- ⭐ **Standard I-Section Proportions (KTU convention):**
  - Flange width: $b_f = 1.5 d_1$
  - Web thickness: $t_w = d_1$
  - Total depth: $h = 5 d_1$
  - Flange thickness: $t_f = 0.75 d_1$
- ⭐ **Area (in terms of $d_1$):** $A = 5.75 d_1^2$
- ⭐ **Radius of Gyration (in terms of $d_1$):** $k \approx 1.897 d_1$
- ⭐ **Types — Cross-section:** I, Rectangular, Circular, Tubular.
- ⭐ **Types — End Construction:** Plain, Marine, Master-Slave, Articulated (Crosshead).
- ⭐ **Major Failures:** Buckling, Tensile, Bending, Fatigue, Bolt failure, Bearing wear/seizure.
- ⭐ **Lubrication:** Oil hole drilled through shank from big end → small end for gudgeon pin lubrication.
- ⭐ **High-Performance Variants:** Forged aluminum alloy (motorcycle/F1), Titanium alloy (F1/aerospace).
- ⭐ **KTU Design Verification:** Always compute $\sigma_d = F_c / A$ and confirm it is well below $\sigma_c$ (safety check).
- ⭐ **Master Equation to Memorize:** Rankine–Gordon in its full form, with $a$, $\sigma_c$, $A$, $L_e$, $k$ all labeled.
- ⭐ **Common Examiner Trap:** Confusing "effective length" — for connecting rod, $L_e = L$ (full length) is the **standard KTU assumption** (pinned-pinned).
- ⭐ **Material Grades Often Used in KTU Problems:** Mild Steel, Cast Steel, Forged Steel (NOT cast iron for high-stress applications).

> **End of Section 5 — Connecting Rod Module 1 Notes**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Crank Shaft
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT205 - AUTOMOBILE POWER PLANT (AUTOMOBILE ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Crank Shaft

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **crank shaft** is the main rotating shaft of an internal combustion engine that converts the reciprocating (linear) motion of the pistons into rotational motion, which is ultimately delivered to the flywheel and transmission. It carries the crankpins on which the connecting rods are mounted, supported by main bearings in the engine block, and counterweighted to balance the dynamic inertia forces generated by the rotating and reciprocating masses.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition to Memorize:** The crankshaft is a single-piece (or assembled) shaft incorporating **main journals**, **crankpins (crank webs)**, **crank cheeks (webs)**, and **counterweights** — designed to transmit the gas forces, inertia forces, and torque from the pistons to the flywheel while maintaining concentricity of the main axis.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuitive Understanding)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Heart-Spine" of the Engine:** If the engine block is the "rib-cage" of an automobile, then the **crankshaft is the spine**, and the **pistons are the lungs**. The pistons pump up-and-down (breathing in air-fuel and expelling exhaust), and the crankshaft converts that vertical pump-action into the rotary motion that turns the wheels — exactly like how the human spine converts the lateral motion of legs into rotational hip movement for walking. Without the spine, the legs cannot propel; without the crankshaft, the pistons cannot drive the wheels.

A simpler analogy: imagine pushing a playground **see-saw** up and down. If you strap a stick (connecting rod) to a piston and the other end of the stick to an offset pin (crankpin) on a wheel, every push down rotates the wheel a little. That wheel *is* a single-cylinder crankshaft!

## 1.3 Core Functions of a Crankshaft (Exam-Relevant)

The crankshaft performs **five (5) primary engineering functions**:

1. **Force Reception** — Receives the gas pressure force $F_g$ and inertia force $F_i$ from each piston through the connecting rod.
2. **Motion Conversion** — Transforms reciprocating piston motion (stroke $L$) into continuous rotation through the crank radius $r$ (where $L = 2r$).
3. **Torque Transmission** — Delivers net torque to the flywheel, which is then transmitted via the clutch to the gearbox and ultimately the wheels.
4. **Energy Storage (Flywheel coupling)** — Smooths out the cyclic torque fluctuations, ensuring the engine does not stall between power strokes.
5. **Mass Balancing** — Counterweights on the crank webs reduce the primary and secondary inertia forces, minimizing engine vibrations.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Symbol | Typical Range (Automotive) | Unit |
|-----------|----------------|---------------------------|------|
| Stroke length | $L$ | 70 – 100 | mm |
| Crank radius | $r = L/2$ | 35 – 50 | mm |
| Main journal diameter | $d_m$ | 50 – 80 | mm |
| Crankpin diameter | $d_p$ | 40 – 70 | mm |
| Crank web (cheek) thickness | $t_w$ | 25 – 45 | mm |
| Number of main bearings | – | 2 – 7 (typical: 5) | – |
| Crankshaft material tensile strength | $S_{ut}$ | 550 – 900 | MPa |
| Fatigue endurance limit | $S_e$ | 280 – 450 | MPa |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Crankshaft — Component Breakdown

A typical multi-cylinder crankshaft consists of the following parts, each serving a distinct mechanical role:

### A. Main Journals
- The smooth cylindrical sections that rotate inside the **main bearings** of the engine block (crankcase).
- Their centerline defines the **main axis of rotation** (engine centerline).
- They support the entire weight of the crankshaft and resist the bending loads from the crankpins.

### B. Crankpins (Crankpins / Rod Journals)
- Offset from the main axis by a distance equal to the **crank radius $r$** (half the stroke).
- The big-end of the **connecting rod** is mounted on each crankpin.
- They carry the gas force and inertia force and convert it to torque.

### C. Crank Webs (Crank Cheeks)
- The flat, disc-like portions of metal connecting the main journal to the crankpin.
- They provide the **structural lever arm** through which tangential force generates torque $T = F_t \cdot r$.

### D. Counterweights
- Projections opposite the crankpin on the crank webs.
- They balance the **rotating mass** of the crankpin itself and partially the **reciprocating mass** of the piston and connecting rod (small end).

### E. Oil Galleries (Drillings)
- Internal oil passages that deliver pressurized lubricating oil from the main bearings to the crankpin bearings (big-end).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Favourite Question:** *"List the parts of a crankshaft and state the function of crank webs."* — The crank webs act as the **lever arm** that converts the tangential force component $F_t$ at the crankpin into torque $T = F_t \cdot r$.

## 2.2 Classification of Crankshafts (KTU High-Yield Topic)

### (a) Based on Number of Cylinders
| Type | Configuration | Application | Firing Interval |
|------|---------------|-------------|-----------------|
| Single-cylinder | 1 crankpin, 2 webs | Motorcycles, small gensets | – |
| Inline (I-4, I-6) | All crankpins on one axis | Most passenger cars | Even intervals |
| V-type (V6, V8) | Two banks of crankpins | Sports/luxury cars | – |
| Flat / Boxer | Crankpins on opposite sides | Porsche, Subaru | – |
| W-type | Three banks | Bugatti Veyron | – |

### (b) Based on Construction
- **Solid (One-piece / Forged) Crankshaft** — Most common; made by forging a single billet of steel.
- **Built-up Crankshaft** — Comprises separate main journals, crank webs, and crankpins bolted or shrink-fitted together (used in very large marine engines).

## 2.3 Materials Used for Crankshafts

| Material | Ultimate Tensile Strength ($S_{ut}$) | Application |
|----------|--------------------------------------|-------------|
| Medium-carbon steel (e.g., EN8, 45C8) | 550 – 650 MPa | Light commercial vehicles |
| Alloy steel (e.g., 40Cr, 42CrMo4) | 700 – 900 MPa | Passenger cars (most common) |
| Nodular / Spheroidal Graphite Iron (SGI) | 550 – 700 MPa | Modern high-speed engines |
| Forged steel with surface induction hardening | 800 – 1000 MPa | Performance / racing engines |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why forged steel?** Forging aligns the grain flow along the principal stress direction (web-to-journal), giving superior fatigue life. Cast crankshafts are cheaper but have ~30 % lower fatigue strength.

## 2.4 Force Analysis on the Crankshaft — The "Why" and "How"

The gas force $F_g$ acting on the piston is transmitted through the connecting rod to the crankpin at an angle $\theta$ (crank angle measured from TDC) and the connecting rod angle $\phi$. We **decompose** this force into two components at the crankpin:

### Tangential Component (causes rotation, generates torque):
$$F_t = F_g \cdot \sin(\theta + \phi) - F_i \cdot \sin\theta$$

(where $F_i = m_r \cdot r \cdot \omega^2 (\cos\theta + r\cos 2\theta / L)$ is the inertia force of reciprocating parts)

### Radial Component (causes bending, no torque):
$$F_r = F_g \cdot \cos(\theta + \phi) + F_i \cdot \cos\theta$$

### Resultant Bending Force on the Crankpin:
$$F_b = \sqrt{F_t^2 + F_r^2}$$

### Torque generated at the crankpin:
$$T = F_t \cdot r$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The radial component $F_r$ creates **bending stress** in the crank web (this is the dominant failure mode). The tangential component $F_t$ creates **torsional stress** in the main journal. Together, they produce a state of combined bending + torsion that the crankshaft must survive for billions of cycles (fatigue life).

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains the **most important equations** for crankshaft design. Memorize these for the KTU End Semester Examination (ESE).

| # | Quantity | Formula | Variables / Units | Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------------------|-------|
| 1 | Stroke length | $L = 2r$ | $L$ = stroke (m), $r$ = crank radius (m) | Geometric relationship |
| 2 | Connecting rod ratio | $n = L / l$ | $l$ = connecting rod length | Typically $n \approx 3.5$ |
| 3 | Crank angle at TDC | $\theta = 0°$ | Reference position | Piston at topmost |
| 4 | Gas force on piston | $F_g = p_g \cdot A_p$ | $p_g$ = gas pressure (Pa), $A_p$ = piston area (m²) | From indicator diagram |
| 5 | Inertia force | $F_i = m_r \cdot r \cdot \omega^2 (\cos\theta + \frac{r}{L}\cos 2\theta)$ | $m_r$ = reciprocating mass (kg), $\omega$ = rad/s | $F_i$ positive downward |
| 6 | Tangential force | $F_t = F_g \sin(\theta+\phi) - F_i \sin\theta$ | N | Generates torque |
| 7 | Radial force | $F_r = F_g \cos(\theta+\phi) + F_i \cos\theta$ | N | Generates bending |
| 8 | Crankpin torque | $T = F_t \cdot r$ | N·m | |
| 9 | Bending moment at crankpin | $M_b = F_r \cdot r$ | N·m | |
| 10 | Bending stress (circular section) | $\sigma_b = \dfrac{32 \, M_b}{\pi \, d_p^3}$ | Pa | $d_p$ = crankpin diameter |
| 11 | Torsional shear stress | $\tau = \dfrac{16 \, T}{\pi \, d_m^3}$ | Pa | $d_m$ = main journal diameter |
| 12 | Equivalent torque (Guest's theory, max shear) | $T_e = \sqrt{M_b^2 + T^2}$ | N·m | Most common in KTU |
| 13 | Equivalent bending moment (Rankine / normal stress) | $M_e = \dfrac{M_b + \sqrt{M_b^2 + T^2}}{2}$ | N·m | Used in shaft design |
| 14 | ASME Equivalent torque | $T_{eq} = \sqrt{M_b^2 + T^2}$ | N·m | Same as Guest |
| 15 | Diameter from ASME code | $d^3 = \dfrac{16}{\pi \, \tau_{allow}} \cdot \sqrt{M_b^2 + T^2}$ | m | For solid round shaft |
| 16 | Bearing pressure | $p_b = \dfrac{F_r}{l_b \cdot d_p}$ | Pa | $l_b$ = bearing length |
| 17 | Critical speed (Rayleigh) | $\omega_c = \sqrt{\dfrac{g}{\delta}}$ | rad/s | $\delta$ = static deflection |
| 18 | Fillet stress concentration | $K_t = 1 + 2\sqrt{\dfrac{t}{r_f}}$ | dimensionless | $t$ = web thickness, $r_f$ = fillet radius |
| 19 | Endurance limit (rotating bending) | $S_e' = 0.5 \, S_{ut}$ (for $S_{ut} < 1400$ MPa) | Pa | Marin-modified for KTU |
| 20 | Factor of safety (fatigue) | $n_f = \dfrac{S_e \cdot K_c}{\sigma_b \cdot K_f}$ | – | $K_c, K_f$ = load, fatigue factors |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** In the formula for **equivalent torque / bending moment**, students often confuse Guest's theory (max shear stress) with Rankine's theory (max normal stress). For ductile materials like crankshaft steel, KTU papers **prefer Guest's theory**:
> $$T_{eq} = \sqrt{M_b^2 + T^2}$$

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The crankshaft is **the single most fatigue-loaded component in the engine**. It is engineered against two simultaneous failure modes:
1. **Bending fatigue** at the fillet radius between the crankpin and the crank web.
2. **Torsional fatigue** due to the cyclic gas torque pulses from each cylinder.

Modern high-speed engines (8,000+ RPM) operate the crankshaft at stress amplitudes approaching the endurance limit, making it a **fatigue-critical** part. Hence, design emphasizes:
- **Fillet rolling** (cold-working the fillet) to introduce compressive residual stresses.
- **Shot peening** of the webs.
- **Induction hardening** of the journals.
- **Nitriding** for high-end alloy-steel crankshafts.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following derivations are essential for the **14-mark KTU ESE questions** on crankshaft design. Every algebraic step is shown explicitly — no shortcuts.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Inertia Force on a Reciprocating Mass

Let a piston of mass $m_r$ move with simple harmonic motion approximation. The displacement of the piston from TDC is:

$$x = r (1 - \cos\theta) + \frac{r^2}{L}(1 - \cos 2\theta)$$

The acceleration is the second derivative of $x$ with respect to time, using $\dot\theta = \omega$ (constant for steady engine speed).

**Step 1 — Differentiate displacement once (velocity):**

$$\dot{x} = r \omega \sin\theta + \frac{r^2 \omega}{L} \sin 2\theta$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate velocity (acceleration):**

$$\ddot{x} = r \omega^2 \cos\theta + \frac{r^2 \omega^2}{L} \cos 2\theta$$

**Step 3 — Factor out $r \omega^2$:**

$$\ddot{x} = r \omega^2 \left( \cos\theta + \frac{r}{L}\cos 2\theta \right)$$

**Step 4 — Apply Newton's second law ($F_i = m_r \cdot \ddot{x}$):**

$$\boxed{F_i = m_r \cdot r \cdot \omega^2 \left( \cos\theta + \frac{r}{L}\cos 2\theta \right)}$$

**Step 5 — Identify the two components:**
- **Primary inertia force:** $F_{i1} = m_r r \omega^2 \cos\theta$ — frequency = engine speed.
- **Secondary inertia force:** $F_{i2} = m_r r \omega^2 \cdot \frac{r}{L}\cos 2\theta$ — frequency = 2 × engine speed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The primary component is balanced by a suitable crank arrangement (e.g., 6-cylinder inline). The secondary component is much smaller (because $r/L \ll 1$) but still contributes to vibration at high RPM.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Tangential and Radial Force Components

The gas force $F_g$ acts axially on the piston. The connecting rod transmits this force to the crankpin at the **connecting rod angle** $\phi$ measured from the cylinder axis.

**Step 1 — Geometry of the slider-crank mechanism:**

By the sine rule on the triangle formed by the crank, connecting rod, and cylinder axis:

$$\frac{\sin\phi}{r} = \frac{\sin\theta}{L}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $\phi$ (small-angle approximation):**

$$\sin\phi = \frac{r}{L}\sin\theta \quad \Rightarrow \quad \phi \approx \frac{r}{L}\sin\theta$$

**Step 3 — Decompose the force $F_g$ along and perpendicular to the crank:**

The total force along the connecting rod is $F_g$ (assuming piston is frictionless, this is the rod axial force). At the crankpin, decompose $F_g$:

$$F_t = F_g \sin(\theta + \phi) \quad \text{(tangential, perpendicular to crank)}$$

$$F_r = F_g \cos(\theta + \phi) \quad \text{(radial, along crank arm)}$$

**Step 4 — Including the inertia force $F_i$:**

The inertia force acts downward on the piston (opposite to acceleration). The net force along the cylinder axis is $F_{net} = F_g - F_i$. This net force is what is transmitted through the rod. Substituting:

$$\boxed{F_t = (F_g - F_i)\sin(\theta + \phi) + F_i\sin\phi}$$

$$\boxed{F_r = (F_g - F_i)\cos(\theta + \phi) - F_i\cos\phi}$$

**Step 5 — Approximation for long connecting rod ($\phi \to 0$):**

For $L \gg r$, we set $\phi \approx 0$:

$$F_t = (F_g - F_i)\sin\theta$$

$$F_r = (F_g - F_i)\cos\theta$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Equivalent Torque Using Guest's Theory (Max Shear Stress)

The crankpin experiences **simultaneous** bending moment $M_b$ and torque $T$. We need an equivalent single quantity for sizing.

**Step 1 — State the max shear stress hypothesis (Guest / Tresca):**

Failure occurs when the maximum shear stress in a complex state equals the maximum shear stress in a simple tension test:

$$\tau_{max, complex} = \tau_{max, simple}$$

**Step 2 — Express max shear in a bending + torsion shaft:**

For a circular shaft under bending moment $M_b$ and torque $T$, the principal stresses at the surface are:

$$\sigma_1 = \frac{16 M_b}{\pi d^3}, \quad \sigma_2 = 0, \quad \sigma_3 = -\frac{16 T}{\pi d^3}$$

**Step 3 — Max shear stress (one-half of principal stress difference):**

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2} = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{16 M_b}{\pi d^3} + \frac{16 T}{\pi d^3}\right) = \frac{8}{\pi d^3}(M_b + T)$$

**Step 4 — Compare with shaft under pure torsion only:**

If the equivalent torque $T_{eq}$ produced the same max shear, then:

$$\tau_{max, torsion} = \frac{16 T_{eq}}{\pi d^3} \cdot \frac{1}{2} = \frac{8 T_{eq}}{\pi d^3}$$

**Step 5 — Equate the two expressions:**

$$\frac{8 T_{eq}}{\pi d^3} = \frac{8}{\pi d^3}(M_b + T)$$

**Step 6 — This gives $T_{eq} = M_b + T$ — but this is INCORRECT for combined stresses.** The correct Guest formulation uses:

$$\tau_{max} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{16 M_b}{2\pi d^3}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{16 T}{\pi d^3}\right)^2}$$

**Step 7 — Simplify (factor out $\frac{16}{\pi d^3}$):**

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{16}{\pi d^3}\sqrt{M_b^2 + T^2}$$

**Step 8 — For an equivalent pure-torsion shaft:**

$$\frac{16 T_{eq}}{\pi d^3} = \frac{16}{\pi d^3}\sqrt{M_b^2 + T^2}$$

**Step 9 — Final expression:**

$$\boxed{T_{eq} = \sqrt{M_b^2 + T^2}}$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Crankshaft Diameter from ASME Code

**Step 1 — Write the torsional shear stress for a solid round shaft:**

$$\tau = \frac{16 T_{eq}}{\pi d^3}$$

**Step 2 — Equate to allowable shear stress $\tau_{allow}$:**

$$\tau_{allow} = \frac{16 T_{eq}}{\pi d^3}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $d^3$:**

$$d^3 = \frac{16 T_{eq}}{\pi \tau_{allow}}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $T_{eq} = \sqrt{M_b^2 + T^2}$:**

$$\boxed{d = \left[ \frac{16}{\pi \tau_{allow}}\sqrt{M_b^2 + T^2} \right]^{1/3}}$$

**Step 5 — Account for stress concentration at the fillet:**

Modify the allowable stress by the fatigue stress concentration factor $K_f$:

$$d = \left[ \frac{16 K_f}{\pi \tau_{allow}}\sqrt{M_b^2 + T^2} \right]^{1/3}$$

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Bending vs Torsion Magnitudes

| Crank Angle $\theta$ | $F_t$ | $F_r$ | $M_b = F_r \cdot r$ | $T = F_t \cdot r$ | Dominant Stress |
|----------------------|-------|-------|---------------------|-------------------|-----------------|
| $0°$ (TDC, start of power) | 0 | Maximum | Maximum | 0 | Bending |
| $90°$ (mid-stroke) | Maximum | 0 | 0 | Maximum | Torsion |
| $180°$ (BDC) | 0 | Maximum | Maximum | 0 | Bending |
| $270°$ | $-$Maximum | 0 | 0 | $-$Maximum (reverse) | Torsion |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Conclusion:** Bending stress peaks at **TDC and BDC** (when the crank is "on the way" or "on the way back"), and torsional stress peaks at **90° and 270°**. Therefore, the crankshaft design must consider the **worst-case combination** of $M_b$ and $T$ over a complete cycle, which is what Guest's equivalent torque accounts for.

## 3.6 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Style)

> **Given:** A 4-stroke, 4-cylinder inline petrol engine. Bore $D = 80$ mm, stroke $L = 90$ mm, connecting rod length $l = 180$ mm, engine speed $N = 3000$ RPM, peak gas pressure $p_g = 3.5$ MPa, reciprocating mass per cylinder $m_r = 0.65$ kg.
> **Find:** (a) Crank radius and crankpin diameter. (b) Bending moment, torque, and equivalent torque at $\theta = 30°$ during the power stroke.

### Solution:

**Step 1 — Crank radius:**
$$r = L/2 = 90/2 = 45 \text{ mm} = 0.045 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Piston area:**
$$A_p = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.080)^2}{4} = 5.027 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Gas force on piston:**
$$F_g = p_g \cdot A_p = 3.5 \times 10^6 \times 5.027 \times 10^{-3} = 17{,}595 \text{ N} \approx 17.6 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 4 — Angular velocity:**
$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi (3000)}{60} = 100\pi = 314.16 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 5 — Inertia force at $\theta = 30°$:**

$$\cos 30° = 0.866, \quad \cos 60° = 0.5$$

$$F_i = 0.65 \times 0.045 \times (314.16)^2 \times (0.866 + (0.045/0.180)(0.5))$$

$$F_i = 0.65 \times 0.045 \times 98{,}696 \times (0.866 + 0.125)$$

$$F_i = 2{,}886.1 \times 0.991 = 2{,}860 \text{ N} \approx 2.86 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 6 — Connecting rod angle $\phi$ at $\theta = 30°$:**

$$\sin\phi = \frac{r}{L}\sin\theta = \frac{45}{180}\sin 30° = 0.25 \times 0.5 = 0.125$$

$$\phi = \sin^{-1}(0.125) = 7.18°$$

**Step 7 — Tangential force at $\theta = 30°$:**

$$F_t = (F_g - F_i)\sin(\theta + \phi) + F_i \sin\phi$$

$$F_t = (17{,}595 - 2{,}860)\sin(30° + 7.18°) + 2{,}860 \sin 7.18°$$

$$F_t = 14{,}735 \times \sin 37.18° + 2{,}860 \times 0.125$$

$$F_t = 14{,}735 \times 0.6044 + 357.5 = 8{,}907 + 357.5 = 9{,}264 \text{ N}$$

**Step 8 — Radial force at $\theta = 30°$:**

$$F_r = (F_g - F_i)\cos(\theta + \phi) - F_i \cos\phi$$

$$F_r = 14{,}735 \cos 37.18° - 2{,}860 \cos 7.18°$$

$$F_r = 14{,}735 \times 0.7967 - 2{,}860 \times 0.9922 = 11{,}737 - 2{,}838 = 8{,}899 \text{ N}$$

**Step 9 — Bending moment and torque:**

$$M_b = F_r \cdot r = 8{,}899 \times 0.045 = 400.5 \text{ N·m}$$

$$T = F_t \cdot r = 9{,}264 \times 0.045 = 416.9 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 10 — Equivalent torque (Guest's theory):**

$$T_{eq} = \sqrt{M_b^2 + T^2} = \sqrt{400.5^2 + 416.9^2} = \sqrt{160{,}400 + 173{,}806} = \sqrt{334{,}206}$$

$$\boxed{T_{eq} = 578.1 \text{ N·m}}$$

**Step 11 — Crankpin diameter (taking $\tau_{allow} = 60$ MPa):**

$$d^3 = \frac{16 \times 578.1}{\pi \times 60 \times 10^6} = \frac{9{,}250}{1.885 \times 10^8} = 4.91 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3$$

$$d = (4.91 \times 10^{-5})^{1/3} = 0.0366 \text{ m} \approx 36.6 \text{ mm}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Decision:** Round up to a standard size of **38 mm or 40 mm** to provide margin for stress concentration at the fillet and to account for fatigue.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Force Transmission in a Slider-Crank Mechanism

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates how the **gas force** travels from the piston, through the connecting rod, into the crankshaft, and finally to the flywheel.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Combustion Chamber] -->|Gas Pressure p_g| B[Piston - Reciprocating Mass m_r]
    B -->|F_g along axis| C[Connecting Rod - Small End and Big End]
    C -->|Force F at angle phi| D[Crankpin - Offset by r from main axis]
    D -->|Decomposes into F_t and F_r| E[Crank Web - Acts as Lever Arm]
    E -->|Tangential F_t generates| F[Torque T = F_t x r]
    E -->|Radial F_r creates| G[Bending Moment M_b = F_r x r]
    F --> H[Main Journal - Transmits Torsion]
    G --> I[Fillet Region - Highest Stress Location]
    H --> J[Flywheel - Stores Rotational Energy]
    I --> K[Counterweight - Balances Inertia]
    J --> L[Clutch and Transmission to Wheels]
    K --> D
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Crankshaft Component Architecture

This diagram maps the **physical layout** of all sub-components of a single-throw crankshaft.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Crankshaft_Assembly[CRANKSHAFT ASSEMBLY]
        A1[Main Journal Front - Cylinder 1 Side]
        A2[Crank Web 1 - With Fillet Radius r_f]
        A3[Crankpin - Offset 45 mm from Main Axis]
        A4[Crank Web 2 - Carries Counterweight]
        A5[Main Journal Middle - Between Throws]
        A6[Crank Web 3]
        A7[Crankpin 2]
        A8[Crank Web 4]
        A9[Main Journal Rear - Flywheel End]
    end

    P1[Big End of Connecting Rod 1] --> A3
    P2[Big End of Connecting Rod 2] --> A7
    CW1[Counterweight 1] --> A4
    CW2[Counterweight 2] --> A8
    A1 --> M1[Main Bearing 1 in Crankcase]
    A5 --> M2[Main Bearing 2]
    A9 --> M3[Main Bearing 3]
    A9 --> FW[Flywheel Mounting Flange]
    A1 --> SG[Signal Wheel for Crank Position Sensor]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Stress Analysis Flow for Crankshaft Design

This block diagram shows the standard **design workflow** that KTU expects students to follow for any crankshaft design problem.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Start: Given Engine Specs]) --> P1[Compute Piston Area A_p and Crank Radius r]
    P1 --> P2[Obtain Gas Pressure p_g theta from Indicator Diagram]
    P2 --> P3[Compute F_g = p_g x A_p at every crank angle]
    P3 --> P4[Compute Inertia Force F_i = m_r r omega squared term]
    P4 --> P5[Compute phi and Decompose into F_t and F_r]
    P5 --> P6[Compute M_b = F_r x r and T = F_t x r for full cycle]
    P6 --> P7[Find Max M_b and Max T and their Crank Angles]
    P7 --> P8{T_b and T_max at same angle?}
    P8 -->|No| P9[Use Guest T_eq = sqrt of M_b squared + T squared at worst case]
    P8 -->|Yes| P10[Use T_eq = M_b + T at that instant]
    P9 --> P11[Apply ASME Equation d cubed = 16 T_eq divided by pi tau_allow]
    P10 --> P11
    P11 --> P12[Apply K_f for Fillet Stress Concentration]
    P12 --> P13[Compare with Standard Crankpin Diameter]
    P13 --> End([Final Diameter Selected with FOS and 1.5 to 2.0 Safety Factor])
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Failure Modes of a Crankshaft

This block diagram maps the **principal failure modes** the design must protect against — a high-yield KTU topic.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Crankshaft Operating Conditions] --> B[Bending Stress at Fillet]
    A --> C[Torsional Stress in Main Journal]
    A --> D[Shear Stress in Crankpin]
    A --> E[Bearing Wear in Main Journal]
    A --> F[Torsional Vibration from Cyclic Torque]

    B --> B1[Fatigue Cracking at Fillet]
    B --> B2[Web Cracking]
    C --> C1[Journal Shear Failure]
    D --> D1[Crankpin Shear]
    E --> E1[Scuffing and Seizure]
    F --> F1[Resonance at Critical Speed]

    B1 --> Solution1[Solution: Fillet Rolling and Shot Peening]
    C1 --> Solution2[Solution: Increase Journal Diameter and Use Alloy Steel]
    D1 --> Solution3[Solution: Use Larger Crankpin and High Strength Material]
    E1 --> Solution4[Solution: Proper Lubrication and Bearing Material Selection]
    F1 --> Solution5[Solution: Torsional Damper and Avoid Resonance Range]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Note on Diagrams:** When asked to *"draw a neat sketch of a crankshaft and label the parts"*, students must include: **main journals, crankpins, crank webs/cheeks, counterweights, oil holes, and fillet radii**. Marks are awarded for both **correct labeling** and **proportional drawing**.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Q: Define a crankshaft. List the various parts of a crankshaft with their functions.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> A **crankshaft** is the main rotating member of an internal combustion engine that converts the reciprocating motion of the pistons into rotary motion and delivers torque to the flywheel.

The principal parts and their functions are:
1. **Main Journals** — Support the crankshaft in the main bearings of the crankcase.
2. **Crankpins (Rod Journals)** — Receive the force from the connecting rod big-end; offset from the main axis by the crank radius $r$.
3. **Crank Webs (Cheeks)** — Connect the main journal to the crankpin and act as the **lever arm** to convert tangential force into torque.
4. **Counterweights** — Balance the inertia forces of the rotating crankpin and partly the reciprocating piston.
5. **Oil Galleries** — Internal drillings supplying oil to the crankpin bearings.

*(Self-explanatory diagram to be drawn for full marks.)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**Q: Explain the significance of counterweights in a crankshaft. Why is a fillet radius provided at the junction of the crank web and crankpin?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Counterweights (2 marks):** Counterweights are masses provided on the crank web **opposite to the crankpin** to balance the rotating mass of the crankpin itself and a portion of the reciprocating mass of the piston. They reduce the **primary inertia force** and minimize engine vibrations, especially at higher RPM.

**Fillet radius (1 mark):** A small radius (called fillet) is provided at the sharp corner where the crank web meets the crankpin. This reduces the **stress concentration factor $K_t$** at that location, which would otherwise be the most fatigue-prone region of the crankshaft.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]

**Q: With the help of a free-body diagram, derive the expressions for tangential force and radial force on the crankpin. Hence, derive the equivalent torque using Guest's theory of failure.**

**Sub-part (a) — Derivation of tangential and radial forces [7 marks]:**

**Model Solution:**

Consider a slider-crank mechanism with crank radius $r$, connecting rod length $L$, crank angle $\theta$ (from TDC), and connecting rod angle $\phi$.

**Step 1 — Geometry:** The piston is acted on by the gas force $F_g$ downward (during power stroke) and the inertia force $F_i$ opposite to acceleration. The net force along the cylinder axis is $F_{net} = F_g - F_i$. This is transmitted by the connecting rod to the crankpin at angle $\phi$ from the cylinder axis.

**Step 2 — Free-body diagram of the crankpin:** At the crankpin, $F_{net}$ acts along the rod. We resolve it into two perpendicular components:
- **Tangential** (perpendicular to the crank, $F_t$) — produces torque.
- **Radial** (along the crank, $F_r$) — produces bending.

**Step 3 — Geometric relations:**

$$\sin\phi = \frac{r}{L}\sin\theta, \quad \phi = \sin^{-1}\left(\frac{r}{L}\sin\theta\right)$$

**Step 4 — Force decomposition:**

$$F_t = (F_g - F_i)\sin(\theta + \phi) + F_i \sin\phi$$

$$F_r = (F_g - F_i)\cos(\theta + \phi) - F_i \cos\phi$$

**Step 5 — Approximation:** For $L \gg r$, $\phi \to 0$:

$$\boxed{F_t = (F_g - F_i)\sin\theta, \quad F_r = (F_g - F_i)\cos\theta}$$

**Sub-part (b) — Equivalent torque using Guest's theory [7 marks]:**

**Step 1 — Crankpin experiences bending moment $M_b$ and torque $T$ simultaneously:**

$$M_b = F_r \cdot r, \quad T = F_t \cdot r$$

**Step 2 — For a circular section of diameter $d$, the stresses are:**

$$\sigma_x = \frac{32 M_b}{\pi d^3} \text{ (bending)}, \quad \tau_{xy} = \frac{16 T}{\pi d^3} \text{ (torsion)}$$

**Step 3 — Principal stresses** (at the surface of the shaft):

$$\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_x}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$$

**Step 4 — Maximum shear stress (Guest's criterion):**

$$\tau_{max} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute and simplify:**

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{16}{\pi d^3}\sqrt{M_b^2 + T^2}$$

**Step 6 — For a pure-torsion equivalent shaft:**

$$\tau_{max, equiv} = \frac{16 T_{eq}}{\pi d^3}$$

**Step 7 — Equating both expressions:**

$$\boxed{T_{eq} = \sqrt{M_b^2 + T^2}}$$

**Step 8 — Diameter from ASME code:**

$$d = \left[\frac{16 T_{eq}}{\pi \tau_{allow}}\right]^{1/3} = \left[\frac{16}{\pi \tau_{allow}}\sqrt{M_b^2 + T^2}\right]^{1/3}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Modified for 2024 Scheme]

**Q: A 4-stroke, 4-cylinder petrol engine has the following specifications: bore = 85 mm, stroke = 100 mm, connecting rod length = 250 mm, engine speed = 2500 RPM, peak gas pressure = 4.0 MPa, reciprocating mass per cylinder = 0.8 kg. Calculate (a) the tangential and radial forces on the crankpin at $\theta = 30°$ during the power stroke, and (b) the diameter of the crankpin using ASME code. Take allowable shear stress = 60 MPa.**

**Sub-part (a) — Tangential and radial force calculation [7 marks]:**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Crank radius and piston area:**

$$r = \frac{L}{2} = \frac{0.100}{2} = 0.050 \text{ m}$$

$$A_p = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.085)^2}{4} = 5.674 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Gas force:**

$$F_g = p_g \cdot A_p = 4.0 \times 10^6 \times 5.674 \times 10^{-3} = 22{,}698 \text{ N} \approx 22.7 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 3 — Angular velocity:**

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi (2500)}{60} = 261.8 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 4 — Inertia force at $\theta = 30°$:**

$$\cos 30° = 0.866, \quad \cos 60° = 0.5, \quad r/L = 50/250 = 0.20$$

$$F_i = 0.8 \times 0.050 \times (261.8)^2 \times (0.866 + 0.20 \times 0.5)$$

$$F_i = 0.04 \times 68{,}539 \times (0.866 + 0.10) = 2{,}742 \times 0.966 = 2{,}649 \text{ N} \approx 2.65 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 5 — Connecting rod angle:**

$$\sin\phi = (r/L)\sin\theta = 0.20 \times 0.5 = 0.10 \Rightarrow \phi = 5.74°$$

**Step 6 — Tangential force:**

$$F_t = (F_g - F_i)\sin(\theta + \phi) + F_i \sin\phi$$

$$F_t = (22{,}698 - 2{,}649)\sin(30° + 5.74°) + 2{,}649 \sin 5.74°$$

$$F_t = 20{,}049 \times \sin 35.74° + 2{,}649 \times 0.100$$

$$F_t = 20{,}049 \times 0.5843 + 264.9 = 11{,}714 + 265 = 11{,}979 \text{ N}$$

$$\boxed{F_t \approx 11.98 \text{ kN}}$$

**Step 7 — Radial force:**

$$F_r = (F_g - F_i)\cos(\theta + \phi) - F_i \cos\phi$$

$$F_r = 20{,}049 \times \cos 35.74° - 2{,}649 \times \cos 5.74°$$

$$F_r = 20{,}049 \times 0.8115 - 2{,}649 \times 0.9950 = 16{,}270 - 2{,}636 = 13{,}634 \text{ N}$$

$$\boxed{F_r \approx 13.63 \text{ kN}}$$

**Sub-part (b) — Crankpin diameter from ASME code [7 marks]:**

**Step 1 — Bending moment and torque:**

$$M_b = F_r \cdot r = 13{,}634 \times 0.050 = 681.7 \text{ N·m}$$

$$T = F_t \cdot r = 11{,}979 \times 0.050 = 598.95 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 2 — Equivalent torque (Guest's theory):**

$$T_{eq} = \sqrt{M_b^2 + T^2} = \sqrt{681.7^2 + 598.95^2}$$

$$T_{eq} = \sqrt{464{,}715 + 358{,}742} = \sqrt{823{,}457} = 907.4 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 3 — Apply ASME equation:**

$$d^3 = \frac{16 \cdot T_{eq}}{\pi \cdot \tau_{allow}} = \frac{16 \times 907.4}{\pi \times 60 \times 10^6}$$

$$d^3 = \frac{14{,}518}{1.885 \times 10^8} = 7.703 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $d$:**

$$d = (7.703 \times 10^{-5})^{1/3} = 0.0425 \text{ m} = 42.5 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5 — Add factor of safety and round up:**

Taking FOS = 1.5 to account for fatigue stress concentration at the fillet:

$$d_{design} = 1.5^{1/3} \times 42.5 = 1.145 \times 42.5 \approx 48.7 \text{ mm}$$

$$\boxed{d \approx 50 \text{ mm (standard size)}}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where students LOSE marks in crankshaft questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the inertia force term** — KTU paper problems often state the engine speed and reciprocating mass, expecting you to compute $F_i$ and **subtract** it from $F_g$. Marks are lost if you only use $F_g$.
>
> 2. **Confusing $L$ (stroke) with $L$ (rod length)** — In the formula $\frac{r}{L}\cos 2\theta$, $L$ is the **connecting rod length**, not the stroke. Read the problem carefully.
>
> 3. **Using Rankine instead of Guest** — For ductile crankshaft steel, KTU expects **Guest's theory** ($T_{eq} = \sqrt{M_b^2 + T^2}$). Writing $M_e = \frac{1}{2}(M_b + \sqrt{M_b^2 + T^2})$ for a crankshaft is **wrong** and loses 2 marks.
>
> 4. **Forgetting to add the stress concentration factor $K_f$** — At the fillet, $K_f = 1.5$ to $2.5$. Multiplying $T_{eq}$ by $K_f$ in the ASME equation adds ~20 % to the diameter. Many students miss this entirely.
>
> 5. **Unit inconsistency** — Mixing mm with m, or N with kN. Always convert to SI base units **before** substituting.
>
> 6. **No free-body diagram** — A 14-mark derivation question without a labelled FBD of the crankpin loses 1 to 2 marks immediately.
>
> 7. **Wrong sign convention for inertia force** — $F_i$ is **opposite to acceleration**. During the upward stroke (TDC → BDC), acceleration is downward, so $F_i$ on the piston is **upward**, hence subtracted from $F_g$.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Crankshaft (Module 1, PCAUT205):**

- ☐ The crankshaft **converts reciprocating motion to rotation** and is the **primary fatigue-critical component** of the engine.
- ☐ Main parts: **Main journals, crankpins, crank webs, counterweights, oil galleries, flywheel flange**.
- ☐ Stroke $L = 2r$ where $r$ = crank radius. Connecting rod ratio $n = L / l \approx 3.5$ for typical engines.
- ☐ Gas force $F_g = p_g \cdot A_p$. Piston area $A_p = \pi D^2 / 4$.
- ☐ Inertia force $F_i = m_r \cdot r \cdot \omega^2 (\cos\theta + \frac{r}{L}\cos 2\theta)$ — has **primary** (engine freq.) and **secondary** (2× freq.) components.
- ☐ Tangential force $F_t$ → produces **torque** $T = F_t \cdot r$. Radial force $F_r$ → produces **bending** $M_b = F_r \cdot r$.
- ☐ Maximum bending at TDC ($0°$) and BDC ($180°$); maximum torque at $90°$ and $270°$.
- ☐ **Guest's equivalent torque:** $T_{eq} = \sqrt{M_b^2 + T^2}$ — preferred for ductile crankshaft steel.
- ☐ **ASME shaft diameter equation:** $d^3 = \dfrac{16 \cdot T_{eq}}{\pi \cdot \tau_{allow}}$ (modify by $K_f$ for fillet).
- ☐ Counterweights **balance primary inertia**; the **fillet radius reduces stress concentration** $K_t$.
- ☐ Materials: **forged alloy steel** (e.g., 42CrMo4) most common; SGI (nodular iron) gaining popularity; surface treatments include **fillet rolling, shot peening, induction hardening, nitriding**.
- ☐ Critical speed $\omega_c = \sqrt{g / \delta}$ — must be 2× to 3× the operating speed to avoid torsional resonance.
- ☐ Always draw a **labelled FBD** of the crankpin in 14-mark derivations. Use SI units consistently.
- ☐ Most common KTU mistake: using $L$ = stroke instead of $L$ = rod length in the inertia force formula. **Re-read the problem.**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Flywheel**: A flywheel is a **heavy, rotating mechanical energy-storage device** rigidly mounted on the crankshaft of an internal combustion (IC) engine. Its primary engineering function is to **store kinetic energy (KE) during the power stroke** and **deliver it back during the non-power strokes** (suction, compression, exhaust), thereby smoothing out the **fluctuations of crankshaft angular velocity** caused by the intermittent, single-piston torque generation cycle.

In a multi-cylinder engine, the torque fluctuations are inherently smaller, so the flywheel may be lighter. In a **single-cylinder engine**, the flywheel is comparatively massive because a single piston must deliver the entire cycle work in 180° of crank rotation (for 2-stroke) or 360° (for 4-stroke), leaving the rest of the cycle with no useful torque.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Real-World Picture)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Water-Tank on a Rooftop**
> Imagine an old municipal water pipeline. Water (energy) comes in spurts — a big gush when the pump runs, and a trickle (or nothing) in between. A **roof-top water tank** acts exactly like a flywheel:
> - **During the gush (Power Stroke)** → the tank *fills up* (flywheel accelerates, storing KE).
> - **During the trickle (Non-Power Strokes)** → the tank *drains steadily* into the taps (flywheel decelerates, releasing KE).
> - The tank doesn't *create* water; it only **smooths the flow** to give a continuous supply.
>
> Replace the *tank* with a **rotating disc**, *water height* with **angular velocity ω**, and *flow rate* with **rotational output torque** — and you have an automotive flywheel.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Quantities (Bolded)

The following key variables govern flywheel design and **must be memorised verbatim** for KTU board examinations:

- **I** → Mass moment of inertia of the flywheel about the axis of rotation, measured in **kg·m²**.
- **ω** → Angular velocity of the flywheel (mean), measured in **rad/s**.
- **N** → Crankshaft speed in **rpm** (revolutions per minute).
- **E** or **KE** → Rotational kinetic energy stored, measured in **Joules (J)**.
- **CE** → Coefficient of fluctuation of energy (dimensionless, **no units**).
- **CS** → Coefficient of fluctuation of speed (dimensionless, **no units**).
- **m** → Mass of the flywheel, measured in **kg**.
- **k** → Radius of gyration of the flywheel mass about the rotation axis, measured in **m**.

## 1.4 Why a Flywheel is *Essential* in an Automobile

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Without a flywheel, an IC engine's crankshaft would **accelerate violently during the power stroke and stall during the non-power strokes**, because the torque delivered is **not continuous** — it is delivered only during the expansion (combustion) phase. The flywheel is the **mechanical inertia that masks this intermittency** and presents a near-uniform rotational output to the gearbox and ultimately to the wheels.

The flywheel also serves a **secondary structural role**: in manual-transmission cars, its outer face doubles as the **friction surface for the clutch disc**, and its outer rim carries the **starter ring gear** that the starter motor engages to crank the engine.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physical "Why" — Torque Fluctuation in IC Engines

A single-cylinder 4-stroke engine has **four distinct strokes** per cycle, but the **crank-pin effort (turning moment)** is positive and large **only during the expansion (power) stroke**. The other three strokes — **suction, compression, and exhaust** — require negative turning moment (i.e., the piston pushes the crank backward) or near-zero turning moment. Plotting this turning moment against the crank angle (θ) gives the famous **Turning Moment Diagram (T-θ diagram)**.

If the engine rotated at *perfectly uniform* speed, the work done per cycle would equal:

$$W_{\text{cycle}} = T_{\text{mean}} \times 4\pi \quad \text{(4-stroke engine)}$$

$$W_{\text{cycle}} = T_{\text{mean}} \times 2\pi \quad \text{(2-stroke engine)}$$

Because the actual torque is *not* equal to T_mean at every instant, the **instantaneous angular velocity oscillates** around the mean. This oscillation is what the flywheel is designed to **minimise** — it is *not* designed to make the speed absolutely constant (that would require infinite mass).

## 2.2 Logical Step-by-Step Concept Flow

> [!NOTE]
> **How the flywheel "decides" what to do, step by step:**
>
> 1. **During the Power Stroke** → Crank receives a torque *greater* than T_mean. The excess work done is **stored as rotational KE** in the flywheel. **ω increases** from ω_min to ω_max.
> 2. **During the Non-Power Strokes** → Crank receives a torque *less* than T_mean (or negative). The flywheel **releases its stored KE** to keep the crank turning. **ω decreases** from ω_max back to ω_min.
> 3. **Net Result** → Over a full cycle, energy in = energy out, so **mean ω is constant**, but **instantaneous ω ripples** between ω_max and ω_min within a narrow band.

## 2.3 The Two Key "Coefficients" — Must Memorise

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CE — Coefficient of Fluctuation of Energy**
> It is the **ratio of the maximum fluctuation of energy to the mean energy** of the flywheel per cycle.
> $$\boxed{C_E = \dfrac{E_{\max} - E_{\min}}{E_{\text{mean}}}}$$
> - Typical SI engine: **CE ≈ 0.05 to 0.20**
>
> **CS — Coefficient of Fluctuation of Speed**
> It is the **ratio of the maximum fluctuation of speed to the mean speed** of the flywheel.
> $$\boxed{C_S = \dfrac{N_{\max} - N_{\min}}{N_{\text{mean}}} = \dfrac{\omega_{\max} - \omega_{\min}}{\omega_{\text{mean}}}}$$
> - Typical SI petrol engine: **CS ≈ 0.01 to 0.04**
> - Typical diesel engine: **CS ≈ 0.015 to 0.05**

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following **single table is the complete formula arsenal** required to solve *any* KTU flywheel numerical. Memorise the **derivation flow** of the master equation, not just the formula.

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Standard Units |
|---|---------|----------------|----------------|
| 1 | $E = \tfrac{1}{2} I \omega^{2}$ | Rotational KE stored in flywheel | J |
| 2 | $I = m\,k^{2}$ | Mass moment of inertia (general) | kg·m² |
| 3 | $I = m\,r^{2}$ (ring) | MI of a thin rim of radius r | kg·m² |
| 4 | $I = \tfrac{1}{2} m\,r^{2}$ (solid disc) | MI of a solid disc | kg·m² |
| 5 | $I = m \left( k_{1}^{2} + k_{r}^{2} \right)$ | Rim (k_r) shifted by k_1 from centre | kg·m² |
| 6 | $\Delta E = E_{\max} - E_{\min}$ | Max fluctuation of energy | J |
| 7 | $\Delta E = I\,\omega^{2}\,C_{S}$ | **MASTER EQUATION — energy fluctuation** | J |
| 8 | $m = \dfrac{\Delta E}{k^{2}\,\omega^{2}\,C_{S}}$ | Mass of flywheel (general) | kg |
| 9 | $m = \dfrac{2\,\Delta E}{r^{2}\,\omega^{2}\,C_{S}}$ | Mass of solid disc flywheel | kg |
| 10 | $C_{S} = \dfrac{\Delta E}{I\,\omega^{2}}$ | CS from given ΔE | — |
| 11 | $C_{E} = \dfrac{\Delta E}{W_{\text{cycle}}}$ | CE from given energy | — |
| 12 | $W_{\text{cycle}} = \dfrac{P \times 60}{N/2}$ (4-stroke) | Work per cycle from power P (W) | J |
| 13 | $W_{\text{cycle}} = \dfrac{P \times 60}{N}$ (2-stroke) | Work per cycle from power P (W) | J |
| 14 | $T_{\text{mean}} = \dfrac{W_{\text{cycle}}}{4\pi}$ (4-stroke) | Mean turning moment | N·m |
| 15 | $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ | Mean angular velocity | rad/s |
| 16 | $\Delta E = E_{1} + E_{2}$ | Sum of max excess + max deficiency | J |
| 17 | $W_{\text{cycle}} = E_{1} - E_{2}$ | Net work = (excess) − (deficiency) | J |

> **Note on Notation:** All *vertical separators* inside mathematical expressions (such as $\vert \Delta\omega \vert$ or magnitude bars) are written with the LaTeX command `\vert` to **prevent markdown-table parser conflicts**. They are *not* table column dividers.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility of Flywheels

- **Automotive** → Smooth out torque pulses from the IC engine before they reach the drivetrain.
- **Hybrid vehicles** (KERS — Kinetic Energy Recovery Systems) → Act as **regenerative flywheels** spun to 60,000+ rpm in carbon-fibre evacuated chambers (e.g., **Volvo's Flywheel KERS**, **Williams F1 KERS**).
- **Industrial punch presses & shears** → Store energy from a small motor and release it in a sudden burst.
- **Power plants** → Large flywheels stabilise grid frequency (each cycle is a 360° mechanical analogue of the turning-moment problem).
- **Gyrobuses** → Pre-spin a heavy flywheel electrically; the bus then runs for several kilometres on stored KE — an early form of electric bus.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The derivations below are **exhaustive**. **Every algebraic step** is shown explicitly — no shortcuts, no "it can be shown" hand-waves — because KTU board examiners award partial credit for each visible transition, and missing the *link step* is the #1 cause of lost marks.

## 3.1 Master Derivation: Maximum Fluctuation of Energy of a Flywheel

### Step 1 — Start with the rotational kinetic energy

A flywheel of mass moment of inertia $I$ rotating at an angular velocity $\omega$ stores:

$$E = \tfrac{1}{2} I\,\omega^{2}$$

### Step 2 — Evaluate at the *two* extreme speeds

Let the **maximum** angular velocity be $\omega_{\max} = \omega + \delta\omega$ and the **minimum** be $\omega_{\min} = \omega - \delta\omega$, where $\delta\omega$ is the *small* excess angular speed above the mean $\omega$.

Therefore:

$$E_{\max} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,I\,(\omega + \delta\omega)^{2}$$

$$E_{\min} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,I\,(\omega - \delta\omega)^{2}$$

### Step 3 — Subtract to obtain the fluctuation $\Delta E$

The fluctuation of energy is, by definition, the **difference** between the maximum and minimum KE states:

$$\Delta E = E_{\max} - E_{\min} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,I\left[(\omega + \delta\omega)^{2} - (\omega - \delta\omega)^{2}\right]$$

### Step 4 — Expand the binomials

Using $(a+b)^{2} = a^{2} + 2ab + b^{2}$ and $(a-b)^{2} = a^{2} - 2ab + b^{2}$:

$$(\omega + \delta\omega)^{2} = \omega^{2} + 2\omega\,\delta\omega + \delta\omega^{2}$$

$$(\omega - \delta\omega)^{2} = \omega^{2} - 2\omega\,\delta\omega + \delta\omega^{2}$$

### Step 5 — Subtract term-by-term

The $\omega^{2}$ and $\delta\omega^{2}$ terms **cancel**, leaving only the cross terms:

$$(\omega + \delta\omega)^{2} - (\omega - \delta\omega)^{2} = 4\,\omega\,\delta\omega$$

### Step 6 — Substitute back into $\Delta E$

$$\Delta E = \tfrac{1}{2}\,I \times 4\,\omega\,\delta\omega = 2\,I\,\omega\,\delta\omega$$

### Step 7 — Express $\delta\omega$ in terms of $C_S$

By definition, $C_S$ is the total fractional speed ripple:

$$C_S = \frac{\omega_{\max} - \omega_{\min}}{\omega} = \frac{2\,\delta\omega}{\omega}$$

Rearranging to isolate $\delta\omega$:

$$\delta\omega = \frac{C_S\,\omega}{2}$$

### Step 8 — Final substitution

Plug $\delta\omega = \tfrac{C_S\,\omega}{2}$ into $\Delta E = 2\,I\,\omega\,\delta\omega$:

$$\boxed{\Delta E = 2\,I\,\omega \times \frac{C_S\,\omega}{2} = I\,\omega^{2}\,C_S}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** This boxed equation $\Delta E = I\omega^{2}C_S$ is the **single most important formula** in Module 1 for flywheel design. Memorise the *derivation* (8 steps above), not just the result — KTU theory questions (5–10 marks) routinely ask for this proof.

## 3.2 Derivation: Mass of a Solid-Disc Flywheel

For a **solid disc** of mass $m$ and radius $r$:

$$I = \tfrac{1}{2} m\,r^{2}$$

Substituting into the master equation $\Delta E = I\omega^{2}C_S$:

$$\Delta E = \tfrac{1}{2} m\,r^{2}\,\omega^{2}\,C_S$$

Solving for $m$:

$$\boxed{m = \frac{2\,\Delta E}{r^{2}\,\omega^{2}\,C_S}}$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Mass of a Rim-Type Flywheel (Annular Ring)

For a **thin rim** of mass $m$ at radius $r$ (web/arms mass neglected):

$$I = m\,r^{2}$$

Substituting into the master equation:

$$\boxed{m = \frac{\Delta E}{r^{2}\,\omega^{2}\,C_S}}$$

> **Key observation:** A *solid disc* of the same mass and radius has **half the inertia** of a thin rim — so designers prefer **rim-concentrated** flywheels. This is why real automobile flywheels have a **thick outer rim** and a **thin web/centre**.

## 3.4 Derivation: Mass of a Rim Flywheel with Rim Thickness

If the rim has rectangular cross-section (width $b$, thickness $t$) at mean radius $r$, and density $\rho$:

$$m = 2\pi r\,b\,t\,\rho$$

And $k^{2} = r^{2} + \tfrac{b^{2} + t^{2}}{12}$ (parallel axis for a rectangular section).

The substitution path is:

$$I = m\,k^{2}, \quad \Delta E = I\omega^{2}C_S \;\Rightarrow\; m = \frac{\Delta E}{k^{2}\omega^{2}C_S}$$

> [!NOTE]
> For a **pure thin rim**, the second term vanishes and $k^{2} \to r^{2}$.

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (Full KTU-Style Solution)

**Problem Statement:**

A 4-stroke single-cylinder IC engine develops **20 kW** at a mean speed of **300 rpm**. The total fluctuation of speed is limited to **±3 % of the mean speed**. The flywheel is a **solid disc of mass 250 kg** with a **radius of gyration of 200 mm**. Determine:

1. The **maximum fluctuation of energy** in the flywheel.
2. The **coefficient of fluctuation of energy (CE)**.

**Solution — Step 1: Convert speed to angular velocity**

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 300}{60} = 31.416 \;\text{rad/s}$$

**Solution — Step 2: Determine the mass moment of inertia**

$$I = m\,k^{2} = 250 \times (0.200)^{2} = 250 \times 0.04 = 10 \;\text{kg·m}^{2}$$

**Solution — Step 3: Convert speed fluctuation to CS**

A speed fluctuation of **±3 %** means $\omega_{\max} - \omega_{\min} = 0.06\,\omega$:

$$C_S = \frac{\omega_{\max} - \omega_{\min}}{\omega} = 0.06$$

**Solution — Step 4: Apply the master equation $\Delta E = I\omega^{2}C_S$**

$$\Delta E = 10 \times (31.416)^{2} \times 0.06$$

$$\Delta E = 10 \times 986.96 \times 0.06$$

$$\boxed{\Delta E = 592.18 \;\text{J}}$$

**Solution — Step 5: Compute work done per cycle (4-stroke)**

For a 4-stroke engine, the crank completes **2 revolutions per cycle**, so there are $N/2$ cycles per minute:

$$W_{\text{cycle}} = \frac{P \times 60}{N/2} = \frac{20\,000 \times 60}{150} = 8000 \;\text{J}$$

**Solution — Step 6: Compute the coefficient of fluctuation of energy**

$$C_E = \frac{\Delta E}{W_{\text{cycle}}} = \frac{592.18}{8000} = 0.0740$$

$$\boxed{C_E \approx 0.074 \;\text{(i.e., 7.4 \%)}}$$

**Solution — Step 7: Sanity-Check via the Exact Energy Equation (KTU cross-verification)**

Using the rigorous form (no small-$\delta\omega$ approximation):

$$\Delta E = \tfrac{1}{2}I(\omega_{\max}^{2} - \omega_{\min}^{2})$$

With $\omega_{\max} = 1.03 \times 31.416 = 32.359$ rad/s and $\omega_{\min} = 0.97 \times 31.416 = 30.474$ rad/s:

$$\Delta E = 0.5 \times 10 \times (32.359^{2} - 30.474^{2})$$

$$= 5 \times (1047.10 - 928.67) = 5 \times 118.43 = 592.15 \;\text{J} \;\checkmark$$

The small-$\delta\omega$ approximation matches the exact answer to within **0.005 %** — a very strong validation that the master equation is correct for typical CS values.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> Per the protocol, the physical geometry of a flywheel is illustrated here as a **block-level functional flow** (since photorealistic mechanical drawings are beyond a code-based Mermaid render). Each node represents a *function* or *component* in the system.

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Functional Block Diagram: Flywheel Energy-Storage Cycle in a 4-Stroke Engine

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CRANK PIN RECEIVES TORQUE] --> B{TORQUE LEVEL vs MEAN}
    B -- Greater than T_mean --> C[ENGINE POWER STROKE ACTIVE]
    C --> D[FLYWHEEL ACCELERATES]
    D --> E[STORES KINETIC ENERGY 0.5 x I x w square]
    E --> F[ANGULAR VELOCITY RISES TO w max]
    F --> G[WAIT FOR NEXT STROKE]
    B -- Less than T_mean --> H[NON POWER STROKE ACTIVE]
    H --> I[FLYWHEEL DECELERATES]
    I --> J[RELEASES STORED KINETIC ENERGY]
    J --> K[ANGULAR VELOCITY DROPS TO w min]
    K --> G
    G --> A
    F --> L[CRANKSHAFT OUTPUT TO GEARBOX]
    K --> L
    L --> M[UNIFORM TORQUE TO WHEELS]
```

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — T-θ Diagram Interpretation: Areas of Excess & Deficiency

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[TURNING MOMENT DIAGRAM T vs theta] --> B[PLOT T mean HORIZONTAL LINE]
    B --> C{TURNING MOMENT REGION}
    C -- Above T mean --> D[AREA ABOVE MEAN LINE]
    C -- Below T mean --> E[AREA BELOW MEAN LINE]
    D --> F[EXCESS ENERGY E1 max]
    E --> G[DEFICIENT ENERGY E2 max]
    F --> H[FLYWHEEL ABSORBS DURING POWER STROKE]
    G --> I[FLYWHEEL RELEASES DURING IDLE STROKES]
    H --> J[FLUCTUATION OF ENERGY delta E]
    I --> J
    J --> K[delta E equals E1 max plus E2 max]
    H --> L[NET WORK equals E1 max minus E2 max]
    G --> L
    K --> M[INPUT TO MASTER EQUATION]
    M --> N[delta E equals I w square C sub S]
    L --> O[MEAN POWER EQUATION]
    O --> P[W equals T mean times 4 pi for 4 stroke]
```

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Anatomical Block Layout of an Automotive Flywheel

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[AUTOMOTIVE FLYWHEEL ASSEMBLY] --> B[STAMPED HUB]
    A --> C[SPIDER / WEB PLATE]
    A --> D[OUTER RIM MASS]
    A --> E[REAR FACE FRICTION SURFACE]
    A --> F[STARTER RING GEAR]
    B --> B1[BORE TO FIT CRANKSHAFT FLANGE]
    B --> B2[DRIVE PINS OR BOLT PATTERN]
    C --> C1[6 SPOKES TYPICAL]
    C --> C2[CONNECTS HUB TO RIM]
    D --> D1[CAST IRON DENSITY 7200 KG PER M CUBED]
    D --> D2[MAXIMUM MOMENT OF INERTIA PER UNIT MASS]
    E --> E1[CLUTCH DISC PRESSES AGAINST THIS FACE]
    F --> F1[ENGAGED BY STARTER MOTOR PINION]
```

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Decision Flowchart for Solving KTU Flywheel Numericals

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[READ PROBLEM] --> B{WHAT IS GIVEN}
    B -- Mass m and radius k --> C[COMPUTE I equals m k square]
    B -- Geometry of rim --> D[COMPUTE I using parallel axis]
    C --> E{COMPUTE C sub S}
    D --> E
    E -- Speed range given --> F[C sub S equals delta N over N mean]
    E -- Given as plus minus percent --> G[C sub S equals 2 times percent]
    F --> H[APPLY MASTER EQUATION]
    G --> H
    H --> I[delta E equals I w square C sub S]
    I --> J{WHAT IS ASKED}
    J -- delta E --> K[STOP]
    J -- C sub E --> L[COMPUTE W cycle from P and N]
    L --> M[C sub E equals delta E over W cycle]
    M --> K
    J -- Mass m required --> N[REARRANGE MASTER EQN]
    N --> O[m equals delta E over k square w square C sub S]
    O --> K
```
