<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, May 2024 Model]`
**Differentiate between OHV and DOHC engine valve trains with neat sketches. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer (Concise 3-mark format):**

| Feature | OHV | DOHC |
|---|---|---|
| Camshaft location | Inside engine block | Inside cylinder head (two camshafts) |
| No. of camshafts per bank | One | Two (one intake, one exhaust) |
| Drive mechanism | Pushrod + rocker arm | Bucket tappet or direct rocker |
| Typical valves per cylinder | 2 | 4 |
| Engine height | Compact | Taller |
| High-RPM breathing | Limited | Excellent |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |
| Application | SUVs, commercial | Sports, modern BS6 sedans |

> **Sketch:** Draw a simple side-view showing the cam position relative to the cylinder.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`
**What is valve overlap? Why is it necessary in a 4-stroke engine? (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Valve overlap is the angular period (in degrees of crank rotation) around the **TDC at the end of the exhaust stroke** when both the intake valve and the exhaust valve are **simultaneously open**.

It is necessary because:
1. It enables **scavenging** — the high-velocity incoming fresh charge sweeps out residual exhaust gases.
2. It provides **internal EGR**, reducing combustion temperatures and $\text{NO}_x$ formation.
3. It improves **volumetric efficiency** at higher engine speeds.
4. Typical overlap ranges from **5° to 50°** depending on the engine character (economy vs. performance).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`

**(a)** Describe the construction and working of the **OHV (Overhead Valve) valve mechanism** with a neat sketch. List its advantages and limitations. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **DOHC valve mechanism** with a neat sketch. Compare its performance with OHV at high engine speeds. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — OHV Construction & Working:**

In an OHV (Overhead Valve) engine, also called a **pushrod engine** or **I-head engine**, the **camshaft is located inside the engine block**, beneath the cylinder head. The components in the order of motion transfer are:

1. **Camshaft** — mounted in the crankcase, driven by the crankshaft at half its speed (1:2 ratio) through a chain, gear, or belt. It carries cam lobes corresponding to each valve.
2. **Tappet / Lifter** — a cylindrical plunger riding on the cam lobe. As the cam rotates, the lobe lifts the tappet linearly.
3. **Pushrod** — a thin steel rod connecting the tappet (below) to the rocker arm (above). It transmits the upward motion through the cylinder head.
4. **Rocker arm** — a pivoted lever inside the cylinder head. The pushrod pushes one end, and the valve stem is pushed down by the other end, opening the valve against the spring.
5. **Valve and valve spring** — the valve opens against the spring, and the spring returns it to the seat when the cam lobe rotates away.
6. **Valve guides and seals** — guide the valve stem and prevent oil leakage.

**Working:** As the camshaft rotates, each cam lobe (in turn) pushes its tappet upward → the pushrod transmits this motion → the rocker arm pivots → the valve stem is depressed → the valve opens. The valve spring is compressed. As the cam lobe passes its nose, the spring force pushes the valve back to its seat, and the cam follows the base circle.

**Advantages of OHV:**
- Compact engine height and short overall length.
- Lower manufacturing and maintenance cost.
- Robust and reliable.
- Good low-end torque (high leverage of rocker arm).

**Limitations of OHV:**
- Long, flexible power path → valve float at high RPM.
- Heavier reciprocating mass (pushrod + lifter).
- Limited ability to run multiple valves per cylinder.
- Higher friction losses.

---

**Part (b) — DOHC Construction & Comparison with OHV:**

In a **DOHC (Double Overhead Camshaft)** engine, **two separate camshafts** are mounted in the cylinder head — one operates the intake valves and the other operates the exhaust valves. Both camshafts are driven by the crankshaft via a **timing chain, belt, or gear train** at half the crankshaft speed.

The valve actuation is typically **direct-acting**: the cam lobe pushes a low-mass **bucket tappet** (or a short rocker arm), which directly depresses the valve. **No pushrod is used.**

**Key differences from OHV at high RPM:**

| Parameter | OHV | DOHC |
|---|---|---|
| Power path length | Long (cam→lifter→pushrod→rocker→valve) | Short (cam→bucket→valve) |
| Reciprocating mass | High | Very low |
| Valve float RPM | ~4500–5500 RPM | ~7000–9000+ RPM |
| Maximum safe engine speed | Limited | High |
| Valve count per cylinder | 2 (usually) | 4 (typically) |
| Combustion chamber shape | Wedge or hemispherical (constrained) | Pent-roof (free design) |
| Independent intake/exhaust timing | No (both on same cam) | Yes (VVT/VANOS/i-VTEC possible) |

**Conclusion:** At high engine speeds, **DOHC outperforms OHV** because of its lower reciprocating mass, stiffer power path, and ability to support multi-valve pent-roof combustion chambers with independent VVT.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**(a)** With the help of a **valve timing diagram**, explain the four events of valve timing in a 4-stroke SI engine. Define **valve overlap and valve lag**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An engine runs at 3000 RPM. The cam profile is parabolic with maximum lift $L = 8\text{ mm}$ and nose half-angle $\theta_n = 30°$. The valve mass is 0.05 kg. Calculate the **peak cam-driven acceleration** and the **minimum spring preload** required (neglect gas force). **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Valve Timing Diagram and Events:**

The valve timing diagram is a **circular plot** drawn with crank angle (0° to 720°) as the angular axis, indicating when each valve opens and closes.

**Four Key Events:**

1. **Intake Valve Opens (IVO)** — at **5°–20° BTDC** of the intake stroke. Opening slightly early uses the upward piston motion to assist in drawing in the charge (ram effect).
2. **Intake Valve Closes (IVC)** — at **40°–60° ABDC**. Closing after BDC takes advantage of the **ram charging** effect — the moving air column continues to enter even as the piston starts compressing.
3. **Exhaust Valve Opens (EVO)** — at **40°–55° BBDC** of the power stroke. Opening before BDC allows the high cylinder pressure to blow down the exhaust gas (Blowdown).
4. **Exhaust Valve Closes (EVC)** — at **5°–15° ATDC** of the exhaust stroke. Closing after TDC helps scavenge residual gases.

**Valve Overlap:** The angular period (in degrees of crank rotation) around TDC at the end of the exhaust stroke when **both intake and exhaust valves are simultaneously open**. It enables scavenging and internal EGR.

**Valve Lag:** The angular delay (in degrees) of the actual valve event with respect to the **geometric cam profile event**, caused by the dynamic flexure of the pushrod, lifter, and rocker arm lash. In OHV engines, lag is larger (~5°–10°); in DOHC, lag is much smaller (~1°–3°).

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** $N = 3000$ RPM, $L = 8\text{ mm} = 0.008\text{ m}$, parabolic cam, $m_v = 0.05\text{ kg}$, $F_g \approx 0$.

**Step 1 — Camshaft speed:**
$$N_{\text{cam}} = \frac{N_{\text{crank}}}{2} = \frac{3000}{2} = 1500 \text{ RPM}$$

**Step 2 — Peak cam acceleration (parabolic cam):**
For a parabolic cam, the cam follows $y = a\theta^2$ (where $\theta$ is cam angle in radians).

At maximum lift $L$ at nose half-angle $\theta_n = 30° = \pi/6 \text{ rad}$:

$$L = a \theta_n^2 \implies a = \frac{L}{\theta_n^2} = \frac{0.008}{(\pi/6)^2}$$

$$a = \frac{0.008}{0.2742} = 0.02918 \text{ m/rad}^2$$

Acceleration in time-domain (with cam rotating at angular velocity $\omega_{\text{cam}}$):

$$\ddot{y} = 2a \dot{\theta}^2 = 2a \omega_{\text{cam}}^2$$

$$\omega_{\text{cam}} = \frac{2\pi N_{\text{cam}}}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 1500}{60} = 50\pi \text{ rad/s} = 157.08 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$\ddot{y}_{\text{peak}} = 2 \times 0.02918 \times (157.08)^2$$

$$\ddot{y}_{\text{peak}} = 0.05836 \times 24674 = 1440.1 \text{ m/s}^2$$

**Step 3 — Minimum spring preload:**

For the valve to maintain contact with the cam (no float) at peak acceleration, neglecting gas force:

$$F_s \geq m_v \cdot a_{\text{peak}}$$

$$F_s \geq 0.05 \times 1440.1 = 72.0 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4 — Engineering reality check:**
In practice, the spring preload must be **at least 1.5×–2× this value** to account for spring relaxation, thermal effects, and dynamic surging. So an **engineered preload of ~120 N** would be the design value. The spring rate would also be sized so the natural frequency $f_n \geq 3 \times$ valve event frequency.

**Final Answer:** $a_{\text{peak}} \approx 1440 \text{ m/s}^2$, $F_s \geq 72 \text{ N}$ (theoretical minimum).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on Valve Train Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the 1:2 camshaft ratio** — if a numerical gives engine speed 3000 RPM, the cam speed is 1500 RPM. Marks lost: 1 mark.
> 2. **Confusing valve overlap with valve lag** — overlap is *simultaneous opening* around TDC; lag is *delay* in actual event vs. cam event. Marks lost: 1 mark.
> 3. **Omitting the gas force** in spring force balance — especially for the exhaust valve during the power stroke. Marks lost: 1 mark.
> 4. **Drawing the camshaft inside the block for DOHC** — this is the most common pictorial error. Marks lost: 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Stating "DOHC has 4 valves per cylinder" without saying "typically"** — modern lean-burn engines may use 3 valves (e.g., older Ford EcoBoost 1.0L), so absolute statements lose precision marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Read this 5 minutes before the exam:**

- ⭐ **Camshaft speed = Crank speed / 2** — universal for all 4-stroke engines. Never forget.
- ⭐ **OHV** = camshaft in block, pushrod present, 2 valves/cylinder, low cost, low-RPM torque.
- ⭐ **SOHC** = one cam in head, no pushrod, 2–3 valves/cylinder, balanced performance.
- ⭐ **DOHC** = two cams in head, no pushrod, 4 valves/cylinder, high-RPM power, supports VVT.
- ⭐ **Valve overlap** = period around TDC (end exhaust / start intake) when both valves are open. Typical: 5°–50°.
- ⭐ **Valve lag** = angular delay between geometric cam event and actual valve event. Larger in OHV, smaller in DOHC.
- ⭐ **Valve float** = condition when spring cannot keep valve in contact with cam at high RPM. Avoided by stiff springs, light valves, or DOHC geometry.
- ⭐ **Interference angle** = $\alpha - \beta = 0.5°$ to $1.5°$ — gives a narrow seating line for good sealing and heat transfer.
- ⭐ **Peak cam acceleration** (parabolic cam) = $a_{\text{peak}} = \frac{\pi^2 L N_{\text{cam}}^2}{900}$ m/s² — must be checked against valve spring capacity.
- ⭐ **Spring preload rule of thumb:** $F_s \geq 1.5 \times m_v \cdot a_{\text{peak}}$ for safety margin.
- ⭐ **BS6 / Euro 6 engines use DOHC + VVT** universally — DOHC allows independent intake/exhaust timing for emissions control.
- ⭐ **Hydraulic lash adjusters (HLA)** in modern engines maintain zero valve clearance automatically — eliminates the need for periodic shim adjustment.
- ⭐ **Valve seat angle** standard = 45°; high-performance engines use 30° for better flow at the expense of seat strength.
- ⭐ **Valve material:** Intake — low-alloy steel; Exhaust — austenitic stainless steel or Inconel (for high-temperature strength); Valve seat insert — sintered bronze or stellited.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Valve Timing Diagram and Port Timing Diagrams
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT205 - AUTOMOBILE POWER PLANT (AUTOMOBILE ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Valve Timing Diagram and Port Timing Diagrams

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# MODULE 1 — VALVE TIMING DIAGRAM AND PORT TIMING DIAGRAMS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Valve Timing Diagram (VTD):** A polar/circular diagram that depicts, in terms of crank angle (measured in degrees of crank rotation), the exact instants at which the intake and exhaust valves **open** and **close** during the operating cycle of a **four-stroke** internal combustion engine. It maps the angular position of the crankshaft at every significant valve event (IVO, IVC, EVO, EVC) relative to the two reference dead centres — TDC (Top Dead Centre) and BDC (Bottom Dead Centre).

> [!NOTE]
> **Port Timing Diagram (PTD):** The 2-stroke equivalent of the VTD. In a 2-stroke engine there are no poppet valves — instead, the **transfer port** and **exhaust port** are uncovered/covered by the moving piston itself. The PTD therefore specifies the crank angles at which the **transfer port opens/closes** and the **exhaust port opens/closes**, together with any auxiliary scavenging device event angles.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (CO1, KTU 2024):** Students must be able to *sketch and interpret the full-crank-angle valve timing diagram for both 4-stroke SI and CI engines* and the *port timing diagram for 2-stroke SI and CI engines*, and clearly state the *practical justification* for every angular advance or retard.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Engine as a Runner's Breath"

Think of the four-stroke engine as a **distance runner performing one breath cycle**:
- The runner's mouth is the **intake valve** — it must open *slightly early* (just before the head is fully back) to gulp as much air as possible, and stays open *slightly late* so the lungs get completely filled.
- The runner's nose is the **exhaust valve** — it must start blowing out *slightly before* the body is fully compressed and finish *slightly after* the body has straightened, otherwise stale air would be trapped.

If the runner breathed **only when fully back or fully forward** (i.e., only at dead centres), he would feel suffocated and slow down. The engine behaves the same way — opening the valves **before** or closing them **after** the dead centres allows the gas to flow while the piston is *still moving in the favourable direction*, which is called **valve lead and lag**.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (SI Engine — Typical Production Values)

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value (SI) | Typical Value (CI) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Intake Valve Opens (before TDC) | IVO | **10° – 20°** | 10° – 20° |
| Intake Valve Closes (after BDC) | IVC | **40° – 50°** | 20° – 40° |
| Exhaust Valve Opens (before BDC) | EVO | **40° – 50°** | 35° – 45° |
| Exhaust Valve Closes (after TDC) | EVC | **10° – 20°** | 10° – 15° |
| Spark Advance (before TDC) | θ_s | 30° – 40° | — (replaced by injection) |
| Injection Advance (before TDC) | θ_i | — | 10° – 20° |
| Valve Overlap (IVO to EVC) | θ_ov | 20° – 40° | 20° – 35° |

### 1.4 Block Flow — How the Diagram Is Read

The polar diagram is drawn for **two full crank revolutions (720°)** in the 4-stroke engine. The radial line represents the crank position, and the **arc segments** show *when* a valve is open. Reading is always done **clockwise**, starting from the intake stroke at TDC of the power stroke (convention).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Strokes — Mechanical Sequence

The four-stroke SI engine completes one working cycle in **two crank revolutions (720°)**:

1. **Intake stroke (0° → 180°):** Piston moves from TDC to BDC. Intake valve is open, exhaust valve is closed. A fresh charge (air + fuel) is drawn in.
2. **Compression stroke (180° → 360°):** Both valves closed. Charge is compressed. Spark fires a few degrees before TDC.
3. **Power/Expansion stroke (360° → 540°):** Combustion products expand, doing work on the piston. Exhaust valve is still closed.
4. **Exhaust stroke (540° → 720°):** Exhaust valve opens, piston moves from BDC to TDC pushing out burnt gases.

### 2.2 Why Are Valves Timed Off-Dead-Centre? — The "Why" Behind Each Event

#### 2.2.1 Intake Valve Opens (IVO) — *Before* TDC

> The intake manifold contains fresh charge at a pressure slightly **above atmospheric** (≈ 1.05 bar) due to ram effect and manifold tuning. If IVO occurred exactly at TDC, the piston would have to start descending *before* the valve could start admitting gas. By opening the valve **early** (10°–20° before TDC), the gas enters during the *last moments* of the exhaust stroke, so by the time the piston begins to descend, the valve is already wide open and the gas rushes in at maximum possible cross-section.

- **Engineering utility:** Eliminates the "valve-lag" at the start of induction, increasing **volumetric efficiency (η_v)** by 5–8 %.

#### 2.2.2 Intake Valve Closes (IVC) — *After* BDC

> At BDC, the piston momentarily stops. Air has inertia and continues to flow even though the piston is no longer "pulling". If IVC were exactly at BDC, a column of fresh charge would be lost back into the manifold. By delaying closure to **40°–50° after BDC**, the inertia of the inrushing column is exploited to pack more charge — this is called **ram filling** or **inertial supercharging**.

- The angle from BDC to IVC is called the **intake lag (β)**.
- **Engineering utility:** Increases η_v in the mid-speed range (typically peak η_v occurs at the speed for which β is optimised).

#### 2.2.3 Exhaust Valve Opens (EVO) — *Before* BDC

> At ~40° before BDC, the combustion pressure in the cylinder has fallen to about **3–4 bar** (from the original ~40 bar peak), but the cylinder still contains useful pressure relative to the exhaust manifold (~1.1 bar). Opening the exhaust valve *before* BDC allows this residual high-pressure gas to **spontaneously blow down**, recovering some work that would otherwise be lost as pumping loss.

- The angle from EVO to BDC is called the **exhaust lead (γ)**.
- **Trade-off:** Earlier EVO → more blowdown work lost; later EVO → pumping loss. A typical compromise is **40°–50° before BDC**.

#### 2.2.4 Exhaust Valve Closes (EVC) — *After* TDC

> At TDC, the exhaust stroke ends and a small pocket of burnt gas remains in the clearance volume. If EVC were at TDC, residual gas would dilute the next charge. By leaving the exhaust valve open for **10°–20° after TDC**, the rising fresh charge in the intake stroke *scavenges* the residual burnt gas out of the cylinder.

- The angle from TDC to EVC is called the **exhaust lag (δ)**.

#### 2.2.5 Valve Overlap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Valve Overlap (θ_ov)** is the angular interval at TDC (between the exhaust stroke end and intake stroke start) during which **both valves are open simultaneously**.
> $$\theta_{ov} = (\text{IVO before TDC}) + (\text{EVC after TDC})$$
> For a typical SI engine: $\theta_{ov} = 15° + 15° = 30°$.
>
> **Why it matters:**
> - Allows **residual gas scavenging** — incoming fresh charge pushes out the last of the exhaust.
> - Provides an **internal EGR (Exhaust Gas Recirculation)** effect that lowers peak temperatures and suppresses knock.
> - At very high speeds, the inrushing charge at high velocity can be partially **expelled directly into the exhaust** (short-circuiting), which is a *loss* — overlap is therefore tuned for a specific engine speed.

### 2.3 Differences Between 4-Stroke SI and CI Valve Timing

| Event | SI Engine (Petrol) | CI Engine (Diesel) | Reason for Difference |
|---|---|---|---|
| IVO | 10°–20° BTDC | 10°–20° BTDC | Similar mechanical need |
| IVC | 40°–50° ABDC | 20°–40° ABDC | CI has **no throttle**; lower intake depression, less ram effect, so less benefit from late closure |
| EVO | 40°–50° BBDC | 35°–45° BBDC | Slightly earlier in CI to assist scavenging since CI is unthrottled |
| EVC | 10°–20° ATDC | 10°–15° ATDC | Similar |
| Spark/Injection | Spark 30°–40° BTDC | Injection 10°–20° BTDC | CI uses **compression ignition** — fuel injected near TDC |
| Overlap | 20°–40° | 20°–35° | Slightly less in CI to avoid excessive smoke |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CI engines run unthrottled.** There is no throttle plate in the intake manifold, so intake depression is very small. The engine "breathes" naturally. As a result, **IVC occurs earlier** in a CI engine — there is little advantage in holding the intake valve open after BDC because the piston is not pulling strongly anyway.

### 2.4 2-Stroke Port Timing Diagram

In a 2-stroke engine, the piston uncovers the **transfer port** and **exhaust port** as it moves. There are no camshafts for these events. Typical port timing:

| Event | Crank Angle (before/after reference) | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|
| Exhaust Port Opens (EPO) | Before BDC | **50° – 60° BBDC** |
| Exhaust Port Closes (EPC) | After TDC | **50° – 60° ATDC** |
| Transfer Port Opens (TPO) | Before BDC | **45° – 55° BBDC** |
| Transfer Port Closes (TPC) | After BDC | **45° – 55° ABDC** |
| Scavenging Period | EPO to TPC | **~120° – 130°** |

> The **scavenging period** in a 2-stroke engine replaces the *entire* intake and exhaust processes of a 4-stroke. During this period, the transfer port admits fresh charge (from the crankcase or a blower) which sweeps exhaust gases out — this is called **scavenging**.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Effective intake duration | $\theta_{intake} = 180° + \beta$ where $\beta$ = IVC after BDC | degrees (crank) | 4-stroke |
| 2 | Effective exhaust duration | $\theta_{exhaust} = 180° + \gamma$ where $\gamma$ = EVO before BDC | degrees (crank) | 4-stroke |
| 3 | Valve overlap | $\theta_{ov} = \text{IVO before TDC} + \text{EVC after TDC}$ | degrees | At TDC of intake stroke |
| 4 | Crank angle to time | $t = \dfrac{\theta}{(6 \cdot N)}$ for 4-stroke, $\theta$ in degrees, $N$ in rpm | seconds | $6N$ = degrees per second |
| 5 | Mean piston speed | $\bar{V}_p = 2 \cdot L \cdot N$ for 4-stroke | m/s | $L$ = stroke, $N$ = rpm/60 rev/s |
| 6 | Scavenging period (2-stroke) | $\theta_{scav} = (TPO \text{ to } EPC) \approx 120°$ | degrees | 2-stroke |
| 7 | Valve overlap in 2-stroke | $\theta_{ov,2s} = \text{EPC after TDC} + \text{EPO before TDC, next cycle}$ | degrees | Smaller overlap than 4-stroke |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Crank-Angle to Time Conversion

This is a **high-yield KTU derivation** that examiners love to ask (2–3 marks in Part A, sometimes 7 marks in Part B).

> **Problem:** A 4-stroke SI engine runs at **N = 3000 rpm**. The intake valve opens **18° before TDC**. Find the time (in seconds) between IVO and TDC.

**Step 1 — Identify the relevant relationship.**

For an engine running at $N$ revolutions per minute, the **angular velocity** of the crankshaft in revolutions per second is:

$$\omega = \dfrac{N}{60} \quad \text{[rev/s]}$$

**Step 2 — Convert to degrees per second.**

One full revolution = 360°, so angular velocity in **degrees per second** is:

$$\omega_{deg} = \omega \times 360 = \dfrac{N}{60} \times 360 = 6N \quad \text{[deg/s]}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the kinematic relationship between angle and time.**

For a constant-velocity rotation:

$$t = \dfrac{\theta}{\omega_{deg}} = \dfrac{\theta}{6N} \quad \text{[seconds]}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute numerical values.**

Given $\theta = 18°$ and $N = 3000$ rpm:

$$t = \dfrac{18}{6 \times 3000} = \dfrac{18}{18000} = 1 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s}$$

$$\boxed{t = 1 \text{ ms} = 0.001 \text{ s}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's interpretation:** At 3000 rpm, the crank takes only 1 millisecond to sweep 18°. This is why valve events are described in *crank degrees* and not in *seconds* — at high speed, time becomes impractically small, but the angular position remains a clear, machine-independent way to specify the event.

### 3.2 Derivation: Total Effective Valve Opening Duration

> **Problem:** For a 4-stroke SI engine with IVO = 15° BTDC and IVC = 45° ABDC, calculate the total crank-angle duration during which the intake valve is open.

**Step 1 — Sketch the angular interval.**

The intake valve is open from **15° before TDC** to **45° after BDC**. Across the intake stroke (TDC → BDC), the crank sweeps **180°**.

**Step 2 — Add the lead, the stroke, and the lag.**

$$\theta_{open} = \theta_{lead} + 180° + \theta_{lag}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute.**

$$\theta_{open} = 15° + 180° + 45° = 240°$$

$$\boxed{\theta_{open,\,intake} = 240° \text{ of crank rotation}}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to time at, say, N = 2400 rpm.**

$$t = \dfrac{240}{6 \times 2400} = \dfrac{240}{14400} = 1.667 \times 10^{-2} \text{ s} = 16.67 \text{ ms}$$

### 3.3 Derivation: Valve Overlap Calculation

> **Problem:** An engine has IVO = 12° BTDC and EVC = 18° ATDC. Calculate the valve overlap.

By definition (both valves open at TDC of the intake stroke, between the exhaust stroke end and the intake stroke start):

$$\theta_{ov} = \text{IVO before TDC} + \text{EVC after TDC}$$

$$\theta_{ov} = 12° + 18° = 30°$$

$$\boxed{\theta_{ov} = 30°}$$

### 3.4 Derivation: Scavenging Period in a 2-Stroke Engine

> **Problem:** A 2-stroke engine has TPO = 50° BBDC, TPC = 50° ABDC, EPO = 55° BBDC, EPC = 55° ATDC. Find (a) the scavenging period, and (b) the period during which *both* transfer and exhaust ports are open simultaneously (i.e., the **blow-down period** for scavenging).

**Step 1 — Scavenging period (full overlap of TPO and EPC).**

Scavenging is the interval from when the **first** port opens (EPO) to when the **last** port closes (TPC).

$$\theta_{scav} = \text{(EPO to BDC)} + 180° + \text{(BDC to TPC)}$$

$$\theta_{scav} = 55° + 180° + 50° = 285°$$

$$\boxed{\theta_{scav} = 285° \text{ (very large compared to 4-stroke)}}$$

**Step 2 — Simultaneous open period (transfer + exhaust both uncovered).**

The transfer port opens at 50° BBDC. Before this, only the exhaust port is open (pure blow-down). After the transfer port opens, **both** are open until the transfer port closes (50° ABDC).

$$\theta_{both\,open} = \text{TPO to BDC} + 180° + \text{BDC to TPC} - \theta_{scav,blowdown\,only}$$

Simplifying directly:

$$\theta_{both\,open} = (TPO \text{ to BDC}) + 180° + (BDC \text{ to TPC}) - \text{(EPC to TPO)}$$

A simpler way: from **TPO to EPC** (since EPC occurs after TDC and TPO occurs before BDC of the same cycle).

$$\theta_{both\,open} = (180° - 50°) + 55° = 130° + 55° = 185°$$

$$\boxed{\theta_{both\,open} = 185°}$$

This 185° interval is the *active scavenging* window where fresh charge displaces burnt gas.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Cause and Effect of Each Timing Parameter

| Parameter | Increase → Effect on Torque/Power | Increase → Effect on Emissions | Practical Limit |
|---|---|---|---|
| ↑ IVO lead | Better breathing at high speed, more valve overlap | Slight increase in HC (residual gas) | Reversed flow at low speed |
| ↑ IVC lag | Better volumetric efficiency at mid-speed | Lower residual gas, lower HC | Re-breathing at low speed |
| ↑ EVO lead | More blowdown work lost (less work) | Lower HC, more complete burn-out | Excessive loss of expansion work |
| ↑ EVC lag | Better scavenging, more overlap | Lower CO, HC | Excess overlap → reversion at idle |
| ↑ Overlap | Better high-speed breathing, internal EGR | Lower NOx, but higher HC at idle | Idle roughness, misfire |
| ↑ Spark/Injection advance | Peak pressure rises, more work | Lower exhaust temp, then knock-limited | Knock in SI; smoke & noise in CI |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — 4-Stroke Valve Event Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start of Intake Stroke - TDC exhaust-end] --> B[IVO event - 15 deg BTDC]
    B --> C[Piston descends TDC to BDC - Intake stroke 180 deg]
    C --> D[IVC event - 45 deg ABDC]
    D --> E[Both valves closed - Compression stroke 180 deg]
    E --> F[Spark or Injection event - 30 deg BTDC SI or 15 deg BTDC CI]
    F --> G[Combustion and Power stroke 180 deg - TDC to BDC]
    G --> H[EVO event - 45 deg BBDC]
    H --> I[Exhaust stroke 180 deg - BDC to TDC]
    I --> J[EVC event - 15 deg ATDC]
    J --> K[VALVE OVERLAP window - both valves open]
    K --> A
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Conceptual Valve Timing Diagram (4-Stroke SI)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Cycle[Two Crank Revolutions - 720 degrees total]
        direction LR
        P1[Intake stroke 0 to 180] --> P2[Compression 180 to 360]
        P2 --> P3[Power 360 to 540]
        P3 --> P4[Exhaust 540 to 720]
    end
    subgraph IntV[INTake Valve Open - 240 deg total]
        IVO1[IVO 15 BTDC] --> IVO2[IVC 45 ABDC]
    end
    subgraph ExV[EXhaust Valve Open - 240 deg total]
        EVO1[EVO 45 BBDC] --> EVO2[EVC 15 ATDC]
    end
    subgraph OVL[Overlap window at TDC exhaust-end - 30 deg]
        OV1[EVC 15 ATDC] --> OV2[IVO 15 BTDC]
    end
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — 2-Stroke Port Timing Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Piston at TDC - both ports covered] --> S2[Exhaust Port uncovers - 55 BBDC]
    S2 --> S3[Blow-down phase - exhaust only]
    S3 --> S4[Transfer Port uncovers - 50 BBDC - scavenging begins]
    S4 --> S5[Piston at BDC - both ports fully open]
    S5 --> S6[Transfer Port covers - 50 ABDC]
    S6 --> S7[Exhaust Port covers - 55 ATDC]
    S7 --> S8[Compression continues to next TDC]
    S8 --> S1
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Functional Block Architecture of Valve Timing System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    CRANK[Crankshaft rotating at N rpm] --> CAM[Camshaft geared 1 colon 2 in 4-stroke]
    CAM --> IVCAM[Intake Camshaft Lobe]
    CAM --> EVCAM[Exhaust Camshaft Lobe]
    IVCAM --> IVR[Inlet Valve Rocker and Spring]
    IVR --> IVV[Intake Valve Stem and Head]
    EVCAM --> EVR[Exhaust Valve Rocker and Spring]
    EVR --> EVV[Exhaust Valve Stem and Head]
    IVV -. opens at 15 BTDC .-> CYL[Cylinder - Gas exchange]
    IVV -. closes at 45 ABDC .-> CYL
    EVV -. opens at 45 BBDC .-> CYL
    EVV -. closes at 15 ATDC .-> CYL
    CYL --> OVL[Overlap Window at TDC]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **In an actual KTU examination** you would draw a *polar/circular diagram* with the crank circle and arcs indicating valve-open intervals. Since plain Mermaid cannot render a true polar plot, the linear block diagrams above capture the same logical information. In the answer sheet, the examiner expects the *circular* version, drawn freehand.

### 4.5 Reference Sketch — Polar Valve Timing Diagram (Textual Description for Exam)

> **How to draw the polar VTD in the exam (15 marks question):**
> 1. Draw a circle. The centre is the crank axis.
> 2. Mark **TDC** at the top and **BDC** at the bottom.
> 3. Divide the circle into four quadrants of 180° each: Intake (right half top), Compression (right half bottom), Power (left half top), Exhaust (left half bottom) — *or* follow the convention used in your textbook.
> 4. From TDC (top), mark **15° counter-clockwise** (into the exhaust quadrant) — this is **IVO**.
> 5. From BDC (bottom), mark **45° clockwise** (into the compression quadrant) — this is **IVC**.
> 6. The arc from IVO to IVC represents the **intake valve open** duration (240°).
> 7. From BDC, mark **45° counter-clockwise** (into the power quadrant) — this is **EVO**.
> 8. From TDC, mark **15° clockwise** (into the intake quadrant) — this is **EVC**.
> 9. The small arc from EVC to IVO across the top is the **overlap** (30°).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, May 2024, Module 1, 3 marks]**

> Define the term **valve overlap** in a four-stroke internal combustion engine. State its typical value for a petrol engine and explain its significance.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Valve overlap is the angular interval at TDC (end of exhaust stroke / start of intake stroke) during which both the intake valve and the exhaust valve remain open simultaneously.

For a typical petrol (SI) engine, the overlap is **15° to 40° of crank rotation** (commonly around **30°**).

**Significance (2 marks):**
- It enables **scavenging** of residual exhaust gases by the incoming fresh charge.
- It provides an **internal EGR** effect, lowering peak combustion temperatures and thus reducing **NOx** formation.
- At high engine speeds, it improves **volumetric efficiency** by allowing incoming charge to take advantage of the inrushing exhaust gas momentum (ram effect).

*(Mark distribution: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for typical value, 1 mark for significance.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Dec 2023, Module 1, 3 marks]**

> List any **three** differences between the valve timing diagram of a 4-stroke petrol (SI) engine and a 4-stroke diesel (CI) engine.

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per difference):**

| # | SI Engine | CI Engine |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Spark occurs **30°–40° BTDC** | Fuel injection occurs **10°–20° BTDC** (no spark plug) |
| 2 | **IVC occurs later** (40°–50° ABDC) due to high ram effect from throttled operation | **IVC occurs earlier** (20°–40° ABDC) because CI is unthrottled and ram effect is small |
| 3 | Larger valve overlap (**20°–40°**) to reduce knock via internal EGR | Slightly smaller overlap (**20°–35°**) to avoid excess smoke and short-circuiting |

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A (14 marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Module 1]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **valve timing diagram of a 4-stroke petrol engine**. State typical values for all four events. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain why the intake valve **opens before TDC** and the exhaust valve **closes after TDC**. What is the effect of *increasing* the valve overlap on (i) volumetric efficiency, (ii) NOx emissions, and (iii) idle roughness? **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

*Sketch (3 marks):* Draw the polar/crank-angle diagram as described in Section 4.5. Show:
- IVO = **15° BTDC**
- IVC = **45° ABDC**
- EVO = **45° BBDC**
- EVC = **15° ATDC**
- Mark the **overlap** of 30° at TDC.
- Mark the **intake lag (β) = 45°** and **exhaust lead (γ) = 45°**.

*Explanation (3 marks):* State that one cycle of a 4-stroke engine spans **720°** of crank rotation, comprising the four strokes: intake (0°→180°), compression (180°→360°), power (360°→540°), exhaust (540°→720°). The intake valve is open from IVO to IVC (total 240°), the exhaust valve from EVO to EVC (total 240°).

*Typical values (1 mark):* As listed above, plus the **spark advance** of 30°–40° BTDC, which fires before TDC on the compression stroke.

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

*Why IVO before TDC (2 marks):* The intake manifold pressure is slightly above atmospheric. Opening the valve early allows the cylinder to begin filling **before** the piston starts descending, eliminating valve-lag and giving a fuller charge at high engine speeds.

*Why EVC after TDC (2 marks):* At TDC a small pocket of burnt gas remains in the clearance volume. Leaving the exhaust valve open allows the inrushing fresh intake charge to **scavenge** this residual gas, improving combustion quality and reducing HC and CO.

*Effects of increasing overlap (3 marks — 1 each):*
- **(i) Volumetric efficiency:** Initially **increases** at high engine speed (better scavenging, more fresh charge packed in), but eventually **decreases** at low speed due to reversion of fresh charge back into the intake manifold.
- **(ii) NOx emissions:** **Decreases** — more residual gas dilutes the charge, lowering peak flame temperature.
- **(iii) Idle roughness:** **Increases** — too much overlap at low speed causes the engine to breathe its own exhaust, leading to unstable combustion and rough idle.

---

### **Question B (14 marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2023, Module 1]**

**(a)** Sketch the **port timing diagram of a 2-stroke petrol engine** and state the events with typical crank angles. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 4-stroke petrol engine runs at **3000 rpm**. The intake valve opens **20° before TDC** and closes **50° after BDC**. Calculate:
  (i) the total crank-angle duration for which the intake valve remains open.
  (ii) the corresponding time duration.
  (iii) the mean piston speed, given stroke length L = **80 mm**. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

*Sketch (3 marks):* Draw a polar diagram spanning **360°** (one crank revolution for a 2-stroke). Mark the events:

| Event | Symbol | Typical Crank Angle |
|---|---|---|
| Exhaust Port Opens | EPO | **55° BBDC** |
| Transfer Port Opens | TPO | **50° BBDC** |
| Transfer Port Closes | TPC | **50° ABDC** |
| Exhaust Port Closes | EPC | **55° ATDC** |

*Explanation (3 marks):*
- From **TDC** the piston descends; the **exhaust port is uncovered first** at 55° BBDC, initiating blow-down.
- 5° later, the **transfer port is uncovered** at 50° BBDC, allowing fresh charge from the crankcase to enter and scavenge the burnt gas.
- At **BDC** both ports are fully open — peak scavenging flow.
- During the upward stroke, the **transfer port closes first** (50° ABDC), trapping fresh charge; the **exhaust port closes later** (55° ATDC), completing scavenging.
- Compression continues to the next TDC.

*Scavenging period (1 mark):* The scavenging period = EPO to TPC = **55° + 180° + 50° = 285°** (or the simultaneous-open period = 185°).

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**(i) Total crank-angle duration (2 marks):**

$$\theta_{open} = \text{IVO before TDC} + 180° + \text{IVC after BDC}$$

$$\theta_{open} = 20° + 180° + 50° = 250°$$

$$\boxed{\theta_{open} = 250° \text{ of crank rotation}}$$

**(ii) Time duration (3 marks):**

Using the relationship $t = \dfrac{\theta}{6N}$:

$$t = \dfrac{250}{6 \times 3000} = \dfrac{250}{18000}$$

$$\boxed{t = 1.389 \times 10^{-2} \text{ s} = 13.89 \text{ ms}}$$

**(iii) Mean piston speed (2 marks):**

For a 4-stroke engine:

$$\bar{V}_p = 2 \cdot L \cdot N$$

with $L = 80 \text{ mm} = 0.08 \text{ m}$ and $N = 3000/60 = 50$ rev/s:

$$\bar{V}_p = 2 \times 0.08 \times 50 = 8 \text{ m/s}$$

$$\boxed{\bar{V}_p = 8 \text{ m/s}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit confusion in mean piston speed:** Forgetting that *N* in $\bar{V}_p = 2LN$ must be in **rev/s**, not rpm. Many students write $N = 3000$ and get $\bar{V}_p = 480$ m/s — wrong by a factor of 60. Always divide rpm by 60.
> 2. **Crank-angle-to-time formula:** Students often write $t = \theta/\omega$ with $\omega$ in rad/s, but the problem gives $\theta$ in *degrees*. Use $t = \theta/(6N)$ to avoid the $\pi/180$ conversion trap.
> 3. **Mis-labelling overlap:** Valve overlap is *not* the total of IVO + IVC or EVO + EVC. It is specifically **IVO before TDC + EVC after TDC** — only the small window at TDC.
> 4. **Confusing 2-stroke and 4-stroke cycle length:** 2-stroke = **360°** total; 4-stroke = **720°** total. Mixing these gives factor-of-2 errors.
> 5. **Forgetting the spark/injection advance in the sketch:** Examiners deduct 1 mark if the SI/CI event is not shown on the diagram.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **4-stroke cycle = 720°**; **2-stroke cycle = 360°** of crank rotation.
- [ ] **Typical SI timings:** IVO 15° BTDC | IVC 45° ABDC | EVO 45° BBDC | EVC 15° ATDC.
- [ ] **Typical CI timings:** IVC earlier (20°–40° ABDC); fuel injection 10°–20° BTDC; no spark plug.
- [ ] **Valve overlap = IVO(BTDC) + EVC(ATDC)** — typically 20°–40°; maximum overlap = best scavenging, but worst idle.
- [ ] **Why overlap exists:** (1) Scavenge residual exhaust, (2) Internal EGR reduces NOx, (3) Use exhaust gas momentum for ram charging.
- [ ] **2-stroke port timing:** TPO ≈ 50° BBDC, TPC ≈ 50° ABDC, EPO ≈ 55° BBDC, EPC ≈ 55° ATDC; scavenging period ≈ 285°.
- [ ] **Crank-angle to time:** $t = \theta / (6N)$ where $\theta$ is in degrees and $N$ is in rpm.
- [ ] **Mean piston speed (4-stroke):** $\bar{V}_p = 2 L N$ with *N* in rev/s.
- [ ] **Intake lag (β)** = IVC − BDC; **Exhaust lead (γ)** = BDC − EVO; both improve volumetric efficiency.
- [ ] **CI engines are unthrottled** — this is the root cause of differences from SI timings.
- [ ] **Reading a VTD:** clockwise convention, TDC at top, BDC at bottom, valve-open arcs between the two events.
- [ ] **In the exam:** always draw the *circular* polar diagram freehand, label all four events, and explicitly state the *reason* for each angular advance/retard (this is where most marks are won).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inlet and Exhaust Manifold
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT205 - AUTOMOBILE POWER PLANT (AUTOMOBILE ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Inlet and Exhaust Manifold

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Inlet Manifold (Intake Manifold)

**Formal Definition:**
The **Inlet Manifold** (also called the **Intake Manifold**) is a critical engine component whose primary function is to uniformly distribute the air (or air-fuel mixture, in the case of a PFI gasoline engine) from the fuel delivery system to the individual cylinders of a multi-cylinder internal combustion engine. It acts as an aerodynamic plenum chamber and a runner system that conditions the charge before it enters the combustion chamber.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In a **Diesel (CI) engine**, the inlet manifold supplies **only air**. In a **Petrol (SI) engine with PFI (Port Fuel Injection)**, it supplies air only, with fuel injected at the port. In a **Conventional Carburetted SI engine**, the inlet manifold supplies a **pre-mixed air-fuel charge**.

### 1.2 Exhaust Manifold

**Formal Definition:**
The **Exhaust Manifold** is the component that collects the burnt exhaust gases from the individual cylinder exhaust ports immediately after the exhaust valve opens, and channels them into a single outlet that connects to the exhaust pipe, catalytic converter, and muffler. It operates under high-temperature, high-pressure, and pulsating flow conditions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Constants Used in Manifold Design:**
> - Speed of Sound in Air (20°C): **a = 343 m/s**
> - Speed of Sound in Exhaust Gas (300°C): **a ≈ 520 m/s**
> - Gas Constant for Air: **R = 287 J/(kg·K)**

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Lung and Throat" of the Engine

Imagine the engine as a human body performing intense exercise. The **inlet manifold** is like the **trachea and bronchial tubes** — it is the airway that calmly takes a deep breath (air) and gently guides it deep into multiple identical chambers (the lungs/cylinders). If the airway is too narrow, twisted, or has sharp bends, the person will pant and gasp for air. Similarly, if the inlet manifold has poor design, the engine "chokes" and loses power.

The **exhaust manifold**, on the other hand, is like the **exhalation system** of a marathon runner. It must rapidly and efficiently expel "used air" (burnt gases) out of the lungs (cylinders) to make room for the next breath. If the exhaust cannot leave quickly, the runner cannot inhale again — meaning the engine cannot fill properly for the next cycle, a phenomenon called **residual gas contamination**.

---

### 1.4 Functional Objectives of Manifolds

| Manifold | Primary Objective | Secondary Objectives |
|----------|------------------|----------------------|
| **Inlet** | Distribute charge equally to all cylinders | Maximize volumetric efficiency; minimize pressure drop; act as a silencer for intake noise; provide fuel/air mixing (carburetted) or fuel atomization zone (PFI) |
| **Exhaust** | Collect and expel burnt gases rapidly | Reduce back-pressure; suppress exhaust noise; recover exhaust gas energy (turbine drive); protect surrounding components from heat |

---

### 1.5 Basic Anatomy of a Manifold System

- **Inlet Manifold Components:** Plenum chamber, primary runner, secondary runner, runner taper, bell-mouth entry, flange (cylinder head side), throttle body mount (SI engines).
- **Exhaust Manifold Components:** Header pipes, collector, primary tube, secondary tube, flanges, heat shields, oxygen sensor boss (post-manifold), EGR (Exhaust Gas Recirculation) port.

---

### 1.6 Why is this Topic Crucial for KTU Examinations?

> [!NOTE]
> Manifold design is a **favourite KTU ESE question area** because:
> 1. It directly links to **Volumetric Efficiency (η_v)** — a core 2-mark/3-mark/14-mark concept.
> 2. It involves **pulse tuning**, **Helmholtz resonance**, and **ram effect** — all of which are derivable topics.
> 3. It bridges thermodynamics (pressure waves) and practical design (casting, materials, layout).

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Inlet Manifold — Design Theory and Operation

#### 2.1.1 Types of Inlet Manifolds (KTU-Relevant Classification)

**1. Single-Barrel (Single-Pipe) Inlet Manifold**
- Used in older, low-RPM engines.
- One common pipe feeds all cylinders.
- **Limitation:** Unequal distribution; large pressure pulsations.

**2. Multi-Barrel (Dual/Quad-Barrel) Inlet Manifold**
- Two or four separate runners fed from a common plenum.
- Each runner is opened/closed by a butterfly valve (e.g., two-stage manifold in 4-barrel carburettors).
- Improves **part-throttle efficiency** and **WOT (Wide Open Throttle) breathing**.

**3. Plenum-Type (Common Chamber) Inlet Manifold**
- A large-volume plenum chamber acts as a **pressurization reservoir**.
- Each cylinder draws from the plenum via individual runners.
- **Best for:** Naturally aspirated engines with multiple cylinders.

**4. Tuned (Long-Runner / Short-Runner) Inlet Manifold**
- Runner length is **deliberately tuned** to a specific engine RPM using the **acoustic resonance principle**.
- Long runners → low-end torque; Short runners → high-end power.

**5. Ram-Induction Manifold (Ram-Tube)**
- A straight, tapered, polished tube that exploits the **ram effect** (inertial air ramming at high speeds).
- Used in **motorcycle and racing engines** at high RPM.

#### 2.1.2 Inlet Manifold Design Variables

- **Runner Length (L):** Critical for tuning resonance.
- **Runner Cross-Section Area (A):** Sized to avoid choking at peak flow.
- **Plenum Volume (V_p):** Typically 1.5× to 2× the total displacement for a naturally aspirated engine.
- **Surface Finish:** Polished runners reduce **friction losses** by up to **15 %** at high RPM.
- **Pre-heating (for SI carburetted engines):** Exhaust-heated inlet manifold prevents fuel droplet formation on cold walls.

#### 2.1.3 Pulsation Effects in Inlet Manifold

When the intake valve opens, the piston draws in a charge at near-atmospheric pressure, but upon closing, the in-rushing air has inertia and creates a **pressure wave**. This wave travels up the runner, reflects from the plenum (closed end → high pressure pulse), and returns. If timed correctly, the reflected **high-pressure pulse** arrives at the intake valve precisely as it opens for the next cycle, giving a "ram" effect that boosts volumetric efficiency.

### 2.2 Exhaust Manifold — Design Theory and Operation

#### 2.2.1 Types of Exhaust Manifolds

**1. Log-Type (Cast-Iron) Exhaust Manifold**
- Simple cast design, low cost.
- All primary tubes merge into a single short collector.
- **Drawback:** Tubes are unequal length → unequal scavenging.

**2. Tubular (Header) Exhaust Manifold**
- Made of **equal-length** steel tubes welded together.
- Each primary pipe serves one cylinder.
- **Primary tube length is tuned** to match the firing order.
- Used in performance vehicles.

**3. Siamesed Exhaust Manifold**
- Two cylinders share one port (common in V-twin motorcycles and 2-stroke engines).
- **Disadvantage:** Reverse-flow scavenging interference.

**4. Three- or Four-into-One (4-1) Header**
- Four primaries of equal length merge into one collector.
- Maximizes exhaust scavenging at high RPM.

#### 2.2.2 Critical Functions of the Exhaust Manifold

- **Scavenging:** Use of exhaust pulse energy to actively draw out residual gases.
- **Pressure Wave Tuning:** Tail-pipe reflections arrive back at the exhaust valve at the correct time to assist in-cylinder blowdown.
- **Thermal Management:** Dissipate heat; in turbocharged applications, feed the turbine.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Conceptual Point:** The exhaust manifold is the **hottest** component in the engine bay, with peak temperatures reaching **870 °C (1600 °F)** during full-load operation. Material choice (cast iron, stainless steel, Inconel) is dictated by this thermal load.

### 2.3 The Acoustic Resonance Theory (Pulse Tuning)

The **tuned length** of an intake or exhaust runner is derived from the principle of **quarter-wave standing waves** in a closed-open pipe (intake: open at valve, closed at plenum; exhaust: open at port, closed at head-pipe end or vice versa).

The fundamental **resonance frequency** of a pipe is:

$$f_1 = \frac{a}{4L}$$

where:
- $f_1$ = fundamental resonance frequency (Hz)
- $a$ = local speed of sound (m/s)
- $L$ = runner length (m)

The corresponding **resonance engine RPM** at which volumetric efficiency peaks is:

$$N_{res} = \frac{60 \cdot f_1}{n/2}$$

where $n$ = number of cylinders (for a 4-stroke, the valve event occurs once every 2 revolutions).

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **directly examinable** in KTU 2024 ESE and internal assessments.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variable Definitions | Standard Units |
|---|---------|---------|----------------------|----------------|
| 1 | Speed of sound in ideal gas | $a = \sqrt{\gamma R T}$ | $\gamma$ = ratio of specific heats, $R$ = gas constant, $T$ = absolute temperature | m/s |
| 2 | Resonance frequency (quarter wave) | $f_1 = a / (4L)$ | $a$ = sound speed, $L$ = runner length | Hz |
| 3 | Resonance engine RPM (4-stroke) | $N_{res} = 120 f_1 / n$ | $n$ = number of cylinders | rpm |
| 4 | Resonance engine RPM (2-stroke) | $N_{res} = 60 f_1 / n$ | — | rpm |
| 5 | Volumetric efficiency | $\eta_v = m_{actual} / m_{ideal}$ | — | dimensionless (or %) |
| 6 | Manifold pressure drop (Hagen-Poiseuille approx.) | $\Delta P = 32 \mu L v / d^2$ | $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity, $v$ = velocity | Pa |
| 7 | Pressure drop (Darcy-Weisbach) | $\Delta P = f (L/d) (\rho v^2/2)$ | $f$ = friction factor, $\rho$ = density | Pa |
| 8 | Mean piston speed | $\overline{V_p} = 2 L_s N / 60$ | $L_s$ = stroke length, $N$ = rpm | m/s |
| 9 | Air mass flow rate (ideal) | $\dot{m} = \eta_v \rho_a V_d N / (2 \cdot 60)$ | $\rho_a$ = air density, $V_d$ = displacement | kg/s |
| 10 | Ram-effect velocity gain | $\Delta V_{ram} = V_{piston} - V_{charge}$ | — | m/s |
| 11 | Helmholtz resonator frequency | $f_H = (a/2\pi) \sqrt{A / (V L_{eff})}$ | $A$ = neck area, $V$ = cavity volume, $L_{eff}$ = effective neck length | Hz |
| 12 | Tuning temperature correction | $L_{tuned} = a_{ref} / (4 f_{res}) \cdot \sqrt{T_{op}/T_{ref}}$ | $T_{op}$ = operating temperature | m |
| 13 | Back-pressure (simplified) | $P_{back} = P_{atm} + \Delta P_{pipe} + \Delta P_{silencer}$ | — | Pa |
| 14 | Firing interval (4-stroke) | $\theta_{fire} = 720°/n$ | $n$ = cylinders | degrees |
| 15 | Pulsation frequency | $f_p = n N / (120)$ | — | Hz |

> **Critical LaTeX Note:** Absolute values / divisibility are written as `\vert x \vert` or `x \mid 2` to prevent markdown table corruption.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Significance

- **Performance Tuning:** Tuned intake runners can boost low-end torque by **15–25 %** without any mechanical change to the engine.
- **Emissions:** Manifold design directly affects **HC and CO emissions** by influencing charge homogeneity and EGR distribution.
- **Turbocharging:** The exhaust manifold feeds a turbocharger's turbine; even a 1 % improvement in manifold efficiency can yield 2–3 % more boost.
- **NVH (Noise, Vibration, Harshness):** Inlet manifold acts as a **Helmholtz silencer**, damping induction roar.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Tuned Runner Length for Maximum Volumetric Efficiency

**Problem Context:**
A 4-cylinder, 4-stroke SI engine has a target peak torque at **3000 rpm**. The air temperature at the back of the intake valve is **45 °C (318 K)**. Assuming the intake runner behaves as a quarter-wave resonator (closed at plenum, open at valve), determine the **optimum runner length** to enhance volumetric efficiency at this RPM.

**Given:**
- $N_{target} = 3000$ rpm
- $T_{runner} = 318$ K
- $n = 4$ cylinders
- 4-stroke engine
- $\gamma = 1.4$ for air
- $R = 287$ J/(kg·K)

---

**Step 1: Calculate the local speed of sound in the hot runner.**

Using the fundamental thermodynamic relation for the speed of sound in an ideal gas:

$$a = \sqrt{\gamma R T}$$

Substituting the values:

$$a = \sqrt{1.4 \times 287 \times 318}$$

Compute the inner product step-by-step:

$$1.4 \times 287 = 401.8$$

$$401.8 \times 318 = 127,772.4$$

$$\sqrt{127,772.4} \approx 357.45 \text{ m/s}$$

So, $a \approx 357.5$ m/s at the operating temperature.

---

**Step 2: Relate the target RPM to the resonance frequency.**

For a 4-stroke engine with $n$ cylinders, the number of power strokes per minute per cylinder is $N/2$. The total induction events (valve openings) per second across all cylinders is:

$$f_v = \frac{n N}{2 \times 60} = \frac{n N}{120}$$

For the runner to **resonate** in sync with the induction event of a particular cylinder (and feed maximum pressure pulse back to that valve), the resonance frequency $f_1$ of the runner should equal $f_v$.

$$f_1 = \frac{n N}{120} = \frac{4 \times 3000}{120} = 100 \text{ Hz}$$

---

**Step 3: Apply the quarter-wave resonance condition.**

For a closed-open pipe (closed at plenum, open at valve), the fundamental mode is:

$$f_1 = \frac{a}{4 L}$$

Solving for the runner length $L$:

$$L = \frac{a}{4 f_1}$$

Substituting the values:

$$L = \frac{357.45}{4 \times 100} = \frac{357.45}{400}$$

$$L = 0.8936 \text{ m} \approx 893.6 \text{ mm}$$

---

**Step 4: Engineering sanity check and physical packaging.**

A 893 mm runner is **impractical** for a small car engine. The standard interpretation is that the **second harmonic** (or even third) of the runner may be exploited. For the second harmonic in a quarter-wave pipe:

$$f_2 = \frac{3a}{4L}$$

Then the runner length that resonates at 100 Hz in the 2nd harmonic:

$$L_2 = \frac{3a}{4 f_2} = \frac{3 \times 357.45}{400} \approx 2680 \text{ mm}$$

This is even longer. Hence, the **first harmonic on a 2-pulse system** (where the runner serves two cylinders in a paired firing arrangement) is commonly used in practice, which halves the effective length:

$$L_{practical} = \frac{a}{8 f_v} \approx 447 \text{ mm}$$

This is a feasible length and is used in production intake manifold design.

---

**Final Result:**

| Mode | Resonant Runner Length |
|------|-----------------------|
| 1st harmonic, single-pulse | 893.6 mm |
| 2nd harmonic | 2680 mm |
| **1st harmonic, paired pulse (practical)** | **~447 mm** |

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Pressure Drop Across a Manifold Runner (Darcy-Weisbach)

**Problem Context:**
Air at $\rho = 1.2$ kg/m³ flows through a 350 mm long, 32 mm diameter intake runner at an average velocity of 60 m/s. The friction factor is $f = 0.022$ (smooth pipe, turbulent). Compute the **pressure drop** through the runner.

**Given:**
- $L = 0.350$ m
- $d = 0.032$ m
- $v = 60$ m/s
- $\rho = 1.2$ kg/m³
- $f = 0.022$

---

**Step 1: Write the Darcy-Weisbach equation.**

$$\Delta P = f \cdot \frac{L}{d} \cdot \frac{\rho v^2}{2}$$

---

**Step 2: Compute the dynamic pressure term.**

$$\frac{\rho v^2}{2} = \frac{1.2 \times 60^2}{2} = \frac{1.2 \times 3600}{2} = \frac{4320}{2} = 2160 \text{ Pa}$$

---

**Step 3: Compute the L/d ratio.**

$$\frac{L}{d} = \frac{0.350}{0.032} = 10.9375$$

---

**Step 4: Multiply all terms.**

$$\Delta P = 0.022 \times 10.9375 \times 2160$$

First: $0.022 \times 10.9375 = 0.240625$

Then: $0.240625 \times 2160 = 519.75$ Pa

---

**Step 5: Express as a percentage of atmospheric pressure.**

$$\% \Delta P = \frac{519.75}{101325} \times 100 \approx 0.513 \%$$

---

**Final Result:**

| Quantity | Value |
|----------|-------|
| $\Delta P$ | **≈ 519.75 Pa** |
| $\% \Delta P$ | **≈ 0.51 % of atmospheric** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Implication:** A pressure drop of just 0.5 % reduces volumetric efficiency by approximately the same percentage, costing ~0.5 % of peak torque. **Polishing the runner surface** reduces $f$ by ~20 % and recovers ~0.1 %.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Mean Piston Speed for Manifold Sizing

**Problem Context:**
A 4-cylinder engine has a bore of 75 mm and a stroke of 90 mm. Maximum rated speed is 5500 rpm. Compute the **mean piston speed**, which dictates the volumetric flow rate the manifold must deliver.

---

**Step 1: Recall the definition.**

$$\overline{V_p} = \frac{2 L_s N}{60}$$

where $L_s$ is the stroke.

---

**Step 2: Substitute values.**

$$\overline{V_p} = \frac{2 \times 0.090 \times 5500}{60}$$

$$= \frac{0.180 \times 5500}{60}$$

$$= \frac{990}{60} = 16.5 \text{ m/s}$$

---

**Step 3: Interpretation.**

For a passenger-car petrol engine, $\overline{V_p} \approx 15$ to 22 m/s. Our 16.5 m/s indicates a **moderately high-speed engine**, requiring a generously sized manifold.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Design Trade-offs

| Design Parameter | Increased Value → Effect on Torque (Low RPM) | Effect on Torque (High RPM) | Effect on Emissions | Effect on Cost |
|------------------|-------------------------------------------|------------------------------|---------------------|----------------|
| Runner length (L) | ↑ (longer = more low-end) | ↓ (shorter = more high-end) | Slight worsening at extremes | Low |
| Runner diameter (d) | ↓ (smaller = more velocity) | ↑ (larger = more flow) | Minor | Low |
| Plenum volume (V_p) | ↑ (larger = damping) | ↓ (smaller = response) | Slight worsening | Medium |
| Surface polish | ↑ (lower friction) | ↑ | Slight improvement | Medium |
| Number of runners | Single → multi = better at low | — | Major improvement | High |
| Manifold material (al vs CI) | Aluminium = cooler charge = denser | — | Improved HC/CO | High |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]**
*What is the function of an exhaust manifold? List any three materials commonly used for its construction.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Function:** The exhaust manifold collects the high-temperature burnt exhaust gases from the individual cylinders of a multi-cylinder engine immediately after the exhaust valve opens and channels them into a single exhaust pipe leading to the catalytic converter and muffler.

**Three materials used:**
1. **Cast Iron** — low cost, good high-temperature strength, used in mass-market cars.
2. **Stainless Steel (SS 304 / SS 321)** — used in tubular headers, better thermal-cycling resistance.
3. **Inconel / High-Nickel Alloys** — used in turbocharged and motorsport applications for extreme-temperature capability.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
*Differentiate between a 'log-type' and a 'tubular (header)' exhaust manifold.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Log-Type Manifold | Tubular (Header) Manifold |
|--------|-------------------|----------------------------|
| Construction | Single cast block with integral passages | Welded steel tubes (equal length) |
| Primary length | Unequal across cylinders | Equal and **tuned** |
| Cost | Low | High |
| Scavenging | Poor | Excellent |
| Application | Mass-market cars | Performance / sports cars |
| Weight | Heavy | Light |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 1, 14 Marks]**
*(a)* Explain the **function and design requirements of an inlet manifold** in a multi-cylinder SI engine. Describe the **tuned intake manifold** principle with a neat sketch. **(7 marks)**

*(b)* A 4-cylinder, 4-stroke petrol engine operates at a peak torque speed of **2800 rpm**. The air temperature at the back of the intake valve is **50 °C**. Assuming a quarter-wave resonator and a single-pulse runner, determine the **optimum runner length** to maximise volumetric efficiency. Take $\gamma = 1.4$ and $R = 287$ J/(kg·K). **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to Q-A (a): [7 marks]**

**Functions of Inlet Manifold (3 marks):**
- Uniformly distribute air (or air-fuel mixture) to all cylinders.
- Provide adequate **plenum volume** to damp intake pulsations.
- Act as a **silencer** for induction noise (Helmholtz damping).
- In carburetted engines, provide a region for **fuel atomisation and vaporisation**.

**Design Requirements (2 marks):**
- Minimum pressure drop (polished internal surface, smooth bends).
- Equal runner length for uniform distribution.
- Adequate plenum volume (typically **1.5× to 2× total displacement** for NA engines).
- Pre-heating provision (carburetted SI engines) to prevent fuel droplet wall condensation.

**Tuned Intake Manifold Principle (2 marks):**
A tuned intake runner is sized so that the **pressure wave reflected from the plenum** returns to the intake valve at the precise moment it opens for the next cycle. This delivers a **high-pressure pulse** that rams extra air into the cylinder, raising volumetric efficiency. The principle exploits **quarter-wave standing-wave resonance**, with the runner length derived as:

$$L = \frac{a}{4 f_1}$$

This is illustrated in the resonance block diagram in **Section 4.2** of these notes.

---

**Solution to Q-A (b): [7 marks]**

**Step 1: Operating temperature in Kelvin.**

$$T = 50 + 273 = 323 \text{ K}$$

**Step 2: Local speed of sound.**

$$a = \sqrt{\gamma R T} = \sqrt{1.4 \times 287 \times 323}$$

$$1.4 \times 287 = 401.8$$

$$401.8 \times 323 = 129,781.4$$

$$a = \sqrt{129,781.4} \approx 360.25 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3: Induction event frequency.**

$$f_v = \frac{n N}{120} = \frac{4 \times 2800}{120} = \frac{11200}{120} = 93.33 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 4: Runner length for first-harmonic quarter-wave resonance.**

$$L = \frac{a}{4 f_v} = \frac{360.25}{4 \times 93.33} = \frac{360.25}{373.33}$$

$$L \approx 0.965 \text{ m} = 965 \text{ mm}$$

**Final Answer: $L \approx 965$ mm (single-pulse, 1st harmonic)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students commonly forget to convert the temperature to **Kelvin** before applying the speed-of-sound equation. A mark is deducted. Also, using $N/60$ (per second) instead of $nN/120$ (per-cylinder, 4-stroke) for induction frequency is a frequent error. Always confirm: 4-stroke valves open **once per 2 revolutions**.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 1, 14 Marks]**
*(a)* With the aid of a schematic, explain the **principle of pulse tuning in an exhaust manifold**. Discuss the role of **equal-length primary tubes** and the **collector** in performance engines. **(7 marks)**

*(b)* A 6-cylinder, 4-stroke engine has a bore of 80 mm, stroke of 85 mm, and runs at a maximum speed of 6000 rpm. The intake runner has a length of 400 mm and an average temperature of 60 °C. Calculate (i) the mean piston speed, (ii) the resonance frequency of the runner, and (iii) the engine RPM at which the runner is in resonance. Take $\gamma = 1.4$, $R = 287$ J/(kg·K). **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to Q-B (a): [7 marks]**

**Principle of Pulse Tuning (3 marks):**
When the exhaust valve opens, a high-pressure pulse travels down the primary tube toward the collector. At the collector (a region of larger cross-section), part of the pulse **reflects back** as a rarefaction (low-pressure) wave. If the primary tube length is tuned such that this reflected **low-pressure wave** arrives back at the exhaust valve just as it opens for the next cycle, it actively **draws out (scavenges)** the residual exhaust gas, allowing more fresh charge to enter. This is the principle of **exhaust pulse scavenging**.

**Role of Equal-Length Primaries (2 marks):**
- All cylinders must experience **identical scavenging** conditions.
- Equal lengths ensure that reflected waves arrive at the correct timing for every cylinder's valve event.
- Unequal lengths cause pulse interference (one cylinder's reflected pulse collides destructively with another's outgoing pulse).

**Role of Collector (2 marks):**
- Acts as a **reflection node** for pressure waves.
- Size and volume of the collector must be matched to primary length and firing order.
- A collector that is too large dampens the reflection; too small causes cross-fire interference.

The schematic is captured in the Mermaid diagram in **Section 4.3**.

---

**Solution to Q-B (b): [7 marks]**

**Step 1: Mean piston speed.**

$$\overline{V_p} = \frac{2 L_s N}{60} = \frac{2 \times 0.085 \times 6000}{60}$$

$$= \frac{0.170 \times 6000}{60} = \frac{1020}{60} = 17.0 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2: Local speed of sound in hot runner.**

$$T = 60 + 273 = 333 \text{ K}$$

$$a = \sqrt{1.4 \times 287 \times 333} = \sqrt{1.4 \times 287 \times 333}$$

Compute: $1.4 \times 287 = 401.8$

$401.8 \times 333 = 133,799.4$

$a = \sqrt{133,799.4} \approx 365.78$ m/s

**Step 3: Quarter-wave resonance frequency of the runner.**

$$f_1 = \frac{a}{4L} = \frac{365.78}{4 \times 0.400} = \frac{365.78}{1.6} = 228.6 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 4: Resonant engine RPM (4-stroke, n = 6).**

$$N_{res} = \frac{120 f_1}{n} = \frac{120 \times 228.6}{6} = \frac{27,432}{6} \approx 4572 \text{ rpm}$$

**Final Answers:**

| Quantity | Value |
|----------|-------|
| Mean piston speed $\overline{V_p}$ | **17.0 m/s** |
| Runner resonance frequency $f_1$ | **≈ 228.6 Hz** |
| Resonant engine RPM $N_{res}$ | **≈ 4572 rpm** |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** A common error is to apply the **2-stroke formula** ($60 f / n$) by mistake on a 4-stroke engine. The factor for 4-stroke is **120** (because of the 2-revolution cycle), not 60. This single mark loss is the most frequent in KTU ESE numerical answers.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use this as a 60-second pre-exam revision block.**

- [ ] Inlet manifold supplies **air** (Diesel / PFI Petrol) or **air-fuel mixture** (Carburetted Petrol).
- [ ] Exhaust manifold operates at **600–870 °C** — material choice is critical (CI, SS, Inconel).
- [ ] **Tuned runner length** uses the quarter-wave formula: $L = a / (4 f_1)$.
- [ ] **Resonance RPM (4-stroke)**: $N_{res} = 120 f_1 / n$.
- [ ] **Resonance RPM (2-stroke)**: $N_{res} = 60 f_1 / n$.
- [ ] **Long runners** → low-end torque; **short runners** → high-end power.
- [ ] **Plenum volume** of NA engine ≈ 1.5× to 2× total displacement.
- [ ] **Tubular header** = equal length primaries, welded, performance-oriented.
- [ ] **Log-type manifold** = cast iron, unequal lengths, low-cost, low-performance.
- [ ] **Exhaust pulse tuning** uses reflected rarefaction wave to scavenge residual gas.
- [ ] Speed of sound formula: $a = \sqrt{\gamma R T}$ — always convert **T to Kelvin**.
- [ ] Mean piston speed formula: $\overline{V_p} = 2 L_s N / 60$.
- [ ] **Don't confuse** $N$ (rpm) with $n$ (number of cylinders).
- [ ] **Firing interval (4-stroke)** = $720°/n$ — important for collector design.
- [ ] **Helmholtz resonator** in manifold helps damp induction noise.
- [ ] In a turbocharged engine, exhaust manifold **directly feeds** the turbine — design affects turbo spool time.
- [ ] **Aluminium inlet manifold** gives cooler, denser charge (≈ 5 % η_v benefit over cast iron).
- [ ] **Plenum-type manifold** is best for naturally aspirated multi-cylinder engines.
- [ ] **Ram effect** = inertial supercharging at high intake velocities.
- [ ] **Pressure drop** through manifold = direct loss in volumetric efficiency.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Air Fuel Mixture Requirements and Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT205 - AUTOMOBILE POWER PLANT (AUTOMOBILE ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Air Fuel Mixture Requirements and Types

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Air-Fuel Mixture (AFM):** In an internal combustion (IC) engine, the air-fuel mixture is a homogeneous (or near-homogeneous) blend of air and fuel prepared in a definite proportion before being inducted into the combustion chamber. The ratio of the mass of air to the mass of fuel in this mixture is termed the **Air-Fuel Ratio (AFR)**.

$$\text{AFR} = \frac{m_{\text{air}}}{m_{\text{fuel}}}$$

The **Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio** is the chemically ideal (theoretical) ratio at which all the fuel and all the oxygen in the air are completely consumed during combustion, leaving no excess of either reactant. For petrol (assumed iso-octane C₈H₁₈), the stoichiometric AFR is **14.7 : 1**. For diesel (assumed C₁₂H₂₃), it is approximately **14.5 : 1**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Cooking Pan Analogy

Imagine you are cooking rice on a gas stove. The **rice is the fuel** and the **oxygen in the air is the flame's oxidizer**. If you supply just the right amount of rice to burn completely with the available flame, the cooking is *stoichiometric* — efficient and clean. If you add too much rice (fuel-rich), some rice remains unburned (soot/smoke in an engine). If you add too little rice (fuel-lean), there is leftover flame/oxygen and the food is under-cooked (heat lost in exhaust in an engine). An automobile engine must constantly adjust this "recipe" because the "appetite" of the engine (load, speed, temperature) keeps changing.

### 1.3 Key Terminology Standardized to KTU 2024 Syllabus

| Term | Notation | Meaning |
|------|----------|---------|
| Stoichiometric AFR | (A/F)ₛₜₒᵢcₕ | Theoretical ratio for complete combustion |
| Actual AFR | (A/F)ₐcₜᵤₐₗ | Real ratio supplied to engine |
| Relative AFR | λ (lambda) | (A/F)ₐcₜᵤₐₗ / (A/F)ₛₜₒᵢcₕ |
| Equivalence Ratio | φ (phi) | 1 / λ |
| Excess Air Factor | e | Mass of air supplied / mass of stoichiometric air |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL KTU DISTINCTION:** *Relative Air-Fuel Ratio (λ)* and *Equivalence Ratio (φ)* are reciprocals of each other. When λ = 1, φ = 1 (stoichiometric). When λ < 1, φ > 1 (rich mixture). When λ > 1, φ < 1 (lean mixture).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **STOICHIOMETRIC AFR QUICK VALUES (must memorize):**
> - Gasoline (C₈H₁₈) → **14.7 : 1**
> - Diesel (C₁₂H₂₃) → **14.5 : 1**
> - Methanol (CH₃OH) → 6.4 : 1
> - Ethanol (C₂H₅OH) → 9.0 : 1
> - LPG (C₃H₈) → 15.6 : 1
> - Hydrogen (H₂) → 34.3 : 1
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Classification of Air-Fuel Mixtures

A mixture is classified by comparing its AFR to the stoichiometric value. There are three fundamental types:

1. **Stoichiometric (Chemically Correct) Mixture** — AFR = 14.7 : 1 for petrol. All fuel + all O₂ consumed. Used in modern vehicles with **3-way catalytic converters**.
2. **Rich Mixture** — AFR < 14.7 : 1. Excess fuel. Cooler combustion, more power, higher HC and CO emissions.
3. **Lean Mixture** — AFR > 14.7 : 1. Excess air. Hotter combustion, better fuel economy, higher NOₓ emissions.

### 2.2 Why Mixture Requirements Vary (The Engineering "Why")

An engine is not a steady-state device. From the moment of cold start to high-speed cruising, the engine passes through drastically different thermodynamic and mechanical conditions. Each condition demands a *different* AFR for optimum performance. The reasons are:

- **Volumetric Efficiency (η_v)** varies with throttle position and RPM, changing the mass of air inducted.
- **In-cylinder Temperature** changes with load — affecting fuel vaporization and combustion timing.
- **Combustion Pressure Rise Rate** must be controlled to avoid engine knock.
- **Exhaust Emissions** (HC, CO, NOₓ) are highly sensitive to local AFR.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Print and Pin on Your Wall)

| # | Formula / Expression | Definition | Units |
|---|----------------------|------------|-------|
| 1 | (A/F) = m_air / m_fuel | Air-Fuel Ratio | dimensionless |
| 2 | (F/A) = 1 / (A/F) | Fuel-Air Ratio | dimensionless |
| 3 | λ = (A/F)ₐcₜᵤₐₗ / (A/F)ₛₜₒᵢcₕ | Relative Air-Fuel Ratio | dimensionless |
| 4 | φ = 1 / λ = (F/A)ₐcₜᵤₐₗ / (F/A)ₛₜₒᵢcₕ | Equivalence Ratio | dimensionless |
| 5 | e = m_air_actual / m_air_stoich | Excess Air Factor | dimensionless |
| 6 | m_air,stoich = 4.76 × (32/12) × C% + 4.76 × (32/4) × H% - 4.76 × (32/32) × O% | Theoretical air per kg fuel (by mass balance) | kg/kg |
| 7 | η_comb = (m_fuel_actual_burned / m_fuel_supplied) × 100 | Combustion Efficiency | % |
| 8 | BSFC = m_fuel / (P_brake × t) | Brake Specific Fuel Consumption | kg/kWh |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Memory Trick:** Whenever you see a numerical problem on AFR, immediately translate it into **lambda (λ)** language. This instantly tells you whether the mixture is rich, lean, or stoichiometric without re-calculating each time.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The air-fuel mixture science directly governs:
- **Electronic Control Units (ECUs)** in modern cars — they maintain λ = 1.00 ± 0.01 across all operating conditions.
- **Closed-loop feedback** via the **Lambda (O₂) sensor** in the exhaust manifold.
- **Catalytic converter efficiency** — 3-way catalysts only function when λ is very close to 1.000.
- **Knock prevention** in turbocharged engines (retarding timing during lean burn phases).
- **Emission compliance** with **BS-VI / Euro 6** norms.

### 2.5 Mixture Requirements at Various Operating Conditions

| Operating Condition | Required AFR (Gasoline) | Mixture Type | Engineering Reason |
|---------------------|--------------------------|--------------|---------------------|
| Cold Starting | **1 : 1 to 8 : 1** (very rich) | Extremely Rich | Fuel condenses on cold manifold walls; extra fuel compensates |
| Idling | **12 : 1 to 13 : 1** | Slightly Rich | Low RPM, residual gases present, need stable combustion |
| Cruising (Light Load) | **16 : 1 to 18 : 1** | Lean | Best fuel economy, low pumping losses |
| Acceleration (WOT) | **11 : 1 to 12 : 1** | Rich | Maximum power, prevents detonation/knock |
| Maximum Power | **12 : 1 to 13 : 1** | Rich | Extra fuel cools intake valves and cylinder |
| Best Economy | **15.5 : 1 to 16 : 1** | Slightly Lean | Maximum thermal efficiency at part throttle |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **BS-VI NORM REQUIREMENT:** Modern passenger cars maintain λ = 1.000 ± 0.020 (AFR = 14.5 to 14.9) under all closed-loop conditions to satisfy the 3-way catalytic converter.

### 2.6 Air-Fuel Requirements — SI vs CI Engines

**Spark Ignition (SI/Gasoline) Engines:**
- Use *pre-mixed* homogeneous mixture prepared in the intake manifold.
- AFR range: **11 : 1 to 18 : 1** (wide operational range).
- Quality of mixture preparation is critical for smooth combustion.
- Throttle controls the *quantity* of mixture entering the cylinder.

**Compression Ignition (CI/Diesel) Engines:**
- Only air is inducted and compressed; fuel is *injected directly* into combustion chamber.
- AFR range: **18 : 1 to 70 : 1** (very lean overall; locally rich in spray zones).
- Quality of fuel atomization and spray pattern is critical.
- Throttle controls *quantity of air*; fuel quantity is metered by the injector pump.
- No spark plug; combustion begins when auto-ignition temperature is reached.

### 2.7 Why Diesel Engines Run Excessively Lean

Because CI engines use compression ignition and have no flame-front propagation issues, they can tolerate and benefit from a very lean overall mixture. The advantages are:
- Higher thermal efficiency (Otto cycle peak efficiency occurs at lower equivalence ratios for Diesel cycle).
- Better fuel economy (one of the reasons diesel cars have higher km/l figures).
- Lower CO and HC emissions (insufficient local fuel-air ratio for these to form).
- Higher NOₓ is the *trade-off* — controlled by EGR and SCR systems.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Stoichiometric AFR for Iso-octane (C₈H₁₈)

**Step 1: Write the unbalanced combustion equation.**

Iso-octane burns in air. Air is approximated as 21% O₂ and 79% N₂ by volume, which corresponds to a molar ratio of 1 mol O₂ : 3.76 mol N₂.

$$\text{C}_8\text{H}_{18} + a(\text{O}_2 + 3.76\,\text{N}_2) \longrightarrow b\,\text{CO}_2 + c\,\text{H}_2\text{O} + d\,\text{N}_2$$

**Step 2: Balance carbon atoms.**

Left side: 8 carbon atoms.
Right side: b carbon atoms in CO₂.
Therefore, b = 8.

**Step 3: Balance hydrogen atoms.**

Left side: 18 hydrogen atoms.
Right side: 2c hydrogen atoms in H₂O.
Therefore, 2c = 18 ⇒ c = 9.

**Step 4: Balance oxygen atoms.**

Left side: 2a oxygen atoms from air.
Right side: 2b + c = 2(8) + 9 = 25 oxygen atoms.
Therefore, 2a = 25 ⇒ a = 12.5.

**Step 5: Balance nitrogen atoms.**

Left side: a × 3.76 = 12.5 × 3.76 = 47 mol N₂.
Right side: d = 47 mol N₂.

**Step 6: Write the balanced equation.**

$$\text{C}_8\text{H}_{18} + 12.5(\text{O}_2 + 3.76\,\text{N}_2) \longrightarrow 8\,\text{CO}_2 + 9\,\text{H}_2\text{O} + 47\,\text{N}_2$$

**Step 7: Calculate the molecular masses involved.**

- Molecular weight of C₈H₁₈ = (8 × 12) + (18 × 1) = 96 + 18 = **114 kg/kmol**
- Molecular weight of O₂ = 32 kg/kmol
- Molecular weight of N₂ = 28 kg/kmol
- Molecular weight of air = (0.21 × 32) + (0.79 × 28) = 6.72 + 22.12 = **28.84 kg/kmol**

**Step 8: Calculate the mass of air required per kmol of fuel.**

$$m_{\text{air}} = 12.5 \times (32 + 3.76 \times 28)$$

$$m_{\text{air}} = 12.5 \times (32 + 105.28)$$

$$m_{\text{air}} = 12.5 \times 137.28 = 1716\,\text{kg air per kmol fuel}$$

**Step 9: Calculate the stoichiometric AFR.**

$$(\text{A/F})_{\text{stoich}} = \frac{m_{\text{air}}}{m_{\text{fuel}}} = \frac{1716}{114} = 15.05$$

**Step 10: Apply the practical correction factor.**

Due to dissociation of combustion products at high temperatures and incomplete mixing, the *practical* (effective) stoichiometric AFR for gasoline is taken as **14.7 : 1**.

> This explains why KTU textbooks and modern ECU calibrations use 14.7:1 rather than the theoretical 15.05:1.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — General Theoretical Air Requirement (By Mass Balance)

For a generic fuel of composition C% carbon, H% hydrogen, and O% oxygen (by mass), the theoretical air required per kg of fuel is given by:

$$A_0 = \frac{8}{3}\,C + 8\,H - O \quad \text{[kg air / kg fuel]}$$

**Derivation:**

Each kg of fuel contains:
- C/100 kg of carbon
- H/100 kg of hydrogen
- O/100 kg of oxygen

**For carbon:** Reaction is C + O₂ → CO₂. Oxygen required = (8/3) × C/100 kg per kg fuel.

**For hydrogen:** Reaction is 2H₂ + O₂ → 2H₂O. Oxygen required = 8 × H/100 kg per kg fuel.

**For oxygen already in fuel:** The O% in fuel is *already available* in molecular form. It is assumed to be combined as O₂ and is thus subtracted.

Since air contains 23.2% oxygen by mass, the total mass of air required is:

$$A_0 = \frac{1}{0.232}\left(\frac{8}{3}\cdot\frac{C}{100} + 8\cdot\frac{H}{100} - \frac{O}{100}\right)$$

Simplifying:

$$A_0 = \frac{8}{3}\,C + 8\,H - O \quad \text{kg air/kg fuel}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This formula is **ultra-high-yield** for KTU numerical problems where the fuel composition is given directly as C%, H%, O%.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example
