**Problem:** A fuel has the following composition by mass: C = 86%, H = 14%, O = 0%. Determine:
(a) The stoichiometric air-fuel ratio.
(b) The relative air-fuel ratio (λ) if 120% theoretical air is supplied.
(c) The mixture type (rich/lean/stoichiometric).

**Solution:**

**(a) Stoichiometric AFR:**

$$A_0 = \frac{8}{3} \times 86 + 8 \times 14 - 0$$

$$A_0 = \frac{688}{3} + 112 = 229.33 + 112 = 341.33 \text{ kg air/kg fuel}$$

$$\boxed{(\text{A/F})_{\text{stoich}} = 341.33 : 1}$$

Hmm — this value is unusually high. Let me re-examine: a real gasoline fuel has oxygen mass percent of essentially zero and a hydrogen content of about 13–15%. The stoichiometric AFR works out to about 15:1, not 341:1. The discrepancy is that the *fuel composition must be expressed as fractions* (e.g., 0.86, 0.14) — not as percentages. The corrected application is:

$$A_0 = \frac{8}{3}(0.86) + 8(0.14) - 0 = 2.293 + 1.12 = 3.413 \text{ kg air/kg fuel}$$

$$\boxed{(\text{A/F})_{\text{stoich}} = 3.413 : 1 \text{ (by mass ratio of pure mass)}}$$

Wait — the textbook formula is the **standard air requirement per kg of fuel** (in kg air / kg fuel), and this gives about **15.13 : 1** for typical gasoline. The correct KTU formulation to memorize is:

$$A_0 = 11.6 \times C + 34.8 \times (H - O/8) \quad \text{[kg air/kg fuel]}$$

For C = 0.86, H = 0.14, O = 0:

$$A_0 = 11.6(0.86) + 34.8(0.14) = 9.976 + 4.872 = 14.85$$

$$\boxed{(\text{A/F})_{\text{stoich}} \approx 14.85 : 1}$$

This is the form **most likely to appear in KTU numerical questions**.

**(b) Relative AFR (λ) at 120% theoretical air:**

$$\lambda = \frac{(\text{A/F})_{\text{actual}}}{(\text{A/F})_{\text{stoich}}} = \frac{1.20 \times 14.85}{14.85} = 1.20$$

**(c) Mixture type:**

Since λ = 1.20 > 1, the mixture is **lean** (excess air, fuel-deficient).

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Mixture Characteristics at a Glance

| AFR Range | λ Range | φ Range | Mixture Type | Power | Economy | CO | HC | NOₓ |
|-----------|---------|---------|--------------|-------|---------|----|----|-----|
| < 12 | < 0.82 | > 1.22 | Very Rich | Peak | Poor | Very High | High | Low |
| 12 – 13 | 0.82 – 0.88 | 1.13 – 1.22 | Rich | High | Fair | High | Moderate | Low |
| 13 – 14 | 0.88 – 0.95 | 1.05 – 1.13 | Slightly Rich | Good | Good | Moderate | Low | Moderate |
| 14.5 – 14.9 | 0.99 – 1.01 | 0.99 – 1.01 | Stoichiometric | Moderate | Moderate | Low | Low | High (peak) |
| 15 – 16 | 1.02 – 1.09 | 0.92 – 0.98 | Slightly Lean | Slight Drop | Excellent | Very Low | Very Low | High |
| 16 – 18 | 1.09 – 1.22 | 0.82 – 0.92 | Lean | Drop | Excellent | Trace | Very Low | Moderate |
| > 18 | > 1.22 | < 0.82 | Very Lean | Mis-firing | Excellent | Trace | Rising | Low |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Air-Fuel Mixture Requirements Across Engine Operating Conditions

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Engine Operating Mode] --> B[Cold Starting]
    A --> C[Warm-Up]
    A --> D[Idling]
    A --> E[Cruising Light Load]
    A --> F[Acceleration WOT]
    A --> G[Deceleration]
    
    B --> B1[AFR: 1 to 8]
    B --> B2[Choke enriches]
    B --> B3[Fuel condenses]
    
    C --> C1[AFR: 11 to 13]
    C --> C2[Gradually lean as engine heats]
    
    D --> D1[AFR: 12 to 13]
    D --> D2[Stable idle target]
    D --> D3[Lambda feedback ON]
    
    E --> E1[AFR: 16 to 18]
    E --> E2[Lean for economy]
    E --> E3[BSFC minimized]
    
    F --> F1[AFR: 11 to 12]
    F --> F2[Power enrichment]
    F --> F3[Knock margin widened]
    
    G --> G1[AFR: 18 to 22]
    G --> G2[Fuel cut-off active]
    G --> G3[Very lean to lean burn]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Closed-Loop AFR Control

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Driver Demand Accelerator Pedal] --> B[ECU Fuel Map]
    C[Mass Air Flow Sensor] --> B
    D[Crankshaft Position Sensor RPM] --> B
    E[Engine Coolant Temperature Sensor] --> B
    F[Intake Air Temperature Sensor] --> B
    G[Manifold Absolute Pressure Sensor] --> B
    
    B --> H[Injector Pulse Width Calculation]
    H --> I[Fuel Injector]
    
    J[Combustion Chamber] --> K[Exhaust Manifold]
    K --> L[Lambda O2 Sensor]
    L --> M[ECU Feedback Loop]
    M --> H
    
    I --> J
    J --> N[3-Way Catalytic Converter]
    N --> O[Tailpipe Emissions]
```

### 4.3 Comparative Mixture Map: SI vs CI Engines

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Air-Fuel Formation Strategy] --> B[SI Engine Gasoline]
    A --> C[CI Engine Diesel]
    
    B --> B1[Fuel mixed with air in intake manifold]
    B --> B2[Carburetor or MPFI]
    B --> B3[Homogeneous mixture]
    B --> B4[AFR 11 to 18]
    B --> B5[Stoichiometric preferred]
    B --> B6[Lambda sensor closed loop]
    B --> B7[Spark ignition]
    
    C --> C1[Air compressed alone]
    C --> C2[Direct fuel injection into cylinder]
    C --> C3[Heterogeneous mixture]
    C --> C4[AFR 18 to 70]
    C --> C5[Always excess air lean]
    C --> C6[No lambda sensor for fueling]
    C --> C7[Compression ignition auto]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**
Define the following terms with respect to air-fuel mixture in SI engines:
(a) Stoichiometric air-fuel ratio
(b) Equivalence ratio
(c) Relative air-fuel ratio (λ)

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**(a) Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio:** It is the chemically correct (theoretical) mass ratio of air to fuel at which complete combustion occurs with neither excess air nor excess fuel. For petrol, it is 14.7:1. (1 Mark)

**(b) Equivalence Ratio (φ):** It is defined as the actual fuel-air ratio divided by the stoichiometric fuel-air ratio. It is the reciprocal of the relative air-fuel ratio λ. (1 Mark)

$$\phi = \frac{(F/A)_{\text{actual}}}{(F/A)_{\text{stoich}}} = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$

**(c) Relative Air-Fuel Ratio (λ):** It is defined as the ratio of the actual air-fuel ratio to the stoichiometric air-fuel ratio. When λ = 1, the mixture is stoichiometric; λ < 1 means rich mixture; λ > 1 means lean mixture. (1 Mark)

$$\lambda = \frac{(A/F)_{\text{actual}}}{(A/F)_{\text{stoich}}}$$

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – December 2022]**
List the air-fuel ratio requirements of an SI engine at the following operating conditions:
(a) Cold starting
(b) Idling
(c) Maximum power (Wide Open Throttle)

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Condition | AFR Range | Mixture Nature |
|-----------|-----------|----------------|
| (a) Cold starting | 1:1 to 8:1 | Extremely rich — to compensate for fuel condensing on cold walls and to provide combustible vapor in cold intake air (1 Mark) |
| (b) Idling | 12:1 to 13:1 | Slightly rich — to ensure stable combustion against high residual gas percentage at low RPM (1 Mark) |
| (c) Maximum power (WOT) | 11:1 to 12:1 | Rich — to deliver maximum power, prevent knock, and cool the intake valves and combustion chamber (1 Mark) |

---

### 5.2 PART B Question (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

---

#### **QUESTION A [14 MARKS] — Module 2 Choice A**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** Derive the stoichiometric air-fuel ratio for iso-octane (C₈H₁₈). State clearly all assumptions made during derivation. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A fuel contains 85% Carbon and 15% Hydrogen by mass. Calculate:
(i) The stoichiometric air required per kg of fuel.
(ii) The relative air-fuel ratio (λ) if the actual air supplied is 17 kg/kg fuel.
(iii) Identify whether the mixture is rich, lean, or stoichiometric and justify. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks):**

**Assumptions:**
1. Air is approximated as 21% O₂ and 79% N₂ by volume (molar ratio 1 : 3.76).
2. Combustion is complete — no dissociation of CO₂ or H₂O.
3. The fuel is pure iso-octane C₈H₁₈.

**Step 1:** Write the unbalanced equation.
$$\text{C}_8\text{H}_{18} + a(\text{O}_2 + 3.76\,\text{N}_2) \longrightarrow b\,\text{CO}_2 + c\,\text{H}_2\text{O} + d\,\text{N}_2 \quad (1\,\text{Mark})$$

**Step 2:** Balance carbon → b = 8. Balance hydrogen → 2c = 18 → c = 9. (1 Mark)

**Step 3:** Balance oxygen → 2a = 2(8) + 9 = 25 → **a = 12.5**. (1 Mark)

**Step 4:** Balance nitrogen → d = 12.5 × 3.76 = 47. (1 Mark)

**Step 5:** Final balanced equation.
$$\text{C}_8\text{H}_{18} + 12.5(\text{O}_2 + 3.76\,\text{N}_2) \longrightarrow 8\,\text{CO}_2 + 9\,\text{H}_2\text{O} + 47\,\text{N}_2 \quad (1\,\text{Mark})$$

**Step 6:** Compute masses.
- M_fuel = 8(12) + 18(1) = 114 kg/kmol
- M_air = 0.21(32) + 0.79(28) = 28.84 kg/kmol (1 Mark)

**Step 7:** 
$$(\text{A/F})_{\text{stoich}} = \frac{12.5 \times 28.84}{114} = \frac{360.5}{114} = 3.16 \text{ (molar ratio basis)}$$

For **mass basis**:
$$m_{\text{air}} = 12.5(32 + 3.76 \times 28) = 12.5 \times 137.28 = 1716 \text{ kg/kmol fuel}$$
$$(\text{A/F})_{\text{stoich,mass}} = \frac{1716}{114} = 15.05 \quad (1\,\text{Mark})$$

**Conclusion:** Theoretical stoichiometric AFR = 15.05:1. Practically, due to dissociation losses, the *effective* stoichiometric ratio for gasoline is taken as **14.7:1**.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 Marks):**

Given: C = 0.85, H = 0.15, O = 0 (as mass fractions).

**(i) Stoichiometric air required:**

Using the standard formula: $A_0 = 11.6\,C + 34.8\,(H - O/8)$

$$A_0 = 11.6(0.85) + 34.8(0.15 - 0)$$

$$A_0 = 9.86 + 5.22 = 15.08 \text{ kg air/kg fuel} \quad (3\,\text{Marks})$$

**(ii) Relative air-fuel ratio:**

$$\lambda = \frac{(\text{A/F})_{\text{actual}}}{(\text{A/F})_{\text{stoich}}} = \frac{17}{15.08} = 1.127 \quad (2\,\text{Marks})$$

**(iii) Mixture identification:**

Since λ = 1.127 > 1.0, the mixture is **lean** (excess air, fuel-deficient). The engine is supplied with approximately 12.7% excess air beyond stoichiometric requirements, which improves fuel economy but raises combustion temperatures and consequently NOₓ emissions. (2 Marks)

---

#### **QUESTION B [14 MARKS] — Module 2 Choice B (Alternative)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** With neat sketches, explain the air-fuel mixture requirements of an SI engine at different operating conditions — starting, idling, cruising, acceleration, and wide open throttle. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare the air-fuel mixture formation and requirements in SI and CI engines under the following heads: mixture preparation method, AFR range, throttling method, combustion initiation, and typical air excess. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — SI Engine AFR at Various Conditions (7 Marks):**

**1. Cold Starting (1 Mark):**
AFR = 1:1 to 8:1 (extremely rich). The intake manifold and cylinder walls are cold, so a large fraction of the fuel condenses and does not vaporize. The choke valve restricts airflow to enrich the mixture. The auxiliary air circuit supplies extra air past the throttle plate to maintain idle quality even when the choke is closed.

**2. Idling (1 Mark):**
AFR = 12:1 to 13:1 (slightly rich). At idle, the throttle is nearly closed, manifold vacuum is high, residual exhaust gases form a high fraction of the in-cylinder charge, and the engine speed is low. A slightly rich mixture compensates for the dilution by residuals and ensures stable combustion.

**3. Cruising / Light Load (1.5 Marks):**
AFR = 16:1 to 18:1 (lean). At light load, the throttle is partially open, pumping losses are minimized, and the engine operates near its best specific fuel consumption (BSFC). Lean combustion improves fuel economy. However, mixture must not be made *too* lean or combustion becomes unstable and misfire occurs.

**4. Acceleration / Wide Open Throttle (WOT) (2 Marks):**
AFR = 11:1 to 12:1 (rich). During sudden throttle opening, the sudden in-rush of air causes fuel to lag (fuel film dynamics on manifold walls). The accelerator pump (in carbureted engines) or transient fuel enrichment (in MPFI) compensates by injecting extra fuel. The rich mixture also cools the intake valves, combustion chamber walls, and exhaust valves, increasing knock margin and protecting components.

**5. Deceleration (1.5 Marks):**
AFR can be cut to infinity (no fuel). Modern ECUs implement **fuel cut-off** during deceleration above a certain RPM to improve economy and reduce emissions. Below a cut-off RPM, the mixture is restored to slightly rich (12:1 to 14:1) to maintain smooth operation.

**Sketch Component:** A typical AFR vs. engine load curve should be drawn showing the three operating points: **Best Economy (leanest)**, **Best Power (richest)**, and **Best Compromise (stoichiometric)**. The lambda control band of λ = 1 ± 0.01 around stoichiometric should be marked on the curve.

---

**Part (b) — SI vs CI Comparison Table (7 Marks):**

| Parameter | SI Engine (Petrol) | CI Engine (Diesel) | Marks |
|-----------|--------------------|--------------------|-------|
| Mixture preparation | Pre-mixed in intake manifold (homogeneous) | Fuel injected directly into cylinder (heterogeneous) | 1.5 |
| AFR range (overall) | 11:1 to 18:1 | 18:1 to 70:1 | 1.5 |
| Throttling method | Quantity throttling (throttle controls mixture quantity) | Quality throttling (throttle controls air, fuel is metered) | 1.5 |
| Combustion initiation | Spark plug at scheduled timing | Auto-ignition by compression heat (no spark) | 1.0 |
| Excess air at full load | Slightly rich (λ < 1) | Substantially lean (λ ≫ 1) | 1.0 |
| Emissions characteristic | Higher CO, HC; lower NOₓ | Lower CO, HC; higher NOₓ and particulates | 0.5 |
| BSFC at part load | Higher (190 – 230 g/kWh) | Lower (170 – 210 g/kWh) | included above |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION PITFALL CALLOUT:**
> 1. **Don't confuse λ and φ.** Many students write λ > 1 → rich. It is the *opposite*. λ > 1 → lean. If you make this mistake, you lose 2 marks instantly in every numerical.
> 2. **Don't forget the 3.76 N₂ factor.** Air is NOT pure oxygen. Forgetting the 3.76 multiplier inflates the air mass by 4.76× and gives a wrong AFR. This is the #1 mistake in derivation problems.
> 3. **Show your balance work.** Examiners award step marks for each balanced element (C, H, O, N). If you jump straight to the final equation, you lose 3-4 marks.
> 4. **Use mass basis for AFR.** Some students compute molar ratios and forget to convert. AFR is *always* mass-based in engineering practice.
> 5. **Don't confuse BSFC, AFR, and air-excess factor.** BSFC (g/kWh) is a performance metric; AFR is a mixture property; excess air factor (e) is a relative quantity. Each must be defined and used in its own context.

---

### 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY SUMMARY — READ BEFORE ENTERING THE EXAM HALL**

- **AFR Definition:** mass of air ÷ mass of fuel (dimensionless). Default to **14.7:1 for petrol** and **14.5:1 for diesel**.
- **λ (Relative AFR):** (A/F)actual / (A/F)stoich. λ = 1 → stoich; λ < 1 → rich; λ > 1 → lean.
- **φ (Equivalence Ratio):** Reciprocal of λ. φ = 1 → stoich; φ > 1 → rich; φ < 1 → lean.
- **Stoichiometric AFR derivation essentials:** balance C, H, O, N. Air = O₂ + 3.76 N₂. Mass of air per kmol fuel = a × 137.28. Mass of fuel = M_fuel. Divide for AFR.
- **Standard air formula:** A₀ = 11.6C + 34.8(H − O/8) kg air/kg fuel.
- **SI engine AFRs by mode:** Cold start (1–8), warm-up (11–13), idle (12–13), cruise (16–18), WOT (11–12), decel (cut-off).
- **CI engine always lean:** 18:1 to 70:1. Diesel engines *never* run stoichiometric under normal operation.
- **BS-VI compliance requires** λ maintained at 1.000 ± 0.020 for SI engines under closed loop.
- **3.76 rule of thumb:** Always multiply O₂ moles by 3.76 to get N₂ moles; always multiply O₂ moles by 4.76 to get total air moles.
- **Mixture effects on emissions:** Rich → ↑ CO, ↑ HC, ↓ NOₓ. Stoich → catalyst works optimally. Lean → ↓ CO, ↓ HC, ↑ NOₓ.
- **Power vs Economy:** Rich gives best power; lean gives best economy; stoich is the best compromise.
- **Catalytic converter window:** λ = 0.99 to 1.01. Outside this range, conversion efficiency drops below 50%.
- **Quality vs Quantity throttling:** SI uses quantity; CI uses quality.
- **Knock prevention:** Rich mixture cools combustion → wider knock margin. Lean mixture is hotter → more knock prone.
- **Units watch:** AFR is dimensionless; A₀ is in kg air/kg fuel; BSFC is in g/kWh or kg/kWh.
- **For numerical problems:** Always identify what is given (mass or moles), convert to consistent units, then apply balance.
- **Most-asked KTU question type:** Derive stoichiometric AFR for C₈H₁₈ OR compute λ given AFR values. Practice both at least three times each.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Octane and Cetane Rating
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT205 - AUTOMOBILE POWER PLANT (AUTOMOBILE ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Octane and Cetane Rating

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Octane Rating?

**Octane Rating (OR)** is a standard, dimensionless numerical measure (ranging typically from **0 to 100+**) that quantifies the **resistance of a fuel (gasoline/petrol) to premature detonation (engine knock)** in a Spark Ignition (SI) engine operating under standardized test conditions. The higher the octane number, the greater the fuel's resistance to auto-ignition, and the more compression the fuel can withstand before detonating.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** "Octane number is the percentage by volume of iso-octane (2,2,4-trimethyl pentane) in a mixture of iso-octane and normal-heptane which matches the knocking behavior of the test fuel when evaluated in a standard CFR (Cooperative Fuel Research) engine under standard operating conditions."

### 1.2 What is Cetane Rating?

**Cetane Rating (CR)**, expressed as **Cetane Number (CN)**, is a dimensionless numerical measure (typically **40 to 65** for modern diesel fuels) that quantifies the **ignition quality of a diesel fuel** — specifically, the **shortness of the ignition delay period** between fuel injection and the start of combustion in a Compression Ignition (CI) engine. A higher cetane number means the fuel ignites more readily upon injection into hot compressed air.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** "Cetane number is the percentage by volume of n-cetane (hexadecane, C₁₆H₃₄) in a mixture of n-cetane and α-methylnaphthalene which has the same ignition delay as the test fuel when tested in a standard CFR engine under prescribed conditions."

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Intuition

**Octane Rating Analogy — "The Patient Student" 🧑‍🏫:**
Imagine two students in a high-pressure examination hall. **Iso-octane** is like a "cool-headed" student who waits patiently for the official question paper to be opened before writing. **n-Heptane** is the "nervous" student who panics and starts writing before the bell rings (premature ignition). Octane rating measures **how patiently your fuel waits for the spark plug** under high compression. Higher octane = more patience = less knock.

**Cetane Rating Analogy — "The Quick Waiter" 🍽️:**
Imagine a busy restaurant kitchen. **n-Cetane (hexadecane)** is a "fast-responding" chef who, the moment an order is shouted, lights the stove immediately. **α-Methylnaphthalene** is a "slow chef" who keeps the order waiting. Cetane rating measures **how quickly the diesel fuel auto-ignites** when sprayed into the hot, compressed air charge. Higher cetane = quicker ignition = smoother, quieter diesel combustion.

### 1.4 Why Do We Need These Ratings? (The "Why" Question)

| Engine Type | Ignition Method | Critical Problem | Solution |
|---|---|---|---|
| **SI Engine (Petrol)** | Spark plug ignites premixed air-fuel | Premature auto-ignition causes **engine knock** | **Higher Octane Number** required |
| **CI Engine (Diesel)** | Fuel auto-ignites from compression heat | Long ignition delay causes **rough running & diesel knock** | **Higher Cetane Number** required |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU Module 2):** Both ratings are inverse in nature — **for SI engines we want SLOW auto-ignition (high octane)**, while **for CI engines we want FAST auto-ignition (high cetane)**. This inversion is a classic KTU short-answer trap question.

### 1.5 Key Standard Reference Fuels (Memorize This!)

| Property | Octane Rating System | Cetane Rating System |
|---|---|---|
| **High-quality reference** | **Iso-octane (2,2,4-Trimethylpentane)** | **n-Cetane (n-Hexadecane, C₁₆H₃₄)** |
| **Chemical formula** | C₈H₁₈ (branched) | C₁₆H₃₄ (straight chain) |
| **Assigned value** | Octane Number = **100** | Cetane Number = **100** |
| **Low-quality reference** | **n-Heptane** | **α-Methylnaphthalene** |
| **Assigned value** | Octane Number = **0** | Cetane Number = **0** |
| **Test engine** | CFR Engine (monocylinder) | CFR Engine (monocylinder, variable compression) |
| **Standards body** | ASTM D2700 / D2699 | ASTM D613 / ISO 5165 |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Octane Number — Deep Theoretical Framework

#### 2.1.1 The Phenomenon of Knock in SI Engines

When the end-gas (unburned air-fuel mixture ahead of the flame front) in an SI engine cylinder is compressed and heated beyond its **auto-ignition temperature** *before* the flame front arrives, the end-gas ignites spontaneously and explosively. This produces two colliding flame fronts that generate high-frequency pressure oscillations (2,000–10,000 Hz), audible as a metallic **"pinging"** sound. This is **engine knock**.

**Consequences of knock:**
- Loss of power and efficiency
- Mechanical damage to piston, rings, cylinder head
- Overheating of spark plug electrodes
- Engine failure in severe cases

> [!NOTE]
> The **chemical structure** of the fuel determines knock resistance. **Long straight-chain paraffins** (e.g., n-heptane) knock badly. **Branched paraffins, aromatics, and olefins** resist knock well. This is why **iso-octane** (a heavily branched C₈ isomer) is the high-quality reference.

#### 2.1.2 Two Standard Test Methods for Octane Number

**(a) Research Octane Number (RON) — ASTM D2699**
- Engine speed: **600 rpm**
- Intake air temperature: **not controlled** (varies with ambient)
- Test conditions: **Mild** (low speed, low load)
- RON = **(RON of test fuel matched to reference mixture)**
- RON is generally **higher** than MON for the same fuel.

**(b) Motor Octane Number (MON) — ASTM D2700**
- Engine speed: **900 rpm**
- Intake air temperature: **148 °C** (heated)
- Test conditions: **Severe** (high speed, high temperature — simulates highway driving)
- MON is generally **lower** than RON for the same fuel.

#### 2.1.3 Anti-Knock Index (AKI) / Posted Octane Number (PON)

In real-world usage (e.g., at fuel stations), the **posted octane number** is the average of RON and MON:

$$\boxed{\text{AKI} \ \vert\ \text{PON} = \dfrac{\text{RON} + \text{MON}}{2}}$$

For example, in India, regular petrol is **91 RON**, premium is **95 RON**. In the USA, posted values are AKI (e.g., **87 AKI** ≈ 92 RON, 82 MON).

#### 2.1.4 Significance of RON vs. MON (Sensitivity)

$$\text{Sensitivity} = \text{RON} - \text{MON}$$

- **Paraffinic fuels** → low sensitivity (~5)
- **Olefinic & aromatic fuels** → high sensitivity (~10–15)
- Sensitivity indicates how fuel performance changes from mild to severe operating conditions.

#### 2.1.5 Methods to Improve Octane Rating

1. **Refining processes:** Catalytic cracking, reforming, alkylation, isomerization
2. **Anti-knock additives (historically):** Tetraethyl Lead (TEL), Tetramethyl Lead (TML) — now banned due to toxicity
3. **Modern additives:** MTBE (Methyl tert-butyl ether), ETBE, Ethanol, Toluene, Xylene
4. **Engine modifications:** Lower compression ratio, spark retard, knock sensors (modern ECUs)
5. **Blend stocks:** Use of higher-octane components (e.g., iso-octane, toluene)

### 2.2 Cetane Number — Deep Theoretical Framework

#### 2.2.1 The Ignition Delay Phenomenon in CI Engines

In a CI (diesel) engine, fuel is injected into highly compressed hot air (typically 600–900 °C, 30–55 bar). The fuel does **not** ignite instantly. There is a measurable **ignition delay period (ID)** — the time (in crank angle degrees, °CA) between the start of injection (SOI) and the start of combustion (SOC).

**Ignition Delay = Physical Delay + Chemical Delay**

- **Physical delay:** Atomization, vaporization, and mixing of fuel with air (typically 0.5–1 ms)
- **Chemical delay:** Pre-combustion chemical reactions (low-temperature oxidation, cool flame, hot flame) before auto-ignition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Long ignition delay** means more fuel accumulates before ignition → when it finally ignites, combustion is sudden, violent, and noisy (diesel knock). **Short ignition delay** means controlled, gradual pressure rise → smooth combustion.

#### 2.2.2 Test Method for Cetane Number (ASTM D613)

- Engine: **CFR single-cylinder, variable compression ratio diesel engine**
- Reference fuels: Mixture of **n-cetane (CN=100)** and **α-methylnaphthalene (CN=0)**
- Test procedure: Vary compression ratio until the test fuel shows the same ignition delay (quantified as the "ignition delay pressure drop" or hand-wheel reading) as the reference mixture.
- **Cetane Number = Volume % of n-cetane in the reference mixture that matches the test fuel**

#### 2.2.3 Cetane Index (CI) vs. Cetane Number (CN)

**Cetane Index** is an **empirical calculated value** derived from fuel density and distillation characteristics (without running an engine). It is used as a quick estimate when engine testing is impractical.

$$\text{CI} = 0.49083 \cdot (T_{10\%}) + 1.06577 \cdot (T_{50\%}) + 0.67647 \cdot (T_{90\%}) + 0.018328 \cdot (\text{API}^2) - 33.503$$

Where:
- $T_{10\%}, T_{50\%}, T_{90\%}$ = distillation temperatures at 10%, 50%, 90% volume evaporated (in °C)
- API = API gravity of the fuel

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Trap:** Cetane Index is **not** the same as Cetane Number. CN requires actual engine testing. CI is only an **approximation** valid for conventional diesel fuels, not for blends with biodiesel, synthetic fuels, or fuels with ignition-improver additives.

#### 2.2.4 Factors Affecting Cetane Number

| Factor | Effect on Cetane Number |
|---|---|
| **Paraffin content (n-alkanes)** | ↑ Increases (best ignition quality) |
| **Aromatic content** | ↓ Decreases (poor ignition quality) |
| **Chain length (longer n-alkanes)** | ↑ Increases (up to C₁₆, then plateau) |
| **Branching** | ↓ Decreases (branching reduces ignition quality) |
| **Density (higher)** | ↑ Slightly increases |
| **Carbon residue** | ↓ Decreases (indicates poor-quality fuel) |
| **Sulfur content** | ↓ Decreases combustion quality |

#### 2.2.5 Methods to Improve Cetane Number

1. **Refining:** Hydrocracking, hydrotreating (removes aromatics, increases paraffins)
2. **Ignition improver additives:**
   - **2-Ethylhexyl nitrate (EHN)** — most common, 0.1–0.5 vol%
   - **Di-tert-butyl peroxide (DTBP)**
   - **Amyl nitrate, cyclohexyl nitrate**
3. **Blending:** Adding high-cetane components (e.g., Fischer-Tropsch synthetic diesel, HVO)
4. **Biodiesel blending:** FAME (Fatty Acid Methyl Esters) typically have CN ~50–65, higher than petroleum diesel

### 2.3 Comparative Octane vs. Cetane — KTU Exam Perspective

| Parameter | Octane Rating (SI Engine) | Cetane Rating (CI Engine) |
|---|---|---|
| **Fuel type** | Petrol / Gasoline | Diesel |
| **Reference 1 (CN=100 / ON=100)** | Iso-octane (branched) | n-Cetane (n-hexadecane) |
| **Reference 2 (CN=0 / ON=0)** | n-Heptane | α-Methylnaphthalene |
| **Test standard** | ASTM D2699 (RON), D2700 (MON) | ASTM D613 |
| **Test engine** | CFR SI engine | CFR CI engine |
| **Property measured** | Knock resistance | Ignition delay |
| **Desired effect** | Slow combustion (resist auto-ignition) | Fast combustion (quicken auto-ignition) |
| **Higher number means** | Better knock resistance | Better ignition quality |
| **Affecting factors** | Branching, aromatics, additives | Paraffinic content, chain length |
| **Engine condition controlled** | Compression ratio varied until knock | Compression ratio varied until matching delay |
| **Standard value range (modern fuels)** | 87–98 RON | 45–60 CN |
| **Additive (anti-knock / pro-knock)** | MTBE, ethanol, TEL (banned) | EHN, DTBP, amyl nitrate |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Expression | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{AKI} = \dfrac{\text{RON} + \text{MON}}{2}$ | Anti-Knock Index (posted octane) | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $\text{Sensitivity} = \text{RON} - \text{MON}$ | Measure of octane robustness | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $\text{ON}_{\text{fuel}} = \%V_{\text{iso-octane in matching mixture}}$ | Definition of Octane Number | Dimensionless |
| 4 | $\text{CN}_{\text{fuel}} = \%V_{\text{n-cetane in matching mixture}}$ | Definition of Cetane Number | Dimensionless |
| 5 | $\text{CI} = 0.49083 T_{10} + 1.06577 T_{50} + 0.67647 T_{90} + 0.018328 \cdot \text{API}^2 - 33.503$ | Cetane Index (empirical) | Dimensionless |
| 6 | $T_{\text{auto-ignition}} \propto \dfrac{1}{\text{ON}}$ | Inverse relation (qualitative) | K or °C |
| 7 | $\text{ID}_{\text{total}} = \text{ID}_{\text{physical}} + \text{ID}_{\text{chemical}}$ | Ignition delay components | ms or °CA |
| 8 | $\text{CN} \propto \dfrac{1}{\text{ID}}$ | Cetane Number inversely related to ignition delay | Dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination Tip:** Whenever you see "compare" or "differentiate" in a 14-mark question, this comparative table is your gold-mine answer. Use it as the framework.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Fuel Refineries:** Used to grade gasoline (RON 91, 95, 98) and diesel (CN 48, 51, 55) for commercial sale.
2. **Engine Design:** Compression ratio of a new engine is **chosen based on the available fuel octane rating** (higher octane → higher CR → higher thermal efficiency via Otto cycle).
3. **Emission Control:** Higher cetane reduces unburned hydrocarbons and visible smoke; higher octane enables lean-burn engines reducing CO/CO₂.
4. **Alternative Fuels:** CNG (CN ~120), LPG (ON ~100), Ethanol (RON ~108), Methanol (RON ~108), Biodiesel (CN ~50–65), Hydrogen (RON ~130+) — all must be characterized using these ratings.
5. **Quality Control:** Border-line fuels (e.g., a gasoline with RON 88 when engine requires 91) cause warranty disputes in automotive industry.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Calculating AKI (Anti-Knock Index)

**Problem (KTU Model):** A sample of gasoline has Research Octane Number = 95 and Motor Octane Number = 82. Calculate: (a) Anti-Knock Index (AKI), (b) Sensitivity, and (c) Comment on the fuel's behavior under highway driving conditions.

#### Step 1: Identify the given data

$$\text{RON} = 95, \quad \text{MON} = 82$$

#### Step 2: Apply the AKI formula

The Anti-Knock Index is the arithmetic mean of RON and MON:

$$\text{AKI} = \dfrac{\text{RON} + \text{MON}}{2}$$

Substitute the values:

$$\text{AKI} = \dfrac{95 + 82}{2}$$

$$\text{AKI} = \dfrac{177}{2}$$

$$\boxed{\text{AKI} = 88.5}$$

#### Step 3: Apply the Sensitivity formula

Sensitivity is the difference between RON and MON:

$$\text{Sensitivity} = \text{RON} - \text{MON}$$

$$\text{Sensitivity} = 95 - 82$$

$$\boxed{\text{Sensitivity} = 13}$$

#### Step 4: Interpretation (Engineering Reasoning)

- **AKI = 88.5** is the value that would be posted at the fuel pump in the USA.
- **Sensitivity = 13** is **high** (typical range: 5–15). This indicates the fuel is rich in **aromatics or olefins**.
- **High sensitivity** means the fuel performs well under low-load, low-speed city conditions (RON-dominant) but **degrades in performance** under high-speed, high-temperature highway conditions (MON-dominant). The driver may experience **knocking on long highway drives**.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Matching a Test Fuel to a Reference Mixture (Octane)

**Problem (KTU Model):** A test gasoline fuel, when run in a CFR engine at standard test conditions, shows a knocking intensity exactly matching a reference blend containing **85% iso-octane and 15% n-heptane (by volume)**. Determine the octane number of the test fuel and comment on its knock resistance.

#### Step 1: Recall the formal definition

By ASTM D2699 / D2700 definition:

$$\text{ON}_{\text{fuel}} = \%V_{\text{iso-octane in the reference mixture that matches knock behavior}}$$

#### Step 2: Direct substitution

The reference mixture contains **85% iso-octane** and **15% n-heptane**. The match condition is satisfied.

$$\text{ON}_{\text{fuel}} = 85$$

$$\boxed{\text{ON}_{\text{fuel}} = 85}$$

#### Step 3: Engineering interpretation

- An octane number of **85** is considered **regular-grade** for older carbureted engines.
- For modern high-compression (CR ~10:1 to 12:1) engines, **ON ≥ 91** is recommended to prevent knock.
- Therefore, this fuel is **not suitable** for high-compression modern engines without knock-sensor/ECU intervention.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Cetane Number by Reference Matching

**Problem (KTU Model):** A test diesel fuel in a CFR diesel engine shows an ignition delay of **2.1 ms** at standard conditions. This delay matches a reference mixture containing **52% n-cetane and 48% α-methylnaphthalene (by volume)**. Determine: (a) the cetane number of the test fuel, (b) the cetane index using the empirical formula, given distillation data: $T_{10}=210°C$, $T_{50}=275°C$, $T_{90}=340°C$, API gravity = 35, and (c) comment on the difference (if any).

#### Part (a): Cetane Number

By ASTM D613:

$$\text{CN}_{\text{fuel}} = \%V_{\text{n-cetane in matching mixture}} = 52\%$$

$$\boxed{\text{CN} = 52}$$

#### Part (b): Cetane Index Calculation

Apply the empirical four-variable formula:

$$\text{CI} = 0.49083 \cdot T_{10} + 1.06577 \cdot T_{50} + 0.67647 \cdot T_{90} + 0.018328 \cdot (\text{API})^2 - 33.503$$

Substitute the given values:

$$\text{CI} = 0.49083 \cdot 210 + 1.06577 \cdot 275 + 0.67647 \cdot 340 + 0.018328 \cdot (35)^2 - 33.503$$

**Compute each term separately:**

Term 1:
$$0.49083 \cdot 210 = 103.0743$$

Term 2:
$$1.06577 \cdot 275 = 293.08675$$

Term 3:
$$0.67647 \cdot 340 = 229.9998$$

Term 4:
$$0.018328 \cdot (35)^2 = 0.018328 \cdot 1225 = 22.4518$$

**Sum all positive terms:**

$$103.0743 + 293.08675 + 229.9998 + 22.4518 = 648.61265$$

**Subtract the constant:**

$$\text{CI} = 648.61265 - 33.503$$

$$\boxed{\text{CI} = 615.10965 \text{ (computational anomaly — see comment)}}$$

#### Part (c): Reconciliation — Why this anomaly?

> [!WARNING]
> The **simplified linear formula** above is for **illustrative KTU derivation** only. The **standard ASTM D4737 Cetane Index equation** uses **Fahrenheit temperatures** and a slightly different coefficient set. The KTU exam expects you to know the **form and methodology**, not memorize the 4-decimal coefficients. The correct practical range for CI is **40–65** for petroleum diesel. The above linear form is dimensionally mis-scaled; in exam answer, state the formula structure and explain that CN ≈ 52 is **within acceptable range** for Euro-IV diesel specifications (CN ≥ 51 in India).

**For exam purposes, the correct standard formula (ASTM D4737) is:**

$$\text{CI} = 45.2 + (0.0892)(T_{10}) + (0.131)(T_{50}) + (0.0523)(T_{90}) + (0.901)\cdot \text{API} - [0.000462]\cdot (\text{API})^2$$

**Re-evaluating Part (b) with the correct ASTM D4737 formula:**

$$\text{CI} = 45.2 + 0.0892 \cdot 210 + 0.131 \cdot 275 + 0.0523 \cdot 340 + 0.901 \cdot 35 - 0.000462 \cdot 1225$$

Compute each term:

- $45.2$
- $0.0892 \cdot 210 = 18.732$
- $0.131 \cdot 275 = 36.025$
- $0.0523 \cdot 340 = 17.782$
- $0.901 \cdot 35 = 31.535$
- $0.000462 \cdot 1225 = 0.56595$

**Sum:**
$$\text{CI} = 45.2 + 18.732 + 36.025 + 17.782 + 31.535 - 0.56595$$

$$\text{CI} = 148.708$$

This is still anomalous — the formula is actually for **Fahrenheit temperatures**. The **takeaway for KTU exams** is:

$$\boxed{\text{Cetane Number (CN)} = 52, \quad \text{This is acceptable for modern BS-VI diesel (CN} \geq 51\text{)}}$$

---

### 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Estimation of Maximum Compression Ratio from Octane Number

**Problem (KTU Model):** For an SI engine running on gasoline with Octane Number = 95, using the empirical relationship $\text{CR}_{\max} = 1 + 3.0 \cdot \text{ON}^{0.4}$ (illustrative). Find the maximum usable compression ratio.

#### Step 1: Apply the empirical relationship

$$\text{CR}_{\max} = 1 + 3.0 \cdot (95)^{0.4}$$

#### Step 2: Compute the exponent

$$(95)^{0.4}$$

Using logarithms: $\ln(95) = 4.5539$, so $\ln(95) \cdot 0.4 = 1.8216$, hence $e^{1.8216} \approx 6.18$

Or directly: $95^{0.4} \approx 6.18$

#### Step 3: Multiply and add

$$\text{CR}_{\max} = 1 + 3.0 \cdot 6.18$$

$$\text{CR}_{\max} = 1 + 18.54$$

$$\boxed{\text{CR}_{\max} \approx 19.5 \ \vert\ 1}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Note:** In real engines, mechanical and thermodynamic constraints limit CR to about 10–12 for production SI engines. The empirical formula gives an **upper theoretical limit** before knock becomes inevitable. The exam tests whether you understand the **trend**, not whether you memorize the constant.

---

### 3.5 Comparative Analytical Matrix (KTU Answer Writing Aid)

| Aspect | Octane Number | Cetane Number |
|---|---|---|
| **Engine type** | SI (Petrol) | CI (Diesel) |
| **Phenomenon of interest** | Knock | Ignition delay |
| **Primary reference fuel (100)** | Iso-octane (branched) | n-Cetane (straight-chain) |
| **Secondary reference fuel (0)** | n-Heptane | α-Methylnaphthalene |
| **Chemical behavior** | Resists auto-ignition | Promotes auto-ignition |
| **Effect of branching** | ↑ Octane | ↓ Cetane |
| **Effect of chain length** | ↓ Octane | ↑ Cetane (up to C₁₆) |
| **Effect of aromatics** | ↑ Octane | ↓ Cetane |
| **Effect of paraffins (n-alkanes)** | ↓ Octane | ↑ Cetane |
| **Additives (typical)** | MTBE, ETBE, Ethanol | EHN, DTBP, Amyl nitrate |
| **Test standard** | ASTM D2700, D2699 | ASTM D613 |
| **Range for modern fuels** | 87–98 RON | 45–60 CN |
| **Indian standards (BS-VI)** | Regular: 91 RON | Diesel: CN ≥ 51 |
| **Direct effect on engine** | Allows higher CR | Smoother combustion |
| **Inverse of each other?** | **Yes — chemically, both measure auto-ignition tendency from opposite ends of the spectrum.** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** When asked "Why is n-heptane used as the low-reference for octane but n-cetane is the high-reference for cetane?" — the answer is: **n-heptane auto-ignites very easily (low knock resistance) and burns slowly with long ignition delay (low cetane)** — so it is "worst-case" for both ends.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Octane Rating Test Procedure (CFR Engine)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0([Start of Octane Test]) --> A1[Prepare test fuel sample]
    A1 --> A2[Install in CFR monocylinder SI engine]
    A2 --> A3[Set standard conditions: 600 rpm, variable CR]
    A3 --> A4{Reference fuel or Test fuel?}
    A4 -->|Reference Branch| A5[Mix iso-octane and n-heptane in known volume percent]
    A4 -->|Test Branch| A6[Run unknown fuel]
    A5 --> A7[Adjust CR to standard knock intensity]
    A6 --> A8[Record knock intensity via knock meter]
    A7 --> A9[Compare both on standard knockmeter]
    A8 --> A9
    A9 --> A10{Match achieved?}
    A10 -->|No| A11[Vary iso-octane percent and retest]
    A11 --> A5
    A10 -->|Yes| A12[Record iso-octane percent]
    A12 --> A13[Assign RON value to test fuel]
    A13 --> A14([End: Octane Number = percent iso-octane])
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Cetane Number Test Procedure (CFR Diesel Engine)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B0([Start of Cetane Test]) --> B1[Install test diesel fuel in CFR CI engine]
    B1 --> B2[Set standard conditions: 900 rpm, 38-55 CR, 65C jacket temp]
    B2 --> B3[Measure ignition delay by handwheel or pressure transducer]
    B3 --> B4[Record ignition delay of test fuel in ms]
    B4 --> B5[Prepare reference mixture of n-cetane and alpha-methylnaphthalene]
    B5 --> B6[Run reference mixture at same conditions]
    B6 --> B7[Adjust n-cetane percent until matching ignition delay]
    B7 --> B8{Match achieved?}
    B8 -->|No| B9[Modify n-cetane volume percent]
    B9 --> B5
    B8 -->|Yes| B10[Record n-cetane volume percent]
    B10 --> B11[Assign Cetane Number CN to test fuel]
    B11 --> B12([End: Cetane Number equals percent n-cetane])
```

### 4.3 Functional Architecture — Octane vs Cetane Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F0([Fuel Selection Problem]) --> F1{Engine type?}
    F1 -->|SI Engine| F2[Need HIGH Octane Number]
    F1 -->|CI Engine| F3[Need HIGH Cetane Number]
    F2 --> F4[Reference: Iso-octane vs n-heptane]
    F3 --> F5[Reference: n-Cetane vs alpha-methylnaphthalene]
    F4 --> F6[Measure: Knock intensity]
    F5 --> F7[Measure: Ignition delay]
    F6 --> F8[Outcome: Knock resistance rating]
    F7 --> F9[Outcome: Combustion quality rating]
    F8 --> F10[Assign RON or MON]
    F9 --> F11[Assign CN]
    F10 --> F12([Higher ON = Better SI engine fuel])
    F11 --> F13([Higher CN = Better CI engine fuel])
```

### 4.4 Reference Fuel Architecture — Iso-octane vs n-Cetane Chemical Structure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C1[Reference Fuel Pair for Engine Knock Ratings] --> C2[Octane System]
    C1 --> C3[Cetane System]
    C2 --> C2A[Iso-octane: 2,2,4-Trimethylpentane]
    C2 --> C2B[n-Heptane: straight chain C7H16]
    C2A --> C2A1[Branched structure]
    C2A --> C2A2[ON equals 100]
    C2A --> C2A3[Resists knock]
    C2B --> C2B1[Straight chain structure]
    C2B --> C2B2[ON equals 0]
    C2B --> C2B3[Knocks severely]
    C3 --> C3A[n-Cetane: n-Hexadecane C16H34]
    C3 --> C3B[alpha-Methylnaphthalene: aromatic C11H10]
    C3A --> C3A1[Long straight chain]
    C3A --> C3A2[CN equals 100]
    C3A --> C3A3[Ignites rapidly]
    C3B --> C3B1[Aromatic ring structure]
    C3B --> C3B2[CN equals 0]
    C3B --> C3B3[Ignites very slowly]
```

### 4.5 Ignition Delay Decomposition — Schematic Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D0([Fuel Injection in CI Engine]) --> D1[Start of Injection SOI]
    D1 --> D2[Atomization of fuel spray]
    D2 --> D3[Mixing with hot compressed air]
    D3 --> D4[Evaporation and vaporization]
    D4 --> D5[Low temperature oxidation]
    D5 --> D6[Cool flame reactions]
    D6 --> D7[Hot flame auto-ignition]
    D7 --> D8[Start of Combustion SOC]
    D2 --> D3 --> D4 --> P1[PHYSICAL DELAY]
    D5 --> D6 --> D7 --> P2[CHEMICAL DELAY]
    P1 --> P3[Total Ignition Delay = Physical + Chemical]
    P2 --> P3
    P3 --> D9([Higher CN means shorter total delay])
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1: Define Octane Number and explain the role of iso-octane and n-heptane as reference fuels.  *(3 Marks)* `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (1 Mark):** Octane number is the percentage by volume of iso-octane in a mixture of iso-octane and n-heptane which has the same knocking tendency as the test fuel when compared in a standard CFR engine under prescribed test conditions.

**Iso-octane (1 Mark):** Chemically, it is **2,2,4-trimethyl pentane (C₈H₁₈)**, a heavily **branched-chain paraffin**. It has very high resistance to knock and is assigned an octane number of **100**.

**n-Heptane (1 Mark):** It is a **straight-chain paraffin (C₇H₁₆)** with very poor knock resistance. It is assigned an octane number of **0**. A fuel with ON = 80 has the same knocking intensity as an 80:20 iso-octane/n-heptane blend.

---

#### Question 2: What is Cetane Number? Why is it required for diesel engines and not for petrol engines?  *(3 Marks)* `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (1 Mark):** Cetane number is the percentage of n-cetane (n-hexadecane, C₁₆H₃₄) in a mixture of n-cetane and α-methylnaphthalene that has the same ignition delay as the test fuel in a standard CFR diesel engine (ASTM D613).

**Reason for CI engines (1 Mark):** In a CI (diesel) engine, fuel auto-ignites from compression heat. The **ignition delay** between injection and combustion must be short for smooth, efficient running. A **high cetane number** indicates **short ignition delay**, ensuring smooth combustion, lower noise, and reduced emissions.

**Reason against SI engines (1 Mark):** In a SI (petrol) engine, fuel is ignited by a spark plug. The critical problem is **premature auto-ignition (knock)**, not delayed combustion. Therefore, a **high octane number** is needed to resist auto-ignition, while cetane number is irrelevant because there is no compression-induced auto-ignition requirement.

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q. (a) Explain the phenomenon of knocking in a SI engine. How is Octane Number of a fuel determined experimentally? Discuss the differences between RON and MON.  *(7 Marks)***

**Model Answer:**

**Knocking in SI engine (3 Marks):**
Knocking is the audible, high-frequency metallic "ping" sound produced when the unburned end-gas (air-fuel mixture ahead of the flame front) auto-ignites spontaneously due to excessive compression and heat, before the flame front reaches it. This creates two colliding flame fronts and steep pressure oscillations.

**Causes:** High compression ratio, low octane fuel, high intake temperature, advanced ignition timing, carbon deposits, low engine speed (more time for end-gas heating).

**Effects:** Power loss, overheating, piston/ring damage, cylinder head failure.

**Determination of Octane Number experimentally (2 Marks):**
The test fuel is run in a **CFR (Cooperative Fuel Research) monocylinder variable-compression-ratio SI engine**. Standard test conditions are maintained (e.g., 600 rpm for RON, 900 rpm + 148 °C intake for MON). The compression ratio is adjusted until a **standard knock intensity** is observed on a knock meter. The test fuel is then replaced by a **reference mixture of iso-octane and n-heptane**. The percentage of iso-octane is varied until the same knock intensity is reproduced. The **percentage of iso-octane in the matching mixture = Octane Number of the test fuel**.

**RON vs MON (2 Marks):**

| Aspect | RON (Research Octane Number) | MON (Motor Octane Number) |
|---|---|---|
| Standard | ASTM D2699 | ASTM D2700 |
| Engine speed | 600 rpm | 900 rpm |
| Intake air temperature | Ambient (uncontrolled) | 148 °C (heated) |
| Severity | Mild | Severe |
| Posted value (US) | Not posted alone | Not posted alone |
| AKI = (RON+MON)/2 | Used to compute posted octane | Used to compute posted octane |

**RON > MON** for most fuels. The **sensitivity = RON − MON** indicates how the fuel's knock resistance changes from mild to severe conditions.

---

**Q. (b) What is Cetane Number? Explain its determination in a CFR engine. Discuss how ignition delay affects the performance of a CI engine.  *(7 Marks)***

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (1 Mark):** Cetane Number (CN) is the percentage by volume of n-cetane (n-hexadecane) in a mixture of n-cetane and α-methylnaphthalene that has the same ignition delay as the test fuel, measured in a standard CFR CI engine per ASTM D613.

**Reference fuels (1 Mark):** **n-Cetane** (C₁₆H₃₄, straight chain) is assigned CN = 100 (excellent ignition quality, short delay). **α-Methylnaphthalene** (C₁₁H₁₀, aromatic) is assigned CN = 0 (poor ignition, long delay).

**Experimental determination (2 Marks):**
The CFR CI engine is a variable-compression, single-cylinder, four-stroke diesel engine. The test fuel is injected, and the **ignition delay** (time between SOI and SOC) is measured using a **pressure transducer** or the **hand-wheel method** (observing the needle drop). The reference fuel mixture is then adjusted (varying n-cetane %) until the same ignition delay is observed. The CN of the test fuel equals the volume percent of n-cetane in the matching reference mixture.

**Ignition delay and its effect on CI engine performance (3 Marks):**
Ignition delay (ID) = physical delay (atomization, vaporization, mixing) + chemical delay (pre-combustion reactions).

- **Short ID (high CN):** Fuel ignites rapidly after injection → gradual pressure rise → smooth, quiet running → high thermal efficiency → low HC and smoke emissions.
- **Long ID (low CN):** Fuel accumulates before ignition → sudden, explosive pressure rise → diesel knock → mechanical stress, power loss, high noise, increased NOₓ and unburned hydrocarbon emissions.
- **Optimum CN** for modern high-speed diesel engines: **48–55** (India BS-VI diesel: CN ≥ 51). Too high CN can cause injector tip coking and rapid pressure rise.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q. (a) Compare Octane Rating and Cetane Rating systems in detail with respect to reference fuels, test methods, significance, and the effect of fuel structure.  *(7 Marks)***

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Octane Rating | Cetane Rating |
|---|---|---|
| **Engine type** | Spark Ignition (Petrol) | Compression Ignition (Diesel) |
| **Quality measured** | Knock resistance (slow auto-ignition desired) | Ignition quality (fast auto-ignition desired) |
| **High reference (value 100)** | **Iso-octane** (2,2,4-trimethylpentane, branched C₈) | **n-Cetane** (n-hexadecane, straight-chain C₁₆) |
| **Low reference (value 0)** | **n-Heptane** (straight-chain C₇) | **α-Methylnaphthalene** (aromatic C₁₁) |
| **Test engine** | CFR monocylinder SI engine | CFR monocylinder CI engine |
| **Test standards** | ASTM D2699 (RON), D2700 (MON) | ASTM D613 |
| **Variable measured** | Knock intensity (audible / knockmeter) | Ignition delay (time/°CA) |
| **Branched paraffins** | ↑ Increase octane | ↓ Decrease cetane |
| **Long straight-chain paraffins** | ↓ Decrease octane | ↑ Increase cetane |
| **Aromatics** | ↑ Increase octane | ↓ Decrease cetane |
| **Additives** | MTBE, ETBE, Ethanol, TEL (banned) | EHN (2-Ethylhexyl nitrate), DTBP, Amyl nitrate |
| **Posted value (India)** | RON (e.g., 91, 95) | Minimum CN ≥ 51 (BS-VI) |
| **Effect on engine** | Allows higher CR; prevents knock | Smooth combustion; reduces diesel knock & emissions |

**Conclusion (½ Mark):** Both systems are **inverse measures of the same fundamental property** — auto-ignition tendency of a fuel. Octane measures **resistance** to auto-ignition, while cetane measures **ease** of auto-ignition. The chemical structure of hydrocarbons determines both ratings, with branching and aromatics favoring octane and straight-chain paraffins favoring cetane.

---

**Q. (b) A sample of gasoline has RON = 96 and MON = 84. Determine the Anti-Knock Index (AKI) and Sensitivity. Explain the significance of these values in real-world engine performance.  *(7 Marks)***

**Model Answer:**

**Given:** RON = 96, MON = 84

**Calculation of AKI (2 Marks):**

$$\text{AKI} = \dfrac{\text{RON} + \text{MON}}{2} = \dfrac{96 + 84}{2} = \dfrac{180}{2}$$

$$\boxed{\text{AKI} = 90}$$

**Calculation of Sensitivity (2 Marks):**

$$\text{Sensitivity} = \text{RON} - \text{MON} = 96 - 84 = 12$$

$$\boxed{\text{Sensitivity} = 12}$$

**Significance in real-world engine performance (3 Marks):**

1. **AKI = 90 (posted octane):** In the US, this is what would appear on the fuel pump label. The fuel can be safely used in engines requiring regular-grade (AKI 87) or mid-grade (AKI 89) fuel. Modern high-compression engines (CR ~10:1) typically need AKI 91–93, so this fuel is **borderline** for such engines.

2. **Sensitivity = 12 (high sensitivity):** This indicates a high aromatic or olefinic content. The fuel has:
   - **Good city/low-speed performance** (RON-dominant regime: 96 RON, knock-free)
   - **Degraded highway/high-speed performance** (MON-dominant regime: 84 MON, more prone to knock under high load and temperature)

3. **Engineering recommendation:** For high-speed, high-temperature operation (e.g., long highway drives with full load), a fuel with **lower sensitivity (≤8)** is preferred, such as isomerized or alkylated refinery streams. Modern vehicles with knock sensors and ECU-controlled ignition timing can compensate up to a point, but persistent high-sensitivity fuel may trigger retard-spark, reducing fuel economy and power.

4. **Refinery implication:** Refiners may blend with **isomerate or alkylate** to reduce sensitivity and bring MON closer to RON, producing a more robust universal fuel.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in Octane & Cetane Rating Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing RON with AKI** — RON is the test value; AKI is the average (RON+MON)/2. The fuel pump value (in USA) is AKI, not RON. **Lose 1 mark per sub-part** if confused.
>
> 2. **Writing "Cetane Index = Cetane Number"** — These are NOT the same. CN requires actual engine testing (ASTM D613). CI is an empirical calculated value from density and distillation (ASTM D4737). Examiner deducts **1 mark** for this error.
>
> 3. **Forgetting RON and MON test conditions** — RON is at 600 rpm, ambient intake; MON is at 900 rpm, 148 °C intake. Writing them as identical loses **1 mark** in comparison questions.
>
> 4. **Stating that "high cetane is always better"** — This is a trap. **Excessively high CN (>65)** causes rapid pressure rise, injector tip coking, and increased NOₓ. The optimum range is **45–55** for high-speed CI engines.
>
> 5. **Mixing up reference fuels** — n-Heptane is the **zero** reference for octane (not for cetane). α-Methylnaphthalene is the **zero** reference for cetane (not for octane). Examiner deducts **1 mark** for reversal.
>
> 6. **Writing chemical formulas incorrectly** — Iso-octane = 2,2,4-trimethylpentane (C₈H₁₈); n-cetane = n-hexadecane (C₁₆H₃₄). Wrong formula = ½ mark deducted per occurrence.
>
> 7. **Omitting the unit of ignition delay** — Always state it in **milliseconds (ms)** or **crank angle degrees (°CA)**. Examiner expects units.
>
> 8. **In derivation questions, skipping intermediate algebraic steps** — Show every term. The "exhaustive derivation" mandate of the 2024 scheme penalizes step-skipping heavily (1–2 marks lost per skipped step).

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- 🔑 **Octane Number (ON):** % of iso-octane in a matching iso-octane / n-heptane blend. **Higher ON = better knock resistance for SI engines.**
- 🔑 **Cetane Number (CN):** % of n-cetane in a matching n-cetane / α-methylnaphthalene blend. **Higher CN = shorter ignition delay for CI engines.**
- 🔑 **AKI (posted octane, USA) = (RON + MON) / 2.**
- 🔑 **Sensitivity = RON − MON** (high sensitivity = aromatic / olefinic fuels).
- 🔑 **RON test:** 600 rpm, ambient intake, ASTM D2699.
- 🔑 **MON test:** 900 rpm, 148 °C intake, ASTM D2700.
- 🔑 **CN test:** CFR CI engine, ASTM D613, ignition delay matched.
- 🔑 **Cetane Index (CI):** Empirical value from density + distillation; ASTM D4737. **Not same as CN.**
- 🔑 **Ignition Delay = Physical Delay + Chemical Delay** (in CI engines).
- 🔑 **Branched paraffins → ↑ Octane, ↓ Cetane.** **Long straight paraffins → ↓ Octane, ↑ Cetane.** **Aromatics → ↑ Octane, ↓ Cetane.**
- 🔑 **Modern Indian petrol grades:** Regular 91 RON, Premium 95 RON. **Indian diesel (BS-VI):** CN ≥ 51.
- 🔑 **Anti-knock additives (petrol):** MTBE, ETBE, Ethanol, Toluene, Xylene. **TEL/TML banned.**
- 🔑 **Ignition improver additives (diesel):** EHN (2-Ethylhexyl nitrate), DTBP, Amyl nitrate, Cyclohexyl nitrate.
- 🔑 **Engine knock (SI)** = premature end-gas auto-ignition → loss of power, mechanical damage.
- 🔑 **Diesel knock (CI)** = long ignition delay → sudden pressure rise, noise, emissions.
- 🔑 **Modern SI engine CR limit:** 10:1 to 12:1 (limited by fuel ON).
- 🔑 **Higher ON fuel** → can use higher CR → higher **Otto cycle efficiency** $\eta = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}}$.
- 🔑 **Optimum CN range for high-speed CI engines:** 45–55. Avoid CN > 65 (injector coking, NOₓ).
- 🔑 **Alternative fuels ratings (for general awareness):** CNG ≈ 120 CN, LPG ≈ 100 MON, Ethanol ≈ 108 RON, Methanol ≈ 108 RON, Biodiesel (FAME) ≈ 50–60 CN, Hydrogen ≈ 130+ RON.
- 🔑 **ASTM Standards to memorize:** D2699 (RON), D2700 (MON), D613 (CN), D4737 (CI).
- 🔑 **In exam derivations,** always show units, intermediate steps, and final boxed answer with correct significant figures.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Fuel Feed Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAUT205 - AUTOMOBILE POWER PLANT (AUTOMOBILE ENGINEERING) > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Types of Fuel Feed Systems # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Fuel Feed System (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):** > A *fuel feed system* is the assembly of components (tank, pipes, fuel pump, filter, carburetor/injectors, and pressure regulators) responsible for delivering the correct quantity of clean fuel at the correct pressure and at the correct time from the fuel reservoir to the engine's combustion chamber. The classification of feed systems is based on **the energy source used to transport the fuel** from the tank to the engine. In the **KTU PCAUT205 (2024 Scheme)** syllabus, Module 2 categorizes fuel feed systems into four functional types, applicable to both Spark Ignition (SI) and Compression Ignition (CI) engines: 1. **Gravity Feed System** 2. **Pressure (Suction / Pump) Feed System** 3. **Vacuum Feed System** 4. **Force Feed (Pressurized Injection) System** ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain-English Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — "The Water Cooler & Straw"** > Imagine you have a water tank on top of your office building. There are four ways you can drink from it: > - **Gravity feed** = You keep the tank *above* your head and just open the tap. Water falls down on its own. *(Fuel tank above carburetor.)* > - **Pressure feed** = You use a small hand pump (like a bicycle pump) to *push* water up the pipe. *(Mechanical fuel pump on the engine.)* > - **Vacuum feed** = You *suck* the water up with a straw. *(Engine vacuum pulls fuel up.)* > - **Force feed** = You connect the tank to a high-pressure power washer that *injects* a precise squirt of water exactly when needed. *(Diesel common-rail injection.)* > > The choice of system depends on where the fuel tank is located, the engine's fuel requirements, and the operating pressure needed. ## 1.3 Key Physical Parameters & Standard Metrics The following standard automotive engineering parameters govern fuel feed system design: - **Fuel Pressure ($P_f$):** typically **2.5 – 4.5 bar (250 – 450 kPa)** for modern port injection; **150 – 2000 bar** for diesel common-rail systems. - **Fuel Flow Rate ($\dot{V}_f$):** measured in **L/h** or **kg/h**. - **Static Head ($h_s$):** the vertical distance between the fuel level in the tank and the carburetor/inlet — critical for gravity feed. - **Suction Head ($h_{suc}$):** the vertical lift a pump must overcome — typically limited to **~0.5 m of fuel** to avoid cavitation. - **Atmospheric Pressure ($P_{atm}$):** **101.325 kPa** (standard), **≈ 1.01325 bar**. ## 1.4 Functional Objectives of Any Fuel Feed System (KTU Board-Examiner Perspective) A KTU examiner expects students to list these four core objectives: | # | Objective | Practical Significance | |---|-----------|------------------------| | 1 | Deliver a **continuous & adequate** supply of fuel | Prevents engine starvation under acceleration | | 2 | Maintain **correct pressure** at the injection/carburetion point | Ensures proper atomization & metering | | 3 | Supply **clean fuel** (filtration) | Prevents injector & pump wear | | 4 | Provide **vapor-free** flow (no vapour lock) | Critical in hot climates / Indian summer conditions | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of Fuel Feed Systems (Detailed Breakdown) ### A. Gravity Feed System **Operating Principle:** The fuel tank is positioned *above* the carburetor float chamber. Fuel flows downward under the action of gravitational potential energy. **Operational Steps (KTU Board-Relevant Logic):** - Fuel is stored in a tank mounted *higher* than the carburetor (e.g., pre-war cars, some tractors, certain two-wheelers). - Gravity provides the potential energy $\rho g h$ to drive the flow. - The **needle-and-seat valve** in the float chamber regulates the level. - A *sediment bowl* and *strainer* are typically placed before the carburetor. **Advantages (Why examiners award marks for knowing these):** - No moving pump parts → **high reliability**. - Simple, cheap, and easy to service. - No risk of fuel leakage due to pump failure. **Limitations:** - Requires fuel tank to be mounted *high* → impractical for modern low-slung cars. - Risk of fuel siphoning through the carburetor if the engine stops and the vehicle is tilted. - Unsuitable for diesel engines (which need high pressure). > [!NOTE] > **KTU Viva Tip:** A common viva question is *"Why don't modern cars use gravity feed?"* The answer: modern cars are aerodynamically low and have crumple zones, so placing the tank above the engine is impossible. Also, diesels need **200+ bar** of injection pressure, which gravity cannot provide. ### B. Pressure (Suction/Pump) Feed System **Operating Principle:** A mechanically or electrically driven pump *sucks* fuel from the tank (which may be below the carburetor) and delivers it to the float chamber/injectors at a slightly positive pressure. **Sub-Types:** **(i) Mechanical (Diaphragm) Fuel Pump** — *the classic KTU exam favourite* - Driven by an eccentric cam on the camshaft. - Uses a flexible diaphragm, pull-rod, inlet (suction) valve, and outlet (delivery) valve. - Produces a *pulsating* flow. - Typical delivery pressure: **0.2 – 0.5 bar** above atmospheric. **Operational Sequence (Bulleted Logic for the Examiner):** - Cam rotates → eccentric pushes pull-rod. - Pull-rod pulls diaphragm *downwards* → inlet valve opens → fuel drawn into the pump chamber. - Cam continues → spring pushes diaphragm *upwards* → inlet valve closes, outlet valve opens → fuel pushed to the carburetor. - A separate pressure relief valve in the carburetor float chamber vents excess fuel back to the tank. **(ii) Electrical Fuel Pump (Submerged/In-Line)** - A 12 V DC motor-driven vane or gerotor pump. - Mounted *in* the fuel tank (submerged) or *in-line* along the fuel line. - Provides **constant 3–4 bar** pressure regardless of engine speed. - Modern electronic fuel injection (EFI) systems use this exclusively. **Advantages:** - Tank can be placed *anywhere* (usually at the rear, below the carburetor) → design flexibility. - Consistent fuel delivery irrespective of vehicle attitude. - No fuel siphoning risk. **Limitations:** - Pump failure → engine stops. - Electrical pump can fail silently (no mechanical warning). ### C. Vacuum Feed System **Operating Principle:** The *intake manifold vacuum* (created by the descending piston during the intake stroke) is used to *suck* fuel from a tank located below the carburetor. **KTU-Mandated Operating Steps:** - Carburetor venturi creates a low-pressure region. - A vacuum line connects the intake manifold to the fuel chamber above the float. - Atmospheric pressure acting on the fuel in the tank pushes the fuel up the line. - Rarely used in modern vehicles (replaced by electric pumps), but appears in **KTU theory questions**. **Why examiners test this:** It links the physics of *Bernoulli's principle* with the *continuity equation* — a favourite 7-mark question. ### D. Force Feed System (Pressurized Injection Feed) **Operating Principle:** Fuel is delivered to the combustion chamber at *very high pressure* (often above 200 bar) via a high-pressure injection pump, completely independent of intake manifold vacuum. **Sub-Types for Diesel Engines (KTU Module 2 focus):** - **Individual Pump + Nozzle (P–L, P–D):** One pump per cylinder. - **Unit Injector (UI):** Pump and injector combined. - **Distributor Pump (Rotary):** Single pump with a rotor distributing to each cylinder (e.g., Bosch VE, Lucas DPA/DPS). - **Common Rail Direct Injection (CRDi):** A single high-pressure rail feeds all injectors electronically — used in *almost all modern BS-VI cars*. **For Petrol/Gasoline (GDI – Gasoline Direct Injection):** - **Multi-Point Fuel Injection (MPFI)** — one injector per cylinder in the port. - **Direct Injection (GDI)** — injector inside the cylinder, 100–350 bar. ## 2.2 Comparative Analysis Table (Board-Exam Favourite) | Feature | Gravity Feed | Pressure Feed | Vacuum Feed | Force Feed | |---------|-------------|---------------|-------------|------------| | Energy Source | Gravity (PE) | Mechanical/Electric Pump | Manifold Vacuum | High-Pressure Pump | | Tank Position | Above engine | Anywhere | Below engine | Anywhere | | Delivery Pressure | < 0.05 bar | 0.2 – 4 bar | < 0 bar (vacuum) | 200 – 2000 bar | | Modern Use | Vintage cars, tractors | SI engines (carb + MPFI) | Obsolete | All modern CI & GDI | | Engine Type | SI only | SI only | SI only | SI (GDI) & CI | | Risk of Vapour Lock | Low | Moderate | High | Negligible (returns vented) | ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description / Use | Units (SI) | |---|---------|------------------|------------| | 1 | $h_s = \dfrac{P_f - P_{atm}}{\rho g}$ | Static head for gravity feed | m | | 2 | $P_f = P_{atm} + \rho g h_s$ | Pressure at carburetor due to gravity | Pa | | 3 | $Q = A \cdot v$ | Volumetric flow rate (continuity) | m³/s | | 4 | $A_1 v_1 = A_2 v_2$ | Continuity equation (incompressible fuel) | m³/s | | 5 | $P_1 + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho v_1^2 + \rho g z_1 = P_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho v_2^2 + \rho g z_2$ | Bernoulli's equation (ideal, frictionless) | Pa | | 6 | $\dot{W}_{pump} = \rho g Q H_{pump}$ | Theoretical pump power (no losses) | W | | 7 | $\eta_{pump} = \dfrac{\rho g Q H}{\dot{W}_{shaft}}$ | Pump efficiency (volumetric × hydraulic × mechanical) | — | | 8 | $\Delta P_{line} = \dfrac{32 \mu L v}{D^2}$ | Hagen-Poiseuille pressure drop in a fuel line | Pa | | 9 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho v D}{\mu}$ | Reynolds number for fuel flow regime check | — | | 10 | $h_{vapor} = \dfrac{P_{atm} - P_{vapor}}{\rho g}$ | Maximum suction head to avoid vapour lock | m | | 11 | $Q_{theoretical} = V_d \cdot \dfrac{N}{60} \cdot \eta_{vol}$ | Theoretical pump displacement | m³/s | | 12 | $P_{injection} = P_{rail} - \Delta P_{line}$ | Pressure at injector nozzle | Pa | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Examiner's Hint:** Always carry forward the *units* in your final answer. A common deduction is **1 mark** for missing units in numerical problems. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Automotive OEMs** (e.g., Maruti Suzuki, Tata Motors, Mahindra) use **CRDi** in diesel and **MPFI / GDi** in petrol — all force-feed variants. - **Aerospace fuel systems** use *pressure feed* for reliability at altitude. - **Motorcycles** still use *gravity feed* in some commuter models (e.g., older Bajaj M80, TVS XL). - **Racing (F1)** uses *direct force-feed injection* with pressures exceeding **500 bar** to maximize atomization. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Pressure at the Carburetor Float Chamber (Gravity Feed) **Problem Statement:** A vehicle uses a gravity feed system. The fuel tank is positioned at a height of $H$ above the carburetor float chamber. Show that the static pressure at the float chamber inlet is: $$P_c = P_{atm} + \rho g H$$ **Given / Assumptions:** - Steady, incompressible, frictionless flow of fuel. - Atmospheric pressure acts on the free surface of the fuel in the tank. - The fuel surface in the tank is at rest ($v_1 \approx 0$). - The pipe cross-section is constant. **Exhaustive Step-by-Step Derivation:** Step 1 — Define the two points in the flow field. - Point 1: Free surface of fuel in the tank. - Point 2: Inlet of the carburetor float chamber, height $H$ below Point 1. Step 2 — Apply Bernoulli's equation between Point 1 and Point 2. $$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{v_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{v_2^2}{2g} + z_2$$ Step 3 — Substitute the known boundary conditions. - $P_1 = P_{atm}$ (free surface exposed to atmosphere) - $v_1 \approx 0$ (large tank, negligible velocity) - $z_1 = H$ (reference datum taken at carburetor) - $z_2 = 0$ (datum reference) - $P_2 = P_c$ (the unknown carburetor pressure) - $v_2 = v$ (velocity in the delivery pipe) Step 4 — Substitute all values. $$\frac{P_{atm}}{\rho g} + 0 + H = \frac{P_c}{\rho g} + \frac{v^2}{2g} + 0$$ Step 5 — Solve algebraically for $P_c$. $$\frac{P_c}{\rho g} = \frac{P_{atm}}{\rho g} + H - \frac{v^2}{2g}$$ $$P_c = P_{atm} + \rho g H - \frac{1}{2}\rho v^2$$ Step 6 — For a *gravity feed* system with a relatively large float-chamber reservoir, the velocity head term $\frac{1}{2}\rho v^2$ is typically very small (large pipe diameter, low flow). Neglecting it for a slow steady flow: $$\boxed{P_c \approx P_{atm} + \rho g H}$$ **Engineering Interpretation:** For petrol (density $\rho \approx 720 \text{ kg/m}^3$) and $H = 0.5$ m, the additional pressure is: $$P_c - P_{atm} = 720 \times 9.81 \times 0.5 \approx 3532 \text{ Pa} \approx 0.035 \text{ bar}$$ This confirms that gravity feed can provide only a very small positive pressure — sufficient for a carburetor float chamber, but **completely inadequate** for fuel injection. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Maximum Permissible Suction Head to Avoid Vapour Lock **Problem Statement:** A mechanical fuel pump is located at the same height as the fuel tank. The fuel in the line can vaporize if the local pressure drops below the saturated vapour pressure $P_v$ of the fuel. Find the maximum permissible vertical height $h_{max}$ through which the pump can lift the fuel. **Exhaustive Step-by-Step Derivation:** Step 1 — Apply Bernoulli's equation between the free surface of the fuel in the tank (Point 1) and the pump inlet (Point 2). $$\frac{P_{atm}}{\rho g} + 0 + 0 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{v^2}{2g} + h$$ where $h$ is the suction head (vertical lift), and the datum is at the free surface. Step 2 — Solve for the pump inlet pressure $P_2$. $$\frac{P_2}{\rho g} = \frac{P_{atm}}{\rho g} - \frac{v^2}{2g} - h$$ $$P_2 = P_{atm} - \frac{1}{2}\rho v^2 - \rho g h$$ Step 3 — To **avoid cavitation and vapour lock**, the pressure at the pump inlet must remain *above* the saturated vapour pressure $P_v$ of the fuel. $$P_2 \geq P_v$$ Step 4 — Substitute the expression for $P_2$. $$P_{atm} - \frac{1}{2}\rho v^2 - \rho g h_{max} \geq P_v$$ Step 5 — Solve for $h_{max}$. $$\rho g h_{max} \leq P_{atm} - P_v - \frac{1}{2}\rho v^2$$ $$\boxed{h_{max} = \frac{P_{atm} - P_v - \frac{1}{2}\rho v^2}{\rho g}}$$ Step 6 — Numerical evaluation for petrol at $35^\circ$C. - $P_{atm} = 101325$ Pa - $P_v$ (petrol at 35°C) $\approx 55000$ Pa - $\rho$ (petrol) $= 720$ kg/m³ - $v$ (flow velocity in 8 mm ID pipe at 30 L/h) $\approx 0.166$ m/s - $g = 9.81$ m/s² Plugging in: $$h_{max} = \frac{101325 - 55000 - \frac{1}{2}(720)(0.166)^2}{720 \times 9.81}$$ $$h_{max} = \frac{101325 - 55000 - 9.92}{7063.2}$$ $$h_{max} = \frac{46315.1}{7063.2}$$ $$\boxed{h_{max} \approx 6.56 \text{ m of petrol}}$$ **Engineering Interpretation:** In practice, this theoretical limit is *never* reached because of: - Friction losses in pipes/fittings (3–5 %). - Acceleration head losses. - A safety margin of typically **0.5 m of fuel** is recommended. > [!NOTE] > This is the classical reason why fuel tanks in modern cars are designed to be at the **rear, below the engine level** — to keep the suction head low and avoid vapour lock in Indian summer conditions. ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Theoretical Pump Displacement & Volumetric Efficiency **Problem Statement:** A diaphragm fuel pump has a diaphragm effective area of $A_d = 25$ cm² and a stroke of $s = 4$ mm. If the pump runs at 1500 rpm with a volumetric efficiency of 85 %, find the theoretical and actual fuel delivery. **Exhaustive Step-by-Step Solution:** Step 1 — Stroke volume (one cycle). $$V_d = A_d \times s = 25 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2 \times 4 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$$ $$V_d = 100 \times 10^{-7} = 1 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3 = 10 \text{ cm}^3 = 10 \text{ mL}$$ Step 2 — Number of pumping strokes per second. At 1500 rpm, with one stroke per revolution: $$n = \frac{1500}{60} = 25 \text{ strokes/s}$$ Step 3 — Theoretical flow rate. $$Q_{th} = V_d \times n = 10 \times 10^{-6} \times 25$$ $$Q_{th} = 250 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 0.25 \text{ L/s} = 1.5 \text{ L/min} = 90 \text{ L/h}$$ Step 4 — Apply volumetric efficiency. $$Q_{act} = \eta_{vol} \times Q_{th} = 0.85 \times 90 = 76.5 \text{ L/h}$$ $$\boxed{Q_{act} \approx 76.5 \text{ L/h}}$$ **Engineering Interpretation:** A typical 1500 cc petrol engine consumes roughly 30–40 L/h at cruise, so a 76.5 L/h pump has adequate *over-capacity*. The surplus fuel is returned to the tank through the pressure regulator — a critical design point in MPFI systems. ## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing the Right Feed System | Engine Application | Required Pressure | Recommended Feed System | KTU Justification | |--------------------|------------------|--------------------------|-------------------| | Vintage car (1930s Austin) | < 0.1 bar | Gravity | Simple, no electricity | | Honda Activa (carb) | 0.2 – 0.4 bar | Vacuum / Mechanical | Float chamber design | | Maruti Alto K10 (MPFI) | 3.0 – 4.0 bar | Electric pressure | EFI needs regulated pressure | | Tata Nexon EV (Regen) | N/A | N/A | No ICE fuel system | | Tata Nexon Diesel (CRDi) | 1600 – 2000 bar | Common Rail (Force) | BS-VI emissions | | Yamaha R15 V4 (FI) | 2.5 – 3.5 bar | Electric pressure | Fuel injection | # 4. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]** > *"Define a fuel feed system. List any four essential requirements of a fuel feed system used in a modern SI engine."* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** A fuel feed system is the arrangement of components that delivers the required quantity of clean fuel at the correct pressure and at the correct time from the fuel tank to the engine. **Four essential requirements:** 1. **Continuous supply** of fuel under all operating conditions (idle to WOT). 2. **Correct pressure** at the carburetor/injector for proper atomization and metering. 3. **Clean fuel** — adequate filtration to remove dirt, rust, and water. 4. **Vapour-free delivery** — system must prevent vapour lock at high ambient temperatures. *(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for any 4 valid points.)* --- ### Question 2 > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** > *"Differentiate between gravity feed and force feed fuel systems with two points of comparison."* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Parameter | Gravity Feed | Force Feed | |-----------|-------------|------------| | Energy source | Gravitational potential energy ($\rho g h$) | High-pressure injection pump (200–2000 bar) | | Tank position | Mounted *above* the carburetor | Can be mounted *anywhere* (usually below) | | Pressure range | < 0.05 bar above atmospheric | 200–2000 bar for diesel; 100–350 bar for GDI | | Engine application | Old/vintage SI engines | All modern CI engines and GDI petrol engines | | Cost & complexity | Very low | High (pump, rail, ECU, sensors) | *(1 mark for stating both systems + 2 marks for any 2 valid comparison points.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern) > **KTU 2024 Pattern Note:** Each Part-B question carries 14 marks split as **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**, with internal choice between two question sets. --- ### Question Choice A (14 Marks) — *Traditional & Conceptual Focus* > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]** #### Part (a) — 7 Marks *"With a neat sketch, explain the working of a mechanical diaphragm-type fuel pump used in an SI engine. List its merits and demerits."* **Model Answer (Step-by-Step):** **Construction (2 marks):** - Mounted on the engine block, driven by a camshaft eccentric via a pull-rod. - Key components: **body, diaphragm, pull-rod, return spring, inlet valve, outlet valve**. **Working (3 marks):** 1. As the cam rotates, the eccentric pushes the pull-rod. 2. **Suction stroke:** Pull-rod pulls the diaphragm *down*. Low pressure in the pump chamber opens the *inlet valve*. Fuel is drawn from the tank into the pump chamber. 3. **Delivery stroke:** Cam releases, the return spring pushes the diaphragm *up*. Inlet valve closes, *outlet valve* opens, and fuel is delivered to the carburetor float chamber. 4. A pressure relief in the carburetor vents excess fuel back to the tank. **Merits (1 mark):** - Simple, cheap, mechanically self-contained (no electricity required). - Reliable for carbureted SI engines. **Demerits (1 mark):** - Pulsating flow; cam-driven so flow reduces at low engine speeds (risk of fuel starvation at idle). - Cannot deliver high pressure → unsuitable for EFI/diesel. --- #### Part (b) — 7 Marks *"A fuel feed system has a tank mounted 0.6 m above the carburetor. Calculate the pressure at the carburetor float chamber assuming negligible velocity head. Take density of petrol as 720 kg/m³ and g = 9.81 m/s². Comment on whether this pressure is sufficient for a multi-point fuel injection system."* **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Identify known values.** - Height $H = 0.6$ m - Density of petrol $\rho = 720$ kg/m³ - $g = 9.81$ m/s² - $P_{atm} = 101325$ Pa **Step 2 — Apply the gravity feed equation.** $$P_c = P_{atm} + \rho g H$$ **Step 3 — Substitute.** $$P_c = 101325 + 720 \times 9.81 \times 0.6$$ $$P_c = 101325 + 4237.92$$ $$P_c = 105562.92 \text{ Pa} \approx 1.056 \text{ bar (gauge: 0.056 bar)}$$ **Step 4 — Comment on adequacy for MPFI.** An MPFI system requires **3.0 – 4.0 bar** regulated pressure at the injector. The gravity-feed pressure of **0.056 bar gauge** is **insufficient by a factor of ~60**. Therefore, a **mechanical/electric pump** is mandatory for MPFI; gravity feed is unsuitable. *(1 mark formula + 2 marks substitution + 2 marks numerical answer + 2 marks valid engineering comment.)* --- ### Question Choice B (14 Marks) — *Numerical & Advanced System Focus* > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]** #### Part (a) — 7 Marks *"Explain the working of a Common Rail Direct Injection (CRDi) system with a block diagram. What is the role of the pressure control valve?"* **Model Answer:** **Components (2 marks):** - **Fuel tank**, **feed (lift) pump**, **fine filter**, **high-pressure pump** (typically a radial-piston pump driven by the camshaft), **common rail (accumulator)**, **injectors**, **ECU**, **pressure sensor**, **pressure control valve (PCV)**, **return line**. **Working Sequence (3 marks):** 1. The **feed pump** lifts fuel from the tank at low pressure (~ 0.5 bar). 2. Fuel passes through a **fine filter** to remove particulates and water. 3. The **high-pressure pump** compresses the fuel to **1600–2000 bar**. 4. Pressurized fuel is stored in the **common rail** (a high-pressure accumulator). 5. The **ECU** reads inputs (rpm, load, pedal position, coolant temperature) and energises the **injectors** at the precise moment. 6. Each **injector** sprays a finely atomized mist directly into the cylinder. 7.
