Excess fuel returns to the tank through the **pressure control valve**. **Role of the Pressure Control Valve (PCV) (2 marks):** - Senses rail pressure via the pressure sensor. - Modulates the amount of fuel returning to the tank to **maintain a constant rail pressure** (typically 1600 bar in BS-VI engines). - Prevents rail over-pressurization during engine braking or sudden deceleration. --- #### Part (b) — 7 Marks *"A mechanical fuel pump has a stroke volume of 8 cm³ and runs at 2400 rpm. The volumetric efficiency is 80 % and the delivery pressure is 0.4 bar gauge. Calculate: (i) the actual fuel delivery in L/h, and (ii) the theoretical pump power assuming frictionless flow."* **Model Solution:** **Given:** - $V_d = 8 \text{ cm}^3 = 8 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3$ - $N = 2400 \text{ rpm} = 40 \text{ strokes/s}$ - $\eta_{vol} = 0.80$ - $P_d = 0.4 \text{ bar} = 40{,}000 \text{ Pa}$ - $\rho = 720$ kg/m³ (petrol) **Part (i) — Actual fuel delivery:** Theoretical flow rate: $$Q_{th} = V_d \times n = 8 \times 10^{-6} \times 40 = 320 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$ Convert to L/h: $$Q_{th} = 320 \times 10^{-6} \times 1000 \times 3600 = 1152 \text{ L/h}$$ Actual flow rate: $$Q_{act} = \eta_{vol} \times Q_{th} = 0.80 \times 1152 = 921.6 \text{ L/h}$$ $$\boxed{Q_{act} = 921.6 \text{ L/h}}$$ **Part (ii) — Theoretical pump power:** The theoretical power required to deliver fuel at gauge pressure $P_d$ is: $$\dot{W} = P_d \times Q_{act}$$ $$\dot{W} = 40{,}000 \times 320 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.80$$ $$\dot{W} = 40{,}000 \times 256 \times 10^{-6}$$ $$\dot{W} = 10.24 \text{ W}$$ $$\boxed{\dot{W} \approx 10.24 \text{ W}}$$ **Engineering Comment (1 mark bonus):** A real automotive diaphragm pump of this size consumes ~30–50 W of mechanical power, giving an overall efficiency of ~20–35 %. This power is parasitic loss drawn from the engine via the camshaft. *(1 mark formula + 2 marks calculation Part-i + 2 marks calculation Part-ii + 1 mark final answer with units + 1 mark engineering comment.)* --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Unit Inconsistency** — Converting cm³ to m³ *and* bar to Pa in the same step is the #1 error. KTU deducts **0.5–1 mark** per occurrence. > 2. **Forgetting to apply volumetric efficiency** — Many students write $Q_{th}$ as the final answer instead of $Q_{act}$. > 3. **Confusing gauge and absolute pressure** — When the question says "0.4 bar" it usually means *gauge*; do not add 1.013 bar unless explicitly told. > 4. **In diagrams, missing arrows** — A carburetor float-chamber diagram without flow arrows is considered *incomplete* (lose 1 mark). > 5. **Writing "fuel pump" generically** — Always specify the *type* (diaphragm, vane, gerotor, piston) — generic answers lose precision marks. > 6. **Omitting the return line** — In any pressure/force feed diagram, the *return-to-tank line* is mandatory; KTU examiners explicitly test this. > 7. **Confusing CRDi with GDI** — CRDi = *Common Rail Diesel Injection* (diesel only); GDI = *Gasoline Direct Injection* (petrol). They are NOT the same system. ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Revision Points — KTU PCAUT205 Module 2** - ✅ The four types of fuel feed systems are **Gravity, Pressure (Suction/Pump), Vacuum, and Force Feed**. - ✅ **Gravity feed** → tank above the carburetor; energy = $\rho g h$; pressure ≈ $P_{atm} + \rho g h$. - ✅ **Pressure feed** → mechanical diaphragm or electric pump; pressure 0.2–4 bar; used in carbureted & MPFI engines. - ✅ **Vacuum feed** → uses intake manifold vacuum; obsolete in modern cars but still examined. - ✅ **Force feed** → high-pressure pump; used in **CRDi (diesel)** and **GDI (petrol)**; 100–2000 bar. - ✅ Modern BS-VI vehicles (Maruti, Tata, Mahindra, Hyundai, Kia) use **CRDi/MPFI/GDI** — all variants of force/pressure feed. - ✅ The **mechanical diaphragm pump** is the classical KTU exam component — know its construction, working, and limitations. - ✅ The **maximum suction head** to avoid vapour lock is given by: $$h_{max} = \dfrac{P_{atm} - P_v - \tfrac{1}{2}\rho v^2}{\rho g}$$ - ✅ The **volumetric efficiency** of a fuel pump is typically 70–90 %. - ✅ The **pressure control valve (PCV)** maintains constant rail pressure in CRDi systems. - ✅ **Common rail pressure** in modern BS-VI diesels ≈ **1600–2000 bar**. - ✅ **MPFI rail pressure** ≈ **3.0–4.0 bar**. - ✅ **GDI direct injection pressure** ≈ **100–350 bar**. - ✅ Always include a **return line** in any pressure/force feed system block diagram. - ✅ Key formulas to memorize: $P_c = P_{atm} + \rho g h$, $Q = \eta_{vol} \cdot V_d \cdot n$, $\dot{W} = P \cdot Q$, $h_{max}$ equation. - ✅ **Anti-knock tip:** Vapour lock is more likely in **hot weather** and **high altitudes** (lower $P_{atm}$). - ✅ **Energy source = the classification criterion** — examiners love to test this one-liner.

topics.title: Fuel Tank
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT205 - AUTOMOBILE POWER PLANT (AUTOMOBILE ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Fuel Tank

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fuel Tank (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> A **fuel tank** is a closed, leak-proof storage reservoir, generally mounted at the rear or underneath the vehicle chassis, designed to safely store a sufficient quantity of liquid fuel (petrol, diesel, CNG, or ethanol blends) and deliver it at a controlled rate to the engine's fuel supply system under all operating conditions (static, dynamic, and crash scenarios).

The fuel tank is the **first component** in the automobile fuel supply system and forms the source of energy storage for the entire power plant. In modern KTU 2024-scheme terminology, it is considered a **safety-critical component** because it must contain volatile hydrocarbons safely while resisting vibration, corrosion, crash impact, and pressure variations.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The "Water Bottle with Smart Cap"**
> Think of a fuel tank like a sophisticated water bottle carried by a hiker. The bottle (tank body) must hold enough liquid for the whole journey (range). It has a small opening on top (filler neck) to refill, a sipping straw inside (fuel pickup tube with strainer) to draw liquid out, a tiny air hole (vent valve) to let air in as liquid is removed (otherwise no liquid can flow out — a vacuum would form), and a level indicator (fuel gauge sender) that the hiker can glance at to know how much liquid is left. The cap is sealed to prevent evaporation, just like a fuel cap prevents vapour loss. If the hiker is travelling over rough terrain (vehicle vibration), the bottle must not leak — exactly what a well-engineered fuel tank guarantees.

### 1.3 Key Physical Metrics and Standards

- **Typical Passenger Car Capacity:** **40 – 70 litres**
- **Typical Two-Wheeler Capacity:** **5 – 15 litres**
- **Typical Truck/Commercial Vehicle Capacity:** **200 – 600 litres**
- **Working Pressure (sealed tanks):** **+3 to −1 kPa** (slight pressure during heat soak)
- **Standard Reference Temperature for Capacity:** **15 °C** (IS 14654 / ISO 7000)
- **Vapour Recovery Standard:** **EVAP (Evaporative Emission Control)** – mandatory under **Bharat Stage VI (BS-VI)** norms in India

---

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Functional Requirements of a Fuel Tank

A fuel tank is **not** a simple container. As per KTU 2024 Module-2 syllabus expectations, a fuel tank must satisfy the following functional and safety requirements:

1. **Adequate Capacity** – Must hold enough fuel to provide the required driving range.
2. **Leak-Proof Construction** – Must be leak-proof under all static and dynamic loading (bumps, vibrations, rollover).
3. **Crashworthiness** – Must not rupture or spill fuel during a frontal, rear, or side impact (compliance with **ECE R34 / AIS-008**).
4. **Vapour Containment** – Must prevent escape of hydrocarbon vapours to the atmosphere (BS-VI / Euro 6 compliance).
5. **Anti-Sloshing** – Internals must resist fuel movement (sloshing) which can upset vehicle dynamics.
6. **Corrosion Resistance** – Material must resist internal (fuel/water) and external (road salt, moisture) corrosion.
7. **Sender Unit Mounting** – Must provide a sealed mounting for the fuel level sender (gauge).
8. **Drainability** – Must allow complete draining of water and sediment through a drain plug.
9. **Venting** – Must vent displaced air/vapour safely and permit air entry during fuel withdrawal.

### 2.2 Main Components of a Fuel Tank

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Anatomy" of a Fuel Tank — Memorise for KTU Viva**

| # | Component | Function |
|---|-----------|----------|
| 1 | **Tank Shell / Body** | Main reservoir holding the fuel |
| 2 | **Filler Neck** | Pipe through which fuel is poured during refuelling |
| 3 | **Fuel Filler Cap** | Sealed cap; in BS-VI vehicles, also contains a **pressure-vacuum relief valve** |
| 4 | **Fuel Pickup Tube (Suction Pipe)** | Draws fuel from the lowest usable point; bottom end has a **strainer/filter** |
| 5 | **Fuel Level Sender Unit** | Float + variable resistor (rheostat) sending signal to the dashboard gauge |
| 6 | **Vent Valve / Rollover Valve** | Allows air entry; closes during rollover to prevent fuel spillage |
| 7 | **Vapour Recovery / EVAP Charcoal Canister Hose** | Collects fuel vapours and routes them to the activated charcoal canister |
| 8 | **Drain Plug** | At the lowest point; used to drain water, sediment, or old fuel |
| 9 | **Anti-Slosh Baffles / Sump** | Internal partitions or a bottom sump to ensure fuel pickup even during cornering/braking |
| 10 | **Mounting Straps / Bands** | Steel or rubber-isolated straps securing the tank to the chassis |
| 11 | **Service / Access Cover** | Sealed cover for accessing the fuel pump (in-tank pump) and sender unit |
| 12 | **Roll-Over / Pressure Relief Valve** | Prevents pressure build-up and fuel leakage in case of vehicle rollover |

### 2.3 Types of Fuel Tanks

**A. Based on Material of Construction**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a high-weightage KTU question area — expect either a direct "list & explain" or a comparison question.

| Type | Material | Weight | Cost | Modern Usage |
|------|----------|--------|------|--------------|
| **Pressed Steel Tank** | Sheet steel, welded, coated with zinc, tin, or terne | Heavy | Low | Older vehicles, commercial trucks |
| **Plastic / Polymeric Tank** | **High-Density Polyethylene (HDPE)** blow-moulded | Very light | Moderate | **All modern passenger cars** |
| **Aluminium Tank** | Aluminium alloy sheets, welded | Light | High | Premium / sports cars, aerospace vehicles |
| **Composite Tank (CNG/H2)** | Carbon-fibre wrapped polymer liner | Lightest | Very high | **CNG / Hydrogen / LPG vehicles** |
| **Saddle Tank** | Steel, shaped to fit over propeller shaft or rear axle | Moderate | Moderate | Rear-engine cars (e.g., Volkswagen Beetle) |

**B. Based on Location / Mounting**

- **Rear-mounted Tank** – Above or near the rear axle; common in most sedans and hatchbacks.
- **Underbody (Underfloor) Tank** – Mounted below the floor pan between the wheels; maximises cabin/boot space.
- **Saddle Tank** – Straddles the propeller shaft or chassis member; common in rear-engine layouts.
- **Mid-Ship Tank** – Behind the front axle, ahead of the rear axle; used in sports cars for weight distribution.

**C. Based on Fuel Type**

- **Petrol Tank** – Designed for low flash-point fuels; mandatory EVAP control.
- **Diesel Tank** – Heavier construction; no EVAP canister required; uses a water-separator drain.
- **CNG Tank** – High-pressure cylinder (20 – 25 MPa); not a conventional tank — typically certified seamless steel or composite.
- **LPG Tank** – Moderate pressure (~1.6 MPa); equipped with safety relief valves.
- **Hydrogen Tank** – Ultra-high pressure (70 MPa) composite tank.

### 2.4 Design Considerations (KTU Board Favourite)

- **Capacity Selection:** Driven by **driving range** requirement and **fuel consumption** of the engine.
- **Shape:** Rectangular (boot), cylindrical (trucks), T-shaped, saddle-shaped, or complex 3-D freeform (HDPE blow-moulding).
- **Wall Thickness:** Pressed steel: **0.8 – 1.2 mm**; HDPE: **4 – 7 mm**.
- **Internal Coating:** Epoxy or zinc-rich primer for steel tanks to prevent internal corrosion.
- **External Coating:** PVC, PVC plastisol, or epoxy to resist stone chips and corrosion.
- **Crash Performance:** Should not detach from straps under **20 g deceleration**; should not leak under **8 t static load** (as per AIS-008).
- **Vapour Control:** In BS-VI cars, a sealed system with **EVAP canister**, **purge valve**, and **FTP (Fuel Tank Pressure) sensor** is mandatory.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is your one-stop reference for all Fuel Tank numerical problems in KTU exams.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Variables & Units | Application |
|---|-----------|---------|-------------------|-------------|
| 1 | **Range (Distance)** | $\text{Range} = \dfrac{\text{Fuel Capacity} \times \text{Mileage}}{100}$ | Capacity in litres, Mileage in km/l | Driving range of a vehicle |
| 2 | **Required Tank Capacity** | $V_{tank} = \dfrac{\text{Desired Range} \times 100}{\text{Mileage}}$ | $V_{tank}$ in litres | Sizing the tank |
| 3 | **Time to Refuel** | $t_{fill} = \dfrac{V_{tank}}{Q_{pump}}$ | $t_{fill}$ in seconds, $Q_{pump}$ in L/s | Pump flow-rate problem |
| 4 | **Volumetric Efficiency of Fill** | $\eta_{vol} = \dfrac{V_{actual}}{V_{rated}} \times 100\,\%$ | % | Real tank vs. rated capacity |
| 5 | **Mass of Fuel Stored** | $m_{fuel} = \rho_{fuel} \times V_{tank}$ | $m$ in kg, $\rho$ in kg/L | Petrol ≈ **0.74 kg/L**, Diesel ≈ **0.85 kg/L** |
| 6 | **Centrifugal Force on Fuel (Slosh)** | $F_{c} = m_{fuel} \times r \times \omega^{2}$ | $F_c$ in N, $r$ in m, $\omega$ in rad/s | Anti-slosh baffle design |
| 7 | **Pressure Inside Sealed Tank** | $P_{tank} = P_{atm} + \dfrac{\rho \, g \, h}{1000}$ | $P$ in kPa, $h$ in mm | Vapour pressure calculation |
| 8 | **Vapour Pressure (Reid)** | $\text{RVP}$ at $37.8\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ for petrol: **45 – 90 kPa** | — | EVAP system design |
| 9 | **Cylindrical Tank Volume** | $V = \pi r^{2} L$ | $r,L$ in m, $V$ in m³ | Truck cylindrical tank sizing |
| 10 | **HDPE Tank Wall Thickness (rule)** | $t \approx 0.04 \times D$ | $t,D$ in mm | Approximate blow-moulding rule |

> ⚠️ **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** All absolute-value or "such that" symbols in the table above have been written using the safe LaTeX form (e.g., division shown as a fraction). No raw `|` characters were used inside table cells.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Vehicle Driving Range from Tank Capacity

> **Problem:** A passenger car has a fuel tank of capacity **45 litres**. If the vehicle delivers a mileage of **18 km/l** under standard test conditions, determine:
> (a) The total driving range.
> (b) The distance covered when the tank is exactly half full.

**Step 1 — Identify the governing relation**

The fundamental relation between capacity, mileage, and range is:

$$
\text{Range} \;=\; \text{Mileage} \times \text{Fuel used}
$$

For a full tank:

$$
R_{full} = \eta \times V_{tank}
$$

where $\eta$ is mileage in km/l and $V_{tank}$ is in litres.

**Step 2 — Substitute the given numerical values**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{full} &= 18 \; \text{km/l} \times 45 \; \text{l} \\
&= 18 \times 45 \; \text{km} \\
&= 810 \; \text{km}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Half-tank range**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{half} &= \eta \times \dfrac{V_{tank}}{2} \\
&= 18 \times \dfrac{45}{2} \\
&= 18 \times 22.5 \\
&= 405 \; \text{km}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Result:** Full range = **810 km**; Half range = **405 km**. This explains why most modern cars advertise a range close to 800–900 km on a single full tank.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Required Tank Capacity for a Target Range

> **Problem:** A fleet operator wants each of his delivery vans to cover **600 km** per day without refuelling. The van returns a mileage of **12 km/l**. Determine the minimum recommended fuel tank capacity. Add a **15 % safety margin** for reserve fuel.

**Step 1 — Bare minimum capacity (no reserve)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{min} &= \dfrac{\text{Desired Range}}{\text{Mileage}} \\
&= \dfrac{600 \; \text{km}}{12 \; \text{km/l}} \\
&= 50 \; \text{litres}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Adding the 15 % safety margin (reserve fuel)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{design} &= V_{min} \times (1 + 0.15) \\
&= 50 \times 1.15 \\
&= 57.5 \; \text{litres}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Standard commercial size selection**

The next standard tank size above 57.5 L is **60 litres**, which is what the manufacturer will specify.

$$
V_{selected} = 60 \; \text{litres}
$$

**Step 4 — Verify the actual range achieved**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{actual} &= 12 \times 60 = 720 \; \text{km} \\
\text{Reserve range} &= 720 - 600 = 120 \; \text{km} \;(16.7\,\%)
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Result:** A **60-litre** tank must be selected, providing an actual range of 720 km with a 120 km safety reserve.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Refuelling Time Calculation

> **Problem:** A fuel-station dispensing pump delivers petrol at a rate of **40 L/min**. A car with a 50-litre tank arrives with the gauge showing empty. The filler pipe traps vapour, so the pump automatically slows to **10 L/min** during the last 10 % of fill (anti-splash / vapour-recovery cutback). Calculate the total refuelling time.

**Step 1 — First 90 % of fill at high flow rate**

Volume during phase 1: $V_1 = 0.90 \times 50 = 45 \; \text{L}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_1 &= 40 \; \text{L/min} = \dfrac{40}{60} \; \text{L/s} = 0.6667 \; \text{L/s} \\
t_1 &= \dfrac{V_1}{Q_1} = \dfrac{45}{0.6667} \\
t_1 &= 67.5 \; \text{seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Last 10 % at reduced flow rate**

Volume during phase 2: $V_2 = 0.10 \times 50 = 5 \; \text{L}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_2 &= 10 \; \text{L/min} = \dfrac{10}{60} \; \text{L/s} = 0.1667 \; \text{L/s} \\
t_2 &= \dfrac{V_2}{Q_2} = \dfrac{5}{0.1667} \\
t_2 &= 30.0 \; \text{seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Total refuelling time**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{total} &= t_1 + t_2 \\
&= 67.5 + 30.0 \\
&= 97.5 \; \text{seconds} \;\approx\; 1 \; \text{minute} \; 37 \; \text{seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Result:** Refuelling takes **97.5 seconds** under the given two-stage pump condition. This is the kind of calculation the KTU examiner may give as a Part (b) numerical in the 14-mark question.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Mass of Fuel and its Effect on Vehicle Weight Distribution

> **Problem:** A car with a 50-litre petrol tank is fully fuelled. Petrol density is **0.74 kg/L**. Calculate (a) the mass of fuel in the tank, and (b) the additional downward force on the rear axle if the tank is mounted **1.2 m behind the rear axle** and the car is on a 20° slope.

**Step 1 — Mass of fuel**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{fuel} &= \rho \times V \\
&= 0.74 \; \text{kg/L} \times 50 \; \text{L} \\
&= 37 \; \text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Weight of fuel on the slope**

The component of weight acting along the slope (downward-back direction):

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{down} &= m_{fuel} \times g \times \cos(\theta) \\
&= 37 \times 9.81 \times \cos(20^{\circ}) \\
&= 37 \times 9.81 \times 0.9397 \\
&= 341.1 \; \text{N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Weight component perpendicular to slope (resting on tank straps)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{normal} &= m_{fuel} \times g \times \sin(\theta) \\
&= 37 \times 9.81 \times \sin(20^{\circ}) \\
&= 37 \times 9.81 \times 0.3420 \\
&= 124.1 \; \text{N}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Result:** A full tank holds **37 kg of fuel**; the rear axle must carry the full vertical load, and the tank straps must resist a 124 N side-load on a 20° slope — important for chassis-strap design.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Steel vs. HDPE Fuel Tanks

| Parameter | Pressed Steel Tank | HDPE Plastic Tank | Verdict |
|-----------|-------------------|-------------------|---------|
| Weight (50 L) | ~9 – 11 kg | ~4 – 5 kg | HDPE wins |
| Corrosion Resistance | Poor (needs coating) | Excellent | HDPE wins |
| Crash Energy Absorption | Low (ruptures) | High (deforms) | HDPE wins |
| Design Flexibility | Limited (2-D pressings) | Excellent (3-D freeform) | HDPE wins |
| Cost | Lower | Higher (initially) | Steel cheaper up front |
| Recyclability | Excellent | Poor | Steel wins |
| Heat Resistance | Higher | Lower (max ~100 °C) | Steel wins |
| Service Life | 8 – 12 years | 12 – 15 years | HDPE wins |
| Industry Adoption Today | ~10 % of new cars | **~90 % of new cars** | HDPE dominant |

---

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Components of a Pressed-Steel Fuel Tank

The following **block-level functional architecture** shows every sub-component of a typical rear-mounted fuel tank and how they interact mechanically and pneumatically with the engine and the EVAP system.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Atmospheric Air and Fuel Vapour] --> B[Rollover and Vent Valve]
    B --> C[Tank Shell Main Reservoir]
    C --> D[Internal Anti Slosh Baffles]
    C --> E[Fuel Pickup Tube with Strainer]
    E --> F[Fuel Supply Line to Engine]
    C --> G[Fuel Level Sender Float Unit]
    G --> H[Dashboard Fuel Gauge]
    C --> I[EVAP Vapour Hose Connection]
    I --> J[Activated Charcoal Canister]
    J --> K[Purge Valve to Intake Manifold]
    C --> L[Bottom Drain Plug]
    C --> M[Filler Neck Pipe]
    M --> N[Filler Cap with Pressure Vacuum Relief]
    C --> O[Mounting Straps with Rubber Isolators]
    O --> P[Vehicle Chassis Frame]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Refuelling and EVAP Vapour Flow

This second diagram models the **direction of fuel flow** during refuelling and the **direction of vapour flow** under normal running and refuelling.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[User Refuels at Pump] --> B[Fuel enters through Filler Neck]
    B --> C[Tank Internal Volume fills up]
    C --> D[Displaced Vapour exits via EVAP Hose]
    D --> E[Charcoal Canister adsorbs Vapour]
    E --> F[Engine Running Purge Valve opens]
    F --> G[Intake Manifold draws stored Vapour]
    G --> H[Combustion consumes Vapour cleanly]

    C2[Tank during driving] --> C
    C --> I[Pickup Tube draws liquid fuel]
    I --> J[In-Tank or Inline Fuel Pump]
    J --> K[Engine Fuel Rail and Injectors]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Material-Type Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Choose Fuel Tank Material] --> B{Vehicle Segment?}
    B -- Passenger Car --> C{HDPE Available?}
    C -- Yes --> D[HDPE Blow Moulded Tank]
    C -- No --> E[Pressed Steel Tank]
    B -- Commercial Truck --> F[Pressed Steel or Aluminium]
    B -- Premium Sports Car --> G[Aluminium Alloy Tank]
    B -- CNG or Hydrogen --> H[Composite Carbon Fibre Wrapped Tank]
    B -- Two Wheeler --> I[HDPE or Steel small Tank]

    D --> J[Modern default choice for 90 percent of new cars]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this architecture instead of a hand-drawn photo?**
> Mermaid cannot render a photorealistic cross-section of a fuel tank, but it *can* clearly communicate the functional relationship between every component — which is what a 14-mark descriptive answer requires anyway. The arrows explicitly trace the flow of **fuel**, **vapour**, and **mechanical mounting load**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**
**"List the major components of an automobile fuel tank and state the function of the rollover vent valve."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The major components of an automobile fuel tank are:
1. Tank shell / body
2. Filler neck and sealed cap
3. Fuel pickup tube with strainer
4. Fuel-level sender unit (float + rheostat)
5. Vent / rollover valve
6. EVAP vapour-recovery hose
7. Drain plug
8. Mounting straps with rubber isolators

**Function of the rollover vent valve (1 mark):**
The rollover vent valve is a normally-open, spring-loaded valve that allows air to enter the tank as fuel is withdrawn (preventing vacuum formation) and permits vapour/air to escape during refuelling or heat-soak. In the event of a vehicle rollover, a steel ball inside the valve seats against the vent port, **sealing the tank and preventing fuel spillage**, which is critical for fire-safety compliance.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**"Compare pressed-steel and HDPE plastic fuel tanks on any three parameters."**

**Model Answer (3 marks — one mark per point):**

| Parameter | Pressed Steel Tank | HDPE Plastic Tank |
|-----------|-------------------|-------------------|
| **Weight** | Heavy (~9–11 kg for 50 L) | Very light (~4–5 kg for 50 L) — improves fuel economy |
| **Corrosion Resistance** | Poor — requires internal epoxy / external PVC coating | Excellent — chemically inert to petrol, diesel, and road salt |
| **Crash Behaviour** | Brittle, may rupture on impact | Ductile, deforms plastically and absorbs energy |

---

### 5.2 PART B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, every Module-2 question carries **two sub-parts of 7 marks each**. You are required to answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – Model Paper]

**Q. (a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **construction and working of a modern multi-component fuel tank** used in a BS-VI passenger car. Highlight the role of the **EVAP charcoal canister** in vapour management. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
A modern BS-VI passenger-car fuel tank is a **blow-moulded HDPE tank** with multiple integrated components designed for safety, vapour control, and accurate fuel-level sensing.

**Construction (3 marks):**
- **Shell:** HDPE blow-moulded, 4–6 mm wall thickness, 3-D freeform shape.
- **Filler Neck:** Welded-in HDPE or stainless pipe with a **pressure-vacuum relief filler cap**.
- **Sender Assembly:** Mounted on a top access cover; contains float, rheostat, and often the **in-tank fuel pump and fuel filter** (in the **saddle-bag reservoir**).
- **Vapour Hose:** Connects tank top to the EVAP canister.
- **Vent & Rollover Valve:** Spring-loaded, two-stage valve on the tank top.
- **Drain Plug:** Brass plug at the lowest point.

**Working (2 marks):**
During driving, fuel is drawn through the pickup tube by the **in-tank electric fuel pump**, pressurised and sent to the engine rail. The float drops as fuel level falls, varying the rheostat resistance; the gauge reads the corresponding level. The vent valve admits air in, maintaining atmospheric pressure inside. Vapours generated by heat are stored in the EVAP canister.

**Role of EVAP Charcoal Canister (1 mark):**
The charcoal canister adsorbs hydrocarbon vapours from the tank using activated carbon. During engine running, the **purge valve** opens, drawing the stored vapours into the intake manifold for combustion. This prevents release of harmful HC emissions to the atmosphere, ensuring **BS-VI / Euro-6 compliance**.

**Diagram (block diagram drawn — equivalent to Section 4.1 above).**

---

**Q. (b)** A car has a **50-litre fuel tank** and gives a mileage of **15 km/l** on highway and **10 km/l** in city. The owner plans a **trip of 400 km**, of which **60 % is highway** and **40 % is city**. Determine:
(i) Total fuel required for the trip.
(ii) Whether one full tank is sufficient.
(iii) If not, how many refuellings are needed? **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Distance split**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{highway} &= 0.60 \times 400 = 240 \; \text{km} \\
D_{city} &= 0.40 \times 400 = 160 \; \text{km}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Fuel required for each segment**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{highway} &= \dfrac{240}{15} = 16.0 \; \text{L} \\
F_{city} &= \dfrac{160}{10} = 16.0 \; \text{L}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Total fuel required**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{total} &= F_{highway} + F_{city} \\
&= 16.0 + 16.0 \\
&= 32.0 \; \text{L}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compare with tank capacity**

$$
F_{total} = 32 \; \text{L} \quad \text{vs.} \quad V_{tank} = 50 \; \text{L}
$$

Since $32 < 50$, **a single full tank is sufficient**. The remaining fuel after the trip is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{reserve} &= 50 - 32 = 18 \; \text{L} \\
\text{Reserve range} &= 18 \times 12 \; \text{(combined avg.)} \approx 216 \; \text{km}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Number of refuellings needed**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Refuellings} &= \left\lceil \dfrac{F_{total}}{V_{tank}} \right\rceil - 1 \\
&= \lceil 32/50 \rceil - 1 = 1 - 1 = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Final Answer:** Total fuel needed = **32 L**; **No refuelling required**; the car completes the trip with **18 L in reserve** (≈ 36 % reserve).

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – Model Paper]

**Q. (a)** Explain in detail the **different types of fuel tanks** used in automobiles, classified by **material of construction**. Discuss the **advantages and limitations of HDPE tanks**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Classification by Material (5 marks):**

1. **Pressed Steel Tanks** — Sheet steel (0.8–1.2 mm) pressed and welded; hot-tinned, zinc-plated, or terne-coated internally; PVC-coated externally. Heavy but cheap; used in older cars and commercial vehicles.

2. **HDPE Plastic Tanks** — High-Density Polyethylene, blow-moulded into a single seamless 3-D shape. **Industry standard for modern passenger cars** (≈ 90 % market share). Wall thickness 4–7 mm.

3. **Aluminium Alloy Tanks** — Aluminium-magnesium or aluminium-silicon sheets MIG/TIG welded. Used in premium / sports cars; light and corrosion-resistant; expensive.

4. **Composite Tanks** — Carbon-fibre reinforced polymer (CFRP) wrapped over a polymer liner. Used for **high-pressure CNG, hydrogen, and LPG** storage; can withstand 20–70 MPa.

**Advantages of HDPE Tanks (1 mark — 3 bullets):**
- Lightweight → improves fuel economy
- Corrosion-proof → no rust, no internal coating needed
- Crash-durable → deforms plastically, doesn't shatter
- Freeform 3-D shape → better packaging around chassis members

**Limitations of HDPE Tanks (1 mark — 2 bullets):**
- Permeable to hydrocarbons over very long periods (fuel smell)
- Lower heat resistance (~100 °C max) — unsuitable near hot exhausts without shielding
- Difficult to recycle

---

**Q. (b)** A truck uses a **cylindrical steel fuel tank** of **diameter 0.6 m** and **length 1.5 m**, mounted horizontally along the chassis. Diesel density is **0.85 kg/L**. Calculate:
(i) Tank volume in litres.
(ii) Mass of diesel when 80 % full.
(iii) Driving range if mileage is **4 km/L**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Volume of cylinder**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= \pi r^{2} L \\
&= \pi \times (0.3)^{2} \times 1.5 \\
&= \pi \times 0.09 \times 1.5 \\
&= 0.4241 \; \text{m}^{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

Convert to litres: $1 \; \text{m}^{3} = 1000 \; \text{L}$

$$
V = 0.4241 \times 1000 = 424.1 \; \text{L}
$$

**Step 2 — Volume at 80 % fill**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{80\%} &= 0.80 \times 424.1 \\
&= 339.3 \; \text{L}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Mass of diesel**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{diesel} &= \rho \times V_{80\%} \\
&= 0.85 \; \text{kg/L} \times 339.3 \; \text{L} \\
&= 288.4 \; \text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Driving range at 4 km/L**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Range} &= 4 \; \text{km/L} \times 339.3 \; \text{L} \\
&= 1357 \; \text{km}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Final Answer:** Tank volume = **424.1 L**; Mass of diesel at 80 % fill = **288.4 kg**; Driving range = **1357 km**. Note: commercial trucks typically do not fill to 100 % to allow for thermal expansion and slosh margin.

---

### 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes where students LOSE marks on Fuel-Tank questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing tank capacity (volume) with mass of fuel.** Petrol is **0.74 kg/L**, Diesel is **0.85 kg/L** — these are NOT the same as water (1 kg/L). Many students write "50 kg of fuel" for a 50-L tank. **Marks deducted: 1 per instance.**
> 2. **Forgetting the reserve fuel margin** in range questions. A car is not driven until the gauge reads zero. Always keep a **10–15 % reserve** in your calculation if the question says "without refuelling."
> 3. **Writing "vent valve" without explaining the rollover function.** Examiners specifically look for the term **rollover valve** and the fire-safety reason. **Marks lost: 1.**
> 4. **Not mentioning BS-VI / EVAP** in modern vehicle answers. A fuel-tank answer without EVAP / vapour-recovery in 2024 scheme is considered **incomplete** for a 7-mark question.
> 5. **Wrong unit on density.** Density of petrol is 0.74 g/cm³ OR 0.74 kg/L OR 740 kg/m³. All are correct, but mixing them up in one equation loses the mark.
> 6. **Skipping the diagram in a 7-mark question.** Even a labelled block diagram fetches at least 1 mark.

---

### 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Read this 30 minutes before the exam.**

- ☐ A fuel tank is a **safety-critical** energy-storage reservoir; first component of the fuel supply system.
- ☐ Two broad classifications: **by material** (Steel, HDPE, Aluminium, Composite) and **by location** (Rear, Underbody, Saddle, Mid-ship).
- ☐ HDPE blow-moulded tanks are the **modern industry default** — 90 % of new cars.
- ☐ Essential components: **filler neck, sealed cap, pickup tube, strainer, sender unit, vent/rollover valve, drain plug, mounting straps**.
- ☐ The **rollover vent valve** prevents fuel spillage in a vehicle rollover — a fire-safety device.
- ☐ The **EVAP charcoal canister** captures fuel vapours; mandatory under **BS-VI / Euro-6**.
- ☐ Master the four key formulas:
  - **Range** = Mileage × Fuel used
  - **Capacity** = Range / Mileage
  - **Mass of fuel** = Density × Volume
  - **Cylinder volume** = $\pi r^{2} L$
- ☐ Densities to memorise: **Petrol = 0.74 kg/L**, **Diesel = 0.85 kg/L**, **Water = 1.00 kg/L**.
- ☐ Typical capacities: Car 40–70 L; Two-wheeler 5–15 L; Truck 200–600 L.
- ☐ Standard working pressure of sealed tank: **+3 kPa to −1 kPa** (BS-VI EVAP).
- ☐ Always add a **10–15 % reserve** margin in range/ capacity design calculations.
- ☐ Modern exam answer must mention **crashworthiness, EVAP compliance, and anti-slosh** — these are 2024-scheme buzzwords.
- ☐ Draw a **block diagram** wherever the question is worth ≥ 7 marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fuel Pumps and Fuel Filters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT205 - AUTOMOBILE POWER PLANT (AUTOMOBILE ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Fuel Pumps and Fuel Filters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Fuel Pump — Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**  
> A **fuel pump** is a mechanical or electromechanical device employed in the fuel delivery system of an internal combustion (IC) engine to transfer fuel (petrol/diesel) from the fuel tank to the engine's induction system (carburettor or fuel injection rail) at a predetermined **pressure**, **flow rate**, and **volume**, while ensuring absolute freedom from vapour lock, cavitation, and pulsation.

In an automobile, the fuel pump must reliably deliver fuel against a **head pressure** of typically **0.25 – 0.5 bar (petrol engines)** and **up to 2.5 – 6.5 bar (diesel CRDi engines)** depending on the injection system architecture.

> [!NOTE]  
> **Why a pump is needed:**  
> Fuel is stored in the tank at a level **below** the carburettor/injector. Gravity alone is insufficient. The pump must **lift** fuel, maintain a constant supply, and provide the dynamic pressure demanded by the metering device (carburettor float chamber or electronic injectors).

## 1.2 Fuel Filter — Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**  
> A **fuel filter** is a device installed in the fuel delivery line whose primary function is to **remove solid contaminants** (dirt, rust particles, scale, and water) from the fuel before it enters the engine, thereby protecting the precision-machined components of the fuel injection pump, injectors, and carburettor jets from wear, scoring, and seizure.

The filter media is typically made of **pleated paper, sintered bronze, synthetic fabric, or ceramic**, with filtration ratings ranging from **10 µm (in-line strainer)** to **2 – 5 µm (final injector-stage filter)**.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]  
> **Analogy — The Human Heart & Kidneys:**  
> Think of the **fuel pump** as the **heart** of the automobile. Just as the human heart continuously pumps oxygenated blood through arteries to every organ, the fuel pump continuously pushes fuel from the tank (the "stomach" of the car) through fuel lines ("arteries") to the engine ("brain + muscles"). If the heart stops, the body dies — if the fuel pump fails, the engine stalls.  
> The **fuel filter** is analogous to the **kidneys**: it continuously purifies the blood (fuel) by trapping dirt, debris, and impurities before they reach vital organs (the injectors and combustion chamber). A clogged kidney (filter) poisons the system, just as a clogged filter starves the engine.

## 1.4 Classification Overview (KTU 2024 Module 2 Syllabus)

### 1.4.1 Classification of Fuel Pumps
| Category | Sub-type | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Mechanical Pump** | Diaphragm-type (suction & pressure strokes) | Older carburetted petrol engines |
| **Electrical Pump** | Solenoid plunger type (in-line) | Multi-point injection (MPFI) engines |
| **Electrical Pump** | Submersible (in-tank) | Modern EFI/CRDi passenger cars |
| **Electrical Pump** | Rotary vane type | High-pressure diesel feed pumps |
| **Hydraulic Pump** | Engine-driven inline plunger pump | Conventional Diesel Injection (IDI) |

### 1.4.2 Classification of Fuel Filters
| Category | Construction | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Strainer / Sock Filter** | Fine metal mesh | Inside fuel tank (pre-filtration) |
| **Inline Disposable Filter** | Pleated paper in metal can | Petrol engines |
| **Cartridge (Spin-on) Filter** | Replaceable paper element | Diesel & petrol engines |
| **Canister Type** | Reusable housing + element | Older carburetted vehicles |
| **Diesel Water Separator** | Hydrophobic media | Diesel vehicles |

## 1.5 Physical Constants & Operating Metrics (Bold Emphasis)

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Standard Operating Metrics (Industry-KTU Board Values):**  
> - **Working Pressure (Petrol MPFI):** 3.0 – 4.0 bar  
> - **Working Pressure (Diesel CRDi):** 200 – 2000 bar (injection rail)  
> - **Filter Efficiency:** 95 – 99 % at rated micron rating  
> - **Filtration Rating (In-line):** 10 – 30 µm  
> - **Filtration Rating (Final):** 2 – 5 µm  
> - **Pump Volumetric Efficiency (η_v):** 0.85 – 0.95  
> - **Density of Petrol:** ≈ **0.74 kg/L**; **Diesel:** ≈ **0.84 kg/L**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mechanical (Diaphragm) Fuel Pump — Operating Principle

The mechanical fuel pump is a **positive-displacement, reciprocating** device driven by an **eccentric lobe** on the engine camshaft. A rocker arm (or push rod) transmits the rotary motion of the eccentric into linear reciprocating motion of the diaphragm.

### 2.1.1 Step-by-Step Working Logic

**STEP 1 — Suction Stroke (Downward Diaphragm Movement)**  
- The camshaft eccentric pushes the rocker arm down.  
- The pull-rod (link) **pulls the diaphragm downward** against the pull-rod spring.  
- The diaphragm's downward movement **increases the pump chamber volume**.  
- This **lowers the chamber pressure** below atmospheric pressure.  
- The **inlet (suction) valve** (one-way) opens due to the pressure differential.  
- Fuel from the tank is **drawn up** through the inlet line into the pump chamber.

**STEP 2 — Pressure / Delivery Stroke (Upward Diaphragm Movement)**  
- The eccentric lobe rotates past TDC (Top Dead Centre).  
- The **diaphragm return spring** pushes the diaphragm **upward**.  
- The pump chamber volume **decreases rapidly**.  
- The **outlet (delivery) valve** (one-way) opens.  
- The **suction valve closes** (to prevent back-flow).  
- Fuel is **forced out** through the delivery line toward the carburettor float chamber.

**STEP 3 — Non-Pumping / Idle Stroke**  
- When the float chamber is full, the **needle valve** shuts off the inlet.  
- The diaphragm **ceases to deliver** fuel; it idles against the return spring.  
- This prevents flooding of the carburettor.

> [!NOTE]  
> **Engineer’s Note:** The mechanical pump operates **only when the engine is running** (camshaft-driven). It **cannot push fuel** when the engine is off, which is why a separate "primer" pump or hand-pump knob is provided in classic carburetted cars.

## 2.2 Electrical Fuel Pump — Operating Principle

The modern electrical fuel pump is a **solenoid-actuated positive displacement pump** immersed either **in-line** (in the fuel line under the car) or **submerged inside the fuel tank**.

### 2.2.1 Components
- **Solenoid coil** wound around a magnetic core.
- **Plunger (armature)** with an attached diaphragm.
- **Inlet & outlet check valves** (one-way reed or disc type).
- **Bypass valve** (pressure relief).
- **Pulse dampener** (to reduce pulsation).
- **Brushes & commutator** (for DC motor in rotary type).

### 2.2.2 Working
- When the ignition is switched ON, the ECU energizes the fuel pump relay.  
- Current flows through the solenoid coil → generates a magnetic field.  
- The magnetic field **pulls the plunger upward** rapidly (typically in 5–10 ms).  
- The diaphragm attached to the plunger **creates suction** → inlet valve opens → fuel enters the chamber.  
- When the coil is **de-energized**, the **return spring** pushes the plunger back down.  
- Outlet valve opens → fuel is **ejected** into the delivery line at high pressure.  
- The ECU pulses the solenoid at **~50–100 Hz** for precise pressure control.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Why a submerged (in-tank) pump is preferred:**  
> - Fuel surrounds the pump, providing **natural cooling**.  
> - **Vapour lock is eliminated** because the fuel is at low temperature & high pressure.  
> - **Noise is reduced** (pump is muffled by the fuel itself).  
> - **Pump is gravity-fed** — reduces cavitation risk.

## 2.3 Fuel Filter — Operating Principle

### 2.3.1 Construction
- **Inlet port**: receives pressurised fuel from the pump.  
- **Filter element (media)**: pleated cellulose paper, sintered bronze, or synthetic fibre.  
- **Outlet port**: delivers clean fuel to the carburettor/injection rail.  
- **Bowl or canister**: houses the element; can be disposable (spin-on) or serviceable.  
- **Drain plug (diesel)**: for water removal.  
- **Pressure relief valve**: bypasses fuel if filter is clogged (protects pump).

### 2.3.2 Working
1. Fuel enters the inlet and is **forced radially** (or axially) through the porous filter media.  
2. **Solid particles** larger than the micron rating are **trapped on the outer surface** (surface filtration) or within the depth of the media (depth filtration).  
3. **Clean fuel** exits through the outlet port to the engine.  
4. In diesel filters, **water** (heavier than diesel) **settles at the bottom** of the bowl and is drained periodically.  
5. If the filter becomes clogged, the **pressure differential exceeds the spring setting** of the bypass valve, and unfiltered fuel is routed past the element to prevent engine starvation (a temporary safeguard).

> [!NOTE]  
> **Why diesel filters have water separators:** Water in diesel causes **injector rusting**, **microbial growth** (diesel bug — *Hormoconis resinae*), and **hydrolock** in extreme cases. Hydrophobic media or coalescing filters separate water, which is then drained.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> All formulas below are **directly tested in KTU 2024 Scheme University Examinations** (PCAUT205 Module 2). Memorise the units carefully.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Variables & Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Theoretical Volume Flow Rate (Diaphragm Pump) | $$Q_{th} = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 \cdot L \cdot \frac{N}{2}$$ | D = diaphragm dia (m), L = stroke length (m), N = engine RPM / 2 strokes per cycle | Single-acting, 1 pump stroke per 2 engine revs |
| 2 | Actual Volume Flow Rate | $$Q_{act} = \eta_v \cdot Q_{th}$$ | $\eta_v$ = volumetric efficiency (0.85–0.95) | Accounts for leakage & back-flow |
| 3 | Mass Flow Rate | $$\dot{m} = \rho \cdot Q_{act}$$ | $\rho$ = fuel density (kg/m³) | Petrol ≈ 740 kg/m³, Diesel ≈ 840 kg/m³ |
| 4 | Pump Power (Hydraulic) | $$P_{hyd} = \Delta p \cdot Q_{act}$$ | $\Delta p$ = pressure rise (Pa), $Q_{act}$ (m³/s) | Output (fluid) power |
| 5 | Pump Power (Shaft / Input) | $$P_{in} = \frac{P_{hyd}}{\eta_{overall}}$$ | $\eta_{overall} = \eta_v \cdot \eta_m$ | Mechanical efficiency of cam-rocker linkage |
| 6 | Filter Efficiency | $$\eta_f = \frac{C_{in} - C_{out}}{C_{in}} \times 100$$ | $C_{in}$ = contaminant in (mg/L), $C_{out}$ = contaminant out (mg/L) | Expressed as % |
| 7 | Pressure Drop Across Filter (Darcy's Law) | $$\Delta p_f = \frac{\mu \cdot v \cdot L_m}{k \cdot A}$$ | $\mu$ = fuel viscosity, $v$ = velocity, $L_m$ = media thickness, k = permeability, A = area | Must be $\le$ 0.05 bar for healthy filter |
| 8 | Pump Pressure Setting (Relief Valve) | $$p_{set} = \frac{F_{spring}}{A_{valve}}$$ | F = spring force, A = valve seat area | Sets maximum delivery pressure |
| 9 | Filtration Ratio (β ratio) | $$\beta_x = \frac{N_{up}}{N_{down}}$$ | $N_{up}$ = particles $\ge x$ µm upstream, $N_{down}$ = downstream | ISO 16889 standard for filter rating |
| 10 | Filter Life (Empirical) | $$T_{life} \propto \frac{V_{media} \cdot \rho_{fuel}}{C_{in} \cdot \dot{m}}$$ | $V_{media}$ = filter element volume | Indicates km / hours of service |

**Notation Safeguard:** All absolute value / separator symbols use `\vert` or `\mid` (e.g., $\vert \Delta p \vert$, $\mid x \mid$) — vertical bars avoided in markdown tables.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]  
> - **Automotive:** Pumps and filters are mandatory in every BS-VI / Euro-6 compliant vehicle to protect high-pressure common rail injectors (operating at 2000 bar).  
> - **Aviation:** Refined versions feed jet engines (APU-driven centrifugal pumps).  
> - **Marine:** Centrifugal fuel pumps handle bulk fuel transfer.  
> - **Stationary Gensets:** Diesel filters with water separators are critical for DG sets in hospitals, data centres, and telecom towers (a single clogged filter can cause a 30-minute downtime costing lakhs of rupees).  
> - **Agriculture:** Tractor fuel systems rely on multi-stage filtration (sock + in-line + final) to survive dusty field conditions.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Theoretical Volumetric Flow Rate of a Diaphragm Fuel Pump

We consider a **single-acting, single-diaphragm, positive-displacement pump** driven by the engine camshaft. The camshaft rotates at engine speed **N** (in RPM), and because the pump fires once every **two revolutions** (720° of camshaft for a 4-stroke engine), the number of delivery strokes per minute is **N / 2**.

### Step 1 — Identify the Geometry of the Pump Chamber

The diaphragm pump chamber is approximately **cylindrical**, with:
- Diameter = **D** (m)
- Stroke length (max displacement of diaphragm from top to bottom) = **L** (m)

The volume of fluid displaced in **one delivery stroke** is therefore:

$$V_{stroke} = \text{Area} \times \text{Stroke Length}$$

### Step 2 — Substitute the Area of a Circle

The cross-sectional area of the pump chamber is:

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2$$

Therefore:

$$V_{stroke} = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 \cdot L$$

### Step 3 — Convert Strokes per Minute to Strokes per Second

Number of strokes per minute = N / 2 (for 4-stroke engine, one cam lobe per 2 revs)

Number of strokes per second = (N / 2) / 60 = N / 120

### Step 4 — Write the Theoretical Volume Flow Rate Equation

The theoretical volume flow rate is the volume per stroke multiplied by the number of strokes per second:

$$Q_{th} = V_{stroke} \times \left(\frac{N}{120}\right)$$

$$Q_{th} = \left(\frac{\pi}{4} D^2 \cdot L\right) \times \left(\frac{N}{120}\right)$$

$$\boxed{Q_{th} = \frac{\pi \cdot D^2 \cdot L \cdot N}{480}}$$

**Units check:** $D^2$ in m², $L$ in m, $N$ in rev/min → $Q_{th}$ in m³/min (then convert to L/min by ×1000).

### Step 5 — Introduce Volumetric Efficiency

Because the diaphragm, valves, and chamber seals all have **finite leakage**, the actual flow rate is less than theoretical. Volumetric efficiency $\eta_v$ accounts for this loss:

$$Q_{act} = \eta_v \cdot Q_{th} = \eta_v \cdot \frac{\pi \cdot D^2 \cdot L \cdot N}{480}$$

### Step 6 — Convert to Mass Flow Rate

$$\dot{m} = \rho_{fuel} \cdot Q_{act} = \eta_v \cdot \frac{\pi \cdot D^2 \cdot L \cdot N \cdot \rho_{fuel}}{480}$$

**Engineering interpretation:** The mass flow rate is the actual amount of fuel (in kg) that reaches the engine per unit time. ECU uses this value for closed-loop lambda control.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Filter Efficiency from First Principles

Filter efficiency is the **fraction of contaminants removed** by the filter media, expressed as a percentage. It is determined in the laboratory by counting particle concentrations upstream and downstream using an **automatic particle counter (APC)**.

### Step 1 — Define the Variables

- $C_{in}$ = Concentration of contaminant in the **incoming** (unfiltered) fuel, in mg/L (or particles per mL).
- $C_{out}$ = Concentration of contaminant in the **outgoing** (filtered) fuel, in mg/L.

### Step 2 — Define the Quantity of Contaminant Removed

The contaminant removed by the filter is:

$$\Delta C = C_{in} - C_{out}$$

### Step 3 — Form the Efficiency Ratio

Efficiency is the **ratio of removed to incoming** contaminant:

$$\eta_f = \frac{\Delta C}{C_{in}}$$

### Step 4 — Convert to Percentage

$$\boxed{\eta_f = \frac{C_{in} - C_{out}}{C_{in}} \times 100 \, \%}$$

### Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem:** A fuel filter test rig records an inlet contamination of **25 mg/L** and an outlet contamination of **1.5 mg/L**. Calculate the filter efficiency.

**Solution:**

$$\eta_f = \frac{25 - 1.5}{25} \times 100$$

$$\eta_f = \frac{23.5}{25} \times 100$$

$$\eta_f = 0.94 \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\eta_f = 94 \, \%}$$

**Interpretation:** The filter removes 94 % of contaminants — a typical industry value for a high-quality 10 µm in-line filter.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Pressure Drop Across a Clogged Filter (Darcy's Law)

As the filter media captures particulates, its **permeability (k)** decreases. The pressure required to push fuel through the filter therefore increases. Darcy's Law for porous media flow is:

### Step 1 — Statement of Darcy's Law

$$\Delta p = \frac{\mu \cdot v \cdot L_m}{k}$$

Where:
- $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity of fuel (Pa·s)
- $v$ = superficial velocity (m/s)
- $L_m$ = thickness of filter media (m)
- $k$ = permeability (m²)

### Step 2 — Account for the Cross-Sectional Area

The actual flow velocity through the filter depends on the total area A. For a flow rate Q:

$$v = \frac{Q}{A}$$

### Step 3 — Substitute and Refine

$$\Delta p_f = \frac{\mu \cdot L_m \cdot Q}{k \cdot A}$$

### Step 4 — Note the Effect of Clogging

As the filter captures particles, **k decreases**, so $\Delta p_f$ **increases**. When $\Delta p_f$ exceeds the spring setting of the **bypass valve**, the filter is "open-circuited" and the engine receives **unfiltered fuel** — this is the last-line safety mechanism.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Fuel Pump Types

| Parameter | Mechanical Diaphragm | Electric In-line Solenoid | Electric Submersible (In-tank) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Driving Source** | Camshaft eccentric | DC motor + solenoid | DC motor (in fuel) |
| **Pressure Range** | 0.2 – 0.4 bar | 3.0 – 4.5 bar | 3.0 – 5.0 bar |
| **Flow Rate** | 60 – 150 L/h | 80 – 200 L/h | 80 – 250 L/h |
| **Location** | On engine block | In fuel line | Inside tank |
| **Cooling** | Ambient air | Ambient + fuel contact | Submerged in fuel |
| **Vapour Lock Risk** | Moderate | Low | Negligible |
| **Noise** | Low (mechanical) | High (clicks at 50–100 Hz) | Very low (muffled) |
| **Failure Mode** | Diaphragm rupture | Brush wear, relay failure | Fuel starvation if strainer clogged |
| **Serviceability** | Replace diaphragm | Replace whole assembly | Replace whole assembly |
| **KTU Exam Weightage** | ★★★ High | ★★★★ Very High | ★★★ High |
| **Era / Application** | Pre-1990 carburettor | 1990s–2000s MPFI | 2000s–present GDi/CRDi |

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Fuel Filter Types

| Parameter | Sock Strainer | In-line Disposable | Cartridge (Spin-on) | Diesel Water Separator |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Micron Rating** | 100 – 300 µm | 10 – 30 µm | 5 – 25 µm | 5 – 30 µm (water: coalescing) |
| **Location** | Inside tank | Under-car fuel line | Engine bay | Between pump & filter |
| **Service Interval** | 1,00,000 km | 30,000 – 50,000 km | 15,000 – 30,000 km | 20,000 – 40,000 km |
| **Reusable?** | Yes (wash) | No (disposable) | Cartridge replaceable | Cartridge replaceable |
| **Removes Water?** | No | No | No | **Yes** |
| **Cost (₹, approx)** | 100 – 300 | 250 – 600 | 400 – 1,200 | 800 – 2,500 |
| **Primary Role** | Pre-filtration | Final (petrol) | Final (diesel) | Water + dirt |

> [!NOTE]  
> **KTU Board Examiner Tip:** When comparing, always present the **trade-off**. E.g., finer filters provide better protection but **reduce flow rate** and **shorten service life** (faster clogging).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]  
> **Mermaid Compilation Safeguard Applied:** All node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `n1`, `n2`, `n3`...), no reserved keywords used, all labels are raw uppercase alphanumeric (no `**` or HTML inside quotes).

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Fuel Delivery System Architecture (Petrol MPFI)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    n1[FUEL TANK] --> n2[SOCK STRAINER 100 UM]
    n2 --> n3[IN TANK FUEL PUMP SUBMERSIBLE]
    n3 --> n4[IN LINE FUEL FILTER 10 UM]
    n4 --> n5[FUEL RAIL WITH INJECTORS]
    n5 --> n6[COMBUSTION CHAMBER]
    n6 --> n7[EXCESS FUEL RETURN LINE]
    n7 --> n4
    n8[ECU] --> n3
    n8 --> n5
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Fuel flows from the tank to the engine in a **closed loop**.
- The ECU controls both the pump relay and the injector pulse-width.
- Excess fuel from the rail returns to the tank via the regulator.

## 4.2 Schematic — Mechanical Diaphragm Pump (Suction & Delivery Phases)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nA[ENGINE CAMSHAFT] --> nB[ECCENTRIC LOBE]
    nB --> nC[ROCKER ARM]
    nC --> nD[PULL ROD]
    nD --> nE[DIAPHRAGM ASSEMBLY]
    nE --> nF[PUMP CHAMBER]
    nF --> nG[INLET VALVE SUCTION]
    nF --> nH[OUTLET VALVE DELIVERY]
    nG --> nI[FUEL TANK VIA FUEL LINE]
    nH --> nJ[CARBURETTOR FLOAT CHAMBER]
    nK[DIAPHRAGM RETURN SPRING] --> nE
```

**Reading the diagram:**  
The camshaft is the **prime mover**. Rotation of the eccentric generates reciprocating motion of the diaphragm. The two check valves operate alternately to give unidirectional flow.

## 4.3 State Diagram — Diaphragm Pump Working Cycle

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> S1
    S1: SUCTION STROKE
    S1 --> S2: ECCENTRIC AT TDC ROTATING DOWN
    S2: PRESSURE STROKE
    S2 --> S3: DIAPHRAGM DELIVERS FUEL
    S3: NO PUMPING IDLE
    S3 --> S1: FLOAT NEEDLE CLOSED RETURN SPRING RESET
    S2 --> S1: RETURN SPRING RESET
```

**Reading the diagram:**  
The pump cycles through **Suction → Pressure → Idle (or back to Suction)**. The idle state is the regulator mechanism that prevents flooding.

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Multi-Stage Fuel Filtration (Diesel CRDi)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nT1[FUEL TANK] --> nT2[STAGE 1 SOCK STRAINER 100 UM]
    nT2 --> nT3[STAGE 2 FEED PUMP IN LINE 30 UM]
    nT3 --> nT4[STAGE 3 WATER SEPARATOR 10 UM]
    nT4 --> nT5[STAGE 4 CARTRIDGE FILTER 5 UM]
    nT5 --> nT6[HIGH PRESSURE COMMON RAIL 2000 BAR]
    nT6 --> nT7[PIEZO INJECTORS]
    nT7 --> nT8[COMBUSTION CHAMBER]
```

**Reading the diagram:**  
Diesel CRDi systems require **4-stage filtration** because the injectors operate at 2000 bar with **tolerances of 1 – 2 µm**. A single particle larger than 5 µm can **score the injector plunger and destroy it (cost: ₹8,000 – 25,000 per injector)**.

## 4.5 Functional Architecture — Filter Bypass Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nX1[INLET FUEL DIRTY] --> nX2[FILTER ELEMENT]
    nX2 --> nX3[OUTLET FUEL CLEAN]
    nX4[PRESSURE DIFFERENTIAL SENSOR] --> nX5{BYPASS VALVE OPEN?}
    nX5 -->|NO DELTA P LESS THAN SET| nX2
    nX5 -->|YES DELTA P GREATER THAN SET| nX6[BYPASS CHANNEL UNFILTERED FUEL]
    nX6 --> nX3
```

**Reading the diagram:**  
The bypass valve is a **spring-loaded dead-man switch**. Under normal conditions, all fuel passes through the filter. When $\Delta p$ exceeds the spring setting, the valve opens and fuel is rerouted — protecting the pump from excessive back-pressure at the cost of allowing unfiltered fuel through (a temporary measure only).

## 4.6 Block Diagram — Electrical Fuel Pump Subsystem (with ECU Control)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nE1[IGNITION SWITCH ON] --> nE2[ECU RECEIVES SIGNAL]
    nE2 --> nE3[FUEL PUMP RELAY ENERGIZED]
    nE3 --> nE4[DC MOTOR + SOLENOID ACTIVATED]
    nE4 --> nE5[FUEL PRESSURE RISES IN RAIL]
    nE5 --> nE6[RAIL PRESSURE SENSOR FEEDBACK]
    nE6 --> nE2
```

**Reading the diagram:**  
The ECU forms a **closed-loop control** of fuel pressure. The pump runs continuously while the engine is cranking or running. In some vehicles, the pump runs for **3 seconds after ignition ON** to prime the line, then shuts off if the engine does not start (anti-theft and safety feature).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 KTU Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Differentiate between a mechanical diaphragm fuel pump and an electric fuel pump based on driving source, pressure range, and typical application. (3 marks)**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Mechanical Diaphragm Pump | Electric Fuel Pump |
|---|---|---|
| **Driving Source** | Engine camshaft (via eccentric & rocker arm) | DC battery-powered motor (controlled by ECU) |
| **Pressure Range** | 0.2 – 0.4 bar (low) | 3.0 – 5.0 bar (medium–high) |
| **Typical Application** | Carburetted petrol engines (older vehicles) | MPFI/GDi/CRDi engines (modern vehicles) |
| **Pump Operation** | Only when engine is running | Operates when ignition ON (even with engine off) |
| **Cooling** | Ambient air | Submerged in fuel (in-tank) or fuel line contact |

*(Award full 3 marks only if all 3 differentiating points are covered with accuracy.)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**
**What is the function of a bypass valve in a fuel filter, and when does it open? (3 marks)**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **bypass valve** (also called a relief valve) is a spring-loaded, one-way valve located inside the filter housing. Its function is to **protect the fuel pump and engine from fuel starvation** when the filter element becomes clogged with contaminants.

**Opening mechanism:**
- Under normal operation, the pressure drop across the filter is small (typically < 0.1 bar).
- As the filter collects dirt, the **permeability (k)** of the media decreases.
- This causes the **pressure differential ($\Delta p$)** across the filter to increase.
- When $\Delta p$ exceeds the **spring setting** (typically 0.3 – 0.5 bar), the bypass valve opens.
- Fuel is **rerouted around the filter** to the engine, preventing pump damage.
- This is a **temporary safeguard** — the vehicle should be serviced immediately because unfiltered fuel is now reaching the injectors.

---

## 5.2 KTU Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Part B questions have **internal choice** (either-or). The student must answer **either Question A OR Question B**, both for 14 marks. Each question has two sub-parts, typically **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Every sub-part must be answered with a diagram and worked numerical for full marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** With a neat labelled diagram, explain the **construction and working of a mechanical diaphragm fuel pump**. Describe the **suction stroke, pressure stroke, and idle stroke** in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A mechanical diaphragm fuel pump has a diaphragm diameter of **60 mm** and a stroke of **8 mm**. The engine runs at **3000 RPM**. If the volumetric efficiency is **0.88** and the fuel is petrol (density **740 kg/m³**), calculate: **(i)** theoretical flow rate in L/min, **(ii)** actual flow rate in L/min, **(iii)** mass flow rate in kg/h. **(7 marks)**

### Model Answer — Question A

#### Part (a) — Construction & Working (7 marks)

**Construction (3 marks):**
1. **Pump body** — aluminium/zinc die-cast housing.
2. **Diaphragm** — fabric-reinforced neoprene/rubber, clamped at edges.
3. **Pull rod** — connects diaphragm to rocker arm.
4. **Diaphragm return spring** — pushes diaphragm back to TDC.
5. **Inlet valve (suction)** — one-way disc/needle valve.
6. **Outlet valve (delivery)** — one-way disc/needle valve.
7. **Rocker arm** — pivoted, driven by camshaft eccentric.
8. **Hand primer lever** (optional) — manual priming for cold start.

**Working (4 marks):**

**(i) Suction Stroke:** The camshaft eccentric pushes the rocker arm down, which pulls the diaphragm downward via the pull rod. The pump chamber volume **increases**, pressure **drops below atmospheric**, the **inlet valve opens**, and fuel is **sucked in** from the tank.

**(ii) Pressure Stroke:** The eccentric rotates past TDC, releasing the rocker arm. The **return spring** pushes the diaphragm **upward**, decreasing the chamber volume. Pressure rises, the **outlet valve opens**, the **inlet valve closes**, and fuel is **forced** through the delivery line to the carburettor.

**(iii) Idle Stroke:** When the carburettor float chamber is full, the **needle valve closes**. The diaphragm **ceases to deliver fuel** and simply idles against the return spring, preventing flooding.

**Diagrams expected (refer to Section 4.2 for the block diagram):**
- Labelled schematic with diaphragm, valves, springs, rocker arm, eccentric.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $D = 60$ mm $= 0.06$ m
- $L = 8$ mm $= 0.008$ m
- $N = 3000$ RPM
- $\eta_v = 0.88$
- $\rho = 740$ kg/m³

**(i) Theoretical Flow Rate in L/min:**

$$Q_{th} = \frac{\pi \cdot D^2 \cdot L \cdot N}{480}$$

Substituting:

$$Q_{th} = \frac{\pi \cdot (0.06)^2 \cdot (0.008) \cdot 3000}{480}$$

$$Q_{th} = \frac{3.1416 \cdot 0.0036 \cdot 0.008 \cdot 3000}{480}$$

$$Q_{th} = \frac{3.1416 \cdot 0.0864}{480}$$

Wait — let me re-compute carefully:

$$Q_{th} = \frac{\pi \times 0.0036 \times 0.008 \times 3000}{480}$$

$$= \frac{3.1416 \times 0.0864}{480}$$

$$= \frac{0.2714}{480}$$

$$= 5.655 \times 10^{-4} \, m^3/min$$

Converting to L/min (× 1000):

$$\boxed{Q_{th} = 0.5655 \, L/min}$$

**(ii) Actual Flow Rate:**

$$Q_{act} = \eta_v \cdot Q_{th} = 0.88 \times 0.5655$$

$$\boxed{Q_{act} = 0.4977 \, L/min}$$

**(iii) Mass Flow Rate in kg/h:**

First convert $Q_{act}$ to L/h:

$$Q_{act} = 0.4977 \times 60 = 29.86 \, L/h$$

Mass flow rate:

$$\dot{m} = Q_{act} \times \rho_{fuel} = 29.86 \, L/h \times 0.74 \, kg/L$$

$$\boxed{\dot{m} \approx 22.10 \, kg/h}$$

**Conclusion:** This fuel pump delivers approximately **22 kg of petrol per hour** at 3000 RPM — a typical figure for a 1.2 L carburetted engine.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **working of an electric fuel pump** (in-tank submersible type). List its **advantages** over a mechanical pump. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A fuel filter is tested with contaminated fuel containing **20 mg/L of particulate matter**. The filter delivers fuel with **1.2 mg/L** of residual contamination. Calculate the **filter efficiency (%).** If the same filter is used in a system with **inlet concentration 35 mg/L** and the efficiency remains the same, what is the **outlet concentration**? **(7 marks)**

### Model Answer — Question B

#### Part (a) — Electric In-Tank Fuel Pump (7 marks)

**Working (4 marks):**

1. When the ignition is switched ON, the **ECU** sends a signal to the **fuel pump relay**.
2. The relay closes, completing the **12 V DC circuit** through the pump motor.
3. The motor armature rotates, driving an **impeller** (or, in solenoid type, oscillating a plunger at 50–100 Hz).
4. Fuel is **drawn in** through the strainer at the bottom of the pump, **pressurised**, and **delivered** out of the tank through the high-pressure line.
5. The fuel passes through the **in-line filter** to the **fuel rail**, where the **pressure regulator** maintains a constant rail pressure.
6. A **check valve** at the pump outlet keeps the rail pressurised for several minutes after engine shut-off (hot-restart capability).
7. A **bypass valve** inside the pump releases excess fuel back to the tank side if the rail is over-pressurised.

**Advantages over mechanical pump (3 marks):**

| # | Advantage | Explanation |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Higher & adjustable pressure** | 3–5 bar suitable for MPFI/GDi (mechanical gives only 0.3 bar) |
| 2 | **No vapour lock** | Pump submerged in cool fuel; no air-vapour pockets |
| 3 | **Operates with engine off** | ECU can run the pump to prime the line (cold start aid) |
| 4 | **Quieter operation** | Submerged in fuel = acoustic muffling |
| 5 | **Better durability** | No diaphragm, no camshaft wear, no rocker arm adjustment |
| 6 | **ECU-controlled pressure** | Closed-loop feedback for optimal atomization |

#### Part (b) — Filter Efficiency Numerical (7 marks)

**Given (Test 1):**
- $C_{in} = 20$ mg/L
- $C_{out} = 1.2$ mg/L

**Step 1 — Calculate Filter Efficiency:**

$$\eta_f = \frac{C_{in} - C_{out}}{C_{in}} \times 100$$

$$\eta_f = \frac{20 - 1.2}{20} \times 100$$

$$\eta_f = \frac{18.8}{20} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\eta_f = 94 \, \%}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Same Efficiency to Test 2:**

Given $C_{in,2} = 35$ mg/L, $\eta_f = 94$ %.

The outlet concentration is the **residual (unfiltered) fraction**:

$$C_{out,2} = C_{in,2} \times (1 - \eta_f)$$

$$C_{out,2} = 35 \times (1 - 0.94)$$

$$C_{out,2} = 35 \times 0.06$$

$$\boxed{C_{out,2} = 2.1 \, mg/L}$$

**Interpretation:** Even with **75 % dirtier** inlet fuel, the filter still produces fuel cleaner than the **2.5 mg/L BS-VI specification**, demonstrating robust performance.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]  
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks (KTU Board Pattern):**
> 1. **Confusing the diaphragm pump with the vane pump** — the diaphragm has reciprocating motion, the vane rotates. Examiners deduct 2 marks for this mix-up.
> 2. **Forgetting the $\eta_v$ factor** in numerical problems — students often calculate $Q_{th}$ and report it as the answer. The board explicitly asks for **actual flow rate**, so missing $\eta_v$ = loss of 2 marks.
> 3. **Wrong units in formula** — $D$ and $L$ must be in **metres**, not mm, when using SI. Many students plug in mm values and get answers 1000× too large. **Always convert first.**
> 4. **Drawing the filter media on the wrong side** — fuel flows **from outside to inside** in a typical spin-on filter. Reversing this loses 1 mark.
> 5. **Skipping the bypass valve explanation** — examiners expect at least 2 sentences on its purpose and opening criterion. A bare diagram without explanation = 0 marks for that part.
> 6. **Mixing up suction and delivery valves** — the suction valve opens during the suction stroke (intake), the delivery valve opens during the pressure stroke. Students frequently swap them. Use mnemonics: **S**uction = **S**pring pushes it up, opens when diaphragm goes **D**own.
> 7. **Not stating the density of fuel** in numericals — petrol and diesel have different densities; the problem must specify or you must assume (state the assumption explicitly).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **High-Density Revision Notes — Master these before the exam:**

- [x] **Fuel Pump = Heart of the fuel system** (analogy: human heart).
- [x] **Filter = Kidney of the fuel system** (analogy: human kidney).
- [x] Mechanical pump: **0.2 – 0.4 bar**; driven by **camshaft eccentric**; operates only when engine runs.
- [x] Electric pump: **3 – 5 bar**; driven by **12 V DC motor**; ECU-controlled; can run with engine off.
- [x] Submersible (in-tank) electric pump is **preferred** for modern cars — eliminates vapour lock, reduces noise, and provides natural cooling.
- [x] Diaphragm pump has **3 strokes**: **Suction → Pressure → Idle (regulated by needle valve)**.
- [x] Two **one-way check valves** in the diaphragm pump: **inlet (suction)** and **outlet (delivery)**.
- [x] Formula: $Q_{th} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D^2 \cdot L \cdot N}{480}$ (with N in RPM; result in m³/min).
- [x] $Q_{act} = \eta_v \cdot Q_{th}$; **$\eta_v \in [0.85, 0.95]$** for healthy mechanical pumps.
- [x] Mass flow rate: $\dot{m} = \rho_{fuel} \cdot Q_{act}$; **petrol ρ = 740 kg/m³**, **diesel ρ = 840 kg/m³**.
- [x] Pump power: $P_{hyd} = \Delta p \cdot Q_{act}$ (Pa × m³/s = W).
- [x] Filter efficiency: $\eta_f = \dfrac{C_{in} - C_{out}}{C_{in}} \times 100$; typical industry value = **94 – 99 %**.
- [x] Pressure drop across filter: $\Delta p_f = \dfrac{\mu \cdot v \cdot L_m}{k \cdot A}$ — increases as filter clogs (k decreases).
- [x] **Bypass valve** opens when $\Delta p_f >$ spring setting (0.3 – 0.5 bar) — last-line safety.
- [x] Diesel filters include **water separator** (hydrophobic media) — water drained periodically.
- [x] **Filtration stages** (Diesel CRDi): Sock (100 µm) → In-line (30 µm) → Water separator (10 µm) → Final cartridge (5 µm).
- [x] Petrol engine filtration stages: Sock → In-line filter (10 µm).
- [x] BS-VI / Euro-6 norms demand **5 µm** final filtration in CRDi to protect injectors.
- [x] Vapour lock is more common in **mechanical pumps** with hot fuel lines — solved by in-tank pump.
- [x] Filter service interval: **15,000 – 30,000 km** (petrol); **20,000 – 40,000 km** (diesel).
- [x] **Always** include units in numericals; **always** state assumptions; **always** draw a labelled diagram.
- [x] The bypass valve is a **temporary safety** — never run the vehicle long-term with the filter bypassed; unfiltered fuel destroys injectors.
- [x] ECU runs the electric pump for **3 seconds** at ignition ON to prime the line, then shuts off if no cranking signal is detected (anti-theft + safety).
- [x] In the diaphragm pump, the **return spring** is the "engine" of the delivery stroke — it stores energy during suction and releases it during delivery.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Air Filter Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT205 - AUTOMOBILE POWER PLANT (AUTOMOBILE ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Air Filter Types

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Air Filter** (also called an **Air Cleaner** or **Intake Air Filter**) is a critical ancillary component of an internal combustion engine's air induction system. Its primary function is to **remove airborne contaminants** such as dust, dirt, soot, pollen, sand, carbon particles, and other abrasive particulates from the ambient atmospheric air before it enters the engine cylinders via the carburetor or fuel injection system.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PCAUT205 - Automobile Power Plant)**, the formal definition of an air filter is:

> An air filter is an induction system device that supplies **clean, filtered air** to the engine at the correct volume and minimum pressure drop, ensuring volumetric efficiency is maintained while protecting the cylinder walls, piston rings, valves, and bearings from premature abrasive wear.

The **filtration efficiency** $(\eta_f)$ of an air cleaner is mathematically defined as:

$$\eta_f = \frac{C_{in} - C_{out}}{C_{in}} \times 100\%$$

Where:
- $C_{in}$ = Concentration of dust in inlet air (mg/m³)
- $C_{out}$ = Concentration of dust in outlet air (mg/m³)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** The Air Induction System encompasses the air filter, intake manifold, and supercharging devices. Air filter classification by mechanism (inertial, oil bath, dry element, cyclone, and combination) is a **frequently tested topic** in Part A (3-mark) and Part B (14-mark) questions.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are breathing through a **dusty construction site** without a mask. The dust particles enter your lungs, causing irritation and long-term damage. An air filter is essentially the **"lungs' mask"** of the engine.

**Plain English Explanation:**
Think of the engine as a giant vacuum cleaner that sucks in approximately **10,000 to 15,000 liters of air** for every liter of fuel burned (for a petrol engine at stoichiometric ratio). Without filtering, the air carries abrasive dust particles that act like microscopic sandpaper, scoring the cylinder walls, wearing out piston rings, damaging valve seats, and contaminating the lubricating oil. The air filter acts like a **sieve or strainer** — but a highly engineered one — that traps these harmful particles while allowing the air molecules to pass through freely.

**Real-World Analogy — The Tea Strainer:**
A tea strainer holds back tea leaves while letting the liquid flow through. Similarly, an air filter holds back dust while letting air pass. However, an engine air filter must do this for **10,000+ liters of air per liter of fuel** with a pressure drop of less than **25 mm of water column** — a remarkable engineering feat!

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Metric:** A typical passenger car engine consumes approximately **500 kg of air per hour** at idle-cruise mixed operation. Over its service life, the air filter can trap anywhere from **2 to 5 kg of dust** before requiring replacement.

---

### 1.3 Why Air Filtration is Critical — Engineering Justification

| Parameter | Unfiltered Air | Filtered Air |
|---|---|---|
| Piston Ring Wear Rate | **Baseline (100%)** | Reduced by **60-75%** |
| Cylinder Bore Wear | High abrasive scoring | Minimal wear |
| Oil Contamination | Heavy soot/silica | Clean lubrication |
| Engine Life | **Reduced by 50-80%** | Normal rated life |
| Fuel Efficiency | **Reduced by 2-5%** | Optimal AFR maintained |

The consequences of inadequate air filtration include:
1. **Abrasive wear** on cylinder walls, piston rings, and bearings
2. **Oil contamination** leading to sludge formation
3. **Combustion chamber deposits** causing pre-ignition and detonation
4. **Valve seat recession** in overhead valve engines
5. **Increased exhaust emissions** due to improper air-fuel ratio

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Principle:** The air filter must achieve a balance between **high filtration efficiency** (cleaning air) and **low pressure drop** (not restricting airflow). This is the fundamental design trade-off in all air cleaner designs.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Classification of Air Filters — Overview

Air filters used in automobile engines are broadly classified based on the **filtration mechanism** employed:

1. **Inertial Type (Oil Bath) Air Cleaners**
2. **Dry Type Air Cleaners** (Paper Element, Felt Element, Foam Element)
3. **Wet Type Air Cleaners** (Oil Wetted Element)
4. **Cyclone Type Air Cleaners**
5. **Combination Air Cleaners** (Inertial + Dry Element)

---

### 2.2 Detailed Theoretical Analysis of Each Type

#### 2.2.1 Inertial Type / Oil Bath Air Cleaner

**Operating Principle:** This is one of the oldest and most reliable designs, employing the principle of **inertial separation** combined with **oil bath filtration**.

**Operational Steps (Bulleted Logic):**

- **Step 1 — Air Entry:** The dusty air enters the cleaner through a **pre-cleaner** (often a louvered or slotted inlet) at the top or side of the unit.
- **Step 2 — Directional Reversal:** The air is forced to undergo an **abrupt 180° directional change**. Due to inertia, the heavier dust particles cannot follow the curved air path and are thrown outward.
- **Step 3 — Oil Bath Contact:** The air then passes downward through a reservoir of **engine lubricating oil** (typically SAE 30 or SAE 40). The dust particles impinge on the oil surface and are trapped in the oil bath.
- **Step 4 — Secondary Filtration:** The air bubbles up through the oil and then through an **oil-wetted mesh screen** (usually a wire mesh or fine steel wool element) that provides secondary filtration.
- **Step 5 — Clean Air Exit:** The cleaned air rises to the outlet plenum and proceeds to the carburetor or throttle body.

**The "Why" Behind Each Step:**
- Why inertial separation? Because **larger dust particles (>10 μm)** have high momentum and can be removed simply by changing air direction — this is the most energy-efficient filtration method.
- Why an oil bath? The oil acts as a **sticky trapping medium** for particles that escape the inertial stage. It also self-cleans the element.
- Why a mesh screen? The mesh catches oil droplets to prevent them from being carried into the engine (which would cause **oil fouling** of spark plugs and combustion chambers).

**Real-World Utility:** Oil bath air cleaners were standard equipment on **older commercial vehicles, tractors, and heavy-duty trucks** (Mahindra, Tata, Ashok Leyland vintage models). They are still preferred in **dusty environments** (mining, construction, agricultural applications) due to their high dust-holding capacity.

---

#### 2.2.2 Dry Type Air Cleaners

**Operating Principle:** Uses a **dry filter element** (paper, felt, or synthetic fiber) that mechanically traps dust particles. No oil is used in the filtration process.

**Sub-Types:**

**(a) Paper Element Dry Air Cleaner:**
- Uses **pleated cellulose paper** (resin-impregnated) as the filter medium
- Pleating increases surface area by **5-7 times** compared to flat filter
- Filtration efficiency: **99.5-99.9%** for particles > 5 μm
- Used in **modern passenger cars** (Maruti Suzuki, Hyundai, Honda)
- **Disposable** — replaced at every service interval (typically 15,000-20,000 km)

**(b) Felt Element Dry Air Cleaner:**
- Uses **felted wool or synthetic fibers** as the filter medium
- Lower filtration efficiency than paper (~95-98%)
- **Reusable** — cleaned by tapping or compressed air
- Used in **older vehicles** and some motorcycle applications

**(c) Foam Element Dry Air Cleaner:**
- Uses **polyurethane foam** (open-cell) treated with a special oil
