- Common in **motorcycles** (Royal Enfield, Honda two-wheelers) and **off-road vehicles**
- Washable and reusable

**Operational Steps (Paper Element):**
- **Step 1:** Air enters the cleaner housing and is directed onto the outer surface of the **pleated paper element**.
- **Step 2:** Dust particles are trapped on the outer surface of the paper fibers (mechanical sieving + interception + diffusion).
- **Step 3:** Clean air passes through the paper to the central plenum.
- **Step 4:** The cleaned air exits through the outlet to the carburetor.

**Pressure Drop:** A typical paper element has an **initial pressure drop of 10-25 mm H₂O** when clean, rising to **50-100 mm H₂O** when fully loaded with dust.

---

#### 2.2.3 Wet Type (Oil Wetted) Air Cleaner

**Operating Principle:** Uses an element (usually polyurethane foam or wire mesh) that is **pre-coated with a special viscous oil**. The oil acts as the trapping medium, while the foam/mesh provides the structural support.

**Operational Steps:**
- **Step 1:** Air enters the cleaner housing.
- **Step 2:** The air is forced through the **oil-coated foam/mesh element**.
- **Step 3:** Dust particles impinge on the oil-coated fibers and are trapped by the viscous oil.
- **Step 4:** Clean air exits to the carburetor.

**Maintenance:** The element must be periodically **washed in solvent, dried, and re-oiled** with the specified oil.

**Application:** Commonly used in **two-wheelers** (motorcycles and scooters) and **small displacement engines**.

---

#### 2.2.4 Cyclone Type Air Cleaner

**Operating Principle:** Uses **centrifugal force** to separate dust particles from the air stream. The dust-free air is then passed through a secondary dry element for final cleaning.

**Operational Steps:**
- **Step 1:** Air enters a **tangentially-mounted inlet**, imparting a swirling motion to the air.
- **Step 2:** The swirling air creates a **vortex** inside the cyclone chamber.
- **Step 3:** Due to centrifugal acceleration, **heavier dust particles** are thrown outward against the cyclone wall.
- **Step 4:** The dust particles spiral down the wall and are collected in a **dust cup** at the bottom.
- **Step 5:** The clean air spirals upward through the central tube (vortex finder) and exits at the top.
- **Step 6 (Optional):** The partially cleaned air is then passed through a **dry paper element** for final filtration.

**Theoretical Basis:** The terminal velocity of a particle in a cyclone is governed by:

$$v_t = \frac{d_p^2 (\rho_p - \rho_a) g}{18 \mu}$$

Where:
- $v_t$ = Terminal settling velocity of particle
- $d_p$ = Particle diameter
- $\rho_p$ = Particle density
- $\rho_a$ = Air density
- $g$ = Gravitational acceleration
- $\mu$ = Dynamic viscosity of air

**Advantage:** Pre-cleaning by cyclone action **extends the life** of the secondary dry element significantly — often by 3-5 times in dusty conditions.

---

#### 2.2.5 Combination Air Cleaner

**Operating Principle:** Combines **two or more** filtration mechanisms in series to achieve superior cleaning. The most common combination is **Inertial Pre-cleaner + Dry Element**, but **Cyclone + Dry Element** is also widely used.

**Typical Configuration:**
- **Stage 1:** Centrifugal pre-cleaner (removes 70-85% of coarse dust)
- **Stage 2:** Dry paper element (removes remaining 99% of fine dust)
- **Stage 3 (Optional):** Activated charcoal layer (for vapor absorption and odor removal in some applications)

**Application:** Modern **diesel engines** in commercial vehicles, **construction equipment**, and **high-performance passenger cars** (e.g., Tata trucks, Volvo trucks, Mahindra SUVs).

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Filtration Efficiency | $\eta_f = \dfrac{C_{in} - C_{out}}{C_{in}} \times 100\%$ | % | Quantifies filter performance |
| 2 | Pressure Drop | $\Delta P = P_{in} - P_{out}$ | mm H₂O or kPa | Indicates airflow restriction |
| 3 | Terminal Settling Velocity | $v_t = \dfrac{d_p^2 (\rho_p - \rho_a) g}{18 \mu}$ | m/s | Cyclone separation design |
| 4 | Centrifugal Acceleration | $a_c = \dfrac{v^2}{r}$ | m/s² | Cyclone dust separation |
| 5 | Stoichiometric AFR (Petrol) | $AFR_{st} = 14.7 : 1$ | kg/kg | Air consumption calculation |
| 6 | Stoichiometric AFR (Diesel) | $AFR_{st} = 14.5 : 1$ | kg/kg | Air consumption calculation |
| 7 | Air Mass Flow Rate | $\dot{m}_{air} = \rho_a \cdot Q$ | kg/s | Filter sizing |
| 8 | Filter Surface Area | $A_f = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{air}}{v_f \cdot \rho_a}$ | m² | Element design |
| 9 | Dust Holding Capacity | $DHC = \dfrac{M_{dust}}{V_{filter}}$ | g/L | Service life estimation |
| 10 | Reynolds Number (Filter) | $Re_f = \dfrac{\rho_a \cdot v_f \cdot d_h}{\mu}$ | dimensionless | Flow regime classification |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** The filtration efficiency formula is the most commonly asked derivation question in Part B (14-mark) examinations. The terminal settling velocity equation is crucial for cyclone-type air cleaner design problems.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The choice of air filter type directly impacts:

1. **Engine Longevity:** A diesel truck operating in desert conditions without proper filtration may suffer complete engine failure within **50,000 km**, while a properly filtered engine can exceed **800,000 km**.

2. **Fuel Economy:** A clogged air filter can increase fuel consumption by **5-10%** due to disrupted air-fuel ratio and reduced volumetric efficiency.

3. **Emissions Compliance:** In modern BS-VI/Euro 6 vehicles, air filter restriction can cause **turbocharger surge** and **DPF (Diesel Particulate Filter) clogging**.

4. **Total Cost of Ownership:** Although paper elements are disposable, their **long service life** (15,000-20,000 km) and **high efficiency** make them the industry standard for passenger cars.

## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation: Filtration Efficiency

**Problem Statement:** An air filter receives air with a dust concentration of 25 mg/m³ at the inlet. After filtration, the outlet air has a dust concentration of 0.5 mg/m³. Calculate the filtration efficiency.

**Given Data:**
- $C_{in} = 25$ mg/m³ (inlet dust concentration)
- $C_{out} = 0.5$ mg/m³ (outlet dust concentration)

**Step 1: Write the governing equation for filtration efficiency.**

The filtration efficiency is defined as the ratio of dust removed to the dust entering the filter:

$$\eta_f = \frac{\text{Dust Removed}}{\text{Dust Entering}} \times 100\%$$

**Step 2: Express "dust removed" in terms of inlet and outlet concentrations.**

The amount of dust removed equals the difference between what entered and what exited:

$$\text{Dust Removed} = C_{in} - C_{out}$$

**Step 3: Substitute into the efficiency equation.**

$$\eta_f = \frac{C_{in} - C_{out}}{C_{in}} \times 100\%$$

**Step 4: Substitute the numerical values.**

$$\eta_f = \frac{25 - 0.5}{25} \times 100\%$$

**Step 5: Compute the numerator.**

$$25 - 0.5 = 24.5 \text{ mg/m}^3$$

**Step 6: Form the ratio.**

$$\eta_f = \frac{24.5}{25} \times 100\%$$

**Step 7: Calculate the final result.**

$$\eta_f = 0.98 \times 100\% = 98\%$$

**Final Answer:** The filtration efficiency of the air filter is **98%**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Terminal Settling Velocity of a Dust Particle in a Cyclone

**Problem Statement:** Derive the terminal velocity of a spherical dust particle settling under gravity in still air (Stokes' Law), which governs the minimum particle size that can be removed by a cyclone separator.

**Assumptions:**
- Particle is spherical with diameter $d_p$
- Particle density $\rho_p$ is much greater than air density $\rho_a$
- Flow is laminar (low Reynolds number, $Re < 1$)
- The particle has reached terminal velocity (zero acceleration)

**Step 1: Identify the forces acting on the particle.**

Three forces act on a falling dust particle:
- **Gravitational force (downward):** $F_g = \frac{\pi}{6} d_p^3 \rho_p g$
- **Buoyant force (upward):** $F_b = \frac{\pi}{6} d_p^3 \rho_a g$
- **Drag force (upward, opposing motion):** $F_d = 3 \pi \mu d_p v$

**Step 2: Apply the equilibrium condition at terminal velocity.**

At terminal velocity, the net force on the particle is zero:

$$F_g - F_b - F_d = 0$$

**Step 3: Substitute the force expressions.**

$$\frac{\pi}{6} d_p^3 \rho_p g - \frac{\pi}{6} d_p^3 \rho_a g - 3 \pi \mu d_p v_t = 0$$

**Step 4: Simplify by factoring out common terms.**

The first two terms can be combined:

$$\frac{\pi}{6} d_p^3 (\rho_p - \rho_a) g = 3 \pi \mu d_p v_t$$

**Step 5: Isolate the velocity $v_t$.**

$$v_t = \frac{\pi d_p^3 (\rho_p - \rho_a) g}{6 \cdot 3 \pi \mu d_p}$$

**Step 6: Cancel common factors.**

The $\pi$ cancels, and one power of $d_p$ cancels:

$$v_t = \frac{d_p^2 (\rho_p - \rho_a) g}{18 \mu}$$

**Final Derived Equation:**

$$\boxed{v_t = \frac{d_p^2 (\rho_p - \rho_a) g}{18 \mu}}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** The terminal velocity is proportional to the **square of the particle diameter**. This means that a 10 μm dust particle settles at a rate **100 times slower** than a 100 μm particle. This is why cyclones are highly effective for large particles but require secondary filtration for fine particulates.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Problem: Air Mass Flow Rate

**Problem:** A 1.5-liter, 4-cylinder petrol engine operates at 3000 RPM. Calculate the volumetric air flow rate through the air filter. Assume volumetric efficiency $\eta_v = 85\%$ and ambient conditions of 1 bar and 27°C.

**Given:**
- Displacement volume $V_d = 1.5$ L $= 1.5 \times 10^{-3}$ m³
- Engine speed $N = 3000$ RPM
- Volumetric efficiency $\eta_v = 0.85$
- Atmospheric pressure $P = 101.325$ kPa
- Temperature $T = 27°C = 300$ K
- Gas constant for air $R = 287$ J/(kg·K)

**Step 1: Calculate the theoretical air flow rate per second.**

The engine completes $\frac{N}{2}$ working cycles per second (for a 4-stroke engine):

$$\text{Working cycles per second} = \frac{3000}{2 \times 60} = 25 \text{ cycles/s}$$

**Step 2: Calculate the theoretical volumetric flow rate.**

$$Q_{th} = V_d \times \text{cycles per second} = 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 25$$

$$Q_{th} = 0.0375 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 3: Apply volumetric efficiency.**

$$Q_{actual} = \eta_v \times Q_{th} = 0.85 \times 0.0375$$

$$Q_{actual} = 0.031875 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 4: Calculate air density using ideal gas law.**

$$\rho_a = \frac{P}{R T} = \frac{101325}{287 \times 300}$$

$$\rho_a = \frac{101325}{86100} = 1.177 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

**Step 5: Calculate the mass flow rate of air.**

$$\dot{m}_{air} = \rho_a \times Q_{actual} = 1.177 \times 0.031875$$

$$\dot{m}_{air} = 0.0375 \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step 6: Convert to kg/hour for practical utility.**

$$\dot{m}_{air} = 0.0375 \times 3600 = 135 \text{ kg/hour}$$

**Final Answer:** The engine consumes **0.0375 kg/s** (or **135 kg/hour**) of air at 3000 RPM, which must all pass through the air filter. This is the value used to **size the air filter element**.

## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Air Induction System

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **functional architecture** of the air induction system, showing the role and interaction of each component, including all air filter types.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Atmospheric Air] --> B[Air Inlet Pre-Cleaner]
    B --> C{Filter Type Selection}
    C --> D[Oil Bath Air Cleaner]
    C --> E[Dry Paper Element Cleaner]
    C --> F[Oil Wetted Foam Cleaner]
    C --> G[Cyclone Pre-Cleaner]
    C --> H[Combination Cleaner]
    D --> I[Inertial Separation Stage]
    I --> J[Oil Bath Reservoir]
    J --> K[Oil Wetted Mesh]
    K --> L[Clean Air Plenum]
    E --> M[Pleated Paper Element]
    M --> L
    F --> N[Oil Coated Foam Element]
    N --> L
    G --> O[Centrifugal Vortex Chamber]
    O --> P[Dust Collection Cup]
    P --> Q[Secondary Dry Element]
    Q --> L
    H --> R[Stage 1 Inertial Separation]
    R --> S[Stage 2 Dry Paper Element]
    S --> L
    L --> T[Throttle Body / Carburetor]
    T --> U[Intake Manifold]
    U --> V[Engine Cylinder]
    V --> W[Combustion and Power Generation]
```

**Diagram Interpretation:**
- The diagram shows a **top-down flow** of air from atmosphere to engine cylinder.
- The **diamond-shaped decision node** represents the selection of filter type.
- Each filter type has a **specific path** through its internal stages.
- The **common convergence point** is the clean air plenum, which feeds the throttle body.
- The final **engine cylinder** receives the filtered, metered air for combustion.

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Oil Bath Air Cleaner Internal Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Dust Laden Air Inlet] --> B1[Inlet Louver Pre-Cleaner]
    B1 --> C1[Direction Reversal Chamber]
    C1 --> D1[Coarse Dust Drop Out]
    D1 --> E1[Oil Bath Reservoir]
    E1 --> F1[Air Bubbling Through Oil]
    F1 --> G1[Oil Wetted Wire Mesh Element]
    G1 --> H1[Oil Droplet Separator]
    H1 --> I1[Clean Air Outlet to Carburetor]
    E1 --> J1[Oil Drain Plug for Service]
    G1 --> K1[Removable Element for Cleaning]
```

**Functional Notes:**
- **C1 → D1:** Inertial separation removes **~70%** of coarse dust before reaching the oil.
- **E1 → F1:** Air is forced to bubble through oil, providing wet contact filtration.
- **G1:** Wire mesh retains oil and provides secondary mechanical filtration.
- **J1 / K1:** Service points for oil change and element cleaning.

---

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Cyclone Air Cleaner with Secondary Dry Element

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A2[Tangential Air Entry] --> B2[Swirl Chamber Vortex Generation]
    B2 --> C2[Centrifugal Force Acts on Dust]
    C2 --> D2[Dust Spirals Down Outer Wall]
    D2 --> E2[Dust Collection Cup at Bottom]
    E2 --> F2[Periodic Dust Emptying Service]
    B2 --> G2[Clean Air Spirals Up Center]
    G2 --> H2[Vortex Finder Tube]
    H2 --> I2[Secondary Dry Paper Element]
    I2 --> J2[Final Clean Air to Engine]
    I2 --> K2[Dust Trapped on Element]
    K2 --> L2[Element Replacement Service]
```

**Functional Notes:**
- **A2:** Tangential entry is critical — it imparts the **rotational velocity** necessary for centrifugal separation.
- **C2:** Centrifugal acceleration $a_c = v^2 / r$ can be **1000-5000 times** greater than gravity for typical cyclone geometries.
- **E2:** Dust collection is continuous; the cup must be emptied at regular intervals.
- **I2:** The secondary element is a **safety net** for fine particles (< 5 μm) that escape cyclone action.
