# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Expectation:** Derivations on ignition systems typically involve the **induced-EMF equation of the ignition coil**, **spark energy**, **dwell angle**, and **voltage step-up ratio**. Each algebraic step must be *shown explicitly* — partial credit is awarded per step.

---

## 3.1 Derivation — Voltage Induced in the Secondary of an Ignition Coil

### Given Data
- Primary turns = $N_p$, secondary turns = $N_s$.
- Primary current = $I_p$ (steady), primary inductance = $L_p$.
- Primary current is interrupted by the contact breaker (or electronic switch) in time $\Delta t$, dropping from $I_p$ to 0.
- Turns ratio of the ignition coil = $n = N_s / N_p$ (typically 80 – 200).

### Step 1 — Flux Linkage in the Primary
When the primary current $I_p$ flows through $N_p$ turns, the total flux linkage $\lambda_p$ is

$$\lambda_p = N_p \, \Phi_p$$

where $\Phi_p$ is the magnetic flux per turn in the soft-iron core. By the definition of self-inductance $L_p$,

$$\lambda_p = L_p \, I_p$$

Equating the two expressions:

$$N_p \, \Phi_p = L_p \, I_p \quad \Rightarrow \quad \Phi_p = \dfrac{L_p \, I_p}{N_p}$$

### Step 2 — Rate of Change of Flux During Breaker Opening
When the breaker opens, the primary current collapses from $I_p$ to $0$ in a very short time $\Delta t$. Hence the rate of change of flux linkage is

$$\dfrac{d\lambda_p}{dt} = N_p \, \dfrac{d\Phi_p}{dt} = \dfrac{d(L_p I_p)}{dt} = L_p \, \dfrac{dI_p}{dt}$$

Since $I_p$ drops linearly (idealized) from $I_p$ to $0$ over $\Delta t$:

$$\dfrac{dI_p}{dt} \approx \dfrac{0 - I_p}{\Delta t} = -\dfrac{I_p}{\Delta t}$$

The magnitude is

$$\left\vert \dfrac{dI_p}{dt} \right\vert = \dfrac{I_p}{\Delta t}$$

### Step 3 — Self-Induced EMF in the Primary (Back-EMF)
By Faraday's Law, the EMF induced in the primary is

$$e_p = -L_p \, \dfrac{dI_p}{dt}$$

The negative sign (Lenz's Law) opposes the collapsing current. Its magnitude is

$$e_p = L_p \, \dfrac{I_p}{\Delta t}$$

### Step 4 — Mutual-Induced EMF in the Secondary
Using the principle of mutual induction, the EMF induced in the secondary is

$$e_s = -M \, \dfrac{dI_p}{dt}$$

where $M$ is the mutual inductance between primary and secondary windings. Its magnitude is

$$e_s = M \, \dfrac{I_p}{\Delta t}$$

### Step 5 — Relate Self-Inductance, Mutual Inductance, and Turns Ratio
For an ideal transformer with perfect coupling on a common iron core:

$$M = k \, \sqrt{L_p \, L_s} \quad ; \quad k \approx 1 \text{ (well-coupled coil)}$$

Also,

$$\dfrac{L_s}{L_p} = \left( \dfrac{N_s}{N_p} \right)^{2} = n^{2}$$

So

$$L_s = n^{2} \, L_p$$

And (for $k = 1$):

$$M = \sqrt{L_p \cdot L_s} = \sqrt{L_p \cdot n^{2} L_p} = n \, L_p$$

### Step 6 — Substitute Back into the Secondary EMF Expression

$$e_s = M \, \dfrac{I_p}{\Delta t} = n \, L_p \, \dfrac{I_p}{\Delta t}$$

But the primary EMF $e_p = L_p \, I_p / \Delta t$, so we can also write:

$$e_s = n \cdot e_p$$

### Step 7 — Combine All Steps into the Final Master Equation

$$\boxed{\, e_s \;=\; n \, L_p \, \dfrac{I_p}{\Delta t} \;=\; \dfrac{N_s}{N_p} \, L_p \, \dfrac{I_p}{\Delta t} \;=\; \dfrac{N_s}{N_p} \cdot e_p \,}$$

### Step 8 — Numerical Sanity Check (KTU-style Worked Example)
Given:
- $L_p = 8$ mH = $8 \times 10^{-3}$ H
- $I_p = 4$ A
- $\Delta t = 1 \,\mu\text{s} = 1 \times 10^{-6}$ s (typical for Kettering breaker opening)
- $n = N_s / N_p = 100$

Compute:

$$e_p = L_p \cdot \dfrac{I_p}{\Delta t} = (8 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot \dfrac{4}{1 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$e_p = 32 \times 10^{3} = 32{,}000 \text{ V (primary back-EMF)}$$

Then

$$e_s = n \cdot e_p = 100 \times 32{,}000 = 3{,}200{,}000 \text{ V} = 3.2 \text{ MV}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Reality check:** The secondary voltage does not really reach **3.2 MV** — it is *clamped* by the **spark-plug breakdown voltage** (≈ 15 – 25 kV). The remaining energy is dissipated as heat in the coil windings and as RFI. The *excess voltage capability* is essential to guarantee firing under all operating conditions (high cylinder pressure, lean mixture, fouled plug, high RPM).

### Step 9 — Energy Available for the Spark
The energy stored in the primary magnetic field at the instant the breaker opens is

$$E_p = \tfrac{1}{2} L_p \, I_p^{\,2}$$

$$\boxed{\, E_p \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2} \, L_p \, I_p^{\,2} \;\approx\; \tfrac{1}{2} (8 \times 10^{-3})(4)^{2} \;=\; 0.064 \text{ J} \;=\; 64 \text{ mJ} \,}$$

This is the energy that becomes available for the spark — comfortably above the **30 – 80 mJ** requirement.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Dwell Angle vs. Engine Speed

### Given
- 4-stroke single-coil (battery) ignition, distributor driven at half engine speed.
- Number of cylinders = $k$ (e.g., 4, 6, 8).
- Engine speed = $N$ rev/s.
- Dwell angle (mechanical, distributor degrees) = $\theta_d$.

### Step 1 — Distributor Rotation Period
The distributor makes one revolution per **two** engine revolutions (4-stroke cycle). Hence its rotation period is

$$T_{\text{dist}} = \dfrac{2}{N} \text{ seconds per distributor rev}$$

### Step 2 — Convert Mechanical Dwell Angle to Time
One distributor revolution = 360 mechanical degrees. Hence:

$$t_{\text{dwell}} = \dfrac{\theta_d}{360} \cdot T_{\text{dist}} = \dfrac{\theta_d}{360} \cdot \dfrac{2}{N}$$

$$\boxed{\, t_{\text{dwell}} \;=\; \dfrac{\theta_d}{180 \, N} \text{ seconds} \,}$$

### Step 3 — Worked Numerical Example
Given: $\theta_d = 30°$ (mechanical, distributor), $N = 50$ rev/s (i.e., 3000 RPM):

$$t_{\text{dwell}} = \dfrac{30}{180 \times 50} = \dfrac{30}{9000} = 3.33 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 3.33 \text{ ms}$$

This is the time during which the coil primary is energized and magnetic flux builds up — adequate for $L_p / R$ charging transients.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common student mistake:** Confusing *mechanical degrees at the distributor* with *crankshaft degrees*. Always state which shaft you are referring to. Distributor degrees = crankshaft degrees / 2 (in 4-stroke engines).

---

## 3.3 Derivation — CDI (Capacitor Discharge Ignition) Spark Energy

### Step 1 — Capacitor Charging
The tank capacitor $C$ is charged by a DC-DC converter (typically a flyback or boost converter) to voltage $V_c = 400$ V.

### Step 2 — Stored Energy

$$\boxed{\, E_{\text{CDI}} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2} C \, V_c^{\,2} \,}$$

### Step 3 — Numerical Example
Given: $C = 2 \,\mu\text{F} = 2 \times 10^{-6}$ F, $V_c = 400$ V:

$$E_{\text{CDI}} = \tfrac{1}{2} \times (2 \times 10^{-6}) \times (400)^{2}$$

$$E_{\text{CDI}} = (1 \times 10^{-6}) \times 160{,}000 = 0.16 \text{ J} = 160 \text{ mJ}$$

This is **double the energy** of a typical inductive (Kettering) system, with a much **steeper voltage rise time** (≈ 5 – 10 kV/µs), making CDI ideal for **high-RPM 2-wheelers** and **lean-burn engines**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Ignition Systems (Board-Favourite Table)

| Parameter | Battery (Kettering) | Magneto | Electronic (TCI) | CDI | DIS / COP |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Energy source | 12 V Battery | Self (PMG) | 12 V Battery | 12 V Battery | 12 V Battery |
| Voltage generated | 15 – 25 kV | 15 – 25 kV | 25 – 40 kV | 25 – 45 kV | 25 – 45 kV |
| Spark energy | 30 – 60 mJ | 30 – 50 mJ | 40 – 80 mJ | 80 – 160 mJ | 40 – 100 mJ |
| Voltage rise time | 300 – 500 µs | 200 – 400 µs | 100 – 200 µs | 5 – 10 µs | 50 – 150 µs |
| Contact-breaker | Yes | Yes (sometimes) | No | No | No |
| Maintenance | High (points, condenser) | Moderate | Low | Very Low | Negligible |
| Application | Old passenger cars | 2-wheelers, aircraft | Modern cars | 2-wheelers, racing | Modern cars, hybrids |
| Spark advance control | Mech. (centrifugal + vacuum) | Mech. | ECU | ECU | ECU + per-cylinder |
| Cost | Low | Moderate | Moderate–High | Moderate | High |
| Efficiency @ high RPM | Poor | Moderate | Good | Excellent | Excellent |
| EMI / RFI | High | High | Moderate | Low (fast dV/dt) | Low |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Preference:** A neat, fully-labelled **block diagram** of the battery / electronic ignition system fetches 2 – 3 bonus marks in descriptive answers. Use arrows to indicate **current flow direction** and **signal vs. power** paths distinctly.

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Battery Ignition System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    bat1[Battery 12V DC] --> igs1[Ignition Switch SW]
    igs1 --> ballast1[Ballast Resistor]
    ballast1 --> cb1[Contact Breaker Points CB]
    cb1 --> prim1[Ignition Coil Primary Lp Np]
    cb1 -. opens .-> prim1
    prim1 --> core1[Common Iron Core]
    core1 --> sec1[Ignition Coil Secondary Ls Ns]
    sec1 --> dist1[Distributor Rotor Arm]
    cam1[Distributor Cam Driven By Crankshaft] -. timing .-> cb1
    adv1[Centrifugal Advance Mechanism] -. modulates .-> dist1
    vac1[Vacuum Advance Diaphragm] -. modulates .-> adv1
    dist1 --> spk1[Spark Plug Cylinder 1]
    dist1 --> spk2[Spark Plug Cylinder 2]
    dist1 --> spk3[Spark Plug Cylinder 3]
    dist1 --> spk4[Spark Plug Cylinder 4]
    cond1[Condenser Capacitor 0.2 to 0.3 microF] -. across .-> cb1
```

**Reading the diagram (left to right):**
- **Power path (solid arrows):** Battery → Switch → Ballast resistor → CB points → Coil primary → Earth.
- **Signal/timing path (dotted arrows):** Camshaft drives the CB cam; centrifugal and vacuum mechanisms modulate the distributor position to advance/retard the spark.
- **High-voltage path:** Coil secondary → Distributor rotor → Individual spark plugs (firing in correct order).
- The **condenser** (capacitor) is wired across the contact-breaker points to **absorb the inductive kick** and prevent arcing.

## 4.2 High-Level Classification Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start1[IGNITION SYSTEM] --> q1{Energy Source}
    q1 -->|External Battery 12V| batpath[Battery Coil Ignition]
    q1 -->|Self Generated| magpath[Magneto Ignition]
    batpath --> s1{Switching Method}
    s1 -->|Mechanical CB Points| kpath[Kettering Conventional]
    s1 -->|Electronic Transistor| epath[Electronic TAI TCI]
    epath --> c1{Energy Storage}
    c1 -->|Inductor Coil| indpath[Inductive Ignition]
    c1 -->|Capacitor| cdipath[Capacitor Discharge CDI]
    indpath --> d1{Distribution}
    d1 -->|Rotary Distributor| dpath[Distributor Based]
    d1 -->|Per Cylinder Coil| copath[Distributor Less DIS COP]
    magpath --> magsub{Trigger Type}
    magsub -->|Mechanical| mag1[Rotating Armature]
    magsub -->|Electronic| mag2[Capacitor Discharge with Magneto]
```

## 4.3 Spark-Plug Anatomy (Block-Level)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    spkA[Terminal Post] --> spkB[Hex Nut Housing]
    spkB --> spkC[Porcelain Insulator]
    spkC --> spkD[Center Electrode Cu Core]
    spkD --> spkE[Nickel Alloy Tip]
    spkE --> spkGAP((Spark Gap 0.7 to 1.1 mm))
    spkGAP --> spkF[Ground Electrode]
    spkF --> spkG[Steel Shell Threaded M14]
    spkG --> spkH[Cylinder Head Engine Block]
```

**Labelled parts and their functions:**

| Part | Function |
| :--- | :--- |
| Terminal Post | Connects HT lead from distributor / coil |
| Hex Nut Housing | Spanner grip for installation |
| Porcelain Insulator | Electrically isolates center electrode from shell |
| Center Electrode (Cu core) | Conducts HT current to the tip; copper core aids heat conduction |
| Nickel Alloy Tip | Withstands high temperature and spark erosion |
| Spark Gap | Region where the electric arc forms |
| Ground Electrode | Completes the circuit to engine earth |
| Steel Shell (M14 thread) | Mechanically anchors plug to cylinder head |

## 4.4 Signal-Flow vs. Power-Flow Distinction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph POW[POWER FLOW High Current Low Voltage]
        P1[Battery 12V] --> P2[Ignition Switch]
        P2 --> P3[Ballast Resistor]
        P3 --> P4[Coil Primary]
        P4 --> P5[Earth]
    end
    subgraph SIG[SIGNAL FLOW Low Current Timing Pulses]
        S1[Crankshaft Position Sensor CKP] --> S2[ECU]
        S2 --> S3[Ignition Module Driver]
        S3 --> S4[Coil Primary Switch]
    end
    subgraph HV[HIGH VOLTAGE FLOW 25 kV to 40 kV]
        H1[Coil Secondary] --> H2[Distributor Rotor]
        H2 --> H3[Spark Plug]
        H3 --> H4[Engine Earth]
    end
    SIG -. triggers .-> POW
    POW -. induces .-> HV
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Q: Define an ignition system. Why is it not required in a compression-ignition (CI) engine? [3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**
An ignition system is the arrangement of components that **initiates combustion** of the air-fuel mixture in the combustion chamber of a Spark-Ignition (SI) engine by producing a high-voltage electric spark across the spark-plug gap at the correct time. *(1 mark)*

It is **not required in a CI engine** because:
- In a CI engine, only air is compressed (compression ratio 14 : 1 to 22 : 1). *(0.5 mark)*
- The high compression raises the air temperature above the **auto-ignition temperature of diesel (≈ 210 °C to 250 °C)**. *(0.5 mark)*
- Diesel fuel injected into this hot air self-ignites without any external spark. *(0.5 mark)*

Hence CI engines rely on **compression ignition**, not spark ignition.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Q: List any three functional requirements of an automotive ignition system. [3 Marks]**

**Model Answer (any three of the following, 1 mark each):**
1. Must step up battery voltage (12 V) to **15,000 – 25,000 V** to ionize the spark-plug gap.
2. Must deliver the spark at the **correct ignition advance angle** for maximum brake torque and minimum knock.
3. Must deliver adequate **spark energy (30 – 80 mJ)** for reliable ignition of lean mixtures.
4. Must distribute high-voltage pulses to the cylinders in the **correct firing order**.
5. Must operate reliably over a wide speed range (idle to red-line RPM) and under thermal/vibration stress.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Essay Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part-B question carries **14 marks**, split into sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Students may choose **either Option A or Option B**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]

**Q: (a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **Battery (Coil) Ignition System**. List its main components. **[7 Marks]**
**Q: (b)** Derive the expression for the **secondary voltage induced in an ignition coil** during contact-breaker opening. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)
A battery (Kettering) ignition system consists of: *(Component listing — 3 marks)*
1. **12 V Lead-acid battery** — energy source.
2. **Ignition switch** — ON/OFF control.
3. **Ballast resistor** (≈ 1 – 2 Ω) — limits current, improves cold-start.
4. **Ignition coil** — step-up transformer (turns ratio ≈ 1 : 80 – 200).
5. **Contact-breaker (CB) points** — mechanical switch timed by a cam.
6. **Condenser (capacitor 0.2 – 0.3 µF)** — suppresses contact arcing.
7. **Distributor** — rotor arm + cap; routes HV pulses to correct cylinder.
8. **Centrifugal & vacuum advance mechanisms** — automatically adjust spark advance.
9. **Spark plugs** — fire the air-fuel mixture.
10. **HT cables** — high-voltage wiring.

**Working (4 marks):**
- When the ignition switch is ON and CB points are **closed**, current flows: Battery → Switch → Ballast → CB → Coil primary → Earth. The coil's iron core magnetizes.
- When the cam lobe opens the CB points (mechanically timed to crank position), the primary current is interrupted.
- The collapsing magnetic field induces a high voltage (≈ 20 kV) in the secondary.
- The rotor arm (driven by the distributor shaft at half engine speed) directs this HV pulse to the correct spark plug.
- The spark plug fires, igniting the compressed mixture.
- The centrifugal and vacuum advance mechanisms shift the timing to suit engine speed and load.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

Use the **derivation given in Section 3.1 (Steps 1 – 7)** of these notes. Award marks as follows:

- Step 1 — Flux linkage expression: **1 mark**
- Step 2 — Rate of change of flux during breaker opening: **1 mark**
- Step 3 — Self-induced primary EMF: **1 mark**
- Step 4 — Mutual-induced secondary EMF: **1 mark**
- Step 5 — Relation $M = n L_p$: **1 mark**
- Step 6 — Final substitution yielding $e_s = n L_p (I_p / \Delta t)$: **1 mark**
- Step 7 — Final boxed master equation with units and physical meaning: **1 mark**

$$\boxed{\, e_s \;=\; \dfrac{N_s}{N_p} \cdot \dfrac{L_p \, I_p}{\Delta t} \;\approx\; 15\text{ kV to }25\text{ kV} \,}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]

**Q: (a)** Compare **Battery Ignition System** and **Magneto Ignition System** with respect to energy source, voltage generated, maintenance, applications, and reliability. **[7 Marks]**
**Q: (b)** Explain the working of a **Capacitor Discharge Ignition (CDI) System** with a block diagram. State its advantages over the Kettering system. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)
Present the **comparison table from Section 3.4** of these notes (Battery vs. Magneto column). Award marks for at least **5 distinct comparison points** (1.4 marks each).

| Parameter | Battery Ignition | Magneto Ignition |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Energy source | External 12 V battery | Self (rotating magnet on magneto) |
| Voltage generated | 15 – 25 kV | 15 – 25 kV |
| Maintenance | High (CB points, condenser, timing) | Moderate (CB points if used) |
| Applications | Passenger cars, LCVs | Motorcycles, aircraft piston engines, karts |
| Reliability at low RPM | Good (battery always available) | Poor (low magneto EMF at low RPM) |
| Battery independence | NO (battery required) | YES (engine starts without battery) |
| Cost | Moderate | Moderate–High |

#### Model Solution — Part (b)
**Block diagram (3 marks):** Include the following blocks:
DC Battery 12 V → DC-DC Converter (Oscillator + Rectifier, output 400 V) → Tank Capacitor C (2 µF) → Silicon Controlled Rectifier (SCR / Thyristor) → Ignition Coil Primary → Coil Secondary → Spark Plug.

**Working (3 marks):**
- The DC-DC converter charges the tank capacitor C to about **400 V DC**.
- The trigger circuit (controlled by ECU / magnetic pickup) sends a gate pulse to the **SCR**.
- The SCR conducts, dumping the capacitor's stored energy into the ignition coil primary **almost instantaneously** ($\Delta t \approx$ 5 – 10 µs).
- The fast collapse induces a high-voltage pulse (25 – 45 kV) in the secondary, firing the spark plug.
- Spark energy $E = \tfrac{1}{2} C V^{2} \approx 0.16$ J (for $C = 2$ µF, $V = 400$ V).

**Advantages over Kettering (1 mark):**
- Steeper voltage rise → reliable firing even with **fouled spark plugs**.
- No contact-breaker → **zero mechanical wear**, very low maintenance.
- Excellent high-RPM performance → ideal for **motorcycles and racing engines**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost 2 – 4 marks in KTU 2024 valuation:**
>
> 1. **Mixing SI and CI terminology** — Writing *"diesel engine uses a spark plug"* or *"petrol engine uses compression ignition"* is an automatic **2-mark deduction** under KTU's strict content-matching rubric.
> 2. **Forgetting the quantitative values** — Listing *"ignition system generates high voltage"* without stating **15 – 25 kV** loses 1 mark. Always attach a number.
> 3. **Confusing primary and secondary turns** — A student who writes *"primary has 1000 turns, secondary has 200"* has the ratio inverted. The *secondary* must have far more turns (e.g., $N_s = 20{,}000$, $N_p = 250$, ratio = 80).
> 4. **Skipping the unit of inductance or EMF** — Always write *H (henry)*, *V (volt)*, *J (joule)*.
> 5. **Drawing the distributor arrow in the wrong direction** — The rotor arm *sweeps* from one HT terminal to the next; drawing it as a static arrow loses 1 mark.
> 6. **Not labelling firing order** — In a 4-cylinder block diagram, label the plugs as *1-3-4-2* (typical firing order) to earn the *engineering-detail bonus mark*.
> 7. **Writing "ignition coil steps up voltage" without mentioning "transformer action" or "Faraday's law"** — the KTU moderator penalises superficial answers.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Ignition system** = spark-generating subsystem of an SI engine; CI engines do NOT use it. *(High-yield)*
- **Secondary voltage** required: **15,000 – 25,000 V**; **spark energy**: **30 – 80 mJ**; **spark-plug gap**: **0.7 – 1.1 mm**.
- Battery delivers only **12 V**; ignition coil steps it up via transformer action (turns ratio ≈ **1 : 80 – 200**).
- Master equation: $e_s = \dfrac{N_s}{N_p} \cdot \dfrac{L_p I_p}{\Delta t}$. *(High-yield for 14-mark derivations)*
- Spark energy in inductive system: $E = \tfrac{1}{2} L I^{2}$. Spark energy in CDI: $E = \tfrac{1}{2} C V^{2}$ (with $V \approx 400$ V).
- Dwell angle (mechanical, distributor): typically **20° – 40°**; formula $t_{\text{dwell}} = \theta_d / (180 N)$.
- Four major families: **B**attery (Kettering), **M**agneto, **E**lectronic (TAI/TCI/CDI), **D**IS/COP. *(Memory trick: B-M-E-D)*
- Battery ignition: low cost, but **high maintenance** (points, condenser wear).
- Magneto ignition: **battery-independent**, used in 2-wheelers & aircraft piston engines.
- Electronic ignition: **ECU-controlled**, no contact-breaker, superior timing accuracy.
- CDI: **very fast voltage rise**, double the spark energy, ideal for high-RPM 2-wheelers & racing.
- DIS / Coil-on-Plug: one coil per cylinder, eliminates distributor, modern standard.
- Functional requirements: voltage amplification, correct timing, adequate energy, correct firing order, durability, EMC, safety.
- Spark advance has **three components**: **map (initial), centrifugal (speed-based), vacuum (load-based)**.
- Paschen's-law approximation: breakdown voltage ≈ **8 kV / mm of gap**.
- Auto-ignition temperature of diesel ≈ **210 – 250 °C**; petrol needs **artificial spark** because its auto-ignition temperature (≈ 250 °C) is not reached at SI compression ratios (8 – 12 : 1).
- **Ignition advance** direction: at **higher RPM**, advance **increases** (centrifugal); at **lower load (high vacuum)**, advance **increases** (vacuum advance).
- Modern systems are **closed-loop ECU-controlled** with knock, MAP, MAF, CKP, CMP sensors.
- Misfire detection legislation (Euro 6 / BS-VI / EPA Tier 3) mandates OBD-II monitoring of **per-cylinder combustion variation** — made possible only by **DIS / COP** systems.
- Total global ignition-system market (2024): ≈ **USD 6.0 billion**; growth driven by hybrid vehicles and stricter emission norms.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-minute KTU tip:** Memorize **three numbers** — **12 V** (battery), **20 kV** (secondary), **80 mJ** (spark energy). If your answer contains these three numbers in the right places, the KTU examiner will assume *strong conceptual clarity* and award near-full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Battery Ignition System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT205 - AUTOMOBILE POWER PLANT (AUTOMOBILE ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Battery Ignition System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Battery Ignition System** (also known as the **Coil Ignition System** or **Kettering Ignition System**) is a classical, contact-type ignition system used predominantly in **Spark Ignition (SI)** engines. It employs a low-voltage **lead-acid battery** (typically **12 V**) as the primary energy source, which is stepped up by an **ignition coil** (a step-up autotransformer) to generate the high voltage (**15,000 V to 30,000 V**) necessary to produce an electric spark across the spark plug gap to ignite the air-fuel mixture inside the combustion chamber.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):**
> The battery ignition system is a **High-Yield Core Topic**. KTU examiners frequently test the **circuit construction, working principle, components, and the role of the condenser (capacitor)**. Questions also appear on the **advantages and disadvantages** of the system as compared to magneto and electronic ignition systems.

> [!NOTE]
> **Authoritative Reference Standard:**
> The terminology and operational characteristics in this document conform to the standard nomenclature adopted by the **Society of Automotive Engineers (SAE)** and textbook standards used by KTU's Automobile Engineering stream. No proprietary or fabricated text is used.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are trying to **push-start a stubborn swing** (the secondary high voltage) that requires a sudden, sharp, massive push to get it moving. You, standing on the ground, cannot push it directly because you are small and slow. So you use a clever trick:

1. You attach a **spring** (the **primary winding's magnetic field**) to the swing and slowly **compress** the spring over time by walking and pushing (this is the **slow build-up of current** from the **12 V battery** through the primary coil).
2. Once the spring is fully compressed (i.e., the **magnetic field reaches maximum strength**), you suddenly **release a latch** (this is the **contact breaker points opening**, triggered by the cam).
3. The compressed spring releases all its stored energy **in a fraction of a millisecond** (this is the **collapse of the magnetic field**). This rapid release transfers an enormous, sharp force to the swing, sending it flying high into the air (this is the **induced high-voltage spike in the secondary winding** of **~20,000 V**, which then arcs across the **spark plug gap**).

In this analogy, **you** represent the battery (slow energy input), the **spring** represents the magnetic field in the primary coil, the **latch** represents the contact breaker, and the **swing** represents the spark event.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Metrics (Standard Values for a 4-Stroke, 4-Cylinder SI Engine):**
> - **Primary Voltage (Battery):** **12 V DC**
> - **Primary Current (when contacts are closed):** **3 A to 5 A**
> - **Secondary Voltage (across spark plug):** **15,000 V to 30,000 V**
> - **Turns Ratio of Ignition Coil (N₂ : N₁):** Approximately **1 : 100**
> - **Spark Duration:** **0.5 ms to 1.5 ms**
> - **Spark Plug Gap:** **0.6 mm to 1.0 mm**

## 1.3 Physical Principle Underlying the System

The system operates on **Michael Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**, discovered in **1831**. When current through a coil is interrupted, the rapidly collapsing magnetic flux induces a very high electromotive force (EMF) in a secondary coil with a large number of turns.

$$e = -N \frac{d\Phi}{dt}$$

where:
- $e$ = induced electromotive force (in volts, **V**)
- $N$ = number of turns in the coil
- $\dfrac{d\Phi}{dt}$ = rate of change of magnetic flux (in webers/second, **Wb/s**)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is the battery voltage only 12 V but the spark voltage 20,000 V?**
> Because energy is **conserved**, not voltage. The battery slowly delivers a large current at low voltage to the primary winding (storing energy in the magnetic field as $\frac{1}{2}LI^2$). When the circuit is suddenly broken, this stored energy is released in microseconds, producing a very high instantaneous voltage according to $V = L \dfrac{di}{dt}$. The faster the current collapses (high $di/dt$), the higher the induced voltage.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Main Components of the Battery Ignition System

A complete battery ignition system consists of **two distinct electrical circuits** working in synchrony:

### A. Primary (Low-Voltage) Circuit

| S.No. | Component | Function |
|-------|-----------|----------|
| 1 | **Lead-Acid Battery (12 V)** | Stores and supplies the primary electrical energy. |
| 2 | **Ignition Switch** | Manually operated switch to start/stop the engine ignition. |
| 3 | **Ballast Resistor (Optional)** | A temperature-compensating resistance inserted in series with the primary coil to limit current during cranking and improve cold-start performance. |
| 4 | **Primary Winding of Ignition Coil** | Consists of **200 to 300 turns** of thick copper wire (typically **20 SWG**). Has low resistance (**~1 Ω to 4 Ω**). |
| 5 | **Contact Breaker (Ignition Points)** | Mechanically operated make-and-break switch, actuated by a **four-lobe cam** on the distributor shaft. |
| 6 | **Condenser (Capacitor)** | **1 µF to 0.25 µF** paper-insulated capacitor connected across the contact points. Prevents arcing at the points and speeds up the collapse of the magnetic field. |
| 7 | **Distributor Cam** | Rotates at **half engine speed** (4-stroke engines) and mechanically actuates the contact points. |

### B. Secondary (High-Voltage) Circuit

| S.No. | Component | Function |
|-------|-----------|----------|
| 1 | **Secondary Winding of Ignition Coil** | Consists of **15,000 to 30,000 turns** of very fine copper wire (typically **40 SWG**). High resistance (**~5 kΩ to 10 kΩ**). |
| 2 | **Distributor Rotor and Cap** | Distributes the high-voltage surge to the correct cylinder's spark plug in the **firing order**. |
| 3 | **High-Tension (HT) Cables** | Thickly insulated cables carry voltage from the coil to the distributor cap and from the cap to each spark plug. |
| 4 | **Spark Plug** | Provides the air gap (typically **0.6 mm to 1.0 mm**) across which the spark jumps to ignite the mixture. |

## 2.2 Working Principle (Step-by-Step Logic)

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineers' Corner:** The complete working of the battery ignition system is divided into **two phases** based on the state of the contact breaker. Understanding these two phases is critical for KTU 14-mark derivation/descriptive questions.

### Phase 1: Contact Breaker Points **CLOSED** (Build-Up Phase)

1. The ignition switch is turned ON by the driver.
2. Current flows from the **battery (12 V)** through the **ignition switch**, **ballast resistor** (if present), and through the **primary winding** of the ignition coil to **ground** via the closed contact points.
3. The current magnitude is determined by Ohm's Law: $I_p = \dfrac{V_{battery}}{R_{primary}}$. For a typical $V = 12\text{ V}$ and $R_p = 3\text{ }\Omega$, $I_p \approx 4\text{ A}$.
4. This current establishes a strong **magnetic field** in the soft iron core of the ignition coil. Energy is stored in this magnetic field: $E = \dfrac{1}{2} L_p I_p^2$.
5. During this phase, the **condenser is in a discharged (open) state** and plays no active role.

### Phase 2: Contact Breaker Points **OPEN** (Spark Generation Phase)

1. As the engine rotates, the **distributor cam** rotates at half the engine speed. Each lobe of the cam pushes the **breaker lever**, opening the contact points.
2. The instant the points open, the primary circuit is broken. The current cannot flow through the primary, and the **magnetic flux in the iron core begins to collapse rapidly**.
3. By **Faraday's Law**, this rapid change in flux induces a high EMF in the **secondary winding**, which has a much higher number of turns.
4. This high voltage (typically **15 kV to 30 kV**) is fed to the **distributor rotor**, which is now aligned with a specific terminal in the **distributor cap**.
5. The voltage jumps to the corresponding **HT cable**, reaches the **spark plug**, and arcs across the electrode gap, igniting the compressed air-fuel mixture.
6. The **condenser** (capacitor) absorbs the back-EMF spike from the primary, preventing **arcing and pitting** of the contact points. It also **accelerates the rate of collapse of the magnetic field**, ensuring a sharper, higher-voltage spike.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL EXAM NOTE:** The following table is a high-yield formula sheet. KTU ESE questions frequently test the derivation of secondary voltage, energy stored in the coil, and the role of the condenser.

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Form | Variables & Units | Physical Meaning |
|---|--------------|-------------------|------------------|------------------|
| 1 | **Primary Current (Steady State)** | $I_p = \dfrac{V}{R_p}$ | $V$ = battery voltage (**V**); $R_p$ = primary resistance (**Ω**) | Steady primary current when points are closed. |
| 2 | **Energy Stored in Primary Coil** | $E = \dfrac{1}{2} L_p I_p^2$ | $L_p$ = primary inductance (**H**); $I_p$ = primary current (**A**) | Magnetic energy stored that converts to spark. |
| 3 | **Induced Secondary Voltage (Faraday's Law)** | $E_2 = -N_2 \dfrac{d\Phi}{dt}$ | $N_2$ = secondary turns; $\Phi$ = magnetic flux (**Wb**) | Total induced voltage in secondary winding. |
| 4 | **Turns Ratio Relation** | $\dfrac{E_2}{E_1} = \dfrac{N_2}{N_1}$ | $E_1$ = primary induced voltage; $N_1$ = primary turns | Step-up action of the ignition coil. |
| 5 | **Primary Induced Voltage at Break** | $E_1 = -L_p \dfrac{dI_p}{dt}$ | $L_p$ = primary inductance (**H**); $t$ = time (**s**) | Back-EMF generated when current is interrupted. |
| 6 | **Mutual Inductance (Coupled Coil)** | $E_2 = -M \dfrac{dI_p}{dt}$ | $M$ = mutual inductance (**H**); $M = k\sqrt{L_1 L_2}$ | Secondary voltage via mutual coupling. |
| 7 | **Required Breakdown Voltage** | $E_{bd} = 5 \times 10^5 \cdot p \cdot d$ (approx.) | $p$ = cylinder pressure (**Pa**); $d$ = spark gap (**m**) | Voltage needed to ionize the spark gap. |
| 8 | **Resonant Frequency (with Condenser)** | $f = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L_p C}}$ | $C$ = capacitance (**F**); $L_p$ = primary inductance (**H**) | Natural oscillation frequency of primary circuit. |
| 9 | **Time Constant of Primary Collapse** | $\tau = R_p C$ | $R_p$ = resistance (**Ω**); $C$ = capacitance (**F**) | Time for current to fall to $36.8\%$ of initial value. |
| 10 | **Spark Duration (Approximate)** | $t_s = \pi\sqrt{L_p C}$ | $L_p, C$ as above | Approximate duration of the high-voltage spark. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mathematical Formatting Safeguard:** The vertical pipe symbol `\vert` is used below to denote absolute value or magnitude within tables, in compliance with markdown parser constraints.
> Example: $E_2 \approx \vert \dfrac{N_2}{N_1} \vert \cdot V_{induced}$ — this notation correctly avoids breaking the markdown table syntax.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The battery ignition system, despite being largely replaced by **Electronic Control Units (ECUs)** and **distributor-less ignition systems (DIS)** in modern vehicles, remains **critically relevant** in engineering education for the following reasons:

1. **Pedagogical Foundation:** It provides the **fundamental understanding** of how energy conversion from low-voltage DC to high-voltage AC spark is achieved. Modern systems like **Capacitor Discharge Ignition (CDI)** and **Distributor-Less Ignition (DLI)** are evolved derivatives of the same principles.
2. **Vintage & Small Engine Applications:** The system is still used in **older cars (pre-1990s)**, **motorcycles**, **vintage tractors**, **lawn mowers**, and **small marine outboard engines**.
3. **Diagnostic Relevance:** A mechanic who understands the battery ignition system can troubleshoot **modern electronically controlled engines** because the **ignition coil**, **spark plug**, and **HT cables** still operate on identical electromagnetic principles.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Expression for Secondary Induced Voltage

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Most Repeated Question:** *Derive the expression for the high voltage generated in the secondary winding of an ignition coil during the break of the primary circuit.* This is a **mandatory 7-mark sub-question** in most KTU ESE papers.

### Given Data and Assumptions

- Primary winding has $N_1$ turns with self-inductance $L_1$ (in Henry, H).
- Secondary winding has $N_2$ turns with self-inductance $L_2$.
- Mutual inductance between the windings is $M$ (in Henry, H).
- Coefficient of coupling is $k$, where $M = k \sqrt{L_1 L_2}$.
- Primary current before the break is $I_p$.
- The contact breaker opens in a very small time interval $\Delta t$ (in seconds, s).

### Step 1: Apply Faraday's Law to the Primary Winding

The instant the contact points open, the primary current $I_p$ falls to zero. The rate of change of current is therefore very high. By Faraday's Law, the back-EMF induced in the primary winding is:

$$e_1 = -L_1 \frac{di_1}{dt}$$

where $\dfrac{di_1}{dt}$ is the rate of change of primary current. Since the current drops from $I_p$ to $0$ in time $\Delta t$:

$$\frac{di_1}{dt} \approx \frac{I_p}{\Delta t}$$

Therefore:

$$e_1 = -L_1 \cdot \frac{I_p}{\Delta t}$$

**Logic:** The negative sign indicates that the induced EMF opposes the change in current (per Lenz's Law). However, for magnitude analysis, we often drop the negative sign.

### Step 2: Apply Faraday's Law to the Secondary Winding

The change in primary current also induces an EMF in the secondary winding through **mutual inductance** $M$:

$$e_2 = -M \frac{di_1}{dt}$$

**Logic:** The two coils share the same iron core, so any change in the primary flux links the secondary winding. The mutual inductance $M$ quantifies this coupling.

### Step 3: Substitute the Rate of Change of Current

Substituting $\dfrac{di_1}{dt} = \dfrac{I_p}{\Delta t}$ into the equation for $e_2$:

$$e_2 = -M \cdot \frac{I_p}{\Delta t}$$

**Logic:** This is the **secondary voltage in terms of mutual inductance**. Notice that $e_2$ is inversely proportional to $\Delta t$, confirming that a **faster break** (smaller $\Delta t$) produces a **higher spark voltage**.

### Step 4: Express Secondary Voltage in Terms of Primary Induced Voltage

From Step 1, the magnitude of $e_1$ is $L_1 \cdot \dfrac{I_p}{\Delta t}$. We can write:

$$\frac{e_2}{e_1} = \frac{M \cdot (I_p / \Delta t)}{L_1 \cdot (I_p / \Delta t)} = \frac{M}{L_1}$$

For an **ideal transformer** (which an ignition coil approximates), the turns ratio and inductance ratio are related by:

$$\frac{N_2}{N_1} = \sqrt{\frac{L_2}{L_1}}$$

and for a tightly coupled coil ($k \approx 1$):

$$M = \sqrt{L_1 L_2}$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{e_2}{e_1} = \frac{\sqrt{L_1 L_2}}{L_1} = \sqrt{\frac{L_2}{L_1}} = \frac{N_2}{N_1}$$

### Step 5: Final Expression for Secondary Voltage

Multiplying both sides of the turns-ratio equation by $e_1$:

$$\boxed{e_2 = e_1 \cdot \frac{N_2}{N_1} = \frac{N_2}{N_1} \cdot L_1 \cdot \frac{I_p}{\Delta t}}$$

**Final Insight:** The secondary voltage is proportional to:
- The **turns ratio** ($N_2 / N_1 \approx 100$).
- The **primary inductance** $L_1$.
- The **primary current** $I_p$ just before the break.
- The **inverse of the break time** $\Delta t$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Role of the Condenser (Capacitor) in Spark Enhancement

### Given

- Condenser capacitance = $C$ (in Farads, F).
- Primary winding inductance = $L_1$ (in Henry, H).
- Primary winding resistance = $R_p$ (in Ohms, Ω).
- Primary current before break = $I_p$ (in Amperes, A).

### Step 1: State the Energy Balance Equation

The energy stored in the primary magnetic field before the break must equal the sum of (a) the energy delivered as spark, and (b) the energy dissipated or stored in the condenser (ignoring losses):

$$\frac{1}{2} L_1 I_p^2 = \frac{1}{2} C V_c^2 + \text{Spark Energy}$$

**Logic:** Energy conservation requires that the total magnetic energy converts into electrical and spark energies.

### Step 2: Determine the Peak Voltage Across the Condenser

The primary inductance $L_1$ and the condenser $C$ form a **series LC oscillating circuit** the moment the contact points open. In an ideal lossless LC circuit, the peak voltage across the capacitor is:

$$V_c = I_p \sqrt{\frac{L_1}{C}}$$

This can be derived by equating the peak magnetic energy ($\frac{1}{2}L_1 I_p^2$) to the peak electrical energy ($\frac{1}{2}C V_c^2$):

$$\frac{1}{2} L_1 I_p^2 = \frac{1}{2} C V_c^2$$

$$V_c^2 = \frac{L_1 I_p^2}{C}$$

$$V_c = I_p \sqrt{\frac{L_1}{C}}$$

**Logic:** The condenser first charges up to a peak voltage, then discharges back through the primary, causing a **high-frequency oscillation**. This oscillation rapidly reverses the current direction, making the effective rate of flux collapse even faster, which **boosts the secondary voltage**.

### Step 3: Effect on Spark Voltage

Since the secondary voltage $e_2$ is proportional to the rate of change of primary current, and the LC oscillation makes $\dfrac{di_1}{dt}$ much higher than it would be without the condenser, the **secondary voltage is amplified**. The presence of the condenser typically boosts the secondary voltage by a factor of **1.5 to 2.0**.

### Step 4: Spark Duration

The condenser, in conjunction with the primary inductance, also determines the **spark duration**. For an LC circuit, the period of one oscillation is:

$$T = 2\pi \sqrt{L_1 C}$$

The spark typically lasts for **half an oscillation cycle** (since after half a cycle, the current reverses and the spark may extinguish):

$$t_{spark} = \pi \sqrt{L_1 C}$$

**Numerical Example:** If $L_1 = 0.01\text{ H}$ and $C = 0.25\text{ }\mu\text{F} = 2.5 \times 10^{-7}\text{ F}$:

$$t_{spark} = \pi \sqrt{0.01 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-7}} = \pi \sqrt{2.5 \times 10^{-9}} \approx 1.57 \times 10^{-4}\text{ s} = 0.157\text{ ms}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Voltage Required to Jump the Spark Plug Gap

### Paschen's Law Approximation

For short gaps and atmospheric conditions, the breakdown voltage of a spark plug gap is approximately:

$$V_{bd} = 5 \times 10^5 \cdot p \cdot d$$

where:
- $V_{bd}$ = breakdown voltage (in Volts, V)
- $p$ = pressure inside the cylinder at the time of ignition (in Pascals, Pa, but often expressed in $\text{kg/cm}^2$ for engineering)
- $d$ = spark plug gap (in meters, m)

### Step-by-Step Reasoning

1. As the piston compresses the air-fuel mixture, the cylinder pressure rises to approximately **10 bar to 14 bar** ($10^6$ Pa to $1.4 \times 10^6$ Pa).
2. The spark plug gap is typically set to **0.7 mm** ($7 \times 10^{-4}$ m).
3. Substituting these values:

$$V_{bd} \approx 5 \times 10^5 \times 1.2 \times 10^6 \times 7 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$V_{bd} \approx 4.2 \times 10^8 \text{ V (in SI units)}$$

This is a very high theoretical value. In practice, the mixture of air and fuel is **partially ionized** and the gap is small, so the actual required voltage is around **8,000 V to 15,000 V**. The system is designed to produce **20,000 V to 30,000 V** to provide a safety margin.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Logic:** KTU may ask you to explain **why the secondary voltage must be higher than the breakdown voltage** of the gap. The answer is to provide a **margin of safety** and to ensure reliable spark initiation under varying conditions (cold engine, lean mixture, high altitude, etc.).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Battery Ignition System

```mermaid
graph TD
    Battery[LEAD ACID BATTERY 12V] --> Switch[IGNITION SWITCH]
    Switch --> Ballast[BALLAST RESISTOR OPTIONAL]
    Ballast --> Primary[PRIMARY WINDING N1 LOW TURNS]
    Primary --> Points[CONTACT BREAKER POINTS]
    Points --> Ground1[GROUND CHASSIS]
    
    Condenser[CONDENSER CAPACITOR C] -.-> Points
    
    PrimaryCore[SHARED IRON CORE] === Secondary
    Secondary[SECONDARY WINDING N2 HIGH TURNS]
    Secondary --> Rotor[DISTRIBUTOR ROTOR]
    
    Rotor --> Cap[DISTRIBUTOR CAP]
    Cap --> HT1[HT CABLE TO CYL 1]
    Cap --> HT2[HT CABLE TO CYL 2]
    Cap --> HT3[HT CABLE TO CYL 3]
    Cap --> HT4[HT CABLE TO CYL 4]
    
    HT1 --> SP1[SPARK PLUG CYLINDER 1]
    HT2 --> SP2[SPARK PLUG CYLINDER 2]
    HT3 --> SP3[SPARK PLUG CYLINDER 3]
    HT4 --> SP4[SPARK PLUG CYLINDER 4]
    
    Cam[DISTRIBUTOR CAM HALF ENGINE SPEED] --> Points

    style Secondary fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px

```
