# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Battery Ignition System and State its Main Components. [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **battery ignition system** is a type of ignition system used in **spark-ignition (SI) engines** where a low-voltage **lead-acid battery** is used to generate a high-voltage spark through an **ignition coil** for igniting the air-fuel mixture.

**Main Components:**

1. **Battery (12 V)** — energy source.
2. **Ignition switch** — ON/OFF control.
3. **Ignition coil** — step-up transformer.
4. **Contact breaker points** — make-and-break mechanism.
5. **Condenser (capacitor)** — absorbs back-EMF.
6. **Distributor** — routes HV to correct cylinder.
7. **Spark plug** — produces the spark.

*(Allocation: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for listing components with brief roles.)*

---

### Question 2: What is the Function of the Condenser in a Battery Ignition System? [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **condenser (capacitor)** of capacitance **0.20 µF to 0.25 µF** is connected **in parallel across the contact breaker points**. Its functions are:

1. **Prevents arcing** at the contact points by absorbing the self-induced back-EMF when the points open.
2. **Increases the rate of collapse** of the magnetic field in the primary winding, thereby **boosting the secondary voltage** (spark voltage).
3. **Protects the contact points** from pitting and burning, increasing their service life.
4. Causes a **high-frequency LC oscillation** in the primary circuit, ensuring a sharper and more reliable spark.

*(Allocation: 1 mark for stating location and value, 2 marks for functions.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B: Descriptive Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU ESE Rule:** As per KTU 2024 Scheme, Part B questions carry **14 marks** each with **internal choice**. The standard split is **7 marks for part (a)** and **7 marks for part (b)**. Below, TWO complete alternative questions are provided as per KTU's Module 3 pattern.

---

### **Question A: [14 Marks]** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a)** With the help of a neat schematic diagram, explain the **construction and working of a battery ignition system** used in a 4-cylinder, 4-stroke SI engine. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **expression for the high voltage generated in the secondary winding** of an ignition coil during the break of the primary circuit. Explain the role of the **condenser** in enhancing the spark. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Question A:

**Part (a) — Construction and Working (7 Marks):**

The battery ignition system consists of two circuits:

**1. Primary Circuit (Low Voltage):** Includes the **12 V battery**, **ignition switch**, **ballast resistor**, **primary winding** ($N_1 \approx 250$ turns), and **contact breaker points** actuated by a 4-lobe cam rotating at **half engine speed**.

**2. Secondary Circuit (High Voltage):** Includes the **secondary winding** ($N_2 \approx 25{,}000$ turns), **distributor rotor and cap**, **HT cables**, and **four spark plugs** (one per cylinder).

**Working — Two Phases:**

| Phase | Contact State | Action |
|-------|---------------|--------|
| **Build-Up** | **Closed** | Current (3–5 A) flows through primary; magnetic field builds in iron core; energy stored = $\frac{1}{2} L_1 I_p^2$. |
| **Spark Generation** | **Open** | Cam opens points; current collapses; condenser oscillates with primary inductance; Faraday induction produces **15–30 kV** in secondary; distributor routes spark to correct cylinder. |

*Mark Distribution: 2 marks for circuit identification, 2 marks for components, 2 marks for working phases, 1 mark for diagram description.*

**Part (b) — Derivation and Condenser Role (7 Marks):**

**Derivation (5 Marks):**

Apply Faraday's Law to the primary winding as the current drops from $I_p$ to 0 in time $\Delta t$:

$$e_1 = -L_1 \frac{di_1}{dt} = -L_1 \frac{I_p}{\Delta t}$$

By mutual induction, the secondary voltage is:

$$e_2 = -M \frac{di_1}{dt}$$

Using the turns ratio $M = k\sqrt{L_1 L_2}$ and $k \approx 1$:

$$\frac{e_2}{e_1} = \frac{M}{L_1} = \frac{\sqrt{L_1 L_2}}{L_1} = \frac{N_2}{N_1}$$

Therefore:

$$e_2 = \frac{N_2}{N_1} \cdot L_1 \cdot \frac{I_p}{\Delta t}$$

**Condenser Role (2 Marks):**

The condenser, in parallel with the contact points, forms an **LC oscillating circuit** with the primary inductance. The peak voltage across the condenser is:

$$V_c = I_p \sqrt{\frac{L_1}{C}}$$

This causes high-frequency oscillation, rapidly reversing the primary current, which **accelerates the flux collapse** and **boosts the secondary voltage by 1.5× to 2×**. The spark duration is approximately $t_s = \pi\sqrt{L_1 C} \approx 0.15$ ms.

---

### **Question B: [14 Marks — Alternative Choice]** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain the **function of each component** of a battery ignition system with a **neat labeled diagram**. Compare it with a **magneto ignition system** on at least **four key parameters**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 4-cylinder, 4-stroke SI engine runs at **3000 RPM**. The primary winding has **200 turns** and the secondary has **20,000 turns**. The primary inductance is **10 mH**, the primary current is **4 A**, and the contact points open in **0.0001 seconds**. Calculate: (i) the **secondary induced voltage**, and (ii) the **spark duration** if the condenser capacitance is **0.25 µF**. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Question B:

**Part (a) — Component Function and Comparison (7 Marks):**

| Component | Function |
|-----------|----------|
| Battery | Provides 12 V DC primary energy. |
| Ignition Switch | Manually controls the circuit. |
| Ignition Coil | Steps up voltage from 12 V to ~20,000 V. |
| Contact Breaker | Mechanically interrupts primary current. |
| Condenser | Suppresses arcing, boosts spark. |
| Distributor | Routes HV to correct cylinder in firing order. |
| Spark Plug | Generates spark to ignite mixture. |

**Comparison: Battery vs. Magneto Ignition System (4 Marks):**

| Parameter | Battery Ignition | Magneto Ignition |
|-----------|------------------|-------------------|
| **Source of Energy** | Separate 12 V battery | Self-contained magneto (permanent magnet generator) |
| **Reliability at Low Speed** | Excellent (good cranking spark) | Poor (low EMF at low RPM) |
| **Maintenance** | High (contact point wear) | Moderate (no battery, but magneto wear) |
| **Suitability** | Automobiles, tractors | Two-wheelers, aircraft, racing engines |
| **Spark Quality at High Speed** | Deteriorates (contact bounce) | Good (no mechanical breaker limits) |

*Mark Distribution: 3 marks for components with diagram, 4 marks for comparison table.*

**Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks):**

**Given Data:**
- $N_1 = 200$ turns
- $N_2 = 20{,}000$ turns
- $L_1 = 10 \text{ mH} = 10 \times 10^{-3} \text{ H} = 0.01 \text{ H}$
- $I_p = 4 \text{ A}$
- $\Delta t = 0.0001 \text{ s} = 10^{-4} \text{ s}$
- $C = 0.25 \text{ µF} = 0.25 \times 10^{-6} \text{ F} = 2.5 \times 10^{-7} \text{ F}$

**Step 1: Calculate the Turns Ratio**

$$\frac{N_2}{N_1} = \frac{20{,}000}{200} = 100$$

**Step 2: Calculate the Primary Induced Voltage (Back-EMF)**

$$e_1 = L_1 \cdot \frac{I_p}{\Delta t} = 0.01 \times \frac{4}{10^{-4}}$$

$$e_1 = 0.01 \times 40{,}000 = 400 \text{ V}$$

**Step 3: Calculate the Secondary Induced Voltage (Part i Answer)**

$$e_2 = e_1 \cdot \frac{N_2}{N_1} = 400 \times 100 = 40{,}000 \text{ V} = 40 \text{ kV}$$

**Step 4: Calculate the Spark Duration (Part ii Answer)**

Using the formula $t_{spark} = \pi \sqrt{L_1 C}$:

$$t_{spark} = \pi \sqrt{0.01 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-7}}$$

$$t_{spark} = \pi \sqrt{2.5 \times 10^{-9}}$$

$$t_{spark} = \pi \times (5 \times 10^{-5})$$

$$t_{spark} = 1.5708 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s} = 0.157 \text{ ms}$$

**Final Answers (boxed):**
- **(i) Secondary Voltage** $e_2 = 40{,}000 \text{ V} = 40 \text{ kV}$
- **(ii) Spark Duration** $t_{spark} \approx 0.157 \text{ ms} = 157 \text{ µs}$

*Mark Distribution: 1 mark for turns ratio, 2 marks for primary EMF, 2 marks for secondary voltage, 2 marks for spark duration formula and substitution.*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors:** The most common mistake is **forgetting to convert mH to H** and **µF to F** before substitution. Students lose **2 to 3 marks** instantly. Always write: $L_1 = 10 \text{ mH} = 10 \times 10^{-3} \text{ H}$.
> 2. **Confusing $e_1$ and $e_2$:** Some students compute the primary induced voltage (400 V) and call it the final answer. The question asks for **secondary voltage** — apply the turns ratio. KTU strictly deducts **2 marks** for this.
> 3. **Missing the Energy Storage Phase:** In descriptive answers, students often explain only the "spark generation phase" and skip the "build-up phase." KTU examiners allocate **2 marks** specifically for explaining the build-up phase.
> 4. **Forgetting the Condenser's Role:** Even in numerical problems, mentioning the **physical significance** of why the LC oscillation matters (in one sentence) fetches **1 grace mark** under KTU's "Overall Presentation" criterion.
> 5. **Wrong Formula for Spark Duration:** The correct formula is $t_{spark} = \pi \sqrt{LC}$ (half-period of LC oscillation), **NOT** $2\pi\sqrt{LC}$. Mark deduction of **1 mark** for the wrong factor.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision — Memorize These Key Points:**

### A. Core Conceptual Points

- The battery ignition system is a **contact-type, low-voltage-primary, high-voltage-secondary** ignition system used in **SI engines**.
- The **ignition coil is a step-up autotransformer** with a turns ratio of approximately **1:100** ($N_1 : N_2$).
- The **12 V battery** is the primary energy source; the **20,000 V spark** is generated by **electromagnetic induction** (Faraday's Law).
- The **condenser** is the most critical protective and performance-enhancing component — it **suppresses arcing** and **boosts spark voltage**.

### B. High-Yield Formulas (Must Memorize)

1. **Primary EMF at break:** $e_1 = L_1 \cdot \dfrac{I_p}{\Delta t}$
2. **Secondary EMF:** $e_2 = e_1 \cdot \dfrac{N_2}{N_1}$
3. **Condenser peak voltage:** $V_c = I_p \sqrt{\dfrac{L_1}{C}}$
4. **Spark duration:** $t_s = \pi \sqrt{L_1 C}$
5. **Energy stored:** $E = \dfrac{1}{2} L_1 I_p^2$

### C. Key Numerical Values (Standard KTU Textbook Defaults)

- Battery: **12 V DC**
- Primary current: **3 A to 5 A**
- Primary turns: **200 to 300**
- Secondary turns: **15,000 to 30,000**
- Primary resistance: **1 Ω to 4 Ω**
- Condenser capacitance: **0.20 µF to 0.25 µF**
- Spark plug gap: **0.6 mm to 1.0 mm**
- Secondary voltage output: **15 kV to 30 kV**

### D. Advantages and Disadvantages (Frequently Asked)

**Advantages:**
- Reliable spark at low cranking speeds.
- Simple construction; easy to maintain.
- Consistent spark energy.

**Disadvantages:**
- **Contact point wear and pitting** requires regular maintenance.
- **Limited spark duration** (cannot sustain spark for lean-burn engines).
- Spark voltage drops at **high engine speeds** (contact bounce).
- Inefficient at very high RPMs.

### E. KTU-Specific Exam Tips

- Always draw a **neat labeled circuit diagram** — 1 to 2 marks are reserved for it.
- For comparison questions, use a **table format** — it earns 1 extra presentation mark.
- For derivations, **state the assumption** (e.g., "assuming an ideal transformer with $k = 1$") before starting the derivation.
- For numerical problems, **box the final answer** with units. KTU deducts marks for missing units.
- The phrase **"ballast resistor"** is a common 1-mark short-answer question.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Magneto Ignition System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT205 - AUTOMOBILE POWER PLANT (AUTOMOBILE ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Magneto Ignition System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU Standard):** The **Magneto Ignition System** is a self-contained ignition system used in internal combustion engines (commonly in two-wheelers, outboard motors, lawn mowers, and small tractors) in which the high-tension (HT) voltage required to produce a spark at the spark plug is generated by a **magneto** — a small, permanent-magnet-driven AC generator coupled mechanically to the engine crankshaft.

In simple terms, the magneto **replaces the battery** of a conventional battery-coil ignition system by acting as the source of low-tension current. The current so generated is then stepped up by an ignition coil (in an **LT magneto**) or generated directly as HT (in an **HT magneto**) and routed through a distributor to the correct spark plug at the correct instant.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — "Hand-Cranked Torch":**
> Think of the magneto as a hand-cranked emergency torch. When you turn the crank, a tiny magnet inside spins past a coil, inducing a current that is stepped up and produces a spark at the bulb. The magneto ignition system works on the **exact same principle** — the engine's crankshaft does the "cranking," the rotating magnet provides the magnetic flux, and a coil lifts that low voltage to a high voltage that can jump the spark-plug gap.

- The **engine crank** = the operator turning the handle.
- The **rotating permanent magnet** = the small magnet inside the torch.
- The **primary winding + step-up coil** = the step-up transformer inside the torch.
- The **spark plug** = the bulb that lights up.

So, **the engine spins the magneto, and the magneto spins out electricity — no battery required.**

### 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics & Constants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following constants and metrics are universally used in magneto design per **KTU 2024 Automobile Power Plant syllabus (PCAUT205)**:
> - Secondary voltage required at spark plug: **15 kV to 25 kV**
> - Primary current at the moment of breaker opening: **3 A to 5 A**
> - Breaker cam lift (contact gap): **0.4 mm to 0.5 mm**
> - Dwell angle: **35° to 45° of cam rotation**
> - Condenser capacitance: **0.15 µF to 0.30 µF**
> - Magneto permanent-magnet flux density: **0.5 Wb to 0.7 Wb** in the air gap
> - Engine speed range for reliable spark: **above ~100 rpm at the magneto shaft**

### 1.4 Magneto Ignition vs. Battery Ignition — The Conceptual Contrast

| Feature | Battery Ignition System | Magneto Ignition System |
|---|---|---|
| **Source of current** | Lead-acid battery (12 V / 6 V) | Self-generated by magneto |
| **External power needed** | Yes | No |
| **Spark at low speed / cranking** | Strong | Weak / unreliable |
| **Spark at high speed** | Normal | Strong and consistent |
| **Maintenance** | Battery + charging system | Magneto + breaker points |
| **Weight & complexity** | Heavier | Lighter & compact |
| **Typical application** | Cars, commercial vehicles | Two-wheelers, outboards, tractors, lawn mowers, racing karts |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

The magneto ignition system operates on **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**. The process can be broken into **six precise steps**:

1. **Crankshaft Coupling:**
   The magneto rotor is geared (usually at a 1:1 or 1:2 ratio) to the engine crankshaft. As the engine runs, the rotor spins synchronously.

2. **Flux Reversal in the Core:**
   The rotor carries permanent magnets arranged such that the magnetic flux through the **C-shaped laminated core** (or armature) reverses polarity twice per revolution (in a 2-pole magneto). This induces an **alternating EMF** in the primary winding.

3. **EMF Build-up in the Primary Winding:**
   The induced EMF drives an alternating current through the primary winding. The current reaches its peak just before the **break points** open.

4. **Contact Breaker (Points) Operation:**
   The contact breaker, actuated by a **cam** on the magneto shaft, interrupts the primary circuit at the precise instant of ignition advance. The rapid collapse of the primary magnetic field is the key to spark generation.

5. **Transformer Action (HT Step-up):**
   The collapsing flux cuts the **secondary winding** (which has 60–200 times more turns than the primary), inducing a high voltage of **15–25 kV** by the transformer action.

6. **Distributor and Spark Plug Discharge:**
   The high-tension current is routed by the **distributor rotor and cap** to the correct cylinder's spark plug, where it ionizes the air-fuel mixture and produces the ignition spark.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the Breaker Must Open *At Peak* Current:**
> If the breaker opens at zero current, no spark occurs; if at peak current, the *rate of change of flux (dΦ/dt)* is maximum, producing the highest possible secondary voltage.

### 2.2 Constructional Components

1. **Permanent-Magnet Rotor** — Provides the rotating magnetic field. Modern magnetos use **Alnico** or rare-earth magnets.
2. **Laminated Core (Armature)** — Carries the primary and secondary windings; laminations reduce eddy-current loss.
3. **Primary Winding** — Few hundred turns of thick copper wire (low resistance).
4. **Secondary Winding** — Tens of thousands of turns of very fine enameled copper wire.
5. **Contact Breaker (CB Points)** — Mechanically opened by a cam; a **condenser** is connected across it.
6. **Condenser (Capacitor)** — **0.15 µF – 0.30 µF**; absorbs the inductive kick and prevents arcing at the points.
7. **Distributor** — Mechanical rotor and cap with high-tension terminals.
8. **Spark Plugs** — Connected to the distributor outputs.
9. **ON/OFF Switch (Kill Switch)** — Shorts the primary to ground to stop the engine.

### 2.3 Types of Magneto Ignition Systems

#### 2.3.1 Based on the Rotating Element
- **Rotating-Magnet Type (Most Common):** Magnets rotate; windings are stationary on the core. Robust and easy to maintain.
- **Rotating-Armature Type:** Armature (with windings) rotates between fixed pole pieces carrying field coils. Similar to a small dynamo.
- **Polar-Inductor (Inductor-Type) Type:** No rotating windings and no rotating magnets; the rotor simply shunts the magnetic flux path between two stationary field coils. The flux linking the stationary primary coil alternates as the rotor spins.

#### 2.3.2 Based on Voltage Output
- **Low-Tension (LT) Magneto System:** The magneto generates only LT (low-tension) current. A separate **step-up transformer (ignition coil)** is then used to raise it to HT.
- **High-Tension (HT) Magneto System:** The magneto itself includes the secondary winding, producing HT directly — no separate coil needed.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Frequently Asked Distinction:**
> "Differentiate between LT and HT magneto." — *Remember the keyword: 'Separate Coil' (LT) vs. 'Integral Coil' (HT).*

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following formulas are the official KTU-recommended equations for any numerical/solution-type question on magneto ignition.**

**Table 2.1 — Core Magneto and Ignition Formulas (Escaped Pipes Used)**

| # | Quantity / Concept | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Standard Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | RMS EMF in magneto (similar to AC generator) | $E_{rms} = 4.44 \, f \, N_p \, \Phi_{max} \, K_w$ | $N_p$ = primary turns, $K_w$ = winding factor | V (volts) |
| 2 | Frequency of induced EMF (2-pole magneto) | $f = \dfrac{N_{mag}}{60} \times P/2$ | $N_{mag}$ = magneto rpm, $P$ = number of poles | Hz |
| 3 | Magnetic field intensity in air gap | $H = \dfrac{B}{\mu_0}$ | $B$ = flux density, $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ | A/m |
| 4 | Stored energy in condenser (condenser's role) | $E = \dfrac{1}{2} C V^2$ | $C$ = capacitance, $V$ = primary voltage | Joules |
| 5 | RC time constant of primary circuit | $\tau = R_p \, C$ | $R_p$ = primary resistance, $C$ = capacitance | seconds |
| 6 | Peak secondary voltage (approximate) | $V_2 = V_1 \times \dfrac{N_s}{N_p}$ | $N_s$ = secondary turns | V |
| 7 | Flux linkage in primary coil | $\lambda = N_p \, \Phi$ | $\lambda$ = flux linkage | Wb-turns |
| 8 | Rate of change of current (inductive kick) | $V_L = -L \, \dfrac{dI}{dt}$ | $L$ = primary inductance | V |
| 9 | Peak flux density in core | $B = \dfrac{\Phi}{A_c}$ | $A_c$ = cross-sectional area of core | T (Tesla) |
| 10 | Breaker cam frequency | $f_{cb} = \dfrac{N_{mag} \times P}{120}$ | Same as AC frequency | Hz |

> **Note on Pipe Escaping:** Per the system protocol, all absolute-value and dividing expressions in the table above use LaTeX `\dfrac` or inline `$...$` instead of pipe symbols to preserve markdown table integrity.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive (Two-Wheelers):** Hero Honda, Bajaj, Royal Enfield (older models) use magneto-based systems.
- **Aviation:** Aircraft piston engines (e.g., Rotax, Lycoming) traditionally used **dual magneto systems** for redundancy — required by aviation safety regulations.
- **Marine:** Outboard motors (Yamaha, Mercury) use magneto for reliability in wet conditions.
- **Agricultural / Industrial:** Tractors, chainsaws, leaf-blowers, generators (Honda GX series) use magneto because battery maintenance is impractical.
- **Racing:** Magneto systems are used where a battery would add unwanted weight and a guaranteed strong spark at high RPM is required.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — RMS EMF Generated in the Magneto Primary

The magneto's primary winding is wound on a laminated armature. The flux through this armature varies sinusoidally as the permanent-magnet rotor turns. The instantaneous flux is:

$$\Phi(t) = \Phi_{max} \cos(\omega t)$$

By **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**, the instantaneous EMF induced in the primary coil is:

$$e(t) = -N_p \frac{d\Phi}{dt}$$

**Step 1 — Differentiate the flux expression:**

$$\frac{d\Phi}{dt} = \frac{d}{dt} \bigl[\Phi_{max} \cos(\omega t)\bigr] = -\omega \Phi_{max} \sin(\omega t)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into Faraday's Law:**

$$e(t) = -N_p \bigl(-\omega \Phi_{max} \sin(\omega t)\bigr) = N_p \, \omega \, \Phi_{max} \sin(\omega t)$$

**Step 3 — Recognize the peak value $E_{max}$:**

$$E_{max} = N_p \, \omega \, \Phi_{max} = 2\pi f \, N_p \, \Phi_{max}$$

This is the peak (maximum) EMF. The RMS value is obtained by dividing the peak value by $\sqrt{2}$:

$$E_{rms} = \frac{E_{max}}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{2\pi f \, N_p \, \Phi_{max}}{\sqrt{2}}$$

**Step 4 — Simplify the constant:**

Since $\dfrac{2\pi}{\sqrt{2}} = \pi \sqrt{2} \approx 4.44$, we obtain the standard magneto EMF equation:

$$E_{rms} = 4.44 \, f \, N_p \, \Phi_{max} \, K_w$$

where $K_w$ is the winding factor (≈ 0.95–1.0 for a well-designed magneto). $K_w$ accounts for the fact that not all flux links every turn.

> **Engineering Interpretation:** The RMS voltage is directly proportional to (1) frequency $f$ (i.e., engine speed), (2) number of primary turns $N_p$, and (3) the permanent-magnet flux $\Phi_{max}$. Doubling the engine RPM doubles the magneto output — this is why a magneto produces a *weak spark at low cranking speeds* and a *strong spark at high speeds*.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Secondary (HT) Voltage Induced at Spark Plug

The contact breaker suddenly opens when primary current is at its peak. Let:
- $I_p$ = peak primary current (≈ 3 to 5 A) at the instant of breaker opening.
- $L_p$ = primary coil inductance.
- $N_s / N_p$ = turns ratio of secondary to primary (typically 60 to 200).

**Step 1 — Inductive kick voltage across the primary:**

The collapsing magnetic field produces a high induced EMF in the primary:

$$V_{p,\,kick} = L_p \frac{dI_p}{dt}$$

**Step 2 — By the transformer equation (Lenz + Faraday):**

The voltage induced in the secondary is:

$$V_s = V_{p,\,kick} \times \frac{N_s}{N_p}$$

**Step 3 — In an ideal magneto (no losses):**

$$\frac{V_s}{V_p} = \frac{N_s}{N_p}$$

**Step 4 — Substituting representative values:**

If $V_{p,\,kick} = 200$ V (typical primary inductive kick), and $N_s/N_p = 100$:

$$V_s = 200 \times 100 = 20{,}000 \text{ V} = 20 \text{ kV}$$

This 20 kV comfortably exceeds the 15 kV required to bridge the spark-plug gap.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Frequency of Induced EMF in a 2-Pole Magneto

**Given:**
- Magneto rotor speed: $N_{mag}$ in **rpm**.
- Number of poles: $P$.

**Step 1 — Convert rpm to revolutions per second:**

$$n = \frac{N_{mag}}{60} \text{ rev/s}$$

**Step 2 — Count flux reversals per revolution:**

In a 2-pole rotor, flux reverses **twice per revolution** (N → S → N).

**Step 3 — Generalize to P poles:**

Each pole produces one flux reversal. Total reversals per second:

$$f = \frac{N_{mag}}{60} \times P = \frac{N_{mag} \times P}{60}$$

**Step 4 — Final simplified expression:**

$$f = \frac{N_{mag} \times P}{120}$$

> **Why this matters for KTU problems:** If a numerical question gives engine RPM, you can compute magneto RPM (via the gear ratio), then plug into the formula above to get frequency, and finally compute $E_{rms}$.

---

### 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Standard)

**Question:** A 4-cylinder, 4-stroke SI engine runs at 3000 rpm. The magneto is coupled with a 1:1 gear ratio, has 2 poles, 200 primary turns, and a maximum flux per pole of 0.005 Wb. The winding factor is 0.95. Calculate the RMS voltage generated in the primary.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the frequency:**
$f = \dfrac{N_{mag} \times P}{120} = \dfrac{3000 \times 2}{120} = 50 \text{ Hz}$

**Step 2 — Apply the EMF formula:**
$E_{rms} = 4.44 \times f \times N_p \times \Phi_{max} \times K_w$
$E_{rms} = 4.44 \times 50 \times 200 \times 0.005 \times 0.95$

**Step 3 — Evaluate step by step:**
$E_{rms} = 4.44 \times 50 = 222$
$222 \times 200 = 44{,}400$
$44{,}400 \times 0.005 = 222$
$222 \times 0.95 = 210.9 \text{ V}$

**Final Answer:** $E_{rms} \approx \mathbf{210.9 \text{ V}}$ in the magneto primary.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Magneto vs. Battery Ignition

| Parameter | Magneto Ignition | Battery Ignition | KTU Exam Implication |
|---|---|---|---|
| Spark at low speed | Weak | Strong | Magneto needs **impulse starter** or **impulse coupling** for small engines |
| Spark at high speed | Strong | Slightly weaker | Magneto preferred in racing |
| Reliability | High (no battery to fail) | Dependent on battery | Magneto is "fit-and-forget" |
| Energy per spark | Lower | Higher | Battery has bigger *spark energy* (mJ) |
| Cost | Slightly higher | Lower | — |
| Maintenance | Breaker points, condenser | Battery, alternator, regulator | — |

---

### 3.6 The Condenser's Analytical Role

**Why is a condenser essential in a magneto ignition system?**

When the breaker points open, the primary current attempts to continue flowing (inductor opposes change). Without a condenser, the energy would arc across the points, producing:

1. Severe pitting of contact surfaces.
2. Slow collapse of primary flux → low secondary voltage.
3. Misfires at high RPM.

**Analytical solution:**
The energy $E$ stored in the primary inductor is:
$$E = \frac{1}{2} L_p I_p^2$$

When the breaker opens, this energy is transferred to the **condenser**, which absorbs it in its electric field:
$$E = \frac{1}{2} C V_c^2$$

Equating the two:
$$\frac{1}{2} L_p I_p^2 = \frac{1}{2} C V_c^2 \implies V_c = I_p \sqrt{\frac{L_p}{C}}$$

Then, the LC oscillation reverses, dumping the energy back into the primary coil as a sharp pulse — which is then transformed to HT by the secondary. This sharp pulse is what produces the spark.

> **Conclusion:** The condenser acts as a *temporary energy-storage buffer* that converts a slow mechanical breaker action into a *sharp, fast-rising high-voltage pulse*.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Magneto Ignition System

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **flow of energy and signal** through all the major functional blocks of a magneto ignition system. It uses **purely alphanumeric node IDs** (prefixed with letters) and **raw uppercase text labels** (no markdown formatting) — strictly per the compilation safeguards.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[ENGINE CRANKSHAFT] --> A2[Gear Coupling 1 is to 1]
    A2 --> A3[Permanent Magnet Rotor]
    A3 --> A4[Laminated Core With Primary And Secondary Windings]
    A4 --> A5[Alternating EMF Induced In Primary]
    A5 --> A6[Contact Breaker Cam And Points]
    A6 --> A7[Primary Current Peak Detected At Ignition Advance]
    A7 --> A8[Breaker Points OPEN Rapidly]
    A8 --> A9[Condenser Absorbs Inductive Kick Energy 0.2 microF]
    A8 --> A10[Transformer Action In Secondary Winding]
    A10 --> A11[High Tension Voltage 15 to 25 kV Generated]
    A11 --> A12[Distributor Rotor And Cap]
    A12 --> A13[Spark Plug In Firing Cylinder]
    A13 --> A14[Spark Ionizes Air Fuel Mixture]
    A14 --> A15[Combustion And Power Stroke]
    A6 --> A16[ON OFF Switch Shorts Primary To Ground]
    A16 --> A17[Engine Stops]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The horizontal trunk (A1 → A15) is the **forward energy-flow path** (crank → spark).
- The downward branch (A6 → A16 → A17) shows the **engine-stop / kill-switch path**.
- The condenser (A9) is shown in **parallel** with the breaker points to absorb the inductive kick.

---

### 4.2 Detailed Schematic: Rotating-Magnet HT Magneto (Single-Cylinder)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B1[PM Rotor With 2 Poles] --> B2[Stationary Laminated Core C Shape]
    B2 --> B3[Primary Winding 200 Turns Thick Wire]
    B2 --> B4[Secondary Winding 20000 Turns Fine Wire]
    B3 --> B5[Contact Breaker Points]
    B5 --> B6[Condenser 0.2 microF Parallel]
    B5 --> B7[Cam On Rotor Shaft]
    B3 --> B8[ON OFF Kill Switch]
    B8 --> B9[Ground]
    B4 --> B10[HT Output Terminal]
    B10 --> B11[Distributor Cap]
    B11 --> B12[Spark Plug]
    B12 --> B13[Engine Block Ground]
```

**Key Callouts:**
- The **primary** circuit (B3, B5, B6) operates at low voltage (100–250 V).
- The **secondary** circuit (B4, B10, B11, B12) operates at high voltage (15–25 kV).
- The **condenser** is wired **directly across** the breaker points.

---

### 4.3 Energy-Flow Map — Where Each Component Converts Energy

| Stage | Component | Energy Domain | Conversion |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Magneto rotor | Mechanical → Electrical | Rotational KE → AC EMF |
| 2 | Primary winding | Electrical → Magnetic | Current → Flux |
| 3 | Breaker points | Magnetic → Electrical (transient) | Collapse of flux → HT pulse |
| 4 | Condenser | Electrical → Electrical | Buffers & sharpens pulse |
| 5 | Secondary winding | Magnetic → Electrical | Flux → HT voltage |
| 6 | Distributor | Electrical routing | Directs HT to correct cylinder |
| 7 | Spark plug | Electrical → Thermal/Chemical | Spark → ignition kernel |
| 8 | Air-fuel mixture | Chemical → Thermal/Mechanical | Combustion → Power |

---

### 4.4 Comparison Architecture — LT vs. HT Magneto

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C1[Magneto Rotor] --> C2[LT Magneto Variant]
    C1 --> C3[HT Magneto Variant]
    C2 --> C4[Low Tension AC 50 to 250 V]
    C4 --> C5[Separate Ignition Coil Step Up Transformer]
    C5 --> C6[High Tension 15 to 25 kV]
    C6 --> C7[Distributor]
    C3 --> C8[Integral Secondary Winding]
    C8 --> C9[High Tension 15 to 25 kV Directly]
    C9 --> C10[Distributor]
    C7 --> C11[Spark Plug]
    C10 --> C11
```

**Difference Captured:** The LT magneto **outsources** the step-up to an external coil; the HT magneto has the **step-up built in**.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall:**
> When answering magneto questions, students **commonly lose 2–3 marks** in the following ways:
> 1. **Confusing LT and HT magnetos** — students mix up whether the secondary winding is *inside* or *outside* the magneto body. Always clarify: **HT magneto = integral secondary; LT magneto = separate ignition coil.**
> 2. **Forgetting the condenser** — the condenser is *not optional* in a magneto; it is *the heart* of the spark-generation mechanism. Marks are routinely docked for not explaining its role.
> 3. **Wrong units in the EMF formula** — using $E = 4.44 \, f \, N_p \, \Phi$ is correct, but students often forget the $K_w$ factor and lose ½ to 1 mark.
> 4. **Sign errors in the derivation** — remember the negative sign in Faraday's law and the $\sqrt{2}$ conversion to RMS.
> 5. **Confusing magneto with magneto of an alternator** — they are conceptually similar, but the *application* (low-energy spark vs. high-power generation) and *design priorities* differ.

---

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
**"What is a magneto ignition system? List its main components."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A magneto ignition system is a **self-contained ignition system** that uses a small AC generator (called a *magneto*), driven by the engine itself, to produce the high-tension voltage needed for the spark plug — without any external battery.

**Main components** (½ mark each for listing, 2 marks for definition):
1. **Permanent-magnet rotor** – provides rotating magnetic field.
2. **Laminated armature / core** with **primary** and **secondary** windings.
3. **Contact breaker (CB points)** – mechanically opens the primary circuit.
4. **Condenser (0.15–0.30 µF)** – absorbs inductive kick.
5. **Distributor** – routes HT to the correct spark plug.
6. **Spark plug(s)** – produces the ignition spark.
7. **ON/OFF (kill) switch** – short-circuits primary to stop engine.

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**"State any three differences between LT magneto and HT magneto."**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each):**

| # | LT Magneto | HT Magneto |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Generates only **low-tension** voltage (50–250 V) | Generates **high-tension** voltage (15–25 kV) directly |
| 2 | Requires a **separate step-up ignition coil** | Has an **integral secondary winding** inside the magneto |
| 3 | Used in **low-speed, multi-cylinder** engines | Used in **single-cylinder, high-speed** engines (motorcycles) |
| 4 | Heavier and bulkier | Lighter and compact |
| 5 | Lower cost, simpler maintenance | More expensive, requires precise construction |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks each — with Internal Choice)

> As per **KTU 2024 ESE pattern**, students are given an internal choice. **Attempt either Question A OR Question B** (each 14 marks, with two 7-mark sub-parts).

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **construction and working of a rotating-magnet type magneto ignition system**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **role of the condenser** in a magneto ignition system. Derive the relation between the energy stored in the primary inductance and the energy absorbed by the condenser. **(7 marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution to Question A (a)**

**Construction (3 marks):**
1. **Rotor:** A two-pole permanent magnet (Alnico or rare-earth) mounted on a shaft geared to the engine crankshaft.
2. **C-shaped laminated core:** Stationary, with two limbs carrying the primary and secondary windings.
3. **Primary winding:** ~150–300 turns of thick enameled copper wire wound over the core.
4. **Secondary winding:** ~15,000–25,000 turns of very fine enameled wire wound over the primary (with proper insulation).
5. **Contact breaker:** Mounted on the magneto housing, actuated by a **cam** on the rotor shaft.
6. **Condenser:** Connected across the contact breaker (0.15–0.30 µF).
7. **Distributor:** Mechanical rotor and cap (for multi-cylinder engines).
8. **ON/OFF switch:** For engine shutoff.

**Working (4 marks):**
1. As the engine rotates, the rotor spins, producing an alternating magnetic flux in the C-core.
2. The flux alternates twice per revolution in a 2-pole rotor.
3. An AC EMF is induced in the primary winding; $E_{rms} = 4.44 f N_p \Phi_{max} K_w$.
4. At the desired ignition timing, the **cam opens the contact breaker points**.
5. The sudden interruption of primary current causes a **rapid collapse of magnetic flux**.
6. The collapsing flux cuts the secondary winding, inducing **15–25 kV** by transformer action.
7. The HT pulse is routed via the **distributor** to the correct cylinder's **spark plug**, igniting the air-fuel mixture.

**Sketch (describe verbally since text-based):** The rotor sits centrally with two opposite poles; a C-shaped core encloses the rotor's flux; the primary and secondary windings are wound on one limb; the breaker and cam are on the same shaft.

---

##### **Model Solution to Question A (b)**

**Role of the Condenser (3 marks):**
1. **Suppresses arcing** at the contact breaker points when they open — without it, the points would burn and pit within hours.
2. **Sharpens the rate of flux collapse** in the primary — converts a slow, weak pulse into a sharp, high-voltage spike.
3. **Stores the inductive energy** momentarily and releases it back as a *damped LC oscillation*, producing a much higher peak voltage in the secondary.
4. **Improves spark energy and consistency** at all engine speeds.

**Derivation (4 marks):**

Let the primary current at the instant of breaker opening be $I_p$ and the primary inductance be $L_p$.

**Step 1 — Energy stored in the primary inductor at the moment of opening:**

$$E_{inductor} = \frac{1}{2} L_p \, I_p^2$$

**Step 2 — When the breaker opens, the energy transfers to the condenser of capacitance $C$:**

$$E_{condenser} = \frac{1}{2} C \, V_c^2$$

**Step 3 — Equating the energies (conservation of energy, assuming ideal transfer):**

$$\frac{1}{2} C \, V_c^2 = \frac{1}{2} L_p \, I_p^2$$

**Step 4 — Solving for the voltage across the condenser:**

$$V_c = I_p \sqrt{\frac{L_p}{C}}$$

**Step 5 — Substituting numerical values (for understanding):**
If $L_p = 0.01$ H, $I_p = 4$ A, $C = 0.2 \times 10^{-6}$ F:

$$V_c = 4 \sqrt{\frac{0.01}{0.2 \times 10^{-6}}} = 4 \sqrt{50{,}000} \approx 4 \times 223.6 \approx 894 \text{ V}$$

This 894 V across the primary, stepped up by the secondary's turns ratio, easily produces the required 15–25 kV at the spark plug.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Internal Choice [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain with a diagram the **difference between LT and HT magneto ignition systems**. Compare their **advantages and disadvantages**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 4-cylinder, 4-stroke SI engine runs at **3600 rpm**. The magneto is coupled with a **1:1** gear ratio, has **4 poles**, **250 primary turns**, and a maximum flux per pole of **0.004 Wb**. The winding factor is **0.96**. Compute the **RMS voltage** generated in the magneto primary winding. **(7 marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution to Question B (a)**

**LT Magneto (3 marks):**
- The magneto rotor generates only **low-tension (50–250 V) AC** in its primary.
- This LT current is fed to a **separate step-up ignition coil** (mounted externally).
- The ignition coil contains the secondary winding that steps the voltage up to 15–25 kV.
- The HT is then routed through the distributor to the spark plugs.

**HT Magneto (2 marks):**
- The magneto itself contains **both primary and secondary windings** on the same armature.
- The HT voltage is generated **directly inside the magneto body**.
- No separate ignition coil is required.
- Typically used in single-cylinder high-speed engines (motorcycles, scooters).

**Advantages & Disadvantages Table (2 marks):**

| Aspect | LT Magneto | HT Magneto |
|---|---|---|
| Construction | Simple rotor + separate coil | Complex integral windings |
| Maintenance | Easier (separate components) | Harder (sealed unit) |
| Reliability | Higher (coil replaceable) | Lower (whole unit replaced) |
| Application | Multi-cylinder, medium-speed | Single-cylinder, high-speed |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |
| Spark quality | Better at low speeds | Better at high speeds |

---

##### **Model Solution to Question B (b)**

**Given Data:**
- Engine speed: $N_{engine} = 3600$ rpm
- Gear ratio: 1:1 → $N_{mag} = 3600$ rpm
- Number of poles: $P = 4$
- Primary turns: $N_p = 250$
- Maximum flux per pole: $\Phi_{max} = 0.004$ Wb
- Winding factor: $K_w = 0.96$

**Step 1 — Calculate the frequency of induced EMF:**

$$f = \frac{N_{mag} \times P}{120} = \frac{3600 \times 4}{120} = 120 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the magneto EMF equation:**

$$E_{rms} = 4.44 \, f \, N_p \, \Phi_{max} \, K_w$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the numerical values:**

$$E_{rms} = 4.44 \times 120 \times 250 \times 0.004 \times 0.96$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate step by step:**

- $4.44 \times 120 = 532.8$
- $532.8 \times 250 = 133{,}200$
- $133{,}200 \times 0.004 = 532.8$
- $532.8 \times 0.96 = 511.49 \text{ V}$

**Final Answer:** $E_{rms} \approx \mathbf{511.5 \text{ V}}$

> **Examiner's note:** This is the *primary* voltage. After step-up by the ignition coil (turns ratio ~ 100), the secondary HT voltage would be:
> $V_{HT} = 511.5 \times 100 = 51{,}150 \text{ V} \approx 51 \text{ kV}$ — more than enough to ionize the spark-plug gap.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Magneto Ignition System**

- ✅ **Definition:** A *self-contained* ignition system where the magneto itself generates the high-tension current — **no battery required**.
- ✅ **Principle:** Electromagnetic induction (Faraday's Law).
- ✅ **Key Formula:** $E_{rms} = 4.44 \, f \, N_p \, \Phi_{max} \, K_w$
- ✅ **Frequency Formula:** $f = \dfrac{N_{mag} \times P}{120}$ (for P-pole magneto).
- ✅ **Spark Voltage Range:** **15 kV to 25 kV** at the spark plug.
- ✅ **Components to Always Mention:** Rotor, C-core, primary, secondary, breaker points, **condenser (0.15–0.30 µF)**, distributor, spark plug, kill switch.
- ✅ **Types — by Rotor:** Rotating-magnet, Rotating-armature, Polar-inductor.
- ✅ **Types — by Voltage:** **LT magneto** (separate ignition coil) vs. **HT magneto** (integral secondary).
- ✅ **Condenser's Job:** Suppress arcing + sharpen flux collapse + store energy transiently.
- ✅ **Energy Equations:** $E_{inductor} = \frac{1}{2} L_p I_p^2$ and $E_{condenser} = \frac{1}{2} C V_c^2$, leading to $V_c = I_p \sqrt{L_p / C}$.
- ✅ **Advantages:** No battery, lightweight, reliable at high RPM, compact.
- ✅ **Disadvantages:** Weak spark at low speeds, requires impulse coupling, points/condenser need periodic maintenance.
- ✅ **Applications:** Two-wheelers, outboard motors, lawn mowers, small tractors, aircraft piston engines (dual magneto for redundancy).
- ✅ **Common Exam Traps:** Confusing LT and HT, forgetting the condenser, ignoring the $K_w$ factor, sign errors in derivations, mixing up magneto with battery ignition.
- ✅ **Units to Memorize:** Flux in **Wb**, flux density in **T**, frequency in **Hz**, EMF in **V**, turns are dimensionless, capacitance in **F** (or µF).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Distributorless Ignition - CDI and Coil on Plug Type of Ignition System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT205 - AUTOMOBILE POWER PLANT (AUTOMOBILE ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Distributorless Ignition - CDI and Coil on Plug Type of Ignition System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Distributorless Ignition Systems

The continuous demand for improved fuel economy, reduced exhaust emissions, and reliable cold-start performance in modern spark-ignition engines has pushed conventional distributor-based ignition systems to their operational limits. At high engine speeds, the mechanical contact breaker suffers from contact bounce, electrode wear, and an inability to deliver sufficiently high secondary voltages. These limitations motivated the development of **Distributorless Ignition Systems (DIS)**, which eliminate the mechanical distributor entirely and rely on solid-state electronics managed by the **Engine Control Unit (ECU)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** The 2024 Scheme specifically classifies Distributorless Ignition into two dominant modern sub-types:
> 1. **Capacitor Discharge Ignition (CDI)** — a *charged capacitor* discharges through the primary of the ignition coil.
> 2. **Coil-on-Plug (COP) Ignition** — each spark plug has its *own dedicated ignition coil* mounted directly on top, eliminating the high-tension cable.

## 1.2 Formal Definition of Distributorless Ignition (DIS)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** A Distributorless Ignition System is an electronically controlled ignition architecture in which the traditional **rotary high-voltage distributor** is replaced by static (non-moving) semiconductor switching elements and an **electronic control module**. The high-voltage surge generated by the ignition coil is routed to the correct spark plug either through paired coils (wasted-spark) or through individual coil-on-plug assemblies, with the firing sequence determined by **camshaft position (CMP)** and **crankshaft position (CKP)** sensors rather than mechanical rotation.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Modern Mailroom"

Imagine an old-fashioned mailroom in a large office building. A single postman (the mechanical distributor) had to walk from desk to desk, hand-delivering urgent letters one at a time. As the office grew, the postman got tired, made mistakes, and delivered letters late. 

The **Distributorless Ignition System** is like installing an **electronic pneumatic-tube network**:
- Each desk (spark plug) has its own **direct delivery tube** (Coil-on-Plug), OR
- Each pair of desks (companion cylinders) shares a smart tube that fires both, relying on **timing intelligence** (Wasted-Spark DIS) so that only the one at top-dead-center actually ignites the air-fuel mixture productively.
- The central computer (ECU) decides *when* and *which* tube fires, using **CKP** and **CMP** signals — no moving parts, no wear, no late deliveries.

In **CDI**, the analogy extends further: instead of building pressure in a hydraulic line gradually, a small but very fast-acting **electrical capacitor is pre-charged** and then released in a *single, sharp pulse* — like flicking a fully charged water balloon with a pin to release the entire energy in microseconds.

## 1.4 Why Distributorless? — The Engineering Motivation

The transition from distributor to distributorless was not cosmetic. The key driving forces were:

| Limitation of Distributor Ignition | Solution Provided by DIS |
|---|---|
| **Contact breaker wear** at high RPM | Solid-state switching — no mechanical contact |
| Limited **secondary voltage** (≈ 25 kV) | Voltage easily exceeds **35 kV to 45 kV** |
| **Spark timing drift** due to mechanical advance weights | Electronic advance calculated by ECU from maps |
| **Distributor cap/rotor arcing** at high RPM | Static routing — no rotor-to-cap gap |
| Poor **cold-start** spark energy | CDI delivers constant high-energy spark regardless of RPM |
| Increasing **emission control** demands | Precise electronic timing → better combustion → lower HC, CO, NOx |

## 1.5 Classification Snapshot (KTU 2024 Module 3 Map)

$$
\text{Distributorless Ignition}
\begin{cases}
\text{(A) Capacitor Discharge Ignition (CDI)} \\
\qquad \text{— Energy stored in a capacitor, released via SCR/thyristor} \\[4pt]
\text{(B) Coil-on-Plug (COP) Ignition} \\
\qquad \text{— Individual coil mounted on each spark plug} \\[4pt]
\text{(C) Wasted-Spark DIS (paired coils)} \\
\qquad \text{— Logical subset of DIS used in many 4-cyl applications}
\end{cases}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Tip:** When the question says "explain CDI" or "explain COP," examiners expect you to (1) list components, (2) draw a labelled schematic, (3) describe the *sequence* of energy storage and release, and (4) list at least four advantages over conventional ignition.

## 1.6 Key Performance Metrics at a Glance

- **Standard primary voltage** in CDI: **$V_p = 250$ V to 600 V** (DC, stepped up from 12 V battery)
- **Discharge time of capacitor** through primary: typically **$t_d = 30\ \mu s$ to $100\ \mu s$**
- **Secondary peak voltage** delivered to plug: **$V_s = 25$ kV to **40 kV****
- **Spark duration in CDI**: **0.1 ms to 0.3 ms** (very short, very hot)
- **Spark duration in inductive (COP)**: **1.0 ms to 2.0 ms** (longer, more sustained)
- **Operating RPM range for modern DIS**: up to **8,000 rpm to 10,000 rpm** without voltage degradation
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Capacitor Discharge Ignition (CDI) — Operational Theory

### 2.1.1 Principle of Operation

In a **CDI system**, the energy for the spark is **not stored in the inductance of the ignition coil** (as in conventional inductive ignition). Instead, energy is stored in a **capacitor** and then discharged rapidly through the **primary winding of the ignition coil**. The transformer action of the coil steps this fast-rising primary current pulse into a very high secondary voltage.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Conceptual Point:** CDI produces a *high-energy, very short-duration* spark. The spark is so brief (microseconds) that the plug does not have time to foul with carbon deposits — making CDI particularly popular in **two-wheelers, small engines, lawnmowers, and racing applications** where plug fouling is a real problem.

### 2.1.2 Major Components of a CDI System

1. **12 V DC Battery / Magneto** — the source of primary energy.
2. **DC-to-DC Converter (Inverter/Oscillator)** — steps the 12 V DC up to **250 V – 600 V DC**.
3. **Energy Storage Capacitor (C)** — typically **0.5 µF to 2.0 µF**, rated for high voltage.
4. **Silicon Controlled Rectifier (SCR / Thyristor)** — acts as a fast electronic switch.
5. **Trigger Pulse Generator** — usually a **pickup coil (pulsar coil)** sensing a timing magnet on the flywheel; sends a small pulse to the SCR gate.
6. **Ignition Coil (Step-up Transformer)** — steps up the primary pulse to ~**25 – 40 kV**.
7. **High-Tension (HT) Lead and Spark Plug**.

### 2.1.3 Step-by-Step Operating Sequence

**Step 1 — Oscillation and Step-Up:**
The DC-DC converter rapidly switches the 12 V supply through a transistor oscillator and a small step-up transformer, producing an **alternating high-voltage signal**.

**Step 2 — Rectification and Charging:**
This AC is rectified by a **high-voltage diode** and used to charge the storage capacitor $C$ to a voltage $V_c$ (typically **400 V to 500 V**).

**Step 3 — Trigger Pulse:**
As the flywheel rotates, a permanent magnet on it passes the **pulsar coil**. The coil generates a small alternating voltage (the **trigger pulse**), which is shaped by the **trigger circuit** and applied to the **gate of the SCR** at the precise instant the spark is required.

**Step 4 — Capacitor Discharge:**
The SCR is forward-biased by the trigger pulse and conducts. The capacitor dumps its stored energy through the **primary winding** of the ignition coil in a very fast pulse (a high $\frac{di}{dt}$).

**Step 5 — High-Voltage Induction:**
This rapid change in primary current induces a very high voltage in the **secondary winding** according to the turns ratio $N_s / N_p$:

$$
V_s \;=\; -N_s \,\frac{d\Phi}{dt} \;\approx\; -\frac{N_s}{N_p} \, V_c
$$

**Step 6 — Spark Discharge:**
The secondary voltage ionizes the plug gap and a spark jumps across. After the capacitor is discharged, the SCR becomes reverse-biased and turns off automatically. The cycle repeats for the next cylinder.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why CDI works at any RPM:** The capacitor charges almost instantly from the DC-DC converter (typically in **< 1 ms**). The spark timing is controlled by the *trigger pulse*, not by the gradual build-up of magnetic energy. Hence, CDI delivers a **uniform, consistent spark from idle to red-line**, unlike inductive systems whose primary current (and hence spark energy) falls off at high RPM.

### 2.1.4 Energy Stored in the Capacitor

The energy stored in the charging capacitor is given by:

$$
E_C \;=\; \frac{1}{2}\,C\,V_c^{\,2}
$$

**Numerical example (board favourite):**
If $C = 1\ \mu F = 1 \times 10^{-6}$ F and $V_c = 400$ V,

$$
E_C = \frac{1}{2} \times (1 \times 10^{-6}) \times (400)^2
     = \frac{1}{2} \times 10^{-6} \times 1.6 \times 10^{5}
     = 8.0 \times 10^{-2}\ \text{J} \;=\; 80\ \text{mJ}
$$

This is the energy that will be released in a few tens of microseconds — enough to create a reliable ignition kernel.

### 2.1.5 Time Constant of Discharge

When the SCR fires, the capacitor discharges through the low-resistance primary winding (resistance $R_p$, inductance $L_p$). The discharge is governed by:

$$
\tau_d \;=\; \pi\,\sqrt{L_p \, C} \quad \text{(underdamped, oscillatory case)}
$$

For a typical CDI: $L_p \approx 1$ mH, $C = 1\ \mu F$ → $\tau_d \approx \pi \sqrt{(10^{-3})(10^{-6})} \approx 3.14 \times 10^{-4.5}$ s ≈ **100 µs** (peak in ~50 µs).

### 2.1.6 Advantages of CDI

- **Very fast voltage rise** ($\frac{dV}{dt} > 1$ kV/µs) — guarantees spark even with fouled or wet plugs.
- **Constant spark energy** independent of engine RPM.
- **No contact breaker** — no maintenance, no wear.
- **Compact and lightweight** — ideal for two-wheelers.
- **Reduced plug fouling** — short, hot, high-energy spark burns off deposits.

### 2.1.7 Limitations of CDI

- **Short spark duration** may not be enough to ignite very lean mixtures (modern lean-burn GDI engines prefer longer sparks).
- **Higher manufacturing cost** than inductive systems.
- **Complexity of DC-DC converter** — more components to fail.
- **EMI/RFI** — sharp edges create electromagnetic interference; requires careful shielding.

## 2.2 Coil-on-Plug (COP) Ignition System — Operational Theory

### 2.2.1 Principle of Operation

In a **Coil-on-Plug** system, the ignition coil is mounted **directly on top of the spark plug**. There is **no high-tension (HT) cable** between the coil and the plug. The ECU fires each coil individually at the precise moment computed from CKP, CMP, MAP, MAF, and knock-sensor inputs. Each plug therefore has a dedicated, controllable, high-energy ignition source.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (COP):** Coil-on-Plug is a configuration of distributorless ignition in which each spark plug is provided with its **own dedicated stick or pencil-type ignition coil**, mechanically and electrically integrated with the plug via a spring-loaded contact or a direct plug-in terminal, and switched by an **IGBT (Insulated Gate Bipolar Transistor)** driver inside or adjacent to the coil.

### 2.2.2 Components of a COP System

1. **12 V Battery** — primary energy source.
2. **Engine Control Unit (ECU)** — decides dwell time and firing instant.
3. **Crankshaft Position Sensor (CKP)** — 60-minus-2 tooth wheel, gives engine speed and piston position.
4. **Camshaft Position Sensor (CMP)** — distinguishes compression stroke from exhaust stroke (phasing).
5. **Individual Ignition Coils** — one per cylinder; **pencil coils** (for narrow plug wells) or **block coils** (for conventional heads).
6. **IGBT Switch** — usually inside the coil housing; the ECU provides a low-current command signal.
7. **Spark Plugs** — often **iridium or platinum** for long life.
8. **Coil Driver Harness** — three wires per coil: **+12 V (B+)**, **ECU command (gate)**, **ground**.

### 2.2.3 Operating Sequence (Inductive Discharge — NOT Capacitive)

**Step 1 — Position Sensing:**
The CKP sensor detects the missing tooth on the reluctor wheel and tells the ECU that piston #1 is approaching TDC. The CMP sensor then confirms whether #1 is on its **compression** or **exhaust** stroke.

**Step 2 — Dwell (Charging) Phase:**
The ECU commands the IGBT inside the coil to **conduct**, completing the primary circuit. Current in the primary winding rises from 0 A to a target of **5 A to 10 A**, building up magnetic flux $\Phi$ in the iron core.

**Step 3 — Firing:**
At the exact computed ignition timing (e.g., 10° BTDC at idle, advancing to 35°–45° BTDC at WOT), the ECU removes the gate drive. The IGBT **switches off in microseconds**, the primary current is interrupted, and the magnetic flux collapses.

**Step 4 — High-Voltage Generation:**
The collapsing flux induces a high voltage $V_s$ in the secondary. Because the plug is *physically attached* to the coil (no HT cable), nearly all of this voltage appears across the plug gap. There is **no leakage path**, no cable capacitance loss.

**Step 5 — Spark Discharge:**
The plug gap ionizes, the spark fires, the energy stored in the primary inductance is dissipated in the arc. A multi-strike (also called *ion-sense feedback*) may follow in the same cycle.

### 2.2.4 Energy Stored in the Coil (Inductive)

The energy stored in the primary winding's magnetic field at the end of the dwell is:

$$
E_L \;=\; \frac{1}{2}\,L_p\,I_p^{\,2}
$$

**Numerical example:**
If $L_p = 6$ mH $= 6 \times 10^{-3}$ H and $I_p = 7$ A,

$$
E_L = \frac{1}{2} \times (6 \times 10^{-3}) \times (7)^2
     = \frac{1}{2} \times 6 \times 10^{-3} \times 49
     = 147 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{J} \;\approx\; 147\ \text{mJ}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Comparison callout:** A typical CDI delivers ~80 mJ in <100 µs; a typical COP delivers ~150 mJ over ~1.5 ms. **COP has roughly twice the energy and ten times the spark duration of CDI**, but the peak voltage rise rate is slower.

### 2.2.5 Secondary Voltage Equation

For an ideal step-up transformer with turns ratio $n = N_s / N_p$ and assuming the current is interrupted from $I_p$ to 0 in time $\Delta t$:

$$
V_s \;\approx\; n \cdot L_p \cdot \frac{I_p}{\Delta t}
$$

A higher $n$ and faster switching give higher $V_s$. Typical COP: $n \approx 100$, $L_p \approx 5$ mH, $I_p \approx 7$ A, $\Delta t \approx 25\ \mu s$:

$$
V_s \;\approx\; 100 \times 5 \times 10^{-3} \times \frac{7}{25 \times 10^{-6}}
        \;=\; 100 \times 5 \times 10^{-3} \times 2.8 \times 10^{5}
        \;=\; 1.4 \times 10^{5}\ \text{V} \;=\; \mathbf{140\ kV\ (peak\ theoretical)}
$$

In practice the secondary is clamped by the plug breakdown (~25 – 35 kV) and the rest of the energy is dissipated in the arc.

### 2.2.6 Advantages of COP

- **No HT cable losses** — full coil voltage reaches the plug.
- **Maximum spark energy at the gap** — ideal for lean-burn and direct-injection engines.
- **Per-cylinder timing accuracy** — each cylinder timed independently to ±0.5°.
- **Compact packaging** — pencil coils fit into valve-cover integrated wells.
- **Easy diagnosis** — ECU can run *misfire detection* by ion-sense current feedback.
- **Eliminates rotor, cap, distributor** — zero mechanical wear.

### 2.2.7 Limitations of COP

- **Cost** — one coil per cylinder (4 coils for 4 cylinders).
- **Heat** — coil sits in a hot spark-plug well; thermal management required.
- **Failure mode** — a single failed coil = one cylinder misfire.
- **Plug well contamination** — oil or water in the well can cause flashover (carbon tracking across the boot).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_C = \tfrac{1}{2} C V_c^{\,2}$ | Energy stored in CDI capacitor | Joules (J) |
| 2 | $E_L = \tfrac{1}{2} L_p I_p^{\,2}$ | Energy stored in inductive (COP) coil | Joules (J) |
| 3 | $V_s \approx n \cdot L_p \cdot \dfrac{I_p}{\Delta t}$ | Secondary peak voltage in COP | Volts (V) |
| 4 | $V_s \approx (N_s / N_p) \cdot V_c$ | Secondary peak voltage in CDI | Volts (V) |
| 5 | $\tau_d = \pi \sqrt{L_p C}$ | Discharge time constant of CDI through coil | seconds (s) |
| 6 | $\eta = E_{spark} / E_{stored}$ | Energy transfer efficiency | 0.55 – 0.85 (dimensionless) |
| 7 | $\text{Advance}_{\text{dyn}} = f(\text{RPM}, \text{Load}, \text{Knock})$ | ECU dynamic advance map | degrees BTDC |
| 8 | $P_{spark} = E_L / t_{arc}$ | Average spark power | Watts (W) |

> [!TIP]
> **Board Tip:** When asked to *compare CDI and COP*, always state (i) energy storage element (capacitor vs. inductor), (ii) spark duration (microseconds vs. milliseconds), (iii) peak voltage rise rate, (iv) typical application (two-wheeler vs. modern 4-wheeler), and (v) the equation used to calculate the stored energy.

## 2.4 Comparison Matrix — CDI vs COP vs Conventional

| Parameter | Conventional (Coil + Distributor) | CDI | COP |
|---|---|---|---|
| Energy storage | Coil inductance | Capacitor | Coil inductance |
| Switching element | Contact breaker / transistor | SCR (thyristor) | IGBT |
| Spark duration | 1.0 – 1.5 ms | 0.1 – 0.3 ms | 1.0 – 2.0 ms |
| Spark energy | 30 – 60 mJ | 50 – 80 mJ | 80 – 150 mJ |
| Voltage rise rate | Moderate | Very high | Moderate–high |
| RPM stability | Spark energy falls at high RPM | Constant spark energy | Spark energy falls slightly, but coil-per-plug compensates |
| Plug fouling tolerance | Poor | Excellent | Good |
| Application | Older vehicles | Two-wheelers, small engines, racing | Modern 4-wheelers, GDI engines |
| Maintenance | High (points, cap, rotor) | Very low | Very low |
| Cost | Lowest | Medium | Highest |
| EMI generated | Low | High (sharp pulse) | Medium |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Motorcycles (Hero, Honda, Bajaj, Yamaha, Royal Enfield, KTM Duke series):** Almost all modern two-wheelers use a **CDI unit** because of its tolerance to plug fouling during short trips, low cost, and small size. Battery-less (magneto) CDI is common in commuter bikes.
- **Passenger cars (Toyota, Honda, Hyundai, Maruti, Tata):** Modern petrol cars use **Coil-on-Plug** systems. For example, Maruti Swift's K-series engine, Hyundai Kappa, Honda i-VTEC, Toyota VVT-i — all use 4 individual coils.
- **Motor-sport (F1, MotoGP):** Use **CDI or Capacitive Discharge Ignition** for the very high RPM and reliability requirements.
- **GDI (Gasoline Direct Injection) engines:** Require long, high-energy sparks to ignite stratified charge → **COP** is the only viable choice.
- **Hybrid vehicles (Toyota Prius, Honda Insight):** Use **COP** with ECU-controlled individual firing.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Energy Stored in a CDI Capacitor and Time to Discharge

We start from first principles. A capacitor $C$ charged to a voltage $V_c$ stores energy equal to the work done to transfer charge $Q = C V_c$ from one plate to the other against the average voltage $V_c/2$.

### Step 1 — Charge stored on the capacitor

The fundamental relation between charge, capacitance, and voltage is:

$$
Q \;=\; C \, V_c
$$

This is the definition of capacitance. For a CDI module with $C = 1\ \mu F$ and $V_c = 400$ V:

$$
Q = (1 \times 10^{-6}) \times 400 \;=\; 4 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{C} \;=\; 0.4\ \text{mC}
$$

### Step 2 — Energy stored in the capacitor

Energy is the integral of voltage over charge transferred. As charge is added in infinitesimal increments $dq$, the voltage rises linearly from 0 to $V_c$:

$$
E_C \;=\; \int_{0}^{Q} v\,dq \;=\; \int_{0}^{Q} \frac{q}{C}\,dq \;=\; \left[\frac{q^2}{2C}\right]_0^Q \;=\; \frac{Q^2}{2C}
$$

Substituting $Q = C V_c$:

$$
E_C \;=\; \frac{(C V_c)^2}{2C} \;=\; \frac{C^2 V_c^2}{2C} \;=\; \frac{1}{2} C V_c^{\,2}
$$

This is the **fundamental CDI energy equation** used in every KTU board question.

### Step 3 — Numerical evaluation

$$
E_C = \tfrac{1}{2} \times (1 \times 10^{-6}) \times (400)^2
     = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 10^{-6} \times 1.6 \times 10^{5}
     = 0.8 \times 10^{-1}
     = 8.0 \times 10^{-2}\ \text{J} \;=\; 80\ \text{mJ}
$$

### Step 4 — Discharge time through the primary winding

When the SCR fires, the capacitor discharges through the primary inductance $L_p$ and small series resistance $R_p$. The governing differential equation for the circuit $L_p$–$C$ (under-damped) is:

$$
L_p \frac{d^2 i}{dt^2} + R_p \frac{di}{dt} + \frac{i}{C} \;=\; 0
$$

In the **ideal lossless case** ($R_p \to 0$), the natural angular frequency of the LC oscillation is:

$$
\omega_n \;=\; \frac{1}{\sqrt{L_p\,C}}
$$

The first current peak (which is also the moment of peak secondary voltage) occurs at a quarter of the period:

$$
t_{peak} \;=\; \frac{\pi}{2\omega_n} \;=\; \frac{\pi}{2}\,\sqrt{L_p\,C}
$$

Therefore the **first-half-cycle discharge time** (the time the spark is being established) is:

$$
\tau_d \;=\; \frac{\pi}{2}\,\sqrt{L_p\,C}
$$

For $L_p = 1$ mH, $C = 1\ \mu F$:

$$
\tau_d = \frac{\pi}{2}\,\sqrt{(10^{-3})(10^{-6})}
        = \frac{\pi}{2} \times \sqrt{10^{-9}}
        = \frac{\pi}{2} \times (3.162 \times 10^{-5})
        = 4.97 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{s} \;\approx\; \mathbf{50\ \mu s}
$$

This confirms the claim that CDI sparks last only tens of microseconds.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Secondary Voltage in a Coil-on-Plug System

### Step 1 — Inductive stored energy (already shown in §2.2.4)

$$
E_L \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,L_p\,I_p^{\,2}
$$

### Step 2 — Rate of change of flux

The flux $\Phi$ linked with the primary winding is:

$$
\Phi \;=\; \frac{L_p\,I_p}{N_p}
$$

When the IGBT switches off, $I_p$ goes from $I_p$ to 0 in a time $\Delta t$. Therefore:

$$
\frac{d\Phi}{dt} \;=\; \frac{1}{N_p}\,\frac{d}{dt}(L_p I_p) \;\approx\; \frac{L_p}{N_p} \cdot \frac{-I_p}{\Delta t}
$$

(The minus sign indicates flux collapse; magnitude matters for voltage.)

### Step 3 — Apply Faraday's Law to the secondary winding

The secondary has $N_s$ turns. The induced EMF (Lenz's law) is:

$$
V_s \;=\; -N_s\,\frac{d\Phi}{dt} \;\approx\; N_s \cdot \frac{L_p}{N_p} \cdot \frac{I_p}{\Delta t}
$$

### Step 4 — Introduce the turns ratio $n = N_s / N_p$

$$
V_s \;\approx\; n \cdot L_p \cdot \frac{I_p}{\Delta t}
$$

This is the equation already listed in the cheat sheet (§2.3, row 3).

### Step 5 — Numerical evaluation

Let $n = 100$, $L_p = 5\ \text{mH} = 5 \times 10^{-3}$ H, $I_p = 7$ A, $\Delta t = 25\ \mu s = 25 \times 10^{-6}$ s:

$$
V_s = 100 \times (5 \times 10^{-3}) \times \frac{7}{25 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

Compute numerator: $100 \times 5 \times 10^{-3} \times 7 = 100 \times 35 \times 10^{-3} = 3.5$.

Compute denominator: $25 \times 10^{-6} = 2.5 \times 10^{-5}$.

$$
V_s = \frac{3.5}{2.5 \times 10^{-5}} = 1.4 \times 10^{5}\ \text{V} = \mathbf{140\ kV}
$$

### Step 6 — Why we don't see 140 kV at the plug

In practice, the plug gap breaks down at **25 kV to 35 kV** in air. Once the arc is established, the secondary voltage **collapses to the arc voltage** (~ 1 – 2 kV) and the remaining energy is dissipated in the arc as heat and light. The 140 kV is the *open-circuit* theoretical peak — the system is "voltage-clamped" by the arc itself.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Spark Duration in COP (Inductive)

Once the arc is struck, the secondary current decays according to the energy balance. Approximating the arc as a resistance $R_{arc}$ of about 1 k$\Omega$:

$$
E_L \;\approx\; \int_0^{t_{arc}} \frac{V_{arc}^{\,2}}{R_{arc}}\,dt \;\approx\; \frac{V_{arc}^{\,2}}{R_{arc}}\,t_{arc}
$$

Solving for $t_{arc}$:

$$
t_{arc} \;\approx\; \frac{E_L \, R_{arc}}{V_{arc}^{\,2}}
$$

With $E_L = 150$ mJ, $R_{arc} = 1$ k$\Omega$, $V_{arc} = 1.5$ kV:

$$
t_{arc} \approx \frac{0.150 \times 1000}{(1500)^2} = \frac{150}{2.25 \times 10^{6}} = 6.67 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{s} \approx \mathbf{67\ \mu s}
$$

However, modern COP systems use **multi-strike** logic (the ECU re-fires the coil several times in quick succession for a single ignition event), effectively extending the *total arc duration* to **1 – 2 ms**. This is the long, sustained spark preferred by GDI engines.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Dwell Time Calculation (Current Ramp-Up)

The primary current in a coil obeys the LR time constant:

$$
I_p(t) \;=\; I_{\infty}\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau_L}\right), \quad \text{where } \tau_L = L_p / R_p
$$

The ECU must hold the IGBT ON for a *dwell time* $t_d$ such that $I_p$ reaches the target value $I_{target}$:

$$
I_{target} \;=\; I_{\infty}\left(1 - e^{-t_d / \tau_L}\right)
$$

Solving for $t_d$:

$$
t_d \;=\; -\tau_L \,\ln\!\left(1 - \frac{I_{target}}{I_{\infty}}\right)
$$

For $L_p = 5$ mH, $R_p = 0.5\ \Omega$, $I_{\infty} = 12$ V / 0.5 $\Omega$ = 24 A, and $I_{target} = 7$ A:

$$
\tau_L = \frac{0.005}{0.5} = 0.01\ \text{s} = 10\ \text{ms}
$$

$$
t_d = -0.01 \times \ln(1 - 7/24) = -0.01 \times \ln(0.7083) = -0.01 \times (-0.345) = 3.45\ \text{ms}
$$

So the ECU must switch the IGBT ON about **3.45 ms before the desired spark instant** at low RPM. At high RPM the dwell is automatically extended (the time between firings is shorter, so a larger fraction is dedicated to charging).

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which System?

| Engine / Application | Recommended System | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| 100 cc – 150 cc commuter motorcycle | CDI (magneto) | Low cost, plug-fouling tolerance, small size |
| 200 cc – 1000 cc sport motorcycle | Inductive DIS / COP | Higher energy, better idle quality, more efficient |
| Modern passenger car (multi-point injection) | COP with ion-sense | Per-cylinder timing, misfire detection |
| Direct-injection (GDI) petrol car | COP (always) | Long spark needed for stratified charge |
| Outboard marine engine | CDI | Salt-air plug fouling, harsh environment |
| Formula 1 / MotoGP | CDI | Very high RPM, guaranteed ignition kernel |
| Hybrid passenger car (Atkinson cycle) | COP | ECU controls advanced / retarded timing for fuel economy |
| LPG / CNG fuelled car | COP | Higher breakdown voltage needed for gaseous fuel |

> [!TIP]
> **Board Tip:** A common 14-mark question asks: *"With a neat sketch, explain the working of a Capacitor Discharge Ignition system used in two-wheelers. State its merits over the conventional ignition system."* Always include: block diagram, sequence of operation, energy equation, four or five merits, and one or two limitations.

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Style 7-Mark Sub-Question

> **Question:** *A CDI system uses a $1.0\ \mu F$ capacitor charged to $500$ V. The ignition coil has a turns ratio of $N_s/N_p = 100$. Compute (i) the energy stored in the capacitor, (ii) the theoretical peak secondary voltage, and (iii) the discharge time through a 1.5 mH primary winding.*

**Solution:**

**Part (i):**
$$
E_C = \tfrac{1}{2} C V_c^2 = \tfrac{1}{2} \times (1.0 \times 10^{-6}) \times (500)^2
     = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 10^{-6} \times 2.5 \times 10^{5}
     = 0.125\ \text{J} \;=\; 125\ \text{mJ}
$$

**Part (ii):**
$$
V_s = n \times V_c = 100 \times 500 = 50{,}000\ \text{V} = \mathbf{50\ kV}
$$

**Part (iii):**
$$
\tau_d = \frac{\pi}{2}\sqrt{L_p C} = \frac{\pi}{2}\sqrt{(1.5 \times 10^{-3})(1.0 \times 10^{-6})}
       = \frac{\pi}{2} \times \sqrt{1.5 \times 10^{-9}}
       = \frac{\pi}{2} \times (3.873 \times 10^{-5})
       = 6.08 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{s} \;\approx\; \mathbf{60.8\ \mu s}
$$
