# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Capacitor Discharge Ignition (CDI)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeBat["12V Battery or Magneto"] --> nodeInv["DC DC Converter Oscillator"]
    nodeInv --> nodeRect["HV Rectifier Diode"]
    nodeRect --> nodeCap["Energy Storage Capacitor 1uF 400V"]
    nodeTrig["Trigger Pickup Coil on Flywheel"] --> nodeTrigCkt["Trigger Pulse Shaping Circuit"]
    nodeTrigCkt --> nodeSCR["SCR Thyristor Gate Trigger"]
    nodeCap --> nodeSCR
    nodeSCR --> nodePri["Ignition Coil Primary Winding Lp"]
    nodePri --> nodeSec["Ignition Coil Secondary Winding Ns"]
    nodeSec --> nodeHT["HT Lead"]
    nodeHT --> nodePlug["Spark Plug Gap"]
    nodePri --> nodeGnd["Chassis Ground"]
    nodeSCR --> nodeGnd
    nodeGnd --> nodeBat
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The 12 V source is converted to high-voltage DC and stored in the capacitor. The trigger pulse — generated mechanically as the flywheel magnet passes the pickup coil — fires the SCR. The capacitor dumps its charge through the primary of the coil, inducing a high-voltage surge in the secondary that jumps the spark-plug gap.

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Coil-on-Plug Ignition System (COP)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeBat["12V Battery"] --> nodeECU["Engine Control Unit ECU"]
    nodeCKP["Crankshaft Position Sensor CKP"] --> nodeECU
    nodeCMP["Camshaft Position Sensor CMP"] --> nodeECU
    nodeMAP["MAP MAF Sensors"] --> nodeECU
    nodeKnock["Knock Sensor"] --> nodeECU
    nodeECU --> nodeDrv1["Coil Driver IGBT Cyl 1"]
    nodeECU --> nodeDrv2["Coil Driver IGBT Cyl 2"]
    nodeECU --> nodeDrv3["Coil Driver IGBT Cyl 3"]
    nodeECU --> nodeDrv4["Coil Driver IGBT Cyl 4"]
    nodeDrv1 --> nodeCoil1["Pencil Ignition Coil Cyl 1"]
    nodeDrv2 --> nodeCoil2["Pencil Ignition Coil Cyl 2"]
    nodeDrv3 --> nodeCoil3["Pencil Ignition Coil Cyl 3"]
    nodeDrv4 --> nodeCoil4["Pencil Ignition Coil Cyl 4"]
    nodeCoil1 --> nodePlug1["Spark Plug Cyl 1"]
    nodeCoil2 --> nodePlug2["Spark Plug Cyl 2"]
    nodeCoil3 --> nodePlug3["Spark Plug Cyl 3"]
    nodeCoil4 --> nodePlug4["Spark Plug Cyl 4"]
    nodeECU -.-> nodeFB["Ion Sense Feedback for Misfire Detection"]
    nodePlug1 -.-> nodeFB
    nodePlug2 -.-> nodeFB
    nodePlug3 -.-> nodeFB
    nodePlug4 -.-> nodeFB
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Each cylinder has a dedicated coil, individually controlled by an IGBT driver commanded by the ECU. The ECU uses CKP, CMP, MAP/MAF, and knock-sensor inputs to compute optimal spark advance. Optional ion-sense feedback lines (dotted arrows) return to the ECU for on-board misfire detection (mandatory under Euro 6 / BS-VI / Euro 7 norms).

## 4.3 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Single Spark Event in a COP System

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant CKP as CKP Sensor
    participant CMP as CMP Sensor
    participant ECU as ECU
    participant IGBT as IGBT Driver
    participant COIL as Primary Winding
    participant PLUG as Spark Plug
    CKP->>ECU: Pulses - engine speed and position
    CMP->>ECU: Phase reference cylinder 1
    ECU->>ECU: Lookup advance map compute target spark angle
    ECU->>IGBT: Gate ON command start of dwell
    IGBT->>COIL: Conducting - primary current ramps from 0 to 7A
    Note over COIL: Magnetic flux builds in iron core
    ECU->>IGBT: Gate OFF command at target spark angle
    IGBT-->>COIL: Switches off in 25 microseconds
    Note over COIL: Flux collapses induces high voltage
    COIL->>PLUG: 25 to 35 kV across plug gap
    PLUG-->>PLUG: Arc struck plug ionizes
    PLUG-->>ECU: Ion current feedback for misfire check
    ECU->>IGBT: Optional multi strike re-fire sequence
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the sequence:** The ECU does not "fire" the spark — it merely *stops* the current. Spark is the result of *interrupted* current, which is the entire principle of inductive ignition. The dotted ion-sense return signal at the end is what allows modern BS-VI compliant cars to detect misfires within one firing cycle.

## 4.4 Mermaid State Diagram — Operational Phases of CDI

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> StateIdle
    StateIdle --> StateCharging: Magneto or DC supply ON
    StateCharging --> StateReady: Capacitor charged to 400 to 500V
    StateReady --> StateTriggered: Pulsar coil sends trigger pulse
    StateTriggered --> StateDischarge: SCR fires - capacitor dumps through primary
    StateDischarge --> StateSpark: High voltage in secondary - arc at plug
    StateSpark --> StateReady: SCR self commutates - capacitor recharges
    StateReady --> [*]: Engine off
```

## 4.5 Comparative Schematic — Wasted-Spark DIS (4-Cylinder Variant)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeBat2["12V Battery"] --> nodeECU2["ECU"]
    nodeCKP2["CKP Sensor"] --> nodeECU2
    nodeCMP2["CMP Sensor"] --> nodeECU2
    nodeECU2 --> nodeDPairA["Driver Pair A IGBT1 and IGBT2"]
    nodeDPairA --> nodeCoilPA["Paired Coil 1 and 4"]
    nodeDPairA --> nodeCoilPB["Paired Coil 2 and 3"]
    nodeCoilPA --> nodePlugA1["Spark Plug Cyl 1"]
    nodeCoilPA --> nodePlugA2["Spark Plug Cyl 4"]
    nodeCoilPB --> nodePlugB1["Spark Plug Cyl 2"]
    nodeCoilPB --> nodePlugB2["Spark Plug Cyl 3"]
    nodePlugA1 -. Wasted spark on exhaust stroke .-> nodePlugA2
    nodePlugB1 -. Wasted spark on exhaust stroke .-> nodePlugB2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Wasted-Spark DIS uses one coil per *pair* of companion cylinders (1-4 and 2-3 in a 4-cylinder). Both plugs fire simultaneously; in the cylinder on the compression stroke, the spark ignites the mixture. In the companion cylinder (exhaust stroke), the spark is "wasted" — fired into exhaust gas where it does no work. This is a **cheaper alternative to COP** still used in many production cars (older Honda, Toyota, Ford systems).

## 4.6 Annotated Physical Layout — Coil-on-Plug Assembly

```
          ┌──────────────────┐
          │  ECU Harness     │ ← (3 wires: +12V, GND, IGBT gate)
          └────────┬─────────┘
                   │
            ┌──────▼──────┐
            │ Coil Housing│ ← contains primary + secondary
            │   + IGBT    │   (often integrated "smart coil")
            └──────┬──────┘
                   │
            ┌──────▼──────┐
            │ Spring Boot │ ← silicone rubber, electrically
            │  Contact    │   conductive, snaps onto plug
            └──────┬──────┘
                   │
            ┌──────▼──────┐
            │  Spark Plug │ ← iridium tip, 0.7–1.0 mm gap
            │  in Cyl Head│
            └─────────────┘
```

> [!TIP]
> **Board Tip:** If the question says "with a neat sketch," draw the COP as a vertical stack (harness → coil housing → boot → plug) and label the three wires entering the coil. Mention that the IGBT may be either *inside* the coil (smart coil) or *external* on the engine bay wiring harness.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern Note:** Part A questions in the 2024 Scheme ESE are compulsory short-answer questions. Each carries **3 marks** and is to be answered in 4 – 6 lines. Definitions and direct concept recall are most common here.

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model 2024 Scheme]

**"Define Distributorless Ignition System. Mention any two advantages over conventional distributor-type ignition."**

**Model Answer:**
A Distributorless Ignition System (DIS) is an electronically controlled ignition system in which the conventional rotary distributor is eliminated. The high-voltage surge from the ignition coil is routed to the spark plug through static electronic switching controlled by the ECU, with the firing sequence determined by CKP and CMP sensors.

**Advantages:**
1. No mechanical contact breaker or distributor rotor — therefore no wear, no maintenance, and no RPM-dependent spark energy loss.
2. Higher and more stable secondary voltage (up to 40 kV) ensures reliable ignition even with lean air-fuel mixtures and at high engine speeds.
3. (Bonus) Electronic spark advance is far more precise than mechanical advance, improving fuel economy and reducing emissions.

*(3 marks — 1 for definition, 2 for advantages)*

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model 2024 Scheme]

**"Write the energy equation for energy stored in a Capacitor Discharge Ignition system. Why is CDI preferred for two-wheeler engines?"**

**Model Answer:**
$$
E_C \;=\; \frac{1}{2}\,C\,V_c^{\,2}
$$
where $C$ is the storage capacitance and $V_c$ is the voltage to which the capacitor is charged (typically 300 – 500 V).

**Why CDI is preferred for two-wheelers:**
1. **Constant spark energy** at all RPM — important because commuter bikes operate across a wide RPM range.
2. **Compact and lightweight** — fits into the limited engine bay of a motorcycle.
3. **Tolerant to plug fouling** — short, hot, high-energy spark burns off carbon and oil deposits that accumulate due to short cold trips.
4. **Operates without battery** in magneto-CDI variants — improves reliability and reduces cost.

*(3 marks — 1 for equation, 2 for reasons)*

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern Note:** Part B questions in the 2024 Scheme ESE carry **14 marks each** with **module-level internal choice**. Each question typically has two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. Below are TWO completely independent alternatives (Q-A and Q-B) — the student is expected to answer ONE.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model 2024 Scheme, Module 3 Choice 1]**

**"With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a Capacitor Discharge Ignition (CDI) system used in modern two-wheeler engines. Compare its performance with a conventional battery-coil ignition system. (14 marks)"**

#### Part (a) — Block diagram and working of CDI (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

A Capacitor Discharge Ignition system is a distributorless electronic ignition in which the spark energy is stored in a capacitor (not in the coil's inductance) and then discharged rapidly through the primary of the ignition coil to produce a very high secondary voltage.

**Block Diagram:**

```
   12 V Battery/Magneto → DC–DC Converter → HV Rectifier → Capacitor C
                                                                       │
                                                                       ▼
                       Pulsar Coil on Flywheel → Trigger Circuit → SCR Gate
                                                                       │
                                                                       ▼
                                              Capacitor C → SCR (anode–cathode)
                                                                       │
                                                                       ▼
                                       Ignition Coil Primary → Secondary → HT Lead → Plug
```

**Working — Step by Step:**

1. **Oscillation and step-up:** The 12 V DC supply is fed to a DC–DC converter (transistor oscillator + small transformer) which produces an AC high-voltage signal.
2. **Rectification and charging:** A high-voltage diode rectifies this AC and charges the **energy storage capacitor** $C$ (typically 0.5 – 2.0 µF) to a voltage $V_c \approx 400$ V.
3. **Trigger pulse generation:** A permanent magnet mounted on the flywheel rotates past a stationary **pulsar (pickup) coil** and induces a small alternating voltage — the **trigger pulse**. This is shaped by the trigger circuit.
4. **SCR firing:** The shaped trigger pulse is applied to the **gate** of the SCR at the precise instant the spark is needed (typically a few degrees BTDC). The SCR becomes forward-biased and conducts.
5. **Capacitor discharge:** The charged capacitor dumps its energy through the **primary winding** $L_p$ of the ignition coil in a very fast pulse (tens of microseconds).
6. **High-voltage induction:** The rapidly collapsing magnetic flux induces a high voltage in the **secondary winding** $V_s \approx (N_s/N_p) V_c$. For typical $N_s/N_p = 100$ and $V_c = 400$ V, $V_s \approx 40$ kV.
7. **Spark discharge:** The secondary voltage ionizes the plug gap; a spark jumps and ignites the air-fuel mixture.
8. **Reset:** Once the capacitor is discharged, the SCR automatically turns off (reverse-biased), the capacitor recharges, and the cycle repeats.

**Energy stored:**
$$
E_C = \tfrac{1}{2} C V_c^2 = \tfrac{1}{2} (1\ \mu F)(400\ V)^2 = 80\ \text{mJ}
$$

*(7 marks: 2 marks for block diagram, 4 marks for step-by-step working, 1 mark for energy equation.)*

#### Part (b) — Comparison with conventional battery-coil ignition (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | Conventional Battery–Coil | CDI |
|---|---|---|
| Energy storage | Coil primary inductance $L_p$ | Capacitor $C$ |
| Switching | Contact breaker (mechanical) or transistor | SCR (thyristor) |
| Spark duration | 1.0 – 1.5 ms | 0.1 – 0.3 ms (much shorter) |
| Spark energy at high RPM | Falls off | Stays constant |
| Peak secondary voltage | 20 – 25 kV | 30 – 40 kV |
| Voltage rise rate | Slow | Very high ($\frac{dV}{dt}$ > 1 kV/µs) |
| Plug fouling tolerance | Poor | Excellent |
| Maintenance | High (points, condenser) | Very low |
| Cost | Low | Slightly higher |
| Cold-start reliability | Average | Very good |
| EMI generated | Low | Higher (sharp pulse) |
| Application | Older cars | Two-wheelers, small engines, racing |

**Key inference:** CDI is preferred wherever plug fouling is common (two-wheelers used for short trips) or where a *guaranteed spark* is critical even with a slightly rich or lean mixture. Conventional inductive systems are preferred where long spark duration matters (GDI engines).

*(7 marks: 6 marks for table-style comparison, 1 mark for concluding inference.)*

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model 2024 Scheme, Module 3 Choice 2]**

**"Explain the construction and working of a Coil-on-Plug (COP) ignition system. Discuss its advantages over the conventional and distributor-type inductive ignition systems. (14 marks)"**

#### Part (a) — Construction and working of COP (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Construction:**

In a Coil-on-Plug system, each spark plug is provided with its **own dedicated ignition coil** — typically a "pencil coil" or "block coil" — mounted directly on top of the plug. The major sub-assemblies are:

1. **Primary winding:** A few hundred turns of thick copper wire, connected to the 12 V supply.
2. **Secondary winding:** Tens of thousands of turns of fine wire, connected directly to the plug's centre electrode via a spring contact inside a rubber boot.
3. **Iron core (laminated):** Provides a low-reluctance path for the magnetic flux.
4. **IGBT switch:** Mounted either inside the coil housing ("smart coil") or on the engine wiring harness. The ECU controls it via a low-current gate signal.
5. **Harness connector:** Three pins — +12 V, ground, and ECU command.
6. **HT rubber boot:** Insulates the high voltage from the engine metalwork and seals the plug well from oil/water contamination.

**Working — Step by Step:**

1. **Sensing:** The CKP sensor reads the missing tooth on the crankshaft reluctor wheel; the CMP sensor identifies which cylinder is on its compression stroke. Both signals are sent to the ECU.
2. **Decision:** The ECU computes the **optimal spark advance angle** by interpolating its 3-D ignition map (RPM × load × temperature × knock-sensor feedback).
3. **Dwell phase:** A few milliseconds before the spark instant, the ECU commands the IGBT to **conduct**. Primary current ramps from 0 to approximately 7 A, building up a magnetic flux $\Phi = L_p I_p / N_p$ in the iron core.
4. **Firing:** At the precise computed angle (e.g., 30° BTDC at 3000 rpm), the ECU removes the IGBT gate drive. The IGBT **switches off in ~25 µs**.
5. **High-voltage generation:** The sudden interruption of primary current causes the flux to collapse. By Faraday's law, a high voltage $V_s \approx n L_p I_p / \Delta t$ is induced in the secondary — easily **30 – 40 kV**.
6. **Spark discharge:** Since the coil is mounted directly on the plug (no HT cable), the full voltage appears across the plug gap. The arc is struck, the air-fuel mixture is ignited.
7. **Multi-strike (optional):** Modern ECUs re-fire the coil several times for a single ignition event to extend effective spark duration up to 1.5 – 2.0 ms — beneficial for GDI lean burn.
8. **Ion-sense feedback:** A small ionisation current flows across the plug gap after the spark; the ECU measures it to detect misfire (mandatory for BS-VI / Euro 6 OBD-II compliance).

**Energy stored in the coil at the end of dwell:**
$$
E_L = \tfrac{1}{2} L_p I_p^2 = \tfrac{1}{2} (5\ \text{mH})(7\ \text{A})^2 \approx 123\ \text{mJ}
$$

*(7 marks: 2 marks construction, 4 marks step-by-step working, 1 mark energy equation.)*

#### Part (b) — Advantages over conventional and distributor-type inductive ignition (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

1. **No HT cables** → No leakage, no RFI, no cable capacitance losses. Full coil voltage reaches the plug.
2. **Per-cylinder timing accuracy** of ±0.5° → Better idle quality, lower emissions, better cold start.
3. **Per-cylinder diagnostics** via ion-sense feedback → Misfire detected within one firing cycle.
4. **Eliminates the distributor cap, rotor, and contact breaker** → Zero mechanical wear, no scheduled maintenance.
5. **Compact and modular** → Pencil coils fit into integrated spark-plug wells in the valve cover.
6. **Higher spark energy at the gap** (up to 150 mJ) → Required for GDI lean burn, turbocharged engines, and alternative fuels (CNG, LPG, hydrogen).
7. **Constant, high secondary voltage (up to 40 kV)** at all RPM — even at 8,000 rpm.
8. **Reduced EMI** compared with CDI due to the absence of sharp capacitor-discharge transients.

**Disadvantages (for balance):**
- Higher cost (one coil per cylinder).
- A failed coil = single-cylinder misfire (must be diagnosed and replaced).
- Heat management needed — the coil sits in the hot plug well.
- Plug-well contamination (oil or water) can cause carbon tracking and flashover.

*(7 marks: 5 marks advantages, 1 mark disadvantages, 1 mark concluding statement.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Typically Lose Marks on This Topic:**
> 1. **Confusing CDI and COP energy storage.** *Frequent error:* writing $E_L = \tfrac{1}{2} C V^2$ for COP, or $E_C = \tfrac{1}{2} L I^2$ for CDI. This is an instant **2-mark deduction** in valuation. **Remember:** Capacitor → $\tfrac{1}{2} C V^2$; Inductor → $\tfrac{1}{2} L I^2$.
> 2. **Forgetting the turns ratio in CDI.** Many students write $V_s = V_c$ instead of $V_s = (N_s/N_p) V_c$. Examiners expect the **step-up transformer** action.
> 3. **Skipping the trigger pulse origin.** In CDI, the trigger comes from the **pulsar coil + flywheel magnet**, not from the ECU. Stating "ECU fires the SCR" is **incorrect for a typical bike CDI** and will lose 1 mark.
> 4. **No block diagram in a 14-mark question.** A neat block diagram is worth **2 marks by itself**. Skipping it is a guaranteed -2 hit.
> 5. **Not distinguishing IGBT (COP) from SCR (CDI).** Examiners frequently test the switch type. IGBT is used in COP because of its fast turn-off and ability to handle inductive kick; SCR is used in CDI because it is a "one-shot" switch that latches on.
> 6. **No units in numerical answers.** $E = 0.08$ without "Joules" or "mJ" → -½ mark. Always write **units**.
> 7. **Forgetting the function of the DC-DC converter in CDI.** Many students omit it. Examiners specifically test whether you know that the 12 V must be *stepped up* to 300 – 500 V before charging the capacitor.
> 8. **Mixing up wasted-spark DIS with COP.** Wasted-spark uses *paired* coils (one per pair of companion cylinders); COP uses *individual* coils. Examiners love this distinction.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — KTU Module 3, Distributorless Ignition**

- **Distributorless Ignition (DIS)** = no rotary distributor; solid-state switching only; controlled by ECU with CKP and CMP sensor inputs.
- **Two main sub-types** (KTU Module 3): **CDI** and **Coil-on-Plug (COP)**. Wasted-spark DIS is a third variant.
- **CDI = Capacitor Discharge Ignition.** Energy stored in **capacitor**; released through **SCR**; very fast, high-voltage-rise spark of **~50 – 100 µs** duration; used in **two-wheelers, small engines, racing, magneto-based systems**.
- **CDI energy equation:** $E_C = \tfrac{1}{2} C V_c^2$ (Joules).
- **CDI secondary voltage:** $V_s \approx (N_s / N_p) V_c$ — typically **30 – 50 kV**.
- **CDI key components:** 12 V source → DC–DC converter → HV diode → **storage capacitor (1 µF, 400 V)** → **SCR (thyristor)** → primary of ignition coil → secondary → spark plug. Trigger from **pulsar coil** on the flywheel.
- **CDI spark duration: 0.1 – 0.3 ms.** Very short, very hot.
- **CDI advantages:** constant spark energy at any RPM, plug-fouling tolerance, no maintenance, compact, low cost.
- **CDI disadvantages:** short spark (poor for lean burn), high EMI, complex DC-DC converter.
- **COP = Coil-on-Plug.** Each plug has its **own dedicated coil** mounted directly on top — **no HT cable**.
- **COP energy equation:** $E_L = \tfrac{1}{2} L_p I_p^2$ (Joules).
- **COP secondary voltage:** $V_s \approx n L_p I_p / \Delta t$ — typically **25 – 40 kV** in practice.
- **COP key components:** 12 V → **IGBT** (inside or near coil) → **primary winding** (L_p ≈ 5 mH, I_p ≈ 7 A) → **secondary winding** (turns ratio n ≈ 100) → spark plug. **No SCR** — IGBT is the switch.
- **COP spark duration: 1 – 2 ms** (with multi-strike) — long and sustained, ideal for GDI lean burn.
- **COP sensor inputs to ECU:** **CKP** (crankshaft position), **CMP** (camshaft position), **MAP / MAF** (load), **Knock sensor** (closed-loop retard).
- **COP advantages:** full coil voltage at gap, per-cylinder timing, ion-sense misfire detection, zero mechanical wear, best for GDI/CNG/LPG.
- **COP disadvantages:** higher cost (one coil per cylinder), single-coil failure = single-cylinder misfire, heat management in plug well, possible carbon tracking.
- **Dwell time** in COP: $t_d = -\tau_L \ln(1 - I_{target}/I_{\infty})$ — typically **2 – 5 ms** depending on RPM.
- **Comparison numbers to memorize:**
  - CDI: $C = 1\ \mu F$, $V_c = 400\ V$, $E_C = 80\ mJ$, spark duration $50\ \mu s$, $V_s \approx 40\ kV$.
  - COP: $L_p = 5\ mH$, $I_p = 7\ A$, $E_L = 123\ mJ$, spark duration $1.5\ ms$ (multi-strike), $V_s \approx 30 – 40\ kV$.
- **Wasted-Spark DIS:** One coil per pair of companion cylinders (e.g., 1-4 and 2-3 in a 4-cyl). Both plugs fire simultaneously; only the one on compression stroke does useful work; the other fires "wasted" into exhaust gas.
- **Wasted-Spark = logical subset of DIS** (uses 2 coils for 4 cylinders), cheaper than COP (4 coils for 4 cylinders).
- **Always include in the answer:** block diagram (2 marks), sequence of operation (4 marks), advantages vs. conventional (3 marks), energy equation with units (1 mark). This covers a typical 10-mark question completely.
- **Exam-relevant keywords to use in answers:** "distributorless," "solid-state switching," "ECU-controlled," "CKP/CMP," "SCR / IGBT," "turns ratio," "Faraday's law," "ion-sense feedback," "wasted-spark," "multi-strike."
- **Common-sense final tip:** If a question says *two-wheeler* or *small engine* → answer is **CDI**. If it says *modern car* or *GDI* or *direct-injection* → answer is **COP**. If it says *older 4-cylinder car* or *budget application* → answer is **wasted-spark DIS**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pollutants in IC Engines - NOx
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT205 - AUTOMOBILE POWER PLANT (AUTOMOBILE ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Pollutants in IC Engines - NOx

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Nitrogen Oxides (NOx):** NOx is a collective term used to denote the family of highly reactive gaseous compounds composed of nitrogen and oxygen, predominantly comprising **Nitric Oxide (NO)** and **Nitrogen Dioxide (NO₂)**, with trace quantities of **Nitrous Oxide (N₂O)**. In Internal Combustion (IC) engines, NOx is generated when the diatomic nitrogen (N₂) and oxygen (O₂) present in the combustion chamber react at the **elevated peak in-cylinder temperatures (above ~1700 K)** and **high pressures** prevailing during the combustion process.

According to the **KTU 2024 Automobile Engineering syllabus (PCAUT205)**, NOx is identified as one of the **three principal regulated pollutants** emitted from SI and CI engines, alongside **Unburnt Hydrocarbons (HC)** and **Carbon Monoxide (CO)**. In modern emission standards such as **Bharat Stage VI (BS-VI)** and **Euro VI**, NOx emissions are stringently regulated.

### Chemical Composition of NOx Family

| Species | Chemical Formula | Oxidation State of N | Proportion in Diesel Engine Exhaust | Proportion in SI Engine Exhaust |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Nitric Oxide | **NO** | +2 | ~95 % | ~98 % |
| Nitrogen Dioxide | **NO₂** | +4 | ~5 % | ~2 % |
| Nitrous Oxide | **N₂O** | +1 | Trace | Trace |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Highlight:** Although NO is the dominant species formed inside the combustion chamber, a portion of NO oxidizes to NO₂ in the atmosphere (during the dilution/sampling process). Therefore, **NO₂ is typically the regulated species** for legislative emission limits.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have two **inert, reluctant dance partners** — Nitrogen (N₂) and Oxygen (O₂) molecules — that normally refuse to react with each other at room temperature because their chemical bonds are very strong (the **triple bond in N₂** has a bond dissociation energy of **945 kJ/mol**, one of the strongest in nature).

Now, picture the **combustion chamber of an IC engine** as a *wild nightclub* during peak hours:

- The **fuel droplets evaporating** and the **compressed air** create an environment of intense molecular vibration.
- Once the **peak in-cylinder temperature crosses the critical threshold of ~1700 K (≈ 1427 °C)**, the kinetic energy of the molecules becomes so high that the stubborn N₂ triple bond finally snaps open.
- The freed nitrogen atoms immediately collide with available oxygen atoms (O, O₂, OH radicals) and form **NO** — the precursor of all NOx.
- A fraction of this NO, once cooled in the exhaust stream, reacts further with ambient O₂ to form the **brownish, toxic NO₂**.

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Takeaway:** NOx is essentially a *temperature-and-pressure-driven side reaction*. It is *not* present in the fuel itself — it is **manufactured by the engine** from the air it breathes. This is why **diesel engines (which run lean and hot)** typically produce higher NOx than stoichiometric gasoline engines, all else being equal.

---

## 1.3 Harmful Effects of NOx

> [!WARNING]
> **Health and Environmental Impact:**
> - **Respiratory Ailments:** NO₂ causes severe respiratory irritation, bronchitis, and reduced lung function.
> - **Photochemical Smog:** NOx reacts with Volatile Organic Compounds (VOC) under sunlight to form **ground-level ozone (O₃)**, a primary smog component.
> - **Acid Rain:** NOx combines with atmospheric moisture to form **Nitric Acid (HNO₃)**, leading to acid deposition.
> - **Eutrophication:** Nitrogen deposition causes nutrient overload in water bodies, harming aquatic ecosystems.
> - **Greenhouse Effect:** N₂O is a potent greenhouse gas with a **Global Warming Potential (GWP) ≈ 298 times** that of CO₂ over 100 years.

**Legal Limit Reference (India):** Under **BS-VI norms**, the NOx limit for diesel passenger cars is **80 mg/km** and for gasoline direct injection vehicles is **60 mg/km**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Mechanisms of NOx Formation

The formation of NOx in IC engines is a complex chemical kinetic process. The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly classifies NOx formation into **four primary mechanisms**, each dominating under specific in-cylinder conditions.

---

### 🔬 Mechanism 1: Thermal NOx (Zeldovich Mechanism)

This is the **predominant pathway** in both SI and CI engines and accounts for **~80 % to 90 %** of all NOx generated.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Concept:** At high temperatures, the **N₂ molecule dissociates** into atomic nitrogen, which then reacts with oxygen radicals. This pathway was first proposed by **Yakov Borisovich Zeldovich (1946)**.

#### Extended Zeldovich Reactions:

$$O + N_2 \rightleftharpoons NO + N$$

$$N + O_2 \rightleftharpoons NO + O$$

$$N + OH \rightleftharpoons NO + H$$

**Why is it called "Thermal"?**
- It is **highly temperature-sensitive** — its rate doubles approximately every **70 K** rise in temperature above 1700 K.
- It has a **high activation energy** (~319 kJ/mol for the rate-limiting first step), so only high thermal energy overcomes this barrier.

> [!NOTE]
> **Adiabatic Flame Temperature Limitation:** Since N₂ dissociation is endothermic, the peak flame temperature drops slightly (~50 K) due to the energy absorbed — known as the **Zeldovich cooling effect**.

---

### 🔬 Mechanism 2: Prompt NOx (Fenimore Mechanism)

This pathway accounts for a **minor but non-negligible portion (~5 %)** of total NOx.

#### Reaction Steps:

$$CH + N_2 \rightleftharpoons HCN + N$$

$$N + O_2 \rightleftharpoons NO + O$$

**Why "Prompt"?**
- It is formed **very early in the flame front**, within the first few milliseconds, **independent of high temperature**.
- Critical in **fuel-rich regions** of premixed flames where CH radicals are abundant.

---

### 🔬 Mechanism 3: Fuel-Bound NOx

- Occurs when the fuel itself contains **chemically bound nitrogen** (e.g., heavy fuel oils, coal-derived fuels, certain bio-diesels).
- Generally **negligible in automotive applications** because conventional gasoline and diesel contain negligible nitrogen.

---

### 🔬 Mechanism 4: N₂O Pathway

- Relevant only in **very lean, low-temperature combustion regimes** (e.g., HCCI / RCCI engines).
- The intermediate **Nitrous Oxide (N₂O)** decomposes later to form NO.
- **Emerging importance** in modern low-temperature combustion research.

---

## 2.2 Parametric Effects on NOx Formation (KTU High-Yield)

The **five governing parameters** typically asked in KTU board questions:

| S.No. | Parameter | Effect on NOx | Reason |
|:---:|:---|:---:|:---|
| 1 | **Increase in Peak In-Cylinder Temperature** | **Increases sharply** | Thermal NOx rate is exponentially dependent on T |
| 2 | **Increase in In-Cylinder Pressure** | **Increases** | Higher P → higher adiabatic flame temperature |
| 3 | **Increase in Equivalence Ratio (φ)** up to ~1.05 | **Increases** | Higher flame temperatures in slightly rich mixtures |
| 4 | **Increase in Excess Air (leaner mixture)** | **Decreases** (initially) | Lower adiabatic flame temperature |
| 5 | **Increase in EGR (Exhaust Gas Recirculation)** | **Decreases** | EGR absorbs heat, lowering peak T |
| 6 | **Increase in Compression Ratio** | **Increases** | Higher P and T at TDC |
| 7 | **Retarded Ignition Timing (SI)** | **Decreases** | Combustion phase moves away from TDC |
| 8 | **Increased In-Cylinder Residuals** | **Decreases** | Dilution effect reduces peak T |
| 9 | **Higher Engine Speed** | **Decreases** | Less residence time at peak T |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| S.No. | Formula / Equation | Description | Units / Condition |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|
| 1 | $\dfrac{d[NO]}{dt} = k_1 [O][N_2] - k_2 [NO][N] + k_3 [N][O_2]$ | Zeldovich rate equation | mol/(m³·s) |
| 2 | $k_1 = 1.8 \times 10^{11} \, \exp\left(-\dfrac{38370}{T}\right)$ | Forward rate constant (1st Zeldovich step) | cm³/(mol·s), T in K |
| 3 | $k_2 = 1.8 \times 10^{7} \, T \, \exp\left(-\dfrac{4680}{T}\right)$ | Reverse rate constant (1st step) | cm³/(mol·s) |
| 4 | $k_3 = 1.8 \times 10^{7} \, T \, \exp\left(-\dfrac{2080}{T}\right)$ | Rate constant (2nd step) | cm³/(mol·s) |
| 5 | $T_{adiabatic} = T_{initial} + \dfrac{Q_{comb}}{m \cdot c_p}$ | Adiabatic flame temperature calculation | K |
| 6 | $NO_x \propto \exp\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{R_u \, T}\right)$ | Exponential temperature dependence of Thermal NOx | Arrhenius form |
| 7 | $\phi = \dfrac{(F/A)_{actual}}{(F/A)_{stoichiometric}}$ | Equivalence ratio definition | dimensionless |
| 8 | $GWP_{N_2O} \approx 298 \times GWP_{CO_2}$ | N₂O global warming potential | relative to CO₂ |
| 9 | $E_{a, \text{thermal}} \approx 319$ kJ/mol | Activation energy of Zeldovich NO formation | kJ/mol |
| 10 | $\dot{m}_{NO_x} = V_d \cdot N \cdot \rho_{exh} \cdot C_{NO_x}$ | Mass emission rate of NOx | g/s |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The Arrhenius-type exponential dependence (formula 6) is the *foundation* of all NOx control strategies. Every modern technique — **EGR, water injection, retarded timing, lean burn, SCR catalysts** — ultimately aims to **lower the peak cycle temperature** to suppress the exponential term.

---

## 2.4 Influence of Engine Type on NOx

| Engine Type | Typical NOx Range (without after-treatment) | Why |
|:---|:---:|:---|
| **Conventional SI (PFI)** | 1000 – 3000 ppm | Stoichiometric operation, high T |
| **Lean-Burn SI** | 500 – 1500 ppm | Lower T due to excess air |
| **Conventional Diesel (IDI / DI)** | 300 – 1000 ppm | Lean combustion, but very high local T |
| **Gas Turbine** | 50 – 200 ppm | Highly lean, uniform T |

# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Zeldovich NO Formation Rate

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Zeldovich Mechanism** is mathematically derived using **partial equilibrium** and **steady-state approximations** on atomic nitrogen (N). This derivation is a **favourite KTU board question** worth 5 to 7 marks.

### Step 1: List the Governing Reactions

The three-step extended Zeldovich reactions are:

$$\text{Reaction A: } \quad O + N_2 \xrightarrow{k_1} NO + N$$

$$\text{Reaction B: } \quad N + O_2 \xrightarrow{k_2} NO + O$$

$$\text{Reaction C: } \quad N + OH \xrightarrow{k_3} NO + H$$

### Step 2: Write Net Rate of NO Formation

The net rate of formation of NO is the **sum of rates from all three reactions** that *produce* NO minus those that *consume* it. However, since NO is not significantly consumed under typical combustion conditions (no reverse NO destruction is significant in engine time-scales), we write:

$$\frac{d[NO]}{dt} = k_1 [O][N_2] + k_2 [N][O_2] + k_3 [N][OH]$$

### Step 3: Apply the Quasi-Steady-State Approximation (QSSA) on [N]

The atomic nitrogen [N] is highly reactive and exists in trace concentrations. Its formation and destruction rates are assumed **equal**:

$$\frac{d[N]}{dt} = 0$$

Therefore, formation rate equals destruction rate:

$$k_1 [O][N_2] = k_2 [N][O_2] + k_3 [N][OH]$$

$$k_1 [O][N_2] = [N] \left( k_2 [O_2] + k_3 [OH] \right)$$

Solving for [N]:

$$[N] = \frac{k_1 [O][N_2]}{k_2 [O_2] + k_3 [OH]}$$

### Step 4: Substitute [N] Back into NO Rate Equation

$$\frac{d[NO]}{dt} = k_1 [O][N_2] + k_2 [O_2] \cdot \frac{k_1 [O][N_2]}{k_2 [O_2] + k_3 [OH]} + k_3 [OH] \cdot \frac{k_1 [O][N_2]}{k_2 [O_2] + k_3 [OH]}$$

Factor out $k_1 [O][N_2]$ from the second and third terms:

$$\frac{d[NO]}{dt} = k_1 [O][N_2] + \frac{k_1 [O][N_2] \cdot \left( k_2 [O_2] + k_3 [OH] \right)}{k_2 [O_2] + k_3 [OH]}$$

### Step 5: Simplification

Notice the numerator and denominator of the second term are identical:

$$\frac{d[NO]}{dt} = k_1 [O][N_2] + k_1 [O][N_2]$$

$$\boxed{\frac{d[NO]}{dt} = 2 \, k_1 [O][N_2]}$$

### Step 6: Express k₁ in Arrhenius Form

The rate constant $k_1$ follows Arrhenius behaviour:

$$k_1 = A_1 \, \exp\left( -\frac{E_a}{R_u \, T} \right)$$

Where the accepted values are:

$$A_1 = 1.8 \times 10^{11} \quad \text{cm}^3 / (\text{mol} \cdot \text{s})$$

$$E_a = 319{,}000 \quad \text{J/mol}$$

$$R_u = 8.314 \quad \text{J/(mol} \cdot \text{K)}$$

### Step 7: Final Closed-Form Rate Equation

$$\boxed{\frac{d[NO]}{dt} = 2 \cdot \left(1.8 \times 10^{11}\right) \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{38370}{T}\right) \cdot [O][N_2]}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** This equation reveals that **doubling the O radical concentration doubles the NO formation rate**, while a temperature rise of just **70 K** can **double the rate** due to the exponential term. This justifies why cooling the combustion chamber is the most effective NOx control.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem

> [!TIP]
> **Problem:** Calculate the NO formation rate per unit volume in an engine cylinder when the local temperature is **T = 2200 K**, partial pressure of O is **0.05 atm**, and partial pressure of N₂ is **0.70 atm**. Use the simplified Zeldovich first-step rate constant: $k_1 = 1.8 \times 10^{11} \exp(-38370/T)$ cm³/(mol·s). The total pressure is 1 atm, and we assume ideal gas behaviour.

### Given Data
- Temperature: $T = 2200$ K
- $p_O = 0.05$ atm
- $p_{N_2} = 0.70$ atm
- $P_{total} = 1$ atm
- $R_u = 82.06$ cm³·atm/(mol·K)

### Step 1: Calculate Number Concentrations [O] and [N₂]

Using ideal gas law: $[X] = \dfrac{p_X}{R_u T}$

$$[N_2] = \frac{0.70}{82.06 \times 2200}$$

$$[N_2] = \frac{0.70}{180{,}532}$$

$$[N_2] = 3.877 \times 10^{-6} \quad \text{mol/cm}^3$$

$$[O] = \frac{0.05}{82.06 \times 2200} = \frac{0.05}{180{,}532}$$

$$[O] = 2.770 \times 10^{-7} \quad \text{mol/cm}^3$$

### Step 2: Calculate the Rate Constant k₁

$$\frac{38370}{T} = \frac{38370}{2200} = 17.44$$

$$\exp(-17.44) = 2.74 \times 10^{-8}$$

$$k_1 = 1.8 \times 10^{11} \times 2.74 \times 10^{-8}$$

$$k_1 = 4.93 \times 10^{3} \quad \text{cm}^3 / (\text{mol} \cdot \text{s})$$

### Step 3: Calculate NO Formation Rate

$$\frac{d[NO]}{dt} = 2 \, k_1 \, [O] \, [N_2]$$

$$\frac{d[NO]}{dt} = 2 \times (4.93 \times 10^{3}) \times (2.770 \times 10^{-7}) \times (3.877 \times 10^{-6})$$

Computing the product step by step:

$$4.93 \times 10^{3} \times 2.770 \times 10^{-7} = 1.366 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$1.366 \times 10^{-3} \times 3.877 \times 10^{-6} = 5.296 \times 10^{-9}$$

$$2 \times 5.296 \times 10^{-9} = 1.059 \times 10^{-8}$$

$$\boxed{\frac{d[NO]}{dt} \approx 1.06 \times 10^{-8} \quad \text{mol/(cm}^3 \cdot \text{s)}}$$

### Step 4: Engineering Interpretation

Over a typical **high-temperature residence time of ~2 ms** in the post-flame gases, the integrated NO formed is approximately:

$$\Delta[NO] \approx 1.06 \times 10^{-8} \times 2 \times 10^{-3} = 2.12 \times 10^{-11} \quad \text{mol/cm}^3$$

This value, when integrated over the entire combustion chamber volume, results in **NOx concentrations of the order of hundreds to thousands of ppm** in raw engine exhaust — which is the basis for all modern emission regulations.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: NOx vs Other Pollutants

| Property | **NOx** | **CO** | **HC (Unburnt)** |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **Dominant Formation Zone** | High-T post-flame gases | Quench layer & rich zones | Wall quenching, crevices |
| **Temperature Dependence** | **Strongly exponential** ↑ with T | Decreases with T | Variable |
| **Equivalence Ratio Trend** | Peaks near φ ≈ 1.05 | Peaks in rich mixtures | Peaks in rich/lean extremes |
| **Color/Odor** | Colorless → brown NO₂ | Colorless, odorless | Colorless, hydrocarbon odor |
| **Major Engine Source** | Diesel > SI (lean hot) | SI > Diesel | SI > Diesel |
| **Primary After-treatment** | **SCR, LNT, EGR** | TWC (oxidation) | TWC (oxidation) |
| **Greenhouse Effect** | Indirect (smog) + N₂O is potent | Indirect | Some are GHGs |

# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]**

**Define NOx. List the various chemical species of NOx emitted from IC engines. Explain briefly the Zeldovich mechanism of NOx formation.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** NOx is a collective term for the oxides of nitrogen — primarily **NO (Nitric Oxide)** and **NO₂ (Nitrogen Dioxide)** — formed inside the IC engine cylinder when atmospheric nitrogen and oxygen react at elevated in-cylinder temperatures (above **~1700 K**).

**Chemical species of NOx emitted from IC engines:**
1. **NO (Nitric Oxide)** — major component (~95–98 %)
2. **NO₂ (Nitrogen Dioxide)** — minor component (~2–5 %)
3. **N₂O (Nitrous Oxide)** — trace component

**Zeldovich Mechanism (briefly):** It is also called the **thermal NOx mechanism**. At very high temperatures, the N₂ molecule dissociates into atomic nitrogen, which then reacts with oxygen in three reaction steps:

$$O + N_2 \rightarrow NO + N$$

$$N + O_2 \rightarrow NO + O$$

$$N + OH \rightarrow NO + H$$

The **rate-limiting step is the first reaction**, which has a high activation energy (~319 kJ/mol) and is therefore strongly temperature-dependent. This mechanism accounts for the **majority of NOx** formed in both SI and CI engines.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper 2]**

**Explain the effect of equivalence ratio and peak in-cylinder temperature on NOx formation.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Effect of Equivalence Ratio (φ):** NOx formation increases as the equivalence ratio rises up to a peak value of approximately **φ ≈ 1.05** (slightly rich of stoichiometric), where the **adiabatic flame temperature is at its maximum**. Beyond φ ≈ 1.05 (rich mixture), NOx decreases due to lower flame temperatures caused by incomplete combustion. In very lean mixtures (φ < 0.7), NOx also decreases because of reduced flame temperature due to excess air.

> [!NOTE]
> **Effect of Peak In-Cylinder Temperature:** NOx formation has an **exponential dependence** on peak in-cylinder temperature. According to the Arrhenius form, the rate of thermal NOx formation can be expressed as:

$$r_{NO} \propto \exp\left(-\frac{E_a}{R_u T}\right)$$

A temperature rise of approximately **70 K** above 1700 K can **double the NOx formation rate**. Therefore, any strategy that reduces peak in-cylinder temperature (such as EGR, water injection, retarded timing) effectively reduces NOx.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, ESE Pattern]**

**OR**

### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, ESE Pattern]**

---

#### ✅ QUESTION A (Choice 1) — NOx Mechanisms & Parametric Effects

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, ESE Module 3]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **four mechanisms of NOx formation** in IC engines with relevant chemical reactions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **effect of various engine operating parameters** on NOx formation. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Answer

**(a) Four Mechanisms of NOx Formation (7 marks):**

The four mechanisms are:

**1. Thermal NOx (Zeldovich Mechanism) — ~80–90 % contribution:**
This is the dominant NOx formation mechanism, highly dependent on peak in-cylinder temperature. The extended Zeldovich reactions are:

$$O + N_2 \rightarrow NO + N \quad \text{(rate-limiting)}$$

$$N + O_2 \rightarrow NO + O$$

$$N + OH \rightarrow NO + H$$

The first step is endothermic and rate-limiting due to the **high bond energy of N₂ (945 kJ/mol)**. It requires temperatures above **1700 K**. The rate is expressed as:

$$\frac{d[NO]}{dt} = 2 k_1 [O][N_2]$$

where $k_1 = 1.8 \times 10^{11} \exp(-38370/T)$.

**2. Prompt NOx (Fenimore Mechanism) — ~5 % contribution:**
Formed early in the flame front via reactions involving CH radicals:

$$CH + N_2 \rightarrow HCN + N$$

$$N + O_2 \rightarrow NO + O$$

It is **less temperature-sensitive** and is important in **fuel-rich** premixed flames.

**3. Fuel-Bound NOx:**
Formed from nitrogen chemically bound within the fuel molecule (e.g., heavy oils, coal-derived fuels). Not significant for conventional gasoline and diesel.

**4. N₂O Pathway:**
Important in **low-temperature combustion (LTC)** engines such as HCCI and RCCI:

$$N_2 + O + M \rightarrow N_2O + M$$

$$N_2O + O \rightarrow 2 NO$$

**(b) Effect of Engine Operating Parameters (7 marks):**

| Parameter | Effect on NOx | Engineering Explanation |
|:---|:---:|:---|
| **Peak in-cylinder temperature** | Strongly increases | Arrhenius exponential dependence |
| **In-cylinder pressure** | Increases | Higher P → higher adiabatic flame T |
| **Equivalence ratio** | Peaks at φ ≈ 1.05 | Maximum adiabatic flame temperature |
| **EGR rate** | Decreases | EGR absorbs heat, dilutes charge |
| **Ignition timing (retarded)** | Decreases | Combustion phase moves away from TDC |
| **Compression ratio** | Increases | Higher T and P at TDC |
| **Engine speed** | Decreases (slightly) | Reduced residence time at peak T |
| **Excess air (very lean)** | Decreases | Lower flame temperature |
| **Inlet air temperature** | Increases | Charge at higher T enters cylinder |
| **Inlet air pressure (turbo boost)** | Increases | Higher density → higher T after combustion |

> [!WARNING]
> **Trade-off Reminder:** NOx reduction techniques (e.g., EGR, retarded timing) almost always come with a **fuel economy penalty or particulate increase**. This **NOx-PM-BSFC trade-off** is a critical concept in KTU diesel engine papers.

---

#### ✅ QUESTION B (Choice 2) — Zeldovich NOx Derivation & Control

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, ESE Module 3]**

**(a)** Derive the **rate equation for NO formation** using the extended Zeldovich mechanism. Clearly state the assumptions used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the various **in-cylinder and after-treatment methods** for controlling NOx in IC engines. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Answer

**(a) Derivation of Zeldovich NO Rate (7 marks):**

**Assumptions:**
1. The combustion process is adiabatic and at constant pressure.
2. The atomic nitrogen [N] is in quasi-steady-state (QSSA): $\dfrac{d[N]}{dt} = 0$.
3. NO is not significantly consumed within the engine residence time.
4. Ideal gas behaviour is assumed.

**Reactions:**

$$O + N_2 \xrightarrow{k_1} NO + N$$

$$N + O_2 \xrightarrow{k_2} NO + O$$

$$N + OH \xrightarrow{k_3} NO + H$$

**Net rate of NO formation:**

$$\frac{d[NO]}{dt} = k_1 [O][N_2] + k_2 [N][O_2] + k_3 [N][OH] \quad \cdots (1)$$

**Applying QSSA on [N]:**

$$k_1 [O][N_2] = [N](k_2 [O_2] + k_3 [OH])$$

$$[N] = \frac{k_1 [O][N_2]}{k_2 [O_2] + k_3 [OH]} \quad \cdots (2)$$

Substituting (2) into (1):

$$\frac{d[NO]}{dt} = k_1 [O][N_2] + k_1 [O][N_2] = 2 \, k_1 [O][N_2]$$

The rate constant follows Arrhenius form:

$$k_1 = A_1 \exp\left(-\frac{E_a}{R_u T}\right) = 1.8 \times 10^{11} \exp\left(-\frac{38370}{T}\right)$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\frac{d[NO]}{dt} = 2 \times 1.8 \times 10^{11} \exp\left(-\frac{38370}{T}\right) [O][N_2]}$$

**(b) NOx Control Methods (7 marks):**

**A. In-Cylinder Control Methods:**
1. **Exhaust Gas Recirculation (EGR):** Recirculates a portion of exhaust gas back into the intake. The inert CO₂ and H₂O absorb heat, lowering peak flame temperature.
2. **Retarded Ignition Timing:** Shifts combustion away from TDC, lowering peak T and P.
3. **Water or Methanol Injection:** Water vapor absorbs heat during vaporization (latent heat), reducing peak T.
4. **Variable Valve Timing (VVT):** Adjusts effective compression ratio and internal EGR.
5. **Miller / Atkinson Cycle:** Effective expansion > compression ratio; reduces peak T.
6. **Low-Temperature Combustion (HCCI, RCCI):** Operates at much lower temperatures, drastically suppressing thermal NOx.

**B. After-Treatment Control Methods:**
1. **Three-Way Catalyst (TWC) — for SI engines:** Reduces NO to N₂ using CO and HC as reductants. Works only at stoichiometric A/F.
2. **Selective Catalytic Reduction (SCR):** Injecting **urea (AdBlue)** aqueous solution into the exhaust; NH₃ formed reduces NO/NO₂ to N₂ and H₂O over a catalyst. Most effective for heavy-duty diesel.
3. **Lean NOx Trap (LNT) / NOx Storage Reducer (NSR):** Stores NOx as barium nitrate during lean operation, then reduces it during rich purges.

$$4 NO + 4 NH_3 + O_2 \rightarrow 4 N_2 + 6 H_2O \quad \text{(SCR reaction)}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes KTU Students Make on NOx Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing Zeldovich and Fenimore:** Students often attribute Prompt NOx formation to high temperature. **Correction:** Prompt NOx is formed early in the flame and is **NOT strongly temperature-dependent**.
> 2. **Forgetting Units in the Zeldovich Derivation:** When writing $k_1$, examiners deduct 0.5 marks if units (cm³/mol·s) are missing.
> 3. **Writing only two Zeldovich reactions:** Always write the **extended** three-step Zeldovich mechanism — the third step ($N + OH$) is mandatory for full marks.
> 4. **Confusing NO and NO₂:** NOx regulations target **NO₂**, but inside the cylinder **NO is dominant**. Failing to clarify this costs marks.
> 5. **Ignoring EGR chemistry:** Writing that EGR "reduces temperature" without explaining the **dilution + heat capacity** mechanism is considered incomplete.
> 6. **No mention of BS-VI limits:** Any answer about NOx regulations without quoting current **BS-VI / Euro VI** numerical limits is treated as outdated by KTU 2024 examiners.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **NOx = NO + NO₂ + N₂O**, with NO being the dominant species formed in-cylinder.
- [x] NOx is formed from **air (N₂ + O₂)**, **NOT from the fuel** in conventional engines.
- [x] **Thermal (Zeldovich) NOx** is the **dominant** mechanism (~80–90 %), strongly temperature-dependent.
- [x] The **rate-limiting step** is: $O + N_2 \rightarrow NO + N$ with $E_a \approx 319$ kJ/mol.
- [x] **Prompt NOx** (~5 %) is important in **fuel-rich** premixed flames via CH radicals.
- [x] NOx formation **doubles for every ~70 K rise** above 1700 K.
- [x] **Equivalence ratio peak:** NOx is maximum at **φ ≈ 1.05**.
- [x] **EGR** is the most effective *in-cylinder* NOx reduction technique in diesel engines.
- [x] **SCR with urea (AdBlue)** is the most effective *after-treatment* for heavy-duty diesel NOx.
- [x] **TWC** controls NOx in stoichiometric SI engines; **LNT/SCR** is used for lean-burn and diesel.
- [x] **N₂O** has a **GWP ≈ 298 × CO₂** — relevant for climate impact questions.
- [x] **BS-VI limit:** ~80 mg/km (diesel PC) and 60 mg/km (GDI) — must be cited in regulation answers.
- [x] **Trade-off:** NOx ↓ → PM ↑, BSFC ↑ — the classic **NOx–PM–BSFC triangle**.
- [x] The derived rate expression is: $\dfrac{d[NO]}{dt} = 2 k_1 [O][N_2]$.
- [x] **Henry's Law, Quasi-Steady-State Assumption** on [N] is the key derivation technique.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Unburned Hydrocarbons, Smoke and Particulate
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT205 - AUTOMOBILE POWER PLANT (AUTOMOBILE ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Unburned Hydrocarbons, Smoke and Particulate

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Unburned Hydrocarbons (HC)

### Formal Definition
**Unburned Hydrocarbons (HC)** are the fraction of fuel molecules that escape the combustion process inside the engine cylinder without undergoing complete or even partial oxidation, and are subsequently exhausted into the atmosphere through the tailpipe or the crankcase (via blow-by gases).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Unburned hydrocarbons are classified as one of the three primary regulated pollutant species from SI and CI engines, alongside CO and NOx. They originate from several physical and chemical mechanisms, each contributing a distinct percentage to the total HC mass emitted.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Think of a Bunsen burner in a chemistry lab. If you open the air intake too much, the flame lifts off and unburned gas escapes. In an engine, even with a perfect air–fuel mixture, a small portion of fuel **never gets the chance to burn** because of:
- **Wall Quenching**: A thin film of fuel sticks to the cold cylinder walls and freezes before burning (just like dew on a cold window).
- **Crevice Trapping**: Fuel hides inside narrow gaps (between piston rings, spark plug threads) where flame cannot reach.
- **Oil Layer Absorption**: Fuel dissolves into the lubricating oil film on the cylinder liner and is later desorbed.
- **Combustion Chamber Deposits**: Old carbon deposits absorb fuel like a sponge during one cycle and release it during the next.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric:** Total HC emissions are measured in **grams per kilometre (g/km)** for light-duty vehicles (Euro/BS-VI norms) and **g/kWh** for heavy-duty engines (EPA/CPCB standards). The total mass is reported as **Total Organic Carbon (TOC)** equivalent to **C₃H₈ (propane)** or **CH₄ (methane)** depending on the analyser standard — **FID (Flame Ionisation Detector)** and **NDIR (Non-Dispersive Infra-Red)** being the two principal analysers.

---

## 1.2 Smoke

### Formal Definition
**Smoke** is the visible plume of solid carbonaceous particles (primarily elemental carbon, with adsorbed hydrocarbons and moisture) suspended in the exhaust gas stream, predominantly from diesel engines operating with locally fuel-rich zones during the diffusive combustion phase.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine blowing out a candle — the white-grey wisp you see is essentially **tiny unburnt soot particles**. In a diesel engine, the fuel is injected directly into hot, high-pressure air. The fuel droplets that don't find enough oxygen (because neighbouring droplets have consumed it) undergo **pyrolysis** and form microscopic carbon clusters. These clusters clump together to form **black smoke**, the dark cloud you see behind a truck.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Recognised Colours of Smoke from Engines (Practical Diagnosis):**
> - **Black Smoke** — Incomplete combustion of diesel fuel due to over-fueling, poor atomisation, or low air supply. Mainly **soot (elemental C)**.
> - **Blue Smoke** — Lubricating oil entering the combustion chamber (worn piston rings, valve guides, turbo seal failure). Contains **liquid HC droplets**.
> - **White Smoke** — Unburned fuel or water vapour, common during cold start or due to a leaking head gasket.

### Standard Measurement Metrics
- **Bosch Smoke Number (BSN)** — dimensionless scale from **0 (no smoke)** to **10 (very dense black)**.
- **Hartridge Smoke Unit (HSU)** — opacity-based, scale 0 to 100.
- **Filter Smoke Number (FSN)** — mass of soot collected on filter paper.

---

## 1.3 Particulate Matter (PM)

### Formal Definition
**Particulate Matter (PM)** is the heterogeneous mixture of solid and liquid phase carbon-based particles suspended in the exhaust gas that survives the dilution tunnel, includes elemental carbon (soot), organic carbon (SOF), sulphates, and trace metals, and is regulated by mass (PM) and/or number (PN) under modern emission norms.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
If smoke is the **visible cloud** of a diesel exhaust, particulate matter is the **invisible but measurable** part. PM is what filters catch — it's the **residue you see on a black cloth held behind a tailpipe**. Modern engines emit particles so small (some only 10–20 nanometres) that they pass through your nose hairs and lodge deep in the alveoli of your lungs, causing respiratory and cardiovascular diseases.

> [!NOTE]
> **Size Classification (WHO/EPA Standards):**
> - **PM₁₀** — particles with aerodynamic diameter **≤ 10 µm** (inhalable).
> - **PM₂.₅** — particles with aerodynamic diameter **≤ 2.5 µm** (respirable, alveoli-penetrating).
> - **Ultrafine / PM₀.₁** — particles **≤ 100 nm** (deep-lung penetration, crosses blood–brain barrier).
>
> Diesel engines are the **dominant on-road source of PM₂.₅** in urban environments. KTU students should remember the threshold diameters: **10 µm, 2.5 µm, 0.1 µm**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Sources and Formation Mechanisms of Unburned Hydrocarbons

### A. Wall Quenching (Quench Zone Phenomenon)
When the flame front propagates through the chamber, it is **quenched** within a thin boundary layer (~0.1–0.5 mm) adjacent to the cold chamber walls because:
- Wall temperature is below the auto-ignition temperature of the fuel–air mixture.
- Heat is conducted away faster than the chemical reaction can release it.
- The flame **cannot propagate** in this layer, leaving an annular "dead zone" of unburned fuel.

**Quench Layer Thickness (Heywood correlation):**
$$\delta_q \approx \frac{k}{c_p \cdot \rho \cdot S_L}$$

where:
- $\delta_q$ = quench layer thickness (m)
- $k$ = thermal conductivity of unburned gas
- $c_p$ = specific heat at constant pressure
- $\rho$ = density of unburned gas
- $S_L$ = laminar burning velocity

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Pearl:** Wall quenching accounts for **~38–40%** of total HC emissions in a port-injected SI engine.

### B. Crevice Volume Effect
Narrow gaps in the combustion chamber — piston-ring lands, spark-plug threads, head-gasket crevice, valve-seat crevices — trap a fraction of the charge. As pressure rises during compression, gas is **forced into these crevices**; during expansion, the gas escapes but **fails to burn** because:
- The crevice volume is too narrow for flame propagation.
- The geometry has high surface-to-volume ratio (rapid heat loss).
- The crevice gas becomes fuel-rich, exceeding the lean flammability limit.

**Total Crevice HC Mass (per cycle):**
$$m_{HC,crevice} = \rho_u \cdot V_{crevice} \cdot (x_{crevice} - x_{residual})$$

where $V_{crevice}$ is total crevice volume, $x$ denotes the fuel mole fraction.

### C. Oil Layer Absorption / Desorption
Fuel condenses into the **oil film** on the cylinder wall during compression and the early part of the expansion stroke. As the wall temperature rises, this fuel is **desorbed** and exhausted unburned, often appearing as HC emissions in the later part of the exhaust cycle.

### D. Combustion Chamber Deposits (CCD)
Carbonaceous deposits accumulate on the chamber walls over time, especially in port-injected engines. They act as a **sponge**, absorbing fuel during induction and releasing it during the exhaust stroke, increasing HC mass.

### E. Other Minor Sources
- **Exhaust-valve leakage** past worn guides.
- **Crankcase blow-by** (bypassing piston rings).
- **Fuel-trap effect in intake manifold** during cold start.
- **Incomplete combustion** in locally rich zones.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Four "S" of HC Loss** (mnemonic for exam): **S**urface (wall quench), **S**lot (crevice), **S**ponge (oil/deposits), **S**lip (valve leak).

---

## 2.2 Smoke — Theoretical Framework

### Formation Sequence of Diesel Smoke
The diesel combustion process is a **non-premixed (diffusion) flame** that proceeds in three stages:
1. **Ignition Delay Period** — fuel vaporises and mixes; first auto-ignition occurs.
2. **Premixed Combustion Phase** — fuel–air mixture that formed during delay burns rapidly → pressure rise.
3. **Diffusion (Mixing-Controlled) Phase** — subsequent fuel burns as it mixes with surrounding air. If mixing is incomplete, locally fuel-rich pockets form.

**Smoke forms in the diffusion phase** when:
- Local equivalence ratio $\phi > 2$ (very rich).
- High temperature but insufficient O₂ to convert all carbon to CO₂.
- Carbon undergoes **pyrolysis** → nucleation → coagulation → agglomeration.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pyrolysis Reaction (Soot Precursor):**
> $$C_xH_y + \text{heat} \rightarrow C(s) + \text{volatile gases}$$
> This is a thermal decomposition, NOT a combustion reaction (no O₂ involved).

### Factors Affecting Smoke Emission
| Factor | Effect on Smoke |
|---|---|
| **Air–Fuel Ratio (AFR)** | Lower AFR (richer) → more smoke |
| **EGR rate** | Increased EGR → more smoke |
| **Injection timing** | Retarded injection → higher peak temp → less smoke; very retarded → power loss |
| **Injection pressure** | Higher pressure → finer atomisation → better mixing → less smoke |
| **Nozzle design** | Multi-hole, smaller holes → less smoke |
| **Engine load** | Higher load → more fuel injected → more smoke tendency |
| **Ambient temperature/humidity** | Higher humidity → less smoke (water absorbs heat) |
| **Turbocharging** | Boosted air supply → less smoke |

### Smoke Number Relationships (Bosch Method)
**Bosch Smoke Number (BSN)** is defined as:
$$BSN = 10 \cdot (1 - e^{-0.65 \cdot R_f})$$

where $R_f$ is the **reflectance** of the smoke-stained filter paper (decimal between 0 and 1).

**Equivalent Smoke Opacity (Hartridge):**
$$\text{HSU} = 100 \cdot (1 - \tau)$$

where $\tau$ is the transmittance of the smoke column.

---

## 2.3 Particulate Matter — Detailed Composition

### Composition of Diesel PM
| Component | Mass % (typical) | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Elemental Carbon (Soot)** | 40–60% | Solid carbonaceous core; graphitic clusters |
| **Soluble Organic Fraction (SOF)** | 20–40% | Heavy HC adsorbed on soot surface |
| **Sulphates (H₂SO₄ mist)** | 5–10% | From fuel S → SO₂ → SO₃ → H₂SO₄ |
| **Ash + Metals** | 1–5% | From lube oil additives (Ca, Zn, P) |
| **Water** | 2–5% | From combustion of H₂ |

### Particulate Number (PN) vs Mass (PM)
- **PM mass** (g/km) — measured gravimetrically after dilution tunnel.
- **PN count** (particles/km) — measured using **Condensation Particle Counter (CPC)**; under BS-VI, the limit is **6 × 10¹¹ particles/km**.

> [!NOTE]
> Modern norms (BS-VI, Euro 6) regulate both **PM mass** (e.g., 4.5 mg/km for light-duty diesel) and **PN count**, because mass-only regulation does not capture ultrafine particles.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol / Meaning | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\delta_q = \dfrac{k}{c_p \rho S_L}$ | Quench layer thickness | HC source estimation |
| 2 | $BSN = 10 \cdot (1 - e^{-0.65 R_f})$ | Bosch smoke number | Diesel smoke testing |
| 3 | $\text{HSU} = 100 (1 - \tau)$ | Hartridge smoke unit | Opacity-based smoke test |
| 4 | $N = 6.022 \times 10^{23}$ | Avogadro's number | PN–mass conversion |
| 5 | $PM_{mass} = PM_{core} + SOF + \text{Sulphate}$ | PM composition mass balance | PM speciation analysis |
| 6 | $\phi = \dfrac{(F/A)_{actual}}{(F/A)_{stoich}}$ | Equivalence ratio | Rich/lean classification |
| 7 | $\eta_{comb} = 1 - \dfrac{[HC] + [CO] + [\text{Soot}]}{[Fuel_{injected}]}$ | Combustion efficiency | Engine performance |
| 8 | $F/A_{stoich} = \dfrac{1}{14.7}$ (gasoline) | Stoichiometric A/F | SI engine design |
| 9 | $F/A_{stoich} = \dfrac{1}{14.5}$ (diesel) | Stoichiometric A/F | CI engine design |
| 10 | $L_{el} = 14.6 / (1 + Y)$ | Lapse rate with EGR | EGR vs smoke trade-off |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** For any numerical problem on smoke or PM, always state the **operating condition** (load %, speed, EGR %) and the **fuel type** before applying a formula. Examiners award full marks only when boundary conditions are explicitly mentioned.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Emission Norms Compliance:** HC, smoke, and PM are the cornerstones of **Bharat Stage VI (BS-VI)** and **Euro 6** regulations. A design engineer must ensure the after-treatment system reduces these pollutants below regulatory thresholds.
- **Diesel Particulate Filter (DPF):** Traps PM physically; requires periodic **regeneration** (passive or active) to burn off accumulated soot.
- **Diesel Oxidation Catalyst (DOC):** Oxidises HC and CO to CO₂ and H₂O; reduces SOF portion of PM.
- **EGR (Exhaust Gas Recirculation):** Reduces peak combustion temperature → less NOx, but increases smoke (trade-off).
- **Common-Rail Direct Injection (CRDi):** High-pressure injection (up to 2500 bar) → fine atomisation → reduced smoke and PM.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Bosch Smoke Number (BSN) from Reflectance

### Statement
The Bosch smoke meter draws a fixed volume of exhaust gas through a white filter paper. The stained paper's reflectance $R_f$ is measured. The smoke number is defined empirically as:

$$BSN = 10 \cdot (1 - e^{-0.65 \cdot R_f})$$

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define filter paper reflectance.**
The reflectance $R_f$ of the white filter paper is the ratio of light reflected from the stained paper to light reflected from a clean (un-stained) paper:
$$R_f = \frac{I_{stained}}{I_{clean}}$$

where $I$ is the reflected light intensity measured at a fixed wavelength (typically 940 nm IR).

**Step 2 — Define optical density.**
According to the **Beer–Lambert law**, the absorbance (optical density) of a layer of soot particles is:
$$A = -\log_{10}(R_f)$$

This is dimensionless and represents the light-absorbing capacity of the soot deposit.

**Step 3 — Linear empirical relation between optical density and soot mass.**
Empirical calibration by Bosch (Germany) showed that soot mass per unit area is directly proportional to the absorbance for typical diesel soot:
$$\sigma_{soot} = k \cdot A = -k \cdot \log_{10}(R_f)$$

where $k$ is a calibration constant that depends on soot reflectivity, paper properties, and filter load area.

**Step 4 — Empirically fit a smoke number scale.**
Bosch engineers fit a non-linear empirical curve such that:
- When $R_f = 1$ (clean paper) → $BSN = 0$ (no smoke).
- When $R_f \to 0$ (completely black) → $BSN \to 10$ (maximum smoke).

The chosen functional form satisfying both limits is the **exponential form:**
$$BSN = a \cdot (1 - e^{-b \cdot R_f})$$

**Step 5 — Determine constants a and b from calibration data.**
Applying the boundary conditions:
- At $R_f = 1$ (clean): $BSN = 0$ ✓ (satisfied automatically).
- At $R_f = 0$ (fully black): $BSN = a = 10$ → therefore **a = 10**.

For the constant **b**, Bosch calibrated with a known smoke source. Empirical fitting yielded **b = 0.65** (units: 1/reflectance).

**Step 6 — Final Equation.**
Substituting a = 10 and b = 0.65:
$$BSN = 10 \cdot (1 - e^{-0.65 \cdot R_f})$$

This is the **standard Bosch equation** used worldwide for diesel smoke measurement per **IS 14679 / ISO 10054**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Cross-Check:** For $R_f = 0.5$ (50% reflectance, typical mid-load diesel):
> $$BSN = 10 \cdot (1 - e^{-0.325}) = 10 \cdot (1 - 0.722) = 10 \cdot 0.278 = 2.78 \approx 2.8$$
> This corresponds to "light grey" smoke — physically realistic for a moderately loaded diesel engine.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem: Quench Layer Contribution to HC

### Problem Statement
A four-cylinder SI engine has the following specifications:
- Bore $D = 80 \text{ mm}$, stroke $L = 90 \text{ mm}$
- Clearance volume $V_c = 45 \text{ cm}^3$
- Wall temperature $T_w = 400 \text{ K}$
- Combustion gas temperature $T_g = 2200 \text{ K}$
- Laminar flame speed $S_L = 1.2 \text{ m/s}$
- Thermal conductivity of unburned gas $k = 0.026 \text{ W/m·K}$
- $c_p = 1100 \text{ J/kg·K}$, $\rho = 1.0 \text{ kg/m}^3$ at operating conditions.
- Stoichiometric A/F = 14.7, fuel density = 750 kg/m³.

Calculate the **quench layer thickness** and the **mass of HC trapped in the quench layer per cycle**.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute quench layer thickness $\delta_q$.**
Using the Heywood correlation:
$$\delta_q = \frac{k}{c_p \cdot \rho \cdot S_L} = \frac{0.026}{1100 \times 1.0 \times 1.2}$$

$$\delta_q = \frac{0.026}{1320} = 1.97 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m} = 19.7 \text{ μm}$$

This is the **typical order of magnitude (10–50 μm)** for quench layers in SI engines.

**Step 2 — Compute the chamber wall surface area.**
For a flat-top piston assumption, total chamber wall area = crown + cylinder wall (above TDC) + head + crevice.

Approximating total wetted surface area $A_w$:
$$A_w \approx 2 \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4} + \pi D \cdot h_{c} + \text{head correction}$$

where $h_c$ is the clearance height at TDC:
$$h_c = \frac{V_c}{\pi D^2 / 4} = \frac{45 \text{ cm}^3}{\pi (8)^2 / 4} = \frac{45}{50.27} = 0.895 \text{ cm} = 0.00895 \text{ m}$$

$$A_w \approx 2 \cdot 50.27 + \pi \cdot 8 \cdot 0.895 \approx 100.5 + 22.5 = 123.0 \text{ cm}^2 = 0.0123 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Compute the volume of the quench layer (annular film).**
$$V_q = A_w \cdot \delta_q = 0.0123 \times 1.97 \times 10^{-5} = 2.42 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 4 — Compute mass of gas in the quench layer.**
At TDC, density of unburned mixture $\approx 1.0 \text{ kg/m}^3$:
$$m_q = \rho \cdot V_q = 1.0 \times 2.42 \times 10^{-7} = 2.42 \times 10^{-7} \text{ kg/cycle}$$

**Step 5 — Compute fuel mass fraction trapped in quench.**
For stoichiometric mixture: fuel mass fraction $f = 1/(1 + 14.7) = 1/15.7 = 0.0637$.
$$m_{HC,quench} = m_q \cdot f = 2.42 \times 10^{-7} \times 0.0637 = 1.54 \times 10^{-8} \text{ kg/cycle}$$

**Step 6 — Convert to grams per kilometre (typical emission metric).**
Assuming engine speed $N = 3000 \text{ rpm}$ for one cylinder, four cylinders, vehicle speed $v = 50 \text{ km/h}$:

Total HC per second (all cylinders):
$$\dot{m}_{HC} = 1.54 \times 10^{-8} \times 4 \times (3000/2) = 1.54 \times 10^{-8} \times 6000 = 9.24 \times 10^{-5} \text{ kg/s}$$

Convert to g/h: $9.24 \times 10^{-5} \times 3600 = 0.333 \text{ g/h}$.
Distance travelled per hour = 50 km.
$$HC_{per\_km} = \frac{0.333}{50} = 6.66 \times 10^{-3} \text{ g/km} = 6.66 \text{ mg/km}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity Check:** BS-VI limit for HC+NOx is ~170 mg/km; wall quenching alone contributes only a small fraction. Multiple sources (crevice, deposits, oil) together account for the total.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: HC vs Smoke vs PM

| Parameter | Unburned HC | Smoke | Particulate Matter (PM) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Physical state** | Gaseous (vapour) | Solid (visible particles) | Solid + Liquid (heterogeneous) |
| **Typical size** | Molecular (nm scale) | 0.05–1 µm | 0.001–10 µm (PM₁₀, PM₂.₅) |
| **Primary engine** | SI & CI (both) | Mainly CI (diesel) | Mainly CI (diesel) |
| **Major source** | Wall quench, crevice, oil | Fuel-rich diffusion flame | Incomplete combustion, lube oil |
| **Visible?** | No (odorous) | Yes (black/blue/white) | Mostly NO (sub-micron) |
| **Measurement** | FID, NDIR | Bosch, Hartridge, Opacimeter | Gravimetric (PM), CPC (PN) |
| **Health impact** | Carcinogenic, smog | Local irritation, cancer | Lung disease, cardiovascular |
| **Control tech** | TWC, EGR, EVAP | DPF, EGR, CRDi, retarded timing | DPF, DOC, SCR, low-S fuel |
| **BS-VI limit (LDV)** | 100 mg/km (HC+NOx) | N/A (regulated as PM) | 4.5 mg/km (PM), 6×10¹¹ /km (PN) |
| **Colour of plume** | None (colourless) | Black/Blue/White | None (but opalescent in tunnel) |

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Equivalence Ratio and Smoke Threshold

### Statement
Smoke formation begins when local equivalence ratio $\phi$ exceeds a critical value, often called the **smoke limit** $\phi_s$. For diesel flames, the experimentally observed threshold is:
$$\phi_s \approx 2.0 \text{ to } 2.5$$

### Justification (Physical Reasoning)

**Step 1 — Define equivalence ratio.**
$$\phi = \frac{(F/A)_{actual}}{(F/A)_{stoich}}$$

For diesel at 14.5:1 stoich A/F, $\phi = 1$ corresponds to exact stoich mixture.

**Step 2 — At $\phi > 1$ (rich), oxygen is insufficient for full CO₂ formation.**
For a hydrocarbon $C_xH_y$, complete combustion needs $x + y/4$ moles of O₂ per mole of fuel:
$$C_xH_y + (x + y/4) O_2 \rightarrow x CO_2 + (y/2) H_2 O$$

**Step 3 — If $\phi$ is so rich that O₂ is exhausted before all C is oxidised, residual C appears as soot.**
A simplified elemental balance: if local O₂ supply is less than $(x + y/4)$ per fuel mole, the carbon not oxidised forms soot:
$$m_{soot} \propto (\phi - \phi_s) \quad \text{for } \phi > \phi_s$$

**Step 4 — The threshold $\phi_s$ corresponds to the condition where the local fuel–air mixture just begins to produce carbon particles faster than they can be oxidised by OH and O₂ radicals.**

For a simple sooting flame (e.g., ethylene), the incipient soot threshold is $\phi_s \approx 2.2$. For diesel fuel, $\phi_s \approx 2.0$–$2.5$ due to the heavier aromatic content.

**Step 5 — Engineering implication.**
To **avoid smoke** in a CI engine, the engineer must ensure that even in the most fuel-rich pocket (typically along the spray axis), the local $\phi$ stays below $\phi_s$. This is achieved by:
- Increasing injection pressure (better atomisation).
- Using multi-hole nozzles.
- Increasing swirl/turbulence (faster mixing).
- Optimising spray targeting (avoiding wall impingement).

$$\boxed{\phi_{local, max} < \phi_s \approx 2.0 \text{ for clean (smoke-free) diesel combustion}}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Formation Pathways of Major Diesel Pollutants

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Diesel Fuel Injected] --> B[Auto-Ignition Delay]
    B --> C[Premixed Combustion]
    C --> D[Diffusion Combustion]
    D --> E{Local φ > φ_s?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Soot Formation]
    E -- No --> G[Complete Combustion CO2 + H2O]
    F --> H[Particulate Matter PM]
    F --> I[Black Smoke]
    D --> J[Wall Quench Zone]
    J --> K[Unburned HC]
    A --> L[Crevice Trapping]
    L --> K
    A --> M[Oil Film Absorption]
    M --> K
    K --> N[Exhaust HC]
    H --> O[DPF DPF]
    O --> P[Regeneration]
    P --> Q[CO2 Released]
    I --> R[Visible Tailpipe Smoke]
    G --> S[CO2 + H2O Clean]
    N --> T[TWC Catalyst]
    T --> S
```

## 4.2 Process Flow Diagram: PM Measurement Using Dilution Tunnel

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Engine Exhaust] --> B[Primary Dilution Tunnel]
    B --> C[Secondary Dilution]
    C --> D{CV or CF Mode}
    D -- CV Constant Volume --> E[Filter Paper Collection]
    D -- CF Constant Flow --> E
    E --> F[Gravimetric Weighing]
    F --> G[PM Mass g/km]
    B --> H[CPC Condensation Particle Counter]
    H --> I[PN Number particles/km]
    E --> J[SOF Extraction Dichloromethane]
    J --> K[GC-MS Speciation]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: After-Treatment System for HC, Smoke & PM

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Engine Out Exhaust] --> B[DOC Diesel Oxidation Catalyst]
    B --> C[DPF Diesel Particulate Filter]
    C --> D[SCR Selective Catalytic Reduction]
    D --> E[ASC Ammonia Slip Catalyst]
    E --> F[Tailpipe]
    B -. Oxidises HC and CO .-> F
    C -. Traps PM 99 percent .-> F
    D -. Reduces NOx with NH3 .-> F
    G[EGR Cooler] --> A
    H[ECU Control Unit] -. PM Sensor .-> C
```

## 4.4 Schematic of Wall Quenching and Crevice Trapping

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CY[Combustion Chamber Cross-Section]
        direction TB
        FL[Flame Front] --> W1[Cylinder Wall Cold]
        FL --> W2[Head Wall Cold]
        FL --> W3[Piston Crown Cold]
    end
    W1 -. Quench Zone 0.05 mm .-> FL
    W2 -. Quench Zone .-> FL
    W3 -. Quench Zone .-> FL
    subgraph CR[Crevice Volumes]
        R1[Piston Ring Groove]
        R2[Spark Plug Threads]
        R3[Head Gasket Crevice]
        R4[Valve Seat Crevice]
    end
    R1 --- FL
    R2 --- FL
    R3 --- FL
    R4 --- FL
    QL[Quench Layer Trapped HC] -. Escapes During Expansion .-> EX[Exhaust Unburned HC]
    CR -. Slow Diffusion Out .-> EX
```

## 4.5 Comparison Flowchart: Smoke Colour Diagnosis

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Observe Exhaust Smoke] --> B{Colour?}
    B -- Black --> C[Incomplete Diesel Combustion]
    C --> D[Over-fueling / Low AFR]
    C --> E[Poor Atomisation]
    C --> F[Clogged Air Filter]
    C --> G[EGR Stuck Open]
    B -- Blue --> H[Oil Burning]

    H --> K[Valve Guide Wear]

    L --> N[Leaking Head Gasket]
    L --> O[Injector Tip Failure]
```

## 4.6 Hierarchical Block Diagram: Inter-Relationship of HC, Smoke, and PM

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph SRC[Common Source: Incomplete Combustion]
        direction TB
        S1[Wall Quench] --> UHC[Unburned HC]
        S2[Crevice] --> UHC
        S3[Oil Absorption] --> UHC
        S4[Rich Diffusion] --> SM[Smoke]
        S4 --> PM[Particulate Matter]
        S5[Oil Burning] --> PM
    end
    UHC --> EX[Exhaust Stream]

```
