# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define unburned hydrocarbons (HC) in the context of automobile engine emissions. List any three sources of HC in a spark-ignition engine.**

#### Model Answer:
**Definition:** Unburned hydrocarbons are the fraction of the fuel-air charge that escapes the combustion process without undergoing oxidation, and is exhausted into the atmosphere as gaseous hydrocarbons.

**Three sources of HC in SI engine:**
1. **Wall Quenching** — Flame extinguishes in a thin layer (~0.05 mm) adjacent to the cold chamber walls, leaving fuel unburned.
2. **Crevice Volumes** — Narrow gaps (piston ring grooves, spark plug threads, head gasket) trap fresh charge which escapes unburned during expansion.
3. **Oil Layer Absorption** — Fuel condenses into the lubricating oil film on the cylinder wall and is later desorbed into the exhaust.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Note:** Award full 3 marks only if all three sources are named with a brief physical reason. A simple list without justification scores 2/3.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**Distinguish between "smoke" and "particulate matter" in diesel engine emissions. State two health hazards of PM₂.₅.**

#### Model Answer:
| Aspect | Smoke | Particulate Matter |
|---|---|---|
| Nature | Visible cloud of suspended soot particles | Heterogeneous solid+liquid mixture (often invisible) |
| Size | 0.05–1 µm (visible range) | 0.001–10 µm (PM₁₀, PM₂.₅, ultrafine) |
| Regulation | Smoke number (Bosch/Hartridge) | Mass (g/km) and Number (PN/km) |

**Two health hazards of PM₂.₅:**
1. **Alveolar deposition** — particles lodge deep in lung alveoli, causing chronic bronchitis, asthma, and reduced lung function.
2. **Cardiovascular disease** — ultrafine PM₂.₅ enters the bloodstream via alveolar–capillary membrane, contributing to atherosclerosis, arrhythmia, and premature mortality.

> [!NOTE]
> **Word Limit:** A 3-mark question expects ~80–100 words. Exceed 150 words and the examiner may deduct for irrelevance.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model Paper 2024]**

> (a) **Explain the various sources of unburned hydrocarbons in a spark-ignition engine. With suitable sketches, describe the wall quenching mechanism in detail.** **(7 marks)**
>
> (b) **Describe the principle and procedure of the Bosch smoke number measurement for diesel engines. State the empirical relationship between BSN and filter paper reflectance.** **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Sources of HC and Wall Quenching:**

The major sources of unburned hydrocarbons in an SI engine are:

1. **Wall Quenching (~40%):** A thin layer of unburned mixture adjacent to chamber walls is cooled below the auto-ignition temperature, preventing flame propagation. The thickness of this "quench zone" is given by $\delta_q = k/(c_p \rho S_L)$, typically 0.05–0.5 mm.

2. **Crevice Volume (~30%):** Fresh charge gets forced into narrow crevices (piston-ring grooves, spark plug threads, head-gasket gap, valve-seat) during compression. Flame cannot enter these volumes because of high surface-to-volume ratio and rapid heat loss. The crevice gas escapes unburned during expansion.

3. **Oil Layer Absorption/Desorption (~15%):** Fuel vapor condenses into the thin oil film on the cylinder wall during compression. As wall temperature rises, this fuel desorbs and escapes unburned.

4. **Combustion Chamber Deposits (~10%):** Aged carbonaceous deposits on chamber walls absorb fuel like a sponge and release it in the exhaust stroke.

5. **Other (~5%):** Crankcase blow-by, exhaust valve leakage, cold-start fuel enrichment, incomplete combustion in locally rich zones.

**Sketch of wall quenching** — students should draw a combustion chamber cross-section showing:
- Flame kernel centred in the chamber
- Annular quench zone along all walls
- Arrows indicating flame propagation and heat loss to walls.

**Part (b) — Bosch Smoke Number Measurement:**

**Principle:** A fixed volume of exhaust gas is drawn through a white filter paper. The soot deposits stain the paper; the **reflectance** of the stained paper is measured photoelectrically and converted to the dimensionless **Bosch Smoke Number (BSN)**.

**Procedure:**
1. The engine is run at the specified test condition (e.g., full load, rated speed per IS 14679 / ECE R24).
2. A pump draws a known volume (typically 0.33 L or 1.0 L) of exhaust through the filter paper.
3. The stained paper is placed in a reflectometer, which measures the reflectance $R_f$ (ratio of reflected light intensity to that from a clean paper).
4. The BSN is computed using the empirical equation:
$$BSN = 10 \cdot (1 - e^{-0.65 \cdot R_f})$$

**Boundary values:**
- $R_f = 1$ (clean) → BSN = 0
- $R_f \to 0$ (jet black) → BSN = 10

**Significance:** BSN quantifies visible smoke intensity. Regulatory limits (e.g., BS-VI for HDV) typically demand **BSN ≤ 1.0–1.5 at full load**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. Students often **confuse BSN with HSU** (Hartridge Smoke Unit) — these are different scales.
> 2. Forgetting to mention that the **filter paper volume is fixed** (e.g., 0.33 L) loses 1 mark.
> 3. Writing the BSN equation without stating the **boundary conditions** loses 1 mark.
> 4. In part (a), not drawing a labelled sketch of the quench zone loses 2 marks.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Supplementary Model Paper]**

> (a) **Discuss the formation mechanism of particulate matter in a diesel engine. Briefly describe the components of diesel PM.** **(7 marks)**
>
> (b) **Explain the working principle of a Diesel Particulate Filter (DPF) and its regeneration strategies. Why is DPF regeneration necessary?** **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Formation and Composition of Diesel PM:**

Diesel PM is formed during the **diffusion (mixing-controlled) phase** of combustion. The steps are:

1. **Pyrolysis:** Heavy hydrocarbon fuel molecules in fuel-rich pockets ($\phi > 2$) decompose thermally (without O₂) into smaller fragments and solid carbon (soot nuclei).

2. **Nucleation:** Polycyclic Aromatic Hydrocarbons (PAHs) form and grow into nascent soot particles of size ~1.5 nm.

3. **Coagulation:** Soot particles collide and merge into larger aggregates (~50–500 nm).

4. **Surface Growth:** Acetylene (C₂H₂) and PAH species condense on the soot surface, increasing mass.

5. **Agglomeration:** Multiple soot clusters stick together via van der Waals forces to form chain-like aggregates.

6. **Adsorption:** During exhaust cooling, **Soluble Organic Fraction (SOF)** — heavy unburned HC — condenses onto the soot surface. Sulphates (from fuel sulphur) and metals (from lube oil) deposit as well.

**Components of Diesel PM (mass %):**
- **Elemental Carbon (Soot):** 40–60% — graphitic carbon core.
- **Soluble Organic Fraction (SOF):** 20–40% — heavy HC adsorbed.
- **Sulphate (H₂SO₄):** 5–10% — from fuel S content.
- **Ash/Metals:** 1–5% — Ca, Zn, P from oil additives.
- **Bound Water:** 2–5%.

**Part (b) — DPF Working and Regeneration:**

**Working Principle:** A DPF is a **wall-flow monolith filter** (typically cordierite or silicon carbide) with alternating plugged channels. Exhaust gas is forced through the porous wall, where **particulate matter is trapped** (filtration efficiency > 99%). The cleaned gas exits through the adjacent channel.

**Why regeneration is needed:** As soot accumulates, the back-pressure rises, increasing fuel consumption and eventually choking the engine. The DPF must be **regenerated** periodically by burning off the trapped soot.

**Regeneration Strategies:**

1. **Passive Regeneration:** Occurs automatically during normal driving when exhaust temperature exceeds ~350°C. The DOC oxidises NO to NO₂, which then oxidises soot at low temperature:
   $$2 NO_2 + C \rightarrow 2 NO + CO_2$$
   Requires continuous high-load operation; not always feasible in city driving.

2. **Active Regeneration:** ECU-initiated injection of extra fuel to raise exhaust temperature to ~600°C, at which soot burns:
   $$C + O_2 \rightarrow CO_2$$
   This typically occurs every 300–500 km when back-pressure exceeds a threshold.

3. **Forced Regeneration:** Manual initiation by technician when active regen fails (e.g., due to oil-derived ash accumulation). Higher fuel penalty.

4. **Fuel-Borne Catalyst (FBC):** A catalyst additive (e.g., cerium or iron) lowers soot ignition temperature to ~250°C, allowing more frequent passive regeneration.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Part (b) Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing DPF with DOC** — DOC oxidises gas-phase HC/CO; DPF traps solid PM. Many students mix these up.
> 2. **Not mentioning the back-pressure issue** as the reason for regeneration — at least 1 mark lost.
> 3. **Omitting the chemical equations** for soot oxidation in passive and active regeneration — losing 2 marks.
> 4. **Not labelling the figure of a wall-flow monolith** (alternating plugged channels) — losing 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid-Revision Checklist — HC, Smoke, PM**

### A. Unburned Hydrocarbons (HC)
- ☐ Definition: fuel that escapes combustion unoxidised.
- ☐ Four major sources: **Wall Quench, Crevice, Oil, Deposits** (≈ 40 + 30 + 15 + 10 %).
- ☐ Quench layer thickness: $\delta_q = k/(c_p \rho S_L)$ → typically 0.05–0.5 mm.
- ☐ HC is colourless but causes **photochemical smog + ozone + carcinogens (Benzene, PAH)**.
- ☐ Measurement: **FID** (Flame Ionisation Detector), **NDIR** analyser.
- ☐ Control: TWC (3-way catalyst), EGR, EVAP controls, optimised combustion chamber.

### B. Smoke
- ☐ Three types: **Black (soot), Blue (oil), White (fuel/water)** — diagnostic significance.
- ☐ Mainly from CI (diesel) engines due to diffusion combustion.
- ☐ Smoke forms when local $\phi > \phi_s \approx 2$–2.5.
- ☐ **Bosch equation:** $BSN = 10 (1 - e^{-0.65 R_f})$.
- ☐ **Hartridge:** $\text{HSU} = 100 (1 - \tau)$.
- ☐ Control: DPF, EGR (trade-off with NOx), high-pressure common rail, retarded timing.

### C. Particulate Matter (PM)
- ☐ Composition: Soot (40–60%) + SOF (20–40%) + Sulphate (5–10%) + Ash (1–5%) + Water.
- ☐ Size: PM₁₀, PM₂.₅, PM₀.₁ (ultrafine).
- ☐ Formation: Pyrolysis → Nucleation → Coagulation → Surface Growth → Agglomeration → Adsorption.
- ☐ Diesel engines are the dominant on-road source of urban PM₂.₅.
- ☐ BS-VI limits: **4.5 mg/km PM, 6×10¹¹ /km PN** (light-duty diesel).
- ☐ DPF captures >99% PM; requires **regeneration** (passive at 350°C, active at 600°C).
- ☐ DOC oxidises SOF; lowers visible smoke.

### D. Key Formulas at a Glance
| # | Formula | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\delta_q = k/(c_p \rho S_L)$ | Quench layer thickness |
| 2 | $BSN = 10(1 - e^{-0.65 R_f})$ | Bosch Smoke Number |
| 3 | $HSU = 100(1 - \tau)$ | Hartridge Smoke Unit |
| 4 | $\phi = (F/A)_{actual}/(F/A)_{stoich}$ | Equivalence ratio |
| 5 | $2NO_2 + C \rightarrow 2NO + CO_2$ | Passive DPF regen |
| 6 | $C + O_2 \rightarrow CO_2$ | Active DPF regen |

### E. Frequently Asked KTU Concepts
- ☐ Difference between PM mass and PN count regulations.
- ☐ Why diesel PM is more problematic than SI HC for urban air quality.
- ☐ Why BS-VI mandates **both PM and PN** (mass-only regulation missed ultrafine particles).
- ☐ Trade-off between EGR (less NOx, more smoke) — **NOx–PM trade-off curve**.
- ☐ Role of fuel sulphur content on PM (higher S → more sulphates → more PM mass).
- ☐ Common Rail Direct Injection (CRDi) — high injection pressure (~2000 bar) reduces smoke and PM by improving atomisation and air–fuel mixing.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Strategy for KTU 2024:**
> 1. Always state the **fundamental principle** first, then derive the formula.
> 2. Use **labelled sketches** (combustion chamber cross-section, DPF monolith, smoke meter) for full marks in 7-mark sub-questions.
> 3. For numerical problems, write **boundary values** (R_f limits, φ_s range) explicitly.
> 4. Mention **BS-VI / Euro 6 numerical limits** wherever relevant — it shows industrial awareness and earns "extra" marks from KTU examiners.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Non Exhaust Emissions and Control Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT205 - AUTOMOBILE POWER PLANT (AUTOMOBILE ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Non Exhaust Emissions and Control Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Non-Exhaust Emissions (NEE)** are airborne particulate matter and chemical species released into the environment from motor vehicles through mechanisms that do **not** involve the combustion of fuel in the engine cylinder. Unlike tailpipe (exhaust) emissions — which include CO, HC, NOx, PM, CO₂ — non-exhaust emissions originate from **mechanical wear, frictional contact, fluid evaporation, and system leakage** in the powertrain and chassis.

According to the **IPCC (Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change)** and the **European Environment Agency (EEA)**, non-exhaust emissions constitute the dominant share of particulate pollution from road transport in regions where exhaust after-treatment technology is mature (e.g., Euro 6/VI compliant vehicles). The current scientific consensus classifies NEEs into four primary categories:

1. **Tyre Wear Particles (TWP)**
2. **Brake Wear Particles (BWP)**
3. **Road Surface Wear Particles (RSWP)**
4. **Non-Exhaust Volatile Organic Compounds (NVOC)** — encompassing evaporative fuel losses, crankcase blow-by, and refuelling emissions.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Non-exhaust emissions and control methods form the concluding section of Module 3. Questions typically appear as either a 3-mark direct definition or as a 7-mark sub-part within a 14-mark Part B question on automotive pollution and its mitigation.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine an electric vehicle (EV) silently gliding down a street. Although there is no tailpipe and no combustion, the tyres still grind against the road, the brake pads still rub the brake disc, and the engine oil still evaporates slightly from the crankcase. The dust that rises from the wheels, the fine metallic powder from the brakes, and the faint petrol smell at a refuelling station — **all of these are non-exhaust emissions.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** An EV with zero tailpipe emissions is *not* a zero-emission vehicle. Modern research from the **Empa (Swiss Federal Laboratories for Materials Science and Technology)** indicates that tyre wear alone can release **~100 mg/km of particulate matter per vehicle** — a quantity comparable to the exhaust PM of an older Euro 4 diesel car. This is why NEEs are central to the **Euro 7 / Bharat Stage VII** regulatory roadmap.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value / Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| PM₁₀ (Coarse Particulates) | **≤ 10 µm aerodynamic diameter** | Penetrates upper airways |
| PM₂.₅ (Fine Particulates) | **≤ 2.5 µm aerodynamic diameter** | Penetrates alveoli, carcinogenic (IARC Group 1) |
| Tyre Wear Rate | **50 – 200 mg/km/tyre** | Depends on driving style, load, material |
| Brake Wear Rate | **5 – 20 mg/km/vehicle** | Higher in urban stop-and-go traffic |
| Evaporative HC Limit (SHED Test) | **2.0 g/test** | Euro 4 and later, for typical passenger cars |
| Crankcase HC Limit | **0.0 g/km** | Since Euro 3; PCV system is mandatory |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition — PM₂.₅ (Particulate Matter ≤ 2.5 micrometres):** A complex mixture of extremely small particles and liquid droplets that get deep into the lungs and even into the bloodstream. IARC classifies it as **Group 1 — Carcinogenic to humans**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Major Sources of Non-Exhaust Emissions

### A. Tyre Wear (TWP — Tyre Wear Particles)

Tyres are manufactured from a complex blend of **natural rubber (NR), synthetic rubber (SBR, BR), carbon black, silica, zinc oxide, sulphur, plasticizers, and steel/nylon/rayon reinforcement cords.** As the tyre rolls, two wear mechanisms occur:

- **Mechanical Abrasion:** Frictional shear stress at the road–tyre contact patch (the small flat area, ~150 cm² for a passenger car, where the tyre is in contact with the road).
- **Thermal Degradation:** Localised frictional heating softens the polymer matrix, releasing micro-particles.

The wear rate is governed by the **frictional work** at the contact patch:

$$W_{friction} = \mu \cdot F_N \cdot v \cdot t$$

where **µ** is the coefficient of friction (~0.7 – 1.0 on dry asphalt), **F_N** is the normal load (N), **v** is the vehicle velocity (m/s), and **t** is the time duration (s).

### B. Brake Wear (BWP — Brake Wear Particles)

Brake systems convert kinetic energy into heat via friction between the **brake pad (friction material)** and the **rotor (disc or drum)**. The friction material may be:

- **Semi-metallic** (iron, copper, steel fibres + resin binder)
- **NAO (Non-Asbestos Organic)** — ceramic, aramid, glass fibres
- **Low-metallic** or **ceramic composites**

Brake wear produces both **coarse debris (PM₁₀)** and **ultrafine particulates (PM₀.₁)**. Notably, copper (used in brake pads for heat dissipation) is a **toxic water pollutant** and is being phased out under California's **Copper-Free Brake Initiative** (≤ 5% Cu by weight, dropping to ≤ 0.5% by 2025).

### C. Road Surface Wear (RSWP)

The road itself erodes under tyre and studded-tyre contact. Asphalt surfaces release bitumen-bound aggregates, while concrete surfaces release calcium silicate and limestone particles. RSWP is often **co-collected** with TWP in field studies.

### D. Resuspended Road Dust

Pre-existing dust on the road (from soil, construction, agricultural activity) is lifted by the **turbulent wake** of a moving vehicle — particularly heavy trucks. This is called **non-exhaust traffic-induced resuspension** and is the single largest NEE contributor in dry, arid regions.

### E. Evaporative Emissions (NVOC)

Fuel is a complex mixture of volatile hydrocarbons (C₄ – C₁₂). Loss occurs through four pathways:

| Mechanism | Description | Dominant Phase |
|---|---|---|
| **Diurnal Losses** | Fuel-tank breathing due to daily temperature swings | Parked vehicle |
| **Running Losses** | Vapour generation in hot fuel lines during driving | Driving |
| **Hot-Soak Losses** | Residual heat after engine shutdown vaporises fuel | First 1 hour post-shutdown |
| **Refuelling Losses** | Vapour displacement when liquid fuel enters the tank | At the pump |

### F. Crankcase Emissions (Blow-By Gases)

In a 4-stroke SI engine, a small fraction of the high-pressure combustion gases (containing **unburned HC, CO, and soot**) leaks past the piston rings into the crankcase. This mixture is called **blow-by gas**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Historical Context:** In pre-1970 engines, blow-by gases were simply vented to the atmosphere via a "road draft tube." The **Positive Crankcase Ventilation (PCV)** system — mandated globally since the **1970 U.S. Clean Air Act** — recirculates these gases back to the intake manifold, where they are re-burned in the combustion chamber, achieving **~95% reduction in crankcase HC emissions**.

### G. Fuel System Permeation

Modern polymer fuel tanks and rubber fuel hoses are slightly permeable to hydrocarbon molecules. Permeation rates are governed by **Fick's First Law of Diffusion:**

$$J = -D \cdot \frac{\partial C}{\partial x}$$

where **J** is the molar flux (mol/m²·s), **D** is the diffusion coefficient (m²/s), and **∂C/∂x** is the concentration gradient across the wall.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Tyre wear rate (empirical) | $m_{tw} = k \cdot W_{friction}$ | mg/km | $k$ = wear coefficient |
| 2 | Frictional work | $W_f = \mu F_N v t$ | J | Contact-patch energy input |
| 3 | Brake wear energy | $E_b = \tfrac{1}{2} m v^2$ | J | Per-stop kinetic energy |
| 4 | Particulate mass emission | $E_{PM} = \dfrac{m_{PM}}{d}$ | g/km | $d$ = distance travelled |
| 5 | Fick's diffusion (permeation) | $J = -D \, \dfrac{\Delta C}{\Delta x}$ | mol/m²·s | Through tank wall |
| 6 | Ideal gas (fuel vapour) | $PV = nRT$ | — | Diurnal losses |
| 7 | SHED test (Sealed Housing for Evaporative Determination) | $\Delta C_{HC} \times V_{SHED}$ | g/test | Evap emission quantification |
| 8 | PM size threshold | $d_p \le 10 \;\mu m$ (PM₁₀), $d_p \le 2.5 \;\mu m$ (PM₂.₅) | µm | Health classification |
| 9 | Brake wear (EEA empirical) | $E_{BW} = 7.5 \times 10^{-4} \cdot v^{1.65}$ | mg/km | European fleet average |
| 10 | PCV recirculation efficiency | $\eta_{PCV} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{recirculated}}{\dot{m}_{blow\text{-}by}}$ | % | Typically 90 – 95% |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** The formulas above are used by **automotive OEM emission certification engineers** to type-approve vehicles before market launch, by **tribologists** to design low-wear brake pads and tyre compounds, and by **environmental regulators (CPCB, EU EEA, US EPA)** to set ambient air quality standards (e.g., India's **NAQI — National Ambient Air Quality Index**).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Tyre Wear Mass Calculation

**Problem (KTU Typical 7-Mark Pattern):**
A passenger car has four tyres, each supporting a vertical load of 3500 N. The vehicle travels at 80 km/h on a dry asphalt road for a distance of 100 km. Assume a coefficient of friction µ = 0.85 and a wear coefficient k = 0.04 mg/J. Compute the total tyre wear mass emitted.

### Step 1 — Convert velocity to SI units

$$v = 80 \text{ km/h} = \frac{80 \times 1000}{3600} = 22.22 \text{ m/s}$$

### Step 2 — Compute time of travel

$$t = \frac{d}{v} = \frac{100 \text{ km}}{80 \text{ km/h}} = 1.25 \text{ h} = 4500 \text{ s}$$

### Step 3 — Frictional work per tyre

Using $W_f = \mu F_N v t$:

$$W_{f,\text{tyre}} = 0.85 \times 3500 \times 22.22 \times 4500$$

$$\begin{aligned}
W_{f,\text{tyre}} &= 0.85 \times 3500 \times 22.22 \times 4500 \\
&= 0.85 \times 3500 \times 99990 \\
&= 2.9745 \times 10^{8} \text{ J}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4 — Wear mass per tyre

Using $m_{tw} = k \cdot W_f$ with k = 0.04 mg/J = $4 \times 10^{-5}$ g/J:

$$m_{tw} = 4 \times 10^{-5} \times 2.9745 \times 10^{8}$$

$$m_{tw} = 1.1898 \times 10^{4} \text{ g} = 11.898 \text{ kg per tyre}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sanity check:** The above value is unrealistically high because the empirical formula assumes **all** frictional energy converts to wear. In practice, only a tiny fraction (~10⁻⁴) of frictional energy results in mass loss. The simplified formula $m_{tw} = k \cdot W_f$ is used with much smaller empirical **k** values (e.g., k = 0.05 mg/km/tyre in real wear tests). For exam purposes, the **algebraic structure** is what is graded.

### Step 5 — Total wear for four tyres

$$M_{tw,\text{total}} = 4 \times 11.898 = 47.59 \text{ kg per 100 km}$$

For a more realistic normalised rate:

$$\text{Wear Rate} = \frac{47.59 \text{ kg}}{100 \text{ km}} = 475.9 \text{ g/km per vehicle}$$

This is a **tribological upper bound**; real-world observed TWP is closer to 50 – 200 mg/km/tyre → **0.2 – 0.8 g/km per vehicle**.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Crankcase Blow-By Mass Flow Rate

**Problem:**
A 4-cylinder SI engine running at 3000 rpm has a measured blow-by flow rate of 0.8 L/s at NTP. Blow-by gas composition is: 70% unburned HC, 20% CO, 10% inert gases (CO₂, N₂). Calculate the mass flow of HC vented **without** a PCV system, and the mass flow **with** a PCV system (η = 92%).

### Step 1 — Convert volumetric flow to molar flow at NTP

At NTP: P = 101.325 kPa, T = 293.15 K, R = 8.314 J/mol·K.

Using ideal gas law:

$$\dot{n} = \frac{P \dot{V}}{RT} = \frac{101325 \times 0.8 \times 10^{-3}}{8.314 \times 293.15}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{n} &= \frac{81.06}{2437.4} \\
&= 3.326 \times 10^{-2} \text{ mol/s}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 2 — Molar flow of HC component

$$\dot{n}_{HC} = 0.70 \times 3.326 \times 10^{-2} = 2.328 \times 10^{-2} \text{ mol/s}$$

### Step 3 — Convert to mass flow (assume average HC molar mass = 100 g/mol — typical of gasoline)

$$\dot{m}_{HC} = 2.328 \times 10^{-2} \times 100 = 2.328 \text{ g/s}$$

### Step 4 — Mass flow without PCV (vented to atmosphere)

$$\boxed{\dot{m}_{HC,\text{vented}} = 2.328 \text{ g/s} = 8.38 \text{ kg/h}}$$

### Step 5 — Mass flow with PCV (η = 92% recirculated, 8% leaked)

$$\dot{m}_{HC,\text{leaked}} = 2.328 \times (1 - 0.92) = 0.186 \text{ g/s}$$

$$\boxed{\dot{m}_{HC,\text{leaked}} \approx 0.186 \text{ g/s} = 0.67 \text{ kg/h}}$$

### Step 6 — Reduction in HC emissions

$$\text{Reduction} = \frac{2.328 - 0.186}{2.328} \times 100\% = 92\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A single heavy-duty diesel truck without a PCV system can emit as much as **6 – 8 kg of HC per operating hour** via blow-by. A properly functioning PCV system reduces this by **>90%**, and the recirculated HC is **re-burned** in the cylinder, slightly improving fuel economy.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Sources vs. Control Methods

| NEE Source | Primary Pollutant | Health Impact | Key Control Method | Reduction Efficiency |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Tyre wear | Microplastics, ZnO, PAHs | Lung inflammation | Low-rolling-resistance tyres, silica-rich compounds | 20 – 30% |
| Brake wear | Cu, Fe, Sb, PM₂.₅ | Carcinogenic, neurotoxic | NAO/ceramic pads, regenerative braking | 50 – 80% |
| Road wear | Mineral dust, bitumen | Silicosis risk | Resin-modified asphalt, road sweeping | 30 – 50% |
| Resuspended dust | PM₁₀, PM₂.₅, allergens | Asthma, cardiovascular | Urban greening, dust suppressants | 40 – 60% |
| Diurnal evaporation | HC (C₄ – C₈) | Smog, ozone | Activated carbon canister | > 95% |
| Running losses | HC, benzene | Carcinogenic | Heat-reflective fuel lines | 70 – 90% |
| Refuelling losses | HC vapour | Smog | ORVR (Onboard Refuelling Vapour Recovery) | > 95% |
| Crankcase blow-by | HC, CO | Smog | PCV (Positive Crankcase Ventilation) | 90 – 95% |
| Fuel permeation | HC | Smog | Multi-layer plastic tanks, low-permeation hoses | 70 – 90% |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Taxonomy of Non-Exhaust Emissions (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["NON EXHAUST EMISSIONS NEE"] --> B["PARTICULATE NEE"]
    A --> C["VOLATILE NEE NVOC"]

    B --> B1["TYRE WEAR TWP"]
    B --> B2["BRAKE WEAR BWP"]
    B --> B3["ROAD SURFACE WEAR RSWP"]
    B --> B4["RESUSPENDED ROAD DUST"]

    B1 --> B1a["TREAD ABRASION"]
    B1 --> B1b["THERMAL DEGRADATION"]
    B1 --> B1c["MECHANICAL FATIGUE"]

    B2 --> B2a["PAD DISC FRICTION"]
    B2 --> B2b["ULTRAFINE PM 0 1"]
    B2 --> B2c["HEAVY METAL RELEASE"]

    C --> C1["EVAPORATIVE LOSSES"]
    C --> C2["CRANKCASE BLOW BY"]
    C --> C3["FUEL PERMEATION"]

    C1 --> C1a["DIURNAL BREATHING"]
    C1 --> C1b["RUNNING LOSSES"]
    C1 --> C1c["HOT SOAK"]
    C1 --> C1d["REFUELLING VAPOUR"]

    C2 --> C2a["PISTON RING LEAK"]
    C2 --> C2b["PCV RECIRCULATION"]

    C3 --> C3a["POLYMER TANK DIFFUSION"]
    C3 --> C3b["HOSE PERMEATION"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The root node `A` (NON EXHAUST EMISSIONS NEE) branches into two primary pillars: **Particulate NEE** (left, blue path) and **Volatile NEE / NVOC** (right, orange path). Each sub-branch isolates the exact mechanism, providing a rapid revision map for the exam.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: EVAP Control System (ORVR + Canister)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["FUEL TANK"] --> B["FUEL VAPOUR SPACE"]
    B --> C["ACTIVATED CARBON CANISTER"]
    C --> D["PURGE VALVE"]
    D --> E["INTAKE MANIFOLD"]
    E --> F["ENGINE COMBUSTION"]
    F --> G["EXHAUST AFTER TREATMENT"]
    G --> H["TAILPIPE EMISSION REDUCED"]
    C --> I["FRESH AIR INLET FILTERED"]
    I --> C
    J["REFUELLING NOZZLE VAPOUR"] --> K["ORVR VAPOUR RECOVERY LINE"]
    K --> C
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Functional Flow:** (1) Vapours from the fuel tank migrate to the **activated carbon canister** during diurnal and hot-soak phases. (2) The canister adsorbs HC molecules on its microporous carbon surface. (3) During engine operation, the **purge valve** opens, drawing fresh air through the canister, desorbing the trapped HC and routing it to the **intake manifold** for combustion. (4) During refuelling, vapour displaced from the tank is captured by the **ORVR line** and sent back to the canister — preventing it from escaping to the atmosphere.

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Positive Crankcase Ventilation (PCV) System

```mermaid
graph TD
    P1["COMBUSTION CHAMBER HIGH PRESSURE GAS"] --> P2["PAST PISTON RINGS BLOW BY"]
    P2 --> P3["CRANKCASE VAPOUR MIST"]
    P3 --> P4["PCV VALVE CALIBRATED ORIFICE"]
    P4 --> P5["INTAKE MANIFOLD LOW PRESSURE ZONE"]
    P5 --> P6["REBURNED IN CYLINDER"]
    P3 --> P7["OIL SEPARATOR"]
    P7 --> P3
    P3 --> P8["BREATHER HOSE TO AIR FILTER"]
    P8 --> P3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Note:** The PCV valve is a **spring-loaded, vacuum-operated** device. At idle, high manifold vacuum lifts the valve partially closed, limiting flow. At wide-open throttle, low vacuum allows the valve to open fully. This modulates crankcase pressure and prevents oil foaming.

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Hierarchy of NEE Control Strategies

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1["SOURCE REDUCTION UPSTREAM"] --> S1a["LOW WEAR TYRE COMPOUND"]
    S1 --> S1b["CERAMIC BRAKE PADS"]
    S1 --> S1c["MULTI LAYER FUEL TANKS"]
    S2["SYSTEM INTEGRATION MIDSTREAM"] --> S2a["PCV SYSTEM"]
    S2 --> S2b["EVAP CANISTER ORVR"]
    S2 --> S2c["REGENERATIVE BRAKING EV"]
    S3["END OF PIPE DOWNSTREAM"] --> S3a["DUST SUPPRESSANT SPRAY"]
    S3 --> S3b["ROAD SWEEPING"]
    S3 --> S3c["TYRE AND BRAKE WEAR FILTRATION FUTURE"]
    S1 --> S2
    S2 --> S3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Regulatory Roadmap:** The European **Euro 7 norm (proposed for 2025 – 2027)** and India's **Bharat Stage VII** will, for the first time, set **non-exhaust PM limits** for brake wear (5 mg/km) and tyre wear targets. This is a paradigm shift — NEEs will be regulated like tailpipe emissions.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Define non-exhaust emissions. List four major sources of NEE from an automobile.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Non-exhaust emissions (NEE) are airborne pollutants released from motor vehicles through **mechanical wear, fluid evaporation, and system leakage** that do not involve combustion of fuel. Unlike tailpipe emissions, NEEs are present even in electric vehicles.

**Four major sources (1.5 marks):**

1. **Tyre wear particles (TWP)** — released from the road–tyre contact patch.
2. **Brake wear particles (BWP)** — produced by friction between brake pad and disc.
3. **Road surface wear particles (RSWP)** — bitumen, aggregate dust from the road.
4. **Evaporative emissions** — HC vapour losses from the fuel system (diurnal, running, hot-soak, refuelling).

*Alternative valid sources (accept any 2 more):* Crankcase blow-by, fuel permeation, resuspended road dust.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is a Positive Crankcase Ventilation (PCV) system? Why is it necessary?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **PCV system** is an emission-control device that **recirculates blow-by gases** (combustion gases that leak past the piston rings into the crankcase) back into the engine's intake manifold, where they are re-burned in the combustion chamber. (2 marks)

**Necessity (1 mark):**
- Blow-by gases contain **unburned hydrocarbons (HC) and CO**, which contribute to photochemical smog and ground-level ozone.
- Without a PCV system, these gases would be vented to the atmosphere via a road-draft tube, violating modern emission norms (Euro 3 onwards and Bharat Stage III mandate PCV).
- A PCV system achieves **~90 – 95% reduction in crankcase HC emissions** and slightly improves fuel economy.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]**

> **Q. (a)** Explain the various sources of evaporative emissions from a motor vehicle. With a neat sketch, describe the working of an activated carbon canister used in the evaporative emission control system. **(7 marks)**
>
> **Q. (b)** What is crankcase blow-by? Explain the construction and working of a Positive Crankcase Ventilation (PCV) system. List its advantages. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Q(a) — 7 Marks

**Sources of Evaporative Emissions (3 marks):**

| # | Source | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Diurnal losses** | Temperature cycles (day/night) cause fuel tank to "breathe" — HC vapour expelled through venting |
| 2 | **Running losses** | Heat from engine and exhaust manifold vaporises fuel in lines and rail |
| 3 | **Hot-soak losses** | After engine shut-off, residual heat vaporises fuel for ~1 hour |
| 4 | **Refuelling losses** | Liquid fuel entering tank displaces saturated vapour to atmosphere |
| 5 | **Permeation** | HC molecules diffuse through polymer fuel tank walls and rubber hoses |

**Working of Activated Carbon Canister (3 marks):**

```
[Fuel Tank Vapour] → [Vapour Line] → [Activated Carbon Canister]
                                          ↓ (adsorbed on carbon)
                                    [Carbon Bed: HC trapped]
                                          ↓
                       [Purge Line with ECU-controlled Purge Valve]
                                          ↓
                                  [Intake Manifold]
                                          ↓
                              [Re-burned in Cylinder]
```

**Operating Phases:**

1. **Loading (engine off):** Vapours from the fuel tank enter the canister and are **adsorbed** onto the high-surface-area activated carbon (~1000 m²/g). The canister also has a **fresh-air vent** to allow displaced air to escape.
2. **Purge (engine on, closed loop):** When the engine is warm and under part-load, the **ECU opens the purge valve**. Intake manifold vacuum draws fresh air backwards through the canister, **desorbing** the trapped HC and routing it to the intake manifold.
3. **Regeneration (saturated canister):** Over multiple drive cycles, the canister is fully purged and ready to adsorb again.

**Significance (1 mark):** Reduces evaporative HC loss from ~5 – 10 g/day (uncontrolled) to < 0.05 g/day — a **> 95% reduction**, meeting Euro 4 / BS-IV SHED test limits of 2 g/test.

**Sketch (use the Mermaid block from Section 4.2 above).**

---

#### Solution to Q(b) — 7 Marks

**Definition of Blow-By (1 mark):**
Blow-by refers to the leakage of high-pressure combustion gases from above the piston (combustion chamber) past the **piston rings** into the crankcase. It increases with ring wear, high cylinder pressure (diesel > petrol), and high engine speed.

**Composition:** Unburned HC (~70%), CO (~20%), CO₂, N₂, soot, oil mist.

**Construction of PCV System (3 marks):**
- **PCV valve** (spring-loaded, vacuum-modulated) — calibrated orifice between crankcase and intake manifold.
- **Breather hose** — connects crankcase to the **air cleaner** (or air filter housing) for fresh air inlet.
- **Oil separator / baffle** — removes oil droplets from blow-by gas before they enter the intake.

**Working (2 marks):**
1. **At idle (high manifold vacuum ~ –60 kPa):** The PCV valve is **partially closed** by spring force, restricting flow and preventing excessively lean mixtures.
2. **At cruise / WOT (low vacuum ~ –20 kPa):** The valve **opens fully** under manifold vacuum, allowing crankcase vapours to be drawn into the intake and re-burned.
3. Fresh air enters via the breather hose, **sweeps** the crankcase, and carries vapours to the PCV valve.
4. The system maintains crankcase at **slight negative pressure**, preventing oil leaks.

**Advantages (1 mark):**
- Reduces crankcase HC by **90 – 95%**.
- Slightly improves fuel economy (re-burned HC).
- Prevents sludge formation by removing moisture and combustion by-products.
- Extends oil life.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — **Internal Choice Alternative** **[KTU University Exam – June 2023 Pattern]**

> **Q. (a)** Explain the various sources of non-exhaust particulate emissions from a road vehicle. Discuss the factors affecting tyre wear rate. **(7 marks)**
>
> **Q. (b)** With a block diagram, describe the working of an Onboard Refuelling Vapour Recovery (ORVR) system. How does it differ from a stage-II vapour recovery system at the fuel station? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Q(a) — 7 Marks

**Sources of Non-Exhaust Particulate Emissions (3 marks):**

1. **Tyre wear particles (TWP):** Generated at the road–tyre contact patch; comprise tread rubber, carbon black, silica, ZnO, and small amounts of **polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs)**.
2. **Brake wear particles (BWP):** From friction between pad and disc; contain **Cu, Fe, Sb, Ba, ceramic fibres**.
3. **Road surface wear (RSWP):** Asphalt, bitumen, aggregates, concrete dust.
4. **Resuspended road dust:** Pre-existing dust lifted by vehicle-induced turbulence.
5. **Construction material emissions** (pavement abrasion, markings).

**Factors Affecting Tyre Wear Rate (4 marks):**

| # | Factor | Effect on Wear |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Tyre material / compound** | Higher silica content → lower rolling resistance & wear; softer rubber → higher wear |
| 2 | **Vehicle load (F_N)** | Wear ∝ normal force; overloaded tyres wear faster |
| 3 | **Driving speed (v)** | Higher speed → more frictional work → higher wear |
| 4 | **Driving style** | Hard acceleration, braking, cornering → spike in wear (can be 100× baseline) |
| 5 | **Road surface texture** | Rougher surfaces (e.g., chip-seal) → 2 – 3× higher wear than smooth asphalt |
| 6 | **Tyre pressure** | Under-inflation → shoulder wear; over-inflation → centre wear, both increase mass loss |
| 7 | **Temperature** | Hot road surfaces soften rubber → accelerated wear |
| 8 | **Vehicle alignment** | Misalignment causes uneven, accelerated wear |

**Key equation:** $m_{tw} \propto \mu \cdot F_N \cdot v \cdot t$ (frictional work).

---

#### Solution to Q(b) — 7 Marks

**Working of ORVR (Onboard Refuelling Vapour Recovery) (4 marks):**

The ORVR system is an **integrated vehicle-mounted** system that captures fuel vapours displaced during refuelling. The system consists of:

- A **vapour recovery line** running from the fuel tank to the **fuel filler neck**.
- A **vapour storage canister** (large activated carbon canister, often co-located with the evaporative canister).
- A **fill-limiting valve** in the tank to prevent over-filling and liquid fuel ingress into the vapour line.
- A **vapour-recovery nozzle interface** compatible with the dispensing pump.

**Operating Sequence:**

1. When the refuelling nozzle is inserted into the filler neck, a **vapour seal** is formed.
2. As liquid fuel enters the tank, **displaced vapour** flows through the vapour-recovery line to the carbon canister.
3. The canister **adsorbs** the HC vapour.
4. Later, during engine operation, the **purge valve** opens and the trapped HC is sent to the intake manifold for re-burning.

**ORVR vs. Stage-II Vapour Recovery at Fuel Station (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Stage-II (Station-side) | ORVR (Vehicle-side) |
|---|---|---|
| **Location** | Vapour pump at the fuel station | Integrated in the vehicle |
| **Vapour destination** | Returned to station's underground storage tank | Stored in vehicle's carbon canister |
| **Equipment** | Special vapour-recovery nozzle & pump | Vehicle filler-neck + canister + ECU |
| **Cost burden** | Borne by fuel station operator | Borne by vehicle manufacturer |
| **Emission capture** | ~ 70 – 85% | > 95% (more efficient) |
| **Maintenance** | Frequent calibration of station equipment | Self-maintained by vehicle |
| **Regulation** | Mandated in CPCB Stage-II cities (Delhi, Mumbai) | Will be mandatory under Euro 7 / BS-VII |

**Why ORVR is superior:** It is **closed-loop** (no atmospheric loss at any point), more efficient, and shifts the responsibility to OEMs who can engineer it as a tightly integrated module.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
>
> 1. **Conflating "crankcase emissions" with "evaporative emissions":** Crankcase emissions come from **blow-by gases past piston rings**; evaporative emissions come from **fuel tank and fuel-line vapour losses**. Examiners deduct **1 – 2 marks** for this confusion.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to draw the labelled diagram:** In 7-mark PCV/canister questions, **3 marks are typically reserved for a clearly labelled sketch**. A textual answer without a diagram is capped.
>
> 3. **Stating "PCV reduces emissions by 100%":** PCV efficiency is **90 – 95%**, never 100%. Saying 100% is technically wrong and shows rote memorisation.
>
> 4. **Missing the 4 sub-types of evaporative losses:** Diurnal, running, hot-soak, and refuelling — students often list only 2 or 3, losing 1 mark.
>
> 5. **Ignoring non-exhaust emissions in EV context:** Examiners are increasingly framing NEE questions around EVs ("an EV has no tailpipe, so is it zero-emission?"). A complete answer must address **tyre + brake wear + tyre dust + brake dust** for EVs.
>
> 6. **Unit errors in numerical problems:** Mixing km/h with m/s, mg with g. Always **convert to SI units first** and write units at every step.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **HIGH-YIELD REVISION CHECKLIST — NON-EXHAUST EMISSIONS (Module 3, Unit 5)**

### 📌 Core Concepts to Remember
- **NEE = Non-Exhaust Emissions** = all pollutants from a vehicle **not** from the tailpipe (combustion).
- Four main categories: **TWP, BWP, RSWP, NVOC** (evaporative + crankcase + permeation).
- NEE is **dominant in EVs** and modern Euro 6/BS-VI ICE vehicles (exhaust is now near-zero).

### 📌 Tyre Wear (TWP)
- Composed of tread rubber, **carbon black, silica, ZnO, PAHs**.
- Wear rate ≈ **50 – 200 mg/km/tyre**.
- **Controllable by:** silica-rich compounds, low-rolling-resistance design, correct inflation, smooth driving.

### 📌 Brake Wear (BWP)
- Friction between pad and disc; produces **PM₂.₅, PM₀.₁, Cu, Sb**.
- Copper is **being phased out** (California: ≤ 0.5% by 2025).
- **Controllable by:** ceramic/NAO pads, regenerative braking (EVs and hybrids), brake-by-wire.

### 📌 Evaporative Emissions (4 types)
1. **Diurnal** — daily temperature cycle.
2. **Running** — engine-driven heat.
3. **Hot-soak** — residual heat after shutdown.
4. **Refuelling** — vapour displacement at the pump.

**Control:** **Activated carbon canister** (adsorption/desorption cycle), **ORVR** for refuelling losses.

### 📌 Crankcase Emissions (Blow-By)
- Past piston rings: **unburned HC, CO**.
- **Control: PCV system** (recirculation to intake).
- Mandatory since Euro 3 / BS-III. Efficiency **90 – 95%**.

### 📌 Permeation
- Fick's Law: $J = -D \, \dfrac{\Delta C}{\Delta x}$.
- **Control:** Multi-layer plastic tanks (HDPE with EVOH barrier), low-permeation rubber hoses (FKM, HNBR).

### 📌 Regulatory Future
- **Euro 7** (2025 – 2027): First norm to set NEE PM limits.
- **Bharat Stage VII**: India expected to follow.

### 📌 Key Formulas to Memorise
- $W_f = \mu F_N v t$ — frictional work.
- $m_{tw} = k \cdot W_f$ — tyre wear mass.
- $J = -D \, \dfrac{\Delta C}{\Delta x}$ — permeation (Fick's Law).
- $\dot{n} = \dfrac{P\dot{V}}{RT}$ — molar flow of vapour.

### 📌 High-Frequency KTU Question Triggers
- "Explain the working of PCV system" — 7 marks.
- "Sketch and explain activated carbon canister" — 7 marks.
- "List sources of non-exhaust emissions" — 3 marks.
- "Compare ORVR and Stage-II vapour recovery" — 7 marks.

topics.title: Exhaust Gas Treatment - Catalytic Converter, Thermal Reactor, Particulate Trap
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT205 - AUTOMOBILE POWER PLANT (AUTOMOBILE ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Exhaust Gas Treatment - Catalytic Converter, Thermal Reactor, Particulate Trap

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Exhaust Gas Treatment — Formal Academic Definition

**Exhaust Gas Treatment** refers to the suite of after-treatment devices integrated into the exhaust system of an internal combustion (IC) engine to reduce the concentration of harmful gaseous and particulate emissions (CO, HC, NOx, PM, SOx) before they are released into the atmosphere. These systems are mandated by progressively stringent emission norms such as **Bharat Stage VI (BS-VI)**, Euro 6, and EPA Tier 3.

The three principal after-treatment devices studied under this syllabus are:

1. **Catalytic Converter** — A chemical reactor that uses catalysts (Pt, Pd, Rh) to convert CO, HC, and NOx into harmless products (CO₂, H₂O, N₂).
2. **Thermal Reactor** — A combustion chamber that maintains a high, sustained temperature to oxidize CO and HC using secondary air injection.
3. **Particulate Trap (DPF)** — A physical filter that mechanically captures soot and particulate matter (PM) from diesel exhaust.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This topic is a high-weightage topic for Module 3 and is typically tested as a 14-mark Part B question involving descriptive explanation, neat sketches, and comparison-based sub-parts.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Block — The "Hospital for Exhaust"**
> Think of an engine's exhaust manifold as a **patient exiting an emergency ward** — still carrying many "toxins" (CO, HC, NOx, soot). The exhaust pipe then routes this patient through three specialized "treatment rooms":
>
> - **The Catalytic Converter is the "Chemistry Lab"** — it does not burn the harmful gases; instead, it uses a thin coating of precious metals (platinum, palladium, rhodium) as "magic helpers" to transform them into harmless gases (CO₂, H₂O, N₂) at a much lower temperature than normal combustion would require.
> - **The Thermal Reactor is the "Sauna"** — it simply keeps the exhaust very hot for a longer time and pumps in extra fresh air so that CO and HC are fully burned (oxidized) into harmless products. It works on brute-force thermal oxidation.
> - **The Particulate Trap is the "Strainer/Coffee Filter"** — the dirty exhaust is forced through a porous ceramic wall. Soot particles get physically trapped in the wall pores, while clean gas flows through. Periodically, the trap is cleaned (regenerated) by burning off the trapped soot.
>
> The **standard legislative reference** for permissible emission levels in India is the **Central Motor Vehicle Rules (CMVR), 1989** as amended by **G.S.R. 889(E)** for BS-VI norms, with **CO ≤ 1.0 g/km**, **HC + NOx ≤ 0.16 g/km** for petrol passenger cars (under WLTP-equivalent test).

---

## 1.3 Why These Systems Are Necessary

| Pollutant | Source | Health Impact | Regulatory Limit (BS-VI, Petrol) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **CO** (Carbon Monoxide) | Incomplete combustion of fuel | Binds with haemoglobin (200× stronger than O₂) → hypoxia | ≤ 1.0 g/km |
| **HC** (Unburnt Hydrocarbons) | Flame quenching at walls, crevices | Carcinogenic (e.g., benzene), smog formation | ≤ 0.10 g/km |
| **NOx** (Oxides of Nitrogen) | High in-cylinder temperature (> 1500 K) | Acid rain, respiratory ailments, photochemical smog | ≤ 0.06 g/km |
| **PM** (Particulate Matter) | Especially in diesel — soot, soluble organics | Lung cancer, cardiovascular disease | ≤ 0.0045 g/km (diesel) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The "Three-Way Catalytic Converter (TWC)" simultaneously oxidizes CO and HC **and** reduces NOx using a single honeycomb monolith — making it the cornerstone of modern petrol-emission control.

---

## 1.4 Key Operating Parameter — Light-Off Temperature

> [!NOTE]
> **Light-Off Temperature (T₅₀%)**: The exhaust temperature at which the catalyst achieves **50% conversion efficiency** of the target pollutant.
>
> - Pt/Pd/Rh washcoat: T₅₀% ≈ **250 °C – 300 °C**
> - Modern Pd-Rh TWC: T₅₀% ≈ **220 °C**
> - Operating window for full conversion: **400 °C – 800 °C**

Below the light-off temperature, the catalyst is essentially inactive. This is why modern cars use **cold-start strategies** (HC traps, electrically heated catalysts) to bring the converter up to operating temperature quickly.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Catalytic Converter — Operational Principle

The catalytic converter is a **fixed-bed chemical reactor** placed in the exhaust stream. Its working is governed by three functional layers:

1. **Substrate (Carrier)** — A ceramic (cordierite, 2MgO·2Al₂O₃·5SiO₂) or metal (Fe-Cr-Al) honeycomb monolith with **400–600 cells per square inch (CPSI)**. The honeycomb provides a large geometric surface area (≥ 2 m² per litre of catalyst volume) while keeping exhaust back-pressure low.
2. **Washcoat** — A porous alumina (γ-Al₂O₃) layer impregnated onto the substrate. The washcoat increases the effective surface area to **~ 20,000 m²/g**.
3. **Active Catalyst** — A thin layer of **precious metals**:
   - **Platinum (Pt)** and **Palladium (Pd)** → Oxidation catalysts (CO, HC → CO₂, H₂O)
   - **Rhodium (Rh)** → Reduction catalyst (NOx → N₂)

### 2.1.1 Chemical Reactions Inside a Three-Way Catalytic Converter (TWC)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Oxidation:} \quad & 2\text{CO} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{CO}_2 \\
& \text{C}_x\text{H}_y + \left(x + \tfrac{y}{4}\right)\text{O}_2 \rightarrow x\text{CO}_2 + \tfrac{y}{2}\text{H}_2\text{O} \\
& 2\text{H}_2 + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \\
\text{Reduction:} \quad & 2\text{NO} \rightarrow \text{N}_2 + \text{O}_2 \\
& 2\text{NO} + 2\text{CO} \rightarrow \text{N}_2 + 2\text{CO}_2 \\
& (2x + \tfrac{y}{2})\text{NO} + \text{C}_x\text{H}_y \rightarrow (x + \tfrac{y}{4})\text{N}_2 + x\text{CO}_2 + \tfrac{y}{2}\text{H}_2\text{O}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 2.1.2 The "Lambda Window" (Air-Fuel Ratio Sensitivity)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The TWC is highly effective only when the air-fuel ratio is maintained within a very narrow band called the **Lambda Window (λ = 1 ± 0.01)**.
>
> - **λ < 1 (Rich mixture):** Excess fuel → high CO, HC, low O₂ → reduction reactions dominate but oxidation is starved.
> - **λ > 1 (Lean mixture):** Excess air → low CO, HC but high NOx (high in-cylinder temperature); also catalyst overheating.
> - **λ = 1 ± 0.01 (Stoichiometric):** All three pollutants converted simultaneously with > 98% efficiency.

A **closed-loop oxygen sensor (Lambda probe / O₂ sensor)** — typically a ZrO₂ galvanic cell — placed upstream of the catalyst provides feedback to the ECU, which then modulates fuel injection to maintain λ = 1.00 ± 0.005.

### 2.1.3 Types of Catalytic Converters

| Type | Function | Catalysts | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Two-Way (Oxidation)** | Oxidation of CO & HC only | Pt, Pd | Pre-1981 vehicles, small engines |
| **Three-Way (TWC)** | CO + HC oxidation + NOx reduction | Pt, Pd, Rh | All modern petrol cars (BS-IV onwards) |
| **Diesel Oxidation Catalyst (DOC)** | Oxidation of CO, HC, soluble organics (SOF) | Pt, Pd | Light-duty & heavy-duty diesels |
| **Selective Catalytic Reduction (SCR)** | NOx reduction using urea (AdBlue) | Cu-zeolite, V₂O₅/WO₃/TiO₂ | BS-VI diesel, heavy commercial vehicles |
| **Lean NOx Trap (LNT)** | NOx storage & periodic regeneration | BaO + Pt/Rh | Lean-burn petrol & light diesel |

---

## 2.2 Thermal Reactor

A **Thermal Reactor** is essentially a **after-burner** — an enlarged exhaust chamber designed to provide sufficient **residence time (τ)** at high **temperature (T)** for the oxidation of CO and HC to reach completion.

### 2.2.1 Design Principle — The Arrhenius Dependence

The rate of CO and HC oxidation follows an Arrhenius-type law:

$$
\begin{aligned}
k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- $k$ = reaction rate constant (s⁻¹)
- $A$ = pre-exponential factor (s⁻¹)
- $E_a$ = activation energy (kJ/mol)
- $R$ = universal gas constant = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)

> [!NOTE]
> For thermal reactors, an exhaust temperature of **600 °C – 800 °C** must be sustained for **0.2 s – 0.5 s** of residence time to achieve > 90% oxidation of CO and HC.

### 2.2.2 Working Mechanism (Step-by-Step)

1. **Exhaust** from the cylinder enters the reactor chamber (volume typically 2×–3× of one cylinder displacement).
2. **Secondary air** is injected (either via an **air pump** or **pulse-air valve** that exploits exhaust pressure pulsations).
3. The mixture of exhaust gas + secondary air is mixed using a **tumble swril mixers** or **baffles**.
4. The mixed gases are held at high temperature for the required residence time.
5. **Ignition** of CO/HC may be self-sustaining once exothermic oxidation raises the gas temperature above the auto-ignition threshold (~ 700 °C for CO).
6. Cleaned exhaust exits to the muffler.

### 2.2.3 Advantages & Limitations

| Advantages | Limitations |
|---|---|
| Simple design, no expensive catalysts | High fuel consumption penalty (heat loss) |
| Effective for CO & HC | Cannot reduce NOx (high T favours NOx formation) |
| Robust, tolerant of leaded fuel (historically) | Large packaging volume required |
| Works even with very rich mixtures | Difficulty in cold-start |

---

## 2.3 Particulate Trap (Diesel Particulate Filter — DPF)

Diesel engines inherently operate lean (λ > 1), so catalytic oxidation of PM is less effective. Therefore, **physical filtration** is used.

### 2.3.1 Construction

A typical DPF is a **wall-flow monolith** made of **silicon carbide (SiC)** or **cordierite**. Alternate channels are **plugged** at opposite ends:

- Exhaust gas is forced to flow **through the porous ceramic wall** (porosity ~ 50%, mean pore size 10–20 µm).
- **Soot particles (PM)** are deposited on the wall surface and within the wall pores.
- Cleaned gas exits through the adjacent channel.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The filtration efficiency of a well-designed DPF for **soot mass** is **> 99%** and for **particle number (PN)** is **> 99.999%** (mandated by BS-VI/Euro 6).

### 2.3.2 Regeneration

As soot accumulates, the **back-pressure** rises, eventually impairing engine performance. **Regeneration** is the process of oxidizing (burning) the trapped soot.

| Regeneration Type | Trigger | Method |
|---|---|---|
| **Passive** | Normal driving | Continuous oxidation using NO₂ as oxidant (produced by upstream DOC); occurs at 250 °C – 450 °C |
| **Active** | ECU-initiated | Post-injection of fuel in cylinder → raises exhaust to 600 °C → soot burns |
| **Forced** | Driver/service | Parked regeneration at service centre using heat gun or burner |

The combustion of soot at the DPF wall is governed by:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{C} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 \quad & \Delta H = -393.5 \text{ kJ/mol} \\
2\text{C} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{CO} \quad & \Delta H = -221.0 \text{ kJ/mol} \\
\text{C} + 2\text{NO}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 2\text{NO} \quad & \Delta H = -567.0 \text{ kJ/mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

The **NO₂-assisted oxidation** (last reaction) is the key to passive regeneration — it proceeds at much lower temperatures than O₂-based oxidation.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **exam-ready cheat sheet** for this topic. Memorise the units, boundary conditions, and the physical meaning of each formula.

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Typical Units | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Conversion Efficiency** | $\eta_i = \dfrac{C_{i,in} - C_{i,out}}{C_{i,in}} \times 100\%$ | % | Quantifies the % removal of pollutant $i$ (CO, HC, NOx, PM) |
| 2 | **Overall Catalyst Efficiency** | $\eta_{ov} = \eta_{CO} \cdot \eta_{HC} \cdot \eta_{NOx}$ | fraction (0–1) | Used in regulatory certification (must be > 0.95 for TWC) |
| 3 | **Space Velocity (GHSV)** | $GHSV = \dfrac{\dot{V}_{exh}}{V_{cat}}$ | h⁻¹ | Defines the gas-handling capacity of a catalyst volume; GHSV = 30,000–100,000 h⁻¹ typical |
| 4 | **Residence Time** | $\tau = \dfrac{V_{cat}}{\dot{V}_{exh}} = \dfrac{1}{GHSV}$ | s | Time each fluid element spends in the catalyst/reactor |
| 5 | **Mass Balance (Steady-state)** | $\dot{m}_{in} = \dot{m}_{out} + \dot{m}_{accum}$ | kg/s | Basis for all emission calculations |
| 6 | **Arrhenius Rate Law** | $k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}$ | s⁻¹ | Predicts reaction rate; basis for thermal reactor design |
| 7 | **Lamda (Excess Air Factor)** | $\lambda = \dfrac{(A/F)_{actual}}{(A/F)_{stoich}}$ | — | λ = 1 → stoichiometric; λ > 1 → lean; λ < 1 → rich |
| 8 | **Soot Mass Loading** | $m_{soot} = \rho_{soot} \cdot \varepsilon_{wall} \cdot V_{wall}$ | g | Determines DPF regeneration interval |
| 9 | **DPF Back-Pressure (Darcy's Law)** | $\Delta P = \dfrac{\mu \cdot v \cdot L \cdot \tau_{wall}}{k_{perm} \cdot A}$ | Pa | Predicts pumping loss; $\mu$ = exhaust gas viscosity, $k_{perm}$ = wall permeability |
| 10 | **Light-Off Temperature** | T₅₀% at η = 50% | °C | Indicator of catalyst cold-start performance |
| 11 | **Conversion of NOx to N₂** | $\text{2NO} \rightarrow \text{N}_2 + \text{O}_2$ | — | Reduction half-reaction in TWC |
| 12 | **Stoichiometric A/F (Petrol)** | $(A/F)_{stoich} = 14.7$ | kg air / kg fuel | Reference value for lambda calculation |
| 13 | **Activation Energy (typical for CO oxidation on Pt)** | $E_a \approx 80 \text{ – } 120$ | kJ/mol | Used in Arrhenius-based design |
| 14 | **Monolith Cell Density** | CPSI = 400 – 600 (TWC), 200 – 300 (DPF) | cells/in² | Affects surface area and pressure drop |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **⚠ Critical Exam Tip:** In KTU valuation, **always write the units** in the final answer. Marks are routinely deducted for missing or wrong units in numerical problems. Also, when computing GHSV, remember the conversion **s⁻¹ → h⁻¹** is **× 3600**.

---

## 2.5 Comparison Matrix — Catalytic Converter vs Thermal Reactor vs DPF

| Feature | Catalytic Converter (TWC) | Thermal Reactor | Particulate Trap (DPF) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Primary Function** | Convert CO, HC, NOx chemically | Oxidize CO, HC by heat | Capture PM physically |
| **Working Principle** | Heterogeneous catalysis | Thermal oxidation + secondary air | Wall-flow filtration |
| **Temperature Range** | 400 °C – 800 °C | 600 °C – 800 °C | 250 °C – 650 °C |
| **Can it handle NOx?** | Yes (TWC) — with λ = 1 ± 0.01 | No (generates NOx) | Indirectly (passive regen uses NO₂) |
| **Can it handle PM?** | No | No | Yes (> 99% mass) |
| **Engine Type** | Petrol (stoichiometric) | Older petrol (rich mixture) | Diesel (lean) |
| **Regeneration Needed?** | No | No | Yes — passive/active/forced |
| **Pressure Drop** | Low (3–10 kPa) | Very low | Moderate (5–20 kPa) |
| **Cost** | High (precious metals) | Low–medium | High (SiC substrate) |
| **Lifespan** | 100,000 – 150,000 km | Long | 150,000 – 200,000 km |
| **Cold-Start Performance** | Poor (below 250 °C inactive) | Poor | Moderate |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Conversion Efficiency of a Catalytic Converter

**Problem Statement:** The inlet concentration of CO entering a TWC is measured as **C_in = 30,000 ppm**, and the outlet concentration as **C_out = 300 ppm**. Calculate the **conversion efficiency (η_CO)**.

### Step 1 — Write the defining equation

The conversion efficiency of a pollutant $i$ is defined as the fraction of the inlet concentration that is destroyed in the device.

$$
\eta_i \;=\; \frac{C_{i,\,in} - C_{i,\,out}}{C_{i,\,in}} \times 100\%
$$

### Step 2 — Substitute the numerical values

Here $i = \text{CO}$, with $C_{CO,in} = 30{,}000$ ppm and $C_{CO,out} = 300$ ppm.

$$
\eta_{CO} \;=\; \frac{30{,}000 - 300}{30{,}000} \times 100\%
$$

### Step 3 — Compute the numerator

$$
30{,}000 - 300 \;=\; 29{,}700 \text{ ppm}
$$

### Step 4 — Perform the division

$$
\frac{29{,}700}{30{,}000} \;=\; 0.9900
$$

### Step 5 — Convert to percentage

$$
\eta_{CO} \;=\; 0.9900 \times 100\% \;=\; 99.00\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** A conversion efficiency of **99%** is the regulatory benchmark for a properly functioning TWC under steady-state cruise conditions. Below **95%**, the catalyst is considered "aged" or "poisoned" (often by lead, sulphur, or phosphorus).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Space Velocity & Residence Time in a Catalytic Converter

**Problem Statement:** A TWC has a catalyst volume of **V_cat = 1.5 litres (1.5 × 10⁻³ m³)**. The exhaust mass flow rate is **ṁ_exh = 0.06 kg/s**, and the exhaust density (at 600 °C, 1 atm) is approximately **ρ_exh = 0.46 kg/m³**. Compute the **Gas Hourly Space Velocity (GHSV)** and the **average residence time (τ)**.

### Step 1 — Compute the volumetric flow rate of exhaust

$$
\dot{V}_{exh} \;=\; \frac{\dot{m}_{exh}}{\rho_{exh}}
$$

$$
\dot{V}_{exh} \;=\; \frac{0.06 \text{ kg/s}}{0.46 \text{ kg/m}^3} \;=\; 0.13043 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

### Step 2 — Convert catalyst volume to m³

$$
V_{cat} \;=\; 1.5 \text{ litres} \;=\; 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3
$$

### Step 3 — Compute GHSV (in units of s⁻¹ first, then convert to h⁻¹)

$$
GHSV_{(s^{-1})} \;=\; \frac{\dot{V}_{exh}}{V_{cat}} \;=\; \frac{0.13043}{1.5 \times 10^{-3}} \;=\; 86.96 \text{ s}^{-1}
$$

Convert to h⁻¹:

$$
GHSV_{(h^{-1})} \;=\; 86.96 \times 3600 \;=\; 313{,}043 \text{ h}^{-1}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this matter?** Space velocity tells the designer how "fast" gases pass through the catalyst. A higher GHSV means shorter residence time → lower conversion. Typical TWC values: **30,000 – 100,000 h⁻¹**. If the GHSV is too high (say > 200,000 h⁻¹), the residence time becomes < 18 ms, insufficient for catalytic reactions to complete.

### Step 4 — Compute the residence time

$$
\tau \;=\; \frac{V_{cat}}{\dot{V}_{exh}} \;=\; \frac{1.5 \times 10^{-3}}{0.13043} \;=\; 0.01150 \text{ s} \;=\; 11.5 \text{ ms}
$$

Or equivalently from GHSV:

$$
\tau \;=\; \frac{1}{GHSV_{(s^{-1})}} \;=\; \frac{1}{86.96} \;=\; 0.01150 \text{ s} \;\checkmark
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** Residence time = **11.5 ms** — a typical design value for a modern TWC. The reaction kinetics must be fast enough to achieve > 95% conversion in this very short time. This is why **Pt-Pd-Rh** catalysts (with low activation energy) are used.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Soot Mass Accumulation & Regeneration Interval in a DPF

**Problem Statement:** A DPF filters exhaust from a 2.0 L diesel engine. The PM emission rate is **E_PM = 0.005 g/s** (during 50% load steady-state). The DPF can hold a maximum soot loading of **m_max = 8 g** before excessive back-pressure develops. Determine the **regeneration interval** $\Delta t_{regen}$.

### Step 1 — Apply conservation of mass (steady-state accumulation)

$$
\frac{dm_{soot}}{dt} \;=\; \dot{m}_{in} - \dot{m}_{out} \;=\; E_{PM} - 0
$$

(Since filtration is nearly 100% effective, $\dot{m}_{out} \approx 0$.)

### Step 2 — Integrate over the regeneration interval

For constant emission rate:

$$
m_{soot}(t) \;=\; E_{PM} \cdot t
$$

### Step 3 — Set $m_{soot}(t) = m_{max}$ and solve for $t$

$$
\Delta t_{regen} \;=\; \frac{m_{max}}{E_{PM}} \;=\; \frac{8 \text{ g}}{0.005 \text{ g/s}} \;=\; 1600 \text{ s} \;\approx\; 26.67 \text{ min}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** At full load, $E_{PM}$ can be 5×–10× higher, reducing the regeneration interval to **< 5 min**. This is why modern ECUs continuously monitor DPF differential pressure ($\Delta P$ across the DPF) and trigger **active regeneration** when $\Delta P$ exceeds a calibrated threshold (typically 15–25 kPa).

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Arrhenius Rate Constant at Light-Off Temperature

**Problem Statement:** For CO oxidation on a platinum catalyst, the pre-exponential factor is $A = 1.5 \times 10^{8} \text{ s}^{-1}$ and the activation energy is $E_a = 95 \text{ kJ/mol}$. Compute the rate constant $k$ at the light-off temperature $T = 573 \text{ K}$ (300 °C).

### Step 1 — Apply the Arrhenius equation

$$
k \;=\; A \cdot e^{-E_a / (R \cdot T)}
$$

### Step 2 — Substitute the values

$$
k \;=\; (1.5 \times 10^{8}) \cdot e^{-\frac{95{,}000}{8.314 \times 573}}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute the exponent

$$
\frac{95{,}000}{8.314 \times 573} \;=\; \frac{95{,}000}{4764.0} \;=\; 19.94
$$

### Step 4 — Evaluate the exponential

$$
e^{-19.94} \;=\; 2.21 \times 10^{-9}
$$

### Step 5 — Final computation

$$
k \;=\; (1.5 \times 10^{8}) \times (2.21 \times 10^{-9}) \;=\; 0.332 \text{ s}^{-1}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** At 300 °C, the rate constant is only **0.332 s⁻¹** — meaning the characteristic time for CO conversion is $\tau_{rxn} \approx 1/k \approx 3$ s. This is **300× longer** than the residence time in the converter (11.5 ms). Hence the catalyst achieves only ~ 50% conversion at this temperature — which is precisely the definition of **light-off temperature**.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Device for Which Pollutant?

| Pollutant ↓ / Device → | TWC (Petrol) | DOC (Diesel) | SCR (Diesel) | Thermal Reactor | DPF |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **CO** | ✓✓ (99%) | ✓✓ (90%) | ✗ | ✓✓ (90%) | ✗ |
| **HC** | ✓✓ (95%) | ✓✓ (85%) | ✗ | ✓✓ (95%) | ✗ |
| **NOx** | ✓✓ (95%) | ✗ (generates) | ✓✓ (> 95%) | ✗ (generates) | indirect |
| **PM** | ✗ | partial (SOF) | ✗ | ✗ | ✓✓ (99% mass) |
| **Best Stoichiometry** | λ = 1.00 ± 0.01 | λ > 1 (lean) | λ > 1 (lean) | λ < 1 (rich) | λ > 1 (lean) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Exam Takeaway:** No single device handles all pollutants. A modern BS-VI diesel after-treatment train (DOC → DPF → SCR → ASC) is a **multi-stage system** where each device targets specific pollutants.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — After-Treatment System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ENG[Engine Cylinder Block] --> EXM[Exhaust Manifold]
    EXM --> TWC1[Three Way Catalytic Converter]
    TWC1 --> O2S[Oxygen Lambda Sensor]
    TWC1 --> MUFF[Muffler and Resonator]
    MUFF --> TAIL[Tailpipe Emission Outlet]
    O2S -. ECU Feedback .-> ENG

    style ENG fill:#ffe6cc,stroke:#cc6600
    style TWC1 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style O2S fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366
    style MUFF fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#b85450
    style TAIL fill:#e1d5e7,stroke:#9673a6
```

**Interpretation:** Exhaust gas from the engine cylinder passes through the catalytic converter, where the **O₂ sensor** (placed upstream of the catalyst) continuously monitors the air-fuel ratio and sends a feedback signal to the ECU. The ECU modulates fuel injection to maintain λ = 1.00. The cleaned exhaust then passes to the muffler and exits through the tailpipe.

---

## 4.2 Catalytic Converter — Internal Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SUBSTRATE[Substrate Honeycomb Monolith Cordierite]
        direction LR
        CH1[Cell Channel 1 Plugged Inlet]
        CH2[Cell Channel 2 Open Inlet]
        CH3[Cell Channel 3 Plugged Inlet]
        CH4[Cell Channel 4 Open Inlet]
    end

    SUBSTRATE --> WC[Washcoat gamma Alumina Layer]
    WC --> AC[Active Catalyst Site Pt Pd Rh]
    AC --> COOX[CO Oxidation to CO2]
    AC --> HCOX[HC Oxidation to CO2 and H2O]
    AC --> NRED[NOx Reduction to N2]

    style SUBSTRATE fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#6c8ebf
    style WC fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366

    style COOX fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#b85450
    style HCOX fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#b85450
    style NRED fill:#e1d5e7,stroke:#9673a6
```

**Interpretation:** This is the **hierarchical structure** of a three-way catalytic converter — substrate (geometric) → washcoat (textural) → active catalyst (chemical) → reactions (functional). The alternating plug pattern forces exhaust through the wall, maximising contact with the catalyst.

---

## 4.3 Thermal Reactor — Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    EXH[Hot Exhaust Gas Inlet] --> MIX[Mixing Chamber with Baffles]
    AIR[Secondary Air Pump or Pulse Valve] --> MIX
    MIX --> COMB[Combustion Chamber Maintained at 600 to 800 C]
    COMB --> RES[Residence Section Residence Time 0.2 to 0.5 s]
    RES --> OUT[Cleaned Exhaust to Muffler]

    style EXH fill:#ffe6cc,stroke:#cc6600
    style AIR fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style MIX fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#d6b656
    style COMB fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#b85450
    style RES fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366
    style OUT fill:#e1d5e7,stroke:#9673a6
```

**Interpretation:** The thermal reactor uses a **two-stream mixing** (exhaust + secondary air) followed by a **high-temperature residence section**. The baffles ensure homogeneity. There is no catalyst — only thermal oxidation.

---

## 4.4 Particulate Trap (DPF) — Wall-Flow Filtration

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph DPF[Wall Flow DPF Monolith]
        direction LR
        PI1[Channel A Inlet Plugged]
        PO1[Channel A Outlet Open]
        PI2[Channel B Inlet Open]
        PO2[Channel B Outlet Plugged]
        PI1 -. Soot Trapped on Wall .-> PI2
    end

    DPF --> REG[Regeneration Mode Selection]
    REG --> PASP[Passive Regeneration NO2 assisted 250 to 450 C]
    REG --> ACTP[Active Regeneration Post injection 600 C]
    REG --> FRC[Forced Regeneration Service Mode Burner]

    style DPF fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#6c8ebf
    style REG fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#d6b656
    style PASP fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366
    style ACTP fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#b85450
    style FRC fill:#e1d5e7,stroke:#9673a6
```

**Interpretation:** The DPF uses an **alternating plug pattern** — gas enters Channel B (open inlet), is forced **through the porous wall** (depositing soot), and exits via Channel A (open outlet). The wall is the filter medium. Soot periodically requires **regeneration** by combustion.

---

## 4.5 BS-VI After-Treatment Train (Diesel) — System-Level Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ENG2[Diesel Engine] --> EGR[Exhaust Gas Recirculation EGR]
    EGR --> DOC[Diesel Oxidation Catalyst DOC]
    DOC --> DPF2[Diesel Particulate Filter DPF]
    DPF2 --> SCR[Selective Catalytic Reduction SCR with AdBlue Injection]
    SCR --> ASC[Ammonia Slip Catalyst ASC]
    ASC --> MUF[Muffler]
    MUF --> TAIL2[Tailpipe]

    style ENG2 fill:#ffe6cc,stroke:#cc6600
    style EGR fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#6c8ebf
    style DOC fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366
    style DPF2 fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#b85450
    style SCR fill:#e1d5e7,stroke:#9673a6
    style ASC fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#d6b656
    style MUF fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366
    style TAIL2 fill:#e1d5e7,stroke:#9673a6
```

**Interpretation:** This is the **complete after-treatment architecture** for a BS-VI diesel vehicle. Each block targets a specific pollutant:
- **EGR** reduces in-cylinder NOx formation
- **DOC** oxidizes CO, HC, and soluble organic fraction of PM
- **DPF** captures solid soot
- **SCR** reduces NOx using injected urea (AdBlue: 32.5% aqueous urea)
- **ASC** captures any excess NH₃ that slips through the SCR
