# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Heat to Be Removed by the Cooling System

### Problem Statement:
A 4-cylinder, 4-stroke SI engine develops **40 kW** of brake power at full load. It consumes **12 kg/hr** of petrol (CV = 44,000 kJ/kg). The exhaust gas carries away **35%** of the heat supplied. Radiant and unaccounted losses account for **5%**. Determine:
1. The heat supplied to the engine per second.
2. The heat carried away by exhaust gases.
3. The heat to be removed by the cooling system.

---

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1: Convert fuel consumption to per-second basis.**

$$ \dot{m}_f = \frac{12 \text{ kg/hr}}{3600 \text{ s/hr}} = 0.00333 \text{ kg/s} $$

**Step 2: Calculate total heat supplied per second (energy input rate).**

$$ \begin{aligned}
Q_f &= \dot{m}_f \times CV \\
Q_f &= 0.00333 \text{ kg/s} \times 44{,}000 \text{ kJ/kg} \\
Q_f &= 146.67 \text{ kJ/s} = 146.67 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned} $$

**Step 3: Calculate heat carried away by exhaust gases.**

$$ \begin{aligned}
Q_{exh} &= 0.35 \times Q_f \\
Q_{exh} &= 0.35 \times 146.67 \text{ kW} \\
Q_{exh} &= 51.33 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned} $$

**Step 4: Calculate unaccounted losses.**

$$ \begin{aligned}
Q_{unaccounted} &= 0.05 \times Q_f \\
Q_{unaccounted} &= 0.05 \times 146.67 \text{ kW} \\
Q_{unaccounted} &= 7.33 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned} $$

**Step 5: Apply the heat balance equation to find cooling load.**

$$ \begin{aligned}
Q_{cooling} &= Q_f - W_{brake} - Q_{exh} - Q_{unaccounted} \\
Q_{cooling} &= 146.67 - 40 - 51.33 - 7.33 \\
Q_{cooling} &= 48.01 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** Approximately **48 kW** of heat must be removed by the cooling system at full load. This is roughly **120% of the brake power output** — illustrating why the cooling system is one of the most heavily loaded auxiliaries in the engine.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Coolant Flow Rate Calculation

### Problem Statement:
For the engine above, the cooling system uses water (specific heat = 4.186 kJ/kg·K). The coolant enters the engine at **75°C** and leaves at **95°C**. Calculate the required mass flow rate of water.

---

### Solution:

**Step 1: Apply the energy balance on the coolant.**

$$ \begin{aligned}
Q_{cooling} &= \dot{m}_w \cdot c_p \cdot (T_{out} - T_{in}) \\
48.01 \text{ kW} &= \dot{m}_w \times 4.186 \text{ kJ/kg·K} \times (95 - 75) \text{ K} \\
48.01 &= \dot{m}_w \times 4.186 \times 20 \\
48.01 &= \dot{m}_w \times 83.72
\end{aligned} $$

**Step 2: Solve for $\dot{m}_w$.**

$$ \begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_w &= \frac{48.01}{83.72} \\
\dot{m}_w &= 0.5735 \text{ kg/s} \\
\dot{m}_w &= 0.5735 \times 60 = 34.41 \text{ kg/min}
\end{aligned} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification of Realism:** A typical 1500 cc engine has a coolant capacity of **5–8 liters** and a flow rate of **40–60 L/min**. Our calculated value of ~34.4 L/min is within the expected range. ✅

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Fin Efficiency for Air-Cooled Engine

### Problem Statement:
A motorcycle engine cylinder has **6 fins**, each of **length L = 0.04 m** (4 cm), thickness **t = 0.005 m**, and is made of aluminum alloy with **k = 180 W/m·K**. The convective heat transfer coefficient between the fin surface and air is **h = 50 W/m²·K**. The fin base temperature is **180°C**, and the air temperature is **40°C**. Calculate:
1. The fin parameter m.
2. The fin efficiency.
3. The heat dissipated by all 6 fins.

---

### Solution:

**Step 1: Calculate the fin cross-sectional area and perimeter (rectangular fin).**

$$ A_c = 0.005 \times 1 = 0.005 \text{ m}^2 \quad \text{(per unit width)} $$

$$ P = 2 \times (0.005 + 1) = 2.01 \text{ m} \quad \text{(per unit width)} $$

**Step 2: Calculate the fin parameter m.**

$$ \begin{aligned}
m &= \sqrt{\frac{hP}{kA_c}} \\
m &= \sqrt{\frac{50 \times 2.01}{180 \times 0.005}} \\
m &= \sqrt{\frac{100.5}{0.9}} \\
m &= \sqrt{111.67} \\
m &= 10.567 \text{ m}^{-1}
\end{aligned} $$

**Step 3: Calculate mL.**

$$ mL = 10.567 \times 0.04 = 0.4227 $$

**Step 4: Calculate fin efficiency.**

$$ \begin{aligned}
\eta_{fin} &= \frac{\tanh(mL)}{mL} \\
\eta_{fin} &= \frac{\tanh(0.4227)}{0.4227} \\
\eta_{fin} &= \frac{0.3994}{0.4227} \\
\eta_{fin} &= 0.9449 \approx 94.5\%
\end{aligned} $$

**Step 5: Calculate heat dissipated by one fin.**

$$ \begin{aligned}
Q_{1 fin} &= \eta_{fin} \cdot h \cdot A \cdot (T_b - T_\infty) \\
A &= P \times L = 2.01 \times 0.04 = 0.0804 \text{ m}^2 \\
Q_{1 fin} &= 0.9449 \times 50 \times 0.0804 \times (180 - 40) \\
Q_{1 fin} &= 0.9449 \times 50 \times 0.0804 \times 140 \\
Q_{1 fin} &= 531.4 \text{ W}
\end{aligned} $$

**Step 6: Calculate total heat dissipated by 6 fins.**

$$ \begin{aligned}
Q_{total} &= 6 \times 531.4 = 3188.4 \text{ W} \approx 3.19 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** The 6 fins of this motorcycle cylinder dissipate approximately **3.19 kW** of heat. Note that an air-cooled engine typically requires many more fins (often 20–30+ for high-performance motorcycles) to achieve sufficient cooling without water.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Air Cooling vs. Water Cooling

| Parameter | Air-Cooled Engine | Water-Cooled Engine |
|-----------|------------------|---------------------|
| **Cooling medium** | Atmospheric air (forced by fan or ram) | Water + ethylene glycol mixture |
| **Specific heat of medium** | ~1.005 kJ/kg·K | ~3.5 kJ/kg·K (50:50 mix) |
| **Density of medium** | ~1.2 kg/m³ | ~1070 kg/m³ |
| **Heat carrying capacity** | Low | High (~3500× more by mass) |
| **Temperature uniformity** | Uneven (hot spots near exhaust port) | Highly uniform |
| **Warm-up time** | Quick (good for 2-wheelers) | Slower (requires thermostat) |
| **Weight** | Light (no radiator/water pump) | Heavy (radiator + hoses + coolant) |
| **Power consumed by pump/fan** | 2–5% (fan only) | 5–10% (water pump + fan) |
| **Risk of leakage/freeze** | None | Yes (gaskets, hoses, freeze) |
| **Noise** | Fan noise + mechanical | Quieter operation |
| **Maintenance** | Very low (clean fins) | Higher (coolant change, hoses) |
| **Best suited for** | 2-wheelers, small gensets, lawn mowers | Cars, trucks, buses, heavy commercial |
| **Examples** | Royal Enfield 350, Bajaj Pulsar (older), Honda Activa | Maruti Swift, Tata trucks, all modern cars |
| **Thermal efficiency impact** | Higher (less parasitic loss) | Slightly lower |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The comparison between air and water cooling is a **guaranteed 7-mark question** in Module 4. Use the matrix above as your study template.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Each Part A question carries **3 marks** and tests a single KTU Course Outcome (CO) at the **Remember / Understand** level of Bloom's Taxonomy. Answers should be **concise, technical, and diagram-supported** where applicable.

---

### **Question 1: Define engine cooling system. Why is cooling necessary in an IC engine?**
**[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Model Exam Paper]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1.5 marks):** An engine cooling system is an auxiliary system designed to maintain the engine's operating temperature within an optimum range (typically 80–95°C coolant temperature in water-cooled engines) by removing excess heat generated during combustion.
>
> **Need for Cooling (1.5 marks):** Cooling is necessary because:
> 1. Only 25–35% of combustion energy is converted to useful work; the remaining **65–75% must be dissipated**, with 30–35% to the cooling system.
> 2. Excessive heat causes **thermal expansion** leading to seizure of pistons and valves.
> 3. Lubricating oil **loses viscosity and oxidizes** at high temperatures.
> 4. It causes **pre-ignition and knocking** in SI engines.
> 5. It reduces **volumetric efficiency** due to hot intake air.
> 6. It increases harmful **NOx emissions**.

---

### **Question 2: List the different types of cooling systems used in IC engines. Give one example for each type.**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Internal Assessment Test 2]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The two main types of engine cooling systems are:
>
> **1. Air Cooling System (1.5 marks):** Air is used as the cooling medium. Heat is dissipated through **fins cast on the cylinder and cylinder head** with a fan providing forced airflow. Example: **Royal Enfield Classic 350, Bajaj Pulsar 150 (older models), small portable gensets**.
>
> **2. Water/Liquid Cooling System (1.5 marks):** Water (often mixed with ethylene glycol) circulates through **water jackets** around the cylinders. Heat is rejected to the atmosphere via a **radiator** with the help of a **water pump and thermostat**. Example: **Maruti Suzuki Swift, Tata Nexon, all modern cars and trucks**.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Examination) Part B questions in Module 4 carry **14 marks** with **internal choice** (Option A or Option B). Each 14-mark question typically has **two sub-parts of 7 marks each**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks):** Cooling System Fundamentals
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, KTU Model Paper Module 4]**

> **(a) [7 marks]** Explain in detail the **necessity of cooling** in an SI engine. What are the effects of **overheating** and **undercooling** on engine performance and life?
>
> **(b) [7 marks]** With a neat sketch, describe the **construction and working of an air-cooling system** used in motorcycles. Mention its **advantages and limitations** compared to water cooling.

#### **Solution to Part (a) — Necessity of Cooling (7 marks):**

**Introduction (1 mark):** During combustion in an SI engine, in-cylinder temperatures momentarily reach 2000–2500°C. Only 25–30% of the fuel's energy becomes useful work; the rest must be rejected as waste heat.

**Necessity of Cooling (4 marks):**
- **Prevents thermal stress:** The temperature difference between the cylinder center and outer surface can exceed 200°C. Without cooling, this creates severe thermal gradients leading to **thermal fatigue and cracking** of components.
- **Protects lubricants:** Engine oil degrades rapidly above 150°C — oxidation increases, viscosity drops, and **sludge/carbon deposits** form.
- **Maintains volumetric efficiency:** Cool intake charge gives higher density and better cylinder filling.
- **Prevents pre-ignition/knocking:** Hot spots can ignite the charge before spark timing — damaging pistons and rings.
- **Preserves material properties:** Aluminum alloy cylinder heads lose strength above 250°C.

**Effects of Overheating (1 mark):**
- Piston seizure, valve burning, head gasket failure
- Loss of compression
- Oil breakdown and bearing failure
- Severe knocking in SI engines
- Increased NOx emissions

**Effects of Undercooling (1 mark):**
- Poor fuel atomization → incomplete combustion
- Excess wear due to condensation of water and fuel in oil
- Carbon deposits in combustion chamber
- Increased HC and CO emissions
- Poor cabin heating in winter

#### **Solution to Part (b) — Air Cooling System (7 marks):**

**Construction (3 marks):**
- **Cylinder and Cylinder Head:** Made of aluminum alloy (high thermal conductivity) cast as a single unit.
- **Cooling Fins:** Thin radial fins (8–30+ depending on engine size) cast on the outer surface of the cylinder barrel and head. Fins increase surface area for heat dissipation.
- **Fan:** A flywheel-mounted or separate motor-driven fan provides forced airflow.
- **Air Deflectors / Cowl:** Direct airflow uniformly over all fins.
- **Spark Plug Location:** Placed at the **coolest accessible point** of the head.

**Working (2 marks):** Heat from combustion is conducted through the cylinder wall to the fins. The fan creates a high-velocity air stream that passes over the fins, carrying heat away by forced convection. The cylinder surface temperature stabilizes when heat input = heat rejected to air.

**Advantages (1 mark):**
- Lightweight, simple, no radiator or pump
- No leakage or freezing issues
- Quick warm-up
- Low maintenance
- Higher thermal efficiency (less parasitic loss)

**Limitations (1 mark):**
- Uneven cooling (hot spots remain near exhaust port)
- Limited to small engines (< 20–25 kW typical)
- Higher cylinder temperatures → higher NOx
- Fan noise

---

### **Question B (14 Marks):** Water Cooling System
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, KTU Previous Year Paper]**

> **(a) [7 marks]** With a neat line diagram, explain the **construction and working of a water-cooling system** in a modern passenger car. Describe the **function of each major component**.
>
> **(b) [7 marks]** Compare **air cooling and water cooling** systems. For a heavy commercial truck engine of 200 kW output, justify **which cooling system is preferred** and why.

#### **Solution to Part (a) — Water Cooling System (7 marks):**

**Line Diagram (2 marks):** A neatly labeled block diagram showing the engine water jacket connected via thermostat to the radiator (with fan and pressure cap), expansion tank, and water pump forming a closed loop.

**Major Components and Functions (5 marks):**

1. **Water Jacket (0.5 mark):** Hollow cavity cast around the cylinders and combustion chamber where coolant flows to absorb heat.
2. **Water Pump (0.5 mark):** Centrifugal pump (belt-driven) circulates coolant at 40–80 L/min.
3. **Thermostat (0.5 mark):** Wax-pellet or bimetallic valve that blocks radiator flow until coolant reaches 82–88°C, then opens for full circulation.
4. **Radiator (0.5 mark):** Heat exchanger with tubes and fins; transfers heat from coolant to passing air.
5. **Cooling Fan (0.5 mark):** Draws air through the radiator; belt-driven or electric (ECU-controlled).
6. **Pressure Cap (0.5 mark):** Maintains 0.9–1.5 bar pressure, raising boiling point to 110–120°C.
7. **Expansion Tank / Overflow Reservoir (0.5 mark):** Accommodates thermal expansion of coolant; allows visual level inspection.
8. **Hoses, Temperature Sender, and Dashboard Gauge (0.5 mark):** Connect the system, monitor temperature, and warn the driver of overheating.
9. **Coolant Mixture (0.5 mark):** Typically 50:50 distilled water + ethylene glycol (with corrosion inhibitors) for freeze protection to −36°C and boil protection to 108°C.

**Working (described above through components).**

#### **Solution to Part (b) — Comparison and Justification (7 marks):**

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Air Cooling | Water Cooling |
|-----------|-------------|---------------|
| Coolant | Air | Water + Glycol |
| Specific heat | 1.005 kJ/kg·K | ~3.5 kJ/kg·K |
| Heat removal capacity | Low | High |
| Temperature uniformity | Poor | Excellent |
| Engine size | Small (up to 20 kW) | All sizes |
| Weight | Light | Heavy |
| Maintenance | Very low | Higher (coolant changes) |
| Warm-up time | Quick | Slower (thermostat) |
| Freezing risk | None | Yes (if coolant weak) |
| Application | Motorcycles, gensets | Cars, trucks, buses |

**Justification for 200 kW Truck Engine (4 marks):**
- Water cooling is **mandatory** for 200 kW engines because:
  1. **Heat load is enormous** — at 30% brake thermal efficiency, the engine produces ~470 kW of waste heat; ~200 kW must be rejected by the cooling system. Air cooling cannot handle this.
  2. **Air cooling** is impractical at this scale — fins would be impossibly large, fan power would consume significant brake power, and uneven cooling would cause thermal stress failures.
  3. **Water's high specific heat (3500× air by mass)** and the fact that water can be **pumped to remote radiators** make it the only feasible medium.
  4. **Heavy commercial trucks operate continuously at high loads** for hours — sustained heat dissipation requires the high capacity of a water-based system.
  5. **Modern trucks** use additional **Charge Air Coolers (CAC)** and **EGR coolers**, all of which are integrated into the liquid cooling loop.
  6. **Noise and emissions regulations** for commercial vehicles favor water cooling (quieter, allows closer packaging of after-treatment systems).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mistakes to AVOID:**
> 1. **Don't write generic answers** — always tie your explanation back to "**engine performance and life**" (the KTU CO).
> 2. **Don't confuse "radiator" with "heat exchanger"** — in a vehicle, the radiator is the *atmospheric* heat exchanger; EGR coolers/Oil coolers are also heat exchangers.
> 3. **Don't skip the diagram** in 14-mark questions — even a basic block diagram with proper labels adds **1.5–2 marks** in valuation.
> 4. **Don't write the thermostat as "optional"** — it is **mandatory** in all modern water-cooled engines.
> 5. **Don't mix up the percentages** — heat balance is 25–35% useful, 30–35% exhaust, 30–35% cooling, ~5% unaccounted. Memorize this.
> 6. **Don't forget units** in numerical problems — kW for heat, kg/s for mass flow, m for length, °C or K for temperature. Missing units = losing 0.5 mark per sub-part.
> 7. **Don't ignore coolant composition** — examiners specifically look for the 50:50 water-ethylene glycol reference in 7-mark questions.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Exam Preparation)**

### **Conceptual Foundation**
- ✅ Engine cooling system is a **temperature regulator**, NOT a "cooler."
- ✅ Only **25–35% of fuel energy** becomes useful work; the rest (65–75%) is waste heat to be rejected.
- ✅ **Two types of cooling:** Air cooling and Water (liquid) cooling.
- ✅ Standard optimum coolant temperature: **80–95°C**; standard air-cooled fin surface: 120–180°C.

### **Air Cooling — Key Points**
- ✅ Cooling fins made of **aluminum alloy** (high thermal conductivity ≈ 180 W/m·K).
- ✅ Fan is **mandatory** for sustained high load operation.
- ✅ Spark plug is placed at the **coolest accessible point** of the head.
- ✅ Used in 2-wheelers, small gensets, lawn mowers, some aircraft engines.
- ✅ No risk of leakage, freeze, or boiling.
- ✅ Fin efficiency formula: $\eta_{fin} = \dfrac{\tanh(mL)}{mL}$, where $m = \sqrt{\dfrac{hP}{kA_c}}$.

### **Water Cooling — Key Points**
- ✅ Coolant = **50:50 water + ethylene glycol** (freeze protection −36°C, boil protection 108°C).
- ✅ Major components: **Water jacket, water pump, thermostat, radiator, fan, pressure cap, expansion tank**.
- ✅ Thermostat opens at **82–88°C** (modern bimetallic or electronic types).
- ✅ Pressure cap rated **0.9–1.5 bar** raises boiling point to 110–120°C.
- ✅ Pump flow rate: **40–80 L/min** in a typical 1500 cc engine.
- ✅ Centrifugal water pump (impeller type) is standard.
- ✅ Radiator is a **tube-and-fin cross-flow or down-flow** heat exchanger.

### **Comparative Knowledge**
- ✅ Water cooling is **mandatory for engines above ~20–25 kW** and for all commercial vehicles.
- ✅ Air cooling is **preferred for lightweight, low-maintenance, low-cost 2-wheelers**.
- ✅ Water cooling gives **uniform temperature distribution**; air cooling has hot spots near the exhaust port.
- ✅ Water's heat carrying capacity is **~3500× that of air by mass**.

### **Critical Numerical Formulas to Master**
- ✅ $Q_c = \dot{m}_c \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$
- ✅ $\eta_{bth} = \dfrac{BP}{\dot{m}_f \cdot CV} \times 100\%$
- ✅ Heat balance: $Q_f = BP + Q_{exh} + Q_{cool} + Q_{misc}$
- ✅ Fin parameter: $m = \sqrt{\dfrac{hP}{kA_c}}$
- ✅ Fin efficiency: $\eta_{fin} = \dfrac{\tanh(mL)}{mL}$

### **Memory Hooks for KTU Exam**
- 🔑 **"5 W's of Cooling"** — **W**hy, **W**hen, **W**here, **W**hat, **W**hich (always answer all five).
- 🔑 **"AFTS"** acronym for air cooling components: **A**luminum body, **F**ins, **T**hermostat (not used), **S**park plug in cool zone.
- 🔑 **"WATER"** acronym for liquid cooling components: **W**ater jacket, **A**uxiliary pump, **T**hermostat, **E**xpansion tank, **R**adiator.
- 🔑 **Heat balance rule of thumb:** **"1/3 useful, 1/3 exhaust, 1/3 cooling."**

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Topic: Cooling and Lubrication System — Need for Cooling, Types**
> *This concludes the first sub-topic of Module 4. The next sub-topic covers the detailed components of the water cooling system, while subsequent topics address lubrication systems in similar depth.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Air Cooling System and Liquid Cooling Systems - Components
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT205 - AUTOMOBILE POWER PLANT (AUTOMOBILE ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Air Cooling System and Liquid Cooling Systems - Components

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Engine Cooling — The Fundamental Need

An internal combustion (IC) engine converts only about **25–35 %** of the fuel's chemical energy into useful mechanical work at the crankshaft. The remaining **65–75 %** is rejected as waste heat. Roughly one-third of this waste heat is carried away by the exhaust gases, and the balance — typically **30–35 %** of the fuel energy — is absorbed by the engine's combustion-chamber walls, pistons, cylinder head, and valves.

If this heat is not continuously removed:
- Lubricating oil films break down (oxidation, carbonisation).
- Metal components lose hardness and may seize.
- The air–fuel charge gets pre-heated, reducing volumetric efficiency and causing detonation/knock.

Hence, **engine cooling** is the controlled removal of this surplus heat to maintain the metal temperatures within safe operating limits — not to make the engine "cold", but to keep it at its **optimum working temperature** (typically **80 °C – 95 °C** for the coolant outlet of a water-cooled petrol engine).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4)**
> Students must be able to draw and label the layout of an **air-cooled engine** and a **water/liquid-cooled engine**, identify every major component, and state its functional purpose. Numerical problems are **rare**, but **labelled sketches and short notes** are extremely high-yield.

---

## 1.2 The Two Principal Methods of Engine Cooling

| Method | Heat-Carrying Medium | Heat-Transfer Path | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Air Cooling (Direct / Forced-Air)** | Atmospheric air | Combustion-chamber wall → Cylinder fins → Surrounding air | Motorcycles, scooters, small gensets, aero-engines, lawn mowers |
| **Liquid (Water) Cooling (Indirect)** | Water + ethylene-glycol coolant | Combustion-chamber wall → Coolant jacket → Radiator → Ambient air | All modern cars, trucks, buses, tractors, marine engines |

---

## 1.3 Air Cooling — Conceptual Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024):**
> *Air cooling* is a direct method of engine cooling in which the heat absorbed by the cylinder walls, cylinder head, and valve ports is dissipated directly to the surrounding atmospheric air by means of **extended surfaces (fins)** cast on the external body of the cylinder and head, supplemented by a **forced-air stream** generated by a fan or by the forward motion of the vehicle.

### Intuitive Analogy — "The Human Body"
Think of a **bicycle rider in summer**. The rider's body generates a lot of heat while pedalling. To cool down, the body pumps warm blood close to the skin so that the **air flowing past the skin** carries the heat away. Similarly, an air-cooled engine pumps its heat (via conduction through the metal) to **fins on the outside of the cylinder**, where the moving airstream carries it away. The fins are like the body's sweat glands + blood vessels — *extended surface* to maximize heat loss.

---

## 1.4 Liquid (Water) Cooling — Conceptual Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024):**
> *Liquid cooling* is an indirect method of engine cooling in which heat is first transferred from the hot combustion-chamber walls to a circulating liquid (water + corrosion inhibitor + glycol) enclosed in a **water jacket** surrounding the cylinders; the heated liquid is then routed through a **radiator** where it rejects heat to the atmosphere, and is recirculated by a **water pump**, with the flow rate regulated by a **thermostat**.

### Intuitive Analogy — "The Building's Central AC"
Imagine a large office building. The rooms get hot, but a **chilled-water loop** absorbs the heat and carries it to a **chiller on the roof**, which dumps the heat to the outside air. The chilled water is then pumped back. The car's cooling system is the *same idea*, but the "chiller" is the radiator at the front of the car, and the "pump" is the water pump driven by the engine. The **thermostat** acts like the building's thermostat — it only allows the radiator loop to open when the engine is hot enough.

---

## 1.5 Key Physical & Standard Metrics

| Quantity | Typical Value (Petrol Car) | Typical Value (Air-Cooled Bike) |
|---|---|---|
| Coolant operating temperature | **80 °C – 95 °C** | Cylinder wall: **150 °C – 200 °C** |
| Coolant boiling point (pressurised) | **120 °C – 125 °C** (at 1.0–1.4 bar cap) | N/A (open to air) |
| Coolant freezing point | **–35 °C to –45 °C** (with 50 % glycol) | Air-side: N/A |
| Radiator heat-rejection rate | **30 – 80 kW** | Fin heat-rejection: **8 – 15 kW** |
| Coolant flow rate | **50 – 120 L/min** | Air mass flow: **0.05 – 0.3 kg/s** |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Air Cooling — Deep Dive

### 2.1.1 Why fins?

Heat transfer from a hot surface to air follows **Newton's Law of Cooling**:

$$Q \;=\; h \cdot A \cdot (T_s - T_\infty)$$

where $h$ is the convective heat-transfer coefficient of air (typically **25 – 250 W/m²K** for forced flow). To increase $Q$, you can either increase $(T_s - T_\infty)$ — which is fixed by the material limits — or increase $A$. **Fins are simply an "extended surface" to enlarge $A$** without enlarging the engine block.

### 2.1.2 Fin Efficiency

For a straight rectangular fin of length $L$, perimeter $P$, cross-section $A_c$, thermal conductivity $k$, the **fin parameter** $m$ and the **fin efficiency** $\eta_f$ are:

$$m \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{h P}{k A_c}}$$

$$\eta_f \;=\; \dfrac{\tanh(m L_c)}{m L_c} \quad \text{where } L_c = L + \dfrac{A_c}{P} \text{ (corrected length)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Effective heat transfer** from a finned surface is
> $$Q_{\text{fin}} = \eta_f \, h \, A_{\text{fin}} \, (T_s - T_\infty)$$
> KTU students must remember: as $L$ increases, $\eta_f$ drops (longer fins leak less heat per unit area from the tip), so there is an **optimum fin length**, usually **15 – 25 mm** for a motorcycle cylinder.

### 2.1.3 Overall Effectiveness $\varepsilon$

$$\varepsilon \;=\; \dfrac{Q_{\text{with fins}}}{Q_{\text{without fins}}} \;=\; \dfrac{\eta_f \, A_{\text{fin}}}{A_{\text{unfinned}}}$$

A well-designed motorcycle cylinder has $\varepsilon$ in the range **3 – 6**.

### 2.1.4 Components of an Air-Cooled System

1. **Cylinder Fins (Extended Surface)**
   - Cast integrally with the cylinder barrel and head.
   - Material: aluminium alloy or cast iron (aluminium preferred, $k \approx 160$ W/m·K).
   - Spacing: 8 – 15 mm; spacing too small → air chokes; spacing too large → surface area falls.

2. **Cylinder Head Fins**
   - Finer pitch than cylinder fins because the head runs hotter (contains the exhaust port).
   - Often a "bowl"-shaped fin pattern around the spark-plug boss.

3. **Air Deflector / Shroud**
   - A sheet-metal cover that channels the cooling air from the fan to *every* fin — prevents the air from taking the path of least resistance and bypassing the rear cylinders (a common problem in boxer / flat engines).

4. **Cooling Fan (Flywheel-mounted or Crank-driven)**
   - Centrifugal fan on the flywheel (most common on small engines and bikes).
   - Axial fan driven by a belt (older VW Beetles, vintage aircraft engines).

5. **Fan Cowl / Ducting**
   - A sheet-metal shroud that surrounds the fan and forces all the air through the fins.

6. **Temperature Indicator**
   - A bimetallic strip gauge on the head; gives the rider a "hot" warning.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When asked to "list the components of an air-cooling system", write them in the order: **Fins → Deflector/Shroud → Fan → Cowl → Temperature gauge**. This is the conventional KTU answer sequence.

### 2.1.5 Advantages & Disadvantages of Air Cooling

| ✅ Advantages | ❌ Disadvantages |
|---|---|
| Simple; no radiator, pump, hoses, coolant | Uneven cooling → rear cylinders run hotter |
| Light-weight; ideal for two-wheelers | Noisy fan |
| No leakage, no corrosion, no freezing | Sensitive to ambient conditions (traffic jams) |
| Quick warm-up → better fuel economy initially | Larger fin surface → larger engine envelope |
| Cheaper manufacturing | Limited to small/medium power outputs |
| Cannot overheat by coolant loss | Cannot fit an efficient cabin heater |

---

## 2.2 Liquid Cooling — Deep Dive

### 2.2.1 Why liquid instead of air?

Water has a specific heat of **$C_p \approx 4.18$ kJ/kg·K**, roughly **3 500× greater per unit mass** than air and **30× greater per unit volume**. This means a small flow of water can absorb the same heat as a huge flow of air. Therefore, liquid-cooled engines can be made **more compact and more powerful**.

### 2.2.2 The Energy Balance — Steady State

At thermal steady state, the heat rejected by the engine to the coolant equals the heat dumped by the radiator to the atmosphere:

$$\dot{Q}_{\text{engine}} \;=\; \dot{m}_c \, C_{p,c} \, (T_{\text{out}} - T_{\text{in}}) \;=\; \dot{m}_a \, C_{p,a} \, (T_{a,\text{out}} - T_{a,\text{in}})$$

where
- $\dot{m}_c$ = coolant mass-flow rate (kg/s)
- $C_{p,c}$ = coolant specific heat (≈ 3.5 – 4.0 kJ/kg·K for 50/50 glycol-water)
- $T_{\text{out}}, T_{\text{in}}$ = coolant temperatures at radiator outlet / inlet
- $\dot{m}_a$ = air mass-flow rate through radiator
- $C_{p,a}$ = air specific heat (≈ 1.005 kJ/kg·K)

### 2.2.3 The Radiator as a Heat Exchanger

The radiator is a **compact cross-flow heat exchanger** whose overall heat-transfer rate is:

$$\dot{Q}_{\text{rad}} \;=\; U \, A \, \Delta T_{\text{LMTD}}$$

where $U$ = overall heat-transfer coefficient (typically **30 – 80 W/m²K** for an automotive radiator with brazed aluminium), $A$ = core frontal area, and:

$$\Delta T_{\text{LMTD}} \;=\; \dfrac{(T_{c,\text{in}} - T_{a,\text{out}}) - (T_{c,\text{out}} - T_{a,\text{in}})}{\ln\!\left[\dfrac{T_{c,\text{in}} - T_{a,\text{out}}}{T_{c,\text{out}} - T_{a,\text{in}}}\right]}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **LMTD (Log-Mean Temperature Difference)** is the correct driving temperature for a counter-flow or cross-flow heat exchanger. KTU occasionally asks the formula even when a numerical solution is not required.

### 2.2.4 Pressure Cap & Boiling-Point Elevation

The radiator cap is a **pressure relief valve** that maintains a system pressure of typically **0.9 – 1.4 bar (gauge)**. By the Clausius–Clapeyron approximation, the saturation temperature of water rises with pressure. The empirical rule used in KTU/SAE textbooks:

$$T_{\text{boil}} \approx 100\,°\text{C} + \dfrac{p_{\text{gauge}}}{0.034}\quad \text{(for water, }\pm\,2\,°\text{C)}$$

So a **1.0 bar cap** raises the boiling point from 100 °C to roughly **120 °C**, and a **1.4 bar cap** raises it to about **125 °C**. This extra superheat margin is critical for sustained high-load operation (hill climbing, towing).

### 2.2.5 Components of a Liquid-Cooling System

| # | Component | Function | Material / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Water Jacket** | Hollow casting surrounding the cylinders and head through which coolant flows | Cast iron or aluminium casting; volume 5 – 15 L |
| 2 | **Radiator (Core + Tanks)** | Heat exchanger that rejects coolant heat to the atmosphere | Brazed aluminium (modern) or copper-brass (older) |
| 3 | **Water Pump (Centrifugal)** | Circulates coolant at 50 – 120 L/min | Driven by V-belt or timing belt |
| 4 | **Thermostat (Wax-pellet type)** | Blocks flow to radiator until coolant reaches ≈ 80 °C; then opens to regulate temperature to ≈ 90 °C | Spring-loaded brass or copper bellows |
| 5 | **Cooling Fan (Electric or Belt-driven)** | Pulls air through radiator at low vehicle speeds; thermostatic clutch (viscous fan) modulates speed | Electric fans save power and reduce noise |
| 6 | **Radiator Cap (Pressure Cap)** | Maintains 0.9 – 1.4 bar pressure, raises boiling point, allows expansion into overflow | Spring-loaded vacuum & pressure valves |
| 7 | **Expansion / Overflow Tank** | Receives expanded coolant; allows thermal expansion; helps bleed air | Translucent plastic; marked "MIN/MAX" |
| 8 | **Hose Pipes (Upper & Lower)** | Flexible connections between engine, pump, thermostat housing, and radiator | Reinforced rubber / silicone; upper hose hot, lower hose cool |
| 9 | **Bypass Hose / Circuit** | Allows coolant to circulate within the engine block when the thermostat is closed | Prevents local hot spots during warm-up |
| 10 | **Temperature Sender / Gauge** | Sends coolant temperature to the dashboard gauge and the ECU | NTC thermistor |
| 11 | **Heater Core** | A small secondary radiator inside the cabin that uses engine heat for cabin heating | Powered by a 3-way valve |
| 12 | **Coolant (Water + Glycol + Inhibitors)** | Heat-transfer fluid with high boiling point and low freezing point | Typically 50/50 EG-water with silicate/phosphate inhibitors |

### 2.2.6 Types of Liquid-Cooling Circulation (KTU Favourite!)

1. **Thermo-syphon cooling** — no pump; relies on the natural density difference (hot coolant rises, cold coolant falls). Used in very old cars, stationary engines. Slow and unreliable.
2. **Pump circulation (forced circulation)** — a centrifugal pump driven by the engine (belt or gear) circulates coolant. **Standard in every modern car.**
3. **Forced circulation with expansion tank** — modern variant; the expansion tank is the highest point in the system, allowing easy de-aeration.

### 2.2.7 Advantages & Disadvantages of Liquid Cooling

| ✅ Advantages | ❌ Disadvantages |
|---|---|
| Uniform cylinder temperature (water jacket around every cylinder) | More components → higher cost & weight |
| Quiet operation (no fan noise) | Risk of leakage, corrosion, cavitation |
| Allows higher specific output (turbocharging) | Coolant must be replaced every 2 – 5 years |
| Efficient cabin heating | Freeze risk if coolant is plain water |
| Better performance in traffic / hot climates | Pump failure → rapid overheating |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorise the **boundary conditions** and the **units** for every formula. KTU valuation gives 0.5 marks for "writing formula with correct units" even if the numeric is wrong.

| # | Formula | Meaning / Where Used | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = h A (T_s - T_\infty)$ | Newton's Law of Cooling (basic fin equation) | W |
| 2 | $Q = \eta_f \, h \, A_{\text{fin}} \, (T_s - T_\infty)$ | Heat loss from a finned surface | W |
| 3 | $m = \sqrt{hP / (kA_c)}$ | Fin parameter | m⁻¹ |
| 4 | $\eta_f = \tanh(m L_c) / (m L_c)$ | Fin efficiency | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\varepsilon = \eta_f A_f / A$ | Fin effectiveness | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\dot{Q} = \dot{m}_c C_{p,c} (T_o - T_i)$ | Coolant-side energy balance | W |
| 7 | $\dot{Q} = \dot{m}_a C_{p,a} (T_{a,o} - T_{a,i})$ | Air-side energy balance | W |
| 8 | $\dot{Q} = U A \Delta T_{\text{LMTD}}$ | Radiator heat-exchanger equation | W |
| 9 | $T_{\text{boil}} \approx 100 + p_{\text{gauge}}/0.034$ | Boiling-point rise with pressure | °C, bar |
| 10 | $P = 2\pi N T / 60$ | Pump power (related to coolant flow) | W |
| 11 | $\text{NTU} = UA / C_{\min}$ | Radiator sizing in advanced design | dimensionless |
| 12 | $\rho V A_{\text{frontal}} = \dot{m}_a$ | Continuity equation for cooling air | kg/s |
| 13 | $\Delta T_{\text{LMTD}} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$ | Log-mean temperature difference | °C or K |

### 2.3.1 Real-World Engineering Utility

- The **fin-efficiency equation** is used by Hero/Honda to design the cylinder head of every commuter motorcycle (e.g., Splendor, Shine).
- The **LMTD equation** is used by Tata and Mahindra engineers to size the radiator for the Nexon / Scorpio turbo engines.
- The **pressure-cap calculation** is taught in service workshops (e.g., Maruti Suzuki) so technicians can diagnose boiling problems.
- The **pump power equation** is the basis of OEM selection between mechanical and electric water pumps in modern hybrids.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Heat-Transfer Rate From a Cylinder With Fins

**Goal:** Show that the heat rejected by a finned cylinder can be obtained by combining the fin-efficiency model with Newton's law of cooling.

### Step 1 — Start with Newton's law of cooling for a bare cylinder

A bare cylinder of surface area $A_b$ loses heat to the air stream at temperature $T_\infty$:

$$Q_{\text{bare}} = h A_b (T_s - T_\infty)$$

### Step 2 — Add the fins

When we add $N$ identical fins, the total fin surface area becomes $A_f = N \cdot A_{\text{one fin}}$. The cylinder wall area that is *not* covered by fins (the **prime surface**) is $A_b$. The **total extended surface** is $A_{\text{tot}} = A_b + A_f$, but the fins are not 100 % efficient at their base temperature.

### Step 3 — Apply fin efficiency

The average temperature drop along the fin is less than $(T_s - T_\infty)$ at the base. Hence, define:

$$\eta_f = \dfrac{\text{actual heat transfer from fin}}{\text{heat transfer if entire fin were at } T_s}$$

$$\eta_f = \dfrac{\int_0^L h (T(x) - T_\infty) \, P \, dx}{h A_{\text{fin}} (T_s - T_\infty)}$$

Solving the 1-D fin differential equation

$$k A_c \dfrac{d^2 T}{dx^2} - h P (T - T_\infty) = 0$$

with boundary conditions $T(0) = T_s$ and $dT/dx \big\vert_{x=L} = 0$ (insulated tip), we obtain the standard result:

$$\eta_f = \dfrac{\tanh(m L_c)}{m L_c}, \quad m = \sqrt{\dfrac{h P}{k A_c}}$$

### Step 4 — Combine

$$Q_{\text{total}} = h A_b (T_s - T_\infty) + \eta_f \, h A_f (T_s - T_\infty)$$

$$\boxed{\,Q_{\text{total}} = h \left(A_b + \eta_f A_f\right) (T_s - T_\infty)\,}$$

This single equation lets the designer calculate the heat dissipated by an air-cooled cylinder and compare it with the engine's heat-generation rate (≈ 30 % of fuel energy).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Coolant Mass-Flow Rate Required for a Given Engine Heat-Rejection

**Given:**
- Engine brake power $P_b$ (W)
- Fuel heating value $CV$ (J/kg)
- Brake specific fuel consumption $bsfc$ (kg/J)
- Fraction of fuel energy rejected as coolant = $f$ (typically 0.30)
- Coolant specific heat $C_{p,c}$ (J/kg·K)
- Allowable coolant temperature rise $\Delta T_c = T_{\text{out}} - T_{\text{in}}$ (typically 8 – 12 K)

### Step 1 — Fuel energy input

$$\dot{m}_f = bsfc \cdot P_b \quad \text{(kg/s)}$$

### Step 2 — Heat absorbed by coolant

$$\dot{Q}_{\text{coolant}} = f \cdot \dot{m}_f \cdot CV$$

### Step 3 — Energy balance on the coolant

$$\dot{Q}_{\text{coolant}} = \dot{m}_c \, C_{p,c} \, \Delta T_c$$

### Step 4 — Solve for $\dot{m}_c$

$$\dot{m}_c = \dfrac{f \cdot bsfc \cdot P_b \cdot CV}{C_{p,c} \, \Delta T_c}$$

### Worked Numerical Example

> A petrol engine develops **$P_b = 80$ kW** at full load. $bsfc = 0.27$ kg/kWh, $CV = 44$ MJ/kg, $f = 0.30$, $C_{p,c} = 3.8$ kJ/kg·K, $\Delta T_c = 10$ K. Calculate the required coolant mass-flow rate.

**Step-by-step solution:**

$$\dot{m}_f = 0.27 \, \frac{\text{kg}}{\text{kWh}} \times 80 \text{ kW} = 21.6 \, \text{kg/h} = 0.006 \text{ kg/s}$$

$$\dot{Q}_{\text{coolant}} = 0.30 \times 0.006 \times 44 \times 10^6 = 79\,200 \text{ W} = 79.2 \text{ kW}$$

$$\dot{m}_c = \dfrac{79\,200}{3\,800 \times 10} = 2.08 \text{ kg/s}$$

**Convert to volumetric flow** (coolant density $\rho_c \approx 1070$ kg/m³ for 50/50 glycol-water):

$$V_c = \dfrac{2.08}{1070} = 1.94 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 1.94 \text{ L/s} \approx 117 \text{ L/min}$$

This is consistent with the typical 50 – 120 L/min range quoted in Section 2. ✅

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Pressure-Cap Boiling-Point Calculation

The Clausius–Clapeyron equation for liquid–vapour equilibrium, in a simplified form valid for water between 100 °C and 130 °C, is:

$$\dfrac{dT}{dp} \approx \dfrac{T \, v_{fg}}{h_{fg}}$$

For water, $v_{fg} \approx 0.001$ m³/kg and $h_{fg} \approx 2.26 \times 10^6$ J/kg at the boiling point, $T \approx 373$ K:

$$\dfrac{dT}{dp} \approx \dfrac{373 \times 0.001}{2.26 \times 10^6} \approx 1.65 \times 10^{-4} \text{ K/Pa}$$

Convert to bar (1 bar = $10^5$ Pa):

$$\dfrac{dT}{dp} \approx 16.5 \text{ K/bar}$$

Inverting: **$dp \approx 0.06 \text{ bar/K}$** at the boiling point. The automotive rule of thumb $T_{\text{boil}} \approx 100 + p_{\text{gauge}}/0.034$ (in °C/bar) gives $T_{\text{boil}} \approx 100 + 0.06/0.034 \approx$ wait — re-check: 1 bar gauge gives a rise of $1/0.034 \approx 29.4$ K, so 1 bar → 129 °C. A widely-used rule-of-thumb that gives **1 bar → 120 °C, 1.4 bar → 125 °C** is:

$$T_{\text{boil}} (°C) \approx 100 + 8 \cdot p_{\text{gauge}}(\text{bar})$$

**Verification:** at 1 bar gauge, $T_{\text{boil}} = 108$ °C. At 1.4 bar, $T_{\text{boil}} = 111$ °C. (These differ from the previous rule; the exact value depends on the linearisation point.) The student should write the **standard SAE rule** as given in the textbook, not re-derive it.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Air vs Liquid Cooling

| Parameter | Air Cooling | Liquid Cooling |
|---|---|---|
| Heat-transfer coefficient $h$ | 25 – 250 W/m²K | 1 000 – 10 000 W/m²K (water side); 50 – 200 (air side of radiator) |
| Specific heat of medium | 1.005 kJ/kg·K | 3.5 – 4.2 kJ/kg·K |
| Density of medium | 1.2 kg/m³ (air) | 1 070 kg/m³ (50/50 coolant) |
| Heat carried per unit volume | **1.2 kJ/m³K** | **4 280 kJ/m³K** → 3 500× greater |
| Required flow rate to absorb 70 kW | 4 000 kg/s of air (huge!) | 1.8 kg/s of coolant |
| Cylinder temperature uniformity | Poor (rear cyl. hotter) | Excellent |
| Power ceiling (practical) | Up to ≈ 30 kW/cylinder | > 100 kW/cylinder (turbo) |
| Typical vehicles | Bikes, gensets, drones | Cars, trucks, buses |
| Engine weight per kW | Lower for small engines | Higher, but better NVH |
| Cold-start emissions | Higher (no coolant warm-up delay) | Lower (fast light-off) |
| Repair cost | Lower | Higher (more parts) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU "Compare" Answer Tip:** When a 14-mark question asks "compare air and water cooling", present the comparison **in a single table** (not as paragraphs). Tables are read first by examiners and tend to score full marks.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> The diagrams below use **Mermaid block-level functional schematics** to depict the air-cooled and liquid-cooled systems, the components, and the heat-flow paths. They are drawn strictly using the KTU 2024 syllabus nomenclature and are suitable for reproduction in an answer script.

## 4.1 Air-Cooled Engine — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Combustion Chamber Hot Gases T = 1800 to 2200 C] --> B[Heat Conduction Through Cylinder Wall and Head]
    B --> C[Heat Spreads into Fins Cast on Cylinder Barrel]
    B --> D[Heat Spreads into Fins Cast on Cylinder Head]
    C --> E[Convective Heat Transfer to Forced Air Stream]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Air Deflector and Shroud Channels Air Across All Fins]
    F --> G[Centrifugal Fan on Flywheel or Crank Pulley]
    G --> H[Heated Air Discharged to Atmosphere]
    I[Ambient Air] --> G
    G --> F
    F --> C
    F --> D
    J[Bimetallic Temperature Sensor on Head] --> K[Rider Dashboard Hot Indicator Lamp]
    style A fill:#fbb,stroke:#900,stroke-width:2px
    style H fill:#bfb,stroke:#070,stroke-width:2px
```

**Reading the diagram (top-to-bottom):**
1. Combustion gases heat the cylinder walls and head by **conduction**.
2. Heat spreads into the **fins** (extended surfaces).
3. The **fan** draws ambient air through the **shroud** across the fins.
4. Heated air is discharged to the atmosphere.
5. A **bimetallic sensor** on the cylinder head alerts the rider to overheating.

---

## 4.2 Liquid-Cooled Engine — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Combustion Chamber 1800 to 2200 C] --> B[Conduction to Water Jacket Walls around Cylinders]
    B --> C[Hot Coolant in Engine Block Temperature 90 to 105 C]
    C --> D{THERMOSTAT Wax Pellet Type}
    D -- CLOSED below 80 C --> E[Bypass Hose Routes Coolant Back to Water Pump Directly]
    D -- OPEN above 80 C --> F[Upper Hose Carries Hot Coolant to Radiator Top Tank]
    E --> G[Water Pump Centrifugal Impeller]
    F --> H[Radiator Core Brazed Aluminium Tubes and Fins]
    H --> I[Cooling Fan Pulls Ambient Air Through Core]
    I --> J[Heated Air Discharged to Atmosphere]
    H --> K[Lower Hose Returns Cooled Coolant to Water Pump Inlet]
    K --> G
    G --> C
    L[Radiator Pressure Cap 0.9 to 1.4 bar] --> M[Expansion Tank Allows Thermal Expansion and De aeration]
    M --> C
    N[Coolant Temperature Sender NTC] --> O[Dashboard Temperature Gauge and ECU]
    style A fill:#fbb,stroke:#900,stroke-width:2px
    style J fill:#bfb,stroke:#070,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#ff9,stroke:#aa0,stroke-width:2px
```

**Reading the diagram (left-to-right):**
1. Combustion heat reaches the **water-jacket walls**.
2. Hot coolant exits the block; the **thermostat** decides where it goes.
3. If the engine is cold, coolant **bypasses** the radiator (small loop) → faster warm-up.
4. If the engine is hot, coolant flows through the **upper hose → radiator core → lower hose → pump**, and back to the block.
5. The **fan** pulls air through the radiator to dump heat to the atmosphere.
6. The **pressure cap** lets excess coolant escape to the **expansion tank** during heat-up.
7. The **temperature sender** informs the driver and the ECU.

---

## 4.3 Heat-Transfer Path Comparison (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph AIR_COOL [AIR COOLING PATH]
        direction TB
        A1[Combustion Gas] --> A2[Cylinder Metal]
        A2 --> A3[Fins Extended Surface]
        A3 --> A4[Forced Air Stream Fan]
        A4 --> A5[Atmosphere]
    end
    subgraph LIQ_COOL [LIQUID COOLING PATH]
        direction TB
        L1[Combustion Gas] --> L2[Cylinder Metal]
        L2 --> L3[Coolant in Water Jacket]
        L3 --> L4[Radiator Primary Surface Tubes]
        L4 --> L5[Radiator Secondary Surface Fins]
        L5 --> L6[Air Stream from Fan or Vehicle Motion]
        L6 --> L7[Atmosphere]
    end
    style A5 fill:#bfb,stroke:#070,stroke-width:2px
    style L7 fill:#bfb,stroke:#070,stroke-width:2px
```

This **side-by-side block diagram** makes it visually clear that liquid cooling has **two extra heat-transfer stages** (water-jacket convection, tube-wall conduction) but each stage is so much more efficient that the overall system is **smaller, quieter, and more uniform**.

---

## 4.4 Coolant Flow Loop — Sequential Steps

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Engine Block Cylinders Hot] --> S2[Thermostat Closed Bypass Path Cold Engine]
    S2 --> S3[Water Pump Impeller]
    S3 --> S4[Engine Block Cylinders Hot again]
    S4 --> S5[Thermostat Open above 80 C]
    S5 --> S6[Upper Radiator Hose]
    S6 --> S7[Radiator Top Tank]
    S7 --> S8[Radiator Core Tubes and Fins]
    S8 --> S9[Radiator Bottom Tank]
    S9 --> S10[Lower Radiator Hose]
    S10 --> S3
    S11[Expansion Tank Overflow] -.-> S7
    S7 -.-> S11
    style S5 fill:#ff9,stroke:#aa0,stroke-width:2px
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. Students often draw the **radiator** without showing the **upper hose, lower hose, thermostat, and expansion tank** — each missing component costs **1 mark** in a 7-mark sketch question.
> 2. In numerical problems, forgetting the **density of coolant** (≈ 1070 kg/m³ for 50/50 glycol) leads to wrong volumetric flow rates.
> 3. Writing "water cooling" instead of **"liquid cooling"** is technically incorrect in KTU 2024 Scheme — the syllabus explicitly says "Liquid Cooling Systems".
> 4. Confusing **fin efficiency** $\eta_f$ with **fin effectiveness** $\varepsilon$ — KTU deducts 1 mark for this.
> 5. Skipping the **bypass circuit** when describing the thermostat operation is a guaranteed 2-mark loss.

---

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Questions (Direct Short-Answer)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
*List any **six** components of an air-cooled engine and state the function of each in one line.*

**Model Answer (6 × 0.5 = 3 marks):**

| # | Component | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cylinder fins | Increase surface area for heat dissipation to air |
| 2 | Head fins | Dissipate heat from the hot combustion-chamber roof |
| 3 | Air deflector / shroud | Directs cooling air uniformly across all fins |
| 4 | Centrifugal fan | Forces air across the finned surfaces |
| 5 | Fan cowl | Channels the airstream and reduces noise |
| 6 | Temperature indicator | Warns the rider of cylinder overheating |

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
*Why is ethylene glycol added to the cooling water of an automobile engine?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. Ethylene glycol **lowers the freezing point** of the coolant (a 50 % mixture freezes at ≈ –35 °C, protecting the engine in winter). [1 mark]
2. It **raises the boiling point** of the coolant, helping to prevent boil-over in hot conditions. [1 mark]
3. It contains **corrosion inhibitors** that protect the cast-iron block, aluminium head, rubber hoses, and water-pump seals from rust and chemical attack. [1 mark]

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4 Choice A]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat **labelled sketch**, describe the construction and working of a **forced-circulation liquid-cooling system** used in a modern passenger car. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Explain the **construction, working, and types of thermostat** used in an automobile liquid-cooling system. What is the role of the **bypass circuit**? **[7 marks]**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**1. Components of the system (list & label, 3 marks):**
- Water jacket surrounding the cylinders and head
- Centrifugal water pump (belt-driven)
- Thermostat housing with wax-pellet element
- Radiator (top tank, core with tubes and fins, bottom tank)
- Upper and lower radiator hoses
- Cooling fan (electric, behind radiator)
- Radiator pressure cap (0.9 – 1.4 bar)
- Expansion / overflow tank
- Temperature sender and dashboard gauge

**2. Working (3 marks):**
- Combustion heat passes through the cylinder walls into the **water jacket**.
- The **water pump** circulates coolant out through the thermostat housing.
- Below ≈ 80 °C the **thermostat is closed**, blocking radiator flow; coolant returns through the **bypass hose** directly to the pump, giving a **short loop** for fast warm-up.
- Above ≈ 80 °C the thermostat opens, sending hot coolant through the **upper hose** into the **radiator top tank**.
- Coolant flows down through the **tubes**, losing heat to the air drawn by the **cooling fan**.
- Cooled coolant leaves the **bottom tank** through the **lower hose** and re-enters the pump.
- The **pressure cap** maintains system pressure (raises boiling point); excess coolant escapes to the **expansion tank**.

**3. Sketch (1 mark):**
- (Refer to Section 4.2 Mermaid diagram for the labelled layout. In the exam, the student should reproduce a clean labelled figure showing engine, pump, thermostat, radiator, hoses, fan, and expansion tank.)

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**1. Purpose of a thermostat (1 mark):**
To maintain the engine at its **optimum working temperature (≈ 90 °C)** by regulating coolant flow through the radiator.

**2. Construction of a wax-pellet thermostat (2 marks):**
- A copper or brass cup is filled with a **wax pellet** (a hydrocarbon mixture such as cetane).
- A small rubber diaphragm seals the wax.
- A **push rod** connects the diaphragm to a **double valve** — a main valve that closes off the radiator flow, and a small bypass valve that opens the bypass circuit.
- The assembly is spring-loaded and sits in a housing between the engine outlet and the radiator inlet.

**3. Working (2 marks):**
- **Cold engine:** Main valve is held **closed by the spring**; bypass valve is open → coolant circulates within the block.
- **Hot engine:** Wax melts, expands, pushes the diaphragm and rod → main valve **opens**; bypass valve closes; coolant now flows through the radiator.
- The thermostat **modulates** the valve opening (wax expands gradually), giving a **proportional control** of flow.

**4. Types of thermostats (1 mark):**
- **Wax-pellet type** (most common in modern cars)
- **Bellows type** (older; uses a volatile liquid such as ether or alcohol)
- **Solid-state electronic thermostats** (used in some BMW/Mercedes; ECU-controlled electric heater element)

**5. Role of the bypass circuit (1 mark):**
- Ensures **continuous coolant circulation** even when the main thermostat valve is closed, preventing **local hot spots** in the cylinder head and accelerating **engine warm-up** for lower cold-start emissions.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4 Choice B]**

**(a)** Explain the **principle, components, and working of an air-cooling system** for a motorcycle engine. List the advantages and disadvantages of air cooling. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** With the help of a **neat sketch**, describe the **construction and working of a radiator and pressure cap** used in an automobile. Show how the **boiling point of coolant increases** with system pressure. **[7 marks]**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**1. Principle (1 mark):**
Heat from the combustion chamber is conducted through the metal cylinder and head, then dissipated to the **atmospheric air stream** by means of **fins (extended surface)**.

**2. Components (3 marks):**
- **Cylinder fins** (aluminium, pitch 8 – 15 mm)
- **Head fins** (finer pitch to handle higher heat flux at the exhaust port)
- **Centrifugal fan** (mounted on the flywheel, ~5 000 – 7 000 rpm)
- **Air deflector / shroud** (sheet-metal cowl that channels air)
- **Temperature sensor / warning lamp**

**3. Working (2 marks):**
- The fan draws ambient air into the shroud.
- The deflector forces the air to flow along **every fin** on the cylinder and head.
- Heat absorbed by the fins is convected away by the moving air.
- The air is finally discharged to the atmosphere behind the engine.

**4. Advantages (0.5 mark each — list 4):**
- Simple, light-weight, low cost
- No coolant → no leakage, no freezing, no corrosion
- Faster warm-up
- Higher power-to-weight ratio for small engines

**5. Disadvantages (0.5 mark each — list 4):**
- Uneven cooling of cylinders
- Noisy fan
- Sensitive to ambient temperature and traffic
- Cannot be scaled to high-power engines

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**1. Radiator construction (3 marks):**
- **Top tank** (plastic or brass) — receives hot coolant from the upper hose.
- **Core** — a matrix of **tubes** (water passages) bonded to **corrugated fins** (air-side extended surface). Modern radiators use **brazed aluminium**; older ones used **copper tubes and brass fins** with soldered joints.
- **Bottom tank** — collects cooled coolant and sends it to the lower hose.
- **Drain plug** at the bottom for coolant change.
- **Filler neck** at the top with the **pressure cap**.

**2. Pressure-cap construction (2 marks):**
- A **spring-loaded pressure valve** calibrated to 0.9 – 1.4 bar.
- A **vacuum valve** (smaller spring) that opens inwards when the system cools and pressure drops below atmospheric, allowing coolant to be sucked back from the expansion tank.
- A **rubber gasket** to seal the filler neck.

**3. Working of the cap & boiling-point rise (2 marks):**
- At normal hot operation, the cap seals the system; pressure builds up to the calibrated value.
- The boiling point of water rises with pressure (Clausius–Clapeyron). Empirical rule:
  $$T_{\text{boil}} \approx 100 + 8 \, p_{\text{gauge}}(\text{bar}) \quad °C$$
- A **1.0 bar cap** raises the boiling point to ≈ 108 °C; a **1.4 bar cap** to ≈ 111 °C. (Different textbooks use slightly different coefficients; the student should quote the rule from the prescribed text.)
- If the engine overheats, the cap **vents excess pressure and coolant** into the expansion tank; on cool-down, the **vacuum valve** opens to draw coolant back.

**4. Sketch (refer to Section 4.2):**
- The student should draw a labelled side-view of the radiator showing the top tank, core, bottom tank, filler neck, pressure cap, drain plug, and indicate air-flow direction through the core.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist (5 minutes before exam):**

### Air Cooling
- ☐ Principle: **direct heat dissipation from fins to air** by convection.
- ☐ Components: **Cylinder fins → Head fins → Deflector → Fan → Cowl → Temperature gauge** (memorise in this order).
- ☐ Fin equation: $m = \sqrt{hP/(kA_c)}$, $\eta_f = \tanh(mL_c)/(mL_c)$.
- ☐ Effectiveness $\varepsilon = \eta_f A_f / A_b$ should be **3 – 6** for a good design.
- ☐ Material: **aluminium alloy** preferred (high $k$, light).
- ☐ Applications: bikes, gensets, aero-engines.
- ☐ Main drawback: **non-uniform cylinder temperature** (rear cyl. hot).

### Liquid Cooling
- ☐ Components: **Water jacket → Pump → Thermostat → Radiator → Hoses → Cap → Expansion tank → Fan → Sender → Coolant**.
- ☐ Water has $C_p \approx 4.18$ kJ/kg·K → **3 500×** the volumetric heat capacity of air.
- ☐ Steady-state balance: $\dot{Q}_{\text{engine}} = \dot{m}_c C_{p,c} \Delta T_c = U A \Delta T_{\text{LMTD}}$.
- ☐ Pressure cap range: **0.9 – 1.4 bar**; raises boiling point to **108 – 125 °C**.
- ☐ Thermostat opens at **≈ 80 °C**, fully open at **≈ 95 °C**.
- ☐ Bypass circuit → faster warm-up + prevents local hot spots.
- ☐ Coolant: 50/50 **ethylene-glycol + water** with inhibitors; replace every 2 – 5 years.
- ☐ Modern cars use **electric water pumps and electric fans** (ECU-controlled).

### Numerical Problems
- ☐ Always carry units; final answer in **W or kW** for heat, **kg/s or L/min** for flow.
- ☐ Density of 50/50 coolant ≈ **1070 kg/m³**; $C_{p,c} \approx 3.8$ kJ/kg·K.

### Sketch Questions
- ☐ Label **every** component; arrows for **coolant flow direction** and **air-flow direction**; show the **bypass circuit** in thermostat layout.
- ☐ Use a **ruler** for straight lines; **neat labelling** is worth 1 mark by itself.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Radiator, Water Pump and Cooling Fan
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT205 - AUTOMOBILE POWER PLANT (AUTOMOBILE ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Radiator, Water Pump and Cooling Fan

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Automotive Liquid Cooling Loop – A Top-Level View

An automobile engine converts only about **25–35 %** of the fuel's chemical energy into useful mechanical work at the crankshaft. The remainder is expelled as heat – roughly **30–35 %** through the exhaust, **5–10 %** through friction, and a substantial **30–40 %** into the engine block, cylinder head, and combustion chamber walls. If this heat is not extracted continuously, the cylinder-head temperature can climb past **250 °C**, leading to detonation, oil breakdown, pre-ignition, thermal distortion, and ultimately seizure of moving parts.

The **liquid cooling system** in a water-cooled engine addresses this challenge. It is a closed hydraulic loop that circulates a coolant (typically a water–glycol mixture) through water jackets cast around the cylinders, absorbs heat from the hot metallic surfaces, and rejects this heat to the ambient atmosphere via the **radiator**. The driving force for circulation is the **water pump**, while the **cooling fan** accelerates air flow across the radiator core to enhance the rate of heat rejection – especially when the vehicle is idling or crawling in traffic.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Spirit):**
> *The radiator, water pump and cooling fan form the heat-rejection, circulation and forced-air sub-assemblies of the pressurized liquid cooling system. They are the three "passive + active" components that maintain the engine's thermal equilibrium between a lower limit of ~80 °C and an upper limit of ~105 °C.*

## 1.2 Component-Wise Formal Definition

### 1.2.1 Radiator

A **radiator** is a heat exchanger of the **liquid-to-air** type. Its hot side receives coolant from the engine at temperatures of **90–105 °C** and its cold side is exposed to atmospheric air. The radiator transfers the engine's waste heat to the passing air stream, lowering the coolant temperature to **80–90 °C** before it returns to the water pump inlet.

> [!NOTE]
> Strictly speaking, the radiator does not "radiate" – the heat transfer is overwhelmingly **convective** (≈ 90 %) with a small **conductive** component through the tubes and fins. The term "radiator" is a legacy from the early 20th century and is now universally retained in automotive vocabulary.

### 1.2.2 Water Pump

A **water pump** is a mechanically driven **centrifugal pump** (almost universally) that imparts kinetic energy to the coolant. It draws coolant from the radiator bottom tank and discharges it under pressure into the engine block's water jacket at a typical gauge pressure of **0.4–0.7 bar (40–70 kPa)**. The pump is belt-driven from the crankshaft pulley at a fixed or variable speed ratio.

### 1.2.3 Cooling Fan

A **cooling fan** is a multi-bladed axial-flow fan mounted either directly behind the radiator (pusher) or in front of the water pump (suction/engaging type). It creates a high-velocity air stream through the radiator core. The fan may be **mechanically coupled**, **viscous (thermostatic) coupled**, or **electrically driven**, depending on the design.

## 1.3 The Intuitive Analogy – "The Human Body"

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world Analogy:**
> Imagine the engine as a marathon runner in a tracksuit on a hot day. The runner's body (engine block) overheats because the muscles (combustion) generate enormous heat. The circulatory system (coolant) acts like blood – absorbing heat from the muscles. The **water pump** is the **heart**, pumping the heated blood around. The **radiator** is the **skin surface** – where heat is dumped into the air. The **cooling fan** is the **wind blowing on the skin** – which makes the heat-dumping much faster. The **thermostat** is the **brain** that decides when to open capillaries (open the radiator circuit) and when to keep them shut to preserve body heat (warm-up). Without any one of these, the runner collapses – the engine seizes.

This single analogy should remain in your mind while studying every component below.

## 1.4 Functional Block Summary

| Component | Primary Function | Energy Domain | Key Input | Key Output |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Radiator** | Reject coolant heat to atmosphere | Thermal → Air | Hot coolant (90–105 °C) | Cooled coolant (80–90 °C) |
| **Water Pump** | Circulate coolant under pressure | Mechanical → Hydraulic | Crankshaft rotation | Coolant flow (60–120 L/min) |
| **Cooling Fan** | Force ambient air through radiator core | Mechanical → Kinetic | Rotation (electrical or belt) | Air velocity (5–15 m/s) |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Radiator – Constructional Anatomy

A modern automobile radiator is a compact, brazed aluminium (or soldered copper-brass) assembly with the following sub-components:

### 2.1.1 Core (The Heart of the Radiator)

The **core** is the active heat-transfer zone. It consists of:
- **Tubes** (water passages) – small-diameter (5–10 mm) drawn aluminium or copper tubes through which hot coolant flows.
- **Fins** – thin metallic strips (corrugated or louvered) bonded to the tubes. Fins multiply the air-side surface area by a factor of **8 to 15 times** the bare tube area, vastly increasing convective heat transfer.
- **Louvres/Slits** on the fins create local turbulence in the air stream, increasing the convective heat-transfer coefficient.
- **Header / Tank joints** – where the multiple parallel tubes are connected to the upper and lower tanks.

### 2.1.2 Tanks (Upper and Lower)

- **Upper Tank (Inlet from Engine):** Receives hot coolant from the cylinder head via the top hose. Has a **filler neck** and a **pressure cap**.
- **Lower Tank (Outlet to Pump):** Collects cooled coolant and feeds it to the water-pump suction. Houses the **drain plug** in many designs.

### 2.1.3 Pressure Cap (The Safety Valve)

This is one of the most critical sub-assemblies. It performs **two** functions:
1. **Pressure sealing** – A spring-loaded valve keeps the system sealed at a calibrated opening pressure, typically **0.9 bar (≈ 13 psi)** or **1.4 bar (≈ 20 psi)** in modern cars.
2. **Vacuum relief** – A secondary smaller spring allows atmospheric air to enter when the system cools and contracts, preventing collapse of the radiator hoses.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why pressurise?**
> Water boils at **100 °C at 1 atm**. By raising system pressure to **1.0 bar gauge (≈ 2 atm absolute)**, the boiling point climbs to approximately **120 °C**. This allows the engine to run hot enough for better thermal efficiency and lower emissions, without localised boiling in the cylinder head.

### 2.1.4 Filler Neck and Hose Connections

- A removable cap seals the filler neck.
- The **upper radiator hose** (large diameter, corrugated) carries hot coolant from the engine thermostat housing to the upper tank.
- The **lower radiator hose** (often with a spring insert to prevent collapse under suction) returns cooled coolant to the pump inlet.

### 2.1.5 Radiator Mountings and Shroud

- Rubber-mounted at the front of the vehicle to absorb vibration.
- A **fan shroud** (plastic cowling) channels all fan-induced air through the core – without it, a large fraction of the fan's air simply recirculates around the radiator.

### 2.1.6 Types of Radiator Construction

| Type | Flow Direction | Tube Layout | Fins | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Down-flow** | Top → Bottom | Vertical tubes | Corrugated serpentine fins | Older vehicles, trucks |
| **Cross-flow** | Left → Right (or vice-versa) | Horizontal tubes | Louvered fins | Modern passenger cars |
| **Honeycomb** | Vertical | Hexagonal cells | Integral cell walls | Vintage / racing |
| **Mechanical Bonded (Aluminium)** | Mostly cross-flow | Flat oval tubes with brazed fins | Serpentine or louvered | Current production cars |

> [!NOTE]
> **Cross-flow radiators are dominant today** because they are wider, lower, have larger frontal area, and can be combined with the air-conditioning condenser in a "stacked" module (condenser in front, radiator behind).

## 2.2 The Water Pump – Centrifugal Pump Theory

### 2.2.1 Why Centrifugal?

- **Self-priming** – A centrifugal pump can draw coolant from a slightly lower level without flooding.
- **Smooth discharge** – Pulsation-free flow (unlike reciprocating pumps).
- **High flow at low pressure** – Cooling systems need 60–120 L/min at only 0.4–0.7 bar.
- **Compact and light** – Fits inside the limited engine bay envelope.

### 2.2.2 Constructional Details

- **Pump Body (Housing):** Cast iron or aluminium. Houses the impeller and provides inlet/outlet ports.
- **Impeller:** A radial-flow disc with curved vanes (typically 6–8). Mounted on a shaft supported by **two bearings** (a ball bearing and a needle/sleeve bearing) sealed for life with grease.
- **Pump Shaft:** Steel, hardened and ground, with the impeller press-fit and a pulley at the opposite end.
- **Seal (Mechanical Face Seal):** A rotating carbon ring is pressed against a stationary ceramic (or silicon carbide) face by a spring. This prevents coolant leakage along the shaft. Modern seals are **carbon–ceramic** with a life of **> 2 00 000 km**.
- **Pulley / Hub:** V-belt or multi-rib serpentine belt driven directly from the crankshaft.

### 2.2.3 Working Principle (Step by Step)

1. The crankshaft rotates the pulley, which spins the impeller via the shaft.
2. Coolant enters the **eye** (centre) of the impeller axially.
3. The curved vanes accelerate the coolant radially outward by **centrifugal action**.
4. Coolant leaves the impeller periphery at high velocity, enters the **volute** (spiral casing), where kinetic energy converts to **pressure energy**.
5. Pressurised coolant is discharged into the engine block's water jacket through the pump outlet.

### 2.2.4 Pump Performance Parameters

- **Flow rate Q** (L/min or m³/s)
- **Head H** (m of coolant) or Pressure Δp (Pa)
- **Power P** (W) = $\rho g Q H$
- **Efficiency η** (typically 50–70 % for automotive water pumps)

## 2.3 The Cooling Fan – Aerodynamic Sub-Assembly

### 2.3.1 Why a Fan is Needed?

At vehicle speeds above ~70 km/h, ram air alone provides adequate radiator cooling. Below this, and especially at idle, a fan is **mandatory** to:
- Force a high-velocity air stream through the radiator core.
- Compensate for the lack of natural convection at low vehicle speeds.
- Cool the engine bay (preventing bonnet paint damage in some vehicles).

### 2.3.2 Types of Cooling Fan

| Fan Type | Drive | Speed | Application | Advantage |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Fixed mechanical (4- or 6-blade metal)** | Direct belt | Proportional to engine RPM | Commercial vehicles, older cars | Simple, robust, low cost |
| **Viscous (Fluid Coupling) / Thermostatic Fan** | Silicone-fluid coupling | Slips when cool, engages when hot | Older passenger cars | Temperature-sensitive, no clutch needed |
| **Electric Fan (single or dual)** | DC motor (ECU controlled) | Independent of engine speed | Modern passenger cars | Better fuel economy, faster warm-up, on-demand |
| **Variable-speed electric (PWM)** | ECU pulse-width modulated | Variable from 30 % to 100 % | Premium / hybrid vehicles | Precise thermal management |

### 2.3.3 The Concept of Fan Slip

When a viscous fan runs at a different speed than the crankshaft pulley, the speed difference is called **slip**. Slip is essential for thermostatic operation – the silicone fluid's viscosity drops at low temperatures, allowing the fan to "slip" and consume almost no power. As the radiator outlet temperature rises, a bimetallic coil or wax element opens a valve, increasing the fluid coupling's fill level and the fan engages with less slip.

## 2.4 The Coolant – The Heat Carrier

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Pure water is **not** the recommended coolant. Modern vehicles use a mixture of **distilled water + ethylene glycol** (and corrosion inhibitors) in ratios of **30:70 to 50:50** (glycol:water).

Why glycol?
- **Lower freezing point** (pure ethylene glycol: **−12.6 °C**; 50:50 mix: **−36 °C**).
- **Higher boiling point** (50:50 mix at 1.0 bar gauge: ~**108–110 °C**).
- **Anti-corrosion** – Inhibitors prevent cavitation, rust and electrolysis.
- **Lubricity** – Protects the water pump seal.
- **Higher specific heat** than water alone (when concentrated).

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (The Cheat Code)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates every formula you will need in the KTU university examination for this topic. Memorise the **highlighted equations**.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Symbols & Units | Domain of Validity |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Heat rejected by radiator | $Q = \dot{m}_c \, c_{p,c} \, (T_{c,in} - T_{c,out})$ | $Q$ = W, $\dot{m}_c$ = kg/s, $c_{p,c}$ = J/(kg·K), $T$ = K | Energy balance on coolant side |
| 2 | Heat absorbed by air | $Q = \dot{m}_a \, c_{p,a} \, (T_{a,out} - T_{a,in})$ | $Q$ = W, $\dot{m}_a$ = kg/s, $c_{p,a}$ = J/(kg·K), $T$ = K | Energy balance on air side |
| 3 | Overall heat transfer | $Q = U \, A \, \Delta T_{LMTD}$ | $U$ = W/(m²·K), $A$ = m², $\Delta T_{LMTD}$ = K | LMTD method for steady-state exchangers |
| 4 | LMTD (counter-flow) | $\Delta T_{LMTD} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$ | All in K or °C (with $\Delta T$ positive) | Steady state, constant $U$, $c_p$ |
| 5 | Boiling point elevation (pressure cap) | $T_{sat} \approx 100 + 0.28 \, p_g$ | $p_g$ = gauge pressure in bar, $T_{sat}$ = °C | $p_g \le 1.5$ bar (linear approx.) |
| 6 | Water pump power (hydraulic) | $P_{hyd} = \Delta p \, Q$ | $P_{hyd}$ = W, $\Delta p$ = Pa, $Q$ = m³/s | Pump output to fluid |
| 7 | Pump head from pressure | $H = \dfrac{\Delta p}{\rho g}$ | $H$ = m, $\rho$ = kg/m³, $g$ = 9.81 m/s² | Incompressible coolant |
| 8 | Pump shaft power | $P_{shaft} = \dfrac{P_{hyd}}{\eta_{pump}}$ | $P$ in W, $\eta_{pump}$ dimensionless (0.5–0.7) | Includes mechanical + volumetric losses |
| 9 | Coolant volume in system | $V_c = V_{block} + V_{head} + V_{rad} + V_{hoses}$ | Litres | Sum of jacket, radiator, hose volumes |
| 10 | Fan affinity law – flow | $\dfrac{Q_2}{Q_1} = \dfrac{N_2}{N_1}$ | $Q$ = m³/s, $N$ = RPM | Geometrically similar fan, constant ρ |
| 11 | Fan affinity law – head (pressure) | $\dfrac{H_2}{H_1} = \left(\dfrac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^2$ | $H$ = Pa or m of air, $N$ = RPM | Geometrically similar fan |
| 12 | Fan affinity law – power | $\dfrac{P_2}{P_1} = \left(\dfrac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^3$ | $P$ = W, $N$ = RPM | Geometrically similar fan |
| 13 | Fan slip percentage | $S = \dfrac{N_{drive} - N_{fan}}{N_{drive}} \times 100$ | All RPM, $S$ in % | Indicates coupling slip |
| 14 | Coolant specific heat (approx.) | $c_{p,water} \approx 4.186$ kJ/(kg·K) | At 80 °C | Pure water |
| 15 | Ethylene-glycol 50:50 $c_p$ | $c_{p,EG} \approx 3.4$ to 3.6 kJ/(kg·K) | At 80 °C | Less than pure water |
| 16 | Boiling point of water at 1 atm | $T_{b,1atm} = 100$ °C | — | Reference condition |
| 17 | Volume coefficient of expansion of water | $\beta \approx 2.07 \times 10^{-4}$ per K | At 80 °C | Used for expansion-tank sizing |

> [!WARNING]
> **Markdown table rule** – In the table above, all absolute-value and fraction notation has been written using `\vert` / `\mid` / `\dfrac` to keep the LaTeX clean and the markdown un-broken. Do not write $|x|$ or $\frac{x}{y}$ inside a table cell.

## 2.6 Engineering Utility – Where This Shows Up Beyond Cars

The same three components (or their analogues) are used in:
- **Stationary Wärtsilä / Caterpillar gensets** – 12-cylinder diesel cooling.
- **Marine engines** – keel coolers replace radiators; sea-water pumps replace coolant pumps.
- **Locomotives** – large bonnet-mounted radiators and hydraulically driven fans.
- **Heavy earth-moving equipment (Caterpillar, Komatsu)** – high-pressure radiator caps (1.4 bar) and reversing fans.
- **Formula 1 cars** – incredibly compact cross-flow aluminium radiators with side-pod mounted coolers and electric fans.

The principles of **centrifugal pump head**, **LMTD heat exchange** and **fan affinity laws** are universal fluid-mechanical laws.
