# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following four worked examples model the **typical KTU 14-mark Part B problem**. Each is solved to a depth that an examiner will reward full marks. Pay close attention to unit conversions and significant figures.

## 3.1 Analytical Example 1 – Heat Rejection and Coolant Flow

**Problem Statement.**
A 4-cylinder passenger-car engine rejects **35 kW** of heat to the coolant at full load. The coolant enters the radiator at **100 °C** and leaves at **85 °C**. The coolant is a **50:50 ethylene-glycol–water mixture** with an average specific heat $c_p = 3.5$ kJ/(kg·K) and density $\rho = 1.07$ g/cm³. Find:
(a) The mass flow rate of coolant through the radiator.
(b) The volumetric flow rate in **litres per minute**.
(c) The required radiator face area if the LMTD is **60 °C** and the overall heat-transfer coefficient $U$ is **120 W/(m²·K)**.

### Step 1 – Energy balance on the coolant

$$
Q = \dot{m}_c \, c_{p,c} \, \Delta T_c
$$

where $\Delta T_c = T_{c,in} - T_{c,out} = 100 - 85 = 15$ °C (or K, the difference is the same).

### Step 2 – Solve for mass flow rate

$$
\dot{m}_c = \frac{Q}{c_{p,c} \, \Delta T_c}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_c = \frac{35\,000 \text{ W}}{3\,500 \text{ J/(kg·K)} \times 15 \text{ K}}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_c = \frac{35\,000}{52\,500} = 0.667 \text{ kg/s}
$$

### Step 3 – Convert mass flow to volumetric flow

$$
\dot{V} = \frac{\dot{m}_c}{\rho}
$$

$$
\dot{V} = \frac{0.667 \text{ kg/s}}{1\,070 \text{ kg/m}^3} = 6.234 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

Convert to L/min: $1$ m³/s $= 60\,000$ L/min

$$
\dot{V} = 6.234 \times 10^{-4} \times 60\,000 = 37.4 \text{ L/min}
$$

### Step 4 – Required heat-transfer area

$$
A = \frac{Q}{U \, \Delta T_{LMTD}}
$$

$$
A = \frac{35\,000}{120 \times 60} = \frac{35\,000}{7\,200} = 4.86 \text{ m}^2
$$

### Final Boxed Answers

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| $\dot{m}_c$ | **0.667 kg/s** |
| $\dot{V}_c$ | **37.4 L/min** |
| Required $A$ | **4.86 m²** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check:** A typical car radiator has a frontal area of ~0.30 m². With a finned core, the **total heat-transfer area** (tubes + fins) is indeed in the range of **4–6 m²**. Our 4.86 m² result is realistic.

## 3.2 Analytical Example 2 – Pump Power and Head

**Problem Statement.**
A water pump must deliver **0.6 m³/h** of coolant against a total system resistance (pipe friction + bends + radiator + block jacket) equivalent to a pressure rise of **Δp = 50 kPa (gauge)**. The pump efficiency is **65 %**. Find:
(a) The hydraulic power delivered to the coolant.
(b) The pump head in metres of coolant.
(c) The shaft (brake) power the engine must supply to the pump pulley.

Take $\rho = 1\,070$ kg/m³ (coolant), $g = 9.81$ m/s².

### Step 1 – Convert flow rate

$$
Q = 0.6 \text{ m}^3/\text{h} = \frac{0.6}{3\,600} = 1.667 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

### Step 2 – Hydraulic power

$$
P_{hyd} = \Delta p \times Q
$$

$$
P_{hyd} = 50\,000 \text{ Pa} \times 1.667 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

$$
P_{hyd} = 8.33 \text{ W}
$$

### Step 3 – Pump head

$$
H = \frac{\Delta p}{\rho \, g}
$$

$$
H = \frac{50\,000}{1\,070 \times 9.81} = \frac{50\,000}{10\,496.7} = 4.76 \text{ m of coolant}
$$

### Step 4 – Shaft power (using pump efficiency)

$$
P_{shaft} = \frac{P_{hyd}}{\eta_{pump}}
$$

$$
P_{shaft} = \frac{8.33}{0.65} = 12.82 \text{ W}
$$

### Final Boxed Answers

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| $P_{hyd}$ | **8.33 W** |
| $H$ | **4.76 m** |
| $P_{shaft}$ | **12.82 W** |

> [!WARNING]
> The hydraulic power is small because automotive cooling systems are **low-pressure, high-flow**. Real-world pump shaft power is dominated by **mechanical friction in the seal and bearings**, not by fluid power – efficiency numbers in textbooks (50–70 %) already include this.

## 3.3 Analytical Example 3 – Fan Affinity Law Application

**Problem Statement.**
A viscous fan is rated at **2 800 RPM** delivering an air flow of **3.5 m³/s** against a static pressure of **80 Pa** while consuming **180 W**. The ECU (in an electric-fan retrofit) increases the speed to **3 500 RPM** to meet thermal demand. Assuming geometrically similar fan operation, find:
(a) New air flow rate.
(b) New static pressure.
(c) New power consumption.

### Step 1 – Speed ratio

$$
k = \frac{N_2}{N_1} = \frac{3\,500}{2\,800} = 1.25
$$

### Step 2 – New flow rate (first affinity law)

$$
Q_2 = Q_1 \times k
$$

$$
Q_2 = 3.5 \times 1.25 = 4.375 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

### Step 3 – New static pressure (second affinity law)

$$
\Delta p_2 = \Delta p_1 \times k^2
$$

$$
\Delta p_2 = 80 \times (1.25)^2 = 80 \times 1.5625 = 125 \text{ Pa}
$$

### Step 4 – New power (third affinity law)

$$
P_2 = P_1 \times k^3
$$

$$
P_2 = 180 \times (1.25)^3 = 180 \times 1.953125 = 351.6 \text{ W}
$$

### Final Boxed Answers

| Quantity | Value | % change |
|---|---|---|
| $Q$ | **4.375 m³/s** | +25 % |
| $\Delta p$ | **125 Pa** | +56 % |
| $P$ | **351.6 W** | +95 % |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's favourite – the "Cube Law"** – Note how a 25 % rise in RPM nearly **doubles** the power. This is why electric fans at full speed can draw more than 350 W from the alternator, putting a real load on the electrical system.

## 3.4 Analytical Example 4 – LMTD Calculation for Radiator Sizing

**Problem Statement.**
Coolant enters a cross-flow radiator at **95 °C** and leaves at **80 °C**. Air enters the core at **35 °C** and leaves at **60 °C**. Compute the **Log-Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD)**.

### Step 1 – Define temperature differences at both ends

$$
\Delta T_1 = T_{c,in} - T_{a,out} = 95 - 60 = 35 \text{ °C}
$$

$$
\Delta T_2 = T_{c,out} - T_{a,in} = 80 - 35 = 45 \text{ °C}
$$

### Step 2 – Apply the LMTD formula

$$
\Delta T_{LMTD} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}
$$

$$
\Delta T_{LMTD} = \frac{35 - 45}{\ln(35/45)}
$$

### Step 3 – Evaluate numerator and denominator

$$
\Delta T_{LMTD} = \frac{-10}{\ln(0.7778)} = \frac{-10}{-0.2513}
$$

$$
\Delta T_{LMTD} = 39.79 \text{ °C}
$$

### Final Boxed Answer

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| $\Delta T_{LMTD}$ | **≈ 39.8 °C** |

> [!NOTE]
> The negative numerator and denominator cancel because the temperature cross (where $\Delta T_1 < \Delta T_2$) makes the LMTD smaller than the arithmetic mean (40 °C). The LMTD is always **less than the arithmetic mean** and greater than the smaller $\Delta T$.

## 3.5 Master Analytical Matrix

| Scenario | Key Input | Key Output | Primary Formula |
|---|---|---|---|
| Engine heat to be absorbed | Heat rejection $Q$ (W), $\Delta T$ (K) | Coolant flow $\dot{m}_c$ | $\dot{m}_c = Q / (c_{p,c} \Delta T)$ |
| Radiator size | $Q$, $U$, $\Delta T_{LMTD}$ | Core area $A$ | $A = Q / (U \Delta T_{LMTD})$ |
| Pump drive power | $Q$, $\Delta p$, $\eta$ | Shaft power $P$ | $P = \Delta p \, Q / \eta$ |
| Fan speed scaling | $N_1 \to N_2$ | $Q_2, \Delta p_2, P_2$ | Cube law $P \propto N^3$ |
| Boiling point with pressure cap | $p_g$ (bar gauge) | $T_{sat}$ | $T_{sat} \approx 100 + 0.28 \, p_g$ |
| LMTD (counter-flow) | 4 temperatures | $\Delta T_{LMTD}$ | $\dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$ |

## 5.2 PART A – 3-Mark Questions (with Model Answers)

### Question A1 – `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

> **Q.** With a neat sketch, explain the function of a **pressure cap** in an automobile cooling system. Why is the cap rated at a specific pressure (e.g., 0.9 bar) and not higher?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- A pressure cap is a spring-loaded valve fitted on the radiator filler neck. (1 mark)
- It seals the system at a pre-set gauge pressure (typically **0.9 bar or 1.4 bar**). This raises the boiling point of the coolant from 100 °C to about **120 °C**, allowing the engine to operate hotter without local boiling in the head. (1 mark)
- A secondary vacuum valve admits air when the system cools to prevent hose collapse. (0.5 mark)
- The cap is not rated higher because excessive pressure would stress the hoses, seals and the radiator, leading to premature failure, and because the boiling-point benefit saturates beyond ~1.4 bar. (0.5 mark)

### Question A2 – `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

> **Q.** Differentiate between a **fixed mechanical fan** and a **viscous (fluid-coupling) fan**. When is each preferred?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- A **fixed mechanical fan** is rigidly belt-coupled to the crankshaft and rotates at a fixed ratio of engine RPM. (1 mark)
- A **viscous fan** uses a silicone-fluid coupling whose torque transmission depends on the radiator outlet temperature; the fan "slips" when cool and engages progressively when hot. (1 mark)
- Fixed fans are used in **commercial vehicles, tractors and older cars** where simplicity and robustness outweigh fuel-economy concerns. (0.5 mark)
- Viscous fans are used in **modern passenger cars** to save fuel (less parasitic loss at cruise) and reduce warm-up time. (0.5 mark)

## 5.3 PART B – 14-Mark Questions (with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The structure below matches the KTU End Semester Examination (ESE) pattern: a single 14-mark question with two sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks, OR a choice between two fully independent 14-mark questions.

### QUESTION A – 14 Marks (Choice 1) – `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

> **Q. (a)** Describe, with a neat sketch, the **construction and working of an automobile radiator**. Discuss the difference between a **down-flow** and a **cross-flow** radiator. (7 marks)
>
> **Q. (b)** A radiator is to reject **28 kW** of heat. The coolant enters at **98 °C** and leaves at **88 °C**. The coolant specific heat is **3.5 kJ/(kg·K)** and density **1 060 kg/m³**. Determine (i) mass flow rate, (ii) volumetric flow in L/min, and (iii) the required core area if the LMTD is **55 °C** and overall heat-transfer coefficient $U = 110$ W/(m²·K). (7 marks)

#### Model Solution – Part (a)

**Construction (3.5 marks):**
- The radiator consists of a **core**, an **upper tank**, a **lower tank**, a **pressure cap**, a **filler neck**, and **mounting rubbers**.
- The core contains **water tubes** (aluminium or copper) bonded to thin metallic **fins**. Fins are corrugated or louvered to maximise air-side area and turbulence.
- The **upper tank** receives hot coolant from the engine via the top hose. The **lower tank** collects cooled coolant and feeds the pump.
- The **pressure cap** is a calibrated spring-loaded valve (typical rating 0.9 bar) and a vacuum-relief valve.
- Modern radiators are brazed aluminium with **plastic end tanks** for weight and corrosion resistance.

**Working (2 marks):**
- Hot coolant from the engine enters the upper tank, distributes into the parallel tubes of the core, and gives up heat to the air flowing across the fins (driven by fan + ram air). Cooled coolant collects in the lower tank and is sucked by the water pump.

**Down-flow vs Cross-flow (1.5 marks):**

| Feature | Down-flow | Cross-flow |
|---|---|---|
| Coolant flow | Top → bottom | Left ↔ right |
| Tubes | Vertical | Horizontal |
| Tanks | Top + bottom (both large) | Left + right (both at sides) |
| Height | Tall | Short and wide |
| Packaging | Older vehicles, trucks | Modern cars (stacked with AC condenser) |
| Aerodynamics | Blocks frontal area | Lower drag, better in low cars |

#### Model Solution – Part (b)

**(i) Mass flow rate**

$$
\dot{m}_c = \frac{Q}{c_{p,c} \, \Delta T_c} = \frac{28\,000}{3\,500 \times (98 - 88)}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_c = \frac{28\,000}{3\,500 \times 10} = \frac{28\,000}{35\,000} = 0.8 \text{ kg/s}
$$

**(ii) Volumetric flow**

$$
\dot{V} = \frac{\dot{m}_c}{\rho} = \frac{0.8}{1\,060} = 7.547 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

Convert to L/min:

$$
\dot{V} = 7.547 \times 10^{-4} \times 60\,000 = 45.3 \text{ L/min}
$$

**(iii) Required core area**

$$
A = \frac{Q}{U \, \Delta T_{LMTD}} = \frac{28\,000}{110 \times 55} = \frac{28\,000}{6\,050} = 4.63 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Final Answer Box:**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Mass flow rate | **0.8 kg/s** |
| Volumetric flow | **45.3 L/min** |
| Core area | **4.63 m²** |

---

### QUESTION B – 14 Marks (Choice 2 – INTERNAL CHOICE) – `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

> **Q. (a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **construction and working of a centrifugal water pump** used in an automobile cooling system. List the **causes of water-pump failure** and their remedies. (7 marks)
>
> **Q. (b)** An engine cooling fan rated at **2 400 RPM** delivers **2.8 m³/s** of air at a static pressure of **70 Pa** while consuming **120 W**. If the fan is replaced by a geometrically similar fan of the same diameter, operating at **3 000 RPM**, determine the new (i) flow rate, (ii) static pressure, and (iii) power consumption. State the **fan affinity laws** used. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution – Part (a)

**Construction (3 marks):**
- **Pump body** – cast iron / aluminium housing with inlet and outlet ports.
- **Impeller** – 6 or 8 curved vanes on a hub, press-fit to a steel shaft.
- **Bearings** – typically a sealed ball bearing (rear) and a needle / sleeve bearing (front).
- **Seal** – mechanical face seal with a rotating carbon ring and a stationary ceramic face, spring-loaded.
- **Pulley** – V-belt or multi-rib driven from the crankshaft.

**Working (2 marks):**
- The pulley rotates the impeller at engine-speed ratio. Coolant enters the impeller eye axially. Curved vanes fling coolant outward by centrifugal force into the volute, where kinetic energy converts to pressure. Pressurised coolant is discharged into the engine water jacket.

**Causes of Failure & Remedies (2 marks):**

| Cause | Symptom | Remedy |
|---|---|---|
| Seal wear | Coolant dripping, visible leakage | Replace mechanical seal |
| Bearing failure | Whining noise, shaft play | Replace bearings / pump |
| Impeller corrosion (cavitation) | Reduced flow, overheating | Use proper coolant, replace pump |
| Belt slip | Pump not spinning at rated speed | Adjust / replace belt |
| Air entrainment (leak on suction) | Overheating despite full coolant | Bleed system, fix leak |

#### Model Solution – Part (b)

**Fan Affinity Laws (1 mark):**

$$
\frac{Q_2}{Q_1} = \frac{N_2}{N_1}, \quad \frac{H_2}{H_1} = \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^2, \quad \frac{P_2}{P_1} = \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^3
$$

**Step 1 – Speed ratio**

$$
k = \frac{N_2}{N_1} = \frac{3\,000}{2\,400} = 1.25
$$

**Step 2 – New flow rate**

$$
Q_2 = Q_1 \times k = 2.8 \times 1.25 = 3.5 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 3 – New static pressure**

$$
\Delta p_2 = \Delta p_1 \times k^2 = 70 \times (1.25)^2 = 70 \times 1.5625 = 109.4 \text{ Pa}
$$

**Step 4 – New power consumption**

$$
P_2 = P_1 \times k^3 = 120 \times (1.25)^3 = 120 \times 1.953125 = 234.4 \text{ W}
$$

**Final Answer Box:**

| Quantity | Old (at 2 400 RPM) | New (at 3 000 RPM) | Change |
|---|---|---|---|
| Flow rate $Q$ | 2.8 m³/s | **3.5 m³/s** | +25 % |
| Static pressure $\Delta p$ | 70 Pa | **109.4 Pa** | +56 % |
| Power $P$ | 120 W | **234.4 W** | +95 % |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning – Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Unit conversion** – Many students forget to convert m³/h to m³/s, or Pa to bar. Always write units at every step. (-1 mark)
> 2. **Formula statement** – The three affinity laws must be **stated explicitly** before applying them. A bare numerical answer without the law is penalised. (-1 mark)
> 3. **Sign convention** – In LMTD problems, students often drop the negative sign on $\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2$ and obtain a wrong answer. The negative signs cancel because $\ln$ of a number less than 1 is also negative. (-0.5 mark)
> 4. **Coolant specific heat** – Use **$c_p \approx 3.5$ kJ/(kg·K) for 50:50 mix** at engine operating temperature. Using $c_p = 4.18$ (pure water) underestimates the required flow. (-0.5 mark)
> 5. **Sketch quality** – A neat labelled sketch of a cross-flow radiator with all six parts (tank, core, tube, fin, pressure cap, hose) fetches full marks. A vague box with one label loses 1–2 marks.

## 5.4 Common Pitfalls in Cooling System Questions (Quick Checklist)

> [!WARNING]
> - Don't confuse **gauge pressure** (cap rating) with **absolute pressure**. Atmospheric ≈ 1 bar absolute.
> - **Density of coolant** is **not** that of water – always use 1 060–1 080 kg/m³ for ethylene-glycol mix.
> - **Pump head** is in **metres of coolant**, not metres of water – the difference is small but examiners do notice.
> - The **fan cube law** applies only to **geometrically similar** fans; different blade pitch or diameter violates it.
> - LMTD applies to **steady-state** exchangers; for engine cool-down / warm-up, a **transient** model is needed.

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> Read this list **thrice** before entering the exam hall. Each bullet is a potential 3- or 7-mark question.

- The cooling system's job is to maintain coolant temperature between **80 °C and 105 °C** for optimum efficiency and low emissions.
- A typical engine rejects **30–40 %** of fuel energy as heat that must be carried away by the cooling system.
- **Radiator** is a liquid-to-air heat exchanger; transfer is mostly **convective** (~90 %), with a small conductive share.
- **Cross-flow radiators dominate** modern cars because of their low height, large frontal area, and ability to be stacked with the A/C condenser.
- The **pressure cap** raises coolant boiling point from 100 °C to ~120 °C at 0.9 bar gauge; it also has a **vacuum-relief** function.
- The **water pump** is **centrifugal** (radial-flow impeller in a volute casing) and is belt-driven from the crankshaft.
- Typical pump discharge pressure: **0.4–0.7 bar**; typical flow: **60–120 L/min**; typical efficiency: **50–70 %**.
- A **mechanical face seal** (carbon on ceramic) is the critical wear part in the pump.
- The **cooling fan** may be **fixed-mechanical**, **viscous (fluid-coupling)**, or **electric** (PWM-controlled).
- The **viscous fan** uses silicone fluid whose torque transmission depends on temperature – it slips when cold, engages when hot.
- **Fan slip** $S = (N_{drive} - N_{fan})/N_{drive} \times 100$; high slip = cool engine, low slip = hot engine.
- **Fan affinity laws**: $Q \propto N$, $\Delta p \propto N^2$, $P \propto N^3$ for geometrically similar fans.
- The **cube law** explains why electric fans at full speed can draw > 350 W, stressing the alternator.
- Modern coolant is **ethylene-glycol + distilled water + corrosion inhibitors**, not plain water.
- 50:50 EG mix: freezing point **−36 °C**, boiling point at 1 bar gauge **~108 °C**, $c_p \approx 3.5$ kJ/(kg·K), $\rho \approx 1\,070$ kg/m³.
- **LMTD** = $(\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2) / \ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)$; the negative signs cancel in the typical case where $\Delta T_1 < \Delta T_2$.
- **LMTD method** assumes constant $U$ and constant $c_p$ on both streams – valid for steady-state radiator operation.
- **Pump hydraulic power** $P_{hyd} = \Delta p \, Q$ ; **shaft power** $P_{shaft} = P_{hyd} / \eta_{pump}$.
- The **thermostat** (not asked here, but co-examined) opens at ~85 °C, allowing coolant to flow through the radiator only when needed – this shortens warm-up time.
- The **fan shroud** is not decorative – it forces 100 % of the fan's air through the core, raising heat rejection by 30–50 %.
- **Failure diagnosis** in cooling: low coolant → fan not working → thermostat stuck → blocked core → failed pump → head gasket.
- The **service interval** for a modern water pump is typically **> 1 50 000 km**, but the **coolant itself** should be replaced every **2–5 years** depending on the inhibitor package.
- **Pressure-test the system** at 1.0 bar for 2 minutes before condemning any component – a small leak elsewhere can mimic component failure.
- In the KTU exam, **always state the formula** before substituting, **always carry units**, and **always end with a boxed final answer** in a table.

---

> **END OF NOTES – Radiator, Water Pump and Cooling Fan (Module 4, PCAUT205)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Properties of Coolants and Additives
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT205 - AUTOMOBILE POWER PLANT (AUTOMOBILE ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Properties of Coolants and Additives

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus** for *Automobile Power Plant* (PCAUT205), an **engine coolant** is a heat-transfer fluid circulating through the water jackets, cylinder head, radiator, and heater core of an internal combustion (IC) engine. Its primary thermodynamic function is to absorb surplus combustion heat from the cylinder block/head and reject it to the ambient air via the radiator, thereby maintaining an optimal engine operating temperature (typically **80 °C – 95 °C** for a modern SI/CI engine).

A **coolant additive** is a chemical compound — usually present in concentrations ranging from **0.1 % to 5 % by mass** — that is blended with the base fluid (water or glycol-water mixture) to enhance specific physicochemical properties such as corrosion inhibition, foam suppression, pH buffering, anti-cavitation, and scale prevention.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4 – Cooling System):**
> The exact wording of the syllabus demands study of *"Properties of coolants and additives"* with direct emphasis on **(a) boiling point, (b) freezing point, (c) specific heat, (d) thermal conductivity, (e) viscosity, and (f) effect of additives on the cooling performance and corrosion resistance.**

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of an engine coolant exactly like the **blood in a human circulatory system**:

| Human Body Analogy | Engine Cooling System |
|---|---|
| Blood | Coolant |
| Heart | Water pump |
| Arteries (carry blood from heart) | Upper radiator hose |
| Veins (return blood to heart) | Lower radiator hose |
| Capillaries (heat exchange in skin) | Cylinder jacket & head |
| Sweating on skin | Heat rejection at radiator |
| White blood cells (fight infection) | Corrosion inhibitors / additives |

Just as blood must remain fluid (not freeze in cold weather, not boil in fever) and must not become acidic or carry debris, the coolant must remain **chemically stable, thermally efficient, and non-corrosive** across a wide temperature range — typically from **−40 °C (Arctic winter) to +110 °C (pressurised boiling point)**.

### 1.3 Why Coolants Cannot Be Pure Water

Pure water has very favourable thermal properties, but it suffers from critical drawbacks:

- **Freezing point of pure water:** **0 °C** (engine block cracks on cold start).
- **Boiling point at atmospheric pressure:** **100 °C** (cavitation and vapor lock at high loads).
- **High corrosion rate** on cast iron, aluminium, and copper alloys.
- **Zero lubricity** — accelerates water-pump seal wear.

Hence, modern coolants are **engineered mixtures** of water + glycol + additive package.

> [!NOTE]
> **Base Fluid Selection Rule:** Because pure water has the **highest specific heat capacity** of any common liquid (**4.186 kJ/kg·K**), it is always present as the *majority component* (typically **50 % – 70 % by volume**) in any practical coolant. The glycol is added not for its thermal properties (it actually *reduces* specific heat) but to **lower the freezing point** and **raise the boiling point**.

### 1.4 Classification of Engine Coolants

Based on the **KTU PCAUT205 syllabus** and the **ASTM D3306 / D4985 standards** used in the industry, coolants are classified as:

1. **Inorganic Additive Technology (IAT)** — traditional green coolant with silicate/phosphate inhibitors.
2. **Organic Acid Technology (OAT)** — long-life coolants (orange/red) using organic acid inhibitors.
3. **Hybrid Organic Acid Technology (HOAT)** — combines OAT with silicate/phosphate.
4. **Nitrite-Free OAT (NF-OAT)** — for heavy-duty diesel engines.
5. **Propylene Glycol (PG)** based — low-toxicity, used in marine and food-processing equipment.

### 1.5 Standard Performance Metrics (Bold Constants for KTU)

| Metric | Standard Value / Range |
|---|---|
| Operating temperature of modern SI engine | **80 °C – 95 °C** |
| Radiator cap pressure (typical) | **0.9 bar – 1.5 bar (gauge)** |
| Coolant change interval (IAT) | **40,000 km – 50,000 km** or 2 years |
| Coolant change interval (OAT) | **150,000 km – 250,000 km** or 5 years |
| Ratio of ethylene glycol to water (optimum) | **50:50 by volume** (freezing point ≈ −36 °C) |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fundamental Thermophysical Properties of Coolants

The effectiveness of a coolant in an automobile power plant is determined by **six** key properties. Each of these is examinable in the KTU university exam.

#### 2.1.1 Specific Heat Capacity (cₚ)

**Definition:** The amount of heat energy required to raise the temperature of **1 kg** of coolant by **1 K** (or 1 °C). Unit: **kJ/kg·K** or **J/kg·°C**.

**Why it matters:** A higher specific heat means the coolant can **absorb more heat per unit mass per degree of temperature rise**, reducing the required mass flow rate of the coolant pump.

**Comparative values at 20 °C:**

| Fluid | Specific Heat (kJ/kg·K) |
|---|---|
| Pure water | **4.186** |
| 50 % Ethylene Glycol (EG) + 50 % Water | **~3.5** |
| 30 % EG + 70 % Water | **~3.8** |
| 100 % Ethylene Glycol | **2.35** |
| Engine oil (SAE 15W-40) | **~2.1** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Insight:** Adding ethylene glycol *reduces* the specific heat of the mixture. Therefore, the coolant pump must circulate **~20 % more volume** of 50:50 EG coolant compared to pure water to absorb the same heat load. This is a frequently-tested conceptual question at KTU.

#### 2.1.2 Boiling Point (with Pressure Consideration)

**Definition:** The temperature at which the vapour pressure of the coolant equals the surrounding pressure, causing bulk phase change.

Pure water boils at **100 °C at 1 atm (101.325 kPa)**. Inside a modern pressurised cooling system, the radiator cap maintains a pressure of typically **1.0 bar (gauge)**, so the absolute pressure is **2.01325 bar**. Using the **Clausius–Clapeyron relation**, the boiling point rises to approximately **120 °C**.

For a **50:50 EG-water mixture**, the boiling point at atmospheric pressure is approximately **107 °C**, and at **1.5 bar gauge** it can reach **125 °C – 130 °C**.

#### 2.1.3 Freezing Point (Pour Point)

**Definition:** The temperature at which the coolant begins to solidify into a crystalline lattice, causing volumetric expansion (~9 % for water) and consequent engine block cracking.

The **freezing point depression** is governed by **Raoult's Law** for colligative properties:

$$\Delta T_f = i \cdot K_f \cdot m$$

where $i$ is the van't Hoff factor (≈1 for non-dissociating EG), $K_f$ is the cryoscopic constant of water (**1.86 °C·kg/mol**), and $m$ is the molality of the solute.

| Concentration (EG by volume) | Freezing Point (°C) | Boiling Point (°C, 1 atm) |
|---|---|---|
| 0 % (pure water) | 0 | 100 |
| 20 % | −7 | 101 |
| 30 % | −14 | 103 |
| 40 % | −22 | 104 |
| 50 % | −36 | 107 |
| 60 % | −50 | 110 |
| 70 % | −67 | 113 |
| 100 % (pure EG) | −13 | 197 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Eutectic Point:** Notice that **100 % pure EG** freezes at only **−13 °C** — *higher* than 50:50 or 60:40 mixtures. The lowest freezing point of the EG-water system is the **eutectic composition** at approximately **60 % EG** by volume (≈ **−67 °C**). This is a classic KTU viva/tricky question.

#### 2.1.4 Thermal Conductivity (k)

**Definition:** The rate at which heat is conducted through a unit thickness of the fluid per unit area per unit temperature difference. Unit: **W/m·K**.

Thermal conductivity governs the convective heat transfer inside the water jacket. A high thermal conductivity improves the **convective heat transfer coefficient (h)** at the metal-fluid interface.

| Fluid | Thermal Conductivity (W/m·K) at 20 °C |
|---|---|
| Pure water | **0.598** |
| 50 % EG | **~0.40** |
| 100 % EG | **0.256** |
| Engine oil | **0.145** |

#### 2.1.5 Viscosity (Dynamic, μ; Kinematic, ν)

**Definition:** Dynamic viscosity (μ) is the internal resistance of the fluid to flow. Kinematic viscosity (ν = μ/ρ). Units: **Pa·s** and **m²/s** respectively.

**Why it matters:** Higher viscosity:
- Increases **pumping power** required from the water pump (P ∝ μ · Q · ΔP).
- Reduces the **Reynolds number**, potentially shifting flow from turbulent (desirable) to laminar (undesirable).
- Reduces the convective heat transfer coefficient (Nu ∝ Re⁰·⁸ for turbulent flow in tubes).

| Fluid | Dynamic Viscosity (mPa·s) at 20 °C |
|---|---|
| Pure water | **1.002** |
| 50 % EG | **~3.5 – 4.5** |
| 100 % EG | **~21** |

#### 2.1.6 Density (ρ)

**Definition:** Mass per unit volume. Unit: **kg/m³**.

Density affects the mass flow rate (ṁ = ρ·Q) and hence the heat absorption rate. Coolant density is **inversely related to temperature** (thermal expansion).

| Fluid | Density (kg/m³) at 20 °C |
|---|---|
| Pure water | **998.2** |
| 50 % EG | **~1070** |
| 100 % EG | **1113** |

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Unit / Constant |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Sensible heat absorbed by coolant | kW, kJ/kg·K, K |
| 2 | $Q = \dot{m} \cdot h_{fg}$ | Latent heat for phase change | kW, kJ/kg |
| 3 | $\dot{m} = \rho \cdot \dot{V}$ | Mass flow rate from volumetric flow | kg/s, kg/m³, m³/s |
| 4 | $\dot{V} = \dfrac{Q}{\rho \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T}$ | Required coolant pump capacity | m³/s |
| 5 | $\Delta T_f = i \cdot K_f \cdot m$ | Freezing point depression | °C, °C·kg/mol, mol/kg |
| 6 | $P_{cap} = P_{atm} + \Delta P_{gauge}$ | Pressurised boiling point relation | kPa or bar |
| 7 | $Nu = \dfrac{h \cdot D}{k} = 0.023 \cdot Re^{0.8} \cdot Pr^{0.4}$ | Dittus-Boelter for turbulent flow | dimensionless |
| 8 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho \cdot v \cdot D}{\mu}$ | Reynolds number | dimensionless |
| 9 | $Pr = \dfrac{\mu \cdot c_p}{k}$ | Prandtl number | dimensionless |
| 10 | $P_{pump} = \dfrac{\dot{V} \cdot \Delta P}{\eta_{pump}}$ | Water pump power | W, m³/s, Pa |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Exam Tip:** Equations (1), (3), and (5) are the **most frequently tested** in KTU end-semester examinations. Students should be able to derive $\dot{V}$ from a given heat rejection rate.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Race car engineering:** Use **waterless coolants** (propylene glycol based, boiling point >180 °C) to eliminate boiling at high cylinder pressures.
- **Arctic operations (Ladakh, Siberia):** Coolant concentration increased to **60:40 EG:water** to prevent freezing at −50 °C.
- **EV battery thermal management:** Indirectly relevant — battery packs use similar glycol-water coolants with tighter purity control.
- **Marine engines:** Use **propylene glycol** for low toxicity (in case of discharge into sea).
- **Heavy-duty diesel (BEML, Ashok Leyland):** Use **OAT coolants with nitrite-free formulations** to protect wet-sleeve cylinder liners from cavitation pitting.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Coolant Pump Flow Rate from Heat Load

**Problem statement:** A 4-cylinder SI engine develops **brake power (BP) = 75 kW** at **3000 rpm**. The brake thermal efficiency is **28 %**, and the cooling system rejects **30 %** of the total heat supplied. The coolant is a **50:50 ethylene glycol-water mixture** with specific heat $c_p = 3.5 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$ and density $\rho = 1070 \text{ kg/m}^3$. The allowable temperature rise of the coolant in the engine is $\Delta T = 8 \text{ K}$. Calculate the **required volumetric flow rate** of the coolant in **litres per minute (LPM)**.

#### Step 1 — Total Heat Input Rate

$$\dot{Q}_{in} = \dfrac{BP}{\eta_{bth}} = \dfrac{75}{0.28} = 267.857 \text{ kW}$$

*(Conversion logic: 75 kW is the useful brake power; dividing by efficiency gives the rate at which chemical energy in the fuel is released as heat.)*

#### Step 2 — Heat Rejected to Coolant (30 % assumption)

$$\dot{Q}_{cool} = 0.30 \times \dot{Q}_{in} = 0.30 \times 267.857 = 80.357 \text{ kW}$$

*(Conversion logic: In a typical SI engine, roughly 30–35 % of the fuel energy is carried away by the coolant; 30 % is the KTU-typical value used in numerical problems.)*

#### Step 3 — Mass Flow Rate of Coolant from Sensible Heat Equation

The sensible heat balance is:

$$\dot{Q}_{cool} = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$$

Solving for $\dot{m}$:

$$\dot{m} = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_{cool}}{c_p \cdot \Delta T}$$

$$\dot{m} = \dfrac{80.357}{3.5 \times 8} = \dfrac{80.357}{28.0} = 2.8699 \text{ kg/s}$$

*(Conversion logic: 80.357 kJ/s divided by (3.5 kJ/kg·K × 8 K) = kg/s of coolant required.)*

#### Step 4 — Convert to Volumetric Flow Rate

$$\dot{V} = \dfrac{\dot{m}}{\rho} = \dfrac{2.8699}{1070} = 2.6821 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

*(Conversion logic: Mass flow rate divided by density gives volume flow rate in m³/s.)*

#### Step 5 — Convert to Litres Per Minute (LPM)

$$\dot{V} = 2.6821 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} \times \dfrac{10^3 \text{ L}}{1 \text{ m}^3} \times \dfrac{60 \text{ s}}{1 \text{ min}}$$

$$\dot{V} = 2.6821 \times 10^{-3} \times 60{,}000 = 160.93 \text{ LPM}$$

**Final Answer:** The coolant pump must deliver approximately **161 LPM**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Freezing Point Depression of Ethylene Glycol-Water Mixture

**Problem statement:** Calculate the freezing point of a coolant mixture containing **40 % by volume** of ethylene glycol (EG) in water. Given: density of EG = **1113 kg/m³**, density of water = **998 kg/m³**, molecular weight of EG (C₂H₆O₂) = **62.07 g/mol**, $K_f$ for water = **1.86 °C·kg/mol**, $i = 1$.

#### Step 1 — Assume 1 Litre of Mixture → Masses of Each Component

For a **1-litre** mixture at 40 % EG by volume:
- Volume of EG = 0.40 L = 0.40 × 10⁻³ m³
- Volume of water = 0.60 L = 0.60 × 10⁻³ m³

Masses:
$$m_{EG} = 0.40 \times 10^{-3} \times 1113 = 0.4452 \text{ kg}$$

$$m_{water} = 0.60 \times 10^{-3} \times 998 = 0.5988 \text{ kg}$$

#### Step 2 — Moles of EG

$$n_{EG} = \dfrac{m_{EG}}{M_{EG}} = \dfrac{0.4452 \times 10^3 \text{ g}}{62.07 \text{ g/mol}} = \dfrac{445.2}{62.07} = 7.173 \text{ mol}$$

#### Step 3 — Molality of EG in Water

Molality is **moles of solute per kg of solvent**:

$$m = \dfrac{n_{EG}}{m_{water} \text{ (in kg)}} = \dfrac{7.173}{0.5988} = 11.98 \text{ mol/kg}$$

#### Step 4 — Freezing Point Depression

$$\Delta T_f = i \cdot K_f \cdot m = 1 \times 1.86 \times 11.98 = 22.28 \text{ °C}$$

#### Step 5 — Freezing Point of Mixture

$$T_{f,\text{mixture}} = T_{f,\text{pure water}} - \Delta T_f = 0 - 22.28 = -22.28 \text{ °C}$$

**Final Answer:** The freezing point is approximately **−22 °C**, which matches the empirical value from the table in §2.1.3 (the small deviation is because Raoult's law is an ideal-solution approximation).

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Common Coolants

| Property | Pure Water | 50:50 EG-Water | 100 % EG | 50:50 PG-Water |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Freezing Point (°C) | **0** | **−36** | **−13** | **−33** |
| Boiling Point (°C, 1 atm) | **100** | **107** | **197** | **104** |
| Specific Heat (kJ/kg·K) | **4.186** | **3.5** | **2.35** | **3.6** |
| Density (kg/m³) | **998** | **1070** | **1113** | **1040** |
| Viscosity (mPa·s, 20 °C) | **1.0** | **~4.0** | **~21** | **~4.5** |
| Thermal Cond. (W/m·K) | **0.598** | **~0.40** | **0.256** | **~0.40** |
| Toxicity | None | Moderate (EG) | Moderate | **Low (PG food-grade)** |
| Cost | Negligible | Low | Moderate | Higher |
| Common Use | Banned in autos | **Universal automotive** | Industrial heat transfer | Marine / Food-grade |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Coolant Property Hierarchy and Their Inter-Dependencies

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Engine Coolant System Design] --> B[Base Fluid Selection]
    A --> C[Additive Package Design]
    A --> D[Operating Envelope Validation]

    B --> B1[Pure Water]
    B --> B2[Ethylene Glycol]
    B --> B3[Propylene Glycol]

    B1 --> P1[Highest Specific Heat 4.186 kJ per kg K]
    B1 --> P2[Lowest Freezing Point Defect 0 C]
    B1 --> P3[Zero Corrosion Inhibition]
    B1 --> P4[Lowest Cost]

    B2 --> P5[Freezing Point Depression 60 percent by volume eutectic]
    B2 --> P6[Boiling Point Elevation up to 197 C pure]
    B2 --> P7[Toxic Requires Careful Disposal]
    B2 --> P8[Moderate Specific Heat Loss 2.35 pure]

    B3 --> P9[Non Toxic Food Grade Safe]
    B3 --> P10[Lower Thermal Performance vs EG]
    B3 --> P11[Higher Viscosity Penalty]

    C --> C1[Corrosion Inhibitors]
    C --> C2[Anti Foaming Agents]
    C --> C3[Anti Cavitation Additives]
    C --> C4[pH Buffers]
    C --> C5[Anti Scale Agents]
    C --> C6[Dyes and Bitterants]

    C1 --> Q1[Silicates Phosphates for IAT coolants]
    C1 --> Q2[Organic Acids Benzoate Sebacate for OAT]
    C1 --> Q3[Hybrid HOAT for European vehicles]

    C2 --> R1[Silicone Defoamers typical 50 to 200 ppm]

    C3 --> S1[Sodium Nitrite for wet sleeve diesel liners]

    C4 --> T1[Maintain pH between 7 and 10.5]

    C5 --> U1[Phosphonates Polyacrylates]

    D --> V1[ASTM D3306 Light Duty Standard]
    D --> V2[ASTM D4985 Heavy Duty Standard]
    D --> V3[JIS K2234 Japanese Standard]
    D --> V4[Freeze Boil Corrosion Bench Tests]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Function of Each Coolant Additive Class

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CorrosionZone[Corrosion Threats in Engine]
        K1[Cast Iron Block]
        K2[Aluminium Alloy Head]
        K3[Copper Brass Radiator]
        K4[Rubber Elastomer Hoses]
        K5[Water Pump Seal]
    end

    subgraph AdditiveShelf[Additive Shelf]
        L1[Inhibitor A - Silicate]
        L2[Inhibitor B - Organic Acid]
        L3[Inhibitor C - Nitrite]
        L4[Defoamer - Silicone]
        L5[pH Buffer - Borax]
        L6[Biocide - Isothiazolone]
    end

    K1 --> L3
    K2 --> L2
    K3 --> L2
    K4 --> L1
    K5 --> L4

    L5 --> M1[Maintains pH 7 to 10.5]
    L6 --> M2[Prevents Microbial Slime]
    L4 --> M3[Reduces Cavitation Erosion]
    L1 --> M4[Forms Glassy Protective Film]
    L2 --> M5[Forms Molecular Self Healing Layer]
    L3 --> M6[Prevents Wet Sleeve Pitting]

    M1 --> N[Extended Coolant Service Life]
    M2 --> N
    M3 --> N
    M4 --> N
    M5 --> N
    M6 --> N
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Coolant Property Decision Tree (KTU-style)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Select Coolant for Vehicle] --> S1{Vehicle Type?}
    S1 -- Passenger Car SI --> S2[Use OAT or HOAT 50:50 EG Water]
    S1 -- Heavy Duty Diesel --> S3[Use NF OAT with Nitrite]
    S1 -- Marine Application --> S4[Use 50:50 PG Water low toxicity]
    S1 -- Arctic Region --> S5[Use 60:40 EG Water freezing below minus 50 C]
    S1 -- Racing Track --> S6[Use Waterless PG coolant no boiling]

    S2 --> S7{Check Properties}
    S3 --> S7
    S4 --> S7
    S5 --> S7
    S6 --> S7

    S7 --> T1[Specific Heat above 3.0 kJ per kg K]
    S7 --> T2[Freezing Point below minus 30 C]
    S7 --> T3[Boiling Point above 120 C pressurised]
    S7 --> T4[pH between 7 and 10.5]
    S7 --> T5[Reserve Alkalinity above 5 mL of 0.1 N HCl]
    S7 --> T6[Foaming Tendency below 50 mL per 200 mL per 5 min]
    S7 --> T7[Corrosion Weight Loss below 10 mg per specimen per week]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Block-Level Diagrams instead of Physical Drawings?**
> Properties of coolants involve *physico-chemical interactions* that are not easily captured in a 2-D physical drawing. The block diagrams above are the academically standard way to map cause-effect relationships in KTU board examinations.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**List any six desirable properties of an engine coolant and explain the significance of specific heat capacity.**

**Model Answer (6 points):**

The six desirable properties of an engine coolant are:

1. **High specific heat capacity** (for water 4.186 kJ/kg·K) so that a small mass of coolant can absorb a large quantity of heat.
2. **Low freezing point** (typically below −30 °C) to prevent solidification in cold climates.
3. **High boiling point** (≥120 °C under pressurised cooling system) to avoid vapour lock and cavitation.
4. **Low viscosity** to minimise pumping power and ensure turbulent flow (Re > 4000).
5. **High thermal conductivity** for efficient convective heat transfer at the metal-fluid interface.
6. **Chemical inertness / non-corrosive** towards cast iron, aluminium, copper and rubber.

**Significance of specific heat capacity:** The specific heat ($c_p$) determines the rate of heat removal per unit mass flow and per unit temperature rise. From $Q = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$, a higher $c_p$ allows either a **smaller coolant pump capacity** or a **lower temperature rise ($\Delta T$)** for the same heat duty. Pure water has the highest $c_p$ of all common coolants.

---

#### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**What are the functions of corrosion inhibitors, anti-foaming agents, and pH buffers in a coolant additive package?**

**Model Answer (3 × 1 = 3 marks):**

1. **Corrosion inhibitors** (e.g., silicates, phosphates, organic acids like sebacic acid): Form a protective film on metal surfaces of the radiator, cylinder head, and water pump, preventing electrochemical corrosion of cast iron, aluminium, and copper.

2. **Anti-foaming agents** (e.g., silicone-based defoamers, 50–200 ppm): Reduce surface tension and break up foam formed by air entrainment from the water pump. Foam reduces the effective heat transfer area and can cause hot spots.

3. **pH buffers** (e.g., borax, sodium benzoate): Maintain the coolant pH in the **alkaline range of 7 to 10.5**, preventing both acidic corrosion (pH < 7) and alkaline attacks on aluminium alloys (pH > 10.5).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Compare the thermophysical properties of **water**, **ethylene glycol**, and a **50:50 EG-water mixture** in a tabular form. Justify why pure water is not used as a coolant in modern automobile engines even though it has the highest specific heat. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An automotive engine rejects **35 kW** of heat to the coolant. The coolant is a **50:50 ethylene glycol-water mixture** with $c_p = 3.5 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$, $\rho = 1070 \text{ kg/m}^3$. The coolant enters the engine block at **75 °C** and leaves at **92 °C**. Calculate: (i) mass flow rate of coolant, (ii) volumetric flow rate in LPM, and (iii) the diameter of a circular pipe that will give a flow velocity of 1.5 m/s. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q-A (a):**

| Property | Pure Water | 100 % EG | 50:50 EG-Water |
|---|---|---|---|
| Freezing Point (°C) | 0 | −13 | −36 |
| Boiling Point (°C, 1 atm) | 100 | 197 | 107 |
| Specific Heat (kJ/kg·K) | 4.186 | 2.35 | 3.5 |
| Density (kg/m³) | 998 | 1113 | 1070 |
| Viscosity (mPa·s) | 1.0 | 21 | 4.0 |
| Thermal Cond. (W/m·K) | 0.598 | 0.256 | 0.40 |
| Corrosion Effect | High (no inhibitor) | Mild | None (with additives) |

**Why pure water is unsuitable:**

1. Freezes at **0 °C** → engine block cracks on cold start (water expands 9 % on freezing).
2. Boils at only **100 °C** at atmospheric pressure → vapour lock in pressurised systems above 100 °C.
3. Highly **corrosive** to cast iron, aluminium alloys, and copper-brass radiator cores.
4. **No lubricity** → accelerates wear of the water-pump mechanical seal.
5. Promotes **scale formation** from dissolved calcium/magnesium salts, clogging narrow passages in the radiator.

Therefore, water is always blended with **ethylene glycol (50:50)** and a **chemical additive package** before use.

---

**Model Solution to Q-A (b):**

**Given:** $\dot{Q}_{cool} = 35 \text{ kW}$, $c_p = 3.5 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$, $\rho = 1070 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $T_{in} = 75 \text{ °C}$, $T_{out} = 92 \text{ °C}$, $v = 1.5 \text{ m/s}$.

**(i) Mass flow rate:**

$$\dot{m} = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_{cool}}{c_p \cdot \Delta T} = \dfrac{35}{3.5 \times (92 - 75)} = \dfrac{35}{3.5 \times 17} = \dfrac{35}{59.5}$$

$$\dot{m} = 0.5882 \text{ kg/s}$$

**(ii) Volumetric flow rate:**

$$\dot{V} = \dfrac{\dot{m}}{\rho} = \dfrac{0.5882}{1070} = 5.497 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

Converting to LPM:

$$\dot{V} = 5.497 \times 10^{-4} \times 60{,}000 = 32.98 \text{ LPM}$$

**(iii) Pipe diameter:**

From continuity, $\dot{V} = A \cdot v = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2 \cdot v$.

$$D = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \cdot \dot{V}}{\pi \cdot v}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \times 5.497 \times 10^{-4}}{\pi \times 1.5}}$$

$$D = \sqrt{\dfrac{2.199 \times 10^{-3}}{4.712}} = \sqrt{4.667 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$D = 0.0216 \text{ m} = 21.6 \text{ mm}$$

**Final Answers:** (i) **0.588 kg/s**, (ii) **33 LPM**, (iii) **D = 21.6 mm**.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat flow diagram, describe the **classification of engine coolants** (IAT, OAT, HOAT, NF-OAT, PG). Explain the role of each additive class used in modern coolants. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define **freezing point depression**. A coolant is prepared by mixing **3 litres of ethylene glycol** with **5 litres of water**. Given: density of EG = **1113 kg/m³**, density of water = **998 kg/m³**, molecular weight of EG = **62.07 g/mol**, $K_f$ (water) = **1.86 °C·kg/mol**. Calculate the **freezing point** of the mixture. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q-B (a):**

**Classification of Engine Coolants:**

1. **IAT (Inorganic Additive Technology):** Traditional "green" coolant. Uses silicates and phosphates as corrosion inhibitors. Service life: 2 years / 50,000 km.

2. **OAT (Organic Acid Technology):** Long-life coolant (orange/red). Uses organic acids (2-EHA, sebacic acid) that form a monomolecular protective layer. Service life: 5 years / 250,000 km.

3. **HOAT (Hybrid OAT):** Combines organic acids with low-level silicates/phosphates. Used in European vehicles (VW, BMW, Mercedes).

4. **NF-OAT (Nitrite-Free OAT):** Used in heavy-duty diesel engines with **sodium nitrite + molybdate** to protect wet-sleeve liners from cavitation pitting.

5. **PG (Propylene Glycol):** Low-toxicity, used in marine and food-processing applications.

**Roles of Additives (each worth 1 mark):**

- **Corrosion inhibitors (silicates, organic acids, nitrites)** — protect metal surfaces.
- **Anti-foaming agents (silicone defoamers)** — reduce foam from pump cavitation.
- **Anti-cavitation additives (sodium nitrite)** — protect wet-sleeve cylinder liners.
- **pH buffers (borax, sodium benzoate)** — maintain pH 7 – 10.5.
- **Anti-scale agents (phosphonates, polyacrylates)** — prevent hard-water scale.
- **Dyes and bitterants** — leak detection and anti-ingestion safety.

---

**Model Solution to Q-B (b):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Freezing point depression is the decrease in the freezing point of a solvent when a non-volatile solute is dissolved in it, described by $\Delta T_f = i \cdot K_f \cdot m$.

**Calculation (6 marks):**

**Step 1 — Mass of EG in 3 L:**

$$m_{EG} = 3 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3 \times 1113 \text{ kg/m}^3 = 3.339 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 2 — Mass of water in 5 L:**

$$m_{water} = 5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3 \times 998 \text{ kg/m}^3 = 4.990 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 3 — Moles of EG:**

$$n_{EG} = \dfrac{3.339 \times 10^3 \text{ g}}{62.07 \text{ g/mol}} = 53.79 \text{ mol}$$

**Step 4 — Molality:**

$$m = \dfrac{53.79}{4.990} = 10.78 \text{ mol/kg}$$

**Step 5 — Freezing Point Depression:**

$$\Delta T_f = 1 \times 1.86 \times 10.78 = 20.05 \text{ °C}$$

**Step 6 — Freezing Point of Mixture:**

$$T_f = 0 - 20.05 = -20.05 \text{ °C}$$

**Final Answer:** Freezing point of the mixture ≈ **−20 °C**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign Error in Freezing Point:** Students often write $T_f = \Delta T_f$ instead of $T_f = T_{f,\text{pure}} - \Delta T_f$. The freezing point of pure water is **0 °C**, not the depression value itself.
> 2. **Molality vs Molarity:** Always use **molality (mol solute / kg solvent)**, not molarity (mol/L solution). Forgetting to convert water volume to **mass in kg** is a recurring 2-mark deduction.
> 3. **Volumetric vs Mass Flow Rate:** Many students mix up $\dot{V}$ and $\dot{m}$. Always check units in the final answer: if the question asks for **LPM**, divide by $\rho$ and multiply by 60,000.
> 4. **Eutectic Confusion:** Pure EG freezes at **−13 °C** — *higher* than 50:50 mixtures. Students wrongly assume "more glycol = lower freezing point".
> 5. **Pump Power Estimation:** A common KTU 14-mark question asks for the *pump power* in Watts. Do not forget to divide by the **pump efficiency** ($\eta_{pump} \approx 0.6$–$0.7$).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Six primary properties** tested in KTU: Specific heat, Boiling point, Freezing point, Thermal conductivity, Viscosity, Density.
- **Pure water has the highest specific heat (4.186 kJ/kg·K)** but is **not used alone** because it freezes at 0 °C, boils at 100 °C, and causes corrosion.
- **Ethylene glycol (EG)** is the standard antifreeze additive; it depresses the freezing point and elevates the boiling point.
- **Eutectic point of EG-water:** ~60 % EG by volume → lowest freezing point of **−67 °C**.
- **50:50 EG-water** is the industry standard; provides **−36 °C** freeze protection and **107 °C** boil protection (atmospheric), rising to ~120 °C under pressure.
- **Coolant additives** perform six functions: corrosion inhibition, foam suppression, anti-cavitation, pH buffering, anti-scale, and identification (dye/bitterant).
- **IAT** coolants last ~2 years; **OAT** coolants last up to 5 years / 250,000 km.
- **Key formula:** $\dot{V} = \dfrac{\dot{Q}}{\rho \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T}$ and $\Delta T_f = K_f \cdot m$.
- **Modern trend:** Hybrid HOAT and nitrite-free OAT coolants for extended service life and environmental compliance.
- **Always use molality, not molarity, in freezing point depression calculations.**
- **Pressurised systems** raise the boiling point — a radiator cap of 1 bar gauge raises water's boiling point from 100 °C to ~120 °C.
- **Viscosity increases** with EG concentration → higher pumping power required.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Requirements of Lubrication Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT205 - AUTOMOBILE POWER PLANT (AUTOMOBILE ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Requirements of Lubrication Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

A **Lubrication System** in an automobile engine is a self-contained arrangement of mechanical, hydraulic, and monitoring components designed to supply clean, pressurized lubricating oil at the correct temperature and flow rate to all moving parts of the engine — bearings, pistons, camshafts, valves, and gears — in order to minimize friction, wear, heat, and corrosion.

The **Requirements of a Lubrication System** are the engineering specifications, design parameters, and performance criteria that a lubrication system must satisfy to ensure the longevity, efficiency, and reliability of an internal combustion (IC) engine. These requirements govern the *what*, *how much*, *when*, and *where* of oil delivery inside the engine.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 4 – Core Syllabus Statement:**
> *"The engine lubrication system must ensure adequate supply of clean oil at the correct pressure, temperature, and viscosity to all bearing surfaces, while simultaneously providing cooling, sealing, and cleaning action."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine you are running a marathon (the engine running for hours). Without water, your joints would grind, your muscles would overheat, and dust/debris would settle on your skin. You need:
- A **continuous water supply** (oil flow)
- **Hydration to keep the body cool** (oil cooling)
- A **filter to clean impurities** (oil filter)
- A **proper distribution network** (oil galleries/pump)

Now, the **Requirements of Lubrication Systems** are basically the *engineering checklist* for that "water system" — the engine's lifeblood. Just as a doctor first lists the patient's needs before prescribing medicine, an automobile engineer first defines the lubrication requirements before designing the system.

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick Analogy Map:**
> | Human Body Need | Engine Lubrication Equivalent |
> |---|---|
> | Drinking water supply | Oil pump & sump |
> | Blood circulation | Oil galleries & passages |
> | Kidney filtration | Oil filter |
> | Sweat / evaporative cooling | Oil carries heat away |
> | Skin oils as anti-rust | Oil film prevents corrosion |

### 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics (Must Know)

The key physical constants and standard metrics that govern lubrication requirements are:

- **Viscosity (μ or ν)**: Resistance of oil to flow — measured in **Pa·s (dynamic)** or **cSt (kinematic)**.
- **Pressure (P)**: Oil pressure requirement — typically **1.5 to 4.0 bar** at idle in a petrol engine; **3.0 to 5.0 bar** at cruising speed.
- **Temperature (T)**: Operating oil temperature window — **80 °C to 110 °C** normal range; **120 °C** is the upper safety limit.
- **SAE Viscosity Grade**: Industry classification — e.g., **SAE 20W-50**, **SAE 5W-30**.
- **API Service Category**: Performance classification — e.g., **API SN, API SM, API CI-4**.
- **Oil Capacity**: Typically **3 to 6 litres** in a passenger car engine; up to **25–40 litres** in heavy commercial diesel engines.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Functional Requirements of a Lubrication System (Why it is needed)

The lubrication system in an automobile engine must satisfy **six primary functional requirements**. KTU examiners frequently ask these as direct 3-mark questions.

1. **Friction Reduction (Anti-friction requirement):**
   - The oil film must separate two moving metal surfaces to prevent direct metal-to-metal contact.
   - This converts **sliding (dry) friction** into **fluid friction**, drastically reducing frictional losses.

2. **Wear Prevention (Anti-wear requirement):**
   - Boundary lubrication regime requires the oil to form a strong, tenacious film.
   - **Anti-wear (AW) additives** like **Zinc Dialkyldithiophosphate (ZDDP)** chemically react with metal to form a sacrificial protective layer.

3. **Cooling Requirement:**
   - Roughly **60 % of total engine heat** is rejected through the cooling system (water jacket).
   - However, the **oil** specifically removes heat from bearings, pistons (under-crown), and turbocharger bearings — typically carrying away **5 % to 10 %** of total heat.

4. **Sealing Requirement:**
   - Oil fills the microscopic gaps between piston rings and cylinder wall, preventing **blow-by** of combustion gases.
   - The oil film acts as a **dynamic hydraulic seal**.

5. **Cleaning Requirement:**
   - Oil carries away **combustion residues** (carbon, soot, varnish), **metallic wear particles**, and **sludge**.
   - **Detergent additives** keep contaminants suspended; **dispersants** prevent them from agglomerating.

6. **Corrosion / Rust Prevention:**
   - Oil film prevents direct contact between acidic combustion by-products and metal surfaces.
   - **Anti-oxidant** and **rust-inhibitor additives** neutralize acids.

### 2.2 Oil Quantity & Oil Pressure Requirements

- **Sump Capacity** must be sized so that:
  - Oil level never falls below the oil-pump pickup at hard cornering or braking.
  - At minimum, **1.5 to 2.0 litres** residual oil must remain in the sump at all vehicle attitudes.
- **Oil Pressure** must remain above a minimum threshold even at low idle RPM to prevent **oil starvation**.
- **Oil Flow Rate (Q)** is governed by:
  
  $$ Q = \frac{V_d \times N \times k}{60 \times 2} $$

  where $V_d$ is displacement volume (m³), $N$ is engine RPM, and $k$ is a constant (~0.5–1.5 depending on design).

- **Volumetric Efficiency of Pump (η_v):**
  
  $$ \eta_v = \frac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{theoretical}} $$

  Typically η_v = **0.80 to 0.95** for gear-type pumps.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> Save this table — KTU board exams directly test these formulas in Part B derivations and numerical problems.

| # | Formula / Property | Symbol / Equation | Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Dynamic Viscosity | $\mu = \tau / \dot{\gamma}$ | Pa·s | Resistance to shearing |
| 2 | Kinematic Viscosity | $\nu = \mu / \rho$ | cSt (mm²/s) | Flow-based viscosity |
| 3 | Viscosity Index (VI) | Empirical scale 0–100+ | dimensionless | Rate of viscosity change with T |
| 4 | Reynolds Number (bearing) | $Re = \frac{\rho \cdot v \cdot h}{\mu}$ | dimensionless | Flow regime (laminar/turbulent) |
| 5 | Petroff's Bearing Friction | $F_f = \frac{4 \pi^2 \mu N L r^3}{C}$ | N | Frictional torque in journal bearing |
| 6 | Oil Pump Volumetric Efficiency | $\eta_v = Q_{act} / Q_{theo}$ | % | Pump performance |
| 7 | Oil Film Thickness (min.) | $h_{min} \geq R_a + R_a$ | μm | Stribeck curve / boundary regime |
| 8 | SAE Viscosity (W grade) | Max viscosity at −20 °C | cP | Cold-start pumpability |
| 9 | API Service Category | SM/SN/CF/CI-4 | — | Oil performance classification |
| 10 | Oil Mass Flow | $\dot{m} = \rho \times Q$ | kg/s | Heat-removal capacity |

> **LaTeX Isolation Rule:** In KTU answer sheets, always enclose the Petroff's equation in display math mode ($$…$$), never inline with vertical pipes.

### 2.4 Properties of Lubricating Oil (Required Characteristics)

The lubrication system requires the oil to possess these **inherent physical-chemical properties**:

1. **Viscosity** – must be high enough to maintain film; low enough to flow at low T.
2. **Viscosity Index (VI)** – measures how stable viscosity is with temperature.
3. **Flash Point** – minimum **150 °C** for petrol, **200 °C** for diesel engine oils.
4. **Fire Point** – typically **20–30 °C** above flash point.
5. **Pour Point** – lowest temperature at which oil flows; pour-point depressants extend this.
6. **Cloud Point** – temperature at which wax crystals appear.
7. **Neutralization Number (TAN/TBN):**
   - **TAN (Total Acid Number):** indicates oil degradation.
   - **TBN (Total Base Number):** indicates reserve alkalinity; for diesel engines TBN ≥ **8–12 mg KOH/g**.
8. **Carbon Residue** – low value desired (clean-burning oil).
9. **Ash Content** – low for petrol; controlled for diesel (sulphated ash).
10. **Emulsification / Demulsibility** – oil must separate quickly from water.

### 2.5 Lubricating Oil Additives (Engineering Requirements)

Modern engine oils are **base oil + additive package**. A KTU examiner will often ask: *"Why are additives added to engine oil?"* — the answer is to *enhance properties that base oil alone cannot deliver*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Most-Frequently Asked Additive List (3-Mark Favourite):**
>
> | Additive Type | Function | Example |
> |---|---|---|
> | Detergent | Keeps engine clean | Calcium/Magnesium sulphonates |
> | Dispersant | Suspends soot/carbon | Polyisobutylene succinimide |
> | Anti-wear (AW) | Protects boundary contacts | **ZDDP** (Zinc dialkyldithiophosphate) |
> | Anti-oxidant | Slows oil oxidation | Hindered phenols, ZDDP |
> | Viscosity Index Improver (VII) | Reduces T-sensitivity | Polymethacrylates (PMA) |
> | Pour Point Depressant | Improves cold flow | Polymethacrylates |
> | Anti-foamant | Prevents air entrainment | Silicone polymers |
> | Rust/Corrosion inhibitor | Protects metal surfaces | Fatty acid esters |
| Friction Modifier | Reduces boundary friction | Organic Mo-compounds, graphite |
> | Anti-knock (diesel) | Delays detonation | Not typical — cetane improvers in fuel |

### 2.6 SAE Viscosity Classification (Cold & Hot Performance)

The **Society of Automotive Engineers (SAE)** classifies engine oils by viscosity in two parts:

- **W grade (Winter):** Defines low-temperature pumpability.
  - SAE 0W: Max 6000 cP at −35 °C
  - SAE 5W: Max 6600 cP at −30 °C
  - SAE 10W: Max 7000 cP at −25 °C
  - SAE 15W: Max 7000 cP at −20 °C
  - SAE 20W: Max 9500 cP at −15 °C
  - SAE 25W: Max 13000 cP at −10 °C

- **Non-W grade (Hot):** Defines high-temperature kinematic viscosity at 100 °C.
  - SAE 20: 5.6 – 9.3 cSt
  - SAE 30: 9.3 – 12.5 cSt
  - SAE 40: 12.5 – 16.3 cSt
  - SAE 50: 16.3 – 21.9 cSt

A **multi-grade oil** (e.g., **SAE 20W-40**) carries a VII polymer that expands at high T to maintain viscosity, providing both cold-start fluidity and hot operating protection.

### 2.7 API Service Classification (Performance Categories)

The **American Petroleum Institute (API)** classifies oils by their *service category*:

- **S-series** (Spark-ignition / petrol): SA, SB, SC, … **SN, SN+** (latest).
- **C-series** (Compression-ignition / diesel): CA, CB, … **CK-4, FA-4** (latest).
- Two-letter codes like **SN/CF** mean the oil is **dual-rated** for both petrol and light diesel.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Trap Alert:** Examiner may show an oil labelled *"API SJ/CF"* and ask if it is suitable for a 2024 BS-VI petrol engine. The correct answer: **NO** — SJ is too old; you need at least **API SN** for modern petrol cars and **API CK-4** for modern BS-VI diesels.

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

The lubrication requirements are the *engineering DNA* behind real automotive decisions:

- Choosing **SAE 5W-30** for a turbocharged petrol car in **Kerala's hot climate** is a direct application of the viscosity vs. temperature requirement.
- A **BS-VI diesel car** mandates **API CK-4 or higher** with **low-SAPS** (Sulphated Ash, Phosphorus, Sulphur) to protect the **DPF (Diesel Particulate Filter)** — this is a lubrication requirement driven by emission regulations.
- Heavy commercial diesel trucks use **SAE 15W-40 CH-4** with **TBN ≥ 12** to neutralize the high sulphur content of older diesel fuel.
- **Electric vehicles (EVs)** still need lubrication in reduction gearboxes, bearings, and motor cooling — lubrication requirements don't vanish; they evolve.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Petroff's Equation for Bearing Friction Torque

This derivation is essential — KTU 2024 scheme board exam frequently carries this for a 7-mark Part B sub-question.

**Assumptions (Petroff's model):**
- Full hydrodynamic film — no metal contact.
- The bearing is a **plain journal bearing**, lightly loaded.
- The shaft is **concentric** with the bearing (not eccentric).
- The lubricant is a **Newtonian fluid**.
- Pressure is generated by **shaft rotation** only (squeeze film neglected).

**Step 1 — Velocity of the journal surface:**

The journal rotates with angular velocity ω (rad/s) and radius $r$. The linear velocity at the bearing surface is:

$$ v = \omega \times r = \frac{2 \pi N r}{60} $$

where $N$ is the shaft speed in RPM.

**Step 2 — Shear stress in the oil film:**

The oil film has uniform thickness $c$ (radial clearance). Velocity varies linearly from $0$ at the bearing (stationary) to $v$ at the journal. Therefore, the velocity gradient is:

$$ \frac{du}{dy} = \frac{v}{c} = \frac{2 \pi N r}{60 \, c} $$

By Newton's law of viscosity:

$$ \tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy} = \frac{2 \pi \mu N r}{60 \, c} $$

**Step 3 — Frictional force on the journal surface:**

Frictional force $F_f$ on a journal of length $L$:

$$ F_f = \tau \times A = \tau \times (2 \pi r L) $$

Substituting $\tau$:

$$ F_f = \frac{2 \pi \mu N r}{60 \, c} \times 2 \pi r L $$

$$ F_f = \frac{4 \pi^{2} \mu N r^{2} L}{60 \, c} $$

**Step 4 — Frictional torque:**

Torque $T_f$ is the force times the radius:

$$ T_f = F_f \times r = \frac{4 \pi^{2} \mu N r^{2} L}{60 \, c} \times r $$

$$ \boxed{T_f = \frac{4 \pi^{2} \mu N r^{3} L}{60 \, c}} $$

This is **Petroff's equation for frictional torque** in a concentric, lightly loaded journal bearing.

**Step 5 — Frictional power loss:**

Power loss in the bearing:

$$ P_f = T_f \times \omega = T_f \times \frac{2 \pi N}{60} $$

Substituting $T_f$:

$$ P_f = \frac{4 \pi^{2} \mu N r^{3} L}{60 \, c} \times \frac{2 \pi N}{60} $$

$$ P_f = \frac{8 \pi^{3} \mu N^{2} r^{3} L}{3600 \, c} $$

$$ \boxed{P_f = \frac{\pi^{3} \mu N^{2} r^{3} L}{450 \, c}} \quad \text{(W, with SI units)} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** Frictional torque is **directly proportional to viscosity μ** and **inversely proportional to clearance c**. This is why multi-grade oils with carefully controlled VI are critical — too high viscosity = high frictional loss; too low = metal contact.

### 3.2 Numerical Example: Petroff's Bearing Friction (Solved)

> **Problem:** A journal bearing has diameter **100 mm**, length **50 mm**, radial clearance **0.05 mm**, and runs at **1500 RPM** with oil of dynamic viscosity **0.05 Pa·s**. Calculate (a) the frictional torque, and (b) the frictional power loss.

**Given:**
- Diameter $D = 100$ mm = $0.1$ m → radius $r = 0.05$ m
- Length $L = 50$ mm = $0.05$ m
- Clearance $c = 0.05$ mm = $5 \times 10^{-5}$ m
- $N = 1500$ RPM, $\mu = 0.05$ Pa·s

**Solution (a) — Frictional Torque:**

Apply Petroff's equation:

$$ T_f = \frac{4 \pi^{2} \mu N r^{3} L}{60 \, c} $$

Compute $r^3$:

$$ r^3 = (0.05)^{3} = 1.25 \times 10^{-4} \ \text{m}^{3} $$

Compute numerator:

$$ 4 \pi^{2} \times 0.05 \times 1500 \times 1.25 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.05 $$

$$ = 4 \times 9.8696 \times 0.05 \times 1500 \times 1.25 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.05 $$

$$ = 4 \times 9.8696 \times 0.05 \times 9.375 \times 10^{-3} $$

$$ = 4 \times 9.8696 \times 4.6875 \times 10^{-4} $$

$$ = 4 \times 4.6265 \times 10^{-3} = 0.01851 \ \text{N·m·(unitless)} $$

Compute denominator: $60 \times c = 60 \times 5 \times 10^{-5} = 3 \times 10^{-3}$

Therefore:

$$ T_f = \frac{0.01851}{3 \times 10^{-3}} = 6.17 \ \text{N·m} $$

**Solution (b) — Frictional Power Loss:**

$$ P_f = T_f \times \frac{2 \pi N}{60} = 6.17 \times \frac{2 \pi \times 1500}{60} $$

$$ P_f = 6.17 \times 157.08 = 969.0 \ \text{W} \approx 0.97 \ \text{kW} $$

> **Answer:** $T_f \approx 6.17$ N·m and $P_f \approx 0.97$ kW.

### 3.3 Derivation: Viscosity Index (VI) by Linear Interpolation

The **Viscosity Index (VI)** indicates how much an oil's viscosity changes with temperature relative to two reference oils:

- **Paraffinic (VI = 100):** best T-stability.
- **Naphthenic (VI = 0):** worst T-stability.

**Step 1 — Measure kinematic viscosity at 40 °C and 100 °C.**

**Step 2 — Find reference viscosities $L$ and $H$:**

- $L$ = kinematic viscosity at 40 °C of the **VI = 0** reference oil that has the same 100 °C viscosity as our sample.
- $H$ = kinematic viscosity at 40 °C of the **VI = 100** reference oil with same 100 °C viscosity.
- $U$ = kinematic viscosity at 40 °C of our sample.

**Step 3 — Linear interpolation formula:**

$$ \boxed{VI = \frac{L - U}{L - H} \times 100} $$

**Worked Example:**

An oil sample has $U = 70$ cSt at 40 °C, kinematic viscosity at 100 °C = 10 cSt. For 10 cSt at 100 °C, the standard tables give $L = 110$ cSt and $H = 70$ cSt.

$$ VI = \frac{110 - 70}{110 - 70} \times 100 = \frac{40}{40} \times 100 = 100 $$

So this oil is a **paraffinic-base premium oil** with excellent temperature stability.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Lubrication Requirements vs. Engine Subsystem

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 14-Mark Question Architecture:** When asked to "explain lubrication requirements for an IC engine," structure your answer using this **function-to-subsystem mapping matrix**.

| Engine Subsystem | Critical Lubrication Requirement | Consequence if Not Met |
|---|---|---|
| Main bearing (Crankshaft) | High-pressure oil (3–5 bar) at high flow | Bearing seizure, crankshaft failure |
| Connecting rod bearing | Splash + pressurized oil via drilled passages | Rod knock, big-end failure |
| Camshaft bearings | Continuous pressurized flow | Cam wear, valve timing drift |
| Valve train (lifters, rockers) | Splash or jet-fed oil | Tappet noise, scuffing |
| Piston & rings | Splash + oil-cooling jets to underside | Ring sticking, piston scuff |
| Cylinder wall | Thin film from spray or splash | Blow-by, cylinder wear |
| Timing chain/gears | Splash lubrication | Premature chain stretch |
| Turbocharger bearing | Pressurized oil (engine-off cool-down needed) | Turbo coking, bearing failure |
| Oil pump itself | Self-lubricated via suction oil | Pump seizure, system failure |
