## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4]**
**"List any six essential functions that an engine lubrication system must perform."**

**Model Answer (6 points × 0.5 = 3 marks):**

1. **Friction reduction** – forms a hydrodynamic oil film between moving parts to convert dry friction to fluid friction.
2. **Wear prevention** – boundary-lubrication additives (e.g., ZDDP) prevent adhesive and abrasive wear.
3. **Cooling** – circulating oil removes ~5–10 % of total engine heat from bearings, pistons, and turbo bearings.
4. **Sealing** – oil film between piston rings and cylinder wall prevents combustion gas blow-by.
5. **Cleaning** – detergent and dispersant additives keep soot, carbon, and wear debris in suspension.
6. **Corrosion/rust prevention** – oil film and anti-oxidant additives protect surfaces from acidic combustion by-products.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]**
**"Define Viscosity Index. Why is high VI preferred for multi-grade engine oils?"**

**Model Answer:**

- **Viscosity Index (VI)** is an empirical, dimensionless number (0 to 100+) that indicates the rate at which an oil's viscosity changes with temperature. Higher VI = smaller viscosity change with temperature.
- **Reference scale:** Paraffinic oil (best stability) = VI 100; Naphthenic oil (worst stability) = VI 0.
- **Why high VI is preferred for multi-grade oils:**
  1. Ensures the oil remains **fluid at cold start** (low T) to reach bearings quickly.
  2. Maintains **adequate film thickness at high T** during sustained operation.
  3. Reduces the need for **excessive VII polymer**, extending oil life.
  4. Prevents **oil starvation** during cold start (a major cause of engine wear).

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Style, Module 4]**
**"With neat sketches, explain in detail the functional requirements of a lubrication system in an IC engine. Discuss the desired properties of lubricating oil and the role of additives in meeting these requirements."**

**Sub-part (a) — 7 Marks:** Functional requirements of lubrication system
**Sub-part (b) — 7 Marks:** Desired properties + additives

---

#### Model Solution — Sub-part (a) [7 marks]

The **functional requirements** of an engine lubrication system are:

1. **Friction Reduction (1 mark):** A continuous oil film of thickness 1–25 μm must separate the journal from the bearing. In hydrodynamic regime, friction coefficient drops from 0.1–0.5 (dry) to 0.001–0.005 (fluid).

2. **Wear Prevention (1 mark):** Anti-wear additives (ZDDP) form a sacrificial glassy phosphate film that prevents metal-to-metal asperity contact in boundary lubrication.

3. **Cooling (1 mark):** The oil absorbs heat from bearings and piston undercrown, then carries it to the sump where it is cooled. Specific heat of oil ≈ 1.9 kJ/kg·K.

4. **Sealing (1 mark):** Oil fills the ring-to-bore clearance (~0.05–0.10 mm) to act as a hydraulic seal against high-pressure combustion gases (60–80 bar in diesel).

5. **Cleaning (1 mark):** Detergents (Ca/Mg sulphonates) neutralize acids and prevent deposit formation; dispersants suspend soot particles.

6. **Corrosion Prevention (1 mark):** Rust inhibitors form a polar protective film on metal surfaces, blocking moisture and acidic attack.

7. **Pressurized Distribution (1 mark):** The system must maintain oil pressure between 1.5–5.0 bar across all RPMs to overcome leakage paths.

**Sketch Required:** Block diagram of oil flow — sump → pump → filter → gallery → bearings → return. *(Refer to SECTION 4.1 diagram.)*

---

#### Model Solution — Sub-part (b) [7 marks]

**Desired Properties of Lubricating Oil (4 marks):**

| Property | Required Value/Range | Why It Matters |
|---|---|---|
| Viscosity (at 100 °C) | 9.3 – 16.3 cSt (SAE 30/40) | Maintains hydrodynamic film at operating T |
| Viscosity Index (VI) | ≥ 95 for multi-grade | Limits T-induced viscosity change |
| Flash Point | ≥ 200 °C (diesel) | Safety against oil ignition |
| Pour Point | ≤ −20 °C (multi-grade) | Cold-start flow |
| TBN | ≥ 8 mg KOH/g (diesel) | Neutralizes acid from fuel S |
| Carbon Residue | ≤ 1.5 % (Conradson) | Clean burning, low deposits |
| Ash Content | ≤ 1.0 % sulphated ash | Protects catalytic converter/DPF |

**Role of Additives (3 marks):**

1. **Detergents & Dispersants (1 mark):** Calcium/Magnesium sulphonates + PIB succinimides — keep engine internally clean, suspend soot.
2. **Anti-wear (ZDDP) (1 mark):** Forms a sacrificial glassy polyphosphate film at asperity contacts.
3. **Anti-oxidants, VII, Anti-foam, Pour-Point Depressant (1 mark):** Extend oil life and maintain performance envelope.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2 (Internal Choice to Question A)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Retest Pattern, Module 4]**
**"Derive Petroff's equation for the frictional torque in a journal bearing. Using this equation, calculate the frictional power loss in a bearing of diameter 80 mm, length 60 mm, running at 2000 RPM with a radial clearance of 0.06 mm. Oil viscosity is 0.04 Pa·s."**

**Sub-part (a) — 7 Marks:** Derivation of Petroff's equation
**Sub-part (b) — 7 Marks:** Numerical computation

---

#### Model Solution — Sub-part (a) [7 marks]

*(Refer to SECTION 3.1 for full derivation. Key points to write in exam:)*

1. **Assumptions** (1 mark): Concentric shaft, Newtonian fluid, full film, uniform clearance c, no axial flow.
2. **Velocity at journal surface** (1 mark): $v = 2\pi N r / 60$.
3. **Shear stress** (1 mark): $\tau = \mu \cdot v / c = 2\pi \mu N r / (60 c)$.
4. **Frictional force** (1 mark): $F_f = \tau \times 2\pi r L = 4\pi^2 \mu N r^2 L / (60 c)$.
5. **Frictional torque** (1 mark): $T_f = F_f \times r = 4\pi^2 \mu N r^3 L / (60 c)$.
6. **Final boxed result + interpretation** (2 marks):
   $$ \boxed{T_f = \frac{4 \pi^{2} \mu N r^{3} L}{60 \, c}} $$
   - T_f ∝ μ (viscosity), T_f ∝ 1/c (clearance), T_f ∝ N (RPM).
   - This is the basis for the **Stribeck curve** and the reason viscosity is so critical.

---

#### Model Solution — Sub-part (b) [7 marks]

**Given:**
- Diameter $D = 80$ mm → $r = 0.04$ m
- Length $L = 60$ mm = $0.06$ m
- $N = 2000$ RPM
- $c = 0.06$ mm = $6 \times 10^{-5}$ m
- $\mu = 0.04$ Pa·s

**Step 1 — Compute $r^3$:**

$$ r^3 = (0.04)^3 = 6.4 \times 10^{-5} \ \text{m}^3 $$

**Step 2 — Apply Petroff's formula:**

$$ T_f = \frac{4 \pi^{2} \times 0.04 \times 2000 \times 6.4 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.06}{60 \times 6 \times 10^{-5}} $$

Compute numerator:

$$ 4 \pi^2 = 39.478 $$

$$ \text{Numerator} = 39.478 \times 0.04 \times 2000 \times 6.4 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.06 $$

$$ = 39.478 \times 0.04 \times 2000 \times 3.84 \times 10^{-6} $$

$$ = 39.478 \times 0.04 \times 7.68 \times 10^{-3} $$

$$ = 39.478 \times 3.072 \times 10^{-4} = 0.01213 $$

Compute denominator:

$$ 60 \times 6 \times 10^{-5} = 3.6 \times 10^{-3} $$

Therefore:

$$ T_f = \frac{0.01213}{3.6 \times 10^{-3}} = 3.37 \ \text{N·m} $$

**Step 3 — Frictional Power Loss:**

$$ P_f = T_f \times \omega = T_f \times \frac{2\pi N}{60} $$

$$ P_f = 3.37 \times \frac{2\pi \times 2000}{60} = 3.37 \times 209.44 $$

$$ \boxed{P_f = 705.8 \ \text{W} \approx 0.706 \ \text{kW}} $$

**Step 4 — Final interpretation (½ mark):**

- The bearing dissipates about **706 W** as heat.
- If the engine is run for 1 hour, the total frictional heat generated in this single bearing is **~2.54 MJ**, which is why oil cooling and sump heat dissipation are critical design requirements.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks on This Topic:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Viscosity Index with Viscosity Grade** – VI is a *temperature-stability* rating (0–100+), whereas SAE grade is a *viscosity* rating. Don't mix them in definitions.
> 2. **Wrong API code for modern engines** – Many students write "API SJ" for a 2024 engine. **SN or SN+** is the correct minimum for BS-VI petrol; **CK-4** for BS-VI diesel.
> 3. **Petroff's derivation** – Don't skip the assumption statement. KTU examiners deduct 1 mark if assumptions are not listed.
> 4. **Unit Conversion in Numerical** – Converting mm to m is the most common error. **Always write the conversion explicitly** in the solution.
> 5. **Forgetting units in the final answer** – $T_f$ in N·m, $P_f$ in W (or kW). Without units, expect ½ mark deduction.
> 6. **Saying "oil cools the engine" generically** – The correct statement is: "oil cools specific components like bearings, pistons, and turbo bearings, removing ~5–10 % of total engine heat." Water jacket does the rest.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Requirements of Lubrication Systems":**
>
> - **Six core requirements:** Friction ↓, Wear ↓, Cooling, Sealing, Cleaning, Corrosion prevention.
> - **Petroff's equation** for journal bearing friction torque: $T_f = 4\pi^2 \mu N r^3 L / (60 c)$.
> - **Petroff's assumptions:** concentric, Newtonian, full film, no axial flow, lightly loaded.
> - **Oil pressure range:** 1.5 – 5.0 bar in passenger car engines.
> - **Oil temperature window:** 80 – 110 °C normal; 120 °C is upper safety limit.
> - **SAE multi-grade example:** 20W-40 → cold pumpability of SAE 20W + hot viscosity of SAE 40.
> - **API service:** S-series for petrol (latest = SN, SN+); C-series for diesel (latest = CK-4, FA-4).
> - **VI = 100** is paraffinic (best), **VI = 0** is naphthenic (worst).
> - **TBN ≥ 8** mg KOH/g is required for diesel engines to neutralize sulphur-based acids.
> - **ZDDP** is the most important anti-wear additive — forms a sacrificial phosphate glass film.
> - **Multi-grade oil** = base oil + VII polymer (polymethacrylate) that expands at high T.
> - **Splash lubrication** = oil mist from crank rotation; **Pressurized lubrication** = oil pump + gallery.
> - **EVs still need lubrication** for reduction gearboxes, bearings, and motor cooling — a current KTU exam favourite.
> - **Low-SAPS oils** (low Sulphated Ash, Phosphorus, Sulphur) are mandatory for BS-VI vehicles with DPF/GPF to prevent filter clogging.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Lubrication System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT205 - AUTOMOBILE POWER PLANT (AUTOMOBILE ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Types of Lubrication System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of Internal Combustion (IC) engines, a **Lubrication System** is defined as a precisely engineered arrangement of mechanical, hydraulic, and fluidic components designed to deliver clean, pressurized lubricating oil to all relative moving parts of the engine at the correct temperature, pressure, and flow rate, in order to minimize wear, friction, and heat generation, while simultaneously removing contaminants, wear debris, and heat from critical tribological interfaces.

A **Type of Lubrication System** refers to the specific *architecture* or *topology* by which the oil is stored, drawn, circulated, delivered, and returned within the engine. KTU 2024 Scheme (Module 4) classifies the principal types of lubrication systems used in automobile engines as follows:

1. **Wet Sump Lubrication System** (most common in passenger cars)
2. **Dry Sump Lubrication System** (used in racing/high-performance vehicles)
3. **Splash Lubrication System** (simple, low-power engines)
4. **Pressure (Forced) Lubrication System** (high-speed, heavily loaded engines)
5. **Combination (Splash + Pressure) Lubrication System** (modern multi-cylinder engines)
6. **Mist Lubrication System** (two-stroke engines)
7. **Petrol Lubrication System** (small two-stroke engines – total loss)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 4 – Lubrication Systems):**
> The examiner expects students to clearly distinguish between **Total Loss Systems** (e.g., mist, petrol lubrication) where oil is consumed, and **Circulating Systems** (e.g., wet sump, dry sump) where oil is reused continuously. Confusing these two categories is one of the most common answer-sheet pitfalls.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine a small village that needs water delivered to every house every day:

- **Wet Sump System** is like having a *big well dug right under the village*. Every morning, a single pump pulls water up, sends it through pipes to all the houses, and any leftover water drains back into the same well underneath. It is simple, compact, and self-contained — exactly like an oil pan sitting under the engine.
- **Dry Sump System** is like moving the well to a *separate big tank far away from the village*. Now you need **two pumps**: one to push water *out to the houses* (pressure pump) and another to *suck the used water back into the distant tank* (scavenge pump). It is more complex but allows the village to be tilted, turned, and shaken without ever running out of water — perfect for a race car going around sharp corners.
- **Splash System** is like a *watering can* — a scoop attached to a rotating wheel slings droplets of water onto the machinery. Cheap, simple, but uncontrollable.
- **Pressure System** is like a *fire hydrant* — water is shot under high pressure directly where it is needed, through precise pipes.
- **Mist System** is like a *perfume atomizer* — a fine fog of oily air is sucked into the engine with the fuel and lubricates as it flows past the moving parts, then burns away.
- **Petrol Lubrication** is like *mixing sugar into tea* — the oil is dissolved into the petrol, and lubrication happens automatically wherever the fuel goes.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Engine Oil Performance Benchmarks (KTU Board Numerical Data):**
> - **Standard Oil Pressure** in passenger car engines: **2.5 bar to 4.5 bar** (≈ **250 kPa to 450 kPa**) at idle
> - **Engine oil operating temperature window:** **80 °C to 110 °C**
> - **Kinematic viscosity of SAE 20W-40 at 100 °C:** ≈ **9.3 cSt** (centistokes)
> - **Typical two-stroke oil–fuel ratio:** **25:1 to 50:1**
> - **SAE viscosity grades (J300):** 0W, 5W, 10W, 15W, 20W, 25W (winter); 20, 30, 40, 50, 60 (summer)

### 1.4 Quick Categorization Table

| Category | Examples | Oil Reuse | Oil Storage Location |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Circulating Systems** | Wet Sump, Dry Sump, Splash, Pressure, Combination | ✅ Reused | Inside / Outside engine |
| **Total Loss Systems** | Mist, Petrol Lubrication | ❌ Burned/Consumed | Mixed with fuel |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Detailed Classification & Operating Principles

#### 2.1.1 Wet Sump Lubrication System

**Operational Logic Steps:**

- **Step 1 — Storage:** Lubricating oil is stored in the **sump (oil pan)**, a sealed reservoir bolted to the bottom of the **crankcase**. The capacity typically ranges between **3.5 L to 7.0 L** for a typical 4-cylinder petrol car.
- **Step 2 — Suction:** A **gear-type oil pump**, driven mechanically by the **camshaft** (or auxiliary shaft) through a gear train, draws oil from the sump through a **strainer** (coarse filter) and **suction pipe**.
- **Step 3 — Pressurization:** The pump raises the oil pressure to a regulated value (≈ **3.0 bar to 4.0 bar**) and pushes it through the **oil gallery** (a network of drilled passages inside the engine block and cylinder head).
- **Step 4 — Distribution:** Pressurized oil is delivered to the **main bearings** (supporting the crankshaft) → through internal crankshaft drillings → to the **connecting rod big-end bearings** → to the **camshaft bearings** → to the **valve train (rocker arms / tappets)**.
- **Step 5 — Lubrication of Cylinders:** Oil escapes from the connecting rod bearings through **clearance holes** and is flung outward by centrifugal force, **splash-lubricating** the cylinder walls, pistons, and gudgeon pins.
- **Step 6 — Return:** After performing its job, oil drains back down into the sump under gravity, completing the closed-loop circulation cycle.
- **Step 7 — Filtration:** A full-flow **oil filter** is mounted in the pressure line, ensuring all oil reaching critical bearings is free of metallic particles and combustion by-products.

**Why this system dominates passenger cars:** Compactness, low cost, self-contained design, gravity-driven return, and minimal auxiliary plumbing.

**Why this system can fail:** During hard cornering, braking, or acceleration, oil can surge away from the **oil pump pickup tube**, momentarily causing **oil starvation** and catastrophic bearing failure.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Application:** The Maruti Suzuki Alto, Hyundai Creta 1.5L MPi, Toyota Etios, and virtually every mass-market sedan worldwide employ the wet sump system.

#### 2.1.2 Dry Sump Lubrication System

**Operational Logic Steps:**

- **Step 1 — Storage:** The crankcase is *empty* (no oil pan) — it only contains a small residual film of oil. All used oil is scavenged into a **separate external oil tank** (often mounted in the front-left wheel well or under the boot floor). Capacity of this remote tank: typically **8 L to 14 L**.
- **Step 2 — Scavenge Phase (1st Pump):** A dedicated **scavenge pump** (often a multi-stage gerotor pump) continuously sucks oil *out* of the crankcase and pushes it into the remote tank. Usually 2 or 3 scavenge stages operate in series.
- **Step 3 — Pressure Phase (2nd Pump):** A separate **pressure pump** draws oil from the remote tank and feeds it into the main oil gallery at regulated pressure (≈ **4.0 bar to 6.0 bar**).
- **Step 4 — Delivery & Return:** Oil is delivered identically to the wet-sump case, but the *return* path is engineered carefully: oil drains to the bottom of the crankcase, where the scavenge pump picks it up.
- **Step 5 — De-aeration:** Air whipped into the oil (foam) is separated in the remote tank before the oil is re-pressurized.

**Why this system is mandatory in racing:** Under sustained lateral g-forces of 1.5 g to 3.0 g in motorsport, a wet sump can completely uncover the pickup. Dry sump guarantees a flood-fed supply at all attitudes, allows a lower vehicle centre of gravity, and provides *much* better oil cooling via the larger remote reservoir.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favourite Comparison Question:** "Compare wet sump and dry sump lubrication systems on at least 6 points." Know the answers: storage location, number of pumps, weight distribution, g-force tolerance, cost, application, oil capacity, oil cooling, and oil aeration.

#### 2.1.3 Splash Lubrication System

**Operational Logic Steps:**

- **Step 1 — Storage:** Oil sits in the sump.
- **Step 2 — Scooping:** A **dipper** (small spoon-shaped scoop) is fixed to the **big end of the connecting rod**. As the crankshaft rotates, the dipper dips into the oil sump.
- **Step 3 — Splash Generation:** The dipper flings oil upwards as a fine spray.
- **Step 4 — Distribution:** Oil droplets coat the **cylinder walls**, **piston pins**, and the **underside of the piston** by gravity and impingement.
- **Step 5 — Bearings:** Main and big-end bearings are lubricated by oil flowing down the cylinder walls and through small holes in the connecting rod.

**Why it is used:** No oil pump required → extremely cheap, simple, and lightweight.
**Why it is unsuitable for modern cars:** Inconsistent oil delivery at varying RPM, poor bearing pressure, oil aeration at high speeds, and inability to lubricate overhead valve mechanisms.

**Application:** Stationary engines, small marine engines, vintage motorcycles, lawn-mower engines.

#### 2.1.4 Pressure (Forced) Lubrication System

**Operational Logic Steps:**

- **Step 1 — Pump:** A positive-displacement oil pump pressurizes oil to 2.5–5.0 bar.
- **Step 2 — Filter:** Oil passes through a full-flow filter.
- **Step 3 — Galleries:** Drilled passages route oil directly to **main bearings, camshaft bearings, timing chain, and rocker shaft**.
- **Step 4 — Relief Valve:** A **pressure relief valve** (set to ≈ 4 bar) opens to dump excess oil back to the sump if pressure exceeds the safe limit (cold engine / blocked filter / high viscosity oil).
- **Step 5 — Cylinder lubrication:** Achieved indirectly by splash from bearings.

**Why it is essential:** The hydrodynamic oil film in high-speed bearings (e.g., main bearings spinning at 6,000 rpm) can only be maintained if oil is *forced* in under pressure. Splash alone cannot build the required film thickness.

#### 2.1.5 Combination (Splash + Pressure) Lubrication System

This is the **industry standard for all modern multi-cylinder, overhead-cam engines**. It is a *hybrid* of pressure lubrication for the bearings and splash lubrication for the cylinders.

**Logical Flow:**
- **Pressure-fed components:** Crankshaft main bearings, big-end bearings, camshaft bearings, valve rockers, timing chain, balance shaft.
- **Splash-fed components:** Cylinder bores, piston skirts, piston pins, thrust bearings.
- **Mist/atomized components:** Upper valve train (bucket tappets, valve stems) receives oil mist from pressurized oil seeping past the camshaft journals.

**Why this is the universal standard:** Combines the *reliability* of pressurized bearing lubrication with the *simplicity* of splash lubrication for reciprocating parts — eliminating the need for oil squirter nozzles on the cylinder walls.

#### 2.1.6 Mist Lubrication System

**Operational Logic Steps:**

- **Step 1 — Mixing:** A metered amount of oil is injected into the **intake manifold** or **crankcase induction**.
- **Step 2 — Atomization:** The incoming air-fuel charge breaks the oil into a fine **mist**.
- **Step 3 — Adhesion:** The mist deposits on the **cylinder walls**, **crankpin**, and **main bearings** as it circulates.
- **Step 4 — Combustion:** Residual oil is burned along with the fuel-air mixture and exits through the exhaust.

**Application:** Two-stroke engines (motorcycles, chainsaws, outboard motors), small aircraft piston engines.
**Why it is "Total Loss":** The oil is consumed during combustion — never recovered.

#### 2.1.7 Petrol Lubrication System

**Operational Logic Steps:**

- **Step 1 — Mixing:** Oil is manually pre-mixed with petrol in a **specific ratio** (commonly **25:1** for older engines, **32:1** for modern two-stroke bikes, **50:1** for chain-saw oil).
- **Step 2 — Carburetion:** The mixture is drawn through the carburettor or fuel-injection system.
- **Step 3 — Lubrication:** Oil separates from petrol in the crankcase, lubricates the **big-end bearing, cylinder, and piston pin**, then burns with the fuel.
- **Step 4 — Exhaust:** Visible blue smoke from the exhaust indicates the oil being burned.

**Application:** Very old motorcycles (vintage two-stroke scooters, mopeds, the Bajaj Chetak-type two-wheelers before 2000s).

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / High-Yield Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are the **core numerical weapons** for KTU 2024 ESE questions on lubrication systems. Memorize them in this exact form.

| # | Formula | Description | Standard Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | Bearing pressure on journal | Pa (N/m²) |
| 2 | $\tau = \dfrac{\mu \cdot \omega \cdot r}{h}$ | Shear stress in hydrodynamic oil film | Pa |
| 3 | $h_{min} = 0.0002 \cdot D$ | Minimum oil-film thickness (Reynolds' rule) | m |
| 4 | $Q_{pump} = V_d \cdot N$ | Theoretical pump displacement flow | m³/s |
| 5 | $Q_{actual} = \eta_v \cdot V_d \cdot N$ | Actual pump flow (with volumetric efficiency) | m³/s |
| 6 | $P_p = \dfrac{Q \cdot \Delta p}{\eta_m}$ | Pump power input | W (watts) |
| 7 | $H_{oil} = \dot{m} \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Heat carried away by oil | W |
| 8 | $VI = \dfrac{L - U}{H - U} \times 100$ | Viscosity Index (Dean & Davis) | dimensionless |
| 9 | $\log \log(\nu + 0.7) = A - B \cdot \log T$ | ASTM D-341 Viscosity-Temperature equation | — |
| 10 | $P_{friction} = f \cdot F_n \cdot v$ | Frictional power loss in bearing | W |
| 11 | $S = \dfrac{\mu \cdot N}{P}$ | Sommerfeld Number (dimensionless bearing parameter) | dimensionless |
| 12 | $h_0 = C \cdot r \cdot (1 - \varepsilon)$ | Minimum film thickness in journal bearing | m |
| 13 | $T_{oil} = T_{amb} + \dfrac{H_{oil}}{h_c \cdot A}$ | Steady-state oil bulk temperature | °C |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL — Pipe Symbol Alert:** In all subsequent LaTeX expressions, the absolute value and magnitude are written using `\vert` and `\mid` to prevent breaking markdown table structures. KTU boards accept both vertical bar forms as long as the equations remain mathematically unambiguous.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Oil Cooling Calculation:** Knowing $H_{oil}$ allows a designer to size the **oil cooler** (engine oil cooler in a turbocharged vehicle).
- **Viscosity Index** determines whether the oil is **monograde** or **multigrade** — critical for cold-start wear.
- **Sommerfeld Number** $S$ predicts the **bearing operating regime** — boundary, mixed, or full-film hydrodynamic lubrication.
- **Pump displacement** $V_d$ determines whether the oil pump can keep up with the engine's oil demand at peak RPM.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This section provides *complete*, examiner-grade derivations. No step is skipped. Every algebraic transition is justified. This is the type of working-out that scores full marks in KTU 2024 ESE numericals.

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Oil Pump Theoretical & Actual Flow Rate

**Given:**
- Pump displacement volume per revolution: $V_d = 12 \text{ cm}^3 / \text{rev}$
- Pump speed: $N = 3000 \text{ rpm}$
- Volumetric efficiency: $\eta_v = 0.85$

**Find:** Theoretical and actual flow rate of oil in L/min.

**Step 1 — Convert pump speed to consistent SI units.**

We need revolutions per second.

$$N_{rps} = \dfrac{N_{rpm}}{60} = \dfrac{3000}{60} = 50 \text{ rev/s}$$

*Conversion logic:* 1 minute = 60 seconds, so dividing rpm by 60 gives rev/s.

**Step 2 — Compute theoretical displacement flow $Q_{th}$.**

The pump displaces $V_d$ cubic centimetres of oil per revolution. Multiplying by revolutions per second yields cubic centimetres per second.

$$Q_{th} = V_d \cdot N_{rps}$$

$$Q_{th} = 12 \text{ cm}^3/\text{rev} \times 50 \text{ rev/s}$$

$$Q_{th} = 600 \text{ cm}^3/\text{s}$$

*Conversion logic:* The product of volume-per-rev and rev-per-second simplifies to volume-per-second.

**Step 3 — Convert to L/min for board-friendly units.**

$$1 \text{ cm}^3/\text{s} = \dfrac{1}{1000} \text{ L/s} = \dfrac{60}{1000} \text{ L/min} = 0.06 \text{ L/min}$$

$$Q_{th} = 600 \times 0.06 = 36 \text{ L/min}$$

*Conversion logic:* $1 \text{ L} = 1000 \text{ cm}^3$ and $1 \text{ min} = 60 \text{ s}$. So $1 \text{ cm}^3/\text{s} = 0.06 \text{ L/min}$.

**Step 4 — Apply volumetric efficiency to get actual flow.**

The volumetric efficiency accounts for internal leakage past the gear tips and clearances back to the suction side. Only $\eta_v$ fraction of theoretical flow actually reaches the discharge.

$$Q_{actual} = \eta_v \cdot Q_{th}$$

$$Q_{actual} = 0.85 \times 36$$

$$Q_{actual} = 30.6 \text{ L/min}$$

**Final Result:** The pump theoretically delivers 36 L/min, and *effectively* delivers **30.6 L/min** to the engine's oil gallery.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Heat Carried Away by Engine Oil (Oil Cooler Sizing)

**Given:**
- Engine oil mass flow rate: $\dot{m} = 1.5 \text{ kg/s}$
- Specific heat of oil: $C_p = 2100 \text{ J/(kg·K)}$
- Oil enters the cooler at: $T_{in} = 130 \text{ °C}$
- Oil exits the cooler at: $T_{out} = 110 \text{ °C}$

**Find:** Heat removed by the oil cooler in kW.

**Step 1 — Express the energy balance on the oil stream.**

The First Law of Thermodynamics applied to the steady-flow oil stream across the cooler gives:

$$H_{oil} = \dot{m} \cdot C_p \cdot (T_{in} - T_{out})$$

*Conversion logic:* The rate of enthalpy change of the oil equals the rate of heat rejected to the coolant (air or water) in the cooler. There is no shaft work crossing the oil-cooler control volume.

**Step 2 — Substitute the temperature difference.**

$$\Delta T = T_{in} - T_{out} = 130 - 110 = 20 \text{ K}$$

*Conversion logic:* A temperature *difference* of 20 °C equals exactly 20 K, since both are equal-interval scales.

**Step 3 — Substitute all numerical values.**

$$H_{oil} = 1.5 \times 2100 \times 20$$

$$H_{oil} = 63{,}000 \text{ W}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to kilowatts.**

$$H_{oil} = \dfrac{63{,}000}{1000} = 63 \text{ kW}$$

**Final Result:** The oil cooler must reject **63 kW** of heat from the lubricating oil to keep the bulk oil temperature within the safe 80–110 °C window. This sets the baseline for cooler surface area design using $\dot{Q} = U \cdot A \cdot LMTD$ in subsequent heat-exchanger design.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Viscosity Index (VI) Calculation

**Given:**
- Viscosity of the test oil at 40 °C: $\nu_{40} = 95 \text{ cSt}$
- Viscosity of the test oil at 100 °C: $\nu_{100} = 10 \text{ cSt}$
- Viscosity of reference oil **L** (paraffinic, VI = 100) at 40 °C: $L = 100 \text{ cSt}$
- Viscosity of reference oil **U** (naphthenic, VI = 0) at 40 °C: $U = 250 \text{ cSt}$

**Find:** Viscosity Index of the oil.

**Step 1 — Recall the Dean & Davis formula.**

$$VI = \dfrac{L - U}{H - U} \times 100$$

*Conversion logic:* H here is the *viscosity of the test oil* at 40 °C (commonly written as $U_{oil}$ in some texts). For KTU 2024, the most common form uses H = viscosity of test oil at 40 °C, where L and U are the reference oils that bracket it.

**Step 2 — Substitute values.**

$$VI = \dfrac{L - U_{oil}}{H_{ref} - U_{oil}} \times 100$$

Let us use the standard form where $L$ and $H$ are the viscosities of two reference oils that both have the same viscosity at 100 °C as the test oil, with $L$ being the more paraffinic (VI = 100) and $H$ being the more naphthenic (VI = 0):

$$VI = \dfrac{L - U_{oil}}{H_{ref} - U_{oil}} \times 100$$

$$VI = \dfrac{100 - 95}{250 - 95} \times 100$$

*Conversion logic:* Numerator = 5 (small difference = small VI loss from paraffinic), Denominator = 155 (full range of naphthenic-to-paraffinic spread).

**Step 3 — Evaluate.**

$$VI = \dfrac{5}{155} \times 100 = 3.226$$

$$VI \approx 3.23$$

**Final Result:** The test oil has a **Viscosity Index of approximately 3.23** — a very *low* VI oil. Such oils exhibit a sharp drop in viscosity with rising temperature and are unsuitable for multi-grade modern engine oils, which require **VI ≥ 95** for SAE 20W-40 multigrade performance.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Minimum Oil-Film Thickness in a Journal Bearing

**Given:**
- Journal diameter: $D = 60 \text{ mm} = 0.060 \text{ m}$
- Therefore journal radius: $r = 30 \text{ mm} = 0.030 \text{ m}$

**Find:** Minimum hydrodynamic oil-film thickness $h_{min}$.

**Step 1 — Apply the Reynolds / Hamrock rule of thumb.**

For a healthy hydrodynamic bearing, the minimum film thickness must be at least **0.0002 × D** to prevent asperity contact.

$$h_{min} = 0.0002 \cdot D$$

**Step 2 — Substitute.**

$$h_{min} = 0.0002 \times 0.060$$

$$h_{min} = 0.000012 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to micrometres.**

$$h_{min} = 12 \text{ μm}$$

*Conversion logic:* $1 \text{ m} = 10^6 \text{ μm}$, so multiplying by $10^6$ converts metres to micrometres.

**Final Result:** The hydrodynamic oil film in this journal bearing must be **at least 12 μm** thick to support the load. Any drop below this threshold (e.g., due to oil starvation, cold-start, or low pressure) leads to metal-to-metal contact and bearing failure.

---

### 3.5 Derivation 5: Friction Power Loss in the Engine's Piston-Cylinder Interface

**Given:**
- Coefficient of friction in the cylinder bore: $f = 0.06$
- Normal force from combustion gas on piston: $F_n = 4000 \text{ N}$
- Mean piston velocity: $v = 12 \text{ m/s}$

**Find:** Friction power loss in watts.

**Step 1 — Recall the friction power formula.**

$$P_{friction} = f \cdot F_n \cdot v$$

*Conversion logic:* Friction force $F_f = f \cdot F_n$. Power = Force × Velocity.

**Step 2 — Substitute values.**

$$P_{friction} = 0.06 \times 4000 \times 12$$

$$P_{friction} = 2880 \text{ W}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to horsepower for engineer's context.**

$$P_{friction} = \dfrac{2880}{745.7} \approx 3.86 \text{ hp}$$

**Final Result:** Roughly **2.88 kW (≈ 3.86 hp)** of useful engine power is consumed overcoming piston-bore friction alone. This is why KTU 2024 Module 4 emphasizes *boundary, mixed, and full-film* lubrication regimes — minimizing $f$ through proper lubrication directly improves engine efficiency.

---

### 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix: All Seven Lubrication Systems

| Parameter | Wet Sump | Dry Sump | Splash | Pressure | Combination | Mist | Petrol |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Oil Storage** | Sump (crankcase) | External tank | Sump | Sump | Sump | Mixed with charge | Mixed with fuel |
| **Number of Pumps** | 1 | 2 (pressure + scavenge) | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 |
| **Oil Reuse?** | Yes (recirculating) | Yes (recirculating) | Yes | Yes | Yes | No (total loss) | No (total loss) |
| **Pressure at Bearings** | 2.5–4.5 bar | 4.0–6.0 bar | ~0 (splash) | 2.5–5.0 bar | 2.5–4.5 bar | ~0 (mist) | ~0 (mixed) |
| **Application** | Passenger cars | Racing cars | Stationary engines | Heavy-duty diesels | Modern multi-cyl. cars | 2-stroke bikes | Small 2-stroke engines |
| **Cost** | Low | Very High | Very Low | Medium | Medium | Low | Very Low |
| **G-Force Tolerance** | Poor (oil surge) | Excellent | Poor | Poor–Fair | Fair | Excellent | Good |
| **Oil Aeration Risk** | Medium | Low (de-aerated in tank) | High | Medium | Medium | N/A (consumed) | N/A (consumed) |
| **Oil Capacity (typical)** | 3.5–7 L | 8–14 L | 1–2 L | 5–15 L | 4–8 L | 0.1–0.5 L | 50–200 mL |
| **Filtration Possible?** | Yes (full-flow) | Yes (full-flow) | Limited | Yes | Yes | No | No |
| **Cooling Capacity** | Moderate | Excellent | Low | Moderate | Moderate | Low | Low |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> Per KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10, the following diagrams are presented as **functional architecture flowcharts** in Mermaid. Node IDs follow the alphanumeric convention. Labels are clean uppercase text (no markdown formatting inside quoted labels).

### 4.1 Diagram 1: Wet Sump Lubrication System — Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["OIL SUMP STORAGE 3.5 TO 7 LITRES"] --> node2["STRAINER COARSE FILTER"]
    node2 --> node3["OIL PUMP GEAR OR ROTOR TYPE"]
    node3 --> node4["PRESSURE RELIEF VALVE SET AT 4 BAR"]
    node4 --> node5["OIL FILTER FULL FLOW"]
    node5 --> node6["MAIN OIL GALLERY DRILLED PASSAGE"]
    node6 --> node7["MAIN BEARINGS CRANKSHAFT"]
    node6 --> node8["BIG END BEARINGS CONNECTING ROD"]
    node6 --> node9["CAMSHAFT BEARINGS"]
    node6 --> node10["ROCKER ARM AND VALVE TRAIN"]
    node7 --> node11["OIL SPLASH ON CYLINDER WALLS"]
    node8 --> node11
    node11 --> node12["OIL DRIPS BACK TO SUMP"]
    node9 --> node12
    node10 --> node12
    node12 --> node1
```

### 4.2 Diagram 2: Dry Sump Lubrication System — Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["CRANKCASE RESIDUAL OIL FILM"] --> node2["SCAVENGE PUMP STAGE 1"]
    node2 --> node3["SCAVENGE PUMP STAGE 2"]
    node3 --> node4["DE AERATION IN REMOTE TANK"]
    node4 --> node5["EXTERNAL OIL TANK 8 TO 14 LITRES"]
    node5 --> node6["PRESSURE PUMP GEROTOR TYPE"]
    node6 --> node7["OIL FILTER FULL FLOW"]
    node7 --> node8["RELIEF VALVE 4 TO 6 BAR"]
    node8 --> node9["MAIN OIL GALLERY"]
    node9 --> node10["CRANKSHAFT BEARINGS"]
    node9 --> node11["CAMSHAFT AND VALVE TRAIN"]
    node10 --> node12["OIL RETURNS TO CRANKCASE"]
    node11 --> node12
    node12 --> node1
```

### 4.3 Diagram 3: Combination Splash and Pressure System — Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["OIL SUMP"] --> node2["OIL PUMP"]
    node2 --> node3["RELIEF VALVE 3 TO 4 BAR"]
    node3 --> node4["OIL FILTER"]
    node4 --> node5["PRESSURE LINE TO MAIN BEARINGS"]
    node5 --> node6["CRANKSHAFT MAIN BEARINGS"]
    node5 --> node7["BIG END BEARINGS"]
    node5 --> node8["CAMSHAFT AND TAPPETS"]
    node7 --> node9["OIL SPLASH FROM ROD"]
    node9 --> node10["CYLINDER WALLS PISTON PIN"]
    node10 --> node11["OIL DRAINAGE BACK TO SUMP"]
    node11 --> node1
```

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["OIL RESERVOIR SEPARATE TANK"] --> node2["OIL INJECTION PUMP METERED"]
    node2 --> node3["INTAKE MANIFOLD MIXING ZONE"]
    node3 --> node4["AIR FUEL OIL MIST ATOMIZATION"]
    node4 --> node5["CRANKCASE TWO STROKE ENGINE"]

    node7 --> node8
```

```mermaid

    node1["AUTOMOBILE LUBRICATION SYSTEMS"] --> node2["CIRCULATING TYPE OIL REUSED"]
    node1 --> node3["TOTAL LOSS TYPE OIL CONSUMED"]
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1["ENGINE CRANKSHAFT"] --> node2["TIMING GEAR TRAIN"]
    node2 --> node3["OIL PUMP DRIVE SHAFT"]

    node4 --> node6["DISCHARGE PORT TO GALLERY"]

```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**List any three differences between a wet sump and a dry sump lubrication system.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Oil Storage Location:** In a wet sump system, oil is stored in the oil pan (sump) attached to the bottom of the crankcase. In a dry sump system, oil is stored in a *separate external tank* mounted away from the engine.

2. **Number of Pumps:** A wet sump system uses a *single* oil pump for both suction and pressure duties. A dry sump system uses *two* distinct pumps — a scavenge pump to return oil to the remote tank, and a pressure pump to feed the oil gallery.

3. **G-Force Behaviour:** Wet sump suffers from *oil surge* during hard cornering, braking, and acceleration, which can uncover the pickup tube and cause oil starvation. Dry sump tolerates sustained high g-forces (1.5 g to 3.0 g) without loss of oil pressure because the remote tank remains flooded.

4. **Application & Cost:** Wet sump is cheap and used in passenger cars. Dry sump is expensive and used in racing cars (e.g., Formula 1, NASCAR) and high-performance supercars (Ferrari 488, Porsche GT3).

5. **Oil Capacity & Cooling:** Dry sump holds 8–14 L versus 3.5–7 L for wet sump, providing superior oil cooling and longer drain intervals.

**Marking Pattern:** 3 points × 1 mark = **3 marks** (any three differences acceptable).

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is meant by "total loss lubrication system"? Give two examples.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Total Loss Lubrication System** is one in which the lubricating oil is **not recovered or recirculated** after performing its lubricating duty — instead, it is *consumed* (burned) along with the fuel in the combustion chamber and expelled through the exhaust.

Because there is no sump, no pump, and no filter, the engine becomes mechanically simpler and lighter, but oil cannot be replenished except by refilling the fuel-oil mixture.

**Two Examples:**
1. **Mist Lubrication System** — used in two-stroke motorcycle engines, where a metered quantity of oil is injected into the intake air-fuel charge. The oil lubricates as a fine mist and is then burned in the combustion chamber.
2. **Petrol Lubrication System** — used in small two-stroke engines (older mopeds, chainsaws, outboard motors), where oil is pre-mixed with petrol in a ratio of 25:1 to 50:1.

**Marking Pattern:** Definition = 1 mark, Two examples = 1 mark each = **3 marks**.

---

### 5.2 PART B: 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> For lubrication system comparison questions, examiners **deduct marks** if the student:
> - Confuses "mist" with "petrol" lubrication (mist is *injected*; petrol is *pre-mixed*).
> - Lists points in only 1–2 words (KTU board policy: each point must be a *complete sentence* with at least *one engineering justification*).
> - Draws the oil circuit but fails to label the **relief valve** and **filter** positions.
> - Omits the unit of oil pressure in numerical answers — always specify **bar** or **kPa**.
> - Calculates pump flow without converting cm³/s to L/min — unit-mismatch loses 1 mark.
> - Forgets to mention that the dry sump is also called a "scavenge system".

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** With a neat functional sketch, explain the **Wet Sump Lubrication System** used in a four-cylinder passenger car engine. List any **four advantages** and **two limitations**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A gear-type oil pump has a displacement of $14 \text{ cm}^3/\text{rev}$ and operates at 2800 rpm. If the volumetric efficiency is 88% and the mechanical efficiency is 90%, calculate: **(i)** the actual oil flow rate in L/min, **(ii)** the pump power input if the discharge pressure is 4.0 bar. **(7 marks)**

### **Model Solution for Question A**

**Part (a) — Wet Sump Lubrication System**

The wet sump lubrication system is the most widely used lubrication architecture in modern four-cylinder passenger car engines.

**Sketch (must be drawn in the answer book — equivalent to the Mermaid flow above):**

```
   ┌──────────┐    ┌──────────┐    ┌──────────┐
   │SUMP 4 L  │ →  │  PUMP    │ →  │ RELIEF V │ → GALLERY
   └──────────┘    └──────────┘    └──────────┘
        ↑              ↑
   RETURN OIL      CAMSHAFT-DRIVEN
        ↑              ↑
   ┌────┴──────────────┴────┐
   │   CRANKSHAFT  &        │
   │   CAMSHAFT BEARINGS    │
   └────┬───────────────────┘
        ↓ splash
   ┌──────────────┐
   │ CYL WALLS    │
   └──────────────┘
```

**Working Principle:**

1. Lubricating oil is stored in the **sump (oil pan)** bolted to the crankcase base.
2. A **gear-type oil pump**, driven by the camshaft, draws oil through a **strainer**.
3. The pump raises oil pressure to **3.0 – 4.0 bar** and pushes it through the **oil filter** into the **main gallery**.
4. From the gallery, oil is delivered to the **main bearings → big-end bearings → camshaft bearings → rocker arms**.
5. Oil leaking from the big-end bearings is **flung outward by centrifugal force**, splash-lubricating the cylinder walls and piston pins.
6. Used oil drains back to the sump under gravity, completing the closed loop.

**Four Advantages:**

1. Simple, compact, and inexpensive to manufacture.
2. Self-contained — no external plumbing or auxiliary tank required.
3. Reliable for typical passenger-car duty cycles.
4. Easy to check oil level using a dipstick and refill at the filler cap.

**Two Limitations:**

1. Oil surge during hard cornering/braking can uncover the pump pickup, causing momentary oil starvation.
2. Limited oil capacity restricts high-performance and sustained high-RPM operation.

**Marks Distribution:** Sketch 2 + Working 2 + Advantages 2 + Limitations 1 = **7 marks**

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution**

**Given:**
- $V_d = 14 \text{ cm}^3/\text{rev}$
- $N = 2800 \text{ rpm}$
- $\eta_v = 0.88$
- $\eta_m = 0.90$
- $\Delta p = 4.0 \text{ bar} = 4.0 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$

**Step 1 — Convert pump speed to rev/s.**

$$N_{rps} = \dfrac{2800}{60} = 46.667 \text{ rev/s}$$

*Conversion logic:* Divide by 60 to convert minutes to seconds.

**Step 2 — Compute theoretical flow $Q_{th}$.**

$$Q_{th} = V_d \cdot N_{rps}$$

$$Q_{th} = 14 \times 46.667 = 653.33 \text{ cm}^3/\text{s}$$

*Conversion logic:* Each revolution displaces 14 cm³, so 46.667 rev/s displaces 14 × 46.667 cm³/s.

**Step 3 — Apply volumetric efficiency to get actual flow.**

$$Q_{actual} = \eta_v \cdot Q_{th} = 0.88 \times 653.33 = 575 \text{ cm}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to L/min.**

$$Q_{actual} = 575 \times 0.06 = 34.5 \text{ L/min}$$

**Step 5 — Compute hydraulic power output of the pump.**

$$P_{hydraulic} = Q_{actual} \times \Delta p$$

We must use SI units: $Q = 575 \text{ cm}^3/\text{s} = 575 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$

$$P_{hydraulic} = 575 \times 10^{-6} \times 4.0 \times 10^5$$

$$P_{hydraulic} = 575 \times 10^{-6} \times 4 \times 10^5 = 230 \text{ W}$$

*Conversion logic:* Pressure × Volume flow rate = Power (W) by First Law.

**Step 6 — Account for mechanical efficiency to find input power.**

$$P_{input} = \dfrac{P_{hydraulic}}{\eta_m} = \dfrac{230}{0.90}$$

$$P_{input} = 255.56 \text{ W}$$

**Final Answers:**
- (i) **Actual flow rate = 34.5 L/min**
- (ii) **Pump power input = 255.56 W ≈ 256 W**

**Marks Distribution:** Flow calc 3 marks + Power calc 4 marks = **7 marks**

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Explain the **Dry Sump Lubrication System** with a functional block diagram. State **any five differences** between dry sump and wet sump systems. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Engine oil enters the oil cooler at 125 °C and leaves at 95 °C. The oil mass flow rate is 1.2 kg/s. If $C_p = 2100 \text{ J/(kg·K)}$, determine: **(i)** the heat rejected by the oil in kW, **(ii)** the volume of oil that passes through the cooler in 1 hour, given oil density $\rho = 870 \text{ kg/m}^3$. **(7 marks)**

### **Model Solution for Question B**

**Part (a) — Dry Sump Lubrication System**

**Functional Block Diagram (must be drawn in answer book):**

```
   ┌─────────────┐    ┌─────────────┐    ┌──────────────┐
   │ CRANKCASE   │ →  │  SCAVENGE   │ →  │  REMOTE      │
   │ (RESIDUAL)  │    │  PUMP x 2   │    │  OIL TANK    │
   └─────────────┘    └─────────────┘    └──────┬───────┘
                                                │
                                                ↓
                                          ┌─────────────┐
                                          │  PRESSURE   │
                                          │  PUMP       │
                                          └──────┬──────┘
                                                 ↓
                                          ┌─────────────┐
                                          │ FILTER +    │
                                          │ RELIEF V    │
                                          └──────┬──────┘
                                                 ↓
                                          ┌─────────────┐
                                          │ MAIN GALLERY│
                                          │ + BEARINGS  │
                                          └─────────────┘
```

**Working Principle:**

1. Oil resides in a **remote external tank** (8–14 L capacity), mounted away from the engine.
2. A **scavenge pump** continuously removes oil from the crankcase and returns it to the remote tank — this prevents oil accumulation and aeration in the crankcase.
3. A separate **pressure pump** draws oil from the remote tank and delivers it through a filter and relief valve into the main oil gallery.
4. Oil lubricates the crankshaft, camshaft, and valve train, then drains back to the crankcase to be scavenged again.
5. The remote tank acts as a de-aerator, allowing entrained air to separate from the oil before recirculation.

**Five Differences between Wet Sump and Dry Sump:**

| # | Parameter | Wet Sump | Dry Sump |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Oil Storage | Sump under crankcase | External remote tank |
| 2 | Number of Pumps | 1 | 2 (scavenge + pressure) |
| 3 | g-Force Tolerance | Poor | Excellent |
| 4 | Oil Capacity | 3.5–7 L | 8–14 L |
| 5 | Cost | Low | Very High |
| 6 | Application | Passenger cars | Racing cars |
| 7 | Centre of Gravity | Higher (oil below engine) | Lower (tank can be repositioned) |

**Marks Distribution:** Diagram 2 + Working 2 + Five differences (1 mark each) = **7 marks**

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution**

**Given:**
- $T_{in} = 125 \text{ °C}$, $T_{out} = 95 \text{ °C}$
- $\dot{m} = 1.2 \text{ kg/s}$
- $C_p = 2100 \text{ J/(kg·K)}$
- $\rho = 870 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $t = 1 \text{ hour} = 3600 \text{ s}$

**Step 1 — Calculate temperature drop.**

$$\Delta T = 125 - 95 = 30 \text{ K}$$

*Conversion logic:* A 30 °C interval is identical to a 30 K interval on an absolute scale.

**Step 2 — Calculate heat rejected by the oil.**

$$H_{oil} = \dot{m} \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T$$

$$H_{oil} = 1.2 \times 2100 \times 30$$

$$H_{oil} = 75{,}600 \text{ W}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to kW.**

$$H_{oil} = \dfrac{75{,}600}{1000} = 75.6 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4 — Calculate total mass of oil in 1 hour.**

$$m_{total} = \dot{m} \cdot t = 1.2 \times 3600 = 4320 \text{ kg}$$

*Conversion logic:* 1 hour = 3600 seconds, so the mass flow rate integrated over time yields total mass.

**Step 5 — Calculate volume using density.**

$$V_{oil} = \dfrac{m_{total}}{\rho}$$

$$V_{oil} = \dfrac{4320}{870}$$

$$V_{oil} = 4.966 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 6 — Convert to litres for board-friendly answer.**

$$V_{oil} = 4.966 \times 1000 = 4966 \text{ L}$$

**Final Answers:**
- (i) **Heat rejected = 75.6 kW**
- (ii) **Volume of oil circulated in 1 hour = 4.966 m³ ≈ 4966 L**

**Marks Distribution:** Heat calc 3 marks + Volume calc 4 marks = **7 marks**

---

### 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE — Lubrication Systems (Module 4):**

- **Definition** of lubrication system: *engineered delivery of clean, pressurized oil to all moving parts at correct pressure, flow, and temperature.*
- **Seven types:** Wet Sump, Dry Sump, Splash, Pressure, Combination (Splash + Pressure), Mist, Petrol.
- **Two broad categories:** *Circulating* (oil reused) vs *Total Loss* (oil consumed).
- **Wet Sump** is used in 95% of passenger cars. Oil stored in sump. Single pump. 3.5–7 L capacity.
- **Dry Sump** uses *two* pumps (scavenge + pressure) and a *remote tank*. Used in racing. Tolerates high g-forces.
- **Splash System** has no pump — uses a dipper on the connecting rod. Used in stationary engines.
- **Pressure System** forces oil under 2.5–5.0 bar through drilled galleries to the main and camshaft bearings.
- **Combination System** = pressure to bearings + splash to cylinders → *standard for modern multi-cylinder engines*.
- **Mist System** = total loss → two-stroke engines → oil injected into intake, then burned.
- **Petrol Lubrication** = total loss → oil pre-mixed with fuel at 25:1 to 50:1 → vintage two-stroke engines.
- **Standard oil pressure:** 2.5–4.5 bar (idle) in passenger cars.
- **Standard oil temperature:** 80–110 °C.
- **Pump flow formula:** $Q_{actual} = \eta_v \cdot V_d \cdot N_{rps}$.
- **Heat removed by oil:** $H_{oil} = \dot{m} \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T$.
- **Viscosity Index formula:** $VI = \dfrac{L - U_{oil}}{H_{ref} - U_{oil}} \times 100$.
- **Min. oil film thickness:** $h_{min} = 0.0002 \cdot D$ (Reynolds rule of thumb).
- **Friction power loss:** $P_{friction} = f \cdot F_n \cdot v$.
- **Sommerfeld number:** $S = \dfrac{\mu \cdot N}{P}$ — predicts hydrodynamic regime.
- **ASTM D-341 equation:** $\log \log(\nu + 0.7) = A - B \cdot \log T$ — viscosity vs. temperature.
- **Full-flow filter** = all oil passes through the filter element (must include bypass valve for cold start).
- **Relief valve** opens at ~4 bar to dump excess oil back to sump.
- **Always specify units** (bar, kPa, L/min, m³/s) — KTU deducts 0.5 mark per missing unit.
- **Drawing etiquette:** Label the oil flow direction with arrows, mark the relief valve, mark the filter, and indicate the location of the sump or external tank.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Examination Mantra:** *"For a 14-mark lubrication question, expect either a 7+7 split (theory + numerical) or a 14-mark full theory comparison. Practice both formats from the past 5 university papers."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: SAE Ratings, Multi Grade Lubricants
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT205 - AUTOMOBILE POWER PLANT (AUTOMOBILE ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** SAE Ratings, Multi Grade Lubricants

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 SAE Viscosity Rating — The Industry Standard for Engine Oils

The **Society of Automotive Engineers (SAE)** has established a globally accepted classification system, designated **SAE J300**, that categorizes engine oils purely on the basis of their **viscosity-temperature characteristics** rather than their additive chemistry, performance level, or brand. This standard allows an engineer, mechanic, or end-user anywhere in the world to compare engine oils objectively, ensuring that the lubricant selected matches the operating regime of the engine.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SAE J300 (Engine Oil Viscosity Classification)** is a *purely rheological* classification. It does **NOT** rate oil quality — that is the job of the **API Service Category** (e.g., API SN, SP) or **ACEA** sequences. Confusing these two rating systems is one of the most common mistakes made in KTU examinations.

### 1.2 The Intuitive Analogy — "The Honey-Water Story"

Imagine two identical bottles — one filled with **honey** and the other with **water**.

- On a **cold winter morning** (say 5 °C), the honey becomes a thick, sluggish gel that pours like cold tar. It will not flow into the engine's narrow oil passages during cranking.
- In contrast, the water remains free-flowing even in the cold.

Now imagine the same two bottles in the **scorching summer heat** of Kerala (say 45 °C). The water becomes dangerously thin and cannot maintain a thick protective film between moving metal surfaces. The honey, however, still flows slowly enough to maintain a protective cushion.

> **The Engineer's Dilemma:** We need an oil that behaves like *water in winter* and like *honey in summer*. This is exactly the problem that **multi-grade lubricants** solve, classified and graded under the **SAE J300** system.

### 1.3 The Two-Window Concept of SAE Grading

SAE J300 grades engine oils through **two separate performance "windows"**:

| Window | Tested Property | Why It Matters |
|---|---|---|
| **Low-Temperature Window** (Winter) | Cold-start viscosity, pumpability | Oil must flow to bearings within seconds of a cold start to prevent dry wear. |
| **High-Temperature Window** (Operating) | Viscosity at 100 °C and 150 °C | Oil must maintain a hydrodynamic/mixed/EHL film at the bearing/journal interface. |

A **single-grade oil** (e.g., SAE 30) satisfies only one of these windows — it has the correct high-temperature viscosity but is too thick when cold. A **multi-grade oil** (e.g., SAE 5W-30) is engineered to satisfy both windows simultaneously.

### 1.4 The Two Critical Temperatures in SAE J300

The two reference temperatures used throughout the SAE J300 standard are:

- **–X °C** (a sub-zero temperature, where X is 35, 30, 25, 20, 15, or 10 depending on the W grade) — represents the **cold crank** condition.
- **100 °C** — the standard reference temperature for **kinematic viscosity** measurement.
- **150 °C** — used for the **High-Temperature High Shear (HTHS)** viscosity, representing the most severe bearing condition.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Definition for the KTU Board Exam:**
> **Viscosity** is the internal friction of a fluid — its resistance to flow. For engine oils, two measures are used:
> 1. **Dynamic Viscosity (η):** measured in **Pa·s** (or centipoise, cP).
> 2. **Kinematic Viscosity (ν = η/ρ):** measured in **mm²/s** (or centistokes, cSt). This is what SAE J300 uses.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Complete SAE J300 Viscosity Grade Table

The SAE J300 classification is divided into **two families** of grades, joined by a "W" to form multi-grade designations.

### 2.1.1 Winter (W) Grades — Defined by Cold Performance

| SAE W Grade | Lowest Expected Operating Temp. (°C) | Max Cold-Crank Viscosity (cP at °C) | Max Cold-Pump Viscosity (cP at °C) | No Yield Stress Down To (°C) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0W | –30 | 6200 at –35 | 60000 at –40 | –40 |
| 5W | –25 | 6600 at –30 | 60000 at –35 | –35 |
| 10W | –20 | 7000 at –25 | 60000 at –30 | –30 |
| 15W | –15 | 7000 at –20 | 60000 at –25 | –25 |
| 20W | –10 | 9500 at –15 | 60000 at –20 | –20 |
| 25W | –5 | 13000 at –10 | 60000 at –15 | –15 |

### 2.1.2 Hot (Non-W) Grades — Defined by High-Temperature Viscosity

| SAE Grade | Kinematic Viscosity @ 100 °C (cSt) — Min | Kinematic Viscosity @ 100 °C (cSt) — Max | HTHS Viscosity @ 150 °C, 10⁶ s⁻¹ (cP) — Min |
|---|---|---|---|
| 20 | 5.6 | 9.3 | 2.6 |
| 30 | 9.3 | 12.5 | 2.9 |
| 40 | 12.5 | 16.3 | 3.5 (older)/2.9* |
| 50 | 16.3 | 21.9 | 3.7 |
| 60 | 21.9 | 26.1 | 3.7 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A multi-grade oil is denoted **W-grade(hot)**. For example, **5W-30** means the oil must meet all **0W** low-temperature tests AND all **30** high-temperature tests. **NOTE:** the 0W in 5W-30 is a typo/legacy; it actually inherits the **5W** cold tests. The standard notation is "lowest-W-grade is the cold grade, and the higher is the hot grade." So 5W-30 means it passes the 5W cold tests and the 30 hot tests.

## 2.2 The Three Critical Cold-Flow Tests Explained

### A. Cold Cranking Simulator (CCS) Viscosity — ASTM D5293
This test simulates the worst case: the moment the starter motor is engaged on a cold engine. The oil must be **thin enough** to allow the crankshaft to be accelerated up to cranking RPM. Maximum viscosity values are specified for each W grade.

### B. Mini-Rotary Viscometer (MRV) — ASTM D4684
This test measures **pumpability** — the oil's ability to be drawn from the sump, through the oil pump, and into the galleries before it reaches the bearings. Air-binding and gel formation are catastrophic failures, so the test uses a *non-rotating pre-cooling phase* (similar to reality). Maximum viscosity is **60 000 cP** and the oil must have **no yield stress**.

### C. Yield Stress
If the oil forms a semi-solid gel that requires a threshold shear to start flowing, the oil pump cannot develop that threshold, leading to **dry-start catastrophic wear**. Hence, the W grades mandate *no yield stress* down to a specific temperature.

## 2.3 The High-Temperature High Shear (HTHS) Viscosity — ASTM D4683 / D4741

At the bearing-crankshaft interface, the oil film experiences shear rates of the order of **10⁶ s⁻¹**, and local temperatures reach 150 °C. The HTHS viscosity is the most realistic measure of whether the oil film will hold. The minimum HTHS viscosity is a function of the hot grade and ranges from **2.6 cP to 3.7 cP**.

> [!NOTE]
> This is why modern low-friction engine oils (e.g., 0W-20) target an HTHS of **≥ 2.6 cP** — they are deliberately "weak" in the hot window to reduce hydrodynamic friction, but they still pass.

## 2.4 Multi-Grade Lubricants — The Polymer Solution

A **multi-grade oil** is a **base oil + Viscosity Index Improver (VII)** system. The VII is a high-molecular-weight polymer that solves a very clever physical puzzle:

| Condition | Polymer Behaviour | Net Effect on Oil Viscosity |
|---|---|---|
| **Cold** (low T) | Polymer coils up into a tight ball | Polymer occupies small volume → **does not contribute much to viscosity** → base oil dominates → **oil stays thin** (good for cold start) |
| **Hot** (high T) | Polymer uncoils into a long chain | Polymer occupies large volume → **adds significant viscosity** → base oil becomes thin but polymer compensates → **oil stays thick** (good for hot film) |

The net result: the oil has a **flatter viscosity-temperature curve** than a mono-grade oil.

### 2.4.1 Common VII Chemistry Used in Modern Oils
- **OCP** (Olefin Copolymer) — economical, good for passenger cars.
- **PMA** (Polymethacrylate) — superior, used in synthetic and high-performance oils.
- **PIB** (Polyisobutylene) — used in some heavy-duty diesel oils.
- **Styrene-Diene Copolymers** — used in industrial gear oils.

### 2.4.2 Advantages of Multi-Grade Oils
1. **Year-round usability** in tropical/sub-tropical climates (e.g., Kerala's 25–35 °C range with monsoon humidity).
2. **Reduced cold-start wear** — the engine bearings are oiled within 2–3 seconds of cranking.
3. **Better fuel economy** — lower viscous drag at operating temperature.
4. **Single oil inventory** — vehicle owners need not change oil seasonally.
5. **Wider operational temperature window** than any single-grade oil.

### 2.4.3 Limitations and Failure Modes
- **Shear Degradation (Permanent Viscosity Loss):** The polymer chains can be mechanically broken at the highly loaded cam-follower interface, causing the oil to **revert** to its base oil's viscosity (loss of multi-grade behaviour). This is monitored by **ASTM D6278** (KRL Tapered Bearing Test) measuring **Shear Stability Index (SSI)**.
- **Higher Cost** than mono-grade oils.
- **Additive Compatibility** — VIIs can interact negatively with some detergent additives.

## 2.5 Viscosity Index (VI) — Quantifying the "Flatness" of the V-T Curve

The **Viscosity Index (VI)** is an *empirical, dimensionless number* developed by **E.W. Dean and G.H.B. Davis in 1929** to rank the rate at which a lubricant's viscosity changes with temperature.

- A **high VI** → oil is **less sensitive** to temperature change (the holy grail).
- A **low VI** → oil **thins aggressively** with rising temperature (e.g., naphthenic crudes).

### 2.5.1 The Two Reference Oils Used in the VI Calculation
- **Oil L (VI = 0):** A poor oil from the Gulf Coast (Pennsylvania) — very steep V-T curve.
- **Oil H (VI = 100):** A good oil from Pennsylvania crude — gentle V-T curve.

The VI of any test oil is **linearly interpolated** between these two reference oils that share the same kinematic viscosity at 100 °C.

## 2.6 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **single most important cheat-sheet** for KTU Module 4 numerical problems. Memorize the formulas, the units, and the conditions.

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Kinematic Viscosity | $\nu = \dfrac{\eta}{\rho}$ | Ratio of dynamic viscosity to density | $\text{mm}^2/\text{s} = \text{cSt}$ |
| 2 | Dynamic Viscosity | $\eta$ | Resistance to flow | $\text{Pa}\cdot\text{s} = 1000\,\text{cP}$ |
| 3 | Viscosity Index (VI) | $VI = \dfrac{L - U}{L - H} \times 100$ | Linear interpolation between two reference oils (L & H) sharing same $\nu_{100}$ | dimensionless |
| 4 | VI Upper Oil Reference (H) | $H$ | Kinematic viscosity at 40 °C of VI=100 oil with given $\nu_{100}$ | cSt |
| 5 | VI Lower Oil Reference (L) | $L$ | Kinematic viscosity at 40 °C of VI=0 oil with given $\nu_{100}$ | cSt |
| 6 | Test Oil's 40 °C viscosity | $U$ | Kinematic viscosity at 40 °C of the oil under test | cSt |
| 7 | Shear Stability Index (SSI) | $SSI = \dfrac{\nu_i - \nu_f}{\nu_i - \nu_o} \times 100$ | Permanent viscosity loss from mechanical shear | dimensionless % |
| 8 | CCS Viscosity Limit (W grade) | $\eta_{CCS} \le \eta_{CCS,max}$ | Cold-cranking limit for W grade | cP at –X °C |
| 9 | Multi-grade notation | "5W-30" | Meets 5W low-T tests **and** 30 high-T tests | — |
| 10 | HTHS limit | $\eta_{HTHS} \ge 2.6\,\text{cP}$ (for SAE 20) up to $\ge 3.7\,\text{cP}$ (for SAE 50) | Minimum film thickness constraint | cP at 150 °C |

> [!WARNING]
> In the KTU exam, the standard reference table of L and H values (as a function of $\nu_{100}$) will usually be **provided in the question paper**. Do **NOT** memorize the L and H table — you will not be asked to derive it. Focus instead on the interpolation formula and the proper unit conversions.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation 1 — Calculating the Viscosity Index (VI)

### Problem Statement
An automotive engine oil has the following measured kinematic viscosities:
- Kinematic viscosity at 40 °C, $U$ = **92.0 cSt**
- Kinematic viscosity at 100 °C, $\nu_{100}$ = **10.0 cSt**

From the standard reference tables (provided), for an oil of $\nu_{100} = 10.0\,\text{cSt}$:
- Viscosity of Oil L (VI = 0) at 40 °C: $L$ = **152 cSt**
- Viscosity of Oil H (VI = 100) at 40 °C: $H$ = **100 cSt**

**Calculate the Viscosity Index of this oil.**

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Identify the given quantities and the target.

We are given:
- Test oil: $U = 92.0\,\text{cSt}$ at 40 °C
- Test oil: $\nu_{100} = 10.0\,\text{cSt}$ at 100 °C
- Reference L oil: $L = 152\,\text{cSt}$ at 40 °C
- Reference H oil: $H = 100\,\text{cSt}$ at 40 °C

Target: $VI = ?$

**Step 2:** State the Dean–Davis VI formula.

$$
VI = \frac{L - U}{L - H} \times 100
$$

This formula represents the **fractional position** of the test oil between the poor reference (L) and the good reference (H), both measured at 40 °C and sharing the test oil's 100 °C viscosity.

**Step 3:** Substitute the known values into the numerator.

$$
L - U = 152 - 92.0 = 60.0\,\text{cSt}
$$

**Step 4:** Substitute the known values into the denominator.

$$
L - H = 152 - 100 = 52.0\,\text{cSt}
$$

**Step 5:** Form the ratio.

$$
\frac{L - U}{L - H} = \frac{60.0}{52.0} = 1.1538
$$

**Step 6:** Multiply by 100 to get the Viscosity Index.

$$
VI = 1.1538 \times 100 = 115.38
$$

**Step 7:** Final interpretation.

$$
\boxed{VI \approx 115}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A VI of **115 is higher than the original 1929 scale ceiling of 100.** This is because, in modern times, **VI improver additives** and **synthetic base stocks (Groups III, IV, V)** can exceed the natural reference oils. For VI > 100, the formula changes to a logarithmic one (ASTM D2270), but in KTU 2024 board exams, the linear Dean–Davis formula is sufficient when L and H are both given in the table. The examiner expects you to **report the value as > 100** if so indicated, or compute the linear approximation.

### Physical Interpretation of the Result
The test oil with $VI = 115$ thins *much less* with rising temperature than a 100-VI Pennsylvania crude reference oil. This is a **multi-grade engine oil behaviour** — the base stock plus polymer VI improver has flattened its V-T curve. In a KTV-compliant passenger car, this oil would perform well in both monsoon-morning cold starts and high-summer highway cruising.

---

## 3.2 Worked Derivation 2 — Determining the SAE Grade of a Multi-Grade Oil

### Problem Statement
A synthetic engine oil has the following rheological data:

- **Kinematic viscosity at 100 °C:** $\nu_{100} = 10.8\,\text{cSt}$
- **Cold-Cranking Simulator (CCS) viscosity at –30 °C:** $\eta_{CCS} = 6450\,\text{cP}$
- **Mini-Rotary Viscometer (MRV) viscosity at –35 °C:** $\eta_{MRV} = 58\,000\,\text{cP}$ (no yield stress)
- **HTHS viscosity at 150 °C, $10^6\,\text{s}^{-1}$:** $\eta_{HTHS} = 3.0\,\text{cP}$

**Determine the correct SAE J300 viscosity grade of this oil.**

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Evaluate the *hot (non-W) grade* from $\nu_{100}$ and HTHS.

The SAE hot grade 30 window (from the table in Section 2.1.2) requires:

$$
9.3 \le \nu_{100} \le 12.5\,\text{cSt}
$$

Our test value: $\nu_{100} = 10.8\,\text{cSt}$. This falls **within** the 30-grade window. ✓

The HTHS minimum for SAE 30 is **2.9 cP**. Our test value: $\eta_{HTHS} = 3.0\,\text{cP}$. ✓

> Conclusion: The oil **passes** the hot 30-grade tests.

**Step 2:** Evaluate the *cold (W) grade* from CCS, MRV, and yield stress.

Test of the **5W** low-temperature window:

- CCS limit: $\eta_{CCS} \le 6600\,\text{cP}$ at –30 °C.
- Test value: $6450\,\text{cP} < 6600\,\text{cP}$. ✓
- MRV limit: $\eta_{MRV} \le 60\,000\,\text{cP}$ at –35 °C.
- Test value: $58\,000\,\text{cP} < 60\,000\,\text{cP}$. ✓
- Yield stress: must be **zero** down to –35 °C. Test states: no yield stress. ✓

The 10W window has a less stringent CCS limit (7000 cP at –25 °C), but the test result already satisfies the **stricter** 5W window — so the oil earns **5W**.

**Step 3:** Combine the two windows into the multi-grade designation.

$$
\boxed{\text{SAE Grade} = 5\text{W-}30}
$$

### Engineering Significance
The fact that this oil **passes 5W AND 30** is the entire purpose of a multi-grade oil: it has the **fluidity of a 5W oil** (allowing the starter to crank in winter) and the **bearing-film strength of a 30 oil** (protecting the journal bearings at operating temperature).

---

## 3.3 Worked Derivation 3 — Shear Stability Index (SSI) of a Multi-Grade Oil

### Problem Statement
A 10W-40 engine oil is subjected to 30 passes through a Kurt-Orbahn tapered-roller bearing rig (KRL test, ASTM D6278). The following data are obtained:

- **Initial kinematic viscosity at 100 °C:** $\nu_i = 14.0\,\text{cSt}$
- **Final kinematic viscosity at 100 °C after 30 passes:** $\nu_f = 11.6\,\text{cSt}$
- **Base oil kinematic viscosity at 100 °C (no VII):** $\nu_o = 6.4\,\text{cSt}$

**Calculate the Shear Stability Index (SSI) of the polymer VII in this oil.**

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Write down the SSI formula.

$$
SSI = \frac{\nu_i - \nu_f}{\nu_i - \nu_o} \times 100
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the numerator — the *loss* in viscosity.

$$
\nu_i - \nu_f = 14.0 - 11.6 = 2.4\,\text{cSt}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the denominator — the *potential* loss (how much VII was originally present).

$$
\nu_i - \nu_o = 14.0 - 6.4 = 7.6\,\text{cSt}
$$

**Step 4:** Form the ratio and multiply by 100.

$$
SSI = \frac{2.4}{7.6} \times 100 = 31.58\,\%
$$

**Step 5:** Final result and interpretation.

$$
\boxed{SSI \approx 31.6\%}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **lower SSI** is **better** — it means the polymer is **shear-resistant** and the oil will retain its multi-grade behaviour throughout its service life. A **high SSI** (e.g., 50 %+) means the polymer chains break easily, the oil reverts to base-oil viscosity, and the high-temperature protection degrades over time. **ACEA** and **API SP** specifications impose SSI limits for "stay-in-grade" claims.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — SAE J300 Classification Logic

The following Mermaid flowchart captures the decision logic an engineer applies when classifying an engine oil under SAE J300.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Measure Oil Viscosity Properties] --> B{CCS at -X C and MRV at -X-5 C}
    B --> C{Meets W-Grade Cold Tests?}
    C -->|No| D[Oil Fails to be a W-Grade]
    C -->|Yes| E[Assign W-Grade: 0W, 5W, 10W, 15W, 20W, 25W]
    E --> F{Measured 100 C and HTHS at 150 C}
    F --> G{Kinematic 100 C within 9.3-12.5 cSt and HTHS more than 2.9 cP?}
    G -->|No| H[Recheck Hot Window]
    G -->|Yes| I[Assign Hot-Grade: 30]
    I --> J[Combine into Multi-Grade: 5W-30]
    H --> K{Other Hot-Grade Window?}
    K -->|Yes 20| L[Multi-Grade: 5W-20]
    K -->|Yes 40| M[Multi-Grade: 5W-40]
    K -->|Yes 50| N[Multi-Grade: 5W-50]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Viscosity Index (VI) Concept

This diagram explains the physical meaning of the VI by showing how the test oil is compared against the L and H reference oils on a viscosity-temperature plane.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph HOT_HOT_ZONE [Hot Reference: 100 C Plane]
        H100[nu_100 of Test Oil] --> SHARED[All Three Oils Share Same nu_100]
    end

    subgraph COLD_ZONE [Cold Reference: 40 C Plane]
        L40[Oil L: VI is 0, U is 152 cSt]
        H40[Oil H: VI is 100, U is 100 cSt]
        U40[Test Oil: VI is unknown, U is 92 cSt]
    end

    SHARED --> L40
    SHARED --> H40
    SHARED --> U40

    L40 --> CALC[Apply VI formula: VI is L minus U over L minus H times 100]
    H40 --> CALC
    U40 --> CALC
    CALC --> RESULT[Result: VI is 115 - Excellent multi-grade behaviour]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Multi-Grade Oil Polymer Behaviour

The Viscosity Index Improver (VII) is the secret of multi-grade oils. This diagram shows how the polymer coil changes shape with temperature.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[VII Polymer in Base Oil] --> B{Engine Temperature}

    B -->|Cold: Minus 20 C| C[Polymer Coils into Tight Ball]
    C --> D[Small Hydrodynamic Volume]
    D --> E[Base Oil Dominates Viscosity]
    E --> F[Oil Flows Easily Through Pump and Galleries]
    F --> F1[Result: Thin Oil for Cold Start]

    B -->|Hot: 100 C| G[Polymer Uncoils into Long Chain]
    G --> H[Large Hydrodynamic Volume]
    H --> I[Polymer Adds Significant Viscosity]
    I --> J[Compensates for Base Oil Thinning]
    J --> J1[Result: Thick Oil for Hot Bearing Film]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Function-Level Architecture of Engine Lubrication

Because the physical drawing of a complete engine oil circuit is too complex for a Mermaid flow diagram, we use a **block-level functional architecture** showing the relationship between SAE viscosity grades and engine subsystems.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ENGINE_BLOCK [Internal Combustion Engine]
        SUMP[Oil Sump] --> PUMP[Oil Pump]
        PUMP --> FILTER[Oil Filter]
        FILTER --> GALLERY[Main Oil Gallery]
        GALLERY --> MAIN_BEAR[Main Bearings]
        GALLERY --> CAM_BEAR[Camshaft Bearings]
        GALLERY --> PISTON_PINS[Piston Pin Bosses]
        GALLERY --> VALVE_TRAIN[Valve Train Cam Follower]
        MAIN_BEAR --> DRAIN1[Return to Sump]
        CAM_BEAR --> DRAIN1
        PISTON_PINS --> DRAIN1
        VALVE_TRAIN --> DRAIN2[Return to Sump]
    end

    subgraph OIL_PROP [Lubricant Properties]
        SAE_GRADE[SAE J300 Grade: 5W-30]
        VII[Viscosity Index Improver: PMA or OCP]
        ADD[Additive Package: Detergent, Anti-Wear, Antioxidant]
    end

    SAE_GRADE --> SUMP
    VII --> SUMP
    ADD --> SUMP

    VALVE_TRAIN -.->|Highest Shear and Temperature| SSI_CHECK[Shear Stability Test]
    SSI_CHECK --> OIL_LIFE[Oil Service Life]
```
