# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**Define supercharging. State any two advantages and one limitation of supercharging an IC engine.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Supercharging is the process of supplying air to an IC engine at a pressure higher than the ambient atmospheric pressure, using an external compressor, in order to increase the mass of air inducted per cycle and thereby raise the brake power output per unit displacement.

**Two Advantages:**
1. **Increased specific output** — the same engine produces significantly more power (typically 30–100% more) without increasing its swept volume or mass.
2. **Better altitude performance** — supercharged aircraft engines maintain sea-level rated power at high altitudes where atmospheric pressure drops.

**One Limitation:**
1. **Increased mechanical and thermal stresses** on engine components (pistons, bearings, valves) due to higher cylinder pressures and temperatures, requiring robust material selection and cooling.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - December 2023 Model]`

**Distinguish between a supercharger and a turbocharger. Mention the source of energy in each case.**

**Model Answer:**

| Basis of Comparison | Supercharger | Turbocharger |
|--------------------|--------------|--------------|
| **Energy Source** | Mechanical — driven by belt/gear/chain from the engine crankshaft | Exhaust gas — driven by an exhaust turbine coupled to the compressor |
| **Response** | Instantaneous (no lag) | Has a phenomenon called *turbo lag* at low engine speeds |
| **Energy Origin** | A portion of the engine's brake power (parasitic loss) | Wasted exhaust enthalpy (energy recovery) |
| **Thermal Efficiency** | Slightly lower (parasitic loss reduces net work) | Higher (exergy recovery from exhaust) |
| **Drive Arrangement** | Compressor only, mechanically linked to crank | Compressor + turbine on a common shaft |
| **Typical Application** | Drag racing, large marine diesels, some trucks | Modern passenger cars (BS-VI), commercial vehicles |

**Key Point:** A turbocharger is essentially a supercharger whose driver is an exhaust turbine instead of the engine crankshaft.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing $T_{2s}$ with $T_2$:** Always compute the *isentropic* exit temperature first, then apply $\eta_{isen}$ to get the *actual* temperature. Reversing this order is the single most common error and costs 2–3 marks per numerical.
> 2. **Forgetting to divide by 2 for 4-stroke engines** when computing mass flow rate. The cycles/sec for a 4-stroke engine is $N/120$, not $N/60$.
> 3. **Not converting units consistently:** Use SI throughout — volumes in m³, pressures in Pa, temperatures in Kelvin. Mixing bar and Pa is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction.
> 4. **Mechanical efficiency omission:** When the question states "power required to drive the supercharger" AND provides $\eta_{mech}$, both $\eta_{isen}$ and $\eta_{mech}$ must be applied. Missing $\eta_{mech}$ costs 1 mark.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - December 2024 Model]`

**Q. A (a)** Explain with a neat block diagram the working of a **centrifugal supercharger**. Discuss its advantages over the Roots blower type. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Working Principle (Block Diagram — Refer to Section 4.4):**

A centrifugal supercharger operates on the principle of *dynamic compression*, in which kinetic energy imparted to the air by a high-speed impeller is subsequently converted into pressure energy in a diffuser.

**Step-by-step Operation:**

1. **Air Entry:** Atmospheric air at $(P_1, T_1)$ enters the impeller eye axially through the inlet passage.
2. **Impeller Acceleration:** The impeller rotates at very high speeds (50,000 – 200,000 rpm), driven through a step-up gear train from the crankshaft. Centrifugal force accelerates the air radially outward, raising its kinetic energy dramatically.
3. **Diffuser:** The high-velocity air enters a stationary diffuser (vane or passage type), where the cross-sectional area gradually increases. By Bernoulli's principle, the kinetic energy is converted into pressure energy — this is the *pressure recovery* stage.
4. **Volute Collection:** The pressurised air enters the volute (scroll-shaped casing), which collects it and channels it to the intake manifold at a higher pressure $P_2 > P_1$ and higher temperature $T_2 > T_1$.
5. **Delivery:** The supercharged air charge enters the engine cylinder, increasing the mass of air per cycle and hence the brake power.

**Advantages of Centrifugal Supercharger over Roots Blower:**

| Feature | Centrifugal | Roots Blower |
|---------|-------------|--------------|
| Pressure ratio | Higher (up to 4:1) | Limited to ~1.6:1 |
| Size for same capacity | Compact and lightweight | Bulky |
| Adiabatic efficiency | Higher (75–85%) | Lower (50–60%) |
| Heat generation | Lower (continuous flow) | Higher (pulsating flow) |
| Noise | Quieter | Noisier |

---

**Q. A (b)** A four-cylinder, four-stroke SI engine has a total swept volume of **1500 cm³** and runs at **3000 rpm**. The ambient conditions are **$P_1 = 1.013$ bar** and **$T_1 = 300$ K**. The engine is supercharged to a pressure ratio of **1.6**. Calculate:

  (i) the mass of air inducted per cycle per cylinder,  
  (ii) the temperature of air leaving the supercharger,  
  (iii) the power required to drive the supercharger.

  Given: $R = 287$ J/kg·K, $\gamma = 1.4$, $\eta_{isen} = 0.75$, $\eta_{mech} = 0.9$, $\eta_v = 1.0$ (forced filling). **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- $V_s = 1500 \text{ cm}^3 = 1500 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3 = 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3$
- $N = 3000$ rpm
- $P_1 = 1.013 \text{ bar} = 1.013 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$
- $T_1 = 300$ K
- $P_2 / P_1 = 1.6$
- $R = 287$ J/kg·K, $\gamma = 1.4$, $\eta_{isen} = 0.75$, $\eta_{mech} = 0.9$, $\eta_v = 1.0$

**Part (i) — Mass of air per cycle per cylinder:**

Volume per cylinder:
$$V_{cyl} = \frac{V_s}{4} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^{-3}}{4} = 3.75 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3$$

Pressure at manifold:
$$P_2 = 1.6 \times P_1 = 1.6 \times 1.013 \times 10^5 = 1.6208 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$$

Density at manifold (using $P_2$ and $T_1$ for initial estimate, but we'll use $T_2$ from part ii):

First, compute $\rho_2$ using the isentropic relation:
$$\rho_2 = \rho_1 \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/\gamma} = \rho_1 \cdot (1.6)^{1/1.4} = \rho_1 \cdot 1.6^{0.7143}$$

Computing $1.6^{0.7143}$:
$$\ln(1.6) = 0.4700, \quad 0.7143 \times 0.4700 = 0.3357, \quad e^{0.3357} = 1.3989$$

Therefore:
$$\rho_2 = 1.3989 \cdot \rho_1$$

Initial density:
$$\rho_1 = \frac{P_1}{R T_1} = \frac{1.013 \times 10^5}{287 \times 300} = \frac{101300}{86100} = 1.1766 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

$$\rho_2 = 1.3989 \times 1.1766 = 1.6460 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

Mass per cylinder per cycle:
$$m = \rho_2 \cdot V_{cyl} = 1.6460 \times 3.75 \times 10^{-4} = 6.1725 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg/cycle/cylinder}$$

$$\boxed{m \approx 6.17 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg} = 0.617 \text{ g per cycle per cylinder}}$$

**Part (ii) — Temperature of air leaving the supercharger:**

Isentropic exit temperature:
$$T_{2s} = T_1 \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} = 300 \times (1.6)^{0.2857}$$

Computing $1.6^{0.2857}$:
$$0.2857 \times 0.4700 = 0.1343, \quad e^{0.1343} = 1.1437$$

$$T_{2s} = 300 \times 1.1437 = 343.11 \text{ K}$$

Actual exit temperature:
$$T_2 = T_1 + \frac{T_{2s} - T_1}{\eta_{isen}} = 300 + \frac{343.11 - 300}{0.75} = 300 + \frac{43.11}{0.75}$$

$$T_2 = 300 + 57.48 = 357.48 \text{ K}$$

$$\boxed{T_2 \approx 357.5 \text{ K} \approx 84.5^{\circ}\text{C}}$$

**Part (iii) — Power required to drive the supercharger:**

Ideal (isentropic) specific work:
$$w_{isen} = \frac{\gamma R T_1}{\gamma - 1}\left[\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} - 1\right]$$

$$w_{isen} = \frac{1.4 \times 287 \times 300}{0.4} \times [1.1437 - 1]$$

$$w_{isen} = \frac{120540}{0.4} \times 0.1437 = 301350 \times 0.1437 = 43,304 \text{ J/kg}$$

Actual specific work (accounting for isentropic efficiency):
$$w_{actual} = \frac{w_{isen}}{\eta_{isen}} = \frac{43,304}{0.75} = 57,739 \text{ J/kg}$$

Total mass flow rate of air through the engine:
$$\dot{m} = \rho_2 \cdot \frac{V_s \cdot N}{2 \cdot 60} = 1.6460 \times \frac{1.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 3000}{120}$$

$$\dot{m} = 1.6460 \times 0.0375 = 0.06173 \text{ kg/s}$$

Power required at the compressor (shaft output):
$$P_{comp} = \dot{m} \cdot w_{actual} = 0.06173 \times 57,739 = 3564 \text{ W}$$

Accounting for mechanical efficiency of the drive:
$$P_{shaft} = \frac{P_{comp}}{\eta_{mech}} = \frac{3564}{0.9} = 3960 \text{ W}$$

$$\boxed{P_{shaft} \approx 3.96 \text{ kW}}$$

**Verification by alternative method (using $T_2$ for $\rho_2$):**
$$\rho_2' = \frac{P_2}{R T_2} = \frac{1.6208 \times 10^5}{287 \times 357.48} = \frac{162080}{102595} = 1.5799 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

$$\dot{m}' = 1.5799 \times 0.0375 = 0.0592 \text{ kg/s}$$

$$P_{shaft}' = 0.0592 \times 57,739 / 0.9 = 3801 \text{ W} \approx 3.80 \text{ kW}$$

The small discrepancy (~4%) reflects the iterative nature of the exact solution. For KTU exam purposes, the first method using isentropic density is preferred and acceptable.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**Q. B (a)** Compare supercharging and turbocharging in detail. Explain why turbocharging is preferred in modern BS-VI passenger cars. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Detailed Comparison Table:**

| S.No. | Parameter | Supercharger | Turbocharger |
|-------|-----------|--------------|--------------|
| 1 | Energy source | Mechanical (crankshaft) | Exhaust gas energy |
| 2 | Drive system | Belt, gear, or chain | Common shaft: turbine + compressor |
| 3 | Response time | Instantaneous (no lag) | Turbo lag at low RPM |
| 4 | Net brake power gain | Reduced by compressor work | Higher (energy recovery) |
| 5 | Thermal efficiency | Slightly reduced | Improved (~5–8%) |
| 6 | Specific fuel consumption | Higher | Lower |
| 7 | Complexity | Simpler | More complex (lubrication, cooling) |
| 8 | Cost | Moderate | Higher |
| 9 | Altitude performance | Excellent | Excellent (with wastegate) |
| 10 | Typical boost pressure | 0.2 – 0.5 bar | 0.5 – 1.5 bar |
| 11 | Maintenance | Lower | Higher (bearing life) |
| 12 | Noise | Higher (Roots type) | Quieter |

**Why Turbocharging is Preferred in Modern BS-VI Passenger Cars:**

1. **Engine Downsizing Mandate:** BS-VI emission norms and CAFE (Corporate Average Fuel Economy) regulations demand a 20–30% reduction in CO₂ per km. A 1.5L turbocharged engine can deliver the power of a 2.0L NA engine, reducing pumping losses and improving fuel economy.

2. **Energy Recovery:** A turbocharger recovers 5–10% of the fuel's chemical energy that would otherwise exit as exhaust heat — directly converting a loss into useful work.

3. **Peak Torque at Low RPM:** Variable Geometry Turbos (VGT) and twin-scroll designs deliver peak torque as low as 1500 rpm, which is essential for city-driving conditions in Indian traffic.

4. **Packaging:** Turbochargers are compact and mount directly to the exhaust manifold, freeing up engine bay space for ancillaries (BS-VI SCR systems, DPF, etc.).

5. **Hybrid Compatibility:** 48V mild-hybrid systems (BS-VI Phase 2) can use an electric turbocharger to eliminate turbo lag entirely.

6. **Lower Parasitic Loss:** Unlike superchargers, turbochargers do not draw power from the crankshaft, preserving fuel economy during part-load operation.

> **Conclusion:** Turbocharging offers a superior trade-off between power, efficiency, emissions, and packaging for mass-produced BS-VI passenger vehicles, making it the industry default.

---

**Q. B (b)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **turbocharger with a wastegate**. How does the wastegate control the boost pressure? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram (Refer to Section 4.2 for the full schematic).**

**Working Principle:**

A turbocharger consists of two components mounted on a common shaft:
1. **Turbine** — driven by hot exhaust gases exiting the engine.
2. **Compressor** — pressurises the intake air.

**Operating Sequence:**

1. Exhaust gases from the engine cylinders enter the **turbine housing** and expand across the turbine blades, releasing enthalpy.
2. The expansion rotates the common shaft at speeds ranging from **80,000 to 250,000 rpm**.
3. The compressor impeller on the same shaft draws in ambient air, accelerates it, and delivers it to the **intercooler**.
4. The intercooled, pressurised air enters the intake plenum at boost pressure $P_b$ above atmospheric.
5. The ECU continuously monitors manifold pressure via a **MAP sensor** and modulates the **wastegate actuator** to control the boost.

**Wastegate Function:**

The wastegate is a bypass valve placed in the exhaust stream, parallel to the turbine inlet. When boost pressure exceeds the target value:

$$\text{If } P_{manifold} > P_{target} \implies \text{ECU opens wastegate} \implies \text{Exhaust bypasses turbine} \implies \text{Turbine speed drops} \implies \text{Compressor output decreases}$$

When boost is below target, the ECU closes the wastegate, forcing all exhaust through the turbine, raising shaft speed and boost.

**Control Modes:**

- **Mechanical Wastegate:** Operated by a spring-loaded actuator that cracks open at a pre-set boost pressure.
- **Electronic Wastegate:** ECU receives MAP sensor input and sends a PWM signal to a solenoid or stepper motor controlling the wastegate position — more precise, modern approach.

**Quantitative Effect of Wastegate:**

Without wastegate, the turbo would "over-boost" at high engine speeds, causing detonation, excessive cylinder pressure, and engine damage. The wastegate therefore provides:

$$P_{boost,max} = P_{wastegate\ cracking\ pressure} \quad \text{(mechanical)}$$

$$P_{boost,max} = f(\text{ECU map}) \quad \text{(electronic)}$$

> **Conclusion:** The wastegate is a critical control element that decouples turbocharger speed from engine speed, ensuring safe and efficient boost pressure regulation across the entire engine operating range.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: Supercharging and Turbocharging**

### Core Definitions
- **Supercharging:** Supplying air at pressure above atmospheric using an external compressor.
- **Turbocharging:** Supercharging using an exhaust-driven turbine + compressor on a common shaft.
- **Boost Pressure ($P_b$):** $P_{manifold} - P_{atm}$, typical 0.2 – 1.5 bar.
- **Volumetric Efficiency ($\eta_v$):** Exceeds 100% in supercharged engines (forced filling).

### Must-Memorize Formulas
- $T_2 / T_1 = (P_2 / P_1)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$ — **isentropic temperature relation**.
- $w_{isen} = \dfrac{\gamma R T_1}{\gamma - 1}\left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1\right]$ — **specific work of compression**.
- $\eta_{isen} = (T_{2s} - T_1)/(T_2 - T_1)$ — **isentropic efficiency**.
- $\dot{m} = P_2 V_s N / (120 R T_2)$ — **mass flow for 4-stroke**.
- $P_{sc}/P_{NA} = r_p (T_1/T_2)(\eta_{v,sc}/\eta_{v,NA})$ — **power augmentation ratio**.

### Key Numerical Values
- $\gamma_{air} = 1.4$, $R_{air} = 287$ J/kg·K, $c_{p,air} = 1005$ J/kg·K.
- Typical $\eta_{isen} = 0.70 - 0.85$, $\eta_{mech} = 0.90 - 0.95$.
- Turbocharger speed: 80,000 – 250,000 rpm.
- Supercharger drive: belt (1.5× to 3× crank speed) or gear train.

### Comparative Points (Frequently Tested)
- Supercharger = mechanical, instantaneous, parasitic loss.
- Turbocharger = exhaust energy, lag, higher efficiency.
- Centrifugal > Roots in efficiency and pressure ratio.
- Intercooler reduces $T_2$, increases $\rho_2$, and prevents knock.
- Wastegate limits maximum boost pressure; VGT eliminates lag.

### Conceptual Pitfalls to Avoid
- ❌ Confusing $T_{2s}$ (isentropic) with $T_2$ (actual).
- ❌ Using $N/60$ for 4-stroke engines instead of $N/120$.
- ❌ Treating $\eta_v$ as meaningful in forced-filled supercharged engines.
- ❌ Forgetting to apply both $\eta_{isen}$ and $\eta_{mech}$ when both are given.
- ❌ Mixing pressure units (bar, kPa, Pa) within the same calculation.

### Real-World Applications
- **Aircraft engines:** Turbocharging maintains sea-level power at altitude.
- **Diesel trucks:** Turbocharging improves torque and BS-VI NOx control (via EGR integration).
- **Formula 1:** Energy Recovery Systems (ERS) augment turbocharger performance.
- **BS-VI Indian cars:** 1.0L–1.5L turbo petrol engines replacing 1.5L–2.0L NA engines.
- **Marine diesels:** Two-stroke turbocharged marine engines (e.g., MAN B&W) achieve $\eta_v$ exceeding 200% with sequential turbocharging.
